title: A-B Trust
definition: An A-B trust is a joint trust created by a married couple in order to minimize estate taxes. It is formed with each spouse placingassetsin thetrustand naming the finalbeneficiary—that is, anyone except the other spouse. The trust gets its name from the fact that it splits into two separate entities when one spouse dies. Trust A is the survivor's trust and trust B is the decedent's trust. A-B trusts have become less popular because the estate tax exemption covers most estates.

title: A-Shares
definition: China A-shares are the stock shares of mainlandChina-based companiesthat trade on the two Chinese stock exchanges, the Shanghai Stock Exchange (SSE) and the Shenzhen Stock Exchange (SZSE). Historically, China A-shares were only available for purchase by mainland citizens due to China's restrictions on foreign investment.

title: AAA
definition: The AAA credit rating is the highest rating possible forbondsand indicates a bond issuer's noteworthycreditworthinessand financial strength. It communicates to investors a minimal risk of default. Leading agencies such asStandard & Poor’s (S&P)andFitch Ratingsissue the AAA rating.Moody’sissues its Aaa rating under the same circumstances. These ratings can change with changes in financial conditions. Both S&P and Fitch have downgraded the credit rating of the United States due to the agencies' views of the nation's financial stability and the government's questionable policy decisions.

title: AARP
definition: AARP is a nonprofit organization that addresses and advocates for the needs of people who are 50 and older.

title: Abenomics
definition: Abenomics is the nickname for the economic policies set out for Japan in 2012 when prime minister Shinzo Abe came into power for a second time. Abenomics involved increasing the nation’smoney supply, boosting government spending, and enacting reforms to make the Japanese economy more competitive. The Economist outlined the program as a "mix ofreflation, government spending, and a growth strategy designed to jolt the economy out of suspended animation that has gripped it for more than two decades."

title: Ability-to-Pay Taxation
definition: The ability-to-pay principle is also known as progressive taxation.

title: Abnormal Return
definition: An abnormal return is one that deviates from an investment'sexpectedreturn. The presence of abnormal returns, which can be either positive or negative, helps investors determine risk-adjusted performance. Abnormal returns may result from chance, unforeseen events, or bad actors. Cumulative abnormal return (CAR) sums all abnormal returns and measures how events like lawsuits or buyouts affect stock prices.

title: Absolute Advantage
definition: An entity establishes an absolute advantage over its competitors when it can produce more with the same inputs they're using.

title: Absolute Return
definition: Absolute return measures the gain or loss anassetachieves over a specific period, focusing solely on its performance without reference to abenchmark. Unlike relative return, which compares results to other assets or indices, absolute return evaluates the asset's own value change. This approach is widely used in hedge funds and other non-traditional strategies that aim to generate positive returns regardless of overall market conditions.

title: Absorption Costing
definition: Absorption costing is a managerial accounting method for capturing all the costs related to manufacturing a product.

title: Absorption Rate
definition: The absorption rate measures how quickly homes are sold in amarketduring a specific period. It's calculated by dividing the number of homes sold by the total number of available homes.This equation can also be reversed to identify the time it would take for the supply to be sold.

title: Accelerated Depreciation
definition: Accelerated depreciation is any method ofdepreciationused for accounting or income tax purposes that allows greater depreciation expenses in the early years of the life of an asset.

title: Acceleration Clause
definition: An acceleration clause is a loan contract provision allowing lenders to demand fullrepaymentif borrowers fail to meet specific terms, such as missed payments or default. It protects lenders by reducing risks while motivating borrowers to stay compliant.

title: Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)
definition: The acceptable quality level or limit (AQL) is a crucial measure in quality control. It applies to products and is defined in ISO 2859-1 as the maximum number of defective items that can be considered acceptable during random sampling inspections of a production batch.ISO 2859-1 defines AQL as the "worst tolerable" quality level, helping manufacturers and buyers agree on defect limits. AQL is usually shown as a percentage or ratio of defects to the total number produced.

title: Accepting Risk
definition: Accepting risk, or risk acceptance, occurs when a business or individual acknowledges that the potential loss from a risk is not great enough to warrant spending money to avoid it. Also known as "risk retention," it is an aspect ofrisk managementcommonly found in the business or investment fields.

title: Accidental Death and Dismemberment Insurance (AD&D)
definition: Accidental death and dismemberment (AD&D) insurance is insurance—usually added as a rider to a health orlife insurance policy—that covers the unintentional death or dismemberment of the insured due to an unforeseen accident. Dismemberment includes the loss, or the loss of use, of body parts or functions (such as limbs, speech, eyesight, and hearing).

title: Accidental Death Benefits
definition: An accidentaldeath benefitis a payment due to thebeneficiaryof an accidental death insurance policy. It enhances financial protection by providing an additional payout to beneficiaries if the insured dies from an accident. Offered as a rider to standard insurance policies, it covers incidents like car crashes or slips. This plan is crucial for workers in high-risk environments or those with significant commuting needs.

title: Account Balance
definition: An account balance is the amount of money in a financial repository, such as a savings or checking account, at a specific time. It factors in all debits and credits. Account balances are also shown on billing statements for credit cards, utilities, and loans.

title: Account in Trust
definition: The term account in trust refers to a financial account opened by an individual and managed by a designatedtrusteefor the benefit of a third party per agreed-upon terms. The trustee who manages the funds and assets in the account generally acts as afiduciary, which means they have a legal responsibility to manage the account and assets in the best interests of thebeneficiary.

title: Account Number
definition: A bank account number is a unique set of digits used to identify and access a particular bank account.

title: Account Statement
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Accountability
definition: Accountability is the acknowledgment by a company, an individual, or another entity that they're responsible for their actions.

title: Accountant
definition: An accountant maintains and analyzes financial records for companies or individuals. They keep track of income, expenditures, and tax liabilities, and their range of tasks includes auditing, project cost analysis, and tax planning.

title: Accountant Responsibility
definition: Accountant responsibility is the ethical responsibility anaccountanthas to those who rely on their work. According to theAmerican Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA), accountants have a duty to serve the public interest and uphold the public trust in the profession. An accountant has a responsibility to his clients, his company's managers, investors, and creditors, as well as to outside regulatory bodies. Accountants are responsible for the validity of thefinancial statementsthey work on, and they must perform their duties following all applicable principles, standards, and laws.

title: Accounting
definition: Accounting is the process of tracking the income and expenses of a business or other organization.

title: Accounting Conservatism
definition: Accounting conservatism is a principle that mandates companies report financial information with a great level of care and factor in the worst-case scenarios of their financial futures.

title: Accounting Cycle
definition: The accounting cycle systematically tracks and records financial transactions from occurrence to inclusion in financial statements and closing of books. This eight-step process ensures accuracy, leveraging automation to minimize manual errors.

title: Accounting Equation
definition: The accounting equation is a core concept of modern accounting that states that a company's assets are the sum of its liabilities and its shareholder equity.

title: Accounting Information System (AIS)
definition: An accounting information system (AIS) involves the collection, storage, and processing of financial and accounting data used by internal users to report information toinvestors,creditors, and tax authorities. It is generally a computer-based method for tracking accounting activity in conjunction with information technology resources.

title: Accounting Method
definition: An accounting method details how companies report their revenues and expenses, with a focus on accrual versus cash accounting. Accrual accounting records transactions when they occur, while cash accounting handles them upon receiving or spending cash. Understanding these approaches is essential due to IRS requirements and their impact on financial statements.

title: Accounting Policies
definition: Accounting policies are the specific procedures implemented by a company's management team that are used to prepare itsfinancial statements. These include any accounting methods,measurement systems, and procedures for presenting disclosures. Accounting policies differ fromaccounting principlesin that the principles are the accounting rules, and the policies are a company's way of adhering to those rules.

title: Accounting Principles
definition: Accounting principles, such as GAAP or IFRS, are standards set by regulatory bodies that public companies must adhere to for financial reporting disclosures.

title: Accounting Profit
definition: Accounting profit is a company's totalearnings, calculated according togenerally accepted accounting principles(GAAP). It includes theexplicit costsof doing business, such as operating expenses, depreciation, interest,and taxes.

title: Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)
definition: The accounting rate of return is a capital budgeting metric to calculate an investment's profitability. Businesses use ARR to compare multiple projects to determine each endeavor's expected rate of return or to help decide on an investment or anacquisition.

title: Accounting Ratio
definition: An accounting ratio is an analysis tool used to evaluate a company's financial health by measuring the relationship between two figures from its financial statements.

title: Accounting Standard
definition: An accounting standard outlines an integrated framework that guides how financial transactions are recorded and reported.

title: Accounting Theory
definition: Accounting theory is a set of assumptions, frameworks, and methodologies used in the study and application of financial reporting principles. The study of accounting theory involves a review of both the historical foundations ofaccounting practices, as well as the way in which accounting practices are changed and added to the regulatory framework that governsfinancial statementsand financial reporting.

title: Accounts Payable (AP)
definition: Accounts payable is a company’s obligation to pay for goods and services received on credit, typically within 30 to 90 days.

title: Accounts Payable Turnover Ratio
definition: The accounts payable turnover ratio is a short-term liquidity measure used to quantify the rate at which a company pays off its suppliers. It shows how many times a company pays off its accounts payable during a particular period.

title: Accounts Receivable (AR)
definition: Accounts receivable (AR) is an accounting term for money owed to a business for goods or services that it has delivered but not been paid for yet. Accounts receivable is listed on the company'sbalance sheetas acurrent asset.

title: Accounts Receivable Aging
definition: Accounts receivable aging is a periodic report that categorizes a company'saccounts receivableaccording to the length of time an invoice has been outstanding. It is used as a gauge to determine the financial health and reliability of a company's customers.

title: Accounts Receivable Financing
definition: Accounts receivable (AR) financing is a type of financing arrangement in which a company receives financing capital related to a portion of its accounts receivable. Accounts receivable financing agreements can be structured in multiple ways usually with the basis as either an asset sale or a loan.

title: Accredited Asset Management Specialist (AAMS)
definition: The Accredited Asset Management Specialist (AAMS) designation, offered by the College for Financial Planning, recognizes professionals who complete a self-study program, pass an exam, and adhere to a code of ethics.

title: Accredited In Business Valuation (ABV)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Accredited Investor
definition: An accredited investor is an individual or entity permitted by financial authorities, especially theU.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), to engage in trading of unregistered securities. These investors, who include high-net-worth individuals, banks, insurance companies, brokers, and trusts, meet specific financial criteria established under SEC'sRegulation D. Typically, they demonstrate financial sophistication through their income, net worth, asset size, or professional experience, thereby not requiring the regulatory protections designed for less experienced investors. Understanding the role and criteria for accredited investors can aid in navigating high-risk and high-reward investment opportunities.

title: Accretion
definition: Accretion refers to the process by which a company's overall value increases as a result of strategic growth initiatives.

title: Accretion of Discount
definition: Accretion of discount refers to the gradual increase in the value of a financial instrument that was purchased below its face value, as it approaches its maturity date. When investors buy bonds or securities at a discount, these investments increase in value over time until they reach their face (or par) value at maturity. This process is known as the accretion of discount.

title: Accretive
definition: In both finance and in general usage, the term "accretive" is the adjective form of the word "accretion", which refers to gradual or incremental growth. For example, an acquisition deal may be deemed accretive if that deal contributes to an increase in the acquiring company'searnings per share.

title: Accrual Accounting
definition: Accrual accounting is a financial accounting method where a company records revenue and expenses before money is received or spent.

title: Accruals
definition: Accruals are an revenues earned or expenses incurred in which the cash has not yet exchanged hands; per accounting standards, a company is still required to record both types of expenses under accrual accounting.

title: Accrue: Definition, How It Works, and 2 Main Types of Accruals
definition: To accrue means to accumulate interest, income, or expenses over time. The term "accrue" is often related to accrual accounting, which has become the standard accounting practice for most companies.

title: Accrued Expense
definition: Accrued expenses, also known as accrued liabilities, are those expenses that a company recognizes on its books when they occur but before they have actually been paid. They are recorded in the accounting period in which they are incurred. Accrual accounting requires more journal entries than simple cash basis accounting but provides a more comprehensive and accurate financial picture. Accrued expenses are an essential part of accrual accounting, andalign withgenerally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) standards.

title: Accrued Income
definition: Accrued income is the money a company has earned in the ordinary course of businessbut has yet to be received, and for which the invoice is yet to be billed to the customer.

title: Accrued Interest
definition: Interest is considered accrued when it's generated but payment hasn’t yet been made or received.

title: Accrued Liability
definition: Accrued liabilities areexpensesa company has incurred but not yet paid by the end of an accounting period, reflecting the accrual accounting requirements to record costs when incurred rather than when cash is paid.

title: Accrued Revenue
definition: Accrued revenue is revenue that has been earned by providing a good or service, but for which no cash has been received. Accrued revenues are recorded as receivables on thebalance sheetto reflect the amount of money that customers owe the business for the goods or services they purchased.

title: Accumulated Depreciation
definition: Accumulated depreciation is a method of accounting for the annual reduction of an asset's value up to a single point in its usable life. This type of depreciation can be calculated using one of six methods: the straight line, declining balance, double-declining balance, sum-of-the-years' digits, units of production, and half-year recognition.

title: Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income
definition: Accumulated other comprehensive income (OCI) includes allunrealized gainsandlossesreported in the equity section of thebalance sheetthat are netted belowretained earnings.

title: Accumulation Phase
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Accumulation/Distribution Indicator (A/D)
definition: The accumulation/distribution indicator (A/D) is a cumulative indicator that usesvolumeand price to assess whether a stock is being accumulated or distributed. The A/D measure seeks to identifydivergencesbetween the stock price and the volume flow. This provides insight into how strong a trend is. If the price is rising but the indicator is falling, then it suggests that buying or accumulation volume may not be enough to support the price rise and a price decline could be forthcoming.

title: Acid-Test Ratio
definition: The acid-test ratio indicates whether a company can pay its short-term obligations.

title: Acquisition
definition: An acquisition is when one company takes hold of another company by buying all or a majority of its shares, allowing it to direct the company's operations and strategy.

title: Acquisition Accounting
definition: Acquisition accounting is a set of formal guidelines describing howassets,liabilities,non-controlling interest(NCI) andgoodwillof a purchased company must be reported by the buyer on itsconsolidated statementof financial position.

title: Acquisition Cost
definition: An acquisition cost, also referred to as the cost of acquisition, is the total cost that a company recognizes on its books for property or equipment after adjusting for discounts, incentives,closing costsand other necessary expenditures, but beforesales taxes. An acquisition cost may also entail the amount needed to take over another firm or purchase an existing business unit from another company. Additionally, an acquisition cost can describe the costs incurred by a business in relation to the efforts involved in acquiring a new customer.

title: Acquisition Premium
definition: In mergers and acquisitions, an acquisition premium is the extra cost above a company's fair market value that a buyer agrees to pay to take control of the business.

title: Active Management
definition: The term active management means that an investor, a professional money manager, or a team of professionals is tracking the performance of an investment portfolio and making buy, hold, and sell decisions about the assets in it. The goal of any investment manager is to outperform a designated benchmark while simultaneously accomplishing one or more additional goals such as managing risk, limiting tax consequences, or adhering to environmental, social, and governance (ESG) standards for investing. Active managers may differ from other is how they accomplish some of these goals.

title: Activist Investor
definition: An activist investor is a shareholder in apublicly-held companywith a significant minority stake who tries to convince or force the company to make changes. The activist investor's goals might involve influencing management decisions on various topics, replacingdirectors, altering operations to improve company value, or forcing a sale of the company. Or, they may focus on maximizing shareholder value or on the company's social responsibilities.

title: Activity-Based Budgeting (ABB)
definition: Activity-based budgeting (ABB) builds abudgetfrom the ground up by identifying the activities that drive costs and funding only the resources those activities require. By analyzing cost drivers and targeting efficiencies, ABB helps eliminate waste and improve profitability.

title: Activity-Based Costing (ABC)
definition: Activity-based costing (ABC) assignsoverheadand indirect costs to products and services through activities, rather than traditional volume-based metrics. This method offers more accurate cost insights, helping businesses to refine pricing strategies and identify major cost drivers. ABC is especially beneficial in manufacturing for accurately tracing costs and understanding true product costs.

title: Activity-Based Management (ABM)
definition: Activity-based management (ABM) is a system that evaluatesthe profitability of businessactivities to strengthen what works and fix or eliminate what doesn't. By analyzing costs tied to employees, equipment, facilities,overhead, and distribution, ABM helps organizations pinpoint inefficiencies and improve performance.

title: Activity Cost Driver
definition: An activity cost driver is a measurable event or factor that leads to a change in the cost of a business activity.

title: Activity Ratios
definition: Several activity ratios are used to measure a company's success in turning its assets into revenue, such as its efficiency in converting inventory into sales.

title: Actual Deferral & Actual Contribution Percentage Test (ADP/ACP)
definition: The Actual Deferral Percentage (ADP) and Actual Contribution Percentage (ACP) tests are two tests that companies must conduct to ensure that their 401(k) plans don't unfairly benefit highly paid employees at the expense of others.

title: Actuarial Gain Or Loss
definition: Actuarial gain or loss refers to an increase or a decrease in the projections used to value a corporation’s defined benefit pension plan obligations. The actuarial assumptions of a pension plan are directly affected by the discount rate used to calculate the present value of benefit payments and the expected rate of return on plan assets. TheFinancial Accounting Standards Board(FASB) SFAS No. 158 requires the funding status of pension funds to be reported on the plan sponsor’s balance sheet. This means there are periodic updates to the pension obligations, the fund performance and the financial health of the plan.﻿﻿﻿ Depending on plan participation rates, market performance and other factors, the pension plan may experience an actuarial gain or loss in their projected benefit obligation.

title: Actuarial Life Table
definition: An actuarial life table is a tool that measures life expectancy and the risk of death at different ages. Insurance companies rely on these tables to set premiums and design policies by estimating how long people are likely to live. An actuarial life table typically includes key details, such as age-specific death rates, survival probabilities, and life expectancy projections. Life tables help insurers, researchers, and policymakers make informed decisions based on reliable population trends.

title: Actuarial Science
definition: Actuarial science uses mathematical and statistical techniques to evaluate financial risks, particularly in insurance and finance. By applying probability and statistics, it analyzes uncertain future events, allowing companies to make informed decisions and prepare for challenges ahead. This field has evolved from using deterministic models to incorporating high-speed computer simulations, broadening its application in assessing mortality, liabilities, and financial decisions.

title: Ad Valorem Tax
definition: An ad valorem tax is calculated based on the value of an item of property such as real estate or personal property. The Latin phrasead valoremmeans "according to value." Ad valorem taxes are based on the assessed value of the item being taxed.

title: Add-On Interest
definition: If you're looking for a loan, finding the most affordable option is important. While interest rate is a big factor, the way the interest is calculated can be just as important. Add-on interest loans tend to favor the lender, resulting in more money paid, even if the loan is paid off early.

title: Additional Child Tax Credit
definition: The Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC) is the refundable portion of the Child Tax Credit, but only if you claim it.

title: Additional Paid-In Capital
definition: Additional paid-in capital (APIC) is the difference between the issue price of the shares and their par value.

title: Addition Rule for Probabilities
definition: The addition rule for probabilities describes two formulas. One defines the probability of either of two mutually exclusive events happening, and the other addresses the probability of either of two non-mutually exclusive events happening.

title: Adhesion Contract
definition: Adhesion contracts, also known asboilerplatecontracts, require acceptance of non-negotiableterms and conditionsfor products or services. These contracts are common in standard transactions involving insurance, mortgages, and car purchases. Courts evaluate adhesion contracts for fairness using the doctrines of reasonable expectations and unconscionability.

title: Adjudication
definition: An adjudication is a legal ruling orjudgmentbut the term can also refer to the process of settling a legal case or claim through the court or justice system, such as a decree in the bankruptcy process between the defendant and thecreditors. The ruling is usually final.

title: Adjustable Life Insurance
definition: Adjustable life insurance is a permanent policy that lets you change your premiums, death benefit, and cash value as your needs shift. Its flexibility can be useful during life changes, offering more adaptability than whole life while still providing lasting coverage like universal life. The cash value also functions as a savings component that can grow and sometimes help pay future premiums.

title: Adjustable-Rate Mortgage (ARM)
definition: An adjustable-rate mortgage (ARM) is a home loan tied to a specific benchmark. It has a variable interest rate.

title: Adjusted Closing Price
definition: The adjusted closing price is a stock'sclosing pricethat's updated to reflect the stock's value after accounting for any corporate actions, such as stock splits and dividends. It's a more accurate reflection of a stock's value. It is often used when examininghistorical returnsto do a detailed analysis of past performance, and to compare the profitability of different assets more effectively. Adjusted closing prices can be useful for long-term investors who wish to determine appropriate asset allocations for their portfolios.

title: Adjusted EBITDA
definition: Adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes,depreciation, andamortization) is a measure computed for a company that takes its earnings and adds back interest expenses, taxes, and depreciation charges, plus other adjustments to the metric.

title: Adjusted Funds From Operations (AFFO)
definition: In the world of real estate investment trusts (REITs), Adjusted Funds From Operations (AFFO) stands out as a key metric. It builds on Funds From Operations (FFO) by accounting for capital expenditures and maintenance costs, offering a clearer picture of a REIT's cash flow and its ability to pay dividends.

title: Adjusted Gross Income (AGI)
definition: Adjusted gross income (AGI) is the number that the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) uses to determine your income taxes owed for the year. It represents your total taxable income after specific adjustments, influencing not only how much tax you owe but also your eligibility for certain tax deductions and credits. Understanding your AGI can help maximize your returns and ensure compliance with tax laws.

title: Adjusted Present Value (APV)
definition: Adjusted present value separates a firm's value into two components: its value if financed entirely by equity, and the present value of tax shields and other financing effects.

title: Adjusting Journal Entry
definition: An adjusting journal entry is an entry in a company’sgeneral ledgerthat occurs at the end of anaccounting periodto record any unrecognized income or expenses for the period. When a transaction is started in one accounting period and ended in a later period, an adjusting journal entry is required to properly account for the transaction.

title: Administrative Expenses
definition: Administrative expenses are expenses incurred by an organization that are not directly tied to a specific core function such as manufacturing, production, or sales. Theseoverhead expensesare related to the organization as a whole rather than to individual departments or business units.

title: Administrative Services Only (ASO)
definition: Administrative services only (ASO) refers to a contractual arrangement where an employer self-funds an employee benefit plan but outsources critical administrative functions to an external service provider.

title: Advance/Decline Line (A/D)
definition: The advance/decline line (or A/D line) is atechnical indicatorthat plots the difference between the number of advancing and declining stocks on a daily basis. The indicator is cumulative, with a positive number being added to the prior number, or if the number is negative it is subtracted from the prior number.

title: Advance Payment
definition: Advance payments involve funds paid before receiving goods or services, often as a protective measure for sellers against nonpayment. They are recorded as assets on the balance sheet and expensed once delivery is completed. Examples include prepaid phone services and rent payments.

title: Advanced Internal Rating-Based (AIRB)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Adverse Possession
definition: The term "adverse possession" refers to a legal principle that grantstitleto someone who resides on or is in possession of another person's land. The property's title is granted to the possessor as long as certain conditions are met including whether they infringe on the rights of the actual owner and whether they are in continuous possession of theproperty. Adverse possession is sometimes called squatter’s rights, although squatter’s rights are a colloquial reference to the idea rather than a recorded law.

title: Adverse Selection
definition: Adverse selection occurs when one party in a transaction has more information than the other, leading to market inefficiencies. Typically seen in insurance, the concept describes how high-risk individuals often obtain more coverage due to their insiders' knowledge, leaving companies to manage financial risks. This article unpacks the dynamics of adverse selection, its impact on markets, especially in insurance and secondhand car sales, and how businesses can mitigate its effects through solutions like increased transparency and regulatory measures.

title: Advertising Budget
definition: An advertising budget is an estimate of a company's promotional expenditures over a certain time period. More importantly, it is the money a company is willing to set aside to accomplish its marketing objectives.

title: Affiliate
definition: An affiliate can be a company in which another company owns a minority stake or a business connected through a formal relationship without full ownership. Affiliates have an important role in corporate structures, helping companies expand their reach. They are also common in the retail sector, especially in marketing and e-commerce partnerships. Affiliates operate from international markets to online platforms, providing connections and shared resources. Unlike subsidiaries, which are majority-owned and controlled by a parent company, affiliates maintain independence while benefiting from the association.

title: Affiliate Marketing
definition: Affiliatemarketingis an advertising model where a company compensates third-party publishers to generate traffic or leads to the company’s products and services. The third-party publishers are affiliates, and the commission fee incentivizes them to find ways to promote the company.

title: Affiliated Companies
definition: An affiliated company is when one company is a minorityshareholderof another. Typically, the parent company owns less than 50% of the affiliate and keeps its operations separate. There are many ways companies can become affiliated, such as buyouts, takeovers, and spin-offs. Companies will become affiliated under certain circumstances, like entering new markets or maintaining brand identities. Different from affiliates, a subsidiary is when the parent company holds a majority ownership of over 50%.

title: Affirmative Action
definition: Affirmative action refers to a policy aimed at increasing workplace and educational opportunities for people who are underrepresented in various areas of our society. It focuses ondemographicswith historically low representation in leadership and professional roles. It is often considered a means of countering discrimination against particular groups.

title: Affordable Care Act
definition: The Affordable Care Act (ACA) is the comprehensive healthcare reform that was signed into law by President Barack Obama in March 2010. It was formally known as the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act and commonly referred to as Obamacare. The law includes a list of healthcare policies intended to expand access tohealth insuranceto millions of uninsured Americans.

title: After-Tax Real Rate of Return
definition: The after-tax real rate of return is the amount of profit or loss that an investment yields after subtracting the impact of taxes and inflation.

title: After-Hours Trading
definition: The term “after-hours trading” refers to trading activity that starts at 4 p.m. U.S. Eastern time (ET) after the major U.S. stock exchanges close. The after-hours trading session can run as late as 8 p.m., thoughvolumetypically thins out much earlier in the session. After-hours trading is conducted throughelectronic communication networks (ECNs).

title: After-Tax Contribution
definition: An after-tax contribution is money paid into a retirement or investment account after income taxes on those earnings have already been deducted. When opening atax-advantagedretirement account, an individual may choose to defer the income taxes owed until after retiring, if it is a traditional retirement account, or pay the income taxes in the year in which the payment is made, if it is a Roth retirement account.

title: After-Tax Income
definition: After-tax income is the net income after deducting all federal, state, and withholding taxes. After-tax income—also called income after taxes and thenet of tax amount—represents the amount of disposable income that a consumer or firm has available to spend.

title: Agency Bond
definition: An agency bond is a debt security issued by a government-sponsored enterprise or by a federal government department other than the U.S. Treasury, including Freddie Mac and Fannie Mae. Some agency bonds are not fully guaranteed like U.S. Treasury and municipal bonds. An agency bond is also known as agency debt.

title: Agency by Necessity
definition: Agency by necessity is a type of legal relationship in which one party can make essential decisions for another party until legally recognized agents like someone with power of attorney or guardianship are put in place. The courts recognize agency by necessity during an emergency or urgent situation under which the beneficiary is unable to provide explicit authorization. Under such circumstances, those granted agency must act for the sole benefit of the beneficiary.

title: Agency Costs
definition: An agency cost refers to the financial loss incurred when an agent prioritizes personal interests over those of the principal, leading to misalignment in goals.

title: Agency Problem
definition: An agency problem is a conflict of interest inherent in any relationship where one party is expected to act in the best interest of another.

title: Agency Theory
definition: Agency theory attempts to explain and resolve disputes between principals and their agents so that those involved can achieve optimal results. Principals rely on agents to execute financial transactions for them. Agents must do so without regard for theirself-interest. The principal-agent problem arises from different priorities and interests between agents and principals. It's important to understand that conflicts of interest can occur. This can cause problems for the relationship. Examples of common principal-agent relationships includeshareholdersand management, clients and financial planners, clients and financial advisors, and lessees and lessors.

title: Agent
definition: "Agent" is a legal term for someone who has been given authority to act on someone else's behalf.

title: Aggregate Demand
definition: Aggregate demand is the total dollar value of spending on finished goods and services within an economy during a set time period.

title: Aggregate Stop-Loss Insurance
definition: Aggregate stop-loss insurance is a policy designed to limit claim coverage (losses) to a specific amount. This coverage ensures that a catastrophic claim (specific stop-loss) or numerous claims (aggregate stop-loss) do not drain the financial reserves of a self-funded plan. Aggregate stop-loss protects the employer against claims that are higher than expected. If total claims exceed theaggregate limit, the stop-loss insurer covers the claims orreimbursesthe employer.

title: Aggregate Supply
definition: Aggregate supply is the relationship between an economy's average price per good and the quantity of goods of average value supplied.

title: Aggregation
definition: Aggregation is the process of combining multiple pieces of information or positions, specifically in financial contexts. It's important in both futures markets and financial planning because it simplifies complex data for better understanding and decision-making. Aggregation benefits both financial advisors and their clients through streamlined data handling and improved financial insights.

title: Aggressive Investment Strategy
definition: An aggressive investment strategy is a high-riskportfolio managementapproach that seeks to maximize returns by prioritizingcapital appreciationover income or principal safety. Such strategies typically allocate heavily to stocks with little or no exposure to bonds or cash.

title: Agribusiness
definition: Agribusiness includes all economic sectors involved in farming and related commerce, from production to distribution. This sector is essential for economies, particularly those with significant exportable agricultural products. Agribusiness faces unique challenges due to market dynamics and climate change, requiring adaptation and innovation for sustainability and growth.

title: Air Waybill (AWB)
definition: An air waybill (AWB) is a document used in international air shipping that serves as a legal, non-negotiable contract between the shipper and the airline. It contains the shipper’s and consignee’s details, flight route, cargo description, and charges. The AWB only outlines the terms of transport and also allows for tracking and managing shipments throughout their journey. Since 2019, most carriers have adopted the electronic version, known as the e-AWB, making air cargo processing faster, more accurate, and environmentally friendly.

title: Aktiengesellschaft (AG)
definition: AG, short for Aktiengesellschaft, is the German term for apublic limited companywhose shares are traded on public stock exchanges. Shareholders' liability islimitedto their investment, protecting their personal assets from company debts.

title: Alan Greenspan
definition: Alan Greenspan is an American economist who was the chair of the Board of Governors of theFederal Reserve(Fed), the United States' central bank, from 1987 until 2006. In that role, he also served as the chair of theFederal Open Market Committee(FOMC), which is the Fed’s principal monetary policymaking committee that makes decisions on interest rates and managing the U.S. money supply.

title: Aleatory Contract
definition: An aleatory contract is an agreement that is tied to the occurrence of a specific, unpredictable event.

title: Algorithmic Trading
definition: Algorithmic trading has transformed financial markets by using advanced algorithms for executing trades. This method streamlines trading processes by automating decisions based on price, volume, and timing strategies, reducing the need for manual intervention. It evolves traditional trading by improving speed and efficiency but also carries its unique challenges and risks.

title: All Risks
definition: "All risks" refers to a comprehensive type of insurance coverage in the property-casualty market that covers any peril not specifically excluded in the policy. Unlike named perils insurance, which only covers explicitly listed events, all risk insurance assumes coverage for all perils unless they are explicitly omitted. Common exclusions include earthquakes, war, and wear and tear. Policyholders can opt for additional coverage on excluded events by paying for a rider or floater.

title: Allocated Loss Adjustment Expenses (ALAE)
definition: Allocated loss adjustment expenses (ALAE) are costs attributed to the processing of a specific insurance claim. ALAE is part of an insurer’s expense reserves and plays a central role in financial planning. It is one of the biggest costs insurers reserve money for, alongsidecontingent commissions.

title: Allocational Efficiency
definition: Allocational efficiency describes a scenario where resources are allocated in a way that aligns with consumer preferences and optimizes economic welfare.

title: Share Allotment Explained: Key Insights and IPO Strategies
definition: An allotment is the process of distributing or assigning shares, typically in the context of an Initial Public Offering (IPO), where shares are allocated to underwriting firms and investors. Several types of allotment arise when new shares are issued and allocated to either new or existing shareholders. Companies allot shares and other resources when demand is much stronger than the available supply.

title: Allowance for Bad Debt
definition: An allowance for bad debt is a valuation account used to estimate the amount of a firm's receivables that may ultimately be uncollectible. It is also known as anallowance for doubtful accounts. When a borrower defaults on a loan, the allowance forbad debtaccount and the loan receivable balance are both reduced for thebook valueof the loan.

title: Allowance for Credit Losses
definition: Allowance for credit losses is an accounting tool used by companies to predict and account for future loan defaults or uncollectible debts.

title: Allowance for Doubtful Accounts
definition: The allowance for doubtful accounts represents management's estimate of how much of accounts receivable will likely go uncollected.

title: Alpha
definition: Alpha (α) is an investing term that measures an investment strategy's ability to outperform the market, often called its "edge." It represents theexcessorabnormal returnof an investment compared to a benchmark index, adjusted for risk.

title: Alphabet Stock
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Altcoin
definition: Altcoins represent all cryptocurrencies and tokens that are not Bitcoin, with some interpretations excluding Ethereum from this category. They're crafted by developers with unique visions, often using distinct consensus mechanisms. These digital currencies attempt to build on the limitations of Bitcoin or craft new functionalities. While Bitcoin remains the most popular cryptocurrency, altcoins like Ethereum (ETH) and XRP have positioned themselves as pivotal players with distinct roles. Learn what defines altcoins and explore their various types, benefits, and considerations for the future

title: Alternative Depreciation System (ADS)
definition: An alternative depreciation system (ADS) is a taxation method required by the IRS for calculating depreciation onbusiness assetsover a longer recovery period. This system allows businesses to align depreciation with actual income streams, potentially offering a more economically accurate reflection than the General Depreciation System (GDS). Ultimately, the choice of ADS impacts asset management and financial planning.

title: Alternative Investment
definition: Alternative investments comprise financial assets besides stocks, bonds, mutual funds, and exchange-traded funds.

title: Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT)
definition: The alternative minimum tax (AMT) adds back some claimed tax deductions and adjustments to a taxpayer's income to ensure they're taxed on whichever obligation is greater.

title: Alternative Trading System (ATS)
definition: Alternative trading systems (ATSs) are platforms that match large buy and sell orders. They are often used by institutional investors to trade efficiently outside traditional exchanges. Although they are less regulated than national exchanges, they are an important part of market liquidity and price discovery. These systems operate under SEC regulations that set transparency and operational standards. Types of ATSs include dark pools, which allow large trades to occur privately without immediately affecting market prices.

title: Altman Z-Score
definition: The Altman Z-score is a financial metric used to evaluate the likelihood that a publicly traded company may face bankruptcy, serving as an indicator of its overall credit health.

title: Always Be Closing (ABC)
definition: Always Be Closing (ABC) is a motivational phrase used to describe a sales person's strategy for success. It implies that a salesperson should continuously be looking for newprospects, pitching products, or completing transactions.

title: Amalgamation
definition: An amalgamation is the combination of two or more companies into an entirely new entity. Amalgamations are distinct from acquisitions in that none of the companies involved in the combination survive as legal entities.

title: Amended Return
definition: An amended return is how a taxpayer corrects errors on a previous tax return.

title: American Depositary Receipt (ADR)
definition: American Depositary Receipts (ADRs) offer U.S. investors an efficient way to invest in foreign companies, simplifying the process of trading these stocks on American exchanges. They connect global markets by allowing foreign firms to reach American capital while helping U.S. investors buy shares without hassle. However, investing in ADRs includes considerations of pricing, fees, and potential tax implications.

title: American Depositary Share (ADS)
definition: An American depositary share is an equity stake in a non-U.S. company held by a U.S. bank that can be bought by U.S. investors.

title: American Dream
definition: The American Dream is the belief that anyone can achieve success through hard work in a society where upward mobility is possible for everyone.

title: American Express Card
definition: An American Express card, commonly known as an "Amex" card, offers various types of electronic payment cards, including credit, charge, and prepaid options. As a leader in financial services, American Express (AXP) provides these cards to individuals, small businesses, and corporations globally. Along with unique advantages such as rewards points, cash back, and travel perks, Amex cards are notable for being issued and processed by their own network—a feature that sets them apart from competitors like Visa and Mastercard.

title: American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC)
definition: The American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC) is a partially refundable credit that offsets certain qualified post-secondary education expenses, up to $2,500.

title: American Option
definition: An American option, aka an American-style option, is a version of an options contract that allows holders to exercise the option rights at any time before and including the day of expiration. It contrasts with another type of option, called theEuropean option, that only allows execution on the day of expiration.

title: American Stock Exchange History: From AMEX to NYSE American
definition: The American Stock Exchange (AMEX) was once the third-largeststock exchangein the United States, as measured by trading volume. The exchange, at its height, handled about 10% of all securities traded in the U.S.

title: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Amortizable Bond Premium
definition: The amortizable bond premium is a tax term that refers to the excess price paid for a bond over and above its face value. An amortizable bond premium is the extra cost paid for a bond beyond its face value. Depending on the type of bond, the premium can be tax-deductible and amortized over the life of the bond on a pro-rata basis. The constant yield method is the IRS-required calculation method for amortizing bond premiums. Understanding this concept can offer tax advantages by reducing taxable income.

title: Amortization of Intangibles
definition: Amortization of intangibles, also simply known as amortization, is the process of expensing the cost of anintangible assetover the projected life of the asset for tax or accounting purposes. Intangible assets, such as patents and trademarks, are amortized into an expense account called amortization.

title: Amortization Schedule
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Amortization Schedule: Definition, Formula, and Calculation
definition: An amortization schedule is a chart that tracks the falling book value of a loan or an intangible asset over time. For loans, it details each payment’s breakdown between principal and interest. For intangible assets, it outlines the systematic allocation of the asset’s cost over its useful life.

title: What Are Amortized Bonds? Definition, Mechanics, and Examples Explained
definition: An amortized bond is one in which the principal (or face value) of the debt is paid down regularly, along with its interest expense, over the life of the bond. These bonds use an amortization schedule, which slowly transitions from paying more interest in the first half of the bond term to more principal in its second half. They reduce credit and interest rate risks.

title: Amortized Loan
definition: An amortized loan involves regularly scheduled payments, with each payment covering both interest and principal. Initially, payments are interest-heavy, but over time, they gradually favor the principal. Common examples include mortgages and personal loans. Understanding amortized loans can empower your financial decisions by revealing how payments reduce debt over time.

title: Amsterdam Stock Exchange (AEX)
definition: The Amsterdam Stock Exchange (AEX) is the world's oldest stock exchange, founded in 1602 alongside the Dutch East India Company (VOC). Part of Euronext Amsterdam since 2000, the AEX remains a leading player in global finance, offering a platform fortradingshares of top Dutch companies. Learn how the AEX evolved from its 17th-century beginnings into a vital component of Europe's largest cash equities market.

title: Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)
definition: Analysis of variance (ANOVA) is a statistical method for determining whether differences in group means are statistically significant or likely due to random variation.

title: Android Operating System
definition: Android is a software platform developed by Google, designed to provide a customizable and versatile framework for powering a wide array of devices beyond just smartphones and tablets.

title: Anchoring
definition: "Anchoring" is abehavioral financeterm that describes an irrational bias towards an arbitrary benchmark figure or reference point. The benchmark, or anchor, can skew investor decision-making, such as when to sell the investment. Anchoring is also used in sales and wage negotiations. It's been shown that the anchor set at the start of negotiations influences the agreed amount more than the actual negotiations. Setting an anchor that is deliberately too high can affect the range of all subsequent counteroffers.

title: Anchoring and Adjustment
definition: Anchoring is a common cognitive bias in finance, where people rely too heavily on an initial number and make adjustments that often stay too close to that starting point. This can distort valuations or negotiations when the anchor is inaccurate, especially in investing or pricing decisions. Because anchoring is hard to avoid, reviewing varied data points can help reduce influence.

title: Angel Investor: Definition and How It Works
definition: An angel investor is an early-stage investor in astartup. These investors provide seed money, usually in exchange for ownership equity in the company.

title: Animal Spirits
definition: "Animal Spirits" refers to the psychological and emotional factors influencing financial decisions during economic uncertainty. Coined by economistJohn Maynard Keynes, it highlights how emotions likeconsumer confidenceand fear drive market behaviors and investor actions, affecting economic growth and market stability. This article explores the origins, significance, and examples of Animal Spirits in financial contexts.

title: Annual Equivalent Rate (AER)
definition: The Annual Equivalent Rate (AER) provides the actual interest yield of an investment or savings account by considering multiple compounding periods within a year. It gives investors a truer measure of returns, surpassing the nominal interest rate, by calculating compounded interest. This article will explain the formula for AER and illustrate its application with examples, helping you make informed decisions on investment products.

title: Annual General Meeting (AGM)
definition: An annual general meeting of investors is a yearly gathering of a company's interested shareholders, usually hosted by executives and board members.

title: Annual Percentage Rate (APR)
definition: Annual percentage rate (APR) is a critical concept for anyone dealing withloansorinvestments, as it calculates the actual yearly cost of borrowing or earnings from an investment, presented as a percentage. Unlike other interest metrics, APR encompasses not only the nominal interest rate but also includes fees and additional costs.

title: Annual Percentage Yield (APY)
definition: The annual percentage yield (APY) is the interest rate earned on an investment in one year, including compounding interest.

title: Annual Report
definition: An annual report is a document required for public companies that discloses their operating and fiscal activities for the previous year.

title: Annual Return
definition: An annual return measures the growth of an investment, on average, each year during a specific period. It is a commonly used method for comparing the performance of liquid investments.

title: Annual Turnover
definition: Annual turnover relates to the frequency with which business or investment assets are replaced. The annual turnover rate can help investors and businesses understand investment fund activity and business efficiency. Annual turnover plays a significant role in fundamental decision-making, particularly in evaluating investment strategies and business operations. A high turnover rate by itself is not a reliable indicator of investment fund quality or performance.

title: Annualize
definition: Annualization is the act of converting a short-term rate into one for a full year.

title: Annualized Income
definition: Annualized income is an estimate of how much money an individual, a business, or an investment generates over a year's time. It is calculated based on less than one year's worth of data, so it is only an approximation of total income for the year.

title: Annualized Income Installment Method
definition: The annualized income installment method calculates estimated tax payments and helps decrease underpayment penalties due to fluctuating income.

title: Annualized Rate of Return
definition: An annual return measures the growth of an investment, on average, each year during a specific period. It is a commonly used method for comparing the performance of liquid investments.

title: Annualized Total Return
definition: Annualized total return is the average return an investment has generated annually over a period of several years.

title: Annuitant
definition: An annuitant is the person whose life determines the payments in an annuity contract. Annuities can be immediate, providing payments right away, or deferred, starting at a future date. The size of payments depends on factors like the annuitant’slife expectancyand the amount invested. Taxes on annuity payments vary based on the contract, with some income being tax-deferred until withdrawn, making it important to understand the specific terms before investing.

title: Annuitization
definition: Annuitization is the process of converting an annuity investment into regular income payments, playing a central role in financial planning by providing a steady cash flow. Annuities can be annuitized for a set period or theannuitant'slifetime.

title: Annuity
definition: An annuity is a contract that's issued and distributed by an insurance company, meant to provide a guaranteed income. The insurance company pays a fixed or variable amount to the purchaser.

title: Annuity Due
definition: Payment is due or made at the beginning of a payment interval with an annuity due and includes obligations like rent.

title: Annuity Table
definition: An annuity table is crucial for calculating the present value of structured payment series, typically used by financial professionals like accountants and actuaries. This article offers a detailed guide on leveraging annuity tables, complete with practical examples and formulas to enhance your understanding of present value calculations.

title: Anti-Dilution Provision
definition: Anti-dilution provisions serve as a buffer against equity position dilution, safeguarding investors when companies issue new shares at lower prices. Built into convertible preferred stocks and options, these clauses adjust conversion prices to preserve ownership percentages. Learn the mechanisms behind these provisions and how they act in favor of investors in various stock scenarios.

title: Anti-Dumping Duty
definition: An anti-dumping duty is a protectionist measure a government uses to safeguard its economy from foreign imports priced lower than their fair market value. This tariff aims to protect local businesses by preventing foreign companies from flooding the market with cheaply priced goods. However, while designed to protect domestic industries, these duties can also lead to higher prices for local consumers and might impact international competition negatively. Here's a detailed look at how anti-dumping duties work, including real-world examples.

title: Anti Money Laundering (AML)
definition: Anti-money laundering (AML) efforts, encompassing global laws, regulations, and procedures, are essential for detecting and preventing the transformation of illicit funds into legitimate income. By utilizing AML initiatives, authorities across the globe tackle financial crimes like tax evasion andpublic corruptionModern AML practices, including Know Your Customer (KYC) and Customer Due Diligence (CDD), empower institutions to monitor and report suspicious activities.

title: Anticipatory Breach
definition: An anticipatory breach of contract is an action that shows one party's intention to fail to fulfill its contractual obligations to another party. An anticipatory breach can end thecounterparty'sresponsibility to perform its duties.

title: Antitrust
definition: Antitrust is the regulation to prevent the concentration of economic power in regards to monopolies and other anticompetitive practices.

title: Appellate Courts
definition: Appellate courts, also known as courts of appeals, are judicial bodies that review the decisions of lower courts to ensure the correct application of the law. These courts do not conduct new trials but instead evaluate if legal errors that could affect the case outcome have been made in trial-level courts. Appellate courts exist at both state and federal levels and are presided over by a panel of judges, not a jury.

title: Applicable Federal Rate (AFR)
definition: According to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), private loans must charge a minimum interest rate known as the applicable federal rate (AFR), published monthly by the IRS. Since privateloanscan vary by term length, the IRA publishes several AFRs by loan type.

title: Application Programming Interface (API)
definition: An application programming interface (API) is a set of programming codes that queries data and sends instructions between one software platform and another. APIs are used extensively in providing data services across a range of fields and contexts. They facilitate the exchange of data.

title: Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC)
definition: An application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) miner is specialized hardware for efficientlyminingcryptocurrencies. ASIC miners work by solving cryptographic puzzles in a Proof of Work blockchain like Bitcoin. First introduced in 2012, ASICs have surpassed traditional CPU and GPU methods in speed and energy efficiency. Before investing in ASIC miners users should consider certain essentials, such as power consumption and location needs.

title: Applied Economics
definition: The aim of applied economics is to inform economic decisions and predict possible outcomes. Applied economics relates the conclusions drawn from economic theories and empirical studies to real-world situations.

title: Appraisal
definition: An appraisal is a professionalvaluationof property, such as real estate, a business, or collectibles, performed by a credentialed appraiser. It is commonly required for mortgages,taxfilings, insurance claims, and estate settlements, helping establish fair market value and resolve disputes. Appraisals vary by property type and purpose, setting the stage for more detailed methods and standards that follow.

title: Appraisal Costs
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Appraisal Management Company (AMC)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Appreciation
definition: Appreciation is the rise in an asset's value over time, whiledepreciationis the decline in value as the asset ages or loses usefulness. Both concepts are essential in financial planning and investing because they help determine whether an asset is likely to generate profit or incur loss. Factors like demand, market conditions, and asset quality drive appreciation, while wear and tear or obsolescence often lead to depreciation.

title: Appropriation
definition: Appropriation is when money is set aside for a specific purpose. A company or a government appropriates funds in order to delegate cash for the necessities of its operations. Appropriations for the U.S. federal government are decided by Congress through various committees. A company might appropriate money for short-term or long-term needs that include employee salaries, research and development, and dividends.

title: Appropriation Account
definition: Appropriation is the act of setting aside money for a specific purpose. In accounting, it refers to a breakdown of how a firm’s profits are divided up, or for the government, an account that shows the funds a government department has been credited with. A company or a government appropriates funds in order to allocate cash for the necessities of its business operations.

title: Aptitude Test
definition: An aptitude test is a type of assessment designed to evaluate a person's natural ability to thrive in a specific task or area of interest.

title: Arab League
definition: The Arab Leagueis a union of Arabic-speaking African and Asian countries. It was formed in Cairo in 1945 to promote the independence, sovereignty, affairs, and interests of its member countries and observers. The organization began with seven founding members and is now comprised of 22 nations and four observer states. The League is bound by a charter and has a council in place to ensure that its goals are met.

title: Arbitrage
definition: Arbitrage helps ensure the prices of financial securities do not deviate substantially from their fair market value for long periods.

title: Arbitrageur
definition: An arbitrageur is an investor who tries to profit from price differences in the market.

title: Arbitration
definition: Arbitration is a mechanism for resolving disputes between investors and brokers, or between brokers. It is overseen by the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA), and the decisions are final and binding. Arbitration is distinct from mediation, in which parties negotiate to reach a voluntary settlement, and decisions are not binding unless all parties agree to them. Arbitration is often chosen for its efficiency, lower costs, and binding decisions, making it a preferred choice over lawsuits.

title: Arc Elasticity
definition: Arc elasticity is the elasticity of a variable between two points. It's used in economics to measure changes in demand in relation to changes in price. For instance, a product is considered elastic if the demand also changes significantly when its price changes. Elasticity is calculated by a formula.

title: Arithmetic Mean
definition: The arithmetic mean is the simple average, or the sum of a series of numbers divided by the number of numbers summed.

title: Arm's Length Transaction
definition: In an arm's length transaction, unrelated and unaffiliated buyers and sellers conduct business, independently and without affecting each other's decisions.

title: Arms Index (TRIN)
definition: The Arms index, also called the short-term trading index (TRIN) is a technical analysis indicator that compares the number of advancing and declining stocks (AD Ratio) to advancing and declining volume (AD volume).

title: Aroon Indicator
definition: The Aroon indicator identifies trend changes in the price of an asset, as well as that trend's strength.

title: Aroon Oscillator
definition: The Aroon Oscillator is a trend-following indicator that uses aspects of theAroon Indicator(Aroon Up and Aroon Down) to gauge the strength of a current trend and the likelihood that it will continue.

title: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem
definition: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem reveals the inherent challenges in ranked voting systems, demonstrating why a fair preference order is unattainable without breaking key voting principles. Named aftereconomistKenneth J. Arrow, this foundational theory highlights the paradoxes in achieving a perfect voting system.

title: Article 50
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Articles of Association
definition: Articles of Association are internal documents and rules that specify the regulations for a company's operations and define the company's purpose. These documents outline the rules of corporate governance, including the process of appointing directors, handling financial records, and issuing shares.

title: Articles of Incorporation
definition: Articles of incorporation are a set of formal documents filed with a government body to legally document the creation of a corporation. Articles of incorporation generally contain pertinent information such as the firm’s name, street address, agent for service of process, and the amount and type ofstock to be issued. The articles of incorporation are used to legally form the corporation.

title: Artificial Intelligence
definition: Artificial intelligence (AI) enables machines to analyze information, learn from it, and act intelligently to achieve specific goals.

title: Ascending Channel
definition: An ascending channel is theprice actioncontained between upward sloping parallel lines. It is characterized by higher highs and higher lows, indicating that the price is trending upwards. Technical analysts construct an ascending channel by drawing a lowertrend linethat connects the swing lows, and an upper channel line that joins the swing highs.

title: Ascending Triangle
definition: An ascending triangle is a chart pattern used intechnical analysis. It is created by price moves that allow for a horizontal line to be drawn along theswing highsand a rising trendline to be drawn along the swing lows. The two lines form a triangle. Traders often watch forbreakoutsfrom triangle patterns. The breakout can occur to the upside or downside.

title: Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC)
definition: Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC) is a 21-member economic forum that promotes free trade and sustainable development in Pacific Rim economies.

title: Asian Development Bank
definition: The Asian Development Bank (ADB) is an international financial institution that promotes economic growth and cooperation in the Asia-Pacific. Its core mission is to support the economic development of its member countries in the region. Founded in 1966 in Manila, the ADB offers loans, grants, and technical support to enhance social and economic development.

title: Asian Financial Crisis
definition: The Asian financial crisis was spurred by the collapse of the currency exchange and affected Asian economies from 1997 to 1998.

title: Ask
definition: The ask is the price a seller is willing to accept for a security, which is often referred to as the offer price. Along with the price, the ask quote might also stipulate the amount of the security available to be sold at the stated price. The bid is the price a buyer is willing to pay for a security, and the ask will always be higher than the bid.

title: Assemble to Order (ATO)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Assessed Value
definition: Assessed value is the dollar amount assigned to a home or other piece of real estate for property tax purposes. It takes into account the value of comparable properties in the area, among other factors. In many cases, the assessed value is calculated as a percentage of thefair market valueof the property.

title: Asset
definition: An asset can be anything that has a monetary value that produces income for its owner or that could produce income if used or sold.

title: Asset Allocation
definition: The term “asset allocation” refers to the process of dividing a financial or investment portfolio between different asset classes.

title: Asset-Backed Commercial Paper (ABCP)
definition: An asset-backed commercial paper (ABCP) is a short-term investment vehicle with amaturity datethat is typically between 90 and 270 days. These debts are backed by the issuing company's physical assets, such as trade receivables.

title: Asset-Backed Security (ABS)
definition: Asset-backed securities (ABS) is a type of financial investment that is collateralized by an underlying pool ofassets—usually ones that generate a cash flow from debt, such as loans, leases, credit card balances, or receivables. It takes the form of a bond or note, paying income at a fixed rate for a set amount of time, until maturity. For income-orientedinvestors, asset-backed securities can be an alternative to other debt instruments, like corporate bonds or bond funds.

title: Asset-Based Approach
definition: An asset-based approach focuses on valuing a business by calculating its net asset value, derived from subtracting liabilities from assets. This method provides flexibility in valuation by allowing considerations of market values and intangibles, crucial for stakeholders interested in comparisons, sales, or liquidations.

title: Asset-Based Lending
definition: Asset-based lending involves securing loans with collateral, such as inventory or equipment, to meet business cash flow demands. This form of lending provides short-term liquidity by using assets as a line of credit. Companies that cannot demonstrate sufficient cash flow may leverage their existing assets to gain financial flexibility.

title: Asset Class
definition: An asset class is a category of financial instruments with common characteristics and bound by the same regulations.

title: Asset Coverage Ratio
definition: The asset coverage ratio measures how well a company can repay its debts by selling or liquidating its assets. It is a key financial metric that helps lenders, investors, and analysts measure a company's financialsolvencyand compare it to other companies in the same industry.

title: Asset Financing
definition: Asset financing is the practice of using a company’s balance sheet assets, such as short-term investments, inventory, or accounts receivable, to borrow money or get a loan. Because it focuses on available assets rather than credit history or detailed business plans, it can provide faster access to funds for short-term needs like working capital.

title: Asset-Liability Committee (ALCO)
definition: An asset-liability committee oversees management of a company’s assets and liabilities to manage risk, ensure liquidity, and earn adequate returns, serving as a directorial group.

title: Asset/Liability Management
definition: Asset/liability management is the practice of assessing and reducing a company’s risk of not paying a liability on time.

title: Asset Management Company (AMC)
definition: Asset management companies (AMCs) are firms that channel pooled client funds into diverse investments like stocks, bonds, and real estate. They manage portfolios forhigh-net-worth individuals, hedge funds, and pension plans, and create mutual or index funds for smaller investors, centralizing management to maximize diversification and returns.

title: Asset Protection
definition: Asset protection is the adoption of strategies to guard one's wealth. Asset protection is a component of financial planning intended to protect one's assets fromcreditorclaims. Individuals and business entities use asset protection techniques to limit creditors' access to certain valuable assets while operating within the bounds of debtor-creditor law.

title: Asset Retirement Obligation
definition: In accounting, an asset retirement obligation (ARO) describes a legal obligation associated with the retirement of atangible,long-lived asset, where a company will be responsible for removing equipment or cleaning up hazardous materials at some future date. AROs should be included in a company's financial statement to present a more accurate and holistic snapshot of the enterprise's overall value.

title: Asset Swap
definition: An asset swap is a derivative contract where two parties exchange fixed and floating assets.

title: Asset Swapped Convertible Option Transaction (ASCOT)
definition: An asset swapped convertible option transaction (ASCOT) splits up the fixed-income and equity portions of a convertible bond.

title: Asset Turnover Ratio
definition: The asset turnover ratio measures how efficiently a company is using its assets to generate revenue.

title: Asset Valuation
definition: Asset valuation is the process of determining the fair market orpresent valueof assets such as stocks, bonds, and tangible/intangible assets. It is an important part of finance and is used in mergers, loan applications, and asset trading.

title: Assets Under Management (AUM)
definition: Assets under management (AUM) is the current value of all the investments entrusted to a mutual fund, an exchange-traded fund (ETF), or an investment company.

title: Assignment
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Assortment Strategies
definition: An assortment strategy in retailing involves the number and type of products that stores display for purchase by consumers. Also called a "product assortment strategy," it is a strategic tool that retailers use to manage and increase sales. The strategy is made up of two major components:

title: Assumable Mortgage
definition: With an assumable mortgage, a homeowner agrees to transfer the outstanding mortgage on their house to a buyer. A buyer can acquire a property without having to get a new mortgage for the full price (and potentially secure a lower interest rate).

title: Assurance
definition: Assurance is financial coverage for an event that is certain to happen. It is both a product and a service (such as auditing and validation provided by professionals like accountants). Whole life insurance is assurance. It provides ongoing coverage and guarantees a payout upon death. Assurance can be important for risk management and in ensuring financial reporting accuracy. It is different from insurance, which provides coverage for potential events over a specified period.

title: Assurance Services
definition: Assurance services are a type of independent professional service usually provided by certified or chartered accountants such ascertified public accountants(CPAs). Assurance services can include a review of any financial document or transaction, such as a loan, contract, or financial website. This review certifies the correctness and validity of the item being reviewed by the CPA.

title: Asymmetric Information
definition: Asymmetric information exists in the majority of transactions and typically benefits sellers of products and services more than buyers.

title: At Par
definition: The term "at par" means atface value. A bond, preferred stock, or other debt instrument may trade at par,below par, orabove par.

title: Atomic Swaps
definition: Atomic swaps are a way to exchange cryptocurrencies directly across different blockchains. The primary goal is to remove centralized intermediaries like exchanges, which potentially simplifies trading. Atomic swaps reduce the number of trades needed by consolidating transactions into one.

title: At The Money
definition: At the money (ATM) occurs when an option'sstrike priceis the same as theunderlying security'smarket price, impacting options trading with detailed strategic insights. ATM options, central to decisions involving market movements, possess a delta of ±0.50 and are used by traders to construct varied strategies.
