title: Attorney-in-Fact
definition: An attorney-in-fact, also known as an agent, is empowered through a legal document called a power of attorney to act on behalf of a principal in business, financial, and personal matters. Whether managing finances or executing legal agreements, these individuals serve a critical role. Unlike attorneys-at-law, attorneys-in-fact do not require legal qualifications, but they carry significant responsibility and must prioritize the principal's interests.

title: Attribution Analysis
definition: Attribution analysis evaluates a portfolio's performance, focusing on a manager's investment choices, style, and market timing. Known as return or performance attribution, it identifies the sources of excess returns compared to benchmarks. This sophisticated method is crucial for both portfolio managers and investors in understanding the effectiveness of their strategies.

title: Attrition
definition: Attrition in a business context describes the process whereby a company reduces its workforce by not filling vacant positions when employees leave.

title: Auction Market
definition: In an auction market, buyers entercompetitive bidsand sellers submit competitive offers at the same time. The price at which astock tradesrepresents the highest price that a buyer is willing to pay and the lowest price that a seller is willing to accept. Matching bids and offers are then paired together, and the orders are executed. TheNew York Stock Exchange (NYSE)is an example of an auction market.

title: Audit
definition: An audit is an official assessment of a person or company's financial accounts by professional accountants.

title: Audit Committee
definition: An audit committee is one of the major operating committees of a company'sboard of directorsthat is in charge of overseeing financial reporting and disclosure.

title: Audit Risk
definition: Audit risk is the risk that financial statements are materially incorrect, even though the audit opinion states that the financial reports are free of any material misstatements.

title: Auditor
definition: An auditor is a professional authorized to examine and confirm the accuracy of financial records to ensure that companies comply with laws and regulations.

title: Auditor's Opinion
definition: An auditor's opinion is a certification that accompaniesfinancial statements. It is based on anauditof the procedures and records used to produce the statements and delivers an opinion as to whether material misstatements exist in the financial statements. An auditor's opinion may also be called anaccountant's opinion.

title: Auditor's Report
definition: The auditor's report is a document containing the auditor's opinion on whether a company's financial statements comply with GAAP and are free from material misstatement.

title: Augmented Product
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Austerity
definition: Austerity involves economic policies that governments implement to control public sector debt. When debt threatens a government's financialstability, austerity measures can help restore confidence and balance by managing expenditures and revenues effectively.

title: Australian Securities Exchange (ASX)
definition: The Australian Securities Exchange is headquartered in Sydney, Australia. The Exchange in its current form was created through the merger of the Australian Stock Exchange and Sydney Futures Exchange in 2006.The ASX acts as a market operator,clearing house, and payments facilitator. It also provides educational materials toretail investors.

title: Autarky
definition: Autarky refers to a nation that operates in a state of self-reliance. Nations that follow a policy of autarky are characterized by self-sufficiency and limited trade with global partners. The word "autarky" originates from Greek: "autos" meaning "self" and "arkein" meaning "to ward off" or "suffice."

title: Authorized Stock
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Autocorrelation
definition: Autocorrelation is the degree of similarity of a variable between two successive time intervals.

title: Automated Clearing House (ACH)
definition: The Automated Clearing House (ACH) is an electronic funds transfer system that processes millions of transactions daily. It facilitates the movement of money between financial institutions. Managed by Nacha, ACH supports a wide range of payments, including payroll deposits, tax refunds, bill payments, and debit transfers. Established in the mid-1970s, the network handles billions oftransactionsannually across domestic and international markets. ACH connects banks and businesses and ensures smooth financial operations, providing a fast, secure, and reliable way to transfer money electronically.

title: Automated Customer Account Transfer Service (ACATS)
definition: The Automated Customer Account Transfer Service (ACATS) facilitates the transfer ofsecuritiesfrom one trading account to another at a different brokerage firm or bank.

title: Automated Teller Machine (ATM)
definition: An automated teller machine (ATM) is an electronic banking outlet that facilitates the completion of basic transactions for bank customers without interaction with a teller or a branch representative. In fact, anyone with a credit card or debit card can withdraw money at most ATMs, either in the U.S. or other countries. Introduced in the 1960s, ATMs have evolved over time and now offer a range of benefits, including convenience due to their many locations, security, and availability, speedy transactions, access to a variety of transactions (such as deposits, withdrawals,bill payments, transfer of money between accounts), and access to cash 24 hours a day.

title: Automatic Bill Payment
definition: An automatic bill payment (auto pay) is a money transfer scheduled on a predetermined date to pay arecurring bill. Auto pay is usually set up with the company receiving the payment, though it’s also possible to schedule automatic payments through a checking account’s online bill pay service.

title: Automatic Premium Loan
definition: An automatic premium loan (APL) is an insurance policy provision that allows the insurer to deduct the amount of an outstandingpremiumfrom the value of the policy when the premium is due.

title: Automatic Stabilizer
definition: Automatic stabilizers are built-in fiscal tools designed to mitigate economic fluctuations by adjusting certain government revenue and expenditure levels in response to changes in the economy.

title: Autonomous Consumption
definition: Autonomous consumption is defined as theexpendituresthat consumers must make even when they have nodisposable income. Certain goods need to be purchased, regardless of how much income or money a consumer has in their possession at any given time. When a consumer is low on resources, paying for these necessities can force them to borrow or access money that they had previously been saving.

title: Autonomous Expenditure
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Autoregressive
definition: Autoregressive models are statistical models that use past data to predict future values, such as by forecasting stock prices based on historical prices.

title: Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA)
definition: The Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) is a crucial tool in statistical analysis, primarily utilized for forecasting and understandingtime series data. By examining the differences between past and future values, ARIMA assists in predicting trends in financial markets, such as stock prices or company earnings, based on historical performances. This model leverages autoregressive properties, making it effective for short-term forecasting where past data significantly influences future outcomes.

title: Available Balance
definition: When you log into your online banking portal, you typically see two balances: available and current. The available balance is what you can immediately use, while the current balance includes pending transactions. Understanding these differences is crucial for managing your finances effectively and avoiding overdrafts.

title: Available-for-Sale Security
definition: An available-for-sale security (AFS) is a debt or equity security purchased with the intent of selling before it reaches maturity, or holding for a long period of time should it not have a maturity date.

title: Average Age Of Inventory
definition: The average age of inventory, also referred to as days' sales in inventory (DSI), is the typical number of days a company holds its inventory stock before it is sold.

title: Average Annual Growth Rate (AAGR)
definition: The average annual growth rate (AAGR) is the mean annualized rate of growth of a variable over a specified period.

title: Average Annual Return (AAR)
definition: Average annual return (AAR) is a key measure used to evaluate how a mutual fund has performed over time. It looks at factors like share price increases, capital gains, and dividends to show the fund’s historical performance over periods such as three, five, or 10 years. Investors use AAR to compare funds and make their investment decisions. There are limitations, though, as it doesn’t account for compounding or how much the returns may fluctuate.

title: Average Inventory
definition: Average inventory measures a company's typical stock level over a period, helping to evaluate inventory management efficiency.

title: Average Collection Period
definition: A business’s average collection period is the average amount of time it takes that business to collect payments owed to by its clients.

title: Average Cost Basis
definition: The average cost basis method is a system of calculating the value ofmutual fundpositions held in a taxable account to determine profit or loss for tax reporting.Cost basisrepresents the initial value of a security or mutual fund that an investor owns.

title: Average Cost Method
definition: Average cost method assigns a cost to inventory items based on the total cost of goods purchased or produced in a period divided by the total number of items purchased or produced. Average cost method is also known as weighted-average method.

title: Average Daily Balance Method
definition: The average daily balance method calculates the amount of interest to be charged to a borrower on an outstanding loan, taking into account the balance of the account on each day of the billing period.

title: Average Daily Rate (ADR)
definition: The average daily rate (ADR) reflects the financial efficiency of a hotel's pricing strategy by measuring how much income is generated per room usage over a specific period.

title: Average Daily Trading Volume (ADTV)
definition: Average daily tradingvolume(ADTV) indicates how actively a stock is traded, influencinginvestordecisions by assessing liquidity and market interest. High ADTV can simplify entering or exiting positions, while low ADTV may present challenges.

title: Average Directional Index (ADX)
definition: The average directional index (ADX) is atechnical analysis indicatorused by some traders to determine the strength of atrend.

title: Average Life
definition: Average life is the time period that adebt'sprincipal is expected to remain outstanding. It involves only principal payments rather than principal and interest payments. Average life calculations can help investors understand when to expect returns and to assess the risk ofamortizingfixed income securities. Prepayments and defaults can affect average life. Investors can use average life to compare and select from investment opportunities.

title: Average Outstanding Balance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Average Propensity to Consume
definition: Average propensity to consume (APC) measures the percentage of income that is spent rather than saved. This may be calculated by a single individual who wants to know where the money is going or by an economist who wants to track the spending and saving habits of an entire nation.

title: Average Return
definition: The average return is the simple mathematical average of a series ofreturnsgenerated over a specified period of time. It measures investment performance by helping to determine historical performance. The average return is calculated like a simple average for any group of numbers. Add the numbers to get a total, then divide by the quantity of numbers in the set.

title: Average Selling Price (ASP)
definition: Average selling price (ASP) is the typical price at which a product or service sells across differentchannelsor markets. Companies use ASP to benchmark performance, track pricing trends, and guide strategy, especially in industries like tech and retail, where prices change by product type andlife cycle.

title: Average True Range (ATR)
definition: The average true range (ATR) is a technical analysis indicator introduced by market technician J. Welles Wilder Jr. in his book "New Concepts in Technical Trading Systems." It measures market volatility by decomposing the entire range of an asset price for that period.

title: Axe
definition: An axe (or "axe to grind") is the interest that a trader shows in buying or selling a security that is typically already on the books. The term has evolved over the years from addressing only bonds but other securities as well.

title: Baby Bond
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Baby Boomer
definition: “Baby boomer” is a term used to describe those who were born from 1946 to 1964. They formed the largest generational group in U.S. history until the millennial generation slightly surpassed them in 2019. The baby boomer generation makes up a substantial portion of the world’s population, especially in developed nations.

title: Back-End Ratio
definition: The back-end ratio, also known as thedebt-to-income (DTI) ratio, measures how much of a borrower’s income goes toward their debts, including mortgages, car loans, and credit cards. Lenders use this ratio to assess how much financial strain borrowers can handle when approving a mortgage. To calculate it, you divide total monthly debt payments by monthly gross income and express it as a percentage. Most lenders prefer a back-end ratio below 36% to reduce the risk of default and ensure borrowers can manage their debt.

title: Back Office
definition: The back office refers to administrative and support staff who don’t interact with clients. Back-office roles involve settlements, record-keeping, regulatory compliance, accounting, and IT services.

title: Back Stop
definition: In a securitiesoffering, a back stop (or backstop) is a last-resort support or bid for theunsubscribedportion of shares.

title: Back-to-Back Letters of Credit
definition: Back-to-back letters of credit play a crucial role in international trade by using two separate letters of credit to facilitate transactions involving an intermediary. The first letter of credit acts as collateral for the second, providing financial assurance in situations where verifying creditworthiness may be challenging. This mechanism safeguards both parties by using bank credit, ensuring payment upon contract terms fulfillment, and simplifying complex international deals.

title: Backdoor Roth IRA
definition: A backdoor IRA is a legal strategy that allows high-income earners to bypass income limits on Roth IRA contributions.

title: Backflush Costing
definition: Backflush costing is a product costing system generally used in ajust-in-time(JIT) inventory system. In short, it is an accounting method that records the costs associated with producing a good or service only after they are produced, completed, or sold. Backflush costing is also commonly referred to as backflush accounting.

title: Backlog
definition: A backlog is a buildup of work that needs to be completed. It can have a number of uses in accounting and finance. It can refer to a company's sales orders waiting to be filled or a stack of financial paperwork, such as loan applications, that needs to be processed.

title: Backorder
definition: A backorder occurs when an item cannot be filled immediately due to insufficient supply, though it might still be in production. Backorders indicate demand surpasses supply. They reflect essential insights into inventory management, demonstrating how companies balance demand and supply for popular items such as next-gen gaming consoles or new tech devices.

title: Backtesting
definition: Backtesting is vital for traders and analysts as it assesses a trading strategy's potential by applying it to historical data. This process helps simulate trades, analyze risks, and evaluate profitability without risking real capital. Positive backtest results confirm a strategy's soundness, while negative outcomes offer a chance for reassessment before deploying real money.

title: Backup Withholding
definition: Backup withholding is money set aside for tax purposes for withdrawn investment income.

title: Understanding Backward Integration: Benefits and Challenges
definition: Backward integration is a strategic approach where a company seeks to enhance its supply chain by acquiring or merging with its previous suppliers.

title: Backwardation
definition: Backwardation occurs when the current price, or spot price, of a commodity is higher than the prices in thefutures market. This is usually the result of high demand or shortages that are expected to resolve in the long term.

title: Bad Credit
definition: Bad credit refers to an individual’s history of not paying bills on time and the likelihood that they will fail to make timely payments in the future. This is often reflected in a low credit score, typically under 580 on a scale of 300 to 850. Businesses can also have bad credit.

title: Bad Debt
definition: Bad debt is an amount of money that a creditor must write off if a borrower defaults on a loan. If a creditor has a bad debt on the books, it becomes uncollectible and is recorded as a charge-off.

title: Bad Debt Expense
definition: A bad debt expense is recognized when a receivable is no longer collectible because a customer is unable to fulfill their obligation to pay an outstanding debt due to bankruptcy or other financial problems. Companies that extend credit to their customers report bad debts as an allowance for doubtful accounts on thebalance sheet, which is also known as a provision for credit losses.

title: Bag Holder
definition: A bag holder is an informal term used to describe an investor who holds a position in asecuritythat decreases in value until it descends into worthlessness. In most cases, the bag holder stubbornly retains their holding for an extended period, during which time the value of the investment goes to zero.

title: Bail Bond
definition: A bail bond is an agreement by a criminal defendant to appear for trial or pay a sum of money set by the court. The bail bond is co-signed by a bail bond agent, who charges the defendant a fee in return for guaranteeing the payment.

title: Bail-In
definition: Bail-ins offer a critical resolution method for banks teetering on the edge of collapse. Unlike bailouts that use taxpayer funds, bail-ins restructure a bank's debts, putting the onus on creditors and depositors. This approach aims to stabilize failing banks while reducing taxpayer burden, a strategy that gained attention during the 2013 Cyprus crisis.

title: Bailout
definition: A bailout happens when a government or big organization gives money to a struggling company or industry to avoid failure. Bailouts can save jobs, support key industries, boost investor confidence, and stabilize the economy. They can take the form of loans, cash infusions, and/or stock purchases. Governments may also look at corporate restructuring and management changes, if necessary. Major financial institutions and U.S. automakers received bailouts through the Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) from the U.S. federal government during the 2008 financial crisis.

title: Balance of Payments (BOP)
definition: The balance of payments is a summary record of money that enters and exits a country during a defined period.

title: Balance of Trade (BOT)
definition: The balance of trade (BOT) represents the difference between a country's export and import values over a specific timeframe, forming the backbone of the broaderbalance of payments(BOP). Often depicted as the trade balance or net exports, BOT offers insight into a nation's economic interactions with the world.

title: Balance Sheet
definition: A balance sheet is a financial statement that provides a snapshot of a company's assets, liabilities, and shareholder equity at a specific point in time.

title: Balanced Budget
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Balanced Fund
definition: A balanced fund is amutual fundmade up of both stocks and bonds expressed as a fixed percentage. The investment objective is to achieve amixture of growth and income. For instance, balanced funds typically have a fixed asset allocation of 70% stocks and 30%bonds. Bonds are debt instruments that usually pay a stable, fixed rate of return.

title: Balanced Investment Strategy
definition: A balanced investment strategy combines asset classes in aportfolioin an attempt to balancerisk and return. Typically, balanced portfolios are divided between stocks and bonds, either equally or with a slight tilt, such as 60% in stocks and 40% in bonds. Balanced portfolios may also maintain a small cash or money market component for liquidity purposes.

title: Balanced Scorecard
definition: The Balanced Scorecard (BSC) grades a company's performance from the perspective of customer satisfaction, operational excellence, and business innovation, as well as financial success.

title: Balloon Loan
definition: Balloon loans are popular financial tools characterized by low monthly payments with a large final payment due at term's end. These loans attract short-term borrowers with their initial affordability but require careful planning for the large balloon payment. Common in real estate, they pose refinancing risks if interest rates rise unexpectedly.

title: Balloon Payment
definition: A balloon payment is a large, one-time sum due at the end of a loan term, following smaller periodic payments that typically cover primarily or exclusively interest costs. This structure, often found in balloon loans, allows borrowers to maintain lower monthly payments initially. Such loans gained popularity in the years leading up to the2007-2008 financial crisisas part of home mortgages, enabling homebuyers to afford a mortgage in the short term.

title: Ballpark Figure
definition: A ballpark figure is a rough numerical estimate or approximation of the value of something that is otherwise unknown. Ballpark figures are commonly used byaccountants, salespersons, and other professionals to estimate current or future results. A stockbroker could use a ballpark figure to estimate how much money a client might have at some point in the future, given a certain rate of growth. A salesperson could use a ballpark figure to estimate how long a product a customer was thinking about buying might be viable.

title: Baltic Dry Index
definition: The Baltic Dry Index (BDI), created by the London-basedBaltic Exchange, is a shipping and trade index that tracks the cost of transportingraw materialslike coal and steel. As it reflects supply and demand for shipping capacity, the BDI is often seen as a leading economic indicator, giving insight into global trade activity.

title: Bancassurance
definition: Bancassurance is an arrangement between a bank and an insurance company by which the bank makes the insurance company's products available to its own customer base. Bancassurance is beneficial for both parties because the bank earns revenue through the sale of insurance products and the insurance company broadens its sales potential without investing additional money in its salesforce. Customers can benefit as well, as they can choose to buy important and useful financial products conveniently at their bank. In the U.S., regulatory changes in 1999 removed barriers to this relationship, allowing banks to sell insurance products. Bancassurance is most prominent in Europe and the Asia-Pacific region.

title: Bandwagon Effect
definition: The bandwagon effect is a psychological phenomenon in which people do something primarily because other people are doing it, regardless of their own beliefs, which they may ignore or override. This tendency of people to align their beliefs and behaviors with those of a group is also called a herd mentality.

title: Bank
definition: Banks are financial institutions licensed to manage your deposits and extend loans, serving as both a secure place for your savings and a source for financing needs. They offer a variety of services, fromindividual retirement accounts (IRAs)and certificates of deposit (CDs) to currency exchange and safe deposit boxes. Consumers encounter different types of banks, like retail, commercial, and investment banks, each catering to specific financial needs.

title: Bank Bill Swap Rate (BBSW)
definition: The Bank Bill Swap Rate (BBSW), or Bank Bill Swap Reference Rate, is a short-term interest rate used as a benchmark for the pricing of Australian dollar derivatives and securities—most notably, floating rate bonds.

title: Bank Capital
definition: Bank capital serves as a financial buffer for banks, providing resilience against potential losses by representing the difference between total assets and liabilities.

title: Bank Confirmation Letter (BCL)
definition: A bank confirmation letter (BCL) is a formal statement issued by a bank confirming the availability of a loan orline of creditfor a borrower, which could be an individual, company, or organization. This letter serves as assurance to third parties, such as sellers or partners, that the borrower has access to a specified amount of financial resources to complete transactions or projects.

title: Bank Credit
definition: Bank credit represents the total funds available for borrowing from banks, encompassing loans and credit lines for individuals and businesses. This credit is pivotal for buying homes, cars, and for business operations, offering options like secured and unsecured loans based on creditworthiness.

title: Bank Deposits
definition: A bank deposit is money that’s placed in a bank account, such as a savings or checking account.

title: Bank Identification Number
definition: A bank identification number (BIN) is the initial set of four to six numbers on a credit card that identifies the lending institution. It helps merchants track transactions.

title: Bank Rating
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Bank Reserve
definition: Bank reserves, crucial to financial stability, are the minimum cash reserves institutions must maintain to meetcentral bankmandates. Held either in vaults or at the central bank, these reserves ensure banks can manage unexpected withdrawals and play a vital role in both economic stability and monetary policy.

title: Bank Statement
definition: A bank statement presents all account activity and balances to the account holder for review.

title: Bank Stress Test
definition: A bank stress test evaluates how a bank could handle extreme economic conditions, such as a severe recession or market crash. These tests became widely used after the 2008 financial crisis to help prevent another banking collapse and ensure the stability of the financial system. Regulators require large banks to undergo these tests regularly, which involve scenarios to see how their assets and capital would fare under stress. Stress tests help strengthen financial stability by identifying weaknesses, but some critics argue they may not always be rigorous enough or fully transparent.

title: Bank Draft
definition: Bank drafts offer a secure form of payment guaranteed by the issuing bank. Unlike personal checks, the full amount is drawn from the payer’s account and held in the bank’s reserve until the payee cashes the draft. This assurance makes bank drafts a preferred payment method for large transactions, such as buying property, where the seller demands certainty of funds.

title: Bank-Owned Life Insurance (BOLI)
definition: Bank-owned life insurance (BOLI) serves as a tax-efficient tool for banks, helping them fund employee benefits through tax-free provisions. Banks purchase these policies on high-earning executives and board members, acting as the beneficiary themselves to safeguard against potential financial losses from the death of key personnel.

title: Banker's Acceptance
definition: A banker’s acceptance serves a function similar to that of a check but the issuing bank accepts responsibility for payment.

title: Bank Guarantee
definition: A bank guarantee is a commitment from a financial institution to cover financial obligations if a party in a transaction defaults. This instrument is crucial in fostering trust and facilitating transactions such as acquiring goods, buying equipment, or engaging in international trade, especially outside the United States. Should the client fail to meet the contract's terms, the bank steps in to ensure financial coverage, protecting both parties involved.

title: Bank Reconciliation
definition: A bank reconciliation statement is a summary of recent banking activity that is used to reconcile personal or business records against the bank's record of transactions.

title: Bank Run
definition: A bank run refers to a big number of customers pulling their deposits due to concerns about the bank’s financial stability.

title: Bankruptcy
definition: Bankruptcy is a legal process for relieving debt that the borrower cannot repay. It’s a measure of last resort that typically requires liquidating assets or entering a repayment plan.

title: Banner Advertising
definition: Banner advertising refers to the use of a rectangular graphic display that stretches across the top, bottom, or sides of a website or online media property. The horizontal type of banner advertisement is called a leaderboard, while the vertical banners are called a skyscraper and are positioned on a web page's sidebars. Banner ads are image-based rather than text-based and are a popular form of online advertising.

title: Baptism by Fire
definition: "Baptism by fire" is a phrase commonly used to describe a person or employee who is learning something the hard way through a challenge or difficulty.

title: Barbell
definition: The barbell strategy is a technique where investments are divided between short-term and long-term bonds. This strategy is believed to balance interest rate risk and inflation risk with potential returns. The benefits of this strategy include diversification and flexibility in adjusting to market conditions.

title: Bar Chart
definition: Bar charts consist of multiple price bars, with each bar illustrating how the price of an asset or security moved over a specified time period. Each bar typically showsopening, high, low, and closing(OHLC) prices, although this may be adjusted to show only the high, low, andclose (HLC).

title: Bare Trust
definition: A bare trust is a trust structure in which thebeneficiaryhas full rights to the trust'sassetsand income, with the trustee holding legal title but no discretion over how or when funds are distributed. Common in Canada and the U.K., bare trusts are often used to transfer assets tax-efficiently, especially to lower-income beneficiaries who may pay less tax on investment returns.

title: Barrel Of Oil Equivalent (BOE)
definition: A Barrel of Oil Equivalent (BOE) quantifies energy using a standardized measure beyond just oil, assisting investors and analysts in comparing oil and natural gas reserves effectively. Oil, measured in barrels, andnatural gas, measured in cubic feet, can use BOE for like-for-like evaluations. This standard is essential for gauging company assets and investment potential.

title: Barrels Of Oil Equivalent Per Day (BOE/D)
definition: Barrels of oil equivalent per day (BOE/D) is a standardized energy measurement used in the oil and gas industry to account for the combined production of crude oil and natural gas. This measure allows companies and investors to compare production levels across different types of energy sources. A BOE/D quantifies natural gas production in terms of barrels of oil by considering energy equivalence. Typically, one barrel of oil is equivalent to 6,000 cubic feet of natural gas.

title: Barrier Option
definition: Barrier options arederivatives whose payoff relies on theunderlying assetreaching a set price point. There are two main types:knock-outoptions, which become worthless if limits are breached, andknock-inoptions, gaining value only once a specific price is achieved. These options provide unique benefits and strategic uses for traders.Barrier options are consideredexotic optionsbecause they are more complex than basicAmericanorEuropeanoptions. Barrier options are also considered a type of path-dependent option because their value fluctuates as the underlying value changes during the option's contract term. In other words, a barrier option's payoff is based on the underlying asset's price path. The option becomes worthless or may be activated upon the crossing of a price point barrier.

title: Barriers to Entry
definition: Barriers to entry are obstacles that make it difficult for new companies to enter a market. They are important because they protect existing competitors from new rivals. These barriers can be financial, such as highstartup costs, or they can be regulatory, like strict licensing requirements. They can also be operational, such as complex production processes. Industries like pharmaceuticals and electronics often have high entry barriers. Companies looking to enter these markets may overcome barriers through strategies like innovation, partnerships, niche targeting, or securing funding to cover initial costs.

title: Barter
definition: Barter is the exchange of one item or service for another of similar value without using cash or a cash equivalent for payment.

title: Base Effect
definition: The base effect is the effect that choosing a different reference point for a comparison between two data points can have on the result of the comparison. This often involves the use of some kind of ratio or index value between two points in a time-series data set, but can also apply to cross-sectional or other types of data.

title: Understanding Base Pay: Definition, Differences From Annual Pay
definition: Base pay is the initial salary paid to an employee, not including anybenefits, bonuses, or raises. It is the rate of compensation an employee receives in exchange for services. An employee's base pay can be expressed as an hourly rate or weekly, monthly, or annual salary.

title: What Is a Base Year? How It's Used in Analysis and Example
definition: A base year serves as the benchmark year for comparing changes in economic data, enabling analysts to evaluate trends over time.

title: Basel I
definition: Basel I is a set of international banking regulations established by theBasel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS). It prescribes minimum capital requirements for financial institutions, with the goal of minimizing credit risk.

title: Basel II Explained: Banking Regulations, Purpose, and Key Reforms
definition: Released in 2004, Basel II is a pivotalbanking regulationframework by theBasel Committee on Banking Supervision. It strengthens the principles of Basel I by setting comprehensive guidelines for minimum capital requirements and regulatory supervision, aiming to enhance transparency and risk management in the banking sector.

title: Understanding the Basel Accords: Regulations and Global Impact
definition: The Basel Accords established capital requirements and risk measurements for global banks.

title: Basel III
definition: Basel III is an international regulatory accord for reforms designed to mitigate risk within the international banking sector by requiring banks to have more capital on hand.

title: Baseline
definition: A baseline is a fixed point of reference that is used for comparison purposes. In business, the success of a project or product is often measured against a baseline number for costs, sales, or any number of other variables. A project may exceed a baseline number or fail to meet it.

title: Basic Earnings Per Share (EPS)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Basic Materials
definition: The basic materials sector encompasses companies involved in the exploration, extraction, and primary processing of raw materials such as metals, chemicals, and natural resources. This sector supplies foundational resources essential for nearly all industries, especially construction and manufacturing, and is particularly influenced by economic cycles. In this guide, you'll discover what constitutes the basic materials sector, its significance, and how it impacts investments. As a critical component of the global economy, understanding the basic materials sector can aid informed investment decisions and economic insights.

title: Basis
definition: In finance, "basis" primarily refers to the total cost associated with an investment, including the purchase price and related expenses. This measure is critical for calculating capital gains or losses for tax purposes. Basis also refers to the variation between the spot price of a deliverable commodity and the relative price of the futures contract. The term also be used in reference to securities transactions.

title: Basis Point (BPS)
definition: A basis point (BPS) is a way to show changes ininterest ratesor yields. One basis point equals one-hundredth of a percentage point, making it a precise way to discuss even very small rate movements. Basis points are typically expressed with the abbreviations "bp," "bps," or "bips." One basis point is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%. In decimal form, one basis point appears as 0.0001 (0.01/100).

title: Basis Risk
definition: Basis risk is when investments meant to offset each other do not move in opposite directions. This imperfect correlation can cause unexpected gains or losses, adding risk to the hedging strategy.

title: Basket of Goods
definition: A basket of goods is a collection of items that represent consumer spending patterns. It is made up of a fixed set of goods and services whose prices are used to measure inflation. These prices are monitored each month by federal governments so they can set and make changes to theirinflation targets. For example, theconsumer price index (CPI), a common measure of inflation, measures the price change over time for a basket of goods and services. The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) tracks this basket of goods in the United States.

title: BAT Stocks
definition: BAT is an acronym referring to three of the largest tech companies in China: Baidu Inc. (BIDU), Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. (BABA), and Tencent Holdings Ltd. (0700.Hong Kong, TCEHY). These stocks are frequently compared with theFAANGstocks in the United States: Meta, formerly Facebook (META), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Netflix Inc. (NFLX), and Alphabet (GOOG).

title: Batch Processing
definition: Batch processing is a method of handling multipletransactionsat once with minimal user interaction, making it ideal for end-of-cycle tasks likepayrollor bank reconciliations. Originating from early mainframe computing innovations, it remains cost-effective and efficient, though its success depends on accurate input data and careful system setup to avoid processing errors.

title: Baye's Theorem
definition: Bayes' Theorem is a formula used to calculate the probability of an outcome given a known previous outcome that occurred in similar circumstances.

title: BCG Growth-Share Matrix
definition: The Boston Consulting Group (BCG) growth share matrix is a planning tool that uses graphical representations of a company’s products and services to help the company decide what it should keep, invest more money in, or sell.

title: Beacon Score
definition: A Beacon score is one of many credit scoring models developed byFICO, the major company in that field. It is still in use today but is currently branded simply as a FICO score. In years past it was often referred to as an Equifax Beacon score, after the credit bureau Equifax. For example, a Beacon 5.0 score is now known as a FICO Score 5, according to FICO. That score remains in wide use in the mortgage industry.

title: Bear Call Spread
definition: A bear call spread, also known as a bear call credit spread, is used by traders expecting the underlying asset price to decrease.

title: Bear Hug
definition: A bear hug in business is an unsolicited acquisition strategy where a company publicly offers to buy another at a marked premium, persuading shareholders while challenging the target company's board. This tactic aims to benefit shareholders with a high offer and pressures the board to negotiate, risking management changes and drawing intense scrutiny on the company's performance and valuation.

title: Bear Market
definition: A bear market occurs when financial markets experience a decline of 20% or more. This is often marked by falling prices and growing investor pessimism. These downturns are usually triggered by factors that weaken investor confidence, such as economic slowdowns, rising inflation, or geopolitical tensions. This article explores the key phases of a bear market, examples from recent history, and effective investment strategies to help navigate and potentially take advantage of these challenging market conditions.

title: Bear Put Spread
definition: A bear put spread is an options strategy used by investors anticipating a price drop in an asset. It minimizes costs by buying and sellingputswith the sameexpirationdate but differingstrike prices. This approach caps the maximum profit at the difference between the strike prices minus the net cost, offering a safer alternative to short-selling.

title: Bear Spread
definition: A bear spread is a type of options trading strategy used by investors who anticipate a moderate decline in the price of an underlying asset. This strategy allows investors to limit potential losses while aiming for a profit if the price drops. It involves buying and selling options with the same expiration date but at different strike prices.

title: Bear Stearns
definition: Bear Stearns was once a leading Wall Street investment bank, but its heavy exposure to riskymortgage-backed securitiesled to a rapid collapse during the 2008financial crisis.

title: Bear Trap
definition: A bear trap in financial trading occurs when a security or index appears to be in decline. Traders move in, expecting a continuing decline, posting short sales to profit from the downturn. However, the security or index unexpectedly reverses direction, causing those who bet against it (the bears) to lose on their trades.

title: Bearer Bond
definition: Bearer bonds, distinguishable for their lack of registration, grant ownership to whoever holds them, similar to cash. These physical certificates are historically significant but have become rare due to vulnerabilities like theft and untraceability. While obsolete in the U.S. and many other nations due to regulation and security concerns, old bearer bonds may still hold value and can be claimed under specific conditions. Learn about their characteristics, history, and current status in the financial landscape.

title: Bearer Share
definition: A bearer share is equity security wholly owned by the person or entity that holds the physicalstock certificate, thus the name "bearer" share. The issuing firm neither registers the owner of the stock nor tracks transfers of ownership; the company disperses dividends to bearer shares when a physicalcouponis presented to the firm. Because the share is not registered to any authority, transferring the ownership of the stock involves only delivering the physical document.

title: Bearish Engulfing Pattern
definition: In technical analysis, the bearish engulfing pattern is a chart pattern that can signal a reversal in an upward price trend. Comprising two consecutive candles, the pattern features a smaller bullish candle followed by a larger bearish candle that engulfs the first. This formation is considered a strong indicator that the prior upward momentum is waning and a reversal is on the horizon.

title: Behavioral Economics
definition: Behavioral economics is the study of psychology as it relates to the economic decision-making processes of individuals and institutions. It draws on psychology and economics to explore why people sometimes make irrational decisions, and the cognitive biases that cause behavior to diverge from economic models.

title: Behavioral Finance
definition: Behavioral finance is an economic theory that ascribes the irrational behavior of individuals making financial choices to psychological factors or biases.

title: Bell Curve
definition: A bell curve is a graph that shows how values in a dataset are disbursed, with most falling near the average, and fewer appearing at the extremes. It is used to understand patterns, trends, and variation in data.

title: Below-the-Line Advertising
definition: Below-the-line advertising focuses on non-traditional marketing strategies that directly target specific audiences, often at a lower cost and with more personal interaction compared to conventional advertising methods.

title: Ben Bernanke
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Benchmark
definition: Benchmarks serve as a critical standard for measuring an asset's value change or other investment metrics over time. In the realm of investing, benchmarks offer essential reference points for evaluating the performance of securities, mutual funds, and other financial instruments, serving as a comparative tool for investors.

title: Beneficial Owner
definition: A beneficial owner is a person who enjoys the benefits of ownership of an asset, which is held on their behalf by a custodian or broker.

title: Beneficiary
definition: A beneficiary is an individual or organization that inherits assets from someone upon their passing.

title: Benefit-Cost Ratio
definition: The Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) is a crucial metric in cost-benefit analysis, providing insights into project viability by comparing expected benefits to total costs. A BCR over 1.0 suggests profitability, making it a key factor in investment decisions. This article explains how to calculate the BCR, its significance, and how it aids in making informed financial choices.

title: Benjamin Graham: The Father of Value Investing and His Legacy
definition: Benjamin Graham was a key investor in the early 20th century. His work in securities formed the basis for the detailed stock analysis methods used by investors today. His famous book, "The Intelligent Investor", has gained recognition as the foundational work in value investing.

title: Berhad (BHD)
definition: BHD, short for Berhad, is a suffix used in Malaysia to designate apublic limited company (PLC), signaling limited liability for shareholders, much like LLCs or LTDs elsewhere. It distinguishes publicly traded firms from Sendirian Berhad (SDN BHD) companies, which are private limited entities.

title: Berkshire Hathaway
definition: Berkshire Hathaway is a holding company for a multitude of businesses, including GEICO and Fruit of the Loom, that is led byWarren Buffett. What is now Berkshire Hathaway started as a textile company and evolved into one of the largest companies in the world by market cap. The company owns a variety of well-known businesses, such as GEICO and Fruit of the Loom, and has significant minority interests in public companies such as Apple. Its class A shares(BRK.A)are among the most expensive in the stock market and trade at over $700,000 dollars per share. Greg Abel is the heir apparent to Berkshire CEO Warren Buffett who, in his nineties, plans to step down as CEO in 2025.

title: Bermuda Option
definition: A Bermuda option is anexotic optioncontract that can only be exercised on a specific, predetermined date. It offers a middle ground between anAmerican optionand the single exercise date of a European option. These contracts involveunderlying financial instruments, such as stocks or bonds, and specify strike prices for potential exercises. The early exercise feature allows investors to take advantage of favorable market conditions on certain dates, which can influence planning and risk management. In this article, we show you the pros and cons along with real-world examples of Bermuda options to help you understand how they work.

title: Bernie Madoff
definition: Bernie Madoff was a U.S. financier who carried out the largest Ponzi scheme in history.

title: Bespoke CDO
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement (BATNA)
definition: A strong BATNA gives a negotiating party an attractive alternative.

title: Best Endeavors
definition: Best endeavors is a phrase commonly found in commercial contracts that places an obligation on the identified party to use all efforts necessary to fulfill the terms set out. A best endeavors policy places a party under a stricter obligation compared to a reasonable endeavors obligation. It is equivalent to best efforts, a term widely used insecuritiesmarkets and preferred in most commercial contracts signed in the United States.

title: Best Practices
definition: Best practices are a set of guidelines, ethics, or ideas that represent the most efficient or prudent course of action in a given business situation.

title: Beta
definition: Beta is a widely used indicator of a stock's price volatility or the level of risk relative to the broader market.

title: Better Business Bureau (BBB)
definition: The Better Business Bureau (BBB) is a private nonprofit organization that operates a business directory and rating system. It handles consumer complaints, evaluates charities, offers guidance to businesses and consumers, and alerts the public about deceitful and fraudulent advertising and selling practices. Its goal is to ensure companies behave responsibly and are trustworthy in their dealings with consumers.

title: Bicameral System
definition: A bicameral legislature consists of two separate chambers or houses that work together within a single legislative framework to provide diverse representation and improve the lawmaking process.

title: Bid
definition: The term "bid" refers to an offer made by an individual or entity to purchase an asset.

title: Bid and Ask
definition: Current stock prices are quoted with a bid and ask, indicating the highest price a buyer is willing to pay and the lowest price a seller is willing to accept.

title: Bid-Ask Spread
definition: A bid-ask spread is the range between the buying and selling prices offered for a security.

title: Bid Bond
definition: A bid bond is a financial pledge from a bidder to a project owner, confirming the bidder's commitment to a project and that the project owner will be compensated if the bidder fails to come through.

title: Bid Price
definition: A bid price is a price for which somebody is willing to buy something, whether it be a security, asset, commodity, service, or contract. It is colloquially known as a “bid” in many markets and jurisdictions.

title: Bid Size
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Big Data
definition: Big data is a method for processing vast amounts of diverse information to uncover trends and patterns that guide decision-making.

title: Bilateral Contract
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Bilateral Trade
definition: Bilateral trade enables countries to engage in direct economic exchanges, fostering closer economic ties and potential growth by mutually opening markets and enhancing trade flow.

title: Bill Auction: Definition, How It Works, and How to Participate
definition: Treasury bills are issued in electronic form through a bill auction bidding process, which is conducted every week.

title: Bills of Materials (BOM)
definition: A bill of materials (BOM) serves as a comprehensive blueprint for constructing, manufacturing, or repairing a product. It details the raw materials, components, and step-by-step instructions needed to ensure efficient production.

title: Billing Cycle Explained: Definition, How It Works, and Examples
definition: A billing cycle is the interval of time from the end of onebilling statementdate to the next for goods or services a company provides on a recurring basis. Billing cycles are essential for businesses to manage revenue collection and financial planning.

title: Bill of Exchange
definition: A bill of exchange is similar to an invoice presented when goods or services are exchanged.

title: Bill of Lading
definition: A bill of lading is a contract between a carrier and a shipper.

title: Binance Coin (BNB)
definition: Binance Coin (BNB) is a cornerstone cryptocurrency introduced by Binance, originally designed as a utility token for discounted trading fees. Its role has expanded into travel bookings, financial services, and digital payments, while quarterly coin-burning practices reduce supply and influence its value. BNB is one of the largest cryptocurrencies by market cap, reflecting its broad integration across the Binance ecosystem.

title: Binary Option: Definition, How It Trades, and Example
definition: Binary options are based on a yes or no answer, and the buyer receives a payout or nothing at expiration.

title: Binomial Distribution
definition: A binomial distribution is used to determine the probability of a pass or fail outcome in a survey or an experiment.

title: Binomial Option Pricing
definition: A binomial option pricing model is an options valuation method that uses an iterative procedure and allows for node specification in a set period.

title: Bioremediation
definition: Bioremediation is a branch ofbiotechnologythat employs the use of living organisms in the removal of contaminants, pollutants, and toxins from soil, water, and other environments. These organisms can include microbes and bacteria.

title: Bird In Hand
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Bitcoin
definition: Bitcoin is a decentralized digital currency that uses blockchain technology to enable peer-to-peer transactions without the need for a central authority like a bank or government.

title: Bitcoin Cash
definition: Bitcoin Cash (BCH) is a cryptocurrency that was created and launched to bring decentralization back to cryptocurrency. It is the result of a 2017 Bitcoin "hard fork," which occurs when an existing blockchain splits into two. Bitcoin Cash enables more transactions per block than Bitcoin, which reduces fees and transaction times.

title: Bitcoin Mining
definition: Bitcoin mining is a network-wide competition to generate a cryptographic solution that matches specific criteria. When a correct solution is reached, a reward in the form of bitcoin and fees for the work done is given to the miner(s) who reached the solution first.

title: Bitcoin Misery Index
definition: The Bitcoin Misery Index (BMI) is a measurement of Bitcoin's price action. It ranges from 0 to 100 and usescontrarianeconomic indicators—in which opportunities move opposite of conventional indicators. It incorporates several different market factors such as win ratios and volatility.

title: Bitcoin Wallet
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Black Money
definition: Black money is illegally obtained funds not recorded for tax purposes.

title: Black Box Model
definition: In various fields such as finance, computing, and engineering, black box models operate by producing outcomes from inputs without disclosing the internal logic. These models drive significant decision-making processes, making them crucial in modern finance and investment strategies by utilizing advancements in artificial intelligence and machine learning.

title: Black Friday
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Black Market
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Black Monday
definition: Black Monday was the day, Oct. 19, 1987, when theDow Jones Industrial Average(DJIA) lost 22.6% of its value in a single trading session. The S&P 500 declined 30% on the same day. Black Monday triggered a global stock market decline.Economists attributed the crash to geopolitical events and computerized program trading that accelerated the selloff. In the aftermath, the Federal Reserve cut interest rates by half a percentage point to encourage more lending and spending. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) established a number of protective mechanisms, such as trading curbs and circuit breakers, to prevent panic-selling.

title: Black Scholes Model
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Black Swan
definition: A black swan is an unpredictable event that is beyond what is normally expected of a situation and has potentially severe consequences.

title: Black Tuesday
definition: Black Tuesday refers to Oct. 29, 1929, when the stock market experienced a dramatic decline, particularly the Dow Jones Industrial Average, marking the onset of the Great Depression.

title: Blended Rate
definition: A blended rate is an interest rate applied to a loan that merges the previous rate with a new rate. It is used primarily in refinancing. Blended rates help borrowers understand the true cost of debt when refinancing and are crucial for both consumer and corporate financial planning. The most common method of calculating a blended rate is through a weighted average of existing and new loan rates. Blended rates often apply to mortgages and corporate debt, providing financial flexibility and favorable terms.

title: Blind Trust
definition: A blindtrustis a trust whose assets are unknown to its beneficiaries and whose owner, ortrustor, grants control of the trust to an independenttrustee. The trustee has full discretion over the management of the trust and its assets. They make financial decisions and conduct transactions without the involvement of the owner or beneficiaries. Blind trusts are often established to preventconflicts of interestand maintain privacy. They can be revocable or irrevocable.

title: Block Trade
definition: A block trade is a large, privately negotiated securities transaction.

title: Blockchain-as-a-Service (BaaS)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Blockchain Wallet
definition: Blockchain.com's DeFi Wallet (also called Blockchain Wallet) is the name of the decentralized finance wallet service provided by Blockchain.com, a cryptocurrency exchange founded by Peter Smith and Nicolas Cary. Its DeFi wallet allows you to manage, trade, swap, and use cryptocurrencies via Blockchain.com DEX (decentralized exchange).

title: Bloomberg
definition: Bloomberg delivers vital economic and financial data to professionals globally, aiding decision-making in real-time via sophisticated technology and analytics platforms.

title: Bloomberg Terminal
definition: The Bloomberg Terminal is a premium financial software platform that provides professionals with real-time market data, analytical software, and trading capabilities. It is widely regarded as the best all-in-one product for global financial information.

title: Blotter
definition: A financial blotter, also known as a trade blotter, is a physical or digital record comprising all trades made over a specific period, usually a single trading day. This vital document logs details such as time, price, and order details, providing an audit trail for transactions.

title: Blue Book
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Blue Chip
definition: A blue chip is a publicly traded company known for its stability, consistent earnings, sound financials, and long-standing reputation.

title: Blue-Chip Stock
definition: Blue chip stocks are issued by large, financially sound companies with a long history of consistent performance and strong reputations. These companies usually boast largemarket capitalizationsand are typicallyleadersin their respective sectors. Recognizable household names like IBM Corp., Coca-Cola Co., Microsoft, American Express, McDonald’s, and Boeing Co. are prime examples of blue chip stocks.

title: Blue Ocean
definition: The term "blue ocean" was introduced in 2005 to describe markets with little competition or few barriers for innovators. It refers to the vast, untapped market opportunities that arise when new industries or innovations emerge.

title: Blue Sky Laws
definition: Blue sky laws are state-level, anti-fraud regulations that require issuers of securities to be registered and to disclose details of their offerings in every state where they intend to do business. Blue sky laws act as an additional layer of security on top of federal regulations. Although they vary by state, the goal is fairness and consumer protection for investments.

title: Board of Directors (B of D)
definition: A board of directors is the governing body of a company or other organization and is tasked with setting strategy and overseeing its implementation.

title: Board of Governors
definition: A board of governors is an appointed group that oversees an institution's management, policies, and accountability. A leading example is theFederal Reserve'sBoard of Governors, which helps guide U.S. monetary policy and affects the broader economy; its members are nominated by the President, confirmed by the Senate, and serve staggered terms for continuity.

title: Board of Trustees
definition: A board of trustees is an appointed or elected group of individuals that has overall responsibility for the management of an organization. The board of trustees is typically the governing body of an organization and seeks to ensure the best interest ofstakeholdersin all types of management decisions.

title: Boil the Ocean
definition: "Boil the ocean" is an idiomatic phrase that means to undertake an impossible task orprojector to make a job or project unnecessarily difficult. The phrase appears in business and among startup companies, as well as in other group settings, and is considered to be a negative phrase in relation to how one approaches a task.

title: Boilerplate
definition: Boilerplate language is standardized text that can be used repeatedly in similar documents without needing to make major changes to the original.

title: Bollinger Band
definition: Bollinger Bands are a technical analysis tool developed by John Bollinger in the 1980s to help investors and traders gauge market volatility and identify when securities are poised to rise or fall.

title: Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE)
definition: The Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) is the first and largestsecurities marketin India and was established in 1875 as the Native Share and Stock Brokers' Association.Based in Mumbai, India, the BSE lists over 5,300 companies and is one of the largest exchanges in the world, along with the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), Nasdaq, London Stock Exchange Group, Japan Exchange Group, and Shanghai Stock Exchange.

title: Bond
definition: A bond is a fixed-income investment issued by governments or corporations to raise funding.

title: Bond Covenant
definition: A bond covenant is a legally binding agreement between a bond issuer and a bondholder that sets terms meant to protect both sides.Negative covenantslimit the issuer from taking certain activities, whileaffirmative covenantsrequire the issuer to meet specific requirements.

title: Bond Discount
definition: A bond discount occurs when a bond's market price falls below its par value at maturity, often $1,000. Bondholders benefit from capital appreciation as the bond matures at its higherface value. This article explains how different bonds can be traded at a discount or premium, focusing on key factors like interest rates and market demand.

title: Bond Equivalent Yield (BEY)
definition: A bond equivalent yield (BEY) shows the annual return on a bond. Discounted bonds don’t pay regular interest, while traditional bonds do. The BEY converts the discount bond’s return into an annual rate, so investors can compare it directly with the yearly yields of traditional bonds. As an investor, you can calculate the BEY by taking the difference between a bond’s face value and its purchase price, dividing that by the purchase price, and then adjusting it to a yearly rate based on how many days are left until the bond matures.

title: Bond ETF
definition: Bond ETFs areexchange-traded fundsinvested exclusively in bonds. They are passively managed and trade on major exchanges, similar tostock ETFs. The benefits of bond ETFs include market stability, liquidity, transparency, and exposure to various bond categories. Investors can buy bond ETFs with exposure to both U.S. and international markets. The risks of bond ETFs include the potential from a loss of value when interest rates rise.

title: Bond Fund
definition: A bond fund is a mutual fund or ETF that invests primarily in government and corporate bonds.

title: Bond Futures
definition: Bond futures are contracts that fix the future price of a government bond, protecting traders from interest rate swings.

title: Bond Ladder
definition: A bond ladder is a portfolio of fixed-income securities in which each security has a significantly different maturity date. The purpose of purchasing several smaller bonds with varying dates of maturity rather than one large bond with a single maturity date is to minimize interest-rate risk, increase liquidity, and diversify credit risk.

title: Bond Market
definition: The bond market is often referred to as the debt market, fixed-income market, orcredit market. It is the collective name given to all trades and issues ofdebt securities. Governments issue bonds to raise capital to pay debts or fund infrastructural improvements. Publicly traded companies issue bonds to finance business expansion projects or maintain ongoing operations.

title: Bond Quote
definition: A bond quote shows the market price for a bond as well as other details such as coupon rate and the maturity date.

title: Bond Rating
definition: Bond ratings are assessments by rating agencies of how likely a bond issuer is to repay their debt on time, portrayed through a letter grade.

title: Bond Rating Agencies
definition: Bond rating agencies evaluate the creditworthiness of debt securities and their issuers. The primary bond rating agencies in the U.S. include Standard & Poor's Global Ratings, Moody's, and Fitch Ratings.

title: Bond Valuation
definition: Bond valuation helps investors compare the value of a bond's future payments with other investments.

title: Bond Yield
definition: Bond yield is the annual interest that the holder of a bond will receive over its term to maturity.

title: Bondholder
definition: A bondholder is an entity that invests in or owns bonds. Bondholders hold debt securities that are typically issued by corporations and governments. They essentially lend money to bond issuers by giving them capital. In return, bond investors receive theirprincipalor initial investment back when the bonds mature. For most bonds, bondholders also receives periodic interest payments.

title: Bonus
definition: A bonus is a financial compensation that is above and beyond the normal payment expectations of its recipient. Companies may award bonuses to both entry-level employees and to senior-level executives. While bonuses are traditionally given to exceptional workers, employers sometimes dole out bonuses company-wide to stave off jealousy among staffers.

title: Bonus Depreciation
definition: Bonus depreciation allows businesses to reduce their taxable income by writing off a significant portion of the cost of eligible assets in their first year.

title: Bonus Issue
definition: A bonus issue of shares is an amount of additional shares given to stockholders, in proportion to their existing holdings and at no direct cost to them.

title: Book Building
definition: Book building is a method used by investment banks to gauge market interest and set the price for new securities, particularly in the context of an initial public offering (IPO).

title: Book Runners
definition: A book runner is the primary underwriter in investment banking, taking charge of the issuance and pricing of new securities like equity and debt. As a lead manager during these financial operations, the book runner not only coordinates with various firms but also mitigates risks associated with these transactions. Learn more about the integral role of book runners compared to other underwriters in the industry.

title: Book-to-Bill
definition: The book-to-bill ratio is a key metric that compares orders received to units shipped and billed, often used in the technology sector, particularly for semiconductors. This ratio indicates market demand strength—values above 1 suggest high demand, while values below 1 may reflect weaker demand. Understanding this ratio helps investors assess a company’s performance and forecast industry trends.

title: Book-to-Market Ratio
definition: The book-to-market ratio is a financial ratio that compares a company's shareholders' equity to its market cap to determine whether a stock is under- or overvalued.

title: Book Value
definition: A company’s book value equals the value of its assets remaining after accounting for its outstanding debts and other obligations.

title: Book Value of Equity Per Share (BVPS)
definition: Book value per share (BVPS) is a measurement of a company's total net assets minus its liabilities, expressed on a per-share basis.

title: Book Value Per Common Share
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Bookie
definition: "Bookie" is a slang term for "bookmaker." It's someone who facilitates gambling, most commonly on sporting events. A bookiesets odds, accepts and places bets, and pays out winnings on behalf of others.

title: Boom And Bust Cycle
definition: The boom and bust cycle is an informal term for the economic fluctuations between periods of prosperity and depression.

title: Bootstrap
definition: Bootstrapping is a method whereentrepreneursstart companies with minimal capital, using personal finances or operating revenues instead of external investments. This approach allows business owners to maintain control but comes with increased financial risks due to limited resources.

title: Borrowing Base
definition: A borrowing base represents the total loan a lender is willing to extend to a company, derived from the value of pledged collateral. Using "margining," lenders apply a discount rate to asset values to mitigate risks and establish a reliable credit limit for businesses.

title: Both-to-Blame Collision Clause
definition: A both-to-blame collision clause is a provision in marine insurance that requires the owners of two vessels to split the financial liability for a collision when negligence is found on both sides.

title: Bottleneck
definition: A bottleneck is a point of congestion in a production system (such as an assembly line or a computer network) that stops or severely slows the system. The inefficiencies brought about by the bottleneck often create delays and higherproduction costs.

title: Bottom Line
definition: The bottom line refers to a company's net income and is also known as earnings, or profit, or earnings per share (EPS). The reference to the bottom line describes the relative location of the net income figure on a company's income statement.

title: Bottom-Up Investing
definition: Bottom-up investing focuses on analyzing individual companies rather than broader economic trends. Investors who use this method look closely at fundamentals like revenue and earnings to find strong companies. Unlike top-down investing, which focuses on the economy or sector trends, bottom-up investing prioritizes the company itself. For example, an investor may study a company like Meta to evaluate its financial health and growth prospects before making an investment decision.

title: Bounced Check
definition: A bounced check is a check that cannot be processed because the account does not have enough funds to cover the payment.

title: Boundary Conditions: What They are, How They Work
definition: Boundary conditions are the maximum and minimum values used to indicate where the price of anoptionmust lie. Boundary conditions are used to estimate what an option may be priced at, but the actual price of the option may be higher or lower than what is set as the boundary condition.

title: Box Spread
definition: In options trading, a box spread creates a synthetic loan by pairing a bull call spread with a bear put spread and capturing the difference between strike prices at expiration.

title: Brain Drain
definition: Brain drain, often described as the exodus of skilled professionals from one region or industry to another, poses significant challenges to both the source and destination regions. Frequently driven by political instability, economic hardship, or better opportunities elsewhere, this phenomenon leads to a loss of valuable human capital and reduces consumer spending in the areas they depart from.

title: Branch Accounting
definition: Branch accounting involves maintainingseparate accountsfor each branch or operating location of an organization for greater transparency of transactions,cash flows, and performance. Branch accounting provides better accountability and control as profitability and efficiency can be tracked for each location. Typical users of branch accounting include geographically dispersed corporations,multinationals, and chain operators. Technically, the branch account is a temporary or nominal ledger account, lasting for a designated accounting period. Branch accounting has a long history, going back to the Venetian banks of the 14th century.

title: Branch Banking
definition: Branch banking involves having storefront locations distant from the home office, providing convenience to customers.

title: Branch Manager
definition: A branch manager is an individual responsible for managing the operations of a specific location of a financial institution, such as a bank.

title: Brand
definition: A brand is a product or a business with unique products and services that distinguish a company's identity among competitors. A brand is created through design, packaging, and marketing that create the uniqueness. A successful brand communicates qualities that resonate with consumers, giving it a competitive edge over others in the same industry.

title: Brand Awareness
definition: Brand awareness is a marketing term for the degree to which consumers recognize a product by its name. Ideally, brand awareness means consumers recognize and view the product positively compared to competitors.

title: Brand Equity
definition: Brand equity is the value premium that a company generates from a product with a recognizable name, when compared to a generic equivalent.

title: Brand Extension
definition: Brand extension is a strategy where a company uses its existing brand equity to launch new products and attract a broader customer base. Success depends on a clear connection between the original and new products to maintain brand integrity. Companies like Apple and Google have expanded effectively through this approach, while others, like Levi Strauss & Co., have struggled when extensions didn't align with consumer expectations.

title: Brand Identity
definition: A brand identity is the visible elements of a company's brand, including consumer perception, colors, design, and its logo.

title: Brand Loyalty
definition: Companies rely on a brand-loyal consumer base and repeat purchases to grow revenue.

title: Brand Management
definition: Brand management is a marketing function focused on building and maintaining a brand's value over time. It aims to increase brand equity, the recognition, perception, and loyalty customers associate with a product or company.

title: Brand Personality
definition: The term "brand personality" refers to a set of characteristics that is attributed to a brand name. An effective brand increases itsbrand equityby having consistent traits that resonate with one or more consumer segments.

title: Brand Recognition
definition: Brand recognition refers to consumers' ability to recognize a company through elements likelogos, packaging, slogans, or jingles. In advertising andmarketing, it plays an essential role in giving companies a competitive edge by influencing consumer choices and loyalty.

title: Brazil, Russia, India and China (BRIC)
definition: BRICS is an acronym for Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa. Formed in 2009, the bloc has 11 members and 10 partner nations.

title: Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa (BRICS)
definition: BRICS is an acronym for Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa. Formed in 2009, the bloc has 11 members and 10 partner nations.

title: Breadth Indicator
definition: Breadth indicators are mathematical formulas that measure the number of advancing and declining stocks, and/or theirvolume, to calculate the participation in a stock index's price movements. By evaluating how many stocks are increasing or decreasing in price, and how much volume these stocks are trading, breadth indicators help in confirming stock index pricetrends, or can warn of impending pricereversals.

title: Break-Even Price
definition: The break-even price is the point at which investment return equals the total cost of your investment.

title: Break-Even Analysis: What It Is, How It Works, and Formula
definition: A break-even analysis determines the sales volume needed to cover fixed and variable costs, indicating the point at which a business neither makes a profit nor incurs a loss.

title: Breakeven Point: Definition, Examples, and How To Calculate
definition: The breakeven point is the exact level of sales where a company's revenue equals its total expenses, meaning the business neither makes a profit nor has a loss.

title: Breakout
definition: A breakout refers to when the price of an asset moves above a resistance area, or moves below a support area. Breakouts indicate the potential for the price to start trending in the breakout direction. For example, a breakout to the upside from a chart pattern could indicate the price will start trending higher. Breakouts that occur on highvolume(relative to normal volume) show greater conviction which means the price is more likely to trend in that direction.

title: Bretton Woods Agreement and System
definition: The Bretton Woods agreement established a new international monetary system in 1944, with gold as the basis for the U.S. dollar and fixed exchange rates.

title: Brexit
