definition: A fidelity bond is a form ofbusiness insurancethat offers an employer protection against employee actions like fraud, theft, and forgery. Also known as an honesty bond, this form of insurance can protect against monetary or physical losses. There are generally two types: first-party fidelity bonds, which protect businesses from wrongful employee actions, and third-party fidelity bonds, which protect them from actions taken by contracted employees. Fidelity bonds are an important part of a risk management strategy.

title: Fiduciary Definition: Examples and Why They Are Important
definition: Fiduciaries are persons or organizations who act on behalf of others and are required to put clients’ interests ahead of their own.

title: Filing Status
definition: Filing status is a category that defines the type of tax return form a taxpayer must use when filing their taxes. Filing status is closely tied to marital status.

title: Fill Or Kill (FOK)
definition: Fill or kill (FOK) is a conditional type oftime-in-forceorder used insecuritiestrading that instructs a brokerage to execute a transaction immediately and completely or not at all. Active traders mostly use this type of order, usually for a large quantity of stock. The ordermust be filledin its entirety or else canceled (killed).

title: Finance
definition: Finance is the study and management of money and financial resources by individuals, corporations, and governments.

title: Financial Account
definition: A financial account is a section of a nation's economic records that monitors the flow of money to and from foreign investors.

title: Financial Accounting
definition: Financial accounting is a branch of accounting focused on recording, summarizing, and reporting the transactions from business operations over a period of time to present an accurate picture of a company's financial position.

title: Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB)
definition: The Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) is an independent nonprofit that sets accounting and financial reporting standards for U.S. companies and nonprofits undergenerally accepted accounting principles(GAAP). The FASB was formed in 1973 to replace theAccounting Principles Board, and it works with the International Accounting Standards Board (IASB) to promote global consistency and is governed by a board of trustees and full-time members based in Norwalk, Connecticut.

title: Financial Advisor
definition: A financial advisor provides financial advice or guidance to customers for compensation. Financial advisors (sometimes spelled as advisers) can provide many different services, such as investment management, tax planning, andestate planning. Increasingly, financial advisors are acting as a "one-stop-shop" by providing everything from portfolio management to insurance products.

title: Financial Analysis
definition: Financial analysis evaluates a business’s financial health and performance by reviewing its financial data. It helps management run the business better and outside investors decide whether the company is worth buying into.

title: Financial Asset
definition: A financial asset is a non-physical asset that generates income or can be converted to cash.

title: Finance Charge
definition: A finance charge is a fee charged for the use of credit or the extension of existing credit.

title: Financial Crisis
definition: A financial crisis is a state of financial instability marked by a severe drop in asset prices and liquidity shortages in financial institutions as well as the inability of businesses and consumers to meet their financial obligations.

title: Financial Distress
definition: Financial distress occurs when an individual or organization cannot meet financial obligations due to insufficient resources or income.

title: Financial Economics
definition: Financial economics is a branch of economics that analyzes the use and distribution of resources in markets.

title: Financial Engineering
definition: Financial engineering is an area of the financial sector that utilizes computer science, statistics, economics, and applied mathematics to solve quantitative financial problems. Financial engineering is used to analyze the financial markets, address current financial issues, and devise new and innovative financial products.

title: Financial Exposure
definition: Financial exposure is the amount an investor stands to lose in an investment should the investment fail, making it essentially another term for investmentrisk. Understanding financial exposure is important because it influences both the potential gains and losses that guide investment decisions.

title: Financial Guarantee
definition: A financial guarantee is a promise made to repay a loan or debt if the borrower cannot. This gives lenders protection and confidence in financial transactions. This guarantee comes from a third-party guarantor, which can improve a borrower’s credit rating and lower interest rates. Financial guarantees can take different forms, such as corporate or personal guarantees, making them an important tool for securing credit.

title: Financial Health
definition: Financial health is a term used to describe the state of one's personal monetary affairs. There are many dimensions to financial health, likesavings,retirement, and expenditure. These factors can be analyzed by looking at indicators such as net worth, budgeting, emergency funds, and debt management. By understanding financial health, people can achieve personal financial goals.

title: Financial Inclusion
definition: Financial inclusion seeks to make financial services accessible to all by removing barriers for those excluded. The key benefits include economic growth, innovation, and the empowerment of underserved communities. Fintech advancements play a significant role in facilitating financial inclusion with mobile banking, digital payments, and online lending platforms. Let's explore what financial inclusion means, how it works, and its broad importance in today's world.

title: Financial Independence, Retire Early (FIRE)
definition: Financial Independence, Retire Early (FIRE) is a movement of people devoted to a program of extremesavingsand investment with the goal of retiring far earlier than traditional budgets and retirement plans would permit.

title: Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA)
definition: The Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) is the mainself-regulatorybody overseeing brokers andbroker-dealerfirms in the United States, formed in 2007 through the merger of the NASD and the NYSE regulatory functions. Its mission is "to safeguard the investing public against fraud and bad practices" by creating and enforcing rules, monitoring firms, and offering tools like BrokerCheck to help the public review broker backgrounds.

title: Financial Information Exchange (FIX)
definition: The Financial Information eXchange (FIX) is a widely adopted communication protocol that enables real-time electronic sharing ofsecuritiestransaction details. Serving nearly 300 member firms, including majoinvestment banks, it stands as the leading standard for pre-trade, trade, and post-trade messaging. FIX ensures seamless communication within financial markets, fostering a faster and more accountable transaction environment.

title: Financial Institution (FI)
definition: A financial institution (FI) is in the business of facilitating financial and monetary transactions such as deposits, loans,investments, and currency exchange. Financial institutions connect savers and borrowers, playing a crucial part in a capitalist economy. They include banks, insurance companies, asset management companies, brokerage firms, and investment banking firms. Regulation of financial institutions is vital to maintaining the integrity and trust in financial systems.

title: Financial Instrument
definition: A financial instrument is a real or virtual legal agreement of monetary value that can be traded or exchanged, and may be an asset such as stocks, bonds, derivatives, or loans.

title: Financial Intermediary
definition: A financial intermediary is an entity that acts as themiddlemanbetween two parties in a financial transaction, such as acommercial bank, investment bank, mutual fund, or pension fund.

title: Financial Literacy
definition: Financial literacy is the set of skills needed to handle money wisely, invest effectively, and plan for a comfortable future.

title: Financial Market
definition: Financial markets are any marketplace where stocks, bonds, and other investments are traded. Financial markets are an important part of the economy as they match buyers and sellers to promote investment activity. Because of broader potential systematic risk in these types of markets, they can be highly regulated.

title: Financial Modeling
definition: Financial modeling involves analyzing a company's past financial performance to predict its future results.

title: Financial Performance
definition: A company’s financial performance shows how well it generates revenue and how it manages its liabilities and assets.

title: Financial Plan
definition: A financial plan outlines a person’s current circumstances and long-term objectives and establishes the spending, saving, and investing strategies that will help achieve those objectives.

title: Financial Planner
definition: A financial planner helps people manage their money and achieve their long-term financial goals.

title: Financial Risk
definition: Financial risk is the possibility of a financial loss. It includes currency risk, credit risk, liquidity risk, and operational risk.

title: Financial Risk Manager (FRM)
definition: The Financial Risk Manager (FRM) certification, issued by the Global Association of Risk Professionals (GARP), is a globally recognized credential for navigating modern financial risks. Those aspiring to earn this designation need to pass exams and gain relevant experience, offering an indelible mark of expertise in a rapidly changing financial landscape. This recognition opens up lucrative career opportunities across sectors such as banking, insurance, and asset management.

title: Financial Sector
definition: The financial sector is the part of the economy made up of firms and institutions that provide financial services to commercial and retail customers. This sector comprises a broad range of industries, including banks, investment companies, insurance companies, and real estate firms.

title: Financial Statement Analysis
definition: Financial statement analysis is the process of analyzing a company’sfinancial statementsfor decision-making purposes. External stakeholders use it to understand the overall health of an organization and to evaluate financial performance and business value. Internal constituents use it as a monitoring tool for managing the finances.

title: Financial Statements
definition: Financial statements are formal records that summarize a company's financial performance and position, providing a clear picture of its financial health.

title: Financial Structure
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Financial System
definition: A financial system is a network of institutions that facilitates lending, investment, and other financial activities.

title: Financial Technology (Fintech)
definition: Financial technology, commonly known as fintech, revolutionizes how financial services are delivered and consumed. At its essence, fintech helps businesses, entrepreneurs, and consumers efficiently manage their financial activities, leveraging cutting-edge software and algorithms accessible via computers and smartphones. Initially focused on backend technology for financial institutions in the early 21st century, fintech has since expanded into consumer-centric services. Today, it spans diverse sectors, including retail banking, education, investment management, nonprofits, and the burgeoning cryptocurrency market. This transition highlights fintech's versatility and its profound impact on both traditional financial systems and everyday financial engagements.

title: Financial Times Stock Exchange Group (FTSE)
definition: The FTSE (pronounced "footsie") refers to the FTSE Russell Group, owned by theLondon Stock Exchange Group(LSEG), which designs and manages global stock indexes. Its flagship index, the FTSE 100, tracks the performance of the 100 largest companies listed on the London Stock Exchange and serves as a key indicator of the U.K. market's health.

title: Financing
definition: Financing allows corporations and consumers to access capital for business operations, investment activities, or personal expenses.

title: Finder's Fee
definition: A finder's fee (also known as "referral income" or "referral fee") is a payment made to an intermediary in, or the facilitator of, a transaction. The finder's fee is rewarded because the intermediary discovered the deal and brought it to the attention of interested parties. Or, the intermediary brought the interested parties together, and the transaction took place as a result.

title: FINRA BrokerCheck
definition: FINRA BrokerCheck is a free, online tool that helps individuals researchbrokers, investment firms, and financial advisers. Investors can obtain a variety of information, including descriptions, services offered, credentials, sanctions, and registrations, that may be helpful in the selection andvettingof an individual financial advice provider or brokerage firm.

title: Fire Insurance
definition: Fire insurance is a specialized form ofproperty insurancethat safeguards against damages and losses due to fire incidents. While standard homeowners insurance may include some fire protection, additional fire insurance ensures comprehensive coverage for replacement, repair, or reconstruction of property. This type of insurance typically excludes certain risks such as fire damage from war or deliberate fires, encouraging homeowners to evaluate their coverage needs annually for adequate protection.

title: Firm
definition: A firm is an organization that conducts business activities, such as corporations and partnerships, to make profits.

title: First In, First Out (FIFO)
definition: FIFO is an accepted inventory costing method in the U.S. using Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP).

title: First Mortgage
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: First Mover
definition: A first mover is a service or product that gains acompetitive advantageby being the first to market with a product or service. Being first typically enables a company to establish strong brand recognition and customer loyalty before competitors enter the arena. Other advantages include additional time to perfect its product or service and setting the market price for the new item.

title: First Notice of Loss (FNOL)
definition: The first notice of loss alerts an insurance company to a policyholder's claim, beginning the procedure to address compensation for reported incidents.

title: First World
definition: A "first world" country is usually an industrialized, democratic nation with political and economic stability, acapitalisteconomy, and a highstandard of living. The term originated during the Cold War, referring to nations aligned with the United States and Western allies.

title: Fiscal Deficit
definition: A fiscal deficit refers to the shortfall in a government’s revenue compared to its spending during a certain period. When a country runs a fiscaldeficit, it means the government spends beyond its means. Fiscal deficits are calculated either as a percentage of a country’sgross domestic product (GDP)or by determining the amount of spending over revenue.

title: Fiscal Multiplier
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Fiscal Policy
definition: Fiscal policy uses tax policies and government spending to influence macroeconomic conditions, including employment and inflation.

title: Fiscal Year (FY)
definition: A fiscal year consists of a consecutive 12-month period that an organization uses for accounting, budgeting, and financial reporting purposes.

title: Fiscal Year-End
definition: Fiscal year-end is the end of a company's 12-month accounting period.

title: Fisher Effect
definition: The Fisher effect relates to nominal interest rates changing in response to fluctuations in inflation to keep the real interest rate stable.

title: Fisher Transform Indicator
definition: The Fisher Transform Indicator is atechnical indicatorthat converts prices into a Gaussiannormal distribution. It highlights when prices have moved to an extreme, based on recent prices. This may help in spotting turning points in the price of an asset. It also helps show the trend and isolate the price waves within a trend. The Fisher Transform Indicator was created by John F. Ehlers.

title: Fitch Ratings
definition: Fitch Ratings is a company that evaluates the integrity of debt instruments like bonds based on the financial stability of the issuing company or government body. Fitch Ratings determines how likely it is that the issuer will default on its debt.

title: Five Cs of Credit
definition: The five Cs of credit are five traits or characteristics used by lenders to assess a credit applicant's creditworthiness.

title: Five-Year Rule
definition: In the context of individual retirement accounts (IRAs), the 5-year rule commonly refers to a waiting period before you can withdraw funds without penalty from a Roth IRA. Learn more about the various definitions of a "5-year rule" and how they may apply to you.

title: Fixed Annuity
definition: A fixed annuity is a type of insurance contract that promises to pay the buyer a guaranteed interest rate on their contributions to the account. A variable annuity pays interest that fluctuates based on the performance of an investment portfolio chosen by the account's owner.

title: Fixed Asset
definition: Fixed assets are long-term tangible properties or equipment essential to a company's operations. These assets, such as buildings, machinery, and vehicles, appear on the balance sheet asproperty, plant, and equipment(PP&E). Unlike current assets, fixed assets are not easily converted to cash and serve multiple purposes including production, operations, or rental to third parties. Depreciation applies to these assets to reflect wear and tear over time, making them a critical aspect of financial reporting and analysis.

title: Fixed Asset Turnover Ratio
definition: The Fixed Asset Turnover Ratio (FAT) is found by dividing net sales by the average balance of fixed assets.

title: Fixed Capital
definition: Fixed capital is money that has been invested infixed assetsor the long-term assets that a company needs to start up and conduct business, such asproperty, plant, and equipment (PPE). These assets are considered fixed in that they are not consumed or destroyed during the actual production of a good or service but have a reusable value. Key aspects of fixed capital include depreciation and liquidity.

title: Fixed-Charge Coverage Ratio
definition: The Fixed-Charge Coverage Ratio (FCCR) is essential for evaluating how well a company can cover its fixed expenses, including debts and leases. By examining how earnings meet fixed charges, the FCCR provides insight into a company's financial health and serves as a key measure for banks assessing loan eligibility.

title: Fixed Cost
definition: A fixed cost is a business expense that doesn't vary even if the level of production or sales changes given a specific relevant range.

title: Fixed Exchange Rate
definition: A fixed exchange rate is a currency valuation system that fixes a country's currency to the market price of another, a basket of currencies, or to the value of a commodity or a basket of commodities.

title: Fixed Income
definition: Fixed income is a type of investment that provides the investor with a guaranteed return in interest or dividends in return for a lump-sum deposit.

title: Fixed Income Clearing Corporation (FICC)
definition: The term Fixed Income Clearing Corporation (FICC) refers to a regulatory clearing agency that deals with the confirmation, settlement, and delivery offixed-income assetsin the U.S. Founded in 2003, the agency is a part of the Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation (DTCC). It facilitates the orderly and efficient settlement of U.S. government securities andMBS transactions.

title: Fixed-Income Security
definition: A fixed-income security is an investment that provides a return through fixed periodic interest payments and the eventual return of principal at maturity.

title: Fixed Interest Rate
definition: A fixed interest rate offers stability, ensuring level payments throughout your loan's term, unlike variable rates, which fluctuate with market conditions. Understand the implications, calculation methods, and strategic choices involved in selecting a fixed or variable rate to better manage your financial planning.

title: Fixed-Rate Mortgage
definition: The term fixed-rate mortgage refers to a home loan that has a fixed interest rate for the entire term of the loan. In other words, the mortgage carries a constant interest rate for the entire loan, resulting in a fixed monthly payment as well.Mortgage loansare used to finance the purchase of a home or property.

title: Fixed-Rate Payment
definition: With a fixed-rate payment on a loan, the total amount of the payment due remains the same throughout the life of the loan, but the proportions that go to interest and principal change. Fixed-rate payments most often involve mortgage loans. When taking out such a loan, a borrower must decide between a fixed-rate payment and an adjustable-rate payment. Banks generally offer a variety of fixed-rate payment mortgage loans, each with a slightly different interest rate.

title: Flat
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Flat Tax
definition: A flat tax is a single percentage income tax rate applied to all taxpayers regardless of income.

title: Flat Yield Curve
definition: A flat yield curve plots the yield of bonds of different maturity dates, illustrating how the yields aren't changing as the term to maturity increases.

title: Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS)
definition: A flexible manufacturing system (FMS) is designed up front to be readily adapted to changes in the type and quantity of goods being produced. It allows for a make-to-order production strategy. An FMS enhances efficiency and reduces production costs, and its automated nature reduces labor costs. However, it requires a significant initial investment as well as skilled technicians. The FMS concept was pioneered by American inventor Jerome H. Lemelson in mid-20th century.

title: Flexible Spending Account (FSA)
definition: Aflexible spending account (FSA)is an employer-sponsored savings account that lets employees set aside pretax funds for qualified medical expenses. It can help the account holder save money at tax time, especially because the list of allowable expenses that can be paid for with FSA funds has grown in recent years.

title: Flip
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Float
definition: Float refers to double-counted money in banking systems caused by delays in processing deposits such as paper checks. A customer's account is credited upon checkdeposit, yet the amount takes time to clear from the payer's bank. Until cleared, this sum is recorded in two accounts, creating float. Though less common now due to digital transactions, float still plays a role in modern banking.

title: Floating Charge
definition: A floating charge is a dynamic security interest orlienover fluctuatingcurrent assetslike inventory, enabling companies to secure loans while maintaining operational flexibility. It's used to secureloanswith assets that are non-constant or fluctuate in value.

title: Floating Exchange Rate
definition: A floating exchange rate is an exchange rate system where a currency's value is based on supply and demand in the foreign exchange market.

title: Floating Interest Rate
definition: Unlike fixed interest rates, floating interest rates fluctuate over time based on market conditions or a benchmark rate

title: Floating Rate Fund
definition: A floating rate fund is a fund that invests in financial instruments that pays a variable orfloating interest rate. A floating rate fund, which can be a mutual fund or an exchange-traded fund (ETF), invests inbondsand debt instruments whose interest payments fluctuate with an underlying interest rate level. Typically, a fixed-rate investment will have a stable, predictable income. However, as interest rates rise,fixed-rate investmentslag behind the market since their returns remain fixed.

title: Floating Rate Note (FRN)
definition: A floating-rate note (FRN) is a debt instrument with a variable interest rate. The interest rate for an FRN is tied to a benchmark rate. Benchmarks include the U.S. Treasury note rate, the Federal Reserve funds rate—known as the Fed funds rate—the London Interbank Offered Rate (LIBOR), or theprime rate.

title: Floating Stock
definition: Floating stock is the number of shares available for trading of a particular stock. Low float stocks are those with a low number of shares. Floating stock is calculated by subtracting closely-held shares andrestricted stockfrom a firm’s total outstanding shares.

title: Floor Area Ratio (FAR)
definition: The floor area ratio (FAR) defines the balance between a building's usable floor space and the lot size it occupies, playing a vital role inzoning regulations. Local authorities use FAR to decide allowable density, impacting urban development and property valuations.

title: Floor Trader (FT)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Flotation
definition: Flotation is the process of converting a private company into a public company by issuing shares and making them available to the public for purchase.

title: Flotation Cost
definition: Flotation costs are incurred by a publicly traded company when it issues newsecuritiesand incurs expenses, such asunderwriting fees, legal fees, and registration fees. Companies must consider the impact these fees will have on how much capital they can raise from a new issue. Flotation costs, expectedreturn on equity, dividend payments, and the percentage of earnings the business expects to retain are all part of the equation to calculate a company’s cost of new equity.

title: Flow of Funds (FOF)
definition: Flow of funds (FOF) are financial accounts that are used to track the net inflows and outflows of money to and from various sectors of a national economy. Macroeconomic data from flow of funds accounts are collected and analyzed by a country's central bank. In the United States, this data is released by theFederal Reserve Bankapproximately 10 weeks after the end of each quarter.

title: Flow-Through Entity
definition: A flow-through entity is a legal business that passes any income it makes straight to its owners, shareholders, or investors. Only these individuals and not the entity itself are taxed on the revenues as a result. Flow-through entities help avoiddouble taxation, a common issue with income from regular corporations.

title: Folio Number
definition: Inmutual funds, a folio number is a unique number identifying your account with the fund. Like a bank account number, the folio number can be used as a way to uniquely identify fund investors. It also records the amount invested, transaction history, and contact details of each investor.

title: Follow-On Offering
definition: A follow-on offering (FPO) is a sale of stock shares following a company'sinitial public offering(IPO). Companies engage in follow-on offerings to raise capital to pay off debt, invest for growth, or for acquisitions. The two types of follow-on offerings are diluted and non-diluted. Diluted FPOs result in lowerearnings per share(EPS) because the number of shares in circulation increases. Non-diluted FPOs result in an unchanged EPS because it involves bringing existing shares to the market. Companies must register FPOs and provide aprospectusto regulators.

title: Follow On Public Offer (FPO)
definition: A follow-on public offer (FPO) is the issuance of new shares by a public company after itsinitial public offering (IPO). As such, FPOs mean that additional shares are offered to the public by companies that are already listed on exchanges. Follow-on public offerings, which are also known assecondary offerings, are generally used by companies to raise additional capital for their growth.

title: Food And Drug Administration (FDA)
definition: The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) is a government agency that is responsible for the promotion and protection of public health. The FDA was established when the Pure Food and Drugs Act was passed in 1906.The agency has different divisions that oversee a majority of the organization's obligations involving food, drugs, cosmetics, animal food, dietary supplements, medical devices, biological goods, and blood products.

title: Footnotes to the Financial Statements
definition: Footnotes to the financial statements refer to additional information that helps explain how a company arrived at its financial statement figures. They also help to explain any irregularities or perceived inconsistencies in year to year account methodologies. It functions as a supplement, providing clarity to those who require it without having the information placed in the body of the statement. Nevertheless, the information included in the footnotes is often important, and it may reveal underlying issues with a company's financial health.

title: For Sale By Owner (FSBO)
definition: "For sale by owner" (FSBO) indicates that a property is available for purchase directly from the owner rather than through a real estate agent or broker. Some sellers choose this option to avoid paying commissions to a seller's agent (also known as alisting agent or broker). FSBO sales, while less expensive for the owner, have their own set of costs.

title: Forbearance
definition: The term forbearance refers to the temporary postponement of loan payments, typically for amortgageor student loan.Lendersand other creditors grant forbearance as an alternative to forcing a property intoforeclosureor leaving the borrower to default.

title: Force Majeure
definition: Force majeure clauses in contracts are critical for mitigating risks from unforeseeable and uncontrollable events like natural disasters or wars. They free parties fromliabilitieswhenobligationscannot be fulfilled due to such external catastrophes.

title: Forecasting
definition: Forecasting is a planning tool by which historical data is used to predict the direction of future trends.

title: Foreclosure
definition: Foreclosure is the legal process by which a lender attempts to recover the amount owed on adefaulted loanby taking ownership of the mortgaged property and selling it. Typically, default is triggered when a borrower misses a specific number of monthly payments, but it can also happen when the borrower fails to meet other terms in the mortgage document.

title: Foregone Earnings
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA)
definition: Signed into law in 2010, the Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA) aims to curb tax evasion by U.S. citizens through their foreign financial assets. It mandates annual reporting by U.S. citizens and residents, whether living domestically or internationally, and obliges foreign financial institutions to disclose assets held by U.S. account holders. Non-compliance can result in steep penalties, underscoring the significance of understanding FATCA's requirements.

title: Foreign Aid
definition: The term foreign aid encompasses various contributions—gifts,grants, or loans—that one nation offers to another. It extends beyond justcapital, also covering essential food, supplies, and services like military and humanitarian assistance. This assistance often flows from economically advanced to developing nations, aiding in disaster response, conflict situations, or economic crises. The United States, known as a generous provider, leads in global aid contributions.

title: Foreign Corrupt Practices Act
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Foreign Currency Convertible Bond (FCCB)
definition: A foreign currency convertible bond (FCCB) is a hybrid security issued in a currency outside the issuer's home country, offering interest payments along with the option to convert the bond into shares.

title: Foreign Currency Swap
definition: A foreign currency swap is an agreement to exchange currency between two foreign parties, often employed to obtain loans at more favorable interest rates.

title: Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)
definition: Foreign direct investment (FDI) is a substantial, long-term investment made by a company or government into a foreign project or firm.

title: Foreign Earned Income Exclusion
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Foreign Exchange
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Foreign Exchange Reserves
definition: Foreign exchange reserves are assets denominated in a foreign currency held by a central bank. These reserves are used to back liabilities and influence monetary policy.

title: Foreign Exchange Risk
definition: Foreign exchange risk refers to the losses that a business conducting international transactions can incur due to fluctuations in currency rates.

title: Foreign Institutional Investor (FII)
definition: Foreign institutional investors (FIIs) are crucial participants in global markets, investing outside their home countries. They often include pension funds, mutual funds, and hedge funds. This article explores how FIIs contribute to economies, focusing on regulations in countries like India and China, where limits on FIIs seek to balance economic benefits with market stability.

title: Foreign Investment
definition: Foreign investment involves capital flows from one country to another, granting foreign investors extensive ownership stakes in domestic companies and assets.

title: Foreign Portfolio Investment (FPI)
definition: Foreign portfolio investment (FPI) involves investors holding financial assets, such as stocks and bonds, in another country to diversify their portfolios. Unlike direct investments, FPIs generally don't confer management control over the assets.

title: Foreign Tax Credit
definition: The foreign tax credit is a U.S. tax credit used to offset income tax paid abroad. U.S. citizens and resident aliens who pay income taxes imposed by a foreign country or U.S. possession can claim the credit. The credit can reduce your U.S. tax liability and help ensure you aren'ttaxed twiceon the same income.

title: Forensic Accounting
definition: Forensic accounting utilizesaccounting, auditing, and investigative skills to examine the finances of an individual or business. Forensic accountants areCPAsthat look for evidence of crimes and commonly work for insurance companies, financial institutions, and law enforcement agencies.

title: Forensic Audit
definition: A forensic audit is a detailed review of an organization’s financial records and transactions to find financial irregularities.

title: Forex (FX)
definition: Forex is short for "foreign exchange," which refers to the market in world currencies.

title: Forfaiting
definition: Forfaiting is a means of financing that enables exporters to receive immediate cash by selling their medium and long-term receivables—the amount an importer owes the exporter—at a discountthrough an intermediary. It expedites payment and the exporter eliminates risk by making the sale without recourse. It has no liability regarding the importer's possible default on the receivables.

title: Forfeited Share
definition: A forfeited share is what you lose when you do not meet the purchase requirements for shares in a company. For example, a forfeiture may occur if a shareholder fails to pay an owed allotment (call money) or sells or transfers shares during a restricted period. Investors want to avoid forfeited shares because they mean losing any money already paid toward the shares and giving up potential gains. When a share is forfeited, the shareholder no longer owes any remaining balance and surrenders any potential capital gain on the shares, which automatically revert back to the ownership of the issuing company.

title: Form 3
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Form 4
definition: Whenever company insiders, like directors and officers or shareholders with over 10% ownership, alter their stock holdings, they must file SEC Form 4 with theSEC. This form provides details on their relationship to the company and documents purchases and sales ofequityshares, ensuring transparency and compliance with federal securities regulations.

title: Form 144
definition: Form 144 is a notice for proposed stock sales filed with the U.S.Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). It's mandatory when selling stock if the sale exceeds 5,000 shares or $50,000 in a three-month period, and must be filed electronically or in print by executive officers, directors, or affiliates.

title: Form 1040X
definition: The 1040-X, Amended U.S. Individual U.S. Income Tax Return is a form used by taxpayers who need to correct an error in a previously filed federal tax return. Common mistakes corrected with this form include errors in the taxpayer's filing status or number of dependents or omissions of credits or deductions. Form 1040-X should not be used to correct simple mathematical errors in a tax return, as the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) routinely corrects such mistakes while processing returns.

title: Form 1045
definition: Form 1045: Application for Tentative Refund is anInternal Revenue Service(IRS) form used by individuals,estates, and trusts to apply for a quick tax refund.

title: Form 1065
definition: Form 1065, the U.S. Return of Partnership Income, is a crucial IRS tax document for partnerships to report financial activities such as profits, losses, deductions, and credits. Partnerships themselves don’t pay income taxes; instead, profits or losses pass to individual partners to handle on their personal tax returns.

title: Form 1095-A
definition: Every year, theHealth Insurance Marketplacesends Form 1095-A to Americans who have health insurance coverage through a carrier on the exchange.

title: Form 1095-B
definition: Form 1095-B is anInternal Revenue Service (IRS)form that may be sent to taxpayers who receiveminimum essential health insurance coverageas defined by theAffordable Care Act(ACA).

title: Form 1095-C
definition: Form 1095-C: Employer-Provided Health Insurance Offer and Coverage is anInternal Revenue Service(IRS) tax form reporting information about an employee's health coverage offered by an Applicable Large Employer (ALE). ALEs typically have 50 or more full-time employees.

title: Form 1098
definition: Form 1098 is a tax document that homeowners will receive if they pay $600 or more inmortgage interestover the course of a year.Your mortgage lender will send you a copy of Form 1098 so you can prepare to file your annual taxes.

title: Form 1099-B
definition: Form 1099-B, used by brokerages and barter exchanges, records taxpayer gains and losses annually. It helps taxpayers calculate gains and losses via Form 8949 and is essential for completing Schedule D on their tax returns.

title: Form 1099-DIV
definition: The term Form 1099-DIV, Dividends and Distributions refers to anInternal Revenue Service (IRS)form sent by banks and other financial institutions to investors who receive dividends and distributions from any type of investment during a calendar year. Investors can receive multiple 1099-DIVs. Each 1099-DIV form should be reported on an investor’s tax filing. Investors typically will not receive a 1099-DIV form if cumulative dividends are not greater than $10.

title: Form 1099-INT
definition: Form 1099-INT is anInternal Revenue Service (IRS)tax form used to report interest income. Form 1099-INT is issued by all entities that pay interest income to investors during the tax year. The form breaks down all types of interest income and associated expenses.

title: Form 1099-MISC
definition: Form 1099-MISC, an essential tool provided by the IRS, is used to report various types of miscellaneous compensation, including rent, prizes, healthcare payments, and more. Although it no longer reports nonemployee compensation as of the 2020 tax year, it remains crucial for documenting other income sources that businesses pay to individuals. Understanding its purpose helps taxpayers accurately report income and comply with IRS requirements.

title: Form 1099-R
definition: Form 1099-R: Distributions From Pensions, Annuities, Retirement, or Profit-Sharing Plans is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) tax form used to report distributions from retirement accounts like annuities, profit-sharing plans,individual retirement accounts (IRAs), pensions, and insurance contracts.This form tracks income from retirement plans and ensures it is reported correctly for tax purposes.

title: Form 1099-Q
definition: Form 1099-Q: Payments From Qualified Education Programs is anInternal Revenue Service (IRS)tax form sent to individuals who receive distributions from aCoverdell education savings account (ESA or CESA)or a529 plan. These distributions—including rollovers—may be taxable, and you must determine the tax liability of any distribution using other IRS information. The form is then used by taxpayers to fill out both federal and state tax returns if the distributions received are subject to tax.

title: Form 1120S
definition: Form 1120-S: U.S. Income Tax Return for an S Corporation is a tax document that is used to report income, gains, losses, credits, deductions, and other information pertaining to businesses that are registered asS corporationsto theInternal Revenue Service (IRS). The form must be filed by the 15th day of the 3rd month after the corporation's tax year.

title: Form 1310
definition: Form 1310 is an IRS form used to claim a refund on behalf of a deceased taxpayer, notifying the IRS of the taxpayer's death and requesting that their refund be sent to the appropriate party.

title: Form 13F (SEC)
definition: The SEC Form 13F is a crucial component for maintaining transparency in financial markets. Required for institutional managers with at least $100 million in assets under management, this quarterly filing offers a glimpse into the holdings and strategies of major players on Wall Street. Despite its regulatory importance, the form has faced criticism for issues related to data reliability and reporting timeliness, which can complicate its use for investors seeking to replicate top managers' strategies.

title: Form 2106: Employee Business Expenses
definition: Form 2106: Employee Business Expenses is a tax form from theInternal Revenue Service(IRS) that allows employees to deduct ordinary and necessary expenses related to their jobs. Ordinary expenses are generally considered common and accepted in a particular line of business, while necessary expenses are those that help conduct business.

title: Form 2106-EZ: Unreimbursed Employee Business Expenses
definition: Form 2106-EZ: Unreimbursed Employee Business Expenses was a tax form issued by theInternal Revenue Service(IRS) used by employees to deduct ordinary and necessary expenses related to their jobs

title: Form 2439
definition: Form 2439 is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) form that Regulated Investment Companies (RICs)–mutual funds and exchange-traded funds–and Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) are required to distribute to shareholders to report undistributed long-term capital gains. Mutual funds are required to distribute most capital gains to shareholders, and the shareholders report these gains on Form 1099-DIV. However, if the fund company decides to retain these gains, it must pay taxes on behalf of shareholders and report these transactions on Form 2439.

title: Form 2848
definition: Form 2848: Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative is anInternal Revenue Service(IRS) document that authorizes an individual or organization to represent a taxpayer by appearing before the IRS—at an audit, for example.﻿﻿

title: Form 4506: Request for Copy of Tax Return
definition: Our picks for thebest tax softwarecan help you file federal and state taxes accurately and efficiently.

title: Form 4562
definition: Form 4562: Depreciation and Amortization is anInternal Revenue Service(IRS) form used to claimdepreciationoramortizationdeductions on eligible tangible and intangible assets. It's commonly used by businesses to spread an asset's cost over multiple years rather than deducting it all at once.

title: Form 4684
definition: Form 4684 is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) form for reporting gains or losses from casualties and thefts which may bedeductiblefor taxpayers who itemize deductions. Casualty losses can be the result of fires, floods, and other disasters. In most cases, taxpayers can deduct losses in the tax year in which they happened. In the case of theft, the tax year is the year of loss discovery.

title: Form 4797
definition: Form 4797 is used for reporting gains made from selling or exchanging business property.

title: Form 4952
definition: Form 4952: Investment Interest Expense Deduction is a tax form distributed by theInternal Revenue Service(IRS) used to determine the amount ofinvestment interest expensethat can be deducted, as well as any interest expense that can be carried forward to a future tax year.﻿﻿

title: Form 5405
definition: Form 5405: First-Time Homebuyer Credit and Repayment of the Credit is a tax form distributed by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS). Taxpayers used it to claim a tax credit for a percentage of the purchase price of a new home.

title: Form 6251
definition: Form 6251: Alternative Minimum Tax-Individuals is anInternal Revenue Service(IRS) tax form used to determine the amount ofalternative minimum tax(AMT) that a taxpayer may owe.

title: Form 6252
definition: Form 6252: Installment Sale Income is anInternal Revenue Service(IRS) form used to report income from the sale of real or personal property coming from aninstallment salewith the installment method.

title: Form 6781
definition: IRS Form 6781 is used to report gains and losses from Section 1256 contracts (such as futures) and straddles, which are transactions that comply with mark-to-market rules.

title: Form 706
definition: Form 706: United States Estate (and Generation-Skipping Transfer) Tax Return is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) form that an executor of a decedent’s estate uses to calculate theestate taxand the generation-skipping transfer tax.

title: Form 8283
definition: Form 8283: Noncash Charitable Contributions is a tax form distributed by theInternal Revenue Service (IRS)and used by filers who wish to deduct non-cash contributions made to aqualifying charitable organization.Deductionsfor non-cash contributions are reported asitemized deductions.

title: Form 8379
definition: The "injured" spouse on ajointly filedtax return can file Form 8379 to regain their share of a joint refund that was seized to pay a past-due obligation of the other spouse. These obligations might include past-due federal tax, state tax, child support, or federal non-tax debt (such as a student loan). The term "injured" refers to the negatively impacted spouse, who does not owe the debt.

title: Form 8396
definition: Form 8396 is an IRS form that lets lower-income homeowners claim the mortgage interest credit, as long as they receive a mortgage credit certificate (MCC) from a state or local government agency.

title: Form 843
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Form 8606
definition: Internal Revenue Service (IRS)Form 8606, "Nondeductible IRAs," is used by filers who make nondeductible contributions to an individual retirement account (IRA). A separate form should be filed for eachtax yearthat nondeductible contributions are made.

title: Form 8949
definition: Form 8949: Sales and Other Dispositions of Capital Assets is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) form used to report capital gains and losses from investments. Individual taxpayers, partnerships, corporations, trusts, and estates use it.

title: Form ADV
definition: TheSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC)requires all professionalinvestment advisersto submit SEC Form ADV. This form specifies the investment style,assets under management (AUM), and key officers of an advisory firm. This form must be updated annually and it must be made available as a matter of public record for companies that manage funds in excess of $25 million.

title: Fortune 100
definition: The Fortune 100 are the top 100 companies in the United States on theFortune 500,a list of the 500 largest U.S. public and privately held companies that is published annually byFortunemagazine.

title: Fortune 500
definition: The Fortune 500 is an annual list of the 500 biggest United States companies. It was first published byFortunemagazine in 1955 and ranks public and private for-profit companies based on theirrevenuefrom the previousfiscal year.

title: Forward Contract
definition: A forward contract is an over-the-counter (OTC) instrument customized between two parties to buy or sell an asset at a specified price on a future date. Used primarily forhedging, these contracts offer flexibility in terms of commodities, amounts, and delivery dates but come with higher default risks due to their non-standardized nature and lack of a centralized clearinghouse.

title: Forward Dividend Yield
definition: A forward dividend yield is an estimation of a year's dividend expressed as a percentage of the current stock price. It is used to project the expected future income from an investment.

title: Forward Exchange Contract
definition: A forward exchange contract (FEC) is anover-the-counter(OTC) agreement between two parties to exchange currencies at a set rate on a future date, commonly used for currencies that are thinly traded or hard to convert.

title: Forward Integration
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Forward Market
definition: A forward market is an over-the-counter marketplace that sets the price of afinancial instrumentor asset for future delivery. Forward markets are used for trading a range of instruments, but the term is primarily used with reference to the foreign exchange market. It can also apply to markets for securities and interest rates as well ascommodities.

title: Forward Points
definition: In currency trading, forward points are the number ofbasis pointsadded to or subtracted from the currentspot rateof a currency pair to determine the forward rate for delivery on a specific value date. When points are added to the spot rate this is called a forward premium; when points are subtracted from the spot rate it is a forward discount.

title: Forward Premium
definition: A forward premium occurs when a currency's forward rate exceeds its current spot rate, signaling market expectations that the currency will weaken relative to another. This reflects anticipated shifts in exchange rates influenced by factors such as interest rate differentials, inflation, and economic stability.

title: Forward Price
definition: Forward price is the agreed-upon future delivery price in a forward contract for commodities, currencies, or financial assets. It's crucial for buyers and sellers seeking to hedge against market fluctuations. Using specific formulas based on current spot prices and carrying costs, the forward price helps neutralize a contract's initial value, while market dynamics later influence its positive or negative shifts.

title: Forward Price-To-Earnings (Forward P/E)
definition: Forwardprice-to-earnings(P/E) leverages forecasted earnings to assess a company's future value, providing investors with crucial insights despite potential variability in estimates. It is vital to recognize how forward P/E fluctuates by industry, driven by factors like growth rates and risk profiles.

title: Forward Rate
definition: A forward rate is an interest rate applicable to a financial transaction that will take place in the future.

title: Forward Rate Agreement (FRA)
definition: A forward rate agreement is an over-the-counter contract between parties that determines the rate of interest to be paid on an agreed-upon date in the future.

title: Four Asian Tigers
definition: The Four Asian Tigers are the high-growth economies of Hong Kong, Singapore, South Korea, and Taiwan. Fueled by exports andrapid industrialization, the Four Asian Tigers have consistently maintained high levels of economic growth since the 1960s, and have collectively joined the ranks of the world's wealthiest nations.

title: Four Percent Rule
definition: The 4% rule is a retirement withdrawal guideline suggesting retirees withdraw 4% of their retirement account in the first year and adjust that amount forinflationannually. This strategy aims to sustain funds for approximately 30 years, relying oninvestment returnsto provide a steady income stream from interest and dividends. However, this rule might not suit early retirees needing income for longer periods.

title: Four Ps
definition: The four Ps of marketing are product, price, place, and promotion, which are essential elements for successfully marketing a product or service.

title: Fourth World
definition: The Fourth World is an obsolete term that was used to describe the most underdeveloped, poverty-stricken, and marginalized areas of the world. Originating during the Cold War, it characterized nations and regions with limited economic resources, indigenous populations, and without sovereign recognition. Fourth World areas did not participate in the global economy. The concept emphasized the exclusion of certain ethnic and religious groups from global systems.

title: Fractal Indicator
definition: The fractal indicator is based on a simple price pattern frequently seen in financial markets that can suggest potential reversals.

title: Understanding Fractional Reserve Banking: How It Fuels Economic Growth
definition: Fractional reserve banking is a foundational concept of the modern financial system, used by most economies to drive growth by expanding the availability of capital for lending. This system allows banks to keeponly a fraction of their depositson hand, lending out the rest to generate returns and stimulate economic activity.

title: Fractional Share
definition: Less than one full share ofequityis called a fractional share. Fractional shares can result from stock splits, dividend reinvestment plans (DRIPs), or other corporate actions. Typically, fractional shares aren't available from the stock market, and while they have value to investors, they are also difficult to sell. Fractional shares can be common with high-priced stocks like Amazon and Google, making equity investments more accessible.

title: Franchise
definition: A franchise is a business model where the owner licenses operations, products, branding, and knowledge for a fee.

title: Franchise Tax: Definition, Rates, Exemptions, and Example
definition: The term "franchise tax" refers to a tax paid by certain enterprises that do business in some states.

title: Franchisee
definition: A franchisee is an independent business owner who buys the right to operate under a franchisor's established brand,trademarks, and business system. Examples include McDonald's or UPS locations.

title: Franked Dividend
definition: A franked dividend is a distribution of company profits to shareholders that comes with a tax credit, allowing investors to avoid double taxation on their dividend income. This arrangement is specifically used in Australia to ensure that investors do not pay income tax on dividends that have already been taxed at the corporate level. The company issuing the dividend provides a franking credit, which offsets the tax that the investor would otherwise owe for this income.

title: Fraud
definition: Fraud occurs when an entity, such as a person or institution, intentionally deceives others for financial or personal gain.

title: Freddie Mac
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Free Carrier (FCA)
definition: Free carrier is when aselleris responsible for delivering to a destination specified by the buyer. The destination is typically an airport, shipping terminal, warehouse, or other location where thecarrieroperates.

title: Free Cash Flow (FCF)
definition: Free cash flow (FCF) is the cash a company has left after spending money to support and maintain its operations and capital assets.

title: Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE)
definition: Free cash flow to equity (FCFE) indicates the cash a company can provide to its shareholders after covering expenses, reinvestments, and debts. This metric gauges financial health, especially crucial for dividend-less firms. Investors use FCFE to determine if dividends or buybacks are sustainably funded by the company's cash flow.

title: Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF)
definition: Free cash flow to the firm (FCFF) denotes the cash available for distribution after a company covers itsdepreciationexpenses, taxes,working capital, and investments. As a crucial indicator of profitability, FCFF aids in measuring a company’s financial health after all expenses and reinvestments. FCFF highlights the cash a company can return to its investors via dividends, share repurchases, or debt repayments, making it an essentialbenchmarkfor financial analysis.

title: Free Cash Flow Yield
definition: Free cash flow yield measures the financial performance of a company by comparing its free cash flow per share to its market price per share. This metric helps investors assess the attractiveness of the company as an investment, offering insight into how well a company can satisfy its obligations and benefit shareholders. The ratio is calculated by taking the free cash flow per share divided by the current share price.

title: Free Enterprise
definition: Free enterprise describes an economic system in which prices, products, and services are determined by market forces rather than government intervention. In such a system, businesses operate independently with minimal regulatory control. Key features include private property rights and voluntary exchanges.

title: Free-Float Methodology
definition: The free-float methodology is a widely accepted system for calculating the market capitalization of companies listed in stock indexes. By focusing only on shares available for public trading, excluding those held by insiders or restricted from the market, this method offers a more accurate reflection of market dynamics than the full-market capitalization method. Major indices like the S&P 500 utilize this approach, highlighting its importance in financial markets.

title: Free Look Period
definition: The free look period is the required time period in which a new life insurance policy owner can terminate the policy without any penalties, such as surrender charges. A free look period often lasts 10 or more days depending on the insurer and state law.

title: Free Market
definition: A free market is an economy with minimal or no government control of supply, demand, or prices.

title: Free On Board (FOB)
definition: Free on board is a shipping term for the point in the supply chain when a buyer or seller becomes liable for the goods being transported.

title: Free Rider Problem
definition: Free riding inhibits the production and consumption of goods and services in a free market.

title: Free Trade
definition: A free trade agreement between two or more countries reduces or eliminates certain costs and obstacles in order to more freely facilitate the exchange of goods and services.

title: Free Trade Area
definition: A free trade area is a region in which several countries sign a free trade agreement and maintain little to no barriers to trade in the form of tariffs or quotas among one another. Free trade areas facilitate international trade and any associated gains along with the international division of labor and specialization. These deals are highly criticized by some for costs associated with increasing economic integration and artificially restraining free trade.

title: Freemium
definition: A combination of the words “free” and “premium,” freemium is a type of business model that offers basic features of a product or service to users at no cost and charges a premium for supplemental or advanced features.

title: Frequency Distribution
definition: A frequency distribution is a statistical representation that quantifies the number of instances of distinct values or intervals within a dataset.

title: Freudian Motivation Theory
definition: Freudian motivation theory asserts that unconscious psychological forces shape our behavior, including those relating to our investing decisions. Marketers apply it to understand consumer motivations and emotional responses. According to the theory, conscious needs and hidden desires both drive consumer purchase behavior. Techniques like role-playing and word association can uncover deeper consumer motivations.

title: Frictional Unemployment
definition: Frictional unemployment represents a very small percentage of the total unemployment rate because it isn't seen as posing a strong threat to the economy.

title: Friedrich Engels
definition: Friedrich Engels was a German philosopher, writer, and social scientist during the 19th century. Known for his collaboration withKarl Marx, Friedrich Engels helped define moderncommunism. Friedrich Engels is the co-author ofThe Communist Manifesto, an 1848 pamphlet regarded as one of the world's most influential political documents. Engels died on Aug. 5, 1895, in London, England.

title: Friedrich Hayek
definition: Friedrich Hayek was a champion of economic ideas that emphasize limited government intervention and advocate for individual freedoms in economic activities.

title: Fringe Benefits
definition: Fringe benefits are additions to compensation that employers offer workers to recruit, motivate, and retain high-quality talent. Examples include a company car, paid time off, and a gym membership.

title: Front-End Debt-to-Income Ratio (DTI)
definition: Your front-end debt-to-income ratio (DTI) represents the percentage of your monthlygross incomethat goes to paying your total monthly housing expenses. The front-end DTI ratio helpsmortgage lendersdetermine the affordability of borrowers looking to buy a home.

title: Front-End Load
definition: A front-end load is a sales charge orcommissioninvestors pay when they purchase an asset such as amutual fundor insurance product. This fee is deducted from the initial deposit, affecting the invested capital. Front-end loads typically reward financial advisors for guiding clients toward suitable investments. Understanding the range of these fees, which often vary by asset type, is crucial for making informed investment decisions. Additionally, knowing how they differ from back-end loads and other ongoing charges can help investors evaluate the long-term impacts on their portfolio.

title: Front Office
definition: The term front office is used to describe the customer-facing divisions of a firm. Customer service, marketing, sales, and advisory roles are often considered part of a firm's front-office operations, whereas the term "middle office" is used to describe the divisions that manage risk and corporate strategy, and the term "back office" refers to analytical, technical, and administrative support personnel.

title: Front-Running
definition: Front-running is trading of stock or any other financial asset by a broker who has inside knowledge of a future transaction that is about to affect its price substantially. A broker may also front-runbased on insider knowledgethat their firm is about to issue a "buy" or "sell" recommendation to clients that will almost certainly affect the price of an asset.

title: Full Costing
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Full Disclosure
definition: Full disclosure is the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission's(SEC)requirement that publicly traded companies release and provide for the free exchange of all material facts that are relevant to their ongoing business operations.

title: Full Employment
definition: Full employment exists when all willing and available skilled and unskilled labor is being used.

title: Full Ratchet
definition: A full ratchet is an anti-dilution provision that adjusts the option price or conversion ratio for shareholders to the lowest sale price. This prevents ownership dilution for early investors in subsequent fundraising rounds, but can make it harder to attract new rounds of investment. A weighted average approach is a fairer alternative for balancing the interests of founders, early investors, and later investors.

title: Fully Amortizing Payment
definition: A fully amortizing payment refers to a type of periodic repayment on a debt. If the borrower makes payments according to the loan'samortization schedule, the debt is fully paid off by the end of its set term. If the loan is a fixed-rate loan, each fully amortizing payment is an equal dollar amount. If the loan is an adjustable-rate loan, the fully amortizing payment changes as the interest rate on the loan changes.

title: Fully Diluted Shares
definition: Fully diluted shares represent the total number of common shares that would be outstanding and available to trade on the open market if all possible sources of conversion are exercised. Fully diluted shares include not only those currently issued but also all that could be created through conversions.

title: Fully Vested
definition: Being fully vested means a person has rights to the full amount of some benefit, most commonly employee benefits such as stock options, profit sharing, or retirement benefits. Benefits that must be fullyvested benefitsoften accrue to employees each year, but they only become the employee's property according to avesting schedule.

title: Functional Currency
definition: Popular with multinationals, functional currency represents the primary economic environment in which an entity generates and expends cash. It is the maincurrencyused by a business in its business dealings.

title: Functional Obsolescence
definition: Functional obsolescence refers to the diminished usefulness or appeal of a product or feature because its design has become outdated, such as a house with too few electrical outlets or a smartphone that can’t support current apps. It occurs when changing standards, technologies, or consumer preferences make something less practical, even if it still works. Functional obsolescence can affect value, usability, and long-term viability in industries like real estate and technology. Recognizing how and why it happens helps consumers and businesses make smarter purchasing, upgrading, and planning decisions when faced with aging designs.

title: Fund
definition: A fund is a pool of money that is allocated for a specific purpose. A fund can be established for many different purposes: a city government may set aside money to build a new civic center, a college may set aside money to award a scholarship, or an insurance company may set aside money to pay its customers’ claims.

title: Understanding Fund Flow: Definition, Examples, and Insights for Investing
definition: Fund flow is the cash that flows into and out of variousfinancial assetsfor specific periods of time. It's usually measured on a monthly or quarterly basis.

title: Fund Manager
definition: Fund managers devise strategies for and manage investments held in mutual funds, pension funds, trust funds, and hedge funds.

title: Fund of Funds (FOF)
definition: A fund of funds (FOF) pools investment capital into a wider portfolio of several investment funds instead of directly investing in single funds or specific individual securities.

title: Fundamental Analysis
definition: Fundamental analysis is a technique to measure a company's value based on its financials and other objective metrics, as opposed to market price trends.

title: Fundamentals
definition: Fundamentals provide essential qualitative and quantitative data that define a company's financial health and economic standing. This article explores how fundamentals can set the financialvalueof a company or currency throughqualitativeinsights andquantitative analysis. It discusses macroeconomic and microeconomic fundamentals, showcasing how these underpin assessments of profitability, revenue, and growth potential. Readers will also learn about key financial ratios to evaluate investments accurately.

title: Understanding Funded Debt: Types and Implications in Corporate Accounting
definition: Funded debt is a company's debt that matures in more than one year or one business cycle. This type of debt is classified as such because it is funded byinterestpayments made by the borrowing firm over the term of the loan.

title: Funds From Operations (FFO)
definition: Funds from operations (FFO) serve as a key metric for evaluating REIT performance by offering insights into cash flow and operational efficiency. Unlike traditional corporate metrics, FFO includes adjustments for depreciation and amortization, providing a transparent view of REITs' income-generating capabilities.

title: Funds Transfer Pricing (FTP)
definition: Funds Transfer Pricing (FTP) is a significant tool used by financial institutions. FTP is most commonly used in the banking industry to help financial institutions internally analyze their strengths and weaknesses, as well as to assess the profitability of a company's funding sources.

title: Fungibility
definition: Fungibility refers to the ability of assets to be interchangeably used, simplifying trade and exchange. Examples include money and commodities like corn, where identical units can be substituted without loss of value or function. By understanding this concept, readers can grasp its significance in economics and finance.

title: Furniture, Fixtures, and Equipment (FF&E)
definition: Furniture, Fixtures, and Equipment (FF&E) play a crucial role in business operations, comprising essential movable items like desks, chairs, and computers. These tangible assets are critical in financial assessments, impacting business valuation and financial strategies due to their depreciation over time. Understanding FF&E is vital for accurate accounting and budgeting.

title: Future Value (FV)
definition: Future value (FV) is the value of a current asset at a future date based on an assumed growth rate. Investors and financial planners use it to estimate how much an investment today will be worth in the future.

title: Future Value of an Annuity
definition: The future value of an annuity is how much recurring payments will be worth at a future date, based on a specified rate of return ordiscount rate.

title: Futures
definition: Futures are agreements to buy or sell assets at a set price on a set date in the future.

title: Futures Commission Merchant (FCM)
definition: A futures commission merchant (FCM) is an individual or organization involved in the solicitation or acceptance of buy or sell orders for futures or options on futures in exchange for payment of money (commission) or other assets from customers.

title: Futures Contract
definition: Futures contracts are standardized legal agreements that mandate the purchase or sale of a specific commodity, asset, or security at a predetermined price on a specified future date. These contracts facilitate trading onfutures exchangesby ensuring standardized terms for quality and quantity, thus enabling efficient market operations. Buyers commit to purchasing the underlying asset at contract expiration, while sellers commit to delivering it. By understanding futures contracts, market participants can hedge against price fluctuations or speculate on market movements, making these contracts vital tools for both risk management and investment strategies.

title: Futures Market
definition: A futures market is an exchange specializing in futures contract, which are derivatives that lock in the future delivery of a commodity or security at a set price. It is anauction marketfor a variety of commodity and futures contracts. Originally, this trading occurred through theopen outcryof exchange participants and by hand signals intrading pits. In the 21st century,futures exchanges, like most markets, have become mostly electronic.

title: GAFAM Stocks
definition: GAFAM is an acronym for five popular U.S. tech stocks: Google (Alphabet), Apple, Facebook (Meta), Amazon, and Microsoft.

title: Gain
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Gambler's Fallacy
definition: Often called the Monte Carlo fallacy, the gambler's fallacy is the incorrect belief that past outcomes affect future ones, despite each event being independent. This misunderstanding is prevalent in gambling and investing, where individuals wrongly predict outcomes based on previous results.

title: Game Changer
definition: Throughout time, certain ideas have changed how people live, societies function, and even the course of history. These ideas have been brought to life by individuals andcompaniesthat envisioned what could be and committed to creating the changes that they believed in.

title: Game Theory
definition: Game theory examines how individuals and entities, referred to as players, strategize and make decisions in competitive environments. As a theoretical framework, it models scenarios involving conflicts of interest and provides insights into possible outcomes and strategies. Often described as the science of strategy, game theory helps predict and explain the decisions made by independent and competing actors in strategic settings. Its applications are vast, spanning fields including business, psychology, economics, and politics to address complex scenarios such as pricing strategies, mergers, and negotiations.

title: Gamification
definition: Gamification describes the incentivization of people's engagement in non-game contexts and activities by using game-style mechanics.

title: Gamma
definition: Gamma measures how much an option's delta shifts when the price of the underlying asset changes by one point.

title: Gamma Hedging
definition: Gamma hedging is a trading strategy that tries to maintain a constant delta in anoptionsposition, often one that is delta-neutral, as the underlying asset changes price. It is used to reduce the risk created when the underlying security makes strong up or down moves, particularly during the last days before expiration.

title: Gamma Neutral
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Gann Angles
definition: Gann angles are named after their creatorW.D. Gann. Gann believed the angles could predict future price movements based on geometric angles of time versus price. Gann was a 20th-century market theorist. The validity and usefulness of his theories, however, are subject to debate.

title: Gann Fans
definition: Gann fans are a form oftechnical analysisbased on the idea that the market is geometric and cyclical in nature. A Gann fan consists of a series of lines called Gann angles. These angles are superimposed over a price chart to show potentialsupport and resistancelevels. The resulting image is supposed to help technical analysts predict price changes.

title: Gantt Chart
definition: A Gantt chart is a bar chart that displays a project schedule according to a list of tasks.

title: Gap
definition: A stock gap represents a discontinuity in a security's price on a chart, often caused by impactful news or events outside of regular trading hours. These gaps can signal potential market shifts, and understanding the four types—common, breakaway, runaway, and exhaustion—helps traders make informed decisions.

title: Gap Analysis
definition: A gap analysis examines business results to ascertain whether a company is meeting set objectives.

title: Gap Insurance
definition: Gap insurance is auto coverage that pays the difference if your car is totaled and the insurance payout doesn't cover what you still owe on a loan or lease, often a concern with long loan terms and rapid depreciation. For example, if the car is worth $18,000 but you owe $22,000, it can cover the $4,000 gap. You can usually buy it through your insurer, dealership, or lender (at purchase or added later).

title: Gapping
definition: Gapping occurs when the price of a stock, or another asset, opens above or below the previous day'sclosewith no trading activity in between. Agapis the area discontinuity in a security's price chart. Gaps may materialize when headlines cause market fundamentals to change rapidly during hours when markets are typically closed; for instance, the result of anearnings callafter-hours.

title: Garage Liability Insurance
definition: Garage liability insurance is specialty insurance targeted to the automotive industry. Automobile dealerships, parking lots or parking garages operators, tow-truck operators, service stations, and customization and repair shops will add garage liability insurance to their business liability coverage. The policy protects property damage and bodily injury resulting from operations.

title: GARCH Process
definition: The generalized autoregressive conditional heteroskedasticity (GARCH) process is an econometric model for estimating volatility in financial markets, developed in 1982 by Nobel laureateRobert F. Engle. GARCH is widely used by financial institutions to forecast returns, optimize portfolios, and manage the risk of stocks, bonds, and other assets.

title: Gardening Leave
definition: Garden leave, popular in the financial industries of the U.K., Australia, and emerging in the U.S., refers to a period when an employee, while still receiving a paycheck, stays away from the workplace during their notice period.

title: Garn-St. Germain Depository Institutions Act
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Garnishment
definition: Garnishment is a legal mechanism allowing creditors to collect unpaid debts by directing a third party, often the debtor's employer, to withhold earnings or access bank funds. Typically triggered by unpaid taxes, fines, or defaulted loans, garnishments require a court order, though exceptions exist, like IRS levies.

title: Gartley Pattern
definition: The Gartley pattern is a widely recognized harmonic chart pattern that leveragesFibonaccinumbers and ratios to assist traders in pinpointing potential market highs and lows. Originating from H.M. Gartley’s foundational work in 1935, this pattern has become the most commonly used due to its effectiveness in forecasting price movements.

title: Gas (Ethereum)
