definition: Gas fees on the Ethereum blockchain are essential for conducting transactions and executing contracts. Priced in gwei, smaller fractions of Ethereum's cryptocurrency, ETH, these fees compensate validators for the resources expended during transaction processing. The price of gas fluctuates based on supply, demand, and network capacity, making it a competitive and dynamic component of Ethereum's operation.

title: Gas Guzzler Tax
definition: The gas guzzler tax is a surcharge added to the sales or lease price of cars in the U.S. that have poor fuel economy ratings. The tax, which is paid by the manufacturer or importer of the vehicle, varies depending on the miles-per-gallon efficiency of the vehicle and ranges from $1,000 to $7,700.

title: Gate Provision
definition: A gate provision is a restriction placed on a hedge fund limiting the number of withdrawals from the fund during a redemption period.

title: Gatekeeper
definition: In healthcare, a gatekeeper is a professional or system that directs care efficiently by managing how patients access medical services. Gatekeepers commonly control specialist referrals and help reduce unnecessary tests and treatments. As such, they play a key role in controlling costs and improving care coordination. Gatekeepers appear in health insurance, often through primary care physicians, and in long-term care insurance via specific policy requirements. Countries like the U.S. rely on formal gatekeeping systems, while Austria largely doesn't.

title: Gazelle Company
definition: According to the original technical definition, a gazelle company is a high-growth company that has been increasing itsrevenuesby at least 20% annually for four years or more, starting from a revenue base of at least $100,000.

title: GBP
definition: GBP is the abbreviation for the British pound sterling, the official currency of the United Kingdom, its overseas territories, and its crown dependencies. The pound sign (£) represents the currency, often called “sterling” or “quid,” and it holds a longstanding place in global finance as the oldest currency in use.

title: GDAX
definition: GDAX is the former name of Coinbase Advanced Trade, a cryptocurrency trading platform from Coinbase. It is designed for active traders and allows users, especially in the U.S., to trade digital assets like Bitcoin with fiat currencies. It offers lower fees, charts, and advanced order tools. The platform became a central hub for serious crypto trading after it was rebranded because of the expansion of the number of supported cryptocurrencies.

title: GDP Gap
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: GDP Price Deflator
definition: The GDP price deflator is a gauge of inflation that computes changes in the prices of goods and services that are made in the U.S., including exports.

title: Gearing
definition: Gearing, which is also known as leverage, refers to the extent to which a company is financed with debt rather than equity.

title: Gearing Ratio
definition: The gearing ratio is a measure of financial leverage that indicates the degree to which a firm's operations are funded by equity capital vs. creditor (debt) financing.

title: Gemini Exchange
definition: Gemini is a global cryptocurrency exchange. Founded in 2014, the Gemini Exchange, also known as theGemini Trust Company, is the brainchild of Cameron and Tyler Winklevoss, the famous investors, twins, and Harvard classmates of Mark Zuckerberg.

title: Gemology
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: General Account
definition: In insurance, the general account refers to the fund where insurers place the premiums generated through policyholder payments.

title: General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT)
definition: The General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT), signed in 1947 by 23 countries, is a treaty minimizing barriers to international trade by eliminating or reducing quotas,tariffs, and subsidies. It was intended to boost economic recovery after World War II.

title: General Agreements to Borrow (GAB)
definition: An escrow agreement is a vital contract involving parties and an independentthird party, theescrow agent, ensuring security in transactions. Common in real estate and other high-value dealings, it mandates that an asset be held until contract terms are met. The agreement outlines essential roles, conditions, and safeguards, making transactions secure and dependable.

title: General and Administrative Expense (G&A)
definition: General and administrative (G&A) expenses are incurred in the day-to-day operations of a business and may not be directly tied to a specific function or department within the company.

title: General Business Tax Credit
definition: The general business credit (GBC) is the aggregate value of individual tax credits a business claims during a tax year. It consists ofcarryforwardcredits from previous years plus the current year's total business credits. Because it's a tax credit, not atax deduction—the amount comes directly off the tax bill.

title: General Collateral Financing Trades (GCF)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)
definition: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), effective since May 25, 2018, is the most rigorous security and privacy law globally. It was designed to give individuals control over their personal data by dictating the standards for collection and processing of personal information for both residents within and outside theEuropean Union(EU).

title: General Equilibrium Theory
definition: General equilibrium theory, developed by Léon Walras, explains how all markets in an economy interact to achieve overall balance. Unlike partial equilibrium theory, it examines the interconnectedness of supply and demand across markets under assumptions of perfect knowledge and competition. Austrian economists later challenged these ideas, emphasizing real-world complexity and market imperfections.

title: General Depreciation System (GDS)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: General Ledger
definition: A general ledger stores and organizes the data that are itemized in a firm’s financial statements.

title: General Manager (GM)
definition: A General Manager (GM) oversees department operations, drives revenue, and manages costs within a company. Ranking above employees yet below executives, GMs are crucial for improving efficiency and increasing profit. They manage staff, budgets, and marketing, while reporting to higher-level executives and supervising lower-level managers.

title: General Obligation Bond (GO)
definition: A general obligation bond (GO bond) is a type of municipal bond primarily relying on an issuing government's credit and taxation ability for its backing, rather than revenue from a specific project. With no assets held as collateral, GO bonds reflect confidence in a municipality’s capacity to meet its financial obligations via tax revenue. Distinguished from revenue bonds, GO bonds provide local governments with a means to fund public projects like roads and bridges without being tied to project revenue.

title: General Order (GO)
definition: A general order (GO) is a U.S. Customs status forimportsthat can't be cleared due to missing or incorrect paperwork, invoicing, or unpaid duties. If the goods aren't cleared within 15 days, they may be moved to a bonded warehouse, incur storage costs, and potentially be sold at auction, so importers need to resolve issues quickly to avoid delays and losses.

title: General Partner
definition: A general partner is an individual who oversees the operations of a partnership and legally shares in the partnership's liabilities and obligations.

title: General Partnership
definition: A general partnership is a business arrangement by which two or more individuals agree to share responsibilities, assets, profits, and financial and legal liabilities associated with a jointly-owned business. They are less expensive to form than a corporation. Each partner's financial involvement directly affects their personal liabilities and tax responsibilities. Importantly, partners assumeunlimited liability, potentially subjecting their personal assets to seizure if the partnership becomes insolvent. General partnerships arepass-through entitiesby which profits or losses are passed directly to partners, who report them on their personal tax returns. The partnership itself isn't taxed.

title: General Provisions
definition: General provisions are financial allocations made by businesses to account for potential future expenses or losses, ensuring they are prepared for uncertain events.

title: General Public Distribution: What It is, How It Works, Example
definition: In finance, the term general public distribution refers to the process by which aprivate companybecomes apublicly traded companyby selling its shares to the public at large. This is in contrast to a conventional public distribution, in which the shares are sold largely toinstitutional investors.

title: Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)
definition: GAAP stands for generally accepted accounting principles, which set the criteria for preparing, presenting, and reporting financial statements in the U.S.

title: Generally Accepted Auditing Standards (GAAS)
definition: Generally accepted auditing standards (GAAS) are a set of systematic guidelines used by auditors when conducting audits of companies' financial records. GAAS helps to ensure the accuracy, consistency, and verifiability of auditors' actions and reports. TheAuditing Standards Board (ASB)of theAmerican Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA)created GAAS. Its members agree to adhere to the standards.

title: Generally Accepted Principles And Practices (GAPP)
definition: The generally accepted principles and practices, also known as GAPP or the Santiago Principles, are a set of standardized business procedures specific to sovereign wealth funds (SWFs). They focus on financial stability over political agendas, promoting investor transparency and good governance within SWFs.

title: Generation Gap
definition: A generation gap highlights the divergence in beliefs and behaviors across different age cohorts, shaped by evolving social and technological landscapes. In the business realm, these differences present challenges and opportunities. Companies that manage to navigate these gaps gain a competitive edge by aligning products, marketing strategies, and workplace dynamics with the unique traits of each generation.

title: Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GSTT)
definition: The generation-skipping transfer tax is a federal tax on a gift orinheritancethat prevents the donor from avoiding estate taxes by skipping children in favor of grandchildren. With the generation-skipping transfer tax, grandchildren receive the same amount as if the inheritance were coming from their parents.

title: Generation-Skipping Trust
definition: A generation-skipping trust (GST) is a type of legally binding trust agreement in which the contributedassetsare passed down to the grantor's grandchildren. They "skip" the next generation, the grantor's children. The assets avoidestate taxesthat would apply if the children directly inherited them because they're only taxed once.

title: Generation X (Gen X)
definition: Generation X, or Gen X, refers to the generation of people born between the mid-1960s and the early 1980s.

title: Generic Brand
definition: A generic brand is a product that is manufactured and sold without a brand name, often providing a cost-effective alternative to well-known branded items.

title: Genesis Block
definition: The Genesis Block is the very first block in the Bitcoin blockchain, created in 2009 by Bitcoin's mysterious founder,Satoshi Nakamoto. Unlike later blocks, the 50 BTC reward from this block can never be spent, adding to its mystique. Embedded in its code is a message referencing the financial crisis, widely seen as a critique of the traditional banking system. The Genesis Block remains a foundational symbol in the Bitcoin community, marking the start of trusted, decentralized transactions and the birth of modern cryptocurrency.

title: Genetically Modified Food (GMF)
definition: Genetically modified foods (GMFs) are derived from organisms whose genetic material has been altered in a way that does not occur naturally through mating or natural recombination. These modifications typically aim to increase crop yields, enhance resistance to pests and diseases, and allow plants to tolerate harsh environmental conditions.

title: Gentlemen's Agreements
definition: A gentlemen's agreement is an informal, often unwritten agreement or transaction backed only by the integrity of the counterparty to actually abide by its terms. An agreement such as this is generally informal, made orally, and is not legally binding.

title: Gentrification
definition: Gentrification involves transforming neighborhoods from low to high value, often increasing property prices and living costs. While it brings improved infrastructure and services, it also leads to the displacement of long-time residents. As such, gentrification presents both opportunities and challenges for local communities, raising complex social and economic issues.

title: Genuine Progress Indicator (GPI)
definition: The Genuine Progress Indicator (GPI) offers a broader measure of economic health thanGDPby including environmental and social factors. It evaluates whether economic activities enhance the well-being of citizens, considering costs like resource depletion and pollution. This metric provides a more comprehensive view of an economy's real progress.

title: Geographical Diversification
definition: Geographical diversification is used in investing and business operations to distribute assets and activities across multiple countries or regions to decrease risk and potentially increase returns. This approach helps protect against the risks of focusing solely on a single country's economic trends, as different regions may experience growth or decline independently of one another. By diversifying geographically, you can mitigate risks such as currency fluctuations and political instability, while also benefiting from economic growth in different parts of the world.

title: Geographical Labor Mobility
definition: Geographical labor mobility refers to workers' ability to relocate in order to find employment in their field of work.

title: Geographical Pricing
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Geolocation
definition: Geolocation is the ability to track a device’s whereabouts using GPS, cell phone towers, WiFi access points, or a combination of these. Since devices are used by individuals, geolocation uses positioning systems to track an individual’s location down to latitude and longitude coordinates, or more practically, a physical address.

title: Geometric Mean
definition: The geometric mean is the average of a set of products, the calculation of which is commonly used to determine the performance results of an investment portfolio.

title: George Soros
definition: George Soros is a legendaryhedge fund manager. He is widely considered one of the most successful investors of all time, and he is also one of the world's world's foremost philanthropists. Soros managed the Quantum Fund, a fund that achieved an average annual return of 30% from 1970 to 2000. He remains the chair of Soros Fund Management LLC.

title: Ghetto
definition: A tenement is a multi-occupancy residential rental building, often associated with crowded, low-quality housing. It emerged during the 19th-century industrialization and urbanization, especially in low-income and immigrant neighborhoods, and reforms like the Tenement Acts, particularly the 1901 act, pushed standards for safer, healthier living conditions.

title: G.I. Bill
definition: The GI Bill is aU.S. Department of Veterans Affairseducation benefit program for active-duty service members, veterans, plus their families. It began as the Serviceman's Readjustment Act of 1944, enacted by Congress and signed into law by former President Franklin D. Roosevelt for World War II veterans. Today, it covers grants and stipends for college, vocational, or technical programs, plus on-the-job training.

title: Giffen Good
definition: Giffen goods challenge the usual economic laws, as these non-luxury essentials see demand increase with rising prices. Named after Sir Robert Giffen, lower-income necessities such as bread and rice exhibit an unusual upward-sloping demand curve. Understand the dynamics, examples, and history behind this fascinating economic concept.

title: Gift Card
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Gift Causa Mortis
definition: Gift causa mortis is a gift of personalpropertymade with the expectation that the person giving the gift will soon die. Causa mortis gifts differ from an inheritance given or designated in wills.

title: Gift Letter
definition: A gift letter is a piece of legal, written correspondence explicitly stating that money received from a friend or relative is a gift. Gift letters for tax purposes often come into play when a borrower has received assistance in making adown paymenton a new home or other real estate property. Such letters state that the money received is not expected to be paid back in any way, shape, or form.

title: Gift in Trust
definition: A gift in trust is an indirect way to give assets to a beneficiary.

title: Gift Inter Vivos
definition: A gift inter vivos, a gift between the living in Latin, is the legal term that refers to a transfer or gift made during the grantor's life. Inter vivos gifts, which include property related to an estate, are not subject toprobatetaxes since they are not part of the donor's estate at death. An inter vivos transfer is one made during the grantor's lifetime.

title: Gift of Equity
definition: A gift of equity enables a homeowner to assist family by selling a property below market value, offering financial relief and potentially eliminating a down payment. This strategy, often between parents and children, simplifies homeownership while impacting future taxes and mortgage requirements.

title: Gift Splitting
definition: Gift splitting is anestate planningtool that lets married couples combine their annualgift tax exclusions, allowing them to give up to double the standard tax-free amount. To use gift splitting, couples usually have to file a joint tax return and may need to submit Form 709.

title: Gift Tax
definition: The gift tax is a federal tax imposed by theInternal Revenue Service (IRS)on taxpayers who transfer money or property to someone else without receiving anything of substantial value. Agiftcan include cash,real estate, and other forms of property.

title: Gift Tax Return
definition: A gift tax return is afederal tax returnthat must be filed under certain conditions by the giver of a gift. (It isnota tax on returning gifts.) The return is known asForm 709.﻿﻿ ﻿

title: Gig Economy
definition: The gig economy, also known as the sharing or access economy, features temporary, freelance, and part-time positions often filled by independent contractors. While offering flexibility and independence to workers, it removes job security and benefits like health insurance and paid leave. Companies save costs by hiring freelancers without these benefits.

title: Gilt-Edged Bond
definition: Gilt-edged securities are high-grade bonds issued by certain national governments and private organizations. In the past, these instruments referred to the certificates issued by the Bank of England (BOE) on behalf of the Majesty's Treasury, so named because the paper they were printed on customarily featured gilded (golden) edges.

title: Gilt-Edged Securities
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Gilts
definition: Gilts are government bonds issued in the U.K., India, and Commonwealth countries and are similar toU.S. Treasurysecurities. The term gilts is derived from the certificates with gilded edges that were historically issued by the British government and is still used as an indication of the integrity of the investment.

title: Gini Index
definition: Developed by Italian statistician Corrado Gini in 1912, the Gini index is a critical tool for measuring income inequality within a nation by analyzing the distribution of income among its population.

title: Gibraltar Pound (GIP): What It is, Gibraltar Economy
definition: The Gibraltar pound (abbreviated as GIP) is the official currency for the country of Gibraltar. The Gibraltar pound is pegged atpar valueto the British pound sterling, at a fixed exchange rate.

title: Give Up
definition: A give-up trade involves multiple brokers: the executing broker conducting the transaction, the client’s broker who oversees the trade, and the broker on the opposite side. This agreement was more common before electronic trading became prevalent, as brokers relied on others to act on their behalf under challenging circumstances.

title: The Glass Ceiling: Definition, History, Effects, and Examples
definition: The term “glass ceiling” refers to a metaphorical invisible barrier that prevents certain individuals from being promoted to managerial- and executive-level positions within an organization orindustry.

title: Understanding the Glass Cliff Concept and Its Impact on Women Leaders
definition: The term "glass cliff" refers to a situation in which women are promoted to higher positions during times of crisis or duress, or during arecessionwhen the chance of failure is more likely. Put simply, women in these situations are set up for failure. The term was coined by researchers at the University of Exeter, United Kingdom who published research on the 100 companies included in theFinancial Times Stock Exchange(FTSE) 100 Index. These researchers found that promoting women to higher positions often comes with negative implications, and that being set up for failure is the equivalent of standing on the edge of a cliff. If they fail, they fall off.

title: Glass-Steagall Act
definition: The Glass-Steagall Act of 1933 forced commercial banks to refrain from investment banking activities to protect depositors from potential losses through stock speculation. Glass-Steagall aimed to prevent a repeat of the1929 stock market crashand the wave of commercial bank failures.

title: Glide Path
definition: A glide path is the plan that a target date fund follows to automatically adjust its mix of stocks and bonds as it gets closer to its target date. This helps investors by making their portfolio more conservative over time to reduce risk. There are different types of glide paths, such as declining, static, and rising. Each of these types of glide paths offers investors a different strategy for how investments are managed as goals approach.

title: Global Bond
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Global Depositary Receipt (GDR)
definition: A global depositary receipt (GDR) is a financial instrument issued by a depositary bank representing shares in a foreign company. GDRs enable international companies to access investors worldwide by listing on local stock exchanges outside their home markets. Through GDRs, companies can raise capital, and investors can gain exposure to foreign stocks with ease.

title: Global Financial Stability Report (GFSR)
definition: TheInternational Monetary Fund(IMF) is a significant authority in global finance due to its role in producing the Global Financial Stability Report (GFSR). The GFSR tracks changes in financial markets and is released twice a year in April and October.

title: What Is a Global Fund? Definition and Investment Strategy
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS)
definition: The Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) is a method for assigning companies to a specific economic sector and industry group that best defines their business operations.

title: Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS): Definition and Uses
definition: Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS) serve as a framework for investment managers to present their performance results in a consistent and transparent manner.

title: Global Macro
definition: A global macro strategy is a hedge fund or mutual fund strategy that bases its holdings primarily on based on macroeconomic and political forecasts. Holdings may include long and short positions in various equity, fixed income, currency, commodities, and futures markets. Factors that a global macro strategy takes into consideration are interest rates, politics, and trade policies. These strategies require active management and an understanding of the breadth of opportunities in global macro funds.

title: Global Macro Hedge Fund
definition: Global macro hedge funds areactively managedinvestment vehicles that aim to profit from large market moves driven by political or economic events. Managers take long or short positions across assets such as currencies, futures, bonds, commodities, and index products, positioning portfolios to benefit if their forecasts play out.

title: Global Recession
definition: A global recession is an extended period of economic decline around the world. It involves synchronizedrecessionsacross many national economies as trade relations and internationalfinancial systemstransmit economic shocks from one country to another. The IMF declared a global recession in 2020, known as the Great Lockdown, which resulted from the widespread quarantines and social distancing brought on by the COVID-19 outbreak.

title: Global Registered Share (GRS)
definition: An asset class breakdown provides the percentages of core asset classes found within a mutual fund, exchange-traded fund, or another portfolio. Asset classes (in microeconomics and beyond) generally refer to broad categories such as equities,fixed income, and commodities. Often, sub-asset classes may be reported in concentrated portfolios or for more granular detail.

title: Globalization
definition: Globalization is the increasing interconnection of national economies, enabling the free movement of products, investment, technology, and jobs across borders and cultures. Economically, it signifies the interdependence of nations facilitated byfree tradeagreements, which play a crucial role in fostering international commerce. This phenomenon allows corporations to achieve competitive advantages by reducing operating costs, accessing new consumer markets, and benefiting from the removal of trade barriers such as tariffs.

title: Globex
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Glocalization
definition: Glocalization merges globalization and localization, allowing products and services to be tailored to local markets while maintaining a global presence. This strategy adapts global offerings to meet local regulations, cultural preferences, and consumer needs, making them more appealing to diverse audiences across different regions.

title: GmbH
definition: GmbH is an abbreviation of the German phrase “Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung,” which means “company with limited liability.” It’s a suffix used after a private limited company’s name in Germany (vs. AG, for aktiengesellschaft, which is used to indicate a public limited company).GmbH is the equivalent of LLC (limited liability company), used in the United States, or Ltd. (limited), used in the United Kingdom, and is the most common form ofincorporationin Germany.

title: Go-Go Fund
definition: Go-go fund is a slang name for amutual fundthat has an investment strategy focused on high-risk securities in an attempt to capture above-average returns. A go-go fund's aggressive approach usually involves holding large positions ingrowth stocks. Growth stocks offer higher risks, but also higher potential returns.

title: Go-Shop Period
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Goal-Based Investing
definition: Goal-based investing is a relatively new approach towealth managementthat emphasizes investing with the objective of attaining specific life goals. Goal-based investing (GBI) involves a wealth manager or investment firm’s clients measuring their progress towards specific life goals, such as saving for children’s education or building a retirement nest-egg, rather than focusing on generating the highest possibleportfolio returnor beating the market.

title: Goal Seeking
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Godfather Offer
definition: A Godfather offer is an irrefutabletakeover bidmade to a target company by an acquirer. Typically, the offer is priced at an extremely generouspremiumcompared with the target's prevailing share price, making it difficult for the board of directors to reject the bid without angering shareholders and being accused of breaching their fiduciary duty.

title: Going Concern
definition: A going concern is a business that is financially stable and is expected to continue operating indefinitely.

title: Going-Concern Value
definition: Going concern value reflects the intrinsic worth of a business, considering its potential to operate indefinitely while generating sustainable profit.

title: Going Private
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Going Public
definition: Going public is the process of selling shares that were formerly held privately and are now available to new investors for the first time, otherwise known as aninitial public offering (IPO).

title: Gold Bug
definition: The term “gold bug” is an expression used to refer to investors who arebullishongold. Gold bugs promote investment in gold and commonly believe that the purchasing power of fiat currencies will decline due to inflation,expansionary monetary policy, and the rising national debt.

title: Gold Certificate
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Gold Option
definition: A gold option is a financial contract that lets investors buy or sell gold at a set price by a certain date. Under the contract, the buyer has the right (but not the obligation) to purchase gold with acall optionand the right to sell it with aput option. Gold options are mainly traded on the CME COMEX and can involve physical gold or gold futures. Unlike futures contracts, which require buying or selling at expiration, options are more flexible. Trading gold options can offer benefits like hedging and profit opportunities, but they also carry risks that investors should understand.

title: Gold Standard
definition: The gold standard is a monetary system where governments fix their currency value directly to gold. Once universal, this system allowed paper money to be converted into fixed amounts of gold, maintaining currency value based on gold parity. Despite its historical significance, the gold standard lost its dominance in the 20th century, paving the way for today's fiat currency systems.

title: Golden Cross
definition: A golden cross is a pattern that occurs on a chart when a short-term moving average crosses over a longer-term moving average to the upside.

title: Golden Handcuffs
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Golden Handshake
definition: A golden handshake is a pre-negotiated severance package that provides substantial payments to executives, often in cash or stock options. These agreements can be controversial because they can reward executives regardless of their performance. High-profile examples, such as the large payout to F. Ross Johnson in 1989, show how high these packages can be and highlight their impact on executive compensation.

title: Golden Parachute
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Golden Rule
definition: The golden rule of government spending is afiscal policythat a government should borrow only to invest, not to fund current spending. In other words, the government should borrow money only to make investments that will produce long-term benefits for the future. Current spending, or spending on short-term needs, should be funded by tax revenues.

title: Golden Share
definition: A golden share is a type of share that gives its shareholder veto power over changes to the company's charter. It holds special voting rights, giving its holder the ability to block another shareholder from taking more than a ratio of ordinary shares.

title: Goldilocks Economy
definition: A Goldilocks economy refers to a state where economic conditions are balanced to provide ideal conditions for sustainable economic growth without resulting in significant inflationary pressures or leading to an economic slowdown.

title: Good Credit
definition: Good credit is a classification for an individual'scredit history, indicating the borrower has a relatively highcredit scoreand is a safe credit risk. Credit scores are provided through credit reporting agencies. Lenders check credit scores for the purpose of providing credit underwriting decisions and background check details.

title: Good Delivery
definition: Good delivery refers to the unhindered transfer of ownership of asecurityfrom a seller to a buyer, with all necessary requirements having been met. This used to be a rather complex process, although nowadays, thanks to electronic exchanges that facilitate the digital transfer andclearingof many securities, good delivery has become automated and generally much more straightforward.

title: Good Faith Estimate (GFE)
definition: A good faith estimate (GFE) is a document that outlines the estimated costs and terms of areverse mortgageloan offer, enabling borrowers to comparison shop among different lenders and choose the deal that best fits their needs.

title: Good Faith Money
definition: Good faith money is a deposit a buyer makes to signal serious intent to complete a transaction, similar to earnest money or a security deposit. It is commonly used in situations like home purchases and is often applied to the final sale price. Because the funds can be forfeited if the deal falls through, it encourages buyers to follow through with the agreement.

title: Good This Week (GTW)
definition: Good this week (GTW) is a type of order that remains active until the end of the week in which it is issued. If the order is not executed prior to the end of the week, it will be automatically canceled.

title: Good 'Til Canceled (GTC)
definition: Good 'til canceled (GTC) orders allow investors to set buy or sell actions that remain pending until they either execute at specified price points or the investor cancels them. Unlike day orders, GTCordersdo not automatically expire at the end of a trading day but instead provide flexibility for managing trades over extended periods. This overview covers the functionality, benefits, and risks of GTC orders, along with practical examples to help you leverage them in your investment strategy.

title: Goodness-of-Fit
definition: Goodness-of-fit evaluates statistical models for their ability to accurately forecast outcomes and prevent misleading deductions.

title: Goods and Services Tax (GST)
definition: A goods and services tax (GST) is a value-added tax levied on goods and services that is included in the price of a product.

title: Goodwill
definition: When a company purchases another, it often pays more than the net fair value of the target's assets and liabilities. This excess is recorded as goodwill, anintangible assetreflecting brand strength, customer loyalty, and proprietary technology, among other factors. It signifies a competitive edge and justifies premiums paid during acquisitions.

title: Goodwill Impairment
definition: Goodwill impairment is an accounting charge that companies record when goodwill’scarrying valueon financial statements exceeds its fair value. In accounting,goodwillis recorded after a company acquiresassetsandliabilities, and pays a price in excess of their identifiable net value.

title: Google Tax
definition: A Google tax, also known as a diverted profits tax, refers to anti-tax-avoidance provisions that have been introduced in some countries. Several jurisdictions implemented the provision to stop companies from divertingprofitsorroyaltiesto other jurisdictions that have lower or even zero tax rates. For example, internet giant Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOGL) Google paid a negligible amount in taxes in the United Kingdom by completing its transactions in the low-tax city of Dublin, Ireland, even though it earned $6.5 billion in revenue in the U.K.

title: Gordon Gekko
definition: Gordon Gekko is a fictional character who appears as the villain in the popular 1987 Oliver Stone movie "Wall Street" and its 2010 sequel "Wall Street: Money Never Sleeps." The character, a ruthless and wildly wealthy investor andcorporate raider, has become a cultural symbol for greed, as epitomized by the famous "Wall Street" quote "Greed is good."

title: Gordon Growth Model
definition: The Gordon Growth Model (GGM), a crucial tool in financial valuation, helps in determining a stock's intrinsic value based on expected future dividends growing at a constant rate. As a straightforward variant of thedividend discount model (DDM), it is particularly valuable for analyzing companies with stable dividend growth.

title: Governance, Risk Management, and Compliance (GRC)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Government Accountability Office (GAO)
definition: The Government Accountability Office is an independent, non-partisan agency that monitors government spending and operations. It provides Congress and federal agencies with objective, reliable information and recommendations to help the nation work and spend more efficiently and save taxpayer dollars. The GAO is also responsible for establishing standards to streamline government audits and reviews.

title: Government Accounting Standards Board (GASB)
definition: The Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB) is a privatenon-governmental organizationthat creates accounting reporting standards orgenerally accepted accounting principles (GAAP)for state and local governments in the United States.

title: Government Bond
definition: A government bond is a debt instrument issued by federal, state, and local governments to raise capital to support public spending.

title: Government Grant
definition: A government grant is a financial award given by a federal, state, or local government authority for a beneficial project. It is effectively atransfer payment. A grant does not include technical assistance or other financial assistance, such as a loan or loan guarantee, an interest ratesubsidy, directappropriation, or revenue sharing. The grantee is not expected to repay the money but is expected to use the funds from the grant for their stated purpose, which typically serves some larger good.

title: Government National Mortgage Association (Ginnie Mae)
definition: The Government National Mortgage Association (Ginnie Mae) is a federal government corporation that guarantees the timely payment of p

title: Government of Singapore Investment Corporation (GIC)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Government Pension Fund of Norway
definition: The Government Pension Fund of Norway (GPFN) consists of two investment funds managed by Norway's Ministry of Finance: the Government Pension Fund Global (GPFG) and the Government Pension Fund of Norway (GPFN). These funds are designed to save Norway's petroleum revenues to ensure the long-term financial security of the country's public pension system. GPFG, established in 1990 and commonly known as the Oil Fund, is the largest sovereign wealth fund globally. It invests in international real estate, equities, and bonds. The GPFN, on the other hand, focuses on domestic investments within Norway and Scandinavia and holds significant shares in local companies through the Oslo Stock Exchange.

title: Government Purchase
definition: Government purchases are expenditures on goods and services by federal, state, and local governments. The combined total of this spending, excludingtransfer paymentsand interest on the debt, is a key factor in determining a nation's gross domestic product (GDP). Transfer payments are expenditures that do not involve purchases, such asSocial Security payments, welfare, and government subsidies for certain businesses.

title: Government Securities Clearing Corporation (GSCC)
definition: The Government Securities Clearing Corporation (GSCC) was an organization that was responsible for clearing and netting trades for government and agency debt securities. It processed about $1.6 trillion in daily trades until 2002 and handled new issues and the sale of existing government securities.

title: Government Security
definition: A government security is an investment issued by a government that funds its operations and projects.

title: Government Shutdown
definition: A government shutdown occurs when nonessential U.S. government offices can no longer remain open due to a lack of funding. The lack of funding usually occurs when the approval of the federal budget that will finance the government for the upcoming fiscal year is delayed. The shutdown remains in effect until funding legislation is passed.

title: Government-Sponsored Enterprise
definition: A government-sponsored enterprise (GSE) is a quasi-governmental entity established to enhance the flow of credit to specific sectors of the U.S.economy. Created by acts of Congress, these agencies—although they are privately held—provide public financial services. The primary purpose of GSEs is to enhance credit flow across various sectors like housing and agriculture.

title: Government-Sponsored Retirement Arrangement (GSRA)
definition: A Government-Sponsored Retirement Arrangement (GSRA) is a Canadian retirement plan for individuals who are not employees of a local, provincial, or federal government body, but who are paid for their services from public funds. Because this type of retirement plan is not registered with theCanada Revenue Agency(CRA)—the Canadian equivalent of the American IRS—it does not qualify for tax-deferred status or tax deductions.

title: Grace Period
definition: A grace period is a designated timespan following a payment due date, allowing borrowers to make payments without incurring penalties. Commonly featured in mortgage and insurance contracts, grace periods typically last about 15 days, enabling consumers to avoid negative impacts. These periods serve as a buffer against late fees and potential credit report marks, though specifics vary by contract.

title: Graded Vesting
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Graduate Management Admission Test (GMAT)
definition: The graduate management admission test (GMAT) is a standardized test used to measure a test taker's aptitude in mathematics, verbal skills, and analytical writing. The GMAT is most commonly used as the primary exam reviewed by business schools to gain entrance into an MBA program. The exam is generally offered by computer only; in areas of the world where computer networks are limited, the exam may be given as a paper-based test.

title: Graduated Lease
definition: A graduated lease is an agreement under which a tenant andlandlordagree to a periodic adjustment of monthly payments. For example, the agreement may reflect an increase in the tenant’s payments due to market conditions or an increase in the value of the leased property.

title: Graduated Payment Mortgage (GPM)
definition: A graduated payment mortgage (GPM) is a type offixed-rate mortgagefor which the payments increase gradually from an initially low base level to a higher final level. Typically, the payments will grow between 7% to 12% annually from their initial base payment amount until the full monthly payment amount is reached.

title: Graham Number
definition: The Graham number is a tool that can reveal stocks considered to be undervalued.

title: Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act of 1999 (GLBA)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Grandfather Clause
definition: A grandfather clause, also called a “legacy clause,” is an exemption that allows persons or entities to continue with activities or operations that were approved before the implementation of new rules, regulations, or laws. Such allowances can be permanent, temporary, or instituted with limits.

title: Grant
definition: A small-business grant is an award, usually financial, given by one entity (typically a company, foundation, or government) to a company to facilitate a goal or incentivize performance.Grants are essentially giftsthat usually do not have to be paid back.

title: Grant Deed
definition: A grant deed, also known as a special or limited warranty deed, is a legal document used to transfer real estate between a previous owner (thegrantor) and a new owner (thegrantee). A grant deed warrants that:

title: Grant-in-Aid
definition: A federal grant is financial aid awarded to fund a specific project or program. Recipients include state, local, and municipal governments as well as organizations.Federal grants-in-aid are funded with money fromincome taxrevenues. These grants aren't loans; therefore, no repayment is required, but funds must be spent according to the federal government's guidelines for that particular grant.

title: Grantee
definition: A grantee is the recipient of agrant, asset, or property in real estate transactions. The grantor transfers ownership rights to the grantee, establishing their respective roles in the transaction. Legal documents, such as deeds, clearly define these parties to outline their rights and obligations. Depending on the deed type (warranty, quitclaim, or specialty warranty), the grantee's level of protection and ownership assurance can vary.

title: Grantor
definition: A grantor is an individual or other entity that creates atrust. They place their assets into the trust. A grantor may also function as thetrusteeand may be referred to as the settlor, trustmaker, ortrustor.

title: Grantor Trust Rules
definition: Income generated from trusts graduates to a higher tax bracket faster than individual marginal income tax rates.

title: Granular Portfolio
definition: A granular portfolio is an investmentportfoliothat is well diversified across a wide variety of assets, typically with a significant number of holdings. Because this type of portfolio contains a large number of positions in different asset classes and/or sectors, it is considered to have a lower overall risk profile. Conversely, portfolios that have "low granularity" have fewer positions or contain highly correlated assets. They are lessdiversifiedand have a higher overall risk profile.

title: Understanding Graphics Processing Units (GPU): A Comprehensive Guide

title: Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT)
definition: A grantor retained annuity trust (GRAT) is anestate planningtool used to minimize taxes on large financial gifts made to family members. It can avoid using much (if any) of the lifetime gift and estate tax exclusion provided by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS).

title: Gravestone Doji
definition: A gravestone doji is a bearish reversal candlestick pattern that signals potential market downturns. This pattern forms when the open, low, and closing prices align closely, with a long upper shadow indicating a strong shift from bullish to bearish momentum. Traders use it, along with other indicators, to confirm trends before making investment decisions.

title: Gray Box
definition: Gray box refers to the testing of software where there is some limited knowledge of its internal workings. Gray box testing is an ethical hacking technique where the hacker has to use limited information to identify the strengths and weaknesses of a target's security network.

title: Gray List
definition: A gray list is a list of stocks that are ineligible for trade by an investment bank'srisk arbitragedivision. Securities on the gray list aren’t necessarily exceptionally risky or otherwise inherently flawed, but are nonetheless restricted. In such cases, the gray list can include those firms working with the investment bank, often in matters of mergers and acquisitions. Once the firms in question have completed this business, the stocks may be taken off the gray list, allowing the bank to trade them once again.

title: Gray Market
definition: A gray market is an unofficial platform where financialsecuritiesare bought and sold before official trading begins, or when they are suspended from trades. Although gray markets operate outside typical retail channels, they remain legal and offer insights into market demand.

title: Graduate Record Examination (GRE)
definition: The graduate record examination (GRE) is a standardized exam used to measure one's aptitude for abstract thinking in the areas of analytical writing, mathematics, and vocabulary. The GRE is commonly used by manygraduate schoolsin the U.S. and Canada to determine an applicant's eligibility for the program.

title: Great Depression
definition: The Great Depression was the deepest and longest economic contraction in U.S. history.

title: Great Leap Forward
definition: The Great Leap Forward was a five-year plan launched by Mao Zedong in 1958 to transform China from an agrarian to an industrial economy. The initiative forced agricultural collectivization, aiming to boost steel and grain production. However, it resulted in economic disruption and the deaths of millions due to starvation and forced labor, making it the largest non-wartime campaign of mass killing in history.

title: Great Moderation

title: Great Recession
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Great Society
definition: The Great Society was the name given to the domestic policy initiatives designed during President Lyndon B. Johnson's administration in the 1960s. Launched from 1965 to 1965, these initiatives, including Medicare, Medicaid, the Older Americans Act, and the Elementary and Secondary Education Act (ESEA) of 1965, still exist.

title: Greater Fool Theory
definition: The 'Greater Fool Theory' in investing suggests that you can profit from buying overpriced securities by later selling them to someone else, a so-called 'greater fool,' at an even higher price. This investing concept dismisses traditional evaluation metrics like valuations or earnings reports. It implies that as long as someone more optimistic or less informed comes along, your investment can yield profits, though it carries significant risk if the market corrects. For investors, understanding this theory is crucial to avoid potential pitfalls and to know when due diligence is essential.

title: Greeks
definition: The variables that are used to assess risk in the options market are commonly referred to as “the Greeks.” Each risk factor is assigned a different Greek letter, such as delta, theta, and others, toassess options riskand manage options portfolios

title: Green Bond
definition: A green bond is a fixed-income debt instrument earmarked to raise money for climate and environmental projects. It’s asset-linked and backed by the issuing entity’s balance sheet, so it carries the same credit rating as its issuers’ other debt obligations.​

title: Green Card
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Green Chip Stocks
definition: Green chipstocksaresharesof environmentally-friendly companies. Green chip stocks are likely to be concentrated in areas such as alternative energy, pollution control, carbon abatement, and recycling.

title: Green-Field Investment
definition: A greenfield investment is when a company builds a brand-new venture in another country without relying on any existing structures.

title: Green Fund
definition: A green fund is amutual fundor another investment vehicle that will only invest in companies that are deemed socially conscious or directly promote environmental responsibility. A green fund can come in the form of a focusedinvestment vehiclefor companies engaged in environmentally supportive businesses, such as alternative energy, green transport, water and waste management, and sustainable living.

title: Green Investing
definition: Green investing refers to investment activities that support businesses with positive environmental impacts. These investments aim to preserve natural resources, reduce pollution, and promote sustainability. Although it's often linked to socially responsible investing (SRI) or environmental, social, and governance (ESG) strategies, green investing focuses directly on environmental benefits. Understanding how it differs from related approaches and why it matters today can help investors make more informed, environmentally focused decisions.

title: Green Marketing
definition: Green marketing promotes products for their environmental benefits. whether real or perceived. It has a growing importance in an eco-conscious consumer market. Examples of effective green marketing strategies include energy reduction through the manufacturing process or as a result of packaging changes. Companies can also deploy philanthropic initiatives that position them as environmentally concerned. Criticisms of green marketing include greenwashing, which refers to false claims of enviro-friendly efforts, and the economic burdens associated with the practice.

title: Green Monday
definition: Green Monday is one of the retail industry's busiest shopping days. It represents the day many shoppers rush to purchase last-minute holiday gifts and take advantage of deals. Green Monday occurs on the second Monday of every December. The term Green Monday was reportedly created byeBay(EBAY) as a way to represent the rush of last-minute online shoppers in December.

title: Green Tech
definition: Green tech, or green technology, generally refers to environmentally friendly technologies designed to reduce negative impacts on the planet through sustainable practices and innovation.

title: Greenback
definition: "Greenback" is a slang term for U.S. dollars. The first greenbacks were printed to finance the civil war and were named as such because their backs were printed in green. Greenbacks were not fully backed by gold, so they lost value and led to inflation in the northern economy. The first greenbacks were called demand notes and were used to pay for salaries and government expenses in 1861-1862. These were later replaced by the Legal Tender Act of 1862, which authorized the paper notes that would eventually become the official currency.

title: Greenmail
definition: Greenmail is the practice of buying enough shares in a company to threaten a hostile takeover so the target company will instead repurchase its shares at a premium. Regarding mergers and acquisitions, the company makes a greenmail payment as a defensive measure to stop the takeover bid. The target company must repurchase the stock at a substantial premium to thwart the takeover, which results in a considerable profit for the greenmailer. Greenmail has declined due to regulatory changes in the 1980s.

title: Greensheet
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Greenshoe Option
definition: A greenshoe option is a provision in aninitial public offering(IPO)underwriting agreement, allowing underwriters to sell more shares than initially planned if demand exceeds expectations. This over-allotment option is typically used to ensure price stability, liquidity, and to support stock prices in the market.

title: Greenspan Put
definition: Greenspan put was the moniker given to policies implemented by Alan Greenspan during his tenure as Federal Reserve (Fed) Chair. The Greenspan-led Fed was extremely proactive in halting excessive stock market declines, acting as a form of insurance against losses.

title: Greenwashing
definition: Greenwashing is a prevalent and often deceptive practice in today's marketing landscape, where companies portray a misleading image of environmental responsibility to consumers. As investors and consumers increasingly favorsustainableproducts, it becomes crucial to discern genuine eco-friendly initiatives from false claims. This article delves into the mechanics of greenwashing, highlighting examples and exploring how entities like the Federal Trade Commission work to protect consumer interests. Understanding greenwashing is vital for making informed financial decisions and supporting truly sustainable practices.

title: Gresham's Law
definition: Gresham's law is a principle that states that "bad money drives out good" and can be applied to thecurrency markets. The law originated from using precious metals for coins, which affected their value. Since moving away from metal currency, it explains currency stability and movement in global markets.

title: Grexit
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Grey Market
definition: A gray market is an unofficial platform where financialsecuritiesare bought and sold before official trading begins, or when they are suspended from trades. Although gray markets operate outside typical retail channels, they remain legal and offer insights into market demand.

title: Grid Trading
definition: Grid trading is when orders are placed above and below a set price, creating a grid of orders at incrementally increasing and decreasing prices. Grid trading is most commonly associated with theforeign exchangemarket. Overall the technique seeks to capitalize on normal price volatility in an asset by placing buy and sell orders at certain regular intervals above and below a predefined base price.

title: Grinder
definition: A grinder is a slang term for a person who works in the investment industry and makes only small amounts of money at a time on small investments, over and over again. Grinders typically are hard working and highly respected investors who value every cent they make off their investments. Grinders who areinvestment advisorstend to keep in regular contact with their clients.

title: Gross Debt Service Ratio (GDS)
definition: The gross debt service (GDS) ratio is a financial metric that helps lenders determine the portion of a borrower's income allocated to housing debt, influencing mortgage qualification. Understanding the GDS ratio is crucial for borrowers seeking mortgage approval. This ratio aids in evaluating if a borrower's housing costs are within a manageable range.

title: Gross Dividends
definition: Similar in concept to gross income, gross dividends are the sum total of all dividends received by an investor for tax purposes. Gross dividends include allordinary dividendsthat are paid, plus capital-gains distributions andnontaxable distributionsreceived by the taxpayer during the year before taxes, fees, and expenses are deducted.

title: Gross Domestic Income (GDI)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Gross Domestic Product (GDP)
definition: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) can be measured in three different ways to find the total value of everything a country produces in goods and services over a set period.

title: Gross Earnings
definition: Gross earnings are the total income for individuals and businesses before accounting for deductions. For example, they haven't been adjusted for the costs of taxes, debt charges, or indirect costs. For companies, gross earnings are calculated as total revenue minus thecost of goods sold(COGS). For individuals and households, gross earnings are the income earned before the deduction of taxes or adjustments.

title: Gross Estate
definition: A person's gross estate is the full monetary worth of all their assets and property at the time of their passing.

title: Gross Expense Ratio (GER)
definition: The gross expense ratio represents the proportion of total fund assets devoted to covering operational costs, excluding any expense waivers or reimbursements.

title: Gross Exposure
definition: Gross exposure refers to the absolute level of a fund's investments. It takes into account the value of both a fund’slong positionsandshort positionsand can be expressed either in dollar or percentage terms. Gross exposure shows how much is at risk in the markets, helping investors see their potential risk. Higher gross exposure means bigger potential loss or gain.

title: Gross Income
definition: An individual's gross income is their total earnings before taxes or other deductions are taken out. It includesincomefrom all sources, not just from employment. It's not limited to income that's received in cash. It also includes property or services received. It's typically referred to as gross pay when it appears on a paycheck.

title: Gross Income Multiplier
definition: A gross income multiplier (GIM) is a rough measure of the value of an investment property. It is calculated by dividing the property's sale price by its gross annual rental income. Investors can use the GIM—along with other methods like thecapitalization rate(cap rate) anddiscounted cash flowmethod—to valuecommercial real estateproperties like shopping centers and apartment complexes.

title: Gross Income Test
definition: The gross income test determines if a qualifying relative's income is below the IRS limit for dependent eligibility under U.S. tax rules.

title: Gross Interest
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Gross Lease
definition: A gross lease is an agreement that requires the tenant to pay the property owner a flat rental fee in exchange for the exclusive use of the property. It's typically used for commercial properties. The fee includes all of the costs associated with property ownership, including taxes, insurance, and utilities. Grossleasescan be modified to meet the needs of the tenants and are commonly used in thecommercial propertyrental market.

title: Gross Leverage Ratio
definition: The gross leverage ratio is the sum of an insurance company’snet premiums writtenratio, net liability ratio, andceded reinsurance ratio. The gross leverage ratio is used to determine how exposed an insurer is to pricing and estimation errors, as well as its exposure to reinsurance companies.

title: Gross Margin
definition: Gross margin is the percentage of money a company keeps from its sales after covering the direct costs of producing its goods or services. It shows how efficiently a business turns revenue into profit before accounting for overhead and other expenses.

title: Gross Margin Return on Investment (GMROI)
definition: Thegross marginreturn on investment (GMROI) is aninventoryprofitability evaluation ratio that analyzes a firm's ability to turn inventory into cash above the cost of the inventory. It is calculated by dividing the gross margin by theaverage inventory cost.

title: Gross Merchandise Value
definition: Gross Merchandise Value (GMV) represents the total monetary value of goods sold over a specified period viacustomer-to-customer (C2C)or e-commerce platforms. Primarily used to evaluate the growth and health of these businesses, GMV provides a snapshot of a company's sales dynamics and operational performance. In this article, we'll explore GMV's calculation and application, alongside its benefits and limitations.

title: Gross National Happiness (GNH)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Gross National Income (GNI)
definition: Gross National Income (GNI) reflects the total earnings of a nation's residents and businesses, encompassing both domestic and foreign income. This measure, which includes Gross Domestic Product (GDP) alongside income from international sources, offers a comprehensive view of a nation's wealth.

title: Gross National Product (GNP)
definition: Gross national product (GNP) is the value of products and services produced by the citizens of a country both domestically and internationally but not including income earned by foreign residents.

title: Gross National Product (GNP) Deflator
definition: Thegross national product(GNP) deflator provides a vital economic metric that adjusts for inflation to reflect the real value of a country's total economic output. Unlike thegross domestic product (GDP) deflator, the GNP deflator takes into account income from foreign investments, providing a more comprehensive overview of a nation's economic performance. For investors and economists, understanding the GNP deflator is crucial, as it offers insights into inflation's impact on national income and economic conditions.

title: Gross Net Written Premium Income
definition: Gross net written premium income (GNWPI) is the dollar amount of an insurance company’spremiumsthat are used to determine what portion of premiums is owed to a reinsurer. Gross net written premium income is the base to which the reinsurance premium rate is applied, taking into account cancellations, refunds, and premiums paid forreinsurance coverage.

title: Gross Processing Margin (GPM)
definition: The gross processing margin (GPM) is the difference between the cost of a raw commodity and the income it generates once sold as a finished product. The gross processing margin is affected by supply and demand. The prices for raw commodities fluctuate, creating an ever-changingspreadbetween the raw inputs and the processed products.

title: Gross Profit
definition: Gross profit is a company's remaining profit after deducting the costs associated with producing and selling its products or services. It's also known as sales profit or gross income.

title: Gross Profit Margin
definition: Gross profit margin measures a company's profit after subtracting its costs of doing business.

title: Gross Rate of Return
definition: The gross rate of return is the total return on an investment before fees andexpenses. It's measured over a specific timeframe, like a month, quarter, or year. This can be contrasted with the net rate of return, which deducts fees and costs to provide a more realistic measurement of return.

title: Gross Receipts
definition: Gross receipts encompass the total amount of all receipts a business earns in cash or property, which can include sales, tax refunds, and other income—excluding expenses and adjustments. These values are pivotal for corporate taxation in select states, affecting financial decisions and tax obligations. Examples from Texas and Ohio illustrate how different jurisdictions define and apply gross receipts for taxation purposes.

title: Gross Sales
definition: Gross sales measures the total sales of a company, unadjusted for the costs related to generating those sales.

title: Gross Spread
definition: The gross spread is the compensation that the underwriters of aninitial public offering (IPO)receive. An IPO is the process of taking aprivate corporationpublic by issuing shares of stock. Gross spread is the difference between theunderwritingprice received by the issuing company and the actual price offered to the investing public. In other words, the gross spread is the financial institution's cut or profit from the IPO listing. Gross spread is also called "gross underwriting spread," "spread," or "production."

title: Gross Working Capital
definition: Gross working capital is the sum of a company's current assets (assets that are convertible to cash within a year or less). Gross working capital less current liabilities is equal to net working capital, or simply "working capital;" a more useful measure for balance sheet analysis.

title: Gross-Up
definition: A gross-up is an additional amount of money added to a payment to cover theincome taxesthat the recipient will owe on the payment. They are often used when employees receive a cash benefit, including severance plans, relocation expenses, and cash bonuses.

title: Gross Value Added
definition: Grass value added is a metric that measures the economic contributions of producers, industries, sectors, and regions to economies.

title: Gross Yield
definition: The gross yield of an investment is its profit before taxes and expenses are deducted. Gross yield is expressed in percentage terms. It is calculated as theannual returnon an investment (before taxes and expenses) divided by the current price of the investment.

title: Ground Lease
definition: A ground lease is an agreement in which a tenant is permitted to develop a piece of property during theleaseperiod, after which the land and all improvements are turned over to the property owner.

title: Group of 3 (G-3)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Group of 7 (G-7)
definition: The Group of Seven (G-7) is a global organization that meets to discuss economic and financial issues. Its members are France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the United States, the United Kingdom, and Canada. Government leaders of these countries meet periodically to address international economic and monetary issues, with each member taking over the presidency on a rotating basis.

title: Group of 8 (G-8)
definition: The Group of Eight (G-8) was an assembly of the world's largest developed economies that have established a position as pacesetters for theindustrializedworld. Member countries included the United States, the United Kingdom (U.K.), Canada, Germany, Japan, Italy, France, and Russia. Leaders meet periodically to address international economic and monetary issues.

title: Group of 10 (G-10)
definition: The Group of Ten (G10) consists of 11 industrialized nations that meet on an annual basis or more to consult, debate, and cooperate on international finance. The member countries are Belgium, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the Netherlands, Sweden, Switzerland, the United Kingdom, and the United States. The G10 is one of five similar groups, not to be confused with the Group of 7, 8, 20, or 24. Each of these consists of a group with similar economic interests.

title: Group of 11 (G-11)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Group of 20 (G-20)
definition: The Group of 20, also called the G-20, is a group of finance ministers andcentral bankgovernors from 19 of the world's largest economies, including those of many developing nations, along with theEuropean Union. Formed in 1999, the G-20 promotes global economic growth, international trade, and regulation of financial markets.Key agenda items include cryptocurrency regulation, climate change, and international trade issues.

title: Group of 30 (G-30)
definition: Diversification reduces portfolio risk and can increase return, but 100% portfoliodiversificationmay not be possible. Maximum portfolio diversification must consider the global market, all industry sectors, and all styles of investments available. This can add up to 1000s of individual stocks. Owning 30 stocks across a range of industry sectors has become a more attractive rule of thumb for achieving diversification. How should investors shift through these opinions to achieve the best degree of diversification?

title: Understanding Group Health Insurance: Coverage, Benefits, Costs, and More
definition: A group health insurance plan is a form of insurance designed to provide medical coverage to a collective, such as employees of a company or members of an organization, often facilitating shared risk and cost among the insured participants.

title: Group Life Insurance Explained: Types, Benefits, and Drawbacks
definition: Group life insurance provides employees or members with life coverage through their employer or organization. It is typically inexpensive because costs are shared among all insured, and often does not require a medical exam. However, coverage limits are generally lower and terminate upon leaving the organization, highlighting the need for potential supplementary personal insurance.

title: Understanding Group Term Life Insurance: Benefits, Costs, and Key Considerations
definition: Group term life insurance is a temporary insurance policy offered primarily through employers and associations. It typically includes a base amount of coverage at no cost to employees. There is an option to acquire additional coverage for themselves, their spouses, and children.

title: Group Universal Life Policy (GULP)
definition: A group universal life policy is a form ofuniversal life insuranceoffered to a group of people at a lower cost than what is typically offered to an individual. Group universallife insuranceis commonly purchased by corporations that want to provide their employees with life insurance coverage. These policies provide each insured party with permanent insurance coverage with an option to grow their savings.

title: Groupon
definition: Groupon is a website and mobile app that offers coupons, cashback on purchases and group deals to consumers. Restaurants, retailers, and manufacturers use Groupon deals in an effort to lure customers into their establishments or to purchase their products. Groupon allows consumers to purchase goods or services at a reduced price through prepaid vouchers.

title: Groupthink
definition: Groupthink describes the tendency of a group of individuals to make socially acceptable decisions rather than base them on specific and confirmable information.

title: Growing-Equity Mortgage
definition: Home equity is the difference between the amount you owe on amortgageand what the home is worth. It's essentially what you own in a home. The amount of equity in a house can grow over time as you make payments and the property's value increases.

title: Growth and Income Fund
definition: A growth and income fund is class of mutual fund orexchange-traded fund (ETF)that has a dual strategy of bothcapital appreciation(growth) andcurrent incomegenerated through dividends or interest payments. A growth andincome fundmay invest only in equities or in a combination of stocks, bonds, real estate investment trusts (REIT) and other securities.

title: Growth at a Reasonable Price (GARP)
definition: Growth at a reasonable price (GARP) is an equity investment strategy that seeks to combine tenets of bothgrowth investingandvalue investingto select individual stocks. GARP investors look for companies that are showing consistent earnings growth above broad market levels while excluding companies that have very highvaluations. The overarching goal is to avoid the extremes of either growth or value investing; this typically leads GARP investors to growth-oriented stocks with relatively lowprice/earnings (P/E)multiples in normal market conditions.

title: Growth Company
definition: A growth company is any company whose business generates significant positive cash flows or earnings, which increase at significantly faster rates than the overall economy. A growth company tends to have very profitable reinvestment opportunities for its own retained earnings. Thus, it typically pays little to no dividends to stockholders, opting instead to put most or all of its profits back into its expanding business.

title: Growth Curve
definition: A growth curve serves as a vital tool for organizations to understand trends and patterns over a period, aiding in strategic planning and decision-making.

title: Growth Fund
definition: A growth fund is a type of mutual fund or exchange-traded fund with the primary goal of generatingcapital appreciationby investing in fast-growing companies. Growth funds typically reinvest earnings in business expansion,acquisitions, orR&D, which usually means they don't pay dividends. They're ideal for investors with a long-term investment horizon and a high risk tolerance. Keep in mind that the higher returns growth funds offer mean greater risk. They can be more volatile than other funds. Growth funds are separated by market capitalization into small-, mid-, and large-cap.

title: Growth Industry
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Growth Investing
definition: Growth investing is a strategy that focuses on capital appreciation and targets companies that are expected to grow faster than their peers. It aims for big returns but also comes with high risk if these companies don't perform as expected. Investors should consider factors like a company's revenue growth, innovation, market potential, and leadership to determine their future growth potential. It is contrasted withvalue investing, which looks for strong companies that are undervalued. Notable investors like Thomas Rowe Price, Jr., Philip Fisher, and Peter Lynch helped shape growth investing by stressing long-term growth.

title: Growth Rates
definition: Growth rates refer to the percentage change of a specific variable within a specific time period.

title: Growth Stock: What It Is, Examples, vs. Value Stock
definition: A growth stock is any share in a company that is anticipated to grow at a rate significantly above the average growth for the market. These stocks generally do not pay dividends. This is because the issuers of growth stocks are usually companies that want to reinvest any earnings they accrue in order to accelerate growth in the short term. When investorsinvest in growth stocks, they anticipate that they will earn money throughcapital gainswhen they eventually sell their shares in the future.

title: Grunt Work
definition: Grunt work is an expression often used to describe thankless and menial work. It can also refer to jobs that may lack glamour and prestige or are dull.

title: GSCI (S&P)
definition: The S&P GSCI is a compositeindex of commoditiesthat measures the performance of the commodities market. The index often serves as abenchmarkfor commodities investments. Investing in a GSCI fund provides a broadly diversified, unleveragedlong-onlyposition in commodity futures.

title: Guanxi
definition: Guanxi refers to the Chinese cultural practice where personal and professional networks are cultivated to foster trust and obligations that benefit business interactions.

title: Guarantee Company
definition: A guarantee company is a type of corporation designed to protect members from liability. Guarantee companies often form when non-profit organizations wish to attain corporate status. Clubs, sports associations, students' unions, and other membership organizations, workers' co-operatives, social enterprises, andnon-governmental organizations(NGOs) may also form guarantee companies.

title: Guarantee Fees
definition: The term guarantee fee refers to the sum of money paid to the issuer of a mortgage-backed security (MBS) by the holder.
