This charge helps the issuer pay for administrative costs and expenses related to the security and also cuts down on any risk or loss that may arise if any of the mortgages that back the security default. Also called g-fees, guarantee fees refer to charges paid by a mortgagor to a guarantor for services rendered as well.

title: Guaranteed Bond
definition: A guaranteed bond is a debt security that offers a secondary guarantee that interest and principal payments will be made by a third party, should the issuer default due to reasons such as insolvency or bankruptcy. A guaranteed bond can be of either the municipal or corporate variety. It can be backed by a bond insurance company, a fund or group entity, a government authority, or the corporate parents of subsidiaries or joint ventures that are issuing bonds.

title: Guaranteed Death Benefit
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Guaranteed Investment (Interest) Certificate (GIC)
definition: A Canadian guaranteed investment certificate (GIC) is a low-risk, fixed-term savings option provided by financial institutions in Canada, offering a guaranteed return over a specified period.

title: Guaranteed Investment Contract (GIC)
definition: Guaranteed investment contracts (GICs) are agreements betweeninsurancecompanies and investors, often through pension funds or401(k)plans, that promise a fixed interest rate andprincipalreturn.

title: Guaranteed Investment Fund (GIF)
definition: A guaranteed investment fund (GIF) is an insurance-basedinvestment productthat places money into equity, bond, orindex fundswhile guaranteeing a minimum value at maturity or death. Sold by insurance companies, GIFs can offer fixed or variable returns and may include reset features that allow gains to be locked in over time.

title: Guaranteed Issue Life Insurance
definition: Guaranteed issue life insurance provides lifelong coverage and doesn’t require a medical exam or health questions. If you have a serious health condition and can’t get a standard whole life policy, guaranteed issue life insurance can be a good alternative.

title: Guaranteed Lifetime Withdrawal Benefit (GLWB)
definition: A guaranteed lifetime withdrawal benefit (GLWB) is ariderto avariable annuitythat provides a minimumpayoutlevel, even if market losses reduce the cash value of your contract. Most of these riders also allow you to make withdrawals from your cash value as needed. You typically pay for the GLWB rider with annual fees that can vary based on the issuer.

title: Guaranteed Loan
definition: A guaranteed loan is a loan that a third party guarantees—or assumes the debt obligation for—in the event that the borrowerdefaults. Sometimes, a guaranteed loan is guaranteed by a government agency, which will purchase the debt from the lending financial institution and take on responsibility for the loan.

title: Guaranteed Minimum Accumulation Benefit (GMAB)
definition: The guaranteed minimum accumulation benefit (GMAB) is a variable annuity rider that guarantees a minimum value to the annuitant after the accumulation period, or another set period. This is usually somewhere close to 10 years. The GMAB rider protects the value of the annuity from market volatility.

title: Guaranteed Minimum Income Benefit (GMIB)
definition: A guaranteed minimum income benefit (GMIB) is an optional rider added to a variable annuity that ensures theannuitantreceives a set minimum income during retirement, even if market performance is poor. It balances market growth potential with income security, making it a useful tool for retirees seeking predictable payouts.

title: Guaranteed Minimum Withdrawal Benefit (GMWB)
definition: A guaranteed minimum withdrawal benefit (GMWB) is a type of rider or contract attached to some annuity insurance policies. It guarantees the policyholder a steady stream of annual withdrawals via the return of all premiums paid into the contract, regardless of an investment's performance, through a series of annual withdrawals. A GMWB is unlike aguaranteed minimum income benefit(GMIB), where the latter offers a payout of specified minimum periodic income after a waiting period, regardless of the variable annuity's investment performance.

title: Guaranteed Payments to Partners
definition: Guaranteed payments to partners serve as compensation for services or capital provided, acting like a salary independent of partnership profits. These payments, outlined in Section 707(c) of the Internal Revenue Code, create specific tax implications. Without proper handling, these payments can lead to unexpected financial consequences for both partners and the partnership.

title: Guaranteed Renewable Policy
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Guaranteed Stock
definition: Guaranteed stock has two meanings, one applied to dividends and one applied to inventory. The more common reference is to an infrequently used form of common orpreferred stock, in which thedividendsare guaranteed by one or more other companies. Guaranteed stock issues, like guaranteed bonds, have most often used byrailroadsand public utilities. The guaranteed dividend can increase the stock's price.

title: Guarantor
definition: A guarantor is an individual or entity who promises to pay a debt if the main party defaults. They act as a backup source of payment if the borrower can’t cover the debt.

title: Guerrilla Marketing
definition: Guerrilla marketing involves unconventional and creative strategies to reach a target audience and generate excitement for a brand, product, or service.

title: Guidance
definition: Company guidance is a forecast that public companies share to outline expectedearnings, revenue, and spending for the near future. This information is usually released after quarterly earnings reports,helping analystsand investors adjust their expectations and strategies. Companies must use disclaimers to avoid legal issues if projections are not met.

title: Guideline Premium and Corridor Test (GPT)
definition: The guideline premium and corridor test (GPT) is used to determine whether aninsuranceproduct can be taxed as insurance rather than as an investment. GPT limits the amount of premiums that can be paid into an insurance policy relative to the policy’sdeath benefit.

title: Guilder Share (New York Share)
definition: A Guilder share was an ownership stake in a Dutch company that could be traded in the United States because it represented shares that had been canceled in Dutch stock markets.

title: Guinea Franc (GNF)
definition: The Guinea franc is the national currency of the Republic of Guinea, a country in West Africa, often referred to as the currency abbreviation GNF. It floats freely on international markets.

title: Gunnar Myrdal
definition: Gunnar Myrdal was a SwedishKeynesian economistand sociologist who shared the 1974 Nobel Memorial Prize in Economics with Austrian economistFriedrich Hayek. Myrdal was best known for his work in international development and trade economics, as well as for his activism promoting racial equality and opposing American foreign policy.His left-wing political and social views strongly influenced his research and writing.

title: Guns-and-Butter Curve
definition: Theguns-and-buttercurve is the classic economic example of the production possibility curve, which demonstrates the idea ofopportunity cost. It postulates that you can only gain something if something else is given in return.

title: Gunslinger
definition: "Gunslinger" is a slang term for an aggressiveportfolio manager. A gunslinger often useshigh-risk investmenttechniques to hopefully produce big returns. Rather than considering thelong-term valueof the company underlying a stock, gunslingers look at a stock's momentum and seek to benefit from short-term trades based on sharp movements in a stock's price.

title: Guppy Multiple Moving Average (GMMA)
definition: The Guppy Multiple Moving Average (GMMA) is atechnical indicatorthat aims to anticipate a potential breakout in the price of an asset. The term gets its name from Daryl Guppy, an Australian financial columnist and book author who developed the concept in his book, "Trading Tactics."

title: Gwei
definition: Gwei is a denomination of the cryptocurrency ether (ETH), used on theEthereumnetwork to buy and sell goods and services. A gwei is one-billionth of one ETH. Gwei is the most commonly used ether unit. Ethereum transaction fees, known as gas fees, are priced in gwei so that they is easier to understand. Many cryptocurrencies have adopted naming conventions for their micro-denominations because these smaller units can be used more often in transactions.

title: H-Shares
definition: H-shares are shares of Chinese mainland companies that are listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange or other foreign exchanges. Although H-shares are regulated by Chinese law, they are denominated in Hong Kong dollars and are traded in the same way as other equities on the Hong Kong exchange. H-shares are different from A-shares, which were only available to mainland Chinese investors, not foreign investors.

title: Habendum Clause
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hacktivism
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Haggle
definition: To haggle is when two parties involved in a transaction such as the purchase of a good and servicenegotiatethe price until both parties can mutually agree on a fair price. The process of haggling involves two parties making sequential offers andcounteroffersto each other until a price is agreed upon. The individual trying to buy the good and service is trying to pay the least amount possible, while the seller's primary objective is to maximize the selling price. Haggling also may go by the names bargaining, quibbling, dickering, or informal negotiating.

title: Haircut
definition: Broadly speaking, the term “haircut” refers to the reduction, or trimming, of an asset’s value. There are a couple different contexts in which it can be applied.

title: Half Stock
definition: A half stock is a security sold with apar valuethat is 50% of what is considered to be the standard price. The par value refers to the face value of a bond, or in some cases, a stock. Half stock can be eithercommon stockorpreferred stockand, other than the reduced par value, acts as a regular share of stock.

title: Half-Year Convention For Depreciation
definition: The half-year convention for depreciation is thedepreciationschedule that treats all property acquired during the year as being acquired exactly in the middle of the year. This means that only half of the full-year depreciation is allowed in the first year, while the remaining balance is deducted in the final year of the depreciation schedule, or the year that the property is sold.

title: Halloween Massacre
definition: The Halloween Massacre refers to a 2006 decision by the Canadian government to tax allincome trustsdomiciled in the country. On Halloween, Oct. 31, 2006, Canada’s then-minister of finance, Jim Flaherty, announced that all income trusts would be taxed at a rate of over 30% on taxable income just like corporations. The decision causedunitholdervalue to decrease dramatically virtually overnight.Income trusts were popular among Canadian investors because of their favorable tax treatment.

title: Halloween Strategy
definition: The Halloween strategy refers to the investing theory that stocks perform better from Oct. 31 to May 1 than the rest of the year.

title: Halo Effect
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hamada Equation
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hammer Candlestick
definition: The hammer is a bullish reversal candlestick pattern characterized by a small body near the top, a long lower wick, and little to no upper shadow. It signals a shift from selling to buying pressure.

title: Hammer Clause: Definition, Function, and Real-World Example
definition: A hammer clause is contained in aninsurance policy, allowing an insurer to compel the insured to settle a claim. A hammer clause is also known as a blackmail clause, settlement cap provision, or consent to settlement provision.

title: Hammering
definition: Hammering is rapid and concentrated selling of stocksharesin the wake of an unexpected event that is perceived as extremely damaging to the company's short-term performance. The effect of hammering is a steep drop in the price of the stock.

title: Hamptons Effect
definition: The Hamptons Effect refers to a dip in trading that occurs just before the Labor Day weekend that is followed by increased trading volume as traders and investors return from the long weekend. The term references the idea that many of the large-scale traders on Wall Street spend the last days of summer in the Hamptons, a traditional summer destination for the New York City elite.

title: Handelsgesetzbuch (HGB)
definition: Handelsgesetzbuch (HGB) is Germany's commercial code and accounting standard for how companies must prepare and report financial statements. HGB also mandates various corporate ordinances and regulations dealing with the treatment of workers. In many ways, the guidelines spelled out in HGB are similar togenerally accepted accounting principles(GAAP) in the United States andInternational Financial Reporting Standards(IFRS), with a few notable differences. For example, Germany’s HGB and the IFRS both use historical costs as the core of accounting, but German law generally does not allow for revaluations as the IFRS does.

title: Handle
definition: The term "handle" refers to the portion of aprice quotefor securities that is a whole number. For instance, the handle of a price quote of $149.25 for a stock is $149. It's used with a slight variation in theforex market. That is, forcurrency pairquotes, the handle is the portion of the price that is the same for the bid and the ask. For example, where the bid is 1.17742 and the ask is 1.17758, the handle is 1.17. The handle gives traders a quick understanding of where the market stands for various securities. The term "stem" refers to the portion of a stock quote that is to the right of the decimal point.

title: Hands-Off Investor
definition: A hands-off investor prefers to set an investment portfolio and make only minor changes for a long period of time. Many hands-off investors use index funds ortarget-date funds, which make only small and slow changes to their holdings and therefore do not require much monitoring.

title: Hang Seng Index (HSI)
definition: The Hang Seng Index (HSI) is a free-float market capitalization-weighted index that tracks the performance of 88 leading companies across various sectors listed on the Hong Kong Exchange (HKEx).

title: Hanging Man Candlestick
definition: A hanging man is a single candlestick pattern that appears at the end of an uptrend and signals an impending price decline.

title: Happiness Economics
definition: Happiness economics is the formal academic study of the relationship between individual satisfaction and economic issues such as employment andwealth.

title: Harami Cross
definition: A harami cross is a Japanesecandlestickpattern that consists of a large candlestick that moves in the direction of the trend, followed by a smalldojicandlestick. The doji iscompletelycontained within the prior candlestick’s body. The harami cross pattern suggests that the previous trend may be about toreverse. The pattern can be either bullish or bearish. The bullish pattern signals a possible price reversal to the upside, while the bearish pattern signals a possible price reversal to the downside.

title: Hard Asset
definition: A hard asset is a tangible resource with intrinsic value, such as land, real estate, equipment, or commodities. These assets support business operations by enhancing production, generating revenue, and serving as a financial buffer during downturns. Understanding hard assets helps investors and analysts assess a company's underlying strength and long-term value.

title: Hard Call Protection
definition: Hard call protection, or absolute call protection, is a provision in acallable bondwhereby theissuercannot exercise the call and redeem the bond before the specified date, usually three to five years from the date of issuance.

title: Hard Currency
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hard Dollars
definition: Hard dollars are cash fees orpaymentsmade by an investor or customer to a brokerage firm in return for their services. Hard dollar payments are usually set amounts that are known before a customer begins dealing with a broker.

title: Hard Fork (Blockchain)
definition: A blockchain hard fork is a change in programming that is incompatible with the old programming. This essentially creates a newblockchain, and sometimes a newcryptocurrency. Hard forks generally require all nodes or users who wish to stay on the new chain to upgrade to the latest version of the software.

title: Hard Inquiry
definition: A hard inquiry is a credit check that happens when a lender reviews your credit report after you apply for credit. A hard inquiry can lower your credit score slightly, especially if you have several in a short time. Lenders request hard inquiries to decide whether to approve applications for things like credit cards, car loans, or mortgages. In this article, we explain why hard inquiries happen and share simple ways you can reduce their impact on your credit score.

title: Hard Landing
definition: A hard landing refers to a notable economic slowdown or evenrecessionfollowing a period of rapid growth. The term is derived from the high-speed landing of a plane, which potentially can cause damage and injury. The more dependent an economy becomes on fiscal stimulus or easy money, the more difficult it becomes to wean off of expansionary policy, and the more vulnerable the economy becomes to a hard landing.

title: Hard Loan
definition: A hard loan is a foreign loan that must be paid inhard currency, which is the currency of a nation that has political stability and a reputation for economic strength. For example, a country classified as a developing country may borrow via a hard loandenominatedin U.S. dollars.

title: Hard Money
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hard Money Loan
definition: A hard money loan offers quick, short-term financing secured by real property, typically used as a last resort by real estate investors, developers, and property flippers. Unlike traditional bank loans, these loans are based primarily on the value of the collateral property rather than the borrower's creditworthiness, allowing for a faster approval process. However, they often come with higher interest rates, reflecting the increased risk taken on by private individuals or companies who usually serve as lenders in these transactions.

title: Hard Sell
definition: A hard sell refers to an advertising or sales approach that features especially direct and insistent language. A hard sell is designed to get a consumer to purchase a good or service quickly, rather than to decide to evaluate their options and potentially wait on the purchase.

title: Hard Skills
definition: Hard skills are technical skills required for a job. They are learned abilities acquired and enhanced through education and experience. Hard skills are important for your resume, as employers look for them when hiring.

title: Hard Stop
definition: A hard stop is more of a concept than an actual order type. A hard stop presumes aprice levelthat, if reached, will decisively trigger an order to sell an underlying security.

title: Hard-To-Borrow List
definition: A hard-to-borrow list is a tool that brokerages use to identify securities difficult to borrow forshort sales. Short sellers rely on brokers to have stock shares available to borrow; hard-to-borrow stocks can impact trading strategies and impede trading.

title: Hardening
definition: Hardening is a reference to trading conditions in which securities prices are rising andvolatilityis declining in a particular financial market. Although the assessment that a market is hardening can be applied in several contexts, it is most commonly used when referring to the market forcommodities futures.

title: Hardship Exemption
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hardship Withdrawal
definition: A hardship withdrawal is an IRS approved removal of funds from a qualified retirement plan because of an "immediate and heavy financial need."

title: Harmless Warrant
definition: The term harmless warrant refers to a provision that requires bondholders to surrender an existing bond if they want to purchase the same type ofbondfrom the issuer. Put simply, bond issuers put harmlesswarrantson their bonds, requiring investors to swap their existing bond if they want to purchase another one with similar features from the same issuer. Harmless warrants are a safety net for bond issuers as they help keep theirdebtlevels in check.

title: Harmonic Mean
definition: The harmonic mean is a way to average a series of financial ratios.

title: Harmonized Index of Consumer Prices (HICP)
definition: The Harmonized Index of Consumer Prices (HICP) is a measure ofinflationin theEuropean Union (EU). It reflects change over time in the prices paid by households for a representativebasketof goods and services.TheEuropean Central Bank (ECB)uses the HICP for theEurozonecomprising the 20 EU states using theeurocommon currency to pursue its objective of price stability, defined as 2% annualized inflation over the medium term.

title: Harmonized Sales Tax (HST)
definition: Canada’s harmonized sales tax (HST) is aconsumption taxpaid by local consumers and businesses. It combines the federal goods and services tax (GST) and sales taxes. Five provinces use the HST as of 2025.The HST was implemented in 1997 and is administered by the federal government.Businesses collect the tax and remit it to theCanada Revenue Agency (CRA). Proponents believe it improves competition for Canadian businesses by simplifying their administrative costs, leading to lower prices for consumers.

title: Harry Markowitz
definition: In 1952, American economist Harry Markowitz introducedModern Portfolio Theory(MPT), the premise behind a groundbreaking investment strategy that emphasized the structure and performance of an entire portfolio rather than individual stocks. With its Efficient Frontier of asset diversification and its novel approach to risk correlation, MPT fundamentally changed how individuals and institutions manage investments.Markowitz, along withWilliam F. SharpeandMerton Millerwon the 1990 Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences. The Nobel Committee cited Markowitz's theory of asset choice as the “first pioneering contribution in the field of financial economics.” MPT has influenced many investment tools and strategies, including the development of robo-advisors. Yet, despite its wide acceptance and success, MPT has faced criticisms, particularly of its inability to account for systemic risks.

title: Hart-Scott-Rodino Antitrust Improvements Act of 1976
definition: The Hart-Scott-Rodino Antitrust Improvements Act of 1976 requires large companies to file notifications with theFederal Trade Commissionand the anti-trust division of Department of Justice prior to certainmergers and acquisitionsor tender offers.﻿﻿

title: Harvard Business School
definition: The internationally lauded Harvard Business School is the graduate business school of Harvard University.

title: Harvard MBA Indicator
definition: The Harvard MBA Indicator is acontrarianlong-term stock market indicator that evaluates the percentage of Harvard Business SchoolMBAgraduates that accept "market sensitive" jobs. Market sensitive jobs exist in fields such as investment banking, securities sales and trading, private equity, venture capital, and leveraged buyouts.

title: Harvest Strategy
definition: A harvest strategy is a marketing and business approach that involves a reduction or a termination of investments in a product, product line, or line of business. It's intended that the entities involved can reap—or harvest—maximum profits.

title: Hash
definition: A hash is a math function that inputs data of any size and generates a fixed-size output that is encrypted.

title: Hashed Timelock Contract
definition: A hashed timelock contract (HTLC) is a type of smart contract used in blockchain applications. It reduces counterparty risk by creating a time-based escrow that requires a cryptographic passphrase for unlocking.

title: Hashgraph Consensus Mechanism
definition: Hashgraph consensus is an alternative to—or the next generation of—the technology behind blockchain consensus mechanisms. Instead of using the computational power of large networks to verify transactions, transactions are recorded and confirmed via a protocol that uses node communication.

title: Hawala
definition: Hawala, an ancient method of money transfer originating in South Asia, enables the movement of funds without the physical transfer of currency. Known for its reliance on trust rather than officialpromissory notes, it operates outside traditional banking systems, particularly benefiting those in regions with limited access to formal financial networks. By facilitating swift, cost-effective remittances, hawala serves as an alternativeremittancechannel, especially for migrants and expatriates. However, its unregulated nature also attracts illicit activities, leading many countries to impose strict regulations or outright bans.

title: Hawk
definition: In economic discussions, "hawks" are policymakers focused on controlling inflation by raising interest rates, even if it affects growth and employment. This hawkish stance emphasizes inflation stability over short-term economic gains, contrasting with "doves," who favor low rates to boost activity. This exploration compares these monetary philosophies, showing their influence on central banks' efforts to balance economic growth and inflation.

title: Hawthorne Effect
definition: The Hawthorne Effect is the supposed inclination of people who are the subjects of an experiment or study to change or improve the behavior being evaluated only because it is being studied and not because of changes in the experiment parameters or stimulus. It was first identified by organizational researchers in the 1920s.

title: Hazard Insurance
definition: Hazard insurance is coverage that protects a property owner against damage caused by fires, severe storms, hail, sleet, or other natural events. As long as the specific weather event is covered within the policy, the property owner will receive compensation to cover the cost of any damage incurred.

title: Hazard Rate
definition: The hazard rate quantifies the risk of failure for a specific item or system as it ages, useful for optimizing maintenance and reliability strategies in various fields.

title: Hazardous Activity
definition: Hazardous activity refers to a recreational pursuit thatlifeordisability insurancepolicy considers high-risk. The activities are not typically covered by insurance as they carry an increased potential for injury or loss. Hazardous activities include scuba diving, BASE jumping, hang gliding, race car driving, flying a plane, horseback riding, bungee jumping, parasailing, and off-roading. Further, some employment falls into this category such as some construction work, logging, aircraft pilots, offshore oil rig worker, offshore fisherman, structural steelworkers, and underground mining.

title: Head and Shoulders Pattern
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Head-Fake Trade
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Head of Household
definition: HOH filing provides a financial boost to single or separated individuals with dependents.

title: Head Trader
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Headhunter
definition: A headhunter is a company or individual that provides employment recruiting services on behalf of an employer. Headhunters are hired by firms to find talent and to locate individuals who meet specific job requirements.

title: Headline Earnings
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Understanding the Headline Effect: Impact on Corporations and Economies
definition: The headline effect refers to the disproportionate impact that negative news headlines in the popular press can have on economic behavior and market prices. Manyeconomistsbelieve negative headlines make consumers more reluctant to spend money.

title: Understanding Headline Inflation and Its Link to the CPI
definition: Headline inflation represents the overall increase in prices for goods and services within an economy, as measured in total consumption, including volatile items such as food and energy.

title: Headline Risk
definition: Headline risk is the possibility that a news story will adversely affect the price of an investment, such as a stock or commodity. Headline risk can also impact the performance of a specific sector or the entirestock market.

title: Heads of Agreement
definition: A "heads of" agreement is a non-binding document that outlines the basic terms of a tentativepartnershipagreement or transaction. Also known as a "heads of terms," or "letter of intent," a heads of agreement marks the first step on the path to a full legally binding agreement or contract and a guideline for the roles and responsibilities of the parties involved in a potential partnership before any binding documents are drawn up. Such a document is commonly used in commercial transactions, such as the purchase of a business.

title: Health Insurance
definition: Health insurance is a contract between a company and a consumer. The company agrees to pay all or some of the insured person's healthcare costs in return for payment of a monthly premium. Patients are responsible for deductibles and copays, and these can reduce the amount the company pays.

title: Health Insurance Marketplace
definition: The Health Insurance Marketplace is a platform that offers insurance plans to individuals, families, and small businesses. TheAffordable Care Act(ACA) established the Marketplace as a means to extend health insurance coverage to millions of uninsured Americans. Many states offer their own marketplaces, while the federal government manages an exchange open to residents of other states.

title: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
definition: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) law ensures health-care plans are accessible, portable, and renewable, and sets the standards for how medical data is shared.

title: Health Maintenance Organizations (HMOs)
definition: A health maintenance organization (HMO) provides healthcare for a monthly or annual fee. HMOs differ from other types of health insurance plans primarily through their use of an established network of healthcare providers that members must use. They offer potential cost savings for members through lower premiums, but restrictions that keep costs low include the need to first receive medical care services from your designated primary care physician (PCP) and to obtain referrals from your PCP to other physicians for any additional needed care.

title: Health Plan Categories
definition: The term 'health plan categories' refers to the four types of health insurance plans that differ based on the average expenses paid by the plan. The four health plan categories offered by the U.S. federal government's Health Insurance Marketplace are Bronze, Silver, Gold, and Platinum. The category levels are defined by the percentage of expenses each plan type covers and the relative size of their monthly premiums.Understanding these categories, and the expenses associated with them, can help you make informed decisions about buying health insurance.

title: Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA)
definition: A health reimbursement arrangement (HRA) is a plan that reimburses employees for qualified medical expenses and, in some cases, insurance premiums. Reimbursements are tax free for employees and tax deductible for employers.

title: Health Savings Account (HSA)
definition: A Health Savings Account (HSA) is a tax-advantaged account created for or by individuals covered under high-deductible health plans (HDHPs) to save for qualified medical expenses. Contributions are made into the account by the individual or their employer and are limited to a maximum amount each year.

title: Healthcare Power of Attorney (HCPA)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Healthcare Sector
definition: The healthcare sector represents a significant and complex portion of the U.S. economy, comprising industries that provide medical services, manufacture medical equipment and drugs, or offer insurance. Accounting for 18% of the GDP in 2020, this sector is driven by strong demand due to medical research, development, and an aging population. Understanding its structure and economic influences, including government intervention and inelastic demand, is crucial for navigating potential economic issues and reform uncertainties.

title: Heating Degree Day (HDD)
definition: A heating degree day (HDD) measures the number of degrees that a day's average temperature falls below 65 degrees Fahrenheit (18 degrees Celsius), which is the temperature below which buildings need to be heated.

title: Heath-Jarrow-Morton Model
definition: The Heath-Jarrow-Morton Model (HJM Model) is used to modelforward interest rates. These rates are then modeled to an existing term structure ofinterest ratesto determine appropriate prices for interest-rate-sensitive securities.

title: Heatmap
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Heavy Industry
definition: Heavy industry relates to a type of business that typically carries a high capital cost (capital-intensive), highbarriers to entry, and low transportability. The term "heavy" refers to the fact that the items produced by "heavy industry" used to be products such as iron, coal, oil, ships, etc. Today, the reference also refers to industries that disrupt the environment in the form of pollution, deforestation, etc.

title: Heckscher-Ohlin Model
definition: The Heckscher-Ohlin model is an economic theory focused on trade and resource allocation. The model compares a country's export of goods andfactors of productionwith its import of goods that it can't produce as efficiently. It plays an important role in determining a country'sexportand import strategies based on resource abundance. The model applies to commodities and factors like labor.

title: Hedge
definition: A hedge is an investing strategy that aims to reduce risk by taking an opposite position in a related asset.

title: Hedge Accounting
definition: Hedge accounting matches the timing of income statement recognition of the hedging instrument with that of the hedged risk.

title: Hedge Clause
definition: A hedge clause is included in a research report to attempt to absolve the writer of any responsibility for the accuracy of the information included in the report or publication. The hedge clause indemnifies the author(s) against responsibility for any errors, omissions, or oversights contained within the document. Hedge clauses can be found in analyst reports, company press releases, and most investing websites.

title: Hedge Fund
definition: A hedge fund is an actively managed investment that pools money from accredited investors and seeks high returns by using complex strategies and significant risk.

title: Hedge Fund Manager
definition: A hedge fund manager is an individual or financial firm that manages and makes investment decisions and oversees the operations of a hedge fund. Managing ahedge fundcan be an attractive career option because of its high earnings potential.

title: Hedge Ratio
definition: The hedge ratio plays a crucial role in risk management by comparing the value of a protected position to the entire investment. This measure can also apply to futures contracts related to the cash commodity they hedge. Understanding the hedge ratio is essential in scenarios like currency risk where it determines the extent of protection. For example, holding $10,000 in foreign equity while hedging $5,000 offers a hedge ratio of 0.5, safeguarding half the investment from currency fluctuations.

title: Tender in Finance: Definition, How It Works, and Example
definition: A tender is a government or financial institution's invitation to bid for a project.

title: Hedging Transaction
definition: A hedging transaction is a tactical action taken by an investor. It's intended to reduce the risk of losing money or experiencing a shortfall while executing their investment strategy.

title: Hedonic Pricing
definition: Hedonic pricing identifies price factors based on the idea that a good's price is influenced by both its internal characteristics and external factors.

title: Hedonic Regression Method
definition: Hedonic regression is the use of aregression modelto estimate the influence that various factors have on the price of a good, or sometimes the demand for a good. In a hedonic regression model, the dependent variable is the price (or demand) of the good, and the independent variables are the attributes of the good believed to influence utility for the buyer or consumer of the good. The resulting estimated coefficients on the independent variables can be interpreted as the weights that buyers place on the various qualities of the good.

title: Heikin-Ashi Technique
definition: The Heikin-Ashi technique averages price data to create a Japanese candlestick chart that filters outmarket noise.

title: Heir
definition: An heir legally inherits the estate of someone who dies intestate, lacking a will. Heirs, typically children or close relatives, receive property based on state probate laws. Explore the types of heirs, the process of intestate succession, and examples like the Rockefeller family's inheritance.

title: Held by Production Clause
definition: "Held by production" is a provision in an oil or natural gas propertyleasethat allows thelessee, generally an energy company, to continue drilling activities on the property as long as it is economically producing a minimum amount of oil or gas. The held-by-production provision thereby extends the lessee's right to operate the property beyond the initial lease term. This provision is also a feature of mineral property leases.

title: Held-For-Trading Security
definition: A held-for-trading security is a debt or equityinvestmentthat investors purchase with the intent of selling within a short period of time, usually less than one year. Within that time frame, the investor hopes to see appreciation in the value of the security and sell it for a profit.

title: Held Order
definition: A held order is a market order that requires prompt execution for an immediatefill. This can be contrasted with anot-held order, which provides brokers with both time and price discretion to try and get a better fill for a customer.

title: Held-to-Maturity (HTM)
definition: Held-to-maturity (HTM) securities are purchased to be owned untilmaturity. For example, a company’s management might invest in a bond that they plan to hold to maturity.

title: Helicopter Drop (Helicopter Money)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hell or High Water Contract
definition: A hell or high water contract (also known as a promise-to-pay contract) is a non-cancelable contract. A hell or high water contract stipulates that the purchaser must make the specified payments to the seller, regardless of any difficulties they may encounter. Hell or high water clauses bind the purchaser orlesseeto the terms of the contract until the contract's expiration.

title: Help-Wanted Index (HWI)
definition: The Conference Board's Help-Wanted Index (HWI) measures how efficiently employers match jobs to the available workforce, which makes it a useful gauge of labor demand and job-market slack. Rising readings can point to worker shortages that push wages higher, which can affect bond and equity markets. The index began in 1951, was restructured to a 1987 base, and provides U.S. national and regional data.

title: Henry B. Tippie College Of Business
definition: A prediction market is a platform that has become increasingly popular in recent years. Participants on these platforms trade event-based contracts whose prices reflect collective forecasts of future outcomes.

title: Henry Hub
definition: Henry Hub is a natural gas pipeline in Erath, Louisiana, serving as the official delivery spot for NYMEX futures contracts. Owned by Sabine Pipe Line LLC and located in Erath, Louisiana, the hub accesses major U.S. gas markets. It connects to four intrastate and nine interstate pipelines, like the Transcontinental, Acadian, and Sabine pipelines.

title: Herbert A. Simon
definition: Herbert A. Simon was a prominent figure in economics and artificial intelligence. He won theNobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciencesin 1978 for his groundbreaking theory of bounded rationality, which showed that people make decisions with limited information instead of perfect logic. Simon spent much of his career at Carnegie Mellon University, where he was also a pioneer in artificial intelligence (AI) research. He published many influential works over his lifetime and received the A.M. Turing Award for his major contributions to computer science.

title: Herd Instinct
definition: The term herd instinct refers to a phenomenon where people join groups and follow the actions of others under the assumption that other individuals have already done their research. Herd instincts are common in all aspects of society, even within thefinancial sector, where investors follow what they perceive other investors are doing, rather than relying on their own analysis.

title: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)
definition: Regulators use the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) to compare large and dominant companies in an industry and determine whether that industry is close to being a monopoly.

title: Heritage and Stabilization Fund (HSF)
definition: Finance is the study and management of money and financial resources by individuals, corporations, and governments.

title: Heroes Earned Retirement Opportunities Act (HERO)
definition: The Heroes Earned Retirement Opportunities (HERO) Act is a 2006 law that allows military personnel to fund theirindividual retirement accounts (IRAs)with combat pay. It is a tax break designed for those who have served in combat zones. Under the law, combat-related compensation paid since January 2004, which is tax-free, can be used to fund IRAs.

title: Herrick Payoff Index
definition: Financial Writer, Editor, and Economic Consultant

title: Hersey-Blanchard Model
definition: The Situational Leadership Model suggests there is no single leadership style that is better than another.

title: Heston Model
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Heterodox Economics
definition: Heterodox economics is the analysis and study of economic principles considered outside ofmainstream or orthodox schoolsof economic thought. Schools of heterodox economics vary widely and have few common characteristics other than propounding theories, assumptions, or methodologies that fall outside of or contradict the mainstreamKeynesianandneoclassicalmovements.

title: Heteroskedastic
definition: Heteroskedastic refers to a condition in which thevarianceof the residual term, or error term, in aregression modelvaries widely. If this is true, it may vary in a systematic way, and there may be some factor that can explain this. If so, then the model may be poorly defined and should be modified so that this systematic variance is explained by one or more additional predictor variables.

title: Heteroskedasticity
definition: Heteroscedasticity, or heteroskedasticity, emerges in statistics when the standard deviations of a predicted variable vary over time. In finance, this manifests as changing volatility in stock and bond prices, challenging the assumptions in linear regression models. It can affect the precision of econometric and financial analyses, especially those using the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM).

title: Heuristics
definition: Heuristics are mental shortcuts people use that simplify problems and help them avoid cognitive overload.

title: Hidden Taxes
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hidden Values
definition: Hidden values are assets that are undervalued on a company's balance sheet and therefore may not be incorporated into or reflected in the company's share price.

title: Hierarchical Deterministic Wallet (HD Wallet)
definition: A hierarchical deterministic (HD) wallet is adigital walletused to store the cryptographic keys for cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin and Ethereum. It generates a series of private and public keys from a single seed phrase, allowing secure management of multiple accounts. Anyone with access to both the public and private keys can control the associatedcryptocurrency.

title: Hierarchy-of-Effects Theory
definition: The hierarchy-of-effects theory is a model of howadvertisinginfluences a consumer's decision to purchase or not purchase a product or service. The hierarchy represents the progression of learning and decision-making consumer experiences as a result of advertising. A hierarchy-of-effects model is used to set up a structured series of advertising message objectives for a particular product, to build upon each successive objective until a sale is ultimately made. The objectives of acampaignare (in order of delivery): awareness, knowledge, liking, preference, conviction, and purchase.

title: Hierarchy of GAAP
definition: A family opportunity mortgage is aconventional loanwith expanded guidelines allowing a borrower to buy a home for elderly parents or a disabled adult child. This type of loan doesn't require the borrower to live in the home, making it easier to get amortgagefor a family member who otherwise wouldn't qualify for one. This type of loan is supported byFannie Mae and Freddie Mac, though its exact name may vary from one lender to another, as "family opportunity mortgage" isn't officially used anymore.

title: High Beta Index
definition: A high beta index is a basket of stocks that exhibits greater volatility than a broad market index such as the S&P 500 Index. The S&P 500 High Beta Index is the most well-known of these indexes. It tracks the performance of 100 companies in the S&P 500 that are the most sensitive to changes in market returns.﻿﻿

title: High Close
definition: A high close is a trading strategy that stock manipulators use that entails making small trades at high prices during the final minutes of trading so as to give the impression that the stock performed really well.

title: High Earners, Not Rich Yet (HENRYs)
definition: This demographic group includes people who earn a high income of $250,000 to $500,000 per year but aren't wealthy yet.

title: High-Deductible Health Plan (HDHP)
definition: The term high-deductible health plan (HDHP) refers to a health insurance plan with a sizable deductible for medical expenses. An HDHP usually has a larger annual deductible (often four figures) than a typical health plan but charges lower monthly premiums. The minimum deductible varies from year to year. The IRS defines an HDHP as one with a deductible of at least $1,600 for individuals and $3,200 for families in 2024, or $1,650 and $3,300, respectively, in 2025.

title: High-Frequency Trading (HFT)
definition: High-frequency trading (HFT) leverages advanced computer programs and sophisticated algorithms to execute vast numbers of orders in mere fractions of a second. These algorithms rapidly analyze numerous markets and respond to varying market conditions to execute trades efficiently. Having the fastest execution speeds allows traders and financial institutions to capitalize on minimal price discrepancies for higher profitability, which distinguishes HFT through elevated turnover rates and order-to-trade ratios.

title: High-Low Index
definition: The high-low index assesses market trends by comparing the number of stocks reaching their 52-week highs to those hitting their 52-week lows. It serves as a tool for investors and traders to gauge the prevailing market direction, particularly for major indices like the S&P 500. A high reading above 50 indicates a bullish market trend, while a reading below indicates a bearish trend.

title: High-Low Method
definition: Incost accounting, the high-low method is a way of attempting to separate out fixed and variable costs given a limited amount of data. The high-low method involves taking the highest level of activity and the lowest level of activity and comparing thetotal costs at each level.

title: High Minus Low (HML)
definition: High Minus Low (HML), also called the value premium, is one of the three factors in theFama-French three-factor model, developed by economists Eugene Fama and Kenneth French to explain stock returns.

title: High-Net-Worth Individuals (HNWI)
definition: A high-net-worth individual (HNWI) is generally defined as someone with more than $1 million in highly liquid assets.

title: High-Ratio Loan
definition: A loan-to-value (LTV) ratio typically represents the amount of amortgagecompared to the property's value. It measures how much risk a prospective homebuyer represents based on the mortgage amount and the appraised value of the property. Lower LTVs are better in the eyes of lenders, but they require borrowers to come up with larger down payments.

title: High-Speed Data Feed
definition: High-speed data feeds transmit data such as price quotes and yields without delays and are used inhigh-frequency trading(HFT) forreal-timedata analysis.

title: High Street Bank
definition: High street banks are large retail banks in the U.K. that offer various services to individuals and small businesses. High street banks are major, widespread institutions such as those found in the main commercial sector of a town or city. They offer everyday banking services such as deposit accounts and credit facilities to consumers and businesses. People generally refer to high street banks as such in order to differentiate them from other institutions such as investment banks. The term originated in the United Kingdom, where high street is commonly used as the British equivalent of Main Street.

title: High-Water Mark
definition: A high-water mark is the highest value an investment fund or account has reached. This term is often used in the context of fund manager compensation, which is performance-based. The high-water mark ensures the manager does not get paid large sums for poor performance. If the manager loses money over a period, he must get the fund above the high-water mark before receiving a performance bonus from the assets under management (AUM).

title: High-Yield Bond
definition: High-yield bonds, often referred to asjunk bonds, are debt securities issued by corporations that offer higher interest rates due to their lower credit ratings compared toinvestment-gradebonds. While they have the potential to provide attractive yields, these bonds also involve greater risks, including a higher likelihood of default. Typically, high-yield bonds are issued by startups or capital-intensive firms with substantial debt ratios. Some high-yield bonds are known as "fallen angels," as they once held higher credit ratings before being downgraded.

title: High-Yield Bond Spread
definition: A high-yield bond spread measures the difference in yields between high-yield (junk) bonds and safer benchmarks such as investment-grade corporate or government bonds, usually expressed in percentage orbasis points.

title: Higher Education Act of 1965 (HEA)
definition: The Higher Education Act of 1965 (HEA) is a law designed to provide financial assistance to post-secondary school students and strengthen the educational resources of the United States' colleges and universities. The HEA increased the amount of federal money given to post-secondary school institutions, developed scholarship programs, provided low-interest loans to students, and founded the National Teachers Corps.

title: Highest In, First Out (HIFO)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Highly Compensated Employee
definition: A highly compensated employee (HCE) is defined by the IRS as someone who either owns more than 5% of a company or earns above a specified compensation threshold.For 2025, this threshold is $160,000.It can affect 401(k) contribution limits and compliance with IRS nondiscrimination rules. Being classified as an HCE can influence retirement planning strategies, potential tax obligations, and how much an employee can contribute or benefit from employer-sponsored retirement plans.

title: Highly Leveraged Transaction (HLT)
definition: A highly leveraged transaction (HLT) is a bank loan to a company that has a large amount of debt. They were popularized in the 1980s as a way to finance buyouts, acquisitions, orrecapitalizations.

title: Hikkake Pattern
definition: The hikkake pattern is a technical analysis tool that traders use to anticipate short-term market movements. It involves setups where prices initially move downward or upward before reversing, often leading to a breakout. This pattern works by exploiting trader expectations and price behavior, helping traders make more informed decisions. The pattern originated as a recognized trading strategy and is valued for its ability to signal potential market reversals and guide short-term trading strategies.

title: Hindenburg Omen
definition: The Hindenburg Omen is a technical indicator designed to signal the increased probability of a stock market crash.

title: Hindsight Bias
definition: Hindsight bias is the tendency to see events as predictable after they happen, making people think they "knew it all along." In investing, it can drive overconfidence and bad decisions that lead to losses, so it helps to document your assumptions and reasoning at the time to review outcomes more honestly later.

title: HIPAA Waiver of Authorization
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hire Purchase Agreement
definition: The term "hire purchase" refers to a financial arrangement for buying expensive goods and services. With a hire purchase agreement, the buyer makes a down payment and pays the remaining balance plus interest in installments.

title: Hiring Freeze
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Histogram
definition: A histogram graphically represents data distribution, showing the frequency of numerical data within specified intervals. The histogram condenses a data series into an easily interpreted visual by taking many data points and grouping them into logical ranges or bins similar in appearance to abar graph.

title: Historic Pricing
definition: Historic pricing is a unit pricing method used to calculate the value of anassetusing the lastvaluationpoint calculated. Historic pricing is used when the value of an asset does not update in real time.

title: Historic Structure
definition: A historic structure is a sub-category of a historic property as designated by the National Register of Historic Places, referred to as the National Register. In casual conversation, a historic structure refers to a building or other structure, such as a bridge, mine, canal, ship, highway, or locomotive, that is significant because of its link to an important period in the past, but the official designation distinguishes a structure as being distinct from a human shelter.

title: Historical Cost
definition: A historical cost is a measure of value that's used in accounting. The value of an asset on the balance sheet is recorded at its original cost when it's acquired by the company. The historical cost method is used for fixed assets in the United States undergenerally accepted accounting principles(GAAP).

title: Historical Returns
definition: Historical returns are often associated with the past performance of a security or index, such as theS&P 500.Analystsreview historical return data when trying to predict future returns or to estimate how a security might react to a particular situation, such as a drop in consumer spending. Historical returns can also be useful when estimating where future points of data may fall in terms ofstandard deviations.

title: Historical Volatility (HV)
definition: Historical volatility (HV) is a statistical measure of thedispersionof returns for a security orindexover a period of time. It is usually calculated by determining thestandard deviationof the asset from itsaverage price.

title: Hit the Bid
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC)
definition: HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC) is the tax authority of the U.K. government. The agency is responsible for collecting taxes, payingchild benefits, regulating tax and customs laws, and enforcing the payment ofminimum wageby employers.

title: Hoarding
definition: Hoarding is the practice of buying and storing large quantities of a commodity, often to sell later at higher prices or to avoid future shortages. Hoarding can disrupt supply, push prices up, and sometimes lead to inflation or economic instability. Many governments have laws that limit hoarding to protect consumers and keep markets stable—especially in emergencies. While hoarding focuses on physical goods, many people compare it to investing, and over time, investing in stocks is more reliable when building long-term wealth.

title: Hobby Loss
definition: A hobby loss occurs when the IRS determines that your business activities are primarily for personal pleasure rather than generating profit. In such cases, expenses exceeding your hobby income are non-deductible.

title: Hockey Stick Chart
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: HODL
definition: HODL, originally a typo for "hold," has morphed into an investment strategy acronym for "hold on for dear life," echoing a long-term commitment among cryptocurrency enthusiasts.

title: Hodrick-Prescott (HP) Filter
definition: The Hodrick-Prescott (HP) filter refers to adata-smoothingtechnique. The HP filter is commonly applied during analysis to remove short-term fluctuations associated with the business cycle. Removal of these short-term fluctuations reveals long-term trends. This can help with economic or other forecasting associated with thebusiness cycle.

title: Holacracy
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hold
definition: A hold recommendation on a stock is advice from analysts suggesting that investors neither buy nor sell the stock, anticipating that it will perform similarly to other companies in the same sector. If you're wondering whether to buy, sell, or hold onto a stock, understanding a hold recommendation can clarify your decision by indicating that the stock is not expected to outperform or underperform its peers in the near term.

title: Hold Harmless Claus
definition: A hold harmless clause, also known as a hold harmless agreement or hold harmless provision, is a clause in a legal contract absolving one party of legal liability for any injuries or damages suffered by another party. It ensures that one party cannot hold the other party legally responsible for any risks incurred from services provided.

title: Holdco
definition: A holdco, orholding company, is a firm thatcontrolsor influences other companies by owning enough of their voting stock to guide decisions. It earns income primarily from dividends paid by the companies it owns and often benefits from limited liability and structural flexibility. Holdcos are common in industries like real estate and banking, and this article covers examples and important considerations for how they operate.

title: Holder of Record
definition: A holder of record is the person or entity whose name is officially listed as the owner of a financial asset, such as a stock or bond. Being the holder of record is important because it gives you the right to receive dividends, interest payments, and voting notices. It also means your name appears in the company’s shareholder register, which serves as the official record of ownership.

title: Holding Company
definition: A holding company controls subsidiary firms through majority stock ownership.

title: Holding Company Depository Receipt (HOLDR)
definition: A holding company depository receipt (HOLDR) was a security that allowed investors to buy and sell a basket ofstocksin a single transaction. Likeexchange-traded funds(ETFs), HOLDRs allowed investors to trade stocks in a specificindustry, sector, or group. ETFs, however, provide a more efficient and flexible structure for investors and issuers.

title: Holding Costs
definition: Holding costs are those associated with storinginventorythat remains unsold. These costs are one component of total inventory costs, along with ordering and shortage costs.

title: Holding Period
definition: A holding period is the time between buying and selling an investment. It plays an important role in determining capital gains taxes. Investments held for a short-term period are taxed differently than those held over longer periods. You can calculate the holding period and measure your profit or loss using a formula that considers income earned and price changes relative to the initial investment.

title: Holding Period Return (Yield)
definition: Holding period return measures the total return on an investment, expressed as a percentage, by comparing the gains and income earned during the period the asset was held. It's an essential metric for investors as it includes capital appreciation, dividends, and interest, enabling comparison across different investment timelines. Here’s how you can calculate and use it effectively for your financial strategy.

title: Holding the Market
definition: "Holding the market" is the deliberate practice of placing active or pending orders for a security in a market where the price is dropping in an attempt to "hold" the price of the security steady or to create an artificialfloorin the security. This practice is outlawed in most instances, except when a broker or other party is mandated to keep the price of a security steady; this is only done in rare cases where there isn't enoughmarket depthto hold the price.

title: Holdings
definition: Holdings are the assets in an investment portfolio. An individual or entity, like amutual fund, can own the portfolio.

title: Holdover Tenant
definition: A holdover tenant stays in a property after their lease has expired. If the landlord continues to accept rent payments, the holdover tenant can continue to legally occupy the property, and state laws and court rulings determine the length of the holdover tenant’s new rental term. If the landlord does not accept further rent payments, the tenant is considered to be trespassing, and if they do not promptly move out, an eviction may be necessary. Specific laws vary by state in regards to how cases involving holdover tenants are handled.

title: Holdovers
definition: In finance, the term "holdovers" refers to transactions—usuallychecks—that have not yet been processed. In most cases today, the period of time in which checks are held as holdovers typically does not exceed one business day.

title: Hollowing Out
definition: Hollowing out is the deterioration of a country’smanufacturingsector when producers opt for low-cost facilities overseas. Taking away these jobs has helped to concentratewealthamong the very wealthy,hollow out the middle class, and increase the number of working-class and lower-class households.

title: Hollywood Stock Exchange (HSX): Meaning, Pros And Cons, Example
definition: The Hollywood Stock Exchange (HSX) is an onlineprediction marketin which "investors" bet on the performance of various components of the entertainment industry. The bets are made using credits called Moviestocks, Starbonds, Celebstocks, TVStocks, Movie Funds, and various derivatives.

title: Holographic Will
definition: A holographic will is a handwritten will signed by the person making it that needs no witnesses.

title: Home
definition: A home is more than a physical structure where a person or household resides. It's also a legal primary residence; the place you live or intend to return to live. That legal status can affect tax liability, residency rules, and eligibility for state benefits. It often comes up in personal finance decisions like claiming housing-related tax deductions or credits.

title: Home Affordable Modification Program (HAMP)
definition: The Home Affordable Modification Program (HAMP) was aloan modificationprogram introduced by the federal government in 2009 to help struggling homeowners avoid foreclosure. The program's focus was to help homeowners who paid more than 31% of theirgross incometoward mortgage payments. The program expired at the end of 2016.

title: Home Affordable Refinance Program (HARP)
definition: The Home Affordable Refinance Program (HARP) was a program offered by theFederal Housing Finance Agencyto homeowners who own homes that are worth less than the outstanding balance on theloan.

title: Home Banking
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Home Bias
definition: The term home bias refers to the tendency for investors to invest the majority of their portfolio in domestic equities, ignoring the benefits of diversifying into foreign equities. This bias was originally believed to have arisen as a result of the extra difficulties associated with investing in foreign equities, such as legal restrictions and additional transaction costs.

title: Home Buyers' Plan (HBP)
definition: The Home Buyers’ Plan (HBP) is a Canadian program that allows individuals withregistered retirement savings plans(RRSPs) to use up to CAD $35,000 of retirement plan holdings as a loan for a home purchase.

title: Home Country Bias
definition: Home country bias refers to investors' tendency to favor companies from their own country over those from other countries or regions. The tendency to invest in our own backyard is not unusual or surprising; it is a worldwide phenomenon, and certainly not unique to U.S. investors. This bias is also understandable because we are inclined to recognize and value domestic brands.

title: Home Equity
definition: Home equity is the difference between the amount you owe on amortgageand what the home is worth. It's essentially what you own in a home. The amount of equity in a house can grow over time as you make payments and the property's value increases.

title: Home Equity Conversion Mortgage (HECM)
definition: A home equity conversion mortgage (HECM) is a type of reverse mortgage that allows seniors 62 and older to convert the equity in their homes into cash for expenses, retirement income, or home repairs.

title: Home-Equity Loan
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Home Inspection
definition: A home inspection is an examination of the condition and safety of a piece of real estate, often conducted when the home is being sold.

title: Home Market Effect
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Home Modification
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Home Mortgage
definition: A home mortgage is a loan given by abank,mortgage company, or otherfinancial institutionfor the purchase of a residence—a primary residence, a secondary residence, or an investment residence—in contrast to a piece of commercial or industrial property. In a home mortgage, the owner of the property (the borrower) transfers thetitleto thelenderon the condition that the title will be transferred back to the owner once the final loan payment has been made and other terms of the mortgage have been met.

title: Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA)
definition: The Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) is a federal law requiring mortgage lenders to keep key information regarding their lending practices. Lenders must submit these records to regulatory authorities. Established in 1975, the HMDA was implemented by theFederal Reservethrough Regulation C.In 2011, the rule-writing authority of Regulation C was transferred to theConsumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB).

title: Home Mortgage Interest Deduction
definition: The mortgage interest deduction is a commonitemized deductionthat can reduce homeowners' taxable income by allowing them to deduct the interest paid on any loan used to build, purchase, or make improvements on their residence.

title: Home Office
definition: A home office is a space designated in a person's residence for official business purposes. It provides a space for those that are self-employed or work remotely for an employer.

title: Home Office Expense
definition: Home office expenses are expenses incurred from the operation of a business or the performance of employment-related activities within a primary residence.

title: Home Warranty
definition: A home warranty is a residential service contract that helps pay for repairs or replacements of home systems and appliances for a set period, separate fromhomeowners' insurance.

title: Homemade Dividends
definition: Homemade dividends are a form ofinvestment incomegenerated from the sale of a portion of an individual's investmentportfolio. These assets differ from the traditionaldividendsthat a company’s board of directors distributes to certain classes of shareholders.

title: Homemade Leverage
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Homeowners Association (HOA)
definition: Homeowners Associations (HOAs) play a crucial role in managing the rules and maintenance of properties within subdivisions, planned communities, and condo buildings. Each homeowner within an HOA jurisdiction becomes a member, binding them to its rules and fees. Typically managed by a board of directors, HOAs may restrict members' property usage, yet also offer community benefits, from amenities like pools and tennis courts to organized maintenance and landscaping.

title: Homeowners Association Fee (HOA Fee)
definition: A homeowners association (HOA) fee is a monthly charge paid by some residential property owners, either monthly or yearly, to theirhomeowners associations (HOAs). These fees help fund the maintenance, repair, and improvement of shared areas within a community.

title: Homeowners Insurance
definition: Homeowners insurance is vital for protecting one of your most significant investments—your home. This type of property insurance safeguards against losses and damages to your residence, covering everything from your belongings to liability for accidents occurring on your property.

title: Homeowners Protection Act
definition: The Homeowners Protection Act of 1998 is a law designed to reduce the unnecessary payment ofprivate mortgage insurance (PMI)by homeowners who may no longer be required to pay it. The Homeowners Protection Act covers all private, residential mortgages purchased after July 29, 1999.

title: Homestead Exemption
definition: A homestead exemption helps protect a home from creditors after a homeowner's spouse dies or duringbankruptcyand also reducesproperty taxes.

title: Homo Economicus
definition: Homo economicus is a theoretical abstraction that describes a rational human being. As such, neoclassical economic theories represent people as ideal decision-makers with complete rationality, perfect access to information, and consistent, self-interested goals. Modern behavioral economists and those who study neuroeconomics, however, have demonstrated that human beings are, in fact, not rational in their decision-making. Rationality should dictate that the rational business person should use profits from their business to live a fairly frugal existence but that is not always the case.

title: Homogeneous Expectations
definition: Inmodern portfolio theory, homogeneous expectations refer to the assumption that all investors expect the same results and make identical choices in a given situation. It posits that investors are rational actors and are not influenced by anything but the facts of the matter at hand. Critics have questioned that premise, arguing that people and investors are not always rational and have different perceptions and goals that impact their thought processes.

title: Homoskedastic
definition: Homoskedasticity is a statistical condition indicating that the variability of errors or residuals in a regression model is uniform across all levels of an independent variable.

title: Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited (HKEx)
definition: Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited (HKEX) is a publicly tradedholding companybased in Hong Kong. HKEX is one of the largest market operators in the world. Established in 2000, its subsidiaries include theHong Kong Stock Exchange (HKG). HKEx also manages four clearing houses in Hong Kong as well as theLondon Metal Exchange (LME), making it an important institution in the global financial markets.

title: Hong Kong Interbank Offered Rate (HIBOR)
definition: The Hong Kong Interbank Offered Rate (HIBOR) sets the short-term interest rates for interbank lending in the Hong Kong banking sector.

title: Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA)
definition: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) was established to manage the monetary policy for a financial system that is separate from China's.

title: Hong Kong Monetary Authority Investment Portfolio (HKMA)
definition: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority Investment Portfolio is a sovereign wealth fund that invests primarily in the bonds and equities of nations within the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development.

title: Hong Kong SAR, China
definition: Hong Kong is aspecial administrative region (SAR)of China, established after the 1997 handover from British to Chinese rule, and it operates under a separate legal, administrative, and judicial system from the mainland. Known as one of the world's leading financial hubs, Hong Kong consistently ranks among the top global financial centers. Its free market economy, international financial market, and role as a gateway to China make it a central player in global business and investment.

title: Hong Kong Stock Exchange (HKG) .HK
definition: The Hong Kong Stock Exchange (HKG) is astock exchangebased in Hong Kong. Known formally as the Stock Exchange Hong Kong, the Hong Kong Stock Exchange is one of the world's largest securities markets by market capitalization. It is owned and operated as a subsidiary ofHong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited (HKEX), which was established as a holding company in 2000.The exchange lists different securities, including equities and debt securities among others.

title: Honorarium
definition: Honoraria are voluntary payments for services where no fees are traditionally expected, often seen in academic or conference settings. These payments, used to cover costs for guest speakers or volunteers, are considered taxable income. For instance, a speaker at a conference might receive an honorarium for travel expenses.

title: Hook Reversal
definition: Hook reversals are short-termcandlestickpatterns that predict a reversal in the trend's direction. The pattern occurs when a candlestick has a higher low and a lower high than the previous session's candlestick. This pattern differs fromengulfingpatterns in that the size difference between the first and second bar's body can be relatively small.

title: Hope Credit
definition: The Hope Scholarship Tax Credit was a nonrefundable higher education tax credit​​​​​​​ that was replaced in 2009 by the American Opportunity Tax Credit.

title: Hope Now Alliance
definition: Renewable energy certificates (RECs) are issued when electricity is generated from renewable energy sources. They are sold separately from the actual electricity produced.

title: Horizon Analysis
definition: Horizon analysis compares the projected discounted returns of a security or investment portfolio’s total returns over several time frames orinvestment horizons.

title: Horizontal Acquisition
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Horizontal Analysis
definition: Horizontal analysis is used infinancial statement analysisto compare historical data, such as ratios, or line items, over a number of accounting periods. Horizontal analysis can either use absolute comparisons or percentage comparisons, where the numbers in each succeeding period are expressed as a percentage of the amount in thebaselineyear, with the baseline amount being listed as 100%. This is also known asbase-year analysis.

title: Horizontal Channel
definition: Horizontal channels aretrend linesthat connect variable pivot highs and lows to show the price contained between the upper line ofresistanceand the lower line ofsupport. A horizontal channel is also known as a price range or sideways trend.

title: Horizontal Equity
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Horizontal Integration
definition: Horizontal integration involves acquiring or merging with businesses at the same level within an industry, strengthening a firm's market presence. Unlikevertical integration, which involves expansion into different stages of the production process, horizontal integration focuses on increasing market power and reducing competition. This strategy can boost revenues, create economies of scale, and present new market opportunities.

title: Horizontal Line
definition: Intechnical analysis, a horizontal line is often drawn on a price chart to highlight areas ofsupport or resistance.

title: Horizontal Market
definition: A horizontal market isdiversifiedso that the products created are able to meet the needs of more than one industry. A horizontal market is one in which the output good or service is widely used and in wide demand, and so the producers bear little risk in demand for their output. Producers do, however, typically face a great amount of competition within the industry.

title: Horizontal Merger
definition: A horizontal merger occurs when two companies at the same level in an industry combine to improve market efficiency and reduce competition.

title: Horizontal Spread
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Horizontal Well
