definition: A horizontal well is a type of directional drilling technique where an oil or gas well is dug at an angle of at least eighty degrees to a vertical wellbore. This technique has become increasingly common and productive in recent years.Operators use it to retrieve oil and natural gas in situations in which the shape of the reservoir is abnormal or difficult to access.

title: Hospital Insurance Trust Fund
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hospital Revenue
definition: A hospital revenue bond is a type ofmunicipal bondthat finances the construction of new facilities or upgrades for existing hospitals and is secured by the revenues that hospitals receive in the course of their normal operations.

title: Hostile Bid
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hostile Takeover
definition: A hostile takeover happens when an entity takes control of a company against the wishes of the company's management. It's an acquisition strategy requiring that the entity acquire and control more than 50% of the voting shares issued by the company.

title: Hostile Takeover Bid
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hot Hand
definition: The "hot hand" is the notion that because one has had a string of successes, an individual or entity is more likely to have continued success. For example, if one flipped a (fair) coin and guessed correctly that it would land on heads three times in a row, it might be said that they have a "hot hand." Under such circumstances, a person believes that their odds of guessing which side the coin will land on next are greater than the 50% they actually are. When there is a series of failures, the same concept works as the "cold hand."

title: Hot Issue
definition: In finance, a “hot issue” is aninitial public offering (IPO)that attracts unusually high public demand and is oftenoversubscribed, meaning investor orders exceed the shares available. These deals frequently drawspeculativebuying, often around high-tech or other glamorous companies, because investors hope for a first-day pop rather than focusing on long-term fundamentals.

title: Hot IPO
definition: A hot IPO is aninitial public offeringwith a remarkable buzz and demand, often leading to oversubscription. Investors are drawn to these highly anticipated IPOs due to the potential for immediate gains as share prices tend to surge upon market debut. Companies typically partner with banks to underwrite hot IPOs to determine share pricing, quantity, and efficiently handle the influx of investor interest.

title: Hot Money
definition: Hot money refers to capital that swiftly moves across countries and financial markets, seeking the highest short-term interest rates. This flow of money can influence a nation's exchange rates and balance of payments significantly. Investors strategically move funds to take advantage of favorable rates, impacting economic stability and growth.

title: Hot Waitress Economic Index
definition: The hot waitress economic index is an offensive and dubious economic indicator that counts the number of attractive people working as servers.

title: Hot Wallet
definition: Hot wallets, constantly connected to the internet, are used for storing, sending, and receiving digital currencies. These wallets provide ease of access but pose heightened security risks. Understanding their function and how to enhance their security can better safeguard your cryptocurrency assets.

title: Hotelling's Theory
definition: Hotelling's Theory, also known as Hotelling's Rule, is an economic principle that suggests owners of nonrenewable resources like oil or minerals will exploit these resources only if they can achieve a return greater than that of financial instruments such as U.S. Treasury bonds. This theory helps predict how resource prices fluctuate based on interest rates, providing insights into why resource owners might choose to delay or accelerate extraction. Hotelling's rule was named after American statistician Harold Hotelling.

title: House Call
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: House Maintenance Requirement
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: House Money Effect
definition: The house money effect is a behavioral finance idea that describes how people often take bigger risks by using their profits rather than their original money. In investing, this happens when someone reinvests gains and feels more comfortable making risky trades because it “feels” like they are playing with extra money. This can affect decision-making by increasing risk-taking. While it can sometimes lead to higher returns, it can also cause investors to make careless choices and lose their earlier gains.

title: House Poor
definition: "House poor" is a term used to describe a person who spends a large proportion of their total income on homeownership, includingmortgagepayments, property taxes, maintenance, and utilities. Individuals in this situation are short of cash for discretionary items and tend to have trouble meeting other financial obligations, such as vehicle payments.

title: House Price Index (HPI)
definition: The House Price Index (HPI) provides an indicator of housing price trends by analyzing data on single-family home purchases.

title: Household Employee
definition: A household employee, also known as a household worker, is an individual who is paid to provide a service at or within the place of residence of their employer.

title: Household Expenses
definition: Householdexpensesareeveryday living costsdivided by the number of people in a home. These include rent, food, utilities, and clothing. Total costs are split among household members to find each person's share. Consider household expenses when creating a personal budget.

title: Household Income
definition: Household income is the total amount of income earned by all individuals living under the same roof.

title: Housing and Economic Recovery Act (HERA)
definition: The Housing and Economic Recovery Act (HERA) was drafted to address the fallout from thesubprime mortgage crisisof 2008. HERA allowed theFederal Housing Administration (FHA)to guarantee up to $300 billion in new 30-year fixed-rate mortgages forsubprime borrowers. To participate,lenderswere required to write down the balances onprincipalloans up to 90% of their currentappraised value.

title: Housing Authority Bond
definition: Housing authority bonds, or housing bonds, are issued by a state or local government agency to help finance the construction or rehabilitation of affordable rental housing. Under certain programs, the proceeds from such bonds also may be used to help low-income people purchase homes. The interest earned by investors on housing authority bonds is exempt from federal taxes, and may also be exempt from state and localincome taxes.

title: Housing Bonds
definition: Housing bonds are debt securities, a variation of municipalrevenue bonds, issued by state or local governments to raise money for affordable housing development projects.

title: Housing Bubble
definition: A housing bubble is a rapid increase in home prices. This often happens with deregulated financing when demand rises and speculation increases, causing prices to eventually fall. Limited supply and speculation can drive a housing bubble, as seen in the U.S. during the 2000s. When the bubble burst, many homeowners experienced negative equity and foreclosures.

title: Housing Expense Ratio
definition: A housing expense ratio is the percentage of your pre-tax income that goes toward your housing expenses. Lenders often use the housing expense ratio, also called a front-end ratio, when they decide whether to approve you for a mortgage. You get this number by dividing your housing expenses by your income and multiplying by 100.

title: Housing Market Index
definition: The NAHB/Wells Fargo Housing Market Index (HMI) is a monthly sentiment survey of members of the National Association of Home Builders (NAHB). The index measures sentiment among builders of U.S. single-family homes, and is a widely watched gauge of the U.S. housing sector. Since housing represents is a large capital investment and spurs additional consumer spending on appliances and furnishings, housing market indices help to monitor the overall health of the economy.

title: Housing Starts
definition: Housing starts refers to the beginning of construction on new residential housing units. New housing is a big-ticket capital good that spurs additional consumer spending on appliances and furniture. It is a key economic indicator that's closely watched by financial markets participants.

title: Housing Unit
definition: A housing unit is a separate living space for individuals or families, within a larger structure such as an apartment building or house. Housing units provide a space for occupants to live, eat, and sleep independently from other residents. Most new housing units are constructed in locations that require building permits.

title: Howey Test
definition: The Howey Test evaluates whether transactions qualify as "investment contracts" under U.S. securities laws, focusing on the expectation of profits from the efforts of others. Its application is crucial for blockchain and cryptocurrency projects, impacting how these digital assets are classified and regulated.

title: HSA Custodian
definition: An HSA custodian is anInternal Revenue Service(IRS)-approved financial institution that opens and holds ahealth savings account(HSA) and manages its administration.

title: Hub and Spoke Structure
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hubbert Curve
definition: The Hubbert curve is a method for predicting the likelyproduction rateof any finite resource over time. When plotted on a chart, the result resembles a symmetricalbell-shaped curve.

title: Hubbert's Peak Theory
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hubris
definition: Hubris is the characteristic of excessive confidence or arrogance, which leads a person to believe that they may do no wrong. The overwhelming pride caused by hubris is often considered a flaw in character.

title: HUD-1 Form
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: HUF (Hungarian Forint)
definition: The term Hungarianforint(HUF) refers to the official and nationalcurrencyof Hungary. The forint is issued and managed by Magyar Nemzeti Bank, the country'scentral bank. The forint is represented in the international market by the currency code HUF and the symbol Ft. The currency was introduced in Hungary in 1946.Banknotes are issued in denominations between Ft500 and Ft20,000 while the bank mints coins ranging in value from Ft5 to Ft200.

title: Hulbert Rating
definition: A Hulbert rating is a score that tracks the performance of aninvestment newsletterover time. Investment newsletters are paid subscriptions that can offer investors a variety of market-related information, such as trading strategies, stock recommendations, and economic commentary. Some newsletters focus on specific industries or types of trading, such as options trading, investing in utilities, precious metals investing, or cryptocurrency investing. Hulbert Ratings, LLC assigns Hulbert ratings and encourages investors to judge a newsletter by its long-termperformance adjusted for risk.

title: Hull-White Model
definition: The Hull-White model is a single-factor interest model used to priceinterest rate derivatives. The Hull-White model assumes that short rates have a normal distribution and that the short rates are subject tomean reversion. Volatility is thus likely to be low when short rates are near zero, which is reflected in a larger mean reversion in the model.

title: Human Capital
definition: Human capital is the value employees bring to a company that translates to productivity or profitability.

title: Human Development Index (HDI)
definition: The Human Development Index (HDI) ranks countries based on key indicators of well-being, such as health, education, and income levels.

title: Human-Life Approach
definition: The human-life approach is a method of calculating the amount oflife insurancea family would need based on the financial loss they would incur if the insured person in the family were to pass away today. It's based on age and income, and retirement plans.

title: Human Resource Planning (HRP)
definition: Human resource planning (HRP) is the process of determining the right number of skilled employees that an organization needs to meet current and future business needs.

title: Human Resources (HR)
definition: The Human Resources (HR) department is vital in steering a company’s recruitment, training, and benefits administration. By fostering a positive work environment and ensuring labor law compliance, HR enhances employee productivity and retention, contributing significantly to organizational success.

title: Humped Yield Curve
definition: A humped yield curve is a relatively rare type of yield curve that results when the interest rates on medium-term fixed income securities are higher than the rates of both long and short-term instruments. This unique, bell-shaped curve can provide insights into market expectations and economic conditions. It can indicate periods of uncertainty or transition in the economy.

title: Hundredweight (Cwt)
definition: A hundredweight (CWT) is a unit of weight used for measuring certain agricultural commodities.

title: Hurdle Rate
definition: A hurdle rate is the minimum return needed for a manager or investor to accept a project. It's important when companies or investors make important decisions like pursuing a specific project. Riskier projects generally have higher hurdle rates than those with less risk.

title: Hurricane Deductible
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hybrid Annuity
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hybrid ARM
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hybrid Fund
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hybrid Security
definition: A hybrid security blends the features of two financial instruments into one asset, usually combining aspects of debt and equity. Common examples include convertible bonds, which offer the stability of bonds with an option to convert into stock, and preference shares, providing fixed dividends with potential equity conversion. These securities offer diverse investment opportunities but carry unique risks, such as market volatility and repayment uncertainty.

title: Hydraulic Fracturing
definition: Hydraulic fracturing, commonly known as "fracking", is a process that injects high-pressure liquid into an oil- or gas-bearing rock formation to create fractures. This pressure typically yields improved flows, making it useful for oil and gas firms seeking more economical production in areas that would otherwise produce low-flow wells.

title: Hydrocarbon
definition: Hydrocarbons, organic compounds made of hydrogen and carbon, are fundamental to global energy, forming the backbone ofcrude oil,natural gas, andcoal. They play a vital role in energy production, which is central to powering modern economies. Beyond fuels, they are foundational in manufacturing plastics, solvents, and other materials. Their extraction and use, however, come with significant environmental impacts, driving a shift towards cleaner alternatives. Understanding hydrocarbons and their uses highlights both their critical role in global energy and the urgent need for sustainable solutions.

title: Hyperdeflation
definition: Hyperdeflation is an exceptionally swift and large decrease in the prices of goods and services. It is extremely rare. Large increases in money's purchasing power correspond to hyperdeflation. However, should hyperdeflation occur, spending by consumers and businesses could slow dramatically.

title: Hyperinflation
definition: Hyperinflation occurs when a country'sinflationrate surpasses 50% each month, leading to uncontrollable price surges that erode purchasing power and destabilize economies. While rare in developed nations, historical episodes in countries like Yugoslavia, Zimbabwe, and Hungary reveal how excessive money supply and demand-pull inflation can trigger such crises. Understanding these dynamics is essential for protecting your finances.

title: Hyperledger
definition: Hyperledger is a global enterprise blockchain project that offers the necessary framework, standards, guidelines, and tools to buildopen-sourceblockchainsand related applications for use across various industries. Hyperledger's projects include a variety of enterprise-ready permissioned blockchain platforms and plug-and-play options.

title: Hyperledger Composer
definition: Hyperledger Composer is a set ofopen source toolsthat allows business owners, operators, and developers a way to createblockchainapplications andsmart contractsaimed at solving business problems and/or improving operational efficiencies. It is an example of a commercial application ofblockchain-as-a-service(BaaS). Hyperledger Composer is one of the severalHyperledgerprojects hosted by The Linux Foundation in collaboration with corporate members.﻿﻿﻿﻿

title: Hyperledger Explorer
definition: Hyperledger Explorer is an open sourceblockchainutility module that allows users to create a user-friendly web-based application, with which a user can view, initiate, organize or query various artifacts and developments that form an integral part of the blockchain network. It is to be used specifically on deployments of blockchains created using the Hyperledger umbrella.

title: Hyperledger Fabric
definition: Hyperledger Fabric is a blockchain platform designed for businesses to record and share data. It helps companies manage transactions privately and securely. It uses a modular design with plug-and-play parts, which means organizations can customize how the system works to fit their needs. Because of this flexibility, private enterprises can use it for tasks like tracking supply chains, managing contracts, and improving trust between partners.

title: Hyperledger Iroha
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Hyperledger Sawtooth
definition: Hyperledger Sawtooth is anopen sourceproject under theHyperledgerumbrella, and works as an enterprise levelblockchainsystem used for creating and operatingdistributed ledgerapplications and networks particularly for use by enterprises.

title: Hypermarket
definition: A hypermarket is a retail store that combines a department store and a grocery supermarket. Often a very large establishment, hypermarkets offer a wide variety of products such as appliances, clothing, and groceries.

title: HyperText Markup Language (HTML)
definition: HTML, or HyperText Markup Language, is a powerful yet easy-to-understand language utilized globally for website creation. It consists of markup symbols or codes embedded in files for Internet display, instructing web browsers on rendering text and images. Continuously evolving to meet web demands, HTML underpins navigation through its hyperlink feature, enabling seamless browsing.

title: Hypothecation
definition: Hypothecation occurs when an asset is pledged as collateral to secure a loan. The asset owner does not give up title, possession, or ownership rights, such as income generated by the asset. However, the lender can seize the asset if the terms of the agreement are not met. Hypothecation is different from a mortgage, lien, or assignment.

title: Hypothesis Testing
definition: Hypothesis testing is a procedure designed to measure the strength of a hypothesis based on sample data.

title: Hysteresis
definition: Hysteresis in the field of economics refers to an event in the economy that persists even after the factors that led to that event have been removed or otherwise run their course. Hysteresis often occurs following extreme or prolonged economic events such as an economic crash orrecession. After a recession, for example, the unemployment rate may continue to increase despite growth in the economy and the technical end of the recession.

title: Iceberg Order
definition: Iceberg orders cleverly disguise large trades by splitting them into smaller limit orders, concealing the true size to prevent market disruptions. This trading strategy is essential for institutional investors aiming to manage large volumes without drawing attention. Visible orders in this system are a mere "tip of the iceberg," hinting at the sheer volume hidden beneath.

title: Ichimoku Cloud
definition: The Ichimoku Cloud is a technical analysis tool that provides insights into trend direction, momentum, and dynamic support/resistance levels, helping traders recognize bullish potential trades at a glance.

title: Ichimoku Kinko Hyo: Understanding Its 5 Key Components
definition: The Ichimoku Kinko Hyo is a comprehensivetechnical indicatorused to gauge marketmomentumand predict support/resistance areas. It was developed by a Japanese journalist to assess market trends quickly and its five core components are Tenkan-sen, Kijun-sen, Senkou Span A, Senkou Span B, and Chikou span. Despite its all-in-one capability, it's most valuable when used with other technical indicators.

title: ICON Cryptocurrency (ICX)
definition: ICON is a platform that is intended to facilitate the interactions of independent blockchains, also referred to as communities. Within the ICON platform, a community is defined as a network of nodes that follow a single governance system. (A node is a computer that connects to a cryptocurrency network.)

title: Ideation
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Identifiable Asset
definition: An identifiable asset is a tangible or intangibleassetwith afair valuethat can be measured at a point in time, with future benefits expected for the company. It matters in mergers and acquisitions because accurate valuation affects how a deal is priced and allocated on the balance sheet. Identifiable assets differ fromgoodwill, which cannot be separately measured as a specific asset.

title: Identity Theft
definition: Identity theft is a crime where someone uses another person’s identifying information to impersonate them in financial, legal, or administrative transactions.

title: Idiosyncratic Risk
definition: Idiosyncratic risk refers to the risks an asset possesses due to its unique characteristics or other qualities.

title: Understanding Idle Time: Impact on Business Productivity and Costs
definition: Idle time is paid time that an employee, or machine, is unproductive due to factors that can either be controlled or uncontrolled by management. It normally applies to full-time workers rather than consultants, who typically have to bill for every hour of their time.

title: Illiquid
definition: Illiquid assets are securities or properties that you can't easily sell or exchange for cash without incurring significant losses. These assets often experience low trading activity and wider bid-ask spreads, increasing their volatility and risk. They include real estate, antiques, and private company stocks, which are generally harder to sell quickly. Understanding the challenges of illiquidity can help you make informed decisions and avoid potential financial pitfalls.

title: Immediate Family
definition: The term “immediate family” commonly applies to an individual’s closest family members; however, the term has legal meanings for government or company policies. How the term is defined can affect whether a person can use paid or unpaid leave to care for a sick family member, attend a funeral, or access financial resources.

title: Immediate Or Cancel Order (IOC)
definition: An immediate or cancel order (IOC) is an order to buy or sell a security that shouldexecuteimmediately and cancel if any portion of the order remains unfilled. An IOC order is one of severaltime-in-forceorders that specify how long an order remains active and when an order should be canceled. Other such orders includefill or kill(FOK),all or none(AON), and good ‘till canceled (GTC). Most online trading platforms support placing IOC orders manually or integrating them into automated strategies.

title: Immediate Payment Annuity
definition: An immediate payment annuity is acontractbetween an individual and an insurance company that pays theannuitanta guaranteed income starting almost immediately. It differs from adeferred annuity, which begins payments at a future date chosen by the annuity owner. An immediate payment annuity is also known as a single-premium immediate annuity (SPIA), an income annuity, or simply an immediate annuity.

title: Immunization
definition: Immunization, also known as multi-period immunization, is a risk-mitigation strategy that matches the duration of assets and liabilities in order to minimize the impact ofinterest rateson net worth over time. It helps protect net worth and portfolio value. It often is used in large firms, pension funds, and individual investments.

title: Impact Investing
definition: Impact investing involves making investments to help create beneficial social or environmental effects while also generating financial gains. Thisinvestment strategycan involve differentasset classes, such as stocks, bonds, mutual funds, or microloans. The point of impact investing is to use money and investment capital for positive social results.

title: Impaired Asset: Meaning, Causes, How to Test, and How to Record
definition: An impaired asset is a company asset that has declined in value and is no longer worth its original cost.

title: Impaired Credit
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Impairment
definition: In accounting, impairment is an unexpected deterioration in an asset's ability to generate future economic benefits. It requires a write-down to prevent financial overstatement.

title: Impeachment: Meaning, Overview, and Examples
definition: Impeachment is the formal process that allows Congress to bring charges of "treason, bribery, or other high crimes and misdemeanors"against high-ranking civil officers such as the president. It's authorized by Article II, Section 4 of the U.S. Constitution.

title: Imperfect Competition
definition: Imperfect competition is a type of market where companies have some control over prices. This is in contrast toperfect competition, where many sellers don't influence prices. Common examples include monopolies and oligopolies. Imperfect competition is more common in the real world than perfect competition. This article explains the main ideas, examples, and history behind imperfect competition.

title: Imperfect Market
definition: Imperfect markets encompass all real-world economic markets that diverge from the theoretical framework of perfect competition, where numerous buyers and sellers interact with complete information. Unlike the perfect competition model, imperfect markets are characterized by limited competition, barriers to entry or exit, and influence over prices by individual buyers and sellers. This article delves into the various types of imperfect markets, their characteristics, and their broader economic consequences.

title: Implicit Cost
definition: An implicit cost is the financial gain passed up when a firm uses its assets.

title: Implied Authority
definition: Implied authority allows an agent to act on behalf of an organization in ways that are not explicitly stated but are understood to be necessary for carrying out their role.

title: Implied Contract
definition: An implied contract is a legally binding obligation that derives from the actions, conduct, or circumstances of one or more parties in an agreement. It has the same legal force as an express contract, which is a contract that is voluntarily entered into and agreed on verbally or in writing by two or more parties. The implied contract, on the other hand, is assumed to exist, but no written or verbal confirmation is necessary.

title: Implied Rate
definition: The implied rate represents the difference between thespot rateof an asset and its forward or futures rate, serving as a critical measure for comparing potential investment returns. It applies to a variety of securities and helps in evaluating the risk and return characteristics of options or futures contracts.

title: Implied Volatility (IV)
definition: Implied volatility reflects investors' perceptions of uncertainty or risk associated with the future movements of an asset.

title: Import
definition: An import is a good or service bought in one country that was produced in another. Imports are one component of a crucialinternational tradingsystem, with exports serving as the other. Each component has an impact on a country's balance of trade, which is the difference between the amount imported and the amount exported.

title: Import Duty
definition: Import duty is a tax collected onimportsand someexportsby a country's customs authorities. A good's value will usually dictate the import duty. Depending on the context, import duty may also be known as a customs duty, tariff, import tax, or import tariff.

title: Import Substitution Industrialization
definition: Import substitution industrialization (ISI) is an economic strategy where developing nations focus on reducing dependence on developed countries by nurturing domestic industries. This policy, prominent during the 20th century, sought to develop self-sufficient economies. While initially effective, ISI faced challenges in later decades, leading to a shift towards market-driven policies.

title: Impression
definition: An impression is a metric used to quantify the number of digital views or engagements of a piece of content, usually an advertisement, digital post, or web page. Impressions are also referred to as an "ad view."

title: Imprest
definition: An imprest is a cash account used by businesses to handle small, routine expenses efficiently. The account maintains a fixed balance, regularly replenished to preserve its function.

title: Impulse Wave Pattern
definition: An impulse wave pattern is an indication of a strong move in a financial asset's price coinciding with the main direction of the underlying trend. Impulse waves can refer to upward movements inuptrendsor downward movements indowntrends. The pattern is flexible across various timeframes and market conditions subject to three unbreakable rules.

title: Imputed Value
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Imputed Interest: Definition, Calculation, and Key Tax FAQs
definition: Imputed interest occurs when a lender charges no interest or an interest rate below market conditions, yet the IRS taxes the implied rate. This tax concept often applies to family loans and bonds sold below face value. Understanding imputed interest helps prevent tax avoidance by ensuring proper reporting and compliance with IRS regulations.

title: In-App Purchasing
definition: In-app purchasing refers to the buying of goods and services from inside an application on a mobile device, such as a smartphone or tablet. In-app purchases allow developers to provide their applications for free. The developer then advertises upgrades to the paid version, paid feature unlocks, special items for sale, or evenadsother apps and services to anyone who downloads the free version. This allows the developer to profit despite giving the basic app itself away for free.

title: In Escrow
definition: In financial transactions, the term "in escrow" indicates an item, such as money or property, is being held by athird partyuntil legal conditions have been met to transfer it. This transfer is usually done on behalf of a buyer and seller.

title: In-House
definition: In-house operations refer to activities a company handles internally using its own staff and resources rather thanoutsourcingto external providers. Unlike outsourcing, which relies on third parties, in-house work offers greater control, confidentiality, and alignment with company goals.

title: In-House Financing
definition: The term in-housefinancingrefers to financing that is provided directly to consumers by retailers or other firms. It allows people to purchase and finance goods and services directly from the seller. In-house financing eliminates the firm's reliance on third-party lenders in thefinancial sectorfor providing the customer with funds to complete a transaction. It's often used in the automotive and retail sectors for large purchases.

title: In-Service Withdrawal
definition: An in-service withdrawal occurs when an employee takes adistributionfrom a qualified, employer-sponsored retirement plan, such as a 401(k) account, without leaving the employ of their company.

title: In Specie
definition: The phrase "in specie" refers to transferring an asset in its current form instead of converting it into cash.In specieis a Latin phrase that is translated to English as in its actual form. Common assets transferred in specie include property, shares, ordividends. In some cases, an in specie transaction can have favorable tax implications compared to a cash transfer.

title: In the Money (ITM)
definition: In the money is an option with intrinsic value that can currently be cashed in at a profit.

title: Incentive Stock Options (ISOs)
definition: An incentive stock option (ISO) gives an employee the right to buy shares of company stock at a discounted price. The profit on qualified ISOs is usually taxed at the capital gains rate, not the higher rate for ordinary income. Non-qualified stock options (NSOs) are taxed as ordinary income. Generally, ISOs are awarded only to top management and highly-valued employees. ISOs are also called statutory or qualified stock options.

title: Incidence Rate
definition: An incidence rate is the rate at which new events occur in a given period.

title: Incidental Expenses (IE)
definition: Incidental expenses are gratuities and other minor costs that are incurred while conducting business. For example, an employee on a business trip who takes a taxi from the airport to a hotel will incur incidental expenses such as tips to the taxi driver and hotel staff.

title: Income
definition: Income is almost anything you receive in exchange for sales or services and most of it is taxable but there are a few exceptions.

title: Income Annuity
definition: An income annuity is a financial product that converts a lump sum payment into a stream of income, typically starting immediately after the contract is signed. Primarily used by retirees, income annuities provide a regular and dependable income stream, ensuring financial stability during retirement. These annuities guarantee periodic payments that continue for the life of the annuitant, offering peace of mind for those concerned about outliving their savings.

title: Income Approach
definition: The income approach, sometimes referred to as the income capitalization approach, is a type of real estate appraisal method that allows investors to estimate the value of a property based on the income the property generates. It’s used by taking the net operating income (NOI) of the rent collected and dividing it by thecapitalization rate.

title: Income Effect
definition: The income effect expresses how changes in relative market prices and income levels can impact consumption tendencies.

title: Income Elasticity of Demand
definition: Incomeelasticityof demand measures how consumer demand for a specific good reacts to changes inreal income. By examining this relationship using the formula—percent change in quantity demanded divided by percent change in income—one can discern whether a good is a necessity or a luxury. This concept is vital for businesses aiming to predict and respond to shifts in consumer purchasing behavior as income levels fluctuate.

title: Income from Operations (IFO)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Income Fund
definition: An income fund is a mutual fund or ETF designed to prioritize generating regular income over long-term capital growth. By investing in a mix of dividend-paying stocks, bonds, and other income-producing assets, these funds aim to offer investors reliable returns on a monthly or quarterly basis. They suit those who are seeking a steady income stream, like retirees, because they often include government, municipal, and corporate debt obligations known for their stability.

title: Income in Respect of a Decedent (IRD)
definition: Income in respect of a decedent (IRD) refers to untaxed income that adecedenthad earned or had a right to receive during their lifetime. Taxes on IRD are owed by the individual beneficiary or entity that inherits thisincome.

title: Income Inequality
definition: Income inequality describes the uneven distribution of income across a population, highlighting disparities not only in earnings but also in opportunities. It often mirrorswealthinequality, where assets and resources are unevenly allocated.

title: Income Per Capita
definition: Per capita income measures the average income per person in a given area and is determined by dividing the country's national income by its population. It's a metric used to assess standard of living and thequality of lifeof the population. It is limited by its inability to account for inflation, income disparity, poverty, wealth, or savings.

title: Income Property
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Income Smoothing
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Income Statement
definition: The income statement is a financial report that summarizes a company's financial performance over a specific accounting period. It includes a company's revenue, expenses, and net income.

title: Income Stock
definition: Income stocks are equity securities known for providing regulardividends, often with steady or increasing payouts. If you're looking for consistent income with less risk compared to growth stocks, income stocks might be right for you. They typically feature high yields that surpass the 10-year Treasury note rate and offer relatively low volatility. Investors, particularly older adults or those seeking a reliable income, favor income stocks for their consistent dividends.

title: Income Tax
definition: Income tax is integral to government revenue, imposed on income from individuals and businesses alike. Understanding how it's calculated and the types that exist is crucial. With varying systems at federal, state, and local levels, knowing these differences can optimize financial planning. Certain exemptions and deductions offer taxpayers potential savings, impacting what they ultimately owe.

title: Income Tax Payable
definition: Income tax payable is a liability reported for financial accounting purposes. It shows the amount that an organization expects to pay in income taxes within 12 months. It is reported in the current liabilities section on a company'sbalance sheet.

title: Incontestability Clause
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Incorporation
definition: Incorporation is the process of constituting a company, entity, or other organization as a legal corporation.

title: Incoterms
definition: Incoterms, or International Commercial Terms, are a set of standardized trade terms set by the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC) to define the roles of buyers and sellers in international (or sometimes domestic) shipment of goods.

title: Incremental Analysis
definition: Incremental analysis assists businesses in evaluating the financial impact of different decisions by assessing the changes in costs and revenues.

title: Incremental Capital Output Ratio (ICOR)
definition: The incremental capital output ratio (ICOR) assesses how much investment must generate a unit of economic growth, typically in terms ofGDP. Policymakers and economists use it to understand how efficiently investment drives growth, with a lower ICOR indicating that less investment is needed to produce additional output. ICOR has limitations, especially in developed economies where factors like technological change and labor productivity can complicate its interpretation. If a country invests $100 million and achieves $10 million in GDP growth, the ICOR would be 10, showing how much capital is needed for each unit of output.

title: Understanding Incremental Cost of Capital: Definition and Importance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Incremental Cash Flow: Definition, Formula, and Examples
definition: Incremental cash flow is the additional operatingcash flowthat an organization receives from taking on a new project. A positive incremental cash flow means that the company's cash flow will increase with the acceptance of the project. A positive incremental cash flow is a good indication that an organization should invest in a project.

title: Incremental Cost
definition: Incremental cost represents the additional expense incurred from producing one more unit of a product. Calculating these costs involves analyzing variable expenses, such as raw materials and direct labor, tied to increased production. By understanding incremental costs, companies can fine-tune efficiency, assess the profitability of business segments, and make informed production decisions.

title: Incumbency Certificate
definition: An incumbency certificate is an official document issued bya corporation or a limited liability company (LLC)that lists the names of itsdirectors,officers, and somekey shareholders.

title: Incumbent: Definition, Meanings in Contexts, and Examples
definition: The term "incumbent" refers to the holder of an office or post within a corporation or government.

title: Incurred But Not Reported (IBNR)
definition: Incurred But Not Reported (IBNR) are reserves used by insurers to manage claims involving events that have occurred but are not yet reported. Actuaries calculate these reserves to allocate funds for potential losses, accounting for delays in reporting due to various factors like bureaucratic processes. Understanding IBNR is crucial because it affects the financial health and performance of an insurance company, particularly in scenarios involving natural disasters, occupational diseases, and product liabilities.

title: Indemnity Insurance
definition: Indemnity insurance refers to aninsurancepolicy that compensates an insured party for certain unexpected damages or losses up to a certain limit—usually the amount of the loss itself. These policies are commonly used to protect professionals and business owners from liabilities arising from misjudgment or malpractice. Such coverage is crucial in fields like medicine, law, and finance.

title: Indenture
definition: An indenture is a legal agreement that sets the terms of a financial or real estate deal, such as a bond or property contract. In finance, indentures define bond details like maturity dates, interest, and covenants, while in real estate, they outline obligations between parties. They also serve as proof of claims in bankruptcy and help manage bond offerings for issuers and investors. The term originates from old agreements with indented edges.

title: Indentured Servitude
definition: Indentured servitude was a historical labor system in which individuals worked for a set period to repay a debt or loan, often the cost of passage to America. It peaked in the 1600s in the American colonies but declined as laws evolved and other labor systems emerged.

title: Independent Contractor Explained: Definition, Taxes, and Practical Examples
definition: An independent contractor, also referred to as a freelancer, operates as a non-employee to offer services or work under business agreements. Unlike employees, these self-employed individuals manage their own taxes, insurance, and retirement while capitalizing on business deductions to reduce their tax liability. Understanding the distinction between independent contractor and employee status is crucial for both tax compliance and the array of responsibilities involved in this flexible yet challenging work arrangement.

title: Index
definition: An index is a collection of a group of assets such as stocks and bonds that's used to track the performance of a specific sector or the broader market.

title: Index Fund
definition: Index funds use a passive investing strategy and trade less frequently to keep costs low.

title: Index Futures
definition: Index futures are futures contracts where the underlying asset is a stock index. These financial derivatives allow investors to buy or sell the future value of a stock index at a predetermined price and date.

title: Index Investing
definition: Index investing is a passive investment technique that attempts to generate returns similar to a broad market index. Investors use this buy-and-hold strategy to replicate the performance of a specific index, generally an equity or fixed-income index, with minimal active management. They purchase the component securities of the index or invest in an index mutual fund or exchange traded fund (ETF) that closely tracks the underlying index.

title: Index-Linked Bond
definition: An index-linked bond pays interest based on a price index, usually theConsumer Price Index (CPI). This feature protects investors from changes in the index. The bond's cash flows are adjusted to ensure that the holder of the bond receives a knownreal rate of return. In Canada, it's called a real return bond; in the U.S., it's TIPS; and in the U.K., a linker.

title: Index Option
definition: Index options areEuropean-style, cash-settled derivatives that give investors the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell the value of an underlyingindex, such as the S&P 500 or VIX, at a set price. They are widely used to hedge portfolio risks, diversify investments, and capitalize on index movements with limited risk exposure.

title: Indexation
definition: Indexation is a system or technique used by organizations or governments to connect prices and asset values. It’s accomplished by linking adjustments made to the value of a good, the price of a service, or another specified value to a predetermined price or composite index.

title: Indexed Annuity
definition: An indexed annuity is a contract that pays interest based on a market index like the S&P 500, offering a chance for higher yields than fixed annuities. While it provides the potential for gains when markets perform well, certain contract provisions limit the upside. Indexed annuities ensure the principal remains protected, offering a minimum return even during market downturns. This article explains how they work, focusing on participation rates and rate caps, crucial for maximizing your retirement income potential.

title: Understanding Indexing in Economics and Passive Investing
definition: Indexing is the practice of compiling economic or financial market data into a single metric or comparing data to such a metric. In economics, indexes can directly impact people's livelihoods, for example in the form of cost-of-living adjustments that are indexed to inflation. In investing, indexes serve asbenchmarksfor measuring the performance of portfolios and fund managers. Indexing also meanspassively investingin market indexes to replicate broad market returns, instead of selecting individual stocks.

title: Indian Rupee
definition: The official currency of India is the rupee (INR), which is issued and regulated by the Reserve Bank of India. It's denoted by the symbol ₹and is subdivided into 100 paise.

title: Indication of Interest (IOI)
definition: An indication of interest is anunderwritingexpression showing a conditional, non-binding interest in buying a security that is currently in registration and awaiting approval by theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

title: Understanding Indicative Net Asset Value (iNAV): A Guide for Investors
definition: The Indicative Net Asset Value (iNAV) gives you the value of an investment during the trading day. INAV is reported approximately every 15 seconds. It gives investors a measure of the value of the investment throughout the day.

title: Indicator
definition: Financial indicators are crucial tools used to gauge current economic and financial conditions and predict trends. In investing, they often involvetechnicalchart patterns based on a security's price or volume, such as moving averages and the MACD. Economically, indicators like the CPI and GDP help assess the health and direction of the economy.

title: Indifference Curve
definition: An indifference curve is used in contemporary microeconomics to demonstrate the effect of preference and budget limitations when consumers are choosing between products that would give them equal utility and satisfaction.

title: Indirect Loan
definition: An indirect loan can refer to aninstallment loanin which thelender– either the original issuer of the debt or the current holder of the debt – does not have a direct relationship with the borrower.

title: Indirect Method
definition: The indirect method is one of two accounting approaches used to create acash flow statement. It uses increases and decreases in balance sheet line items to modify the operating section of the cash flow statement from theaccrual methodto the cash method of accounting.

title: Indirect Quote
definition: An indirect quote in foreign exchange markets shows the amount of foreign currency needed tobuy or sellone unit of the domestic currency. Also known as a "quantity quotation," this type of quote positions the domestic currency as thebase currencyand the foreign currency as thecounter currency. In contrast, a direct quote does the opposite by pricing foreign currency in terms of the domestic currency.

title: Indirect Tax
definition: An indirect tax is a form oftaxationwhere the tax is collected by an intermediary, such as a manufacturer or retailer, and then passed onto the consumer through the price of a good or service. While the entity collects the tax, the consumer ultimately bears the cost by paying a higher price.

title: Individual Retirement Account (IRA)
definition: An individual retirement account (IRA)—known as an individual retirement arrangement by the IRS—is a long-term, tax-advantaged savings account that individuals with earned income can use to save for the future.

title: Industrial Bank
definition: An industrial bank is a state-chartered financial institution that is typically owned by a commercial company. Unlike most banks, it isn't regulated by a federal banking agency. These banks can only be chartered in a few states, with Utah acting as the main hub for their operations. Industrial banks are controversial because they can take deposits and make loans without direct oversight from the Federal Reserve. This raises concerns about regulatory gaps. Supporters say they increase competition and access to credit, while critics worry they blur the line between banking and commercial businesses.

title: Industrial Goods Sector
definition: The industrial goodssectoris a major part of the economy, consisting of companies that producecapital goods(machinery, equipment, and supplies) used in manufacturing, construction, and the production of goods and services.

title: Industrial Organization
definition: Industrial organization examines the inner workings and dynamics of industries, focusing on how firms interact within markets and respond to external influences like regulations and competition.

title: Industrial Production Index (IPI): Measuring Manufacturing & Mining Output
definition: The industrial production index (IPI) helps evaluate economic conditions by showing how effectively industries use available capacity. Because it measures output and capacity utilization, the index offers insight into growth trends, inflation pressure, and shifts in business cycles. Economists and investors rely on data published by theFederal Reserve Board(FRB) to support analysis and decision-making across financial markets.

title: Industrial Revenue Bonds Explained: Key Benefits and How They Function
definition: Industrial revenue bonds (IRB) are municipal debt securities issued by a government agency on behalf of a private sector company and intended to build or acquire factories or other heavy equipment and tools. IRBs were formerly called Industrial Development Bonds (IDB).

title: Industrial Revolution
definition: The Industrial Revolution took place during the late 1700s and early 1800s and was characterized by significant industrialization and innovation.

title: Industrialization
definition: Industrialization is the transition from agricultural economies to manufacturing-based ones.Mass productionhas been an essential component of industrialization, which is characterized by economic growth, technological innovation, and urban migration. Industrialization typically is associated with an increase in average income over time and improved living standards. The period known as the Industrial Revolution in Great Britain, Europe, and America from the 18th and 19th centuries is a prime example of industrialization and the changes it wrought.

title: Industry
definition: The term “industry” is a classification for a group of companies that have similar business activities. In modern economies, there are dozens of industry classifications. Industries typically are grouped into larger categories called sectors.

title: Industry Life Cycle
definition: The industry life cycle illustrates the progression of an industry through four pivotal stages: introduction, growth, maturity, and decline. Each stage reflects distinct business characteristics, affecting market players and informing strategic decisions. Understanding these phases aids investors and companies in navigating markets effectively.

title: Industry Life Cycle Analysis
definition: Industry life cycle analysis is part of thefundamental analysisof a company involving the examination of the stage an industry is in at a given point in time. There are four stages in an industry life cycle: expansion, peak, contraction, trough. An analyst will determine where a company sits in the cycle and use this information to project future financial performance and estimate forward valuations (e.g.,forward price-earningsratios).

title: Inefficient Market
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Infant-Industry Theory
definition: The infant-industry theory supportsprotectionismfor developing industries until they can compete globally. Alexander Hamilton and Friedrich List developed the theory in the early 19th century. It calls for protective measures like tariffs and quotas to shield new industries from competitive pressures.Economies of scaleare crucial for infant industries to compete with established rivals. Protective measures may persist longer than intended, even after industries mature.

title: Inferior Goods
definition: Inferior goods experience a decline in demand when people begin to generate more income.

title: Inflation
definition: Inflation is an increase in the average price of goods and services over time, which reduces the purchasing power of money.

title: Inflation Accounting
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Inflation-Adjusted Return
definition: Inflation-adjusted return measures investment performance by accounting forinflationeffects, revealing true earning potential. It provides a realistic look at gains or losses, unaffected by external economic factors, essential for comparing domestic and international investments.

title: Inflation Hedge
definition: An inflation hedge is a type of investment aimed at protecting against the decline in purchasing power that a currency might experience due to inflation. It usually involves investing in assets expected to retain or grow in value despite inflation. Common examples include tangible assets like gold and real estate. These investments are crucial for safeguarding your wealth in times of economic uncertainty, as they can help offset the diminished value caused by currency devaluation.

title: Inflation Swap
definition: An inflation swap is a contract where one party exchanges fixed cash flows for payments linked to an inflation index, such as the CPI, transferring inflation risk to the other party.

title: Inflationary Gap
definition: An inflationary gap indicates a situation in which the economy's output surpasses its sustainable capacity, potentially leading to upward pressure on prices.

title: Inflection Point
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Understanding the Information Coefficient (IC): Definition, Formula, and Example
definition: The information coefficient (IC) is a measure used to evaluate the skill of aninvestment analystor an activeportfolio manager. This is different from the information ratio, which compares a manager's returns to the amount of risk they take on.

title: Information Ratio
definition: The information ratio compares portfolio returns that exceed a benchmark to the volatility of those returns.

title: Infrastructure
definition: Infrastructure is defined as the basic physical systems of a business, region, or nation and often involves the production ofpublic goodsor production processes. Examples of infrastructure include transportation systems, communication networks, sewage, water, and school systems.

title: Inherent Risk
definition: Inherent risk arises from the possibility of committing an error or omission in a financial statement for reasons other than a failure of internal controls.

title: Inheritance
definition: An inheritance is the set of assets that an individual bequeaths to their loved ones upon their death. It may be subject to inheritance taxes. A recipient may inherit the assets as anheir, or they may be named as abeneficiaryin a will, retirement plan, or life insurance policy.

title: Inheritance Tax
definition: An inheritance tax is a levy potentially paid by the recipient of assets inherited from a deceased individual. Just five states have an inheritance tax as of 2025.

title: Inherited IRA
definition: An inherited IRA is an individual retirement account that a beneficiary must open after they inherit it. Beneficiaries can include spouses, relatives, or entities like trusts and estates. The rules for handling an inherited IRA can be complex, with different tax rules for spouses and non-spouses. The SECURE Act added a 10-year rule for non-spousal beneficiaries, requiring the account to be fully withdrawn within 10 years. There are options for beneficiaries to take withdrawals and to manage required minimum distributions (RMDs).

title: Initial Coin Offering (ICO)
definition: An initial coin offering (ICO) is the cryptocurrency industry’s equivalent of an initial public offering (IPO). A company seeking to raise money to create a new blockchain app or service with a cryptocurrency can launch an ICO as a way to raise funds.

title: Initial Margin
definition: Initial margin refers to the percentage of a security’s purchase price that an investor must put up when buying on margin. The Federal Reserve regulates it, with Regulation T setting a minimum of 50%.Using margin allows leverage, which can increase potential gains and risks. Futures contracts often have lower initial margin requirements than stocks. It’s important to know that initial margin is the upfront amount needed, while maintenance margin is the minimum account balance required to keep the position open.

title: Initial Public Offerings (IPOs)
definition: An IPO, or initial public offering, is the term for the first time that a private company sells shares of its stock to the public on a stock exchange.

title: Inorganic Growth
definition: Inorganic growth arises frommergersor takeovers rather than an increase in the company's own business activity. Firms that choose to grow inorganically can gain access to new markets through successful mergers and acquisitions. Inorganic growth is considered a faster way for a company to grow compared to organic growth.

title: Input-Output Analysis
definition: Input-output (I-O) analysis is a method designed to explore the complex interconnections among various sectors or industries in an economy.

title: INSEAD
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Inside Day
definition: An inside day is a two-day price pattern that occurs when a second day has arangethat is completely inside the first day's price range. The high of the second day is lower than the first, and the low of the second is higher than the first.

title: Inside Indemnity
definition: Indemnity is a comprehensive form of insurance compensation for damage or loss. When the term indemnity is used in the legal sense, it may also refer to an exemption from liability for damage.

title: Inside Sales
definition: Inside sales refers to the sale of products or services by personnel who reach customers through phone, email, or the internet. Other ways to define inside sales are "remote sales" or "virtual sales." It is called "inside" because these sales reps remain indoors, often at a call center or company office. Inside sales may or may not be outsourced to a third-party vendor.

title: Insider
definition: Insider information is a fact about a public company's plans or finances that has not yet been revealed to shareholders and that could give an unfair advantage to its possessors if acted upon. Buying or selling stock based on insider information can be a criminal offense.

title: Insider Trading
definition: Insider trading is the buying or selling of a company's securities by individuals who possess material, nonpublic information about that company.

title: Insolvency
definition: Insolvency refers to a business that can no longer pay its debts. A company might be unable to repay creditors if it's struggling financially. The company might have had a significant drop in income due to lost sales, increased expenses due to the cost of goods or labor, or the business might be suffering from poor decisions.

title: Installment Debt
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Institute for Supply Management (ISM)
definition: The Institute for Supply Management (ISM) is a leading global organization in supply management, founded in 1915 and serving more than 50,000 members worldwide. It offers certifications like the CPSM and CPSD, along with education, development resources, and networking opportunities for professionals. ISM also publishes the ISM Manufacturing Report on Business, a widely followed economic indicator.

title: Institute of Management Accountants (IMA)
definition: The Institute of Management Accountants (IMA) promotes ethics, education, and development in management accounting and finance.

title: Institutional Brokers' Estimate System (IBES)
definition: The Institutional Brokers' Estimate System (IBES), or I/B/E/S, is a financial database that aggregates analysts' earnings estimates, company guidance, and historical data for publicly traded firms. Launched in 1976 and later acquired by Thomson Reuters, it helps brokers and investors assess market expectations and inform investment decisions with wide company coverage and detailed financial insights.

title: Institutional Investor
definition: An institutional investor is an organization or entity that manages and invests funds given to them by individuals or entities, typically using more advanced strategies and handling larger sums than individual retail investors.

title: Instrument
definition: An instrument is a means by which something of value is transferred, held, or accomplished. In the field of finance, an instrument is a tradable asset, or anegotiableitem, such as a security, commodity,derivative, or index, or any item that underlies a derivative.

title: Insufficient Funds
definition: Non-sufficient funds (NSF) occur when your bank account lacks the cash to meet a transaction, leading to declined payments and fees. This article explains NSF, contrasts it with overdraft occurrences, and offers tips to prevent these costly fees through budgeting, account monitoring, and linking accounts for overdraft protection.

title: Insurable Interest
definition: Insurable interest is a financial share in an event, item, or person that would result in monetary deprivation if destruction, harm, or loss occurred.

title: Insurance
definition: Individuals may purchase insurance policies to protect their health, homes, and automobiles.

title: Insurance Claim
definition: An insurance claim is filed by a policyholder who seeks compensation for a covered loss.

title: Insurance Coverage
definition: Insurance coverage is the amount of risk orliabilitythat is covered for an individual or entity by way of insurance services. Insurance coverage, such as auto insurance,life insurance—or more exotic forms, such as hole-in-one insurance—is issued by an insurer in the event of unforeseen occurrences.

title: Insurance Premium
definition: An insurance premium is a payment made by individuals or businesses to maintain coverage under an insurance policy, which provides protection against various risks. Premiums are required for health, auto, home, and life insurance policies, among others. Understanding how these premiums are calculated, based on factors like coverage type and personal risk factors, can help you make informed insurance decisions and potentially save on costs.

title: Insurance Underwriter
definition: An insurance underwriter is a specialist who assesses the potential risks of providing coverage for individuals or property and determines the appropriate cost of that coverage.

title: Insurtech
definition: Insurtech refers to the use of technology innovations designed to find cost savings and efficiency from the currentinsuranceindustry model. Insurtech is a combination of the words “insurance” and “technology,” inspired by the term fintech.

title: Intangible Asset
definition: Intangible assets are non-physical but hold significant value for businesses through intellectual property, patents, and goodwill. Unliketangible assetslike buildings or office furniture that are easy to see and touch, intangible assets add value and competitive advantage in less obvious ways. These assets can either be indefinite, such as a strong brand name that persists over time, or definite, with a limited lifespan like a patent with an expiration date.

title: Intangible Personal Property
definition: Intangible personal property refers to assets that hold value but cannot be touched, such as intellectual property,copyrights, patents, digital assets, and brand or reputational capital. Unlike tangible property, physical items like machinery or jewelry, these assets exist in non-physical form yet can be highly valuable for individuals and businesses.

title: Intellectual Capital
definition: Intellectual capital involveshuman, structural, and relationship capital. It consists of employee expertise, organizational processes, and proprietary information. A highly valuable asset, it can play a role in developing acompetitive advantageand improving business outcomes for a company. Intellectual capital can be challenging to measure, and there's no one standard for its measurement across companies and industries.

title: Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT)
definition: An Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT) is an estate planning tool designed to help individuals manage the tax treatment of certain assets. The IDGT allows assets to be excluded from the grantor's estate forestate taxpurposes, but it keeps theincome taxresponsibilities with the grantor.

title: Inter-American Development Bank (IDB)
definition: The Inter-American Development Bank (IDB) is a cooperative development bank founded in 1959 to accelerate the economic and social development of its Latin American and Caribbean member countries. It is owned by a total of 48 member countries, including the U.S. and some European nations.The bank provides financing in the form of loans and grants.

title: Interactive Media
definition: Interactive media refers to various types of digital media, where the user has the ability to influence the experience. Examples include social media platforms, video games, apps, and VR. The interactivity aspect can involve text, graphics, sound, and video, or any combination of them.

title: Interbank Deposits
definition: The term "interbank deposit" refers to an arrangement between two banks in which one bank holds funds in an account for the other. The interbank deposit arrangement requires that the holding bank open a "due to" account for the other. This is a general ledger account with funds payable to another party. In the arrangement, the correspondent bank is the one that waits for the deposit.

title: Interbank Market
definition: The interbank market is a decentralized global network where banks trade currencies andderivativesdirectly between themselves. It supports both proprietary trading and transactions on behalf of clients, helping banks manage exchange rate and interest rate risk. As part of the largerinterdealer market, it plays a vital role in shaping global currency exchange rates and maintaining financial stability.

title: Interbank Network for Electronic Transfer (INET)
definition: The Interbank Network for Electronic Transfer (INET) was MasterCard's early system for processingcreditanddebit cardtransactions betweenfinancial institutionsbefore it evolved into Banknet. Banknet unified processing and authorizations into a more efficient global network, improving transaction speed and reliability for users worldwide.

title: Interbank Rate
definition: The interbank rate is the interest charged on short-term loans between banks, helping them manage liquidity and maintain adequate cash reserves. It also applies to wholesale foreign exchange transactions between banks in different countries.

title: What Is Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) and How Does It Work?
definition: The Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) is an American company known for operating global financial markets and exchanges. Founded in 2000, it focuses primarily on transforming energy commodity trading. Since its launch, ICE has expanded through acquisitions in sectors like commodities, securities, and data services. It formed ICE Clear Credit to mitigate systemic risk in the markets in response to the financial crisis. ICE is a major global player in the exchange market and is primarily known as the owner of the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE).

title: Interest
definition: Interest is a charge for borrowing money, typically expressed as a percentage of the principal amount borrowed. For lenders, it's the compensation for temporarily parting with their funds.

title: Interest Coverage Ratio
definition: The interest coverage ratio reveals a company’s solvency and ability to pay interest on its debt.

title: Interest Expense
definition: Interest expense is the price paid to borrow money.

title: Interest-Only Mortgage
definition: An interest-only mortgage requires borrowers to make only interest payments for a specific period. This structure allows for lower initial payments compared to standardmortgages, but it also poses risks. Typically offered as adjustable-rate loans, these mortgages transition to include principal payments after the interest-only phase, significantly raising monthly obligations. They can be appealing for those seeking initial cash flow flexibility; however, they require careful planning to manage future higher payments.

title: Interest Rate
definition: An interest rate is the price an entity pays for borrowing money or the fee they charge for lending it, expressed as a percentage.

title: Interest Rate Call Option
definition: An interest rate call option is a derivative in which the holder has the right to receive an interest payment based on a variable interest rate, and then subsequently pays an interest payment based on a fixed interest rate. If the option is exercised, the investor who sells the interest rate call option will make a net payment to the option holder.

title: Interest Rate Collar
definition: An interest rate collar is an options strategy that limits one's interest rate risk exposure.

title: Interest Rate Derivative
definition: An interest rate derivative is a financial contract that allows entities to manage their exposure to interest rate fluctuations.

title: Interest Rate Differential (IRD)
definition: An interest rate differential (IRD) is the difference between the interest rates of two investment vehicles.

title: Interest Rate Floor
definition: An interest rate floor is a contractual limit that ensures an interest rate will not fall below a specified level, thus providing income predictability.

title: Interest Rate Future
definition: Interest rate futures are a financial derivative contract where the underlying asset is an interest-bearing instrument, typically a government bond.

title: Interest Rate Options
definition: An interest rate option is a financialderivativethat allows the holder to benefit from changes ininterest rates. Investors can speculate on the direction of interest rates with interest rate options. It is similar to an equity option and can be either a put or a call. Interest rate options are option contracts on the rate of bonds like U.S. Treasury securities.

title: Interest Rate Parity
definition: Interest Rate Parity (IRP) serves as the cornerstone of how interest rates influence foreign exchange markets by aligning the interest rate differential with the forward and spot exchange rate differential. It ensures that hedged returns are equitable across different currencies, guiding investors in navigating the complex terrain of forex trading.

title: Interest Rate Risk
definition: Interest rate risk refers to the possibility that shifting interest rates could reduce the market value of a bond or other fixed-income investment.

title: Interest Rate Sensitivity
definition: Interest rate sensitivity measures how much a fixed-incomeasset'sprice will change when interest rates move, reflecting the inverse relationship between rates and bond prices.

title: Interest Rate Swap
definition: Interest rate swaps involve exchanging one stream of interest payments for another to manage exposure to interest rate fluctuations or achieve better borrowing terms. Typically, this swap involves converting fixed-rate payments to floating rates or vice versa. These swaps can be tailored to fit specific financial needs and are commonly used by companies to optimize cash flows.

title: Interim Dividend
definition: An interim dividend is a payment made by a company before itsannual general meetingand the release of finalfinancial statements. Declared by the board of directors, interim dividends are paid from retained earnings and often accompany the company's interim financial statements. This early dividend distribution allows shareholders to receive income before the conclusion of the financial year, usually in the United Kingdom and other regions where dividends are paid semi-annually.

title: Interim Statement
definition: A quarterly report is an example of an interim statement because it's issued before the year's end.

title: Intermediate Good
definition: Intermediate goods are those products that serve as inputs in the production of consumer goods, playing a crucial role in the manufacturing and supply chain process.

title: Internal Audit
definition: An internal audit is an analysis of a business that’s intended to identify opportunities to add value for stakeholders and improve operations. These audits can include processes, procedures, operations, current economic conditions, established controls, company culture, ethics, and product and service quality. They can assess any risks the business faces. The auditors make any recommendations for improvements after the analysis.

title: Internal Auditor (IA)
definition: An internal auditor evaluates the effectiveness and efficiency of a company's operations to ensure compliance with laws and regulations.

title: Internal Controls
definition: Internal controls are essential mechanisms designed to uphold the integrity of a company's financial reporting and ensure compliance with regulations. After the accounting scandals of the early 2000s, these controls became critical under the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002.

title: Internal Growth Rate
definition: The internal growth rate is the highest level of growth achievable for a business without obtaining outside financing.

title: Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Internal Revenue Code (IRC): Tax Laws & Principles Explained
definition: The Internal Revenue Code (IRC) is the section of Title 26 of the U.S. Code that sets the federal tax rules in the U.S. It explains how taxes work, who must pay them, and how they are collected. The Internal Revenue Service (IRS) enforces these laws, giving the IRC its power. The code covers areas like income taxes, payroll taxes, estate taxes, and excise taxes, which affect individuals and businesses.

title: What Is the Internal Revenue Service (IRS)?
definition: The Internal Revenue Service (IRS) is the division of the U.S. Treasury Department tasked with enforcing theInternal Revenue Code (IRC), administering federal tax laws, and collecting federal taxes from U.S. individual and corporate taxpayers. The IRS collectsgift,excise,estate, andincome taxes, and routinely conducts audits to ensure that taxpayers comply with tax laws.

title: Internalization
definition: Internalization occurs when a business decides to handle a transaction internally rather than route it out-of-house to another entity to handle.

title: International Accounting Standards (IAS)
definition: International Accounting Standards (IAS) are a set of rules for financial statements that were replaced in 2001 byInternational Financial Reporting Standards(IFRS). Most major financial markets worldwide have since adopted them

title: International Bank Account Number (IBAN)
definition: An international bank account number (IBAN) is a supplementary account number that identifies the nation of origin of a transaction involving a foreign bank account.

title: International Bank of Reconstruction and Development (IBRD)
definition: The International Bank of Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) is a development bank administered by the World Bank. It offers financial products and policy advice to countries aiming to reduce poverty and promote sustainable development.

title: International Banking Facility (IBF)
