definition: An International Banking Facility allows depository institutions in the U.S. to offer deposit, loan, and other banking services to foreign residents and institutions while being exempted from the Federal Reserve's reserve requirements as well as some state and local income taxes.

title: International Bond
definition: An international bond is an investment in debt that is issued by a foreign entity. For example, a U.S.-based international bond fund might invest in Australian government bonds, Chinese corporate bonds, and other government and corporate bonds issued in foreign countries.

title: International Chamber of Commerce (ICC)
definition: The International Chamber of Commerce is the largest business organization in the world, with 45 million member companies in more than 170 countries. The ICC aims to foster international trade and commerce to promote and protect open markets for goods and services and the free flow of capital.

title: International Depository Receipt (IDR)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: International Finance
definition: International finance encompasses the flow of capital and financial activities that occur between nations in the global economy.

title: International Finance Corporation
definition: The International Finance Corporation (IFC) provides financing of private-enterprise investment in developing countries around the world, through both loans and direct investments. Affiliated with theWorld Bank, the IFC also provides advisory services to encourage the development of private enterprises in nations that might lack the necessary infrastructure orliquidityfor businesses to secure financing.

title: International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS)
definition: International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) are globally recognized accounting principles designed to ensure consistency, transparency, and comparability in financial reporting across 168 jurisdictions, including all European Union nations. Although the United States follows thegenerally accepted accounting principles(GAAP), IFRS remains the most widely adopted accounting standard worldwide.

title: International Fisher Effect (IFE)
definition: The International Fisher Effect (IFE), developed by economist Irving Fisher, is an economic theory that linksexchange ratemovements to differences in nominal interest rates between two countries. According to the IFE, a currency with a higher nominal interest rate is expected to depreciate relative to a currency with a lower nominal interest rate.

title: International Investing
definition: International investing involves selecting global investment instruments as part of a diversified portfolio. People often invest internationally to increase thediversificationof their portfolios and spread investment risk among foreign markets and companies.

title: International Labor Organization (ILO)
definition: The International Labour Organization (ILO) is aUnited Nations (U.N.)agency dedicated to promoting social and economic justice through global labor standards. Founded in 1919 as the first U.N. agency, it operates through a unique three-part structure that brings together governments, employers, and workers. Headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, with approximately field offices around the world, the ILO works to ensure fair, secure, and dignified working conditions across its member states.

title: International Maritime Organization (IMO)
definition: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) is a specialized agency of the United Nations that is responsible for measures to improve the safety and security of international shipping and prevent marine pollution from ships.

title: International Monetary Fund (IMF)
definition: The International Monetary Fund is an intergovernmental financial institution that promotes global economic growth, high employment, and international monetary cooperation, secures global financial stability, facilitates international trade, and reduces global poverty.

title: International Monetary Market (IMM)
definition: The International Money Market (IMM) is a cornerstone of the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) that specializes in the trading of foreign currencies and interest rate futures and options. It has been a pivotal platform for currency trading since its inception in 1972. Key instruments and currencies traded include the U.S. dollar, British pound, euro, and Canadian dollar, along with interest rate futures. In this guide, you'll learn about the IMM's role within the global financial system, its history, and the limitations it faces in the current market environment.

title: International Organization for Standardization (ISO)
definition: The International Organization for Standardization sets quality standards for products distributed across national borders.

title: International Securities Identification Number (ISIN)
definition: An ISIN (International Securities Identification Number) is a unique, 12-character alphanumeric code that is used to identify securities and financial instruments globally

title: International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA)
definition: The International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) is a private trade organization that promotes and standardizes the trading of over-the-counter (OTC) derivatives.

title: Internationalization
definition: Internationalization involves modifying business strategies to facilitate a seamless expansion into foreign markets, thereby enhancing global reach and competitiveness.

title: Internet of Things (IoT)
definition: The Internet of Things (IoT) is a name for the aggregate collection of network-enabled devices, such as Wi-Fi connections, Bluetooth connections, andnear-field communication (NFC). The IoT doesn't include traditional computers like laptops and servers.

title: Interpersonal Skills
definition: Interpersonal skills are the behaviors and tactics a person uses to interact with others effectively. People use interpersonal skills all the time when dealing with others, whether in the workplace, in social situations, or within a family.

title: Interpolated Yield Curve (I Curve)
definition: An interpolatedyield curve(I curve) is created usingon-the-run Treasuries. Since these Treasuries only cover specific maturities, yields for in-between maturities must be estimated. Interpolation, often using numerical analysis, helps in estimating these unknown values.

title: Interpolation
definition: Interpolation is a statistical method for estimating unknown values between known data points. In investing, it helps analysts gauge prices oryieldsusing existing market data, often visualized through line charts intechnical analysis.

title: Inter-American Development Bank (IDB): What it is, How it Works
definition: The Inter-American Development Bank (IDB) is a cooperative development bank founded in 1959 to accelerate the economic and social development of its Latin American and Caribbean member countries. It is owned by a total of 48 member countries, including the U.S. and some European nations.The bank provides financing in the form of loans and grants.

title: Intertemporal Choice
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Inter-Vivos Trust
definition: An inter-vivos trust, or a living trust, enables a trustee to manage the trust's assets while the person who created the trust (thetrustor) is still alive. Often, the trustor names themself the trustee until they can no longer manage their own affairs.

title: Intestate
definition: Intestate refers to dying without a legalwill. When a person dies intestate, deciding how their assets will be distributed becomes the responsibility of a stateprobate court, following state-specific intestate succession laws. An estate is considered intestate if the person dies without a will or if their will is declared invalid by the court. Creating and updating a will is important to avoid unintended legal outcomes, as a significant percentage of Americans die without one.

title: Intraday Momentum Index (IMI)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Intraday Trading
definition: Intraday refers to assets traded within regular market hours, emphasizing daily price movements. Day traders aim to leverage these fluctuations for swift profits, using varied strategies to enter and exit positions before the market closes.

title: Intrapreneur
definition: An intrapreneur is an employee tasked with developinginnovative ideasor projects within a company. This term was coined by Gifford Pinchot III and Elizabeth S. Pinchot. This employee may be given the opportunity to transition into an entrepreneur.

title: Intrapreneurship
definition: Intrapreneurship encourages employees to take initiative and develop ideas, much like an entrepreneur.

title: Intrinsic Value
definition: Intrinsic value is the real value of an asset based on fundamental factors, as opposed to the price assigned by the market.

title: Introducing Broker (IB)
definition: An introducing broker (IB) is a registered intermediary in thefutures marketswho works directly with clients to provide and manage their accounts but leaves the actual trade execution to afutures commission merchant(FCM). IBs focus on building client relationships and guiding trading strategies, while FCMs handle the technical side of placing and clearing trades.

title: Inventory
definition: Inventory is the vitalassetsa company has in production and in goods produced that are available for sale. Inventory encompasses raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods.

title: Inventory Accounting
definition: Inventory accounting is the process of valuing and recording a company's goods across all stages of production (raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished products). Accurateinventoryvaluation is essential to reflect a company's true financial position and ensure compliance with GAAP standards.

title: Inventory Financing
definition: Inventory financing functions as a crucial financial tool for small to mid-sized businesses needing to manage cash flow and stock levels. The term refers to a short-term loan or a revolvingline of creditacquired by a company so it can purchase products to sell at a later date. These products serve as the collateral for the loan.

title: Inventory Management
definition: Inventory managementrefers to the process of ordering, storing, using, and selling a company's inventory. This includes raw materials, components, and finished products, as well as the warehousing and processing of these items. There are different methods of inventory management, each with its pros and cons, depending on a company's needs.

title: Inventory Turnover
definition: The inventory turnover ratio shows how quickly a company sells its products and restocks them over a given period.

title: Inventory Write-Off
definition: An inventory write-off is an accounting term for the formal recognition of a portion of a company's inventory that no longer has value. An inventory write-off can be recorded in two ways. It can be expensed directly to thecost of goods sold(COGS) account or it can offset the inventory asset account in acontraasset account. This is commonly referred to as the allowance forobsolete inventoryor inventory reserve.

title: Inverse ETF
definition: Inverse ETFs are designed to profit from market declines by using derivatives, offering investors an alternative to traditional short selling. Unlike direct shorting, these funds do not require margin accounts and entail specific risks and management costs for investors, making them more suitable for short-term strategic trades.

title: Inverse Head And Shoulders
definition: The inverse head-and-shoulders pattern is a major reversal signal that forms at the end of a downtrend. It has three successive troughs, with the middle trough being the deepest.

title: Inverted Yield Curve
definition: An inverted yield curve means yields decrease the further out the maturity date is.

title: Invested Capital
definition: Invested capital is the total amount of equity and debt capital a company raises to finance its operations and investments.

title: Investigational New Drug (IND)
definition: An indication of interest is anunderwritingexpression showing a conditional, non-binding interest in buying a security that is currently in registration and awaiting approval by theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

title: Investing
definition: Low-risk investments generally garner low expected returns, while higher returns usually accompany riskier investments.

title: Investment
definition: An investment is a purchase of stocks, bonds, real estate, or other assets to acquire capital gains, dividend distributions, or interest payments.

title: Investment Advisers Act of 1940
definition: The Investment Advisers Act of 1940 was created after the 1929 stock market crash and the Great Depression to protect investors and bring more trust to financial markets. The act helps ensure fairness and accountability, while protecting investors. The goal was to regulateinvestment advisorsand make sure they act in their clients’ best interests. The law requires most advisors to register with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) or state regulators and also sets rules on who qualifies as an investment advisor.

title: Investment Advisor
definition: Investment advisers are experts who offer advice and strategies on financial investments to help clients manage their portfolios effectively.

title: Investment Advisory Representative (IAR)
definition: Investment advisory representatives (IARs) are licensed professionals who work for investment advisory companies and provide personalized investment advice and financial planning services to individuals and businesses.

title: Investment Analysis
definition: Investment analysis entails evaluating investments, industries, and economic trends to predict performance and determine suitability for individual investors. It encompasses examining financial securities,managing portfolios, and creating financial strategies. This process aids investment decisions by considering entry price, time horizon, and portfolio role, tailored to diverse investor goals and backgrounds.

title: Investment Bank (IB)
definition: An investment bank facilitates large financial transactions and offers advisory services for IPOs and mergers. With major players like JPMorgan Chase and Goldman Sachs, these banks serve corporations, pension funds, and governmental entities by underwriting securities and offering strategic financial advice.

title: Investment Banker
definition: Investment bankers play a crucial role in the financial world by advising corporations and governments on how to effectively raisecapitalthrough the issuance of stocks and bonds. They facilitate major financial transactions, including acquisitions, mergers, and company sales, guiding clients toward informed, strategic decisions. Leveraging their expertise, investment bankers not only navigate complex regulatory environments but also help businesses capitalize on market opportunities.

title: Investment Banking
definition: Investment banking organizes intricate financial transactions, includingIPOsand mergers. Investment bankers guide corporations through high-finance challenges by raising capital and advising on potential mergers and acquisitions.

title: Investment Center
definition: An investment center is a business unit that uses capital to generate profits and is evaluated on how well its investments perform relative to its costs.

title: Investment Club
definition: An investment club allows a group of people to pool money and make joint investment decisions, often structured as legalpartnerships. Members engage in educational meetings and may benefit from lower transaction fees and shared insights into the investment process.

title: Investment Company
definition: An investment company is a corporation or trust engaged in the business of investing the pooled capital of investors in financial securities. Types of investment companies are mutual funds, closed-end funds, and unit investment trusts. In the United States, most investment companies are registered with and regulated by the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) under theInvestment Company Act of 1940.

title: Investment Company Act of 1940
definition: The Investment Company Act of 1940 regulates how investment companies are organized and operate. It sets industry standards and aims to protect investors by making them aware of securities risks.

title: Investment Consultant
definition: An investment consultant is a financial professional who providesinvestorswith investment products, advice, and/or planning. Investment consultants do in-depth work on formulatinginvestment strategiesfor clients, helping them fulfill their needs and reach their financial goals. Many financial advisors and financial planners would be considered investment consultants.

title: Investment Fund
definition: An investment fund is a supply of capital belonging to numerous investors, used to collectively purchase securities.

title: Investment Grade
definition: An investment grade is a rating that signifies a municipal or corporate bond presents a relatively low risk ofdefault. Bond rating firms like Standard & Poor’s (S&P), Moody's, and Fitch use different designations, consisting of the upper- and lower-case letters "A" and "B," to identify a bond's credit quality rating. "AAA" and "AA" (high credit quality) and "A" and "BBB" (medium credit quality) are considered investment grade.Credit ratingsfor bonds below these designations ("BB," "B," "CCC," etc.) are considered low credit quality and are commonly referred to asjunk bonds.

title: Investment Horizon
definition: An investment horizon is the total length of time that an investor expects to hold a security or a portfolio.

title: Investment Income
definition: Investment income is money received in interest payments, dividends, capital gains realized with the sale of stock or other assets, and any profit made through another investment type. Investment income also includes interest earned on bank accounts,dividendsreceived from stock owned by mutual fund holdings, and the profits on the sale of gold coins.Long-term investment income undergoes different—and often preferential—tax treatment, which varies by country and locality.

title: Investment Management
definition: Investment management refers to the handling of an investment portfolio or a grouping of assets. It involves buying and selling assets, developing investment strategies, creating a tax strategy, and managing asset allocation. It can also include banking, budgeting, and other financial duties.

title: Investment Manager
definition: Investment managers oversee and direct financial assets to help clients achieve their financial goals.

title: Investment Multiplier
definition: The investment multiplier is a critical economic concept rooted in the theories of John Maynard Keynes. It suggests that increases in investment spending can have profound effects on the overall economy, triggering a cycle of increased income and consumption.

title: Investment Objective
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Investment Policy Statement (IPS)
definition: An Investment Policy Statement (IPS) is a crucial document in portfolio management, outlining investment goals, asset allocation, and strategies. Typically used byportfolio managersand financial advisors, it specifies rules and frameworks to guide decisions and align them with the client's financial objectives.

title: Investment Product
definition: Investment products are financial instruments, like stocks, bonds, and derivatives, purchased with the expectation of earning a favorable return. They're based on an underlying security or group of securities, and they vary widely in risk and objective, so selection should match your risk tolerance and investment goals.

title: Investment Property
definition: Investment properties arereal estateassets bought to generate returns either through rental income, future resale, or both. Held by individual investors, groups, or corporations, these properties can be either long-term ventures or short-term flips—where investors renovate and quickly sell for profit. While often associated with real estate, 'investment property' can also refer to assets like art, securities, or collectibles purchased for future appreciation.

title: Investment Securities
definition: Investment securities are tradable financial assets intended for purchase as investments by financial institutions. They include equity stakes and debt securities. Banks often hold investment securities in their portfolios, due to their liquidity and revenue potential. Article 8 of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) governs investment securities. The investment-grade ratings issued by credit rating agencies can help investors purchase debt issues with greater confidence.

title: Investment Strategy
definition: An investment strategy is a comprehensive plan that guides an individual's investing decisions to meet specific financial goals andrisk tolerance. It dictates the approach to asset allocation, whether conservative for wealth protection or aggressive for rapid capital appreciation.

title: Investment Thesis
definition: An investment thesis is a reasoned argument for a particular investment strategy, backed up by research and analysis. Investment theses are commonly prepared by (and for) individual investors and businesses to guide their strategic decisions.

title: Investment Vehicle
definition: An investment vehicle is a product used by investors to gain positive returns. Investment vehicles can be low risk, such ascertificates of deposit(CDs) or bonds, or they can carry a greater degree of risk, such as stocks, options, and futures. Other types of investment vehicles include annuities; collectibles, such as art or coins; mutual funds; andexchange-traded funds(ETFs).

title: Investopedia
definition: Investopedia was founded in 1999 with the mission of helping people improve their financial outcomes.

title: Investor
definition: An investor is an individual or institution that commits money with the aim of earning returns and meeting goals like retirement savings or long-term wealth building. They use vehicles such as stocks, bonds,mutual funds, commodities, and real estate, and choose among them based on their strategy and risk tolerance.

title: Investor Relations
definition: The investor relations (IR) department is a division of a business, usually apublic company, whose job it is to provide investors with an accurate account of company affairs. This helps private and institutional investors make informed decisions on whether to invest in the company.

title: Invisible Hand
definition: The invisible hand, a concept coined by Adam Smith in his seminal work "The Wealth of Nations," describes the unseen market forces that drive afree economythrough self-interest and voluntary trades.

title: Invoice
definition: An invoice is a time-stamped commercial document that itemizes and records a transaction between a buyer and a seller and is important for bookkeeping purposes. If goods or services were purchased on credit, the invoice usually specifies the terms of the deal and provides information on the available payment methods.

title: Invoice Financing
definition: Invoice financing lets businesses access cash by using their unpaid invoices. Companies use it to improve their cash flow, pay suppliers or employees, and fund growth. It typically comes in forms like factoring and discounting, each with its own benefits and drawbacks. Invoice factoring is helpful for businesses with slow-paying customers, and there are alternative financing methods that can also support cash flow needs.

title: IOTA
definition: IOTA (MIOTA) is a distributed ledger designed to record and execute transactions between machines and devices. It's an ecosystem that can include everything from product origin and authenticity to drivers selling the data their cars collect.

title: IOU
definition: An IOU, derived from the phonetic sound of "I owe you," is a written acknowledgment of a debt between parties. Originating as far back as the 18th century, IOUs remain widely utilized today as a less formal alternative to legally binding contracts. While they serve to document debt, their informal nature often leaves them open to interpretation and lacks the enforceability of formal agreements. Businesses and individuals frequently use IOUs as a precursor to more detailed contracts.

title: IRA Rollover
definition: An IRA rollover is a transfer of funds from a retirement account, such as anemployer-sponsored plan, into an individual retirement account (IRA). The purpose of a rollover is to maintain the tax-deferred status of those assets.

title: Iranian Rial (IRR)
definition: IRR is the currency abbreviation, or FX symbol, for the Iranian rial, Iran's officialcurrency. The rial, named after the Spanishreal, first appeared in 1798 and is issued and managed by the Central Bank of the Islamic Republic of Iran.

title: Iron Butterfly
definition: An iron butterfly is a type ofoptionstrade that uses four options contracts. It's used to profit from price movement in a narrow range during a period of decliningimplied volatility. This trade resembles a short straddle but adds a long call and put for protection.

title: IRR Rule
definition: The internal rate of return is a financial metric used to evaluate the profitability of potential investments. The IRR rule states that a project or investment may be worth pursuing if itsinternal rate of return (IRR)exceeds the minimumrequired rate of return, orhurdle rate. This rule can be useful for companies and investors that want to determine whether to take on a certain project or investment or to compare it with others they may be considering.

title: Irrational Exuberance
definition: Irrational exuberance describes a situation where investor enthusiasm inflates asset prices beyond their true economic value. The phrase first gained fame in former Fed chair Alan Greenspan's 1996 speech, "The Challenge of Central Banking in a Democratic Society."It was given near the beginning of the 1990s dot-com bubble, a textbook example of irrational exuberance:

title: Irrevocable Beneficiary
definition: An irrevocable beneficiary is a person or entity named to receive benefits from alife insurancepolicy or asegregated fund, whose rights cannot be changed without their consent.

title: Irrevocable Letter of Credit
definition: An irrevocable letter of credit (ILOC) is a bank-issued document guaranteeing that a buyer's payment to a seller will be honored. This payment assurance becomes crucial in international trade, providing security for both buyers and sellers. Once issued, ILOCs cannot be canceled or altered without the consent of all parties involved, including the buyer, the seller, and the bank. These guarantees enhance trust and facilitate transactions across borders, reducing associated risks.

title: Irrevocable Trust
definition: An irrevocable trust can't be changed or cancelled unless its beneficiary or a court allows it.

title: IRS Form 4868
definition: Form 4868: Application for Automatic Extension of Time to File U.S. Individual Income Tax Return is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) form for individuals who wish to extend the amount of time they have to file their tax returns.

title: IRS Publication 15
definition: IRS Publication 15: Employer's Tax Guide is a document published by theInternal Revenue Servicedetailing an employer's responsibilities for filing and reporting tax information. The document covers thewithholding, depositing, reporting, paying, and correcting of taxes for employees (although not for the corporation itself). IRS Publication 15 is also referred to as Circular E.﻿﻿

title: IRS Publication 463
definition: IRS Publication 463: Travel, Gift, and Car Expenses explains the expenses associated with business activities that an individual taxpayer can deduct to reduce their overall taxable income.

title: IRS Publication 519
definition: IRS Publication 519 is the U.S. Tax Guide For Aliens, a document theInternal Revenue Service (IRS)publishes that details the tax procedures for aliens, individuals that are not citizens of the United States.﻿﻿ Not all aliens are subject to U.S. taxes.Resident aliens, those who have been in the country for a defined period, are subject to taxation on their worldwide income just as citizens are. Non-resident aliens are only taxed on income they earn within the U.S., as well as on certain types of international income.﻿﻿

title: IRS Publication 525
definition: Publication 525, Taxable and Nontaxable Income, is a document published by theInternal Revenue Service (IRS)detailing what types of income taxpayers should consider taxable or nontaxable when filing tax returns.﻿﻿

title: IRS Publication 527
definition: IRS Publication 527, Residential Rental Property, is a document published by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) that provides tax information for individuals who own residential properties that arerented out for income.

title: IRS Publication 535 (Business Expenses)
definition: Publication 535, Business Expenses is anInternal Revenue Service (IRS)document that discusses common business expenses and explains the rules for deducting business expenses. The guide explains what is and is not deductible, and lists some of the most common business deductions.

title: IRS Publication 550
definition: IRS Publication 550 is a document published by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) that provides information on how investment income and expenses are to be treated when filing taxes.IRS Publication 550explains what investment expenses aredeductiblewhen gains and losses from the sale of investment property are to be reported and what type of investments are considered taxable.

title: IRS Publication 590
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: IRS Publication 590-B
definition: IRS Publication 590-Bexplains the tax implications of withdrawing money from any type ofindividual retirement account (IRA), before or after retirement. It specifies when you can't withdraw money without paying a penalty and when youmustwithdraw money.

title: IRS Publication 970
definition: IRS Publication 970 is a document published by theInternal Revenue Service (IRS)that provides information on tax benefits available to students and families saving or paying for college. It explains the tax treatment for the most common forms of college funding, such as scholarships, fellowships and grants, and tuition reductions.

title: IRS Publication 972
definition: Publication 972 was a document published by theInternal Revenue Service (IRS)that provided guidance on determining the exact amount of thechild tax creditthat taxpayers can claim.

title: IS-LM Model
definition: The IS-LM model, standing for "investment-saving" (IS) and "liquidity preference-money supply" (LM), is a foundational Keynesianmacroeconomicconcept. It illustrates the interaction between the goods market and themoney market, using IS and LM curves to determine the short-run equilibrium ofinterest ratesand output. British economist John Hicks introduced this model in 1937 as a formal representation of John Maynard Keynes' earlier theories on employment, interest, and money.

title: ISDA Master Agreement
definition: An ISDA master agreement is a standard document regularly used to govern over-the-counter derivatives transactions.

title: iShares
definition: iShares is a global leader inexchange-traded funds(ETFs) that manages nearly $2 trillion across more than 800 products. iShares is a subsidiary of BlackRock, the world's largest asset management company, and BlackRock is responsible for issuing and marketing iShares products.

title: Islamic Banking
definition: Islamic banking is a system of finance that adheres to Sharia law, which advocates profit-sharing while prohibiting the collection and payment of interest.

title: ISM Manufacturing Index
definition: The ISM manufacturing index, also known as the purchasing managers' index (PMI), is a monthly indicator of U.S. economic activity based on a survey of purchasing managers at manufacturing firms nationwide. It is considered to be a key indicator of the state of the U.S. economy. Formally called the Manufacturing ISM Report on Business, the survey is conducted by theInstitute for Supply Management (ISM).

title: ISO Currency Code
definition: ISO currency codes are three-letter alphabetic identifiers for globalcurrencies, standardized by theInternational Organization for Standardization(ISO) in 1978. In the forex market, these codes appear in pairs, designating base and quote currencies, to price and trade currencies efficiently.

title: Isoquant Curve
definition: An isoquant curve helps manufacturers adjust their levels of labor and capital to maintain output at the lowest cost.

title: ISPs (Internet Service Providers)
definition: Internet Service Providers (ISPs) are key players in the digital world, connecting both consumers and businesses to the vast expanse of the Internet. These companies use various technologies—such as cable, DSL, fiber optics, dial-up, and wireless—to deliver seamless online experiences tailored to individual needs.

title: Issue
definition: An 'issue' in the financial world is the process through which companies offer securities, such as stocks or bonds, to investors in order to raise capital. These offerings help companies finance their operations or expand their business. Companies can issue new stocks or bonds depending on their financial strategy and needs. There are several types of securities offerings, including new issues (initial offerings) and seasoned issues (additional offerings by established firms).

title: Issued Shares
definition: Issued shares refer to the number of shares a company has sold and distributed to its shareholders, including insiders and institutional investors. These shares form part of a company's equity, are detailed in financial reports, and differ from outstanding shares, which exclude treasury stock. Understanding these terms is crucial for grasping a company's ownership structure and market presence.

title: Issuer
definition: An issuer, such as a public company, develops and sells securities to raise the capital it needs tofinanceits business operations.Corporations,investment trusts, and governments can be issuers of securities such as equity, bonds, and warrants.

title: Issuer Identification Numbers (IIN)
definition: Issuer identification numbers (IINs) are the first digits on a payment card and identify the bank or institution that issued it, helping route and authenticate transactions across global networks like Visa and Mastercard.

title: Itemized Deduction
definition: An itemized deduction is an expense that can be subtracted from youradjusted gross income (AGI)to reduce your taxable income and lower the amount of taxes you owe. Taxpayers can itemize deductions likemortgage interest, charitable gifts, and unreimbursed medical expenses, or choose to take thestandard deduction, a fixed dollar amount that varies by filing status.

title: J
definition: Most companies traded on theNasdaqhave four-letteredticker symbols, which are representative of the actual company. For example, the ticker symbol for Nasdaq-traded Microsoft is MSFT.

title: J-Curve
definition: A J Curve is an economic theory which states that, under certain assumptions, a country'strade deficitwill initially worsen after thedepreciation of its currency—mainly because in the near term, higher prices on imports will have a greater impact on total nominal imports than the reduced volume of imports. This results in a characteristic letter J shape when the nominal trade balance is charted as a line graph.

title: J-Curve Effect
definition: A J-curve is a trendline that shows an initial loss immediately followed by a dramatic gain. In a chart, this pattern of activity would follow the shape of a capital "J".

title: Jack Welch
definition: Jack Welch was the chair and chief executive of General Electric from 1981 to 2001. Welch increased the market value of GE dramatically from $14 billion to $410 billion.

title: Jackpot
definition: A jackpot is a large windfall derived from an act of gambling. In finance, jackpots refer to large investment returns reaped over a short period of time. Typical consequences of a financial windfall can include tax obligations and the potential for overspending. Financial planning is critical to manage and retain newfound wealth.

title: Jackson Hole Economic Symposium
definition: The Jackson Hole Economic Symposium is an annual symposium, sponsored by the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City since 1978, and held in Jackson Hole, Wyo., since 1981.

title: Jakarta Stock Exchange
definition: During 2018, investors inemerging market economieshave had to deal with slower economic growth in China, a currency crisis in Turkey and volatile elections in Latin America, all while assessing what impact tradetariffsand a strongU.S. dollarhave on these developing countries.

title: James H. Clark
definition: James H. "Jim" Clark is a serialentrepreneurand computer scientist whose net worth is estimated in the billions, best known for co-founding Netscape with Marc Andreessen in 1994. Netscape Navigator was the market leader in web browsers in the early days of the Internet, becoming the dominant browser in terms of usage share in the 1990s. He also founded Silicon Graphics and Healtheon, later tied to WebMD, made notable investments, and has supported major philanthropic gifts to universities.

title: James M. Buchanan Jr.
definition: James M. Buchanan Jr. was an American economist who earned theNobel Prize in economicsin 1986 for his contribution to public choice theory, which useseconomicsto analyze the behavior of voters and public officials. He developed the public choice theory with fellow economist, Gordon Tullock.

title: James Tobin
definition: James Tobin was aNeo-Keynesianeconomist who was awarded the 1981 Nobel Prize in economics for his research on the financial system and its impact on inflation and employment. He is also known for introducing the idea for a tax on foreign exchange transactions to reduce currency speculation. This became known as theTobin taxafter his death. Tobin wrote several books includingEssays in EconomicsandMoney, Credit and Capital. He died on March 11, 2002.

title: Jan Tinbergen
definition: Jan Tinbergen, a pioneering Dutch economist, was instrumental in the development ofeconometricsand dynamic macroeconomic models, which earned him theNobel Memorial Prize in Economicsin 1969. He is best known for integrating mathematics into economics, setting the stage for modern economic forecasting and policy-making.

title: January Barometer
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: January Effect
definition: The January effect is the supposed increase in stock prices in the first month of the year.

title: Japan Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotation
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Japan Credit Rating Agency (JCR)
definition: The Japan Credit Rating Agency (JCR) is a Japanese financial services company that providescredit ratingson the corporate debt of Japanese companies and foreign bond issuers. The entity also publishes a variety of financial and economic information and serves as a guide for counterparty credit risk.

title: Japan ETF
definition: A Japan ETF is anexchange-traded fund(ETF) that invests the majority of its assets in Japanese assets that trade on local stock exchanges. These ETFs are diversified investments that have a low initial investment requirement and management costs.

title: Japan Inc.
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Japanese Government Bond (JGB)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Japanese Yen (JPY)
definition: JPY is the abbreviation for the Japanese yen, the currency of Japan. The yen is often represented with a symbol that looks like the capital letter Y with two horizontal dashes through the center: ¥.

title: Jarrow Turnbull Model
definition: The Jarrow Turnbull model is one of the first reduced-form models for pricingcredit risk. Developed by Robert Jarrow and Stuart Turnbull, the model utilizesmulti-factorand dynamic analysis ofinterest ratesto calculate theprobabilityofdefault.

title: Jay-Z
definition: Billionaires play an outsized role in shaping the global economy, politics, and philanthropy.Forbesputs the number of billionaires in the world at 3,028 in 2025. The wealthiest among them is Elon Musk, CEO of Tesla and SpaceX.

title: Jean-Baptiste Say
definition: Jean-Baptiste Say was a prominent economist who emphasized the importance of production in stimulating economic growth and its subsequent effect on generating market demand.

title: Jekyll and Hyde
definition: The phrase "Jekyll and Hyde" employs a literary reference to describe a stock market that appears to have a split personality, mixing good and bad character traits.

title: Jensen's Measure
definition: Jensen's measure evaluates how well an investment performs against the market, adjusting for the level of risk taken.

title: Jerome Kerviel
definition: Jerome Kerviel was at the center of one of the largest trading scandals in history. As a junior derivatives trader at Société Générale, he made a series of unauthorized trades that ultimately led to losses of over €4.9 billion between 2006 and 2008.

title: Jesse H. Jones Graduate School Of Business
definition: The S&P 500 index tracks some of the largest U.S. stocks, many of which pay a regular dividends. Reviewing the history of the S&P 500 dividend yield can provide insight into the stock market's direction.

title: Jim Cramer (James Cramer)
definition: Jim Cramer is a former hedge fund manager, TV host, and personality of CNBC's "Mad Money," and he runs the CNBC "Investing Club". He is also the co-founder and chair ofTheStreet.com, Inc.

title: JMD (Jamaican Dollar)
definition: The Jamaican dollar (JMD) is the official currency of Jamaica, and it is divided into 100 cents. It's widely used for everyday transactions across the country. The JMD plays an important role in foreign exchange markets, especially in the USD/JMD exchange rate. Introduced in 1969, the Jamaican dollar has a unique history because it replaced the Jamaican pound rather than being based directly on another dollar system.

title: Job Lot
definition: In finance, the term “job lot” refers to acommodities futures contractwhose denomination is smaller than the typical standard lot for that commodity. For example, the standard lot for acrude oilfutures contract is 1,000 barrels per contract. Any crude oil futures contract corresponding to less than 1,000 barrels would therefore be considered a job lot.

title: Job Market
definition: The job market is the market in which employers search for employees and employees search for jobs. The job market is not a physical place as much as a concept demonstrating the competition and interplay between different labor forces. It is also known as thelabor market.

title: Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS)
definition: The job openings and labor turnover survey (JOLTS) is an economic indicator that tracks U.S. job vacancies, hires, and separations. Published by theBureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), the survey measures metrics like the number of open positions and the quit rate. JOLTS provides insight into labor demand and workforce trends, helping analysts understand how employees and employers interact. This data complements the BLSmonthly jobs report, giving a fuller picture of employment. JOLTS helps analyze major labor shifts, including those seen during the Great Resignation.

title: Jobber
definition: A "jobber" was a market maker on the London Stock Exchange before the mid-1980s. Jobbers, also called "stockjobbers," acted as market makers (MMs). They held shares on their own books and created market liquidity by buying and selling securities. They matched investors' buy and sell orders through their brokers, who were not allowed to make markets.

title: Jobless Claims
definition: Jobless claims are a statistic reported weekly by the U.S. Department of Labor that counts people filing to receiveunemploymentinsurance benefits. There are two categories of jobless claims: initial, which comprises people filing for the first time, and continuing, which consists of unemployed people who have already been receiving unemployment benefits. Jobless claims are an importantleading indicatorof the state of the employment situation and the health of the economy.

title: Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2003 (JGTRRA)

title: Jobs Growth
definition: Jobs growth is measured in the U.S. by the number of employees added tononfarm payrollsmonthly, as reported by theBureau of Labor Statistics (BLS). It is a key indicator of the pace of economic expansion. Nonfarm payrolls are part of the Employment Situation Summary published by the BLS, the widely followed statistical release better known as the monthly jobs report.

title: Jobseeker's Allowance (JSA)
definition: The Jobseeker's Allowance (JSA) is a benefit for unemployed individuals in the U.K. who are actively seeking work. The allowance is intended to help minimize thecost of livingfor those seeking full-time employment.

title: Johannesburg Interbank Average Rate (JIBAR)

title: John B. Taylor
definition: John B. Taylor is a renowned economist and pivotal figure in shaping modern monetary policy. Taylor is the Mary and Robert Raymond Professor of Economics at Stanford University and a Senior Fellow of Economics at the Hoover Institution. He is also the Director of Stanford University Introductory Economics Center.

title: John Bogle
definition: John Bogle, known as "Jack," founded theVanguard Groupand pioneeredindex investing, enabling investors to buy mutual funds that follow the market. He aimed to make investing easier and cheaper for the average person. He introduced the Vanguard 500 fund, which tracks the returns of the S&P 500 and marked the first index fund marketed to retail investors.

title: John Elkann
definition: John Elkann is a leading figure in the automotive industry as chairman of Stellantis and CEO of Exor, the Agnelli family's investment company. As the heir to the Agnelli legacy behind Fiat, he helped steer the merger that formed Stellantis from Fiat Chrysler and PSA, extending the family's influence across global auto brands.

title: John F. Nash Jr.
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: John Maynard Keynes
definition: John Maynard Keynes was an early 20th-century British economist, best known as the founder of Keynesian economics and the father of modernmacroeconomics. One of the hallmarks of Keynesian economics is the idea that governments should actively try to influence the course of economies, especially by increasing spending to stimulate demand in the face ofrecession.

title: John R. Hicks
definition: John Richard Hicks was a Britishneo-Keynesianeconomist. Hicks was born in the United Kingdom in 1904 and studied at Oxford University, where he also lectured. He also taught at the London School of Economics and Cambridge University.

title: John Stuart Mill
definition: John Stuart Mill (1806-1873) was an influential 19th-century British philosopher, economist, and politician, as well as a senior employee of the East India Company. Trained rigorously from a young age by his father, the economist James Mill, and influenced by utilitarian thinker Jeremy Bentham, he helped shapeclassical economicsandutilitarianphilosophy.

title: Joint
definition: Joint is a legal term describing a financialtransactionor agreement where two or more parties act in unison.

title: Joint Account
definition: Joint accounts are shared by two or more people with equal access to and responsibility for the account.

title: Joint and Several Liability
definition: Joint and several liability is a legal concept where multiple parties share responsibility for damages in a lawsuit. This means a plaintiff can hold any or all responsible parties accountable for the full amount of damages awarded. This legal principle favors plaintiffs, allowing them to collect compensation more efficiently, but it has been limited or reformed in many states to address fairness to less culpable defendants. Most states now limit its application to parties responsible for a significant portion of the harm, reflecting a more balanced approach in legal reform.

title: Joint and Survivor Annuity
definition: A joint and survivor annuity is an insurance product designed primarily for retired couples who want a guaranteed monthly income that will continue for as long as either spouse lives.

title: Joint Bond
definition: A joint bond is sold with a guarantee of the payment of principal and interest by at least two parties. In the case of default by the issuer, thebondholdershave the right to claim repayment by any and all of the issuing institutions, corporations, or individuals. This shared responsibility reduces the risk to the investor but also generally means a lower rate ofreturn on the investment.

title: Joint Credit
definition: Joint credit allows two or more people to borrow together, sharing both the credit limit and full responsibility for repayment. It can help borrowers access larger loans or qualify despite limitedcredit, but it also means each party is liable if the other cannot pay. This shared obligation can become complicated in situations like missed payments or divorce.

title: Joint Endorsement
definition: A joint endorsement is a financial agreement that stipulates all endorsed parties must consent before checks made out to multiple individuals can be processed.

title: Joint Liability
definition: Joint liability is when two or more people share the responsibility to fulfill a debt or legal duty, meaning either can be held accountable for the entire obligation.

title: Joint-Life Payout
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Joint Life with Last Survivor Annuity
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Joint Owned Property
definition: Joint-owned property is any property that's held in the name of two or more parties, such as spouses, business partners, family members, or friends. It can be held in several legal forms, including joint tenancy, tenancy by the entirety,community property, or in trust.

title: Joint Probability
definition: The term joint probability is often used to describe the likelihood that two or more events will occur at the same time.

title: Joint Return
definition: A joint return is a tax return filed with the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) on the new, simplifiedForm 1040(as of 2024) by two married taxpayers whose filing status is married filing jointly (MFJ) or by a widowed taxpayer whose filing status is Qualifying Widow or Widower (QW). A joint return allows these taxpayers to combine their tax liability and report their income, deductions, and credits on the same joint return.

title: Joint Return Test
definition: The joint return test is one of theIRStests that potentialdependentsmust pass in order to be claimed as such by another taxpayer.

title: Joint-Stock Company
definition: A joint stock company is a business in which each investor owns a stake based on the amount they invested.

title: Joint Supply
definition: Joint supply is an economic term referring to a product or process that can yield two or more outputs. Common examples occur within the livestock industry: cows can be utilized for milk, beef, and hide. Sheep can be utilized for meat, milk products, wool, and sheepskin. If thesupplyof cows increases, so will the joint supply of dairy and beef products.

title: Joint Tenancy
definition: Joint tenancy is a legal form of property ownership where two or more individuals hold equal rights and obligations to aproperty. This arrangement can be made by married or unmarried couples, business partners, friends, or even family members.

title: Joint Tenants in Common (JTIC)
definition: Joint tenants in common (JTIC) is a legal arrangement where two or more individuals co-own an asset, such aspropertyor investments, without rights of survivorship. Each owner holds a defined share, which can be equal or based on contribution, and is responsible for shared costs and management. Ownership stakes can be sold or transferred through a will, offering flexibility while maintaining joint ownership of the asset as a whole.

title: Joint Tenants with Right of Survivorship (JTWROS)
definition: Joint Tenants With Right of Survivorship (JTWROS) is a legal arrangement that grants co-owners equal ownership rights to an asset, such as a financialaccount, while ensuring that upon the death of one owner, their interest immediately transfers to the surviving owners. This arrangement bypasses the probate process and overrides any contrary instructions in a will, making it a popular choice for those seeking to ensure seamless transfer of assets. However, it's crucial for the joint tenants to maintain a stable relationship and fulfill their responsibilities, as financial or personal disputes can impact the effectiveness of this agreement.

title: Joint Venture (JV)
definition: A joint venture (JV) is a business arrangement in which two or more parties agree to pool their resources to accomplish a specific task, such as launching a new project or entering a new market. Companies often form JVs to share costs, combine expertise, and leverage each other's resources to reduce risk.

title: Jointly and Severally
definition: "Jointly and severally" is a legal term that is used to describe apartnershipor any other group of individuals in which each individual shares responsibility equally.

title: Jones Act
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Jordanian Dinar (JOD)
definition: The Jordanian dinar is the national currency of Jordan and its abbreviation, or currency symbol, is denoted by JOD. The dinar has smaller denominations or subunits. In particular, one dinar is equal to 10 dirhams, 100 qirshes, and 1,000 fils.The dinar is also circulated on Israel's West Bank.

title: Josef Ackermann
definition: Josef Ackermann is a prominent Swiss banker and formerCEOof Deutsche Bank from 2002 to 2012 who led the bank's transformation into a global investment bank. He was a member of theGroup of Thirtywhich analyzed global economic and financial issues. From 2014 to 2019, Josef Ackermann was the chair of the Bank of Cyprus. After the subprime mortgage crisis in 2008, he was investigated by the United States Department of Justice which found that he misled investors about questionable loans resulting in hundreds of millions of dollars in losses.

title: Joseph Effect
definition: The Joseph Effect is a term derived from the Old Testament story about the Pharaoh’s dream as recounted by Joseph. The vision led the ancient Egyptians to expect a crop famine lasting seven years to follow seven years of a bountiful harvest.

title: Joseph Schumpeter
definition: Joseph Alois Schumpeter (1883–1950) was a prominent economist and intellectual from the 20th century. Also a historian and author, the Austrian is considered one of the century's top intellectuals.

title: Joseph Stiglitz
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Journal
definition: A journal is a detailed accounting record that documents a company's financial transactions over a period—usually on a monthly or quarterly basis.

title: Judgment
definition: A judgment is a court's decision that settles a dispute by defining the rights and obligations of the parties involved. It can require one side to pay money or transfer property, which is called a monetary judgment, or it can order non-monetary actions, like requiring someone to perform a service or stop certain behavior. Judgments are legally enforceable, though collecting on them can sometimes be difficult if the losing party refuses to comply.

title: Judgment Lien
definition: A judgment lien is a legal claim placed on adebtor'sproperty following a court ruling that enforces repayment to acreditor. It allows creditors to seize assets such as real estate, business holdings, or personal property if obligations remain unpaid.

title: Judgment Proof
definition: Judgment proof (or judgement proof) refers to a person who does not have enough income or assets to pay a court judgment against them. A debtor who is broke and unemployed can be considered judgment proof, as can one whose only assets or income fall into certain legally protected categories.

title: Judgmental Credit Analysis
definition: Judgmental credit analysis is a method of approving or denying credit based on the lender's judgment rather than on a particular credit scoring model. Judgmental credit analysis entailsevaluating the borrower’s applicationand using prior experience dealing with similar applicants to determine credit approval. This process avoids using anyalgorithmsor empirical processes to determine approvals.

title: Judicial Foreclosure
definition: Judicial foreclosure refers to foreclosure proceedings on a property in which the mortgage lacks a power of sale clause. In this case, the foreclosure proceedings are settled through the courts. Power of sale is a clause written into a mortgage. In the event of default, it authorizes the lender to sell the property to repay the mortgage debt. In this case, legal proceedings can be sidestepped. Power of sale is permitted in many states as part of a lender’s rights to seek a foreclosure.

title: Judo Business Strategy

title: Julian Robertson
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Jumbo CD
definition: A jumbo CD is called "jumbo" because it requires a higher minimum deposit than that required by regular CDs. In return, thebest jumbo CDspay higher interest rates.

title: Jumbo Loan
definition: A jumbo loan is a loan that exceeds Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) conforming limits for conventional mortgages and is often used for luxury or high-cost properties in highly expensive real estate markets. Jumbo loans have specificunderwritingrequirements, including excellent credit scores and lower debt-to-income ratios. An advantage of a jumbo loan is the larger amount that can be borrowed, while a disadvantage is potentially higher costs. The average annual percentage rate (APR)for a jumbo mortgageis often on par with that for conventional mortgages.

title: Jumbo Pool
definition: A jumbo pool is a pass-throughGinnie Mae IImortgage-backed security (MBS) that iscollateralizedby multiple-issuer pools. These pools combine mortgage loans with similar characteristics and are more massive than single-issuer pools. The mortgages contained in jumbo pools are more diverse on a geographical basis than are those in single-issuer pools.

title: Jumpstart our Business Startups Act (JOBS)
definition: The Jumpstart Our Business Startups (JOBS) Act is a piece of U.S. legislation that was signed into law by President Barack Obama on April 5, 2012, that loosens regulations instituted by theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC)on small businesses. It lowers reporting and disclosure requirements for companies with less than $1 billion in revenue and allows the advertising of securities offerings. It also allows greater access tocrowdfundingand greatly expands the number of companies that can offer stock without going through SEC registration.

title: Junior Accountant
definition: A Junior Accountant maintains and compiles financial reports to comply with regulations. Responsibilities include managing general ledger accounts, updating financial statements, and preparing reports. Junior Accountants work under supervision, which aids in developing their industry skills and expertise. This entry-level role offers a pathway to senior accounting positions.

title: Junior Capital Pool (JCP)
definition: A junior capital pool (JCP) is a corporate capital structure that allows early-stage startups to sell shares in the company before actually establishing a line of business. This form of company financing is a Canadian invention and is permitted only in Canada.

title: Junior Company
definition: A junior company is a small company that is looking to find a natural resource deposit or field.

title: Junior Debt
definition: Junior debt, often referred to as subordinated debt, is a financial instrument that ranks lower in repayment priority than senior debt in the event of a default. Because junior debt is not backed by collateral and is reimbursed only after senior debts have been settled, it presents a higher risk for investors. Consequently, it typically offers higher interest rates. In real estate investing, understanding junior debt is crucial because it impacts the structuring of loans and potential returns.

title: Junior Equity
definition: Junior equity isstockissued by a company that ranks at the bottom of the priority ladder in terms of ownership rights. Its owners are the last in line to receive certain payouts, such as dividends or reimbursements in cases of bankruptcy.

title: Junior Mortgage
definition: A junior mortgage is a mortgage that is subordinate to a first or prior (senior) mortgage. A junior mortgage often refers to asecond mortgage, but it could also be a third or fourth mortgage (e.g. home equity loans or lines of credit (HELOCs)). In the case of a foreclosure, thesenior (first) mortgagewill be paid down first.

title: Junior Security
definition: The term junior security refers to asecuritywith a lower priority than others. Put simply, junior security is subordinate to any other type of security. This means that junior security holders get paid only after those who own senior securities if and when a company goes bankrupt or isliquidated. As such, there's a very good chance that some (or even all) of the junior securities to which a company owes money may not be repaid after any leftover cash is distributed.

title: Junk Bond
definition: Junk bonds, often referred to as high-yield bonds, carry higher risk due to lower credit ratings from agencies, compared to investment-grade debt. They represent debt issued by financially struggling companies and offer higher yields to compensate for the increased risk of default. These bonds serve as an indicator of market sentiment: growing interest signifies a willingness to embrace higher risk for the potential of improved returns. Investors can assess the risk by reviewing credit ratings: anything below BB is deemed speculative, or "junk."

title: Junk Fees
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Juris Doctor (JD)
definition: A Juris Doctor (JD) is a three-year professional degree that confers recognition that the holder has a professional degree in law. Upon completion of a JD. program and passing the bar exam, individuals are eligible to practice law in their jurisdiction. This means they can represent clients, provide legal advice, and advocate for their clients in court.

title: Jurisdiction Risk
definition: Jurisdiction risk refers to the risks that can arise when operating in a foreign country or jurisdiction. These risks can arise simply by doing business, or else by lending or borrowing money in another country. Risks could also stem from legal, regulatory, or political factors that exist in different countries or regions.

title: Just Compensation
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Just In Case (JIC)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Just In Time (JIT)
definition: The just-in-time (JIT) inventory system is a management approach that synchronizes raw-material orders from suppliers with production schedules.

title: Justified Wage
definition: A justified wage is a pay level for a job that is determined by market dynamics as well as the degree of skills and experience that the job requires. It is a wage level that is high enough to attract qualified candidates but low enough to be affordable to the business.

title: K
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: K-Percent Rule
definition: The K-Percent Rule, proposed byMilton Friedman, says a central bank should grow themoney supplyby a fixed percentage each year (often framed around roughly 2% to 4% in the U.S.) to support steadier economic conditions.

title: K-Ratio
definition: The K-ratio is a valuation metric that examines the consistency of an equity's return over time. The data for the ratio is derived from avalue-added monthly index(VAMI), which useslinear regressionto track the progress of a $1,000 initial investment in the security being analyzed.

title: Kagi Chart
definition: The Kagi chart is a specialized type oftechnical analysisdeveloped in Japan in the 1870s. It uses a series of vertical lines to illustrate general levels ofsupply and demandfor certain assets, including the price movement of rice, a core Japanese agricultural product. Thick lines are drawn when the price of the underlying asset breaks above the previous high price and is interpreted as an increase in demand for the asset. Thin lines are used to represent increased supply when the price falls below the previous low.

title: Kairi Relative Index
definition: The Kairi Relative Index is a technical analysis metric that traders use to indicate when it is time to buy or sell an asset. It measures the deviation of the price from thesimple moving average(SMA) of that asset's price over a period of time, typically 10 to 20 days.

title: Kaizen
definition: Kaizen is a Japanese business philosophy that aims to make small changes over time to drive ongoing improvement.

title: Kamikaze Defense
definition: A kamikaze defense is an anti-takeover tactic in which management takes extreme steps that intentionally reduce a company's appeal to a hostile bidder, even if the moves harm operations or finances. Common forms include selling the crown jewels, a scorched earth policy, and the fat man strategy. These measures can block atakeover, but they can also leave the company weakened and facing lasting damage to future performance.

title: Kanban
definition: Kanban is an inventory control system used injust-in-time (JIT)manufacturing. It was developed by Taiichi Ohno, an industrial engineer at Toyota, and takes its name from the colored cards that track production and order new shipments of parts or materials as they run out. Kanban is a Japanese word that directly translates to "visual card," so the kanban system simply means to use visual cues to prompt the action needed to keep a process flowing.

title: Kangaroo Bond
definition: A kangaroo bond is a foreign bond issued in Australia by non-Australian entities, denominated in Australian dollars. These bonds offer foreign companies a way to tap into the Australian capital market, often benefiting from Australia's interest rates, and provide local investors with foreign investment opportunities without currency risk.

title: Kangaroos
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Kappa
definition: Kappa measures how sensitive an option’s price is to volatility changes of the underlying asset. This means it shows how much an option’s value might rise or fall when market volatility changes. Understanding kappa is important fortradersbecause volatility can affect option prices and profits or losses. Keep reading to learn kappa’s role and influence in options trading, making it easier to see how volatility can shape trading decisions.

title: Karl Marx
definition: Karl Marx (1818-1883) was a philosopher, author, social theorist, and economist. He is famous for his theories aboutcapitalism, socialism, andcommunism.

title: Kazakhstan National Fund
definition: The Kazakhstan National Fund is a sovereign wealth fund established in 2000 to help stabilize Kazakhstan's economy against fluctuations in oil, gas, and mineral prices. Managed by the National Bank of the Republic of Kazakhstan, the fund is financed mainly through resource-related taxes. Its operations remain largely undisclosed, with past international legal disputes drawing attention to its governance and transparency.

title: KBW Bank Index
definition: The KBW Bank Index is a key stock index that represents the U.S. banking sector. It highlights the largest national money center banks, regional banks, and thrift institutions. Created by Keefe, Bruyette, and Woods (KBW) in 1991, it helpsinvestorswho want to gauge the health and performance of the banking industry. The index, which has 24 bank stocks, serves as a benchmark that helps market analysts and investors track trends and movements across the financial sector. Discover how the KBW Bank Index operates, its historical background, and why it remains an essential tool for banking sector analysis.

title: Keep and Pay
definition: A prepayment is the satisfaction of a debt before its official due date. It can apply to goods, services, or loans.

title: Keepwell Agreement
definition: A keepwell agreement is a contract in which a parent company promises to support its subsidiary so that it can stay solvent and meet its obligations. It's especially useful duringliquidityproblems, since the parent's backing can improve thesubsidiary'screditworthiness and reassure lenders, shareholders, and suppliers.

title: Keiretsu
definition: Keiretsu is a Japanese business network of interlinked companies, such as manufacturers,supply chainpartners, and distributors, that collaborate closely while remaining operationally independent.

title: Kellogg School of Management
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Kelly Criterion
definition: John L. Kelly Jr. created the Kelly criterion formula in 1956 at AT&T's Bell Laboratories. It helps determine how much to invest in a given asset to maximize wealth growth over time.

title: Keltner Channel
definition: Keltner Channels are volatility-based bands that are placed on either side of an asset's price and can aid in determining the direction of a trend.

title: Kenneth Arrow
definition: Note:Although this account has "money market" in its name, it offers no check-writing privileges and instead operates like a savings account.

title: Kenney Rule
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Keogh Plan
definition: "Keogh plan" is a term used to describe qualified retirement plans that coverself-employedpeople and unincorporated businesses. This includesdefined-benefit plans and defined-contribution plans, though most plans are set as the latter. Established in the 1960s with the Self-Employed Individuals Tax Retirement Act, Keogh plans have a long history and have evolved over time.

title: KES (Kenyan Shilling)
definition: The Kenyan shilling (KES) is the official currency of the Republic of Kenya.

title: Key Currency
definition: A key currency refers to a currency which is stable, does not fluctuate much, and provides the foundation for exchange rates for international transactions. Because of their global use, key currencies tend to set the value of other currencies. Also, these currencies tend to have a stable valuation over time. A key currency usually comes from a country that is financially strong, economically stable and developed, and one that is involved in the global market.

title: Key Employee
definition: A key employee is an employee with a major ownership and/or decision-making role in the business. Key employees are usually highly compensated either monetarily with benefits, or both. Key employees may also receive special benefits as an incentive both to join the company and to stay with the company.

title: Key Money
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Key Performance Indicators (KPI)
definition: Key performance indicators (KPIs) are quantifiable measures used to evaluate progress toward specific business objectives.

title: Key Person Insurance
definition: Key person insurance is an importantlife insurancepolicy for businesses. A company buys a key person policy on an owner, top executive, or critical individual, protecting them against financial risk.

title: Key Rate
definition: The key rate is the specific interest rate that determines bank lending rates and the cost of credit for borrowers. The two key interest rates in the U.S. are thediscount rateand thefederal funds rate. These are rates that are set, either directly or indirectly, by the Federal Reserve, in order to influence lending and the supply of money and credit in the economy.

title: Key Rate Duration
definition: Key rate duration is a measure of bond price sensitivity to changes at different points along the yield curve. As opposed to effective duration, which looks at changes in value as a consequence of the yield curve's parallel shifts, key rate duration is used to assess value changes due to non-parallel shifts.

title: Key Ratio
definition: Key ratios convey a company's financial health in a single metric, offering a clear snapshot of its overall performance and stability.

title: Keynesian Economics
definition: Keynesian economics is a macroeconomic theory that advocates for government intervention and spending to help stabilize the economy, especially during times of economic instability.

title: Keynesian Put
definition: Keynesian economics is a macroeconomic theory that advocates for government intervention and spending to help stabilize the economy, especially during times of economic instability.

title: Keystone XL Pipeline
definition: The Keystone XL pipeline project is a controversial plan to construct a 1,700-mile oil conduit from the oil fields of Alberta, Canada to the refineries along the Texas Gulf Coast. The original Keystone pipeline project was abandoned in 2021 but the change in the U.S. presidency in 2025 appears to have reopened the possibility of its construction. Its resumption appears unlikely, however. The original developer has indicated no interest in restarting the project.

title: Kickback
definition: A kickback is an illegal payment intended as compensation for preferential treatment or any other type of improper service received. The kickback may be money, a gift, credit, or anything of value. Paying or receiving kickbacks is a corrupt practice that interferes with an employee's or a public official’s ability to make unbiased decisions. Kickbacks are often referred to as a type of bribery.

title: Kicker
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Kicker Pattern
definition: A kicker pattern as a two-bar candlestick formation used by traders to predict shifts in market trends. This pattern is characterized by a sharp reversal in price over the span of two candlesticks.  It's a reliable indicator for identifying potential reversals in price direction, particularly after significant market news or changes in a company's fundamentals.

title: Kicking The Tires
definition: Kicking the tires is a colloquial expression that refers to performing minimal research into an investment, as opposed to conducting a thorough and rigorous analysis. The process usually includes a cursory reading of the company'sannual report, looking at its historical earning and revenue performance, considering the company's competitive strengths and weaknesses, and reading news articles or headlines about the company.

title: Kiddie Tax
definition: The kiddie tax, introduced with theTax Reform Act of 1986, targets unearnedincomeof minors and young adults to prevent families from evading taxes by shifting investment income to children.

title: Kids In Parents' Pockets Eroding Retirement Savings (KIPPERS)
definition: Kids In Parents' Pockets Eroding Retirement Savings (KIPPERS) is a slang term for adult children who are still living at home with their parents even after finishing school and reaching working age. Their parents face the challenges of managing their own finances and planning for retirement while dealing with the added expense of housing and feeding their adult offspring.

title: Kijun Line (Base Line)
definition: The Kijun Line, also called the Base Line or Kijun-sen, is one of five components that make up theIchimoku Cloudindicator. The Kijun Line is typically used in conjunction with theConversion Line(Tenkan-sen) to generate trade signals when they cross. These signals can be further filtered via the other components of the Ichimoku indicator.

title: Kijun-Sen (Base Line)
definition: The Kijun-sen, or base line, is an indicator and important component of theIchimoku Kinko Hyomethod of technical analysis, which is also known as the Ichimoku cloud.

title: Kill
definition: Fill or kill (FOK) is a conditional type oftime-in-forceorder used insecuritiestrading that instructs a brokerage to execute a transaction immediately and completely or not at all. Active traders mostly use this type of order, usually for a large quantity of stock. The ordermust be filledin its entirety or else canceled (killed).

title: Killer Application
definition: A killer application—or a killer app—is a software program with a user-interface perceived as innovative enough to influence computing trends and sales. The term dates to the early development of personal computers and software in the 1980s, when accounting, database, and word-processing applications were first being developed for mass use.

title: Killer Bees

title: Kimchi Premium
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Kin
definition: Kin was the cryptocurrency for the messaging service Kik. The service was created and maintained by Kik Interactive. Kik developed a cryptocurrency for the messenger community and kicked off an initial coin offering to raise funds for it.

title: Kiosk
definition: A kiosk is a standalone structure designed to facilitate consumer transactions or information sharing in high-traffic locations, potentially functioning without direct human interaction.

title: Kiting
definition: Kiting is a form of financial fraud that exploits timing delays in transaction processing.

title: Kiwi
definition: The New Zealand dollar (NZD) is the official currency of New Zealand and its associated territories, including the Cook Islands, Niue, Tokelau, and the Pitcairn Islands. NZD is made up of 100 cents and is often represented by the symbol $ or NZ$ to set it apart from other currencies based on dollars. It transitioned from the British monetary system to the current decimal system in 1967.

title: Kiwi Bond
