definition: The term Kiwi Bond refers to a type offixed income securityoffered and backed by the government of New Zealand. Kiwi Bonds provide investors with afixed rate of interestfor a set period of time—anywhere from six months to four years—and may be purchased for as little as NZ$1,000 up a maximum of NZ$500,000. These securities are only available to residents of New Zealand.﻿﻿

title: Klinger Oscillator
definition: The Klinger oscillator was developed by Stephen Klinger to determine the long-term trend ofmoney flowwhile remaining sensitive enough to detect short-term fluctuations. The indicator compares thevolumeflowing through securities with the security's price movements and then converts the result into anoscillator. The Klinger oscillator shows the difference between two moving averages which are based on more than price. Traders watch fordivergenceon the indicator to signal potential price reversals. Like other oscillators, asignal linecan be added to provide additional trade signals.

title: KMF
definition: The term Comorian franc (KMF) refers to the official nationalcurrencyof Comoros, a sovereign African island nation located in the Indian Ocean. It is maintained and issued by the country'scentral bank, la Banque Centrale des Comores.It is represented by the symbol KMF on global currency exchanges.The Comorian franc waspeggedto the euro as of 1999 at a rate of 491.96775 Comorian francs to one euro.As of January 2022, $1 U.S. is equal to roughly 437 KMF.

title: Knock-In Option
definition: A knock-in option is a latent option contract that begins to function as a normal option ("knocks in") only once a certain price level is reached before expiration. Knock-ins are a type ofbarrier optionthat are classified as either adown-and-inor anup-and-in. A barrier option is a type of contract in which the payoff depends on whether the underlying security's price hits a specific level within a given time.

title: Knock-Out Option
definition: A knock-out option is a barrier option with a built-in mechanism causing it to expire worthless if the underlying asset hits a specified price level. This design introduces limits on potential gains, allowing these options to be acquired at lower premiums than standard options. Primarily utilized by large institutions in commodity and currency markets, knock-out options may also be traded over-the-counter. Understanding both "down-and-out" and "up-and-out" types helps investors manage risk with specific strategies.

title: Know Sure Thing (KST)
definition: The Know Sure Thing (KST) is a momentumoscillatordeveloped by Martin Pring to simplify rate-of-change readings for traders. It uses smoothed-rate-of-change calculations with signal lines to identify trading signals and is often combined with other technical analysis tools for better market insights.

title: Know Your Client (KYC)
definition: Know Your Client (KYC) is a vital standard in investment and financial services, designed to verify client identities and assess financial profiles. Its implementation through components like the Customer Identification Program (CIP) and other due diligence measures ensures compliance and helps mitigate risks in customer relationships.

title: Knowledge Capital
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Knowledge Economy
definition: A knowledge economy is an economic system that emphasizes the use of information and intellectual capabilities over physical inputs and natural resources.

title: Knowledge Engineering
definition: Knowledge engineering is a field ofartificial intelligence(AI) that creates rules to apply to data to imitate the thought process of a human expert. It looks at the structure of a task or a decision to identify how a conclusion is reached.

title: Knowledge Process Outsourcing (KPO)
definition: Knowledge process outsourcing (KPO) is theoutsourcingof core, information-related business activities. KPO involves contracting out work to individuals who typically have advanced degrees and expertise in a specialized area.

title: KOF Economic Barometer
definition: The KOF Economic Barometer forecasts the performance of the Swiss business cycle and functions as a benchmark for employment trends.

title: Kondratieff Wave
definition: Kondratieff Wave, named after Russian economist Nikolai Kondratieff, refers to cycles, lasting about 40 to 60 years, experienced by capitalist economies. Also known as "Kondratiev waves," "super-cycles," "K-waves," "surges," and/or "long waves."

title: Kondratiev Wave
definition: A Kondratiev Wave is a long-term economic cycle in commodity prices and other prices, believed to result from technological innovation, that produces a long period of prosperity alternating with economic decline. This theory was founded by Nikolai D. Kondratiev (also spelled "Kondratieff"), an agricultural economist who noticed agricultural and industrial commodity prices experienced long-term cycles. Kondratiev believed that these cycles involved periods of evolution and self-correction.

title: Korea Investment Corporation
definition: The Korea Investment Corporation (KIC) is a government-owned investment organization that manages thesovereign wealth fund(SWF) for the Government of South Korea. The KIC was established by law in 2005 and funded in 2006. The KIC received initial deposits of $17 billion from the Bank of Korea and $3 billion from the Korean Ministry of Strategy and Finance.

title: Korea Stock Exchange (KSC)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Korean Composite Stock Price Indexes (KOSPI)
definition: Korean Composite Stock Price Indexes (KOSPI) refers to a series of indexes that track the overall Korean Stock Exchange and its components. Each of the KOSPI indexes are capitalization-weighted market averages.

title: Krugerrands
definition: Krugerrands are South African gold bullion coins first introduced in 1967 in South Africa. Krugerrands became very important to investors because they dominated the global gold-coin market in 1980 and are still a popular way to invest in gold. They are widely available, easy to trade, and valued mainly for their gold content rather than their rarity. This guide provides an overview of Krugerrand gold content, how to buy them, and what affects their current market value.

title: KSOP
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Kuala Lumpur Stock Exchange (KLS)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Kurtosis
definition: Kurtosis is a measurement used in financial analysis to gauge an investment’s risk of price volatility.

title: Kuwait Investment Authority
definition: Norway's Sovereign Wealth Fund - the Government Pension Fund Global- just crossed $1 trillion dollars in assets. Weakness in the U.S. dollar,combinedwith the robust equity markets have driven the fund's dollar value past the landmark.

title: Kuwaiti Dinar (KWD)
definition: The Kuwaiti dinar (KWD) is the national currency of the State of Kuwait, which is located in the Middle East on the Arabian Peninsula right on the Persian Gulf. The dinar name derives from the Roman denarius. The KWD, which is the dinar’sforexacronym, subdivides into 1000 fils, which is a coin used in many Arab countries.

title: KYD (Cayman Islands Dollar)
definition: The forex market is one of the largest and most active financial markets in the world, but it also attracts dishonest players. Commonforex scamsinclude manipulated bid-ask spreads, dishonest signal sellers, and automated trading systems, or “robots,” that don’t perform as promised. Warning signs include unregulated brokers, mixing client funds with company money, and difficulties withdrawing funds. To stay safe, make sure you do your research and choose reputable brokers and trading systems.

title: Kyoto Protocol
definition: The Kyoto Protocol was an international agreement that aimed to reducecarbon dioxideemissions and the presence ofgreenhouse gases(GHG) in the atmosphere. The essential tenet of the Kyoto Protocol was that industrialized nations needed to reduce their CO2 emissions. The protocol was adopted in Kyoto, Japan, in 1997, as greenhouse gas emissions threatened climate stability.It was effectively replaced by the Paris Agreement, which went into effect in 2016.

title: L Share Annuity Class
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Labor Intensive
definition: The term “labor intensive” refers to anindustrythat requires a large amount of labor to produce its goods or services. Since labor-intensive industries need more labor thancapital, labor costs are more important and larger than capital costs.

title: Labor Market
definition: The labor market refers to the supply of and demand for labor. Also known as thejob market, it's based on employees providing the supply and employers providing the demand. It's a major component of any economy, and it's intricately linked to markets forcapital, goods, and services.

title: Labor Market Flexibility
definition: Labor market flexibility is an important aspect of thelabor market. It allows companies to make certain decisions about changing their labor force in response to fluctuations in the market and to help boost production.

title: Labor Productivity
definition: Labor productivity is a measurement of real GDP and aggregate work hours in an economy.

title: Labor Theory Of Value (LTV)
definition: A commodity's worth is determined by the labor time necessary for its production, as proposed by a specific economic theory.

title: Labor Union
definition: A labor union is a collective organization of workers formed to negotiate with employers over pay, benefits, and working conditions. Historicallysignficant in the United States, labor unions represent diverse industries and have played a vital role in improving labor rights since the 1700s. Despite a decline in membership, unions like the NEA continue to advocate for workers today. This article delves into the definition, history, and examples of labor unions, exploring their impact and modern-day relevance.

title: Laddering
definition: Laddering is a financial planning technique that helps investors manage risk and cash flow by purchasing investments that mature at different intervals.

title: Laffer Curve
definition: The Laffer Curve showcases the intricate relationship between tax rates and government revenue, a concept popularized by economist Arthur Laffer in 1974. This theory posits that both excessively high and low tax rates result in reduced tax revenues, suggesting that tax cuts can potentially enhance revenue collection.

title: Laggard
definition: A laggard is a stock or security that is underperforming relative to its benchmark or peers. A laggard will have lower-than-average returns compared to the market. A laggard is the opposite of a leader.

title: Lagging Indicator
definition: A lagging indicator is an economic or business metric that shows changes only after the broader economy has started to shift. They cannot forecast future economic shifts, but they can confirm existing trends.

title: Laissez-Faire
definition: Laissez-faire, an 18th-century economic theory, firmly rejectsgovernmentintervention in business. Rooted in the belief that minimal government involvement benefits both business and society, this theory underpinsfree-marketcapitalism. The term, French for "leave alone" or "let you do," encapsulates the idea that industries and markets should operate without governmental constraints. Laissez-faire champions the notion that a naturally self-regulated market fosters greater efficiency and prosperity.

title: Lambda
definition: In finance and investing, lambda measures how sensitive an option's price is to changes in the underlying asset's implied volatility. The metric is like a volatility gauge for your options portfolio, telling you how quickly your positions will likely change as market turbulence increases or decreases.

title: Land
definition: Land, in the business sense, refers toreal estateor property, without buildings and equipment, defined by fixed boundaries and often including rights to natural resources and even the airspace above. In economics, land is one of three fundamental factors of production, alongside capital and labor.

title: Land Contract
definition: A land contract is a seller-financed loan drawn up by a buyer and seller. Although it's called a land contract, the deal can include properties like homes, pools, sheds, and other real estate. While land contracts can be individual loans, developers also use land contracts when dealing with real estate property.

title: Land Trust
definition: A land trust is a fiduciary relationship where real estate is held and managed by a third party at the owner's request.

title: Land Value
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Land Value Tax (LVT)
definition: A land value tax (LVT) is a method of taxing property based solely on the value of the land itself, excluding any buildings or improvements on the property.

title: Landlocked
definition: Landlocked in the context ofreal estaterefers to a piece of property that is inaccessible via public thoroughfare, except through an adjacent lot. A vacant lot that is located behind a strip mall and can only be reached by walking through the mall qualifies as this type of lot. Landlocked property is locked up, meaning it's surrounded by other property.

title: Landlord
definition: A landlord is someone who owns and rents real estate. Landlords can be individuals, businesses, or other entities. Landlords typically provide the necessary maintenance or repairs during the rental period. Specific duties and obligations are normally outlined in aleaseagreement.

title: Lapse
definition: A lapse is the removal or expiration of a privilege, right, or policy due to the passage of time or inaction. A lapse occurs when the party that meant to receive the benefit does not meet the conditions or requirements outlined in the contract or agreement.

title: Large Cap (Big Cap)
definition: Large-cap stocks represent companies with a market capitalization over $10 billion. They're integral to the U.S. equity market because they're a core component in investment portfolios due to their stability, reliable dividends, and transparency.

title: Large Trader
definition: A large trader is an investor or organization with trades that trades meet or exceed amounts specified by the U.S.Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

title: Larry Ellison
definition: Larry Ellison co-founded the software giant Oracle Corp. (ORCL) in 1977. Under his leadership, Oracle grew from a start-up with three programmers into the largest database software supplier and the second-largest supplier of business applications.

title: Last In, First Out (LIFO)
definition: Last in, first out (LIFO) is a method used to account for business inventory that records the most recently produced items in a series as the ones that are sold first. That is, the cost of the most recent products purchased or produced is the first to be expensed ascost of goods sold (COGS), while the cost of older products, which is often lower, will be reported as inventory.

title: Last Mile
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Last Trading Day
definition: Quantitative easing (QE) is a powerful tool used by central banks, such as the U.S. Federal Reserve, to stimulate economic activity when traditional monetary policy options become ineffective. By purchasing securities in the open market, QE aims to lower interest rates and boost the money supply, providing banks with additional liquidity. This increased liquidity encourages lending and investment, thereby supporting economic growth.

title: Last Twelve Months (LTM)
definition: When evaluating a company's financial health, the performance over the last 12 months provides critical insight into its operations and market position.

title: Last Will And Testament
definition: A last will and testament is a legal document that communicates a person's final wishes pertaining to theirassets. It provides specific instructions about what to do with their possessions. It will indicate whether the deceased leaves them to another person, a group, or wishes todonate them to charity.

title: Late Fee
definition: A late fee is a charge that lenders and other companies impose on you when you fail to make an on-time payment. You may face late fees if you're late making payments on a loan, a credit card, or any other type of financial agreement, such as an insurance policy or rental contract.

title: Law of Demand
definition: The law of demand states that as the price of a good rises, consumers will demand correspondingly lower quantities of that good.

title: Law of Diminishing Marginal Productivity
definition: The law of diminishing marginal productivity states that as the inputs to a productive process increase, the additional benefit tends to decrease. In other words, each additional dollar or unit of input yields slightly less benefit than the last.

title: Law of Diminishing Marginal Returns: Definition, Example, Use in Economics
definition: The law of diminishing marginal returns states that, after a specific threshold, each additional unit of input produces less additional output when other inputs remain fixed.

title: Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility
definition: The law of diminishing marginal utility impacts a company’s pricing because the price must correspond to the consumer’s willingness to consume a product.

title: Law of Large Numbers
definition: The law of large numbers states that the larger a sample size is, the closer it gets to the average of the population being measured.

title: Law of One Price
definition: The law of one price ensures that similar goods are priced consistently across markets, maintaining price uniformity by adjusting for differences like transaction costs.

title: Law of Supply
definition: The law of supply, a fundamental concept inmicroeconomics, asserts that as the price of a good or service rises, suppliers are eager to offer more of it, aiming to increase theirprofits. Conversely, if prices fall, suppliers typically provide less. This law works alongside the law of demand to shape how resources and prices are determined across market economies.

title: Law of Supply and Demand
definition: The law of supply and demand compares supplier preferences (i.e., supply) with consumer preferences (i.e., demand). All else being equal, supply rises while demand declines as the price increases. Levels of supply and demand for varying prices can be plotted on a graph as curves, and the intersection of these curves marks theequilibriumor market-clearing price at which demand equals supply and represents the process ofprice discoveryin the marketplace.

title: Lawful Money
definition: Lawful money refers tocurrencyissued directly by the U.S. Treasury, such as gold and silver coins or certain Treasury notes, and differs from the Federal Reserve's fiat money, which has nointrinsic value.

title: Layaway
definition: Layaway is a method of buying merchandise. A consumer makes a down payment on an item which the store then holds for them while they pay the remainder of the price in installments. They can then take possession of the purchased item when they've fully paid for it. Layaway programs became more common during the Great Depression but declined beginning in the 1980s.

title: Layoff
definition: A layoff is an involuntary job termination, usually initiated by the employer as a cost-cutting measure.Laid-off workers can get governmentunemployment benefits, while those fired for cause can't.

title: Lead Bank
definition: A lead bank is the primary institution responsible for organizing and managingloan syndicationsor underwriting large financial transactions. It recruits syndicate members, negotiates financing terms, and oversees the structure of the deal.

title: Lead Time
definition: Lead time, a crucial component inmanufacturingandsupply chain management, measures the duration from the beginning to the end of a process. Companies assess lead time during the pre-processing, processing, and post-processing phases to pinpoint and address inefficiencies. By reducing lead time, businesses can enhance operations, boost productivity, and increase both output and revenue. Conversely, extended lead times can disrupt sales and manufacturing processes, underlining the importance of efficient management.

title: Leadership
definition: Business leadership defines a company's management capacity to not only set and achieve goals but also inspire high performance among employees. Leadership quality impacts the company’s culture and competitiveness. It is essential that leaders provide direction, motivate teams, and supervise effectively, while embodying core values like integrity and optimism.

title: Leadership Grid
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Leading Indicator
definition: A leading indicator is a measurable set of data that may help to forecast future economic activity. Businesses, policy makers, and investors can use them to predict changes in theeconomybefore it begins to shift.

title: Leads and Lags
definition: Leads and lags in international business usually refer to the deliberate acceleration or delaying of payments due in a foreign currency. The goal is to take advantage of an expected change in currency exchange rates. Corporations and governments may time payments due in a foreign currency if they anticipate a change in currency values that's in their favor.

title: Leakage
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Lean Six Sigma
definition: Lean Six Sigma is a business strategy that aims to create efficiencies by identifying the causes of waste and redundancy and developing solutions to address them.

title: Lean Startup: Key Differences from Traditional Business Models
definition: A lean startup is a business approach focused on creating products that meet proven consumer needs rather than assuming demand. Developed by Eric Ries, it emphasizes validated learning, testing ideas quickly, gathering feedback, and adjusting early to minimize costs and failures.

title: Learning Curve
definition: The learning curve shows that tasks require less time and resources the more often they are performed.

title: Lease Definition and Complete Guide to Renting
definition: Leases are a cornerstone of property rental agreements, defining the relationship between the tenant (lessee) and the landlord (lessor). These legally binding contracts outline the conditions under which one party agrees to rent a property from another. In exchange, the lessee is granted access to the property, while the lessor receives regular payments for a specified duration. If either party fails to meet the obligations, legal consequences may arise. A lease is a form ofincorporeal right.

title: Understanding Lease Extensions: Definition, Process, and Practical Examples
definition: A lease extension refers to a legal agreement that extends the term of an existing lease or rental agreement. Extensions are not a requirement in a business relationship but are often granted just before an original agreement is set to expire. They are common in relationships between landlords and tenants of commercial and residential property, or between parties who lease vehicles, machinery, plants, and equipment.

title: Lease Option
definition: A lease option is an agreement that gives a renter the choice to purchase the rented property during or at the end of the rental period. It also precludes the owner from offering the property for sale to anyone else. When the term expires, the renter must either exercise the option or forfeit it. A lease option is also known as a lease with the option to purchase.

title: Lease Payments
definition: A lease payment is the recurring amount paid for the right to use an asset, like property, equipment, or software, without owning it. Payment levels depend on factors such as asset value, creditworthiness, and contract terms, which may span from monthly agreements to long land leases. Different lease types also vary in what they include, such as whether maintenance is covered.

title: Lease Rate: What it is, How it Works, Types
definition: A lease rate is the amount of money paid over a specified time period for the rental of an asset, such asreal propertyor an automobile. The lease rate—the amount thelessorearns from allowing someone else to use their property—compensates them for not being able to use that property during the term of the lease.

title: Leaseback
definition: A leaseback, or sale-leaseback, is a unique financial arrangement where a company sells anassetand then leases it back from the buyer. This innovative transaction allows the seller, who becomes the lessee, to unlock the capital tied up in assets such as buildings, land, or expensive equipment while continuing to use them as before.

title: Leasehold
definition: A leasehold is an accounting term for an asset being leased. The asset is typically property, such as a building or space in a building. Thelesseecontracts with thelessorfor the right to use the property in exchange for a series of scheduled payments over the term of the lease. Renting space in an office building for a company's use or renting a building to be used for a retail store are two examples of a commercial leasehold arrangement.

title: Leasehold Improvement
definition: The term leasehold improvement refers to changes made to customize a rentalpropertyto satisfy the particular needs of a specific tenant. These changes and alterations may include painting, installing partitions, changing the flooring, or putting in customized light fixtures. Improvements may be undertaken by thelandlordor the tenant andmay be paid by the tenant.

title: Least Squares Criterion
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Least Squares Method
definition: The least squares method determines the line of best fit for a dataset represented on a graph.

title: Least-Preferred Coworker Scale
definition: Developed by American scholar Fred Fiedler, the scale identifies whether an individual'sleadership styleis either relationship-oriented or task-oriented.

title: Ledger Balance
definition: Financial institutions calculate a ledger balance at the close of a business day when they’ve posted all completed transactions.

title: Understanding Ledger Wallets: How Hardware Crypto Wallets Operate
definition: A Ledger wallet is a cryptocurrency wallet made by Ledger, a company that makes physical cryptocurrency wallets that look similar to USB drives or other storage devices. Ledger provides several hardware and cold wallet options and assures users that their wallets are secure for crypto assets.

title: What Was FTX? An Overview of the Exchange
definition: FTX Exchange was a leading centralized cryptocurrency exchange specializing in spot markets, derivatives, options, volatility, and leveraged products. It was founded in 2018 by Sam Bankman-Fried, a Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) graduate and former Jane Street Capital international exchange-traded funds (ETFs) trader.

title: Leg
definition: A leg is one piece of a multi-part trade strategy, often inderivativestrading, in which a trader combines multiple options or futures contracts. In rare cases, they might combine both types of contracts tohedgea position, benefit from arbitrage, or profit from a spread widening or tightening. Within these strategies, each derivative contract or position in the underlying security is called a leg.

title: Legal Lending Limit
definition: A bank's legal lending limit is the maximum amount it can lend to a single borrower, calculated as a percentage of its capital and surplus. Regulated by theOffice of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC), the standard cap is 15%, which may rise to 25% if the excess portion is fully secured by approved collateral.

title: Legal Monopoly
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Legal Rate of Interest
definition: The legal rate of interest sets a cap on how much lenders can charge, helping prevent excessive or usurious rates that could harm borrowers. Usury laws and their penalties vary widely by jurisdiction, adding complexity to how these limits are applied, and some lenders, such as credit card issuers and national banks, can sidestep state caps through incorporation practices.

title: Legal Separation
definition: A legal separation allows married couples to live independently while setting clear financial and parenting boundaries. Unlike divorce, it maintains the marriage's legal status, which might benefit couples unsure about permanently ending their relationship. This separation can simplify financial arrangements, aid in religious adherence, or provide stability for minor children.

title: Legal Tender
definition: Legal tender is the legally recognized medium for settling debts, taxes, and other financial obligations within a country, usually itsnational currency, which creditors must accept as payment. Laws supporting legal tender help ensure a stable monetary system and prevent competing forms of money, supporting monetary policy.

title: Legislative Risk
definition: Legislative risk is the potential that regulations or legislation by the government could significantly alter the business prospects of one or more companies. These changes can adversely affect investment holdings in that company. Legislative risk may occur as a direct result of government action or by altering the demand patterns of the company's customers.

title: Lehman Aggregate Bond Index
definition: The Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based fixed-income index used by bond traders and the managers of mutual funds and exchange-traded funds as a benchmark to measure their relative performance.

title: Lehman Brothers
definition: Lehman Brothers, once the fourth-largestinvestment bankin the U.S., declared bankruptcy on September 15, 2008, marking the largest bankruptcy filing in U.S. history and a pivotal moment in the global financial crisis.

title: Lehman Formula
definition: The Lehman Formula, developed byLehman Brothersin the 1960s, is a compensation model used by investment banks to structure commission fees based on the value of client transactions. Known for its tiered fee structure, the formula provides a transparent and straightforward way for clients to estimate costs and for banks to incentivize profit-driven performance.

title: Lemons Problem
definition: The lemon problem occurs when buyers have less information than sellers about a product's quality. This leads to a decline in the overall quality of goods and market failure.

title: Lender
definition: Lenders, ranging from financial institutions to private investors, play a crucial role in providing funds to individuals and businesses with the expectation of repayment. They thoroughly assess credit histories, collateral, and financial health to decide on loan approvals. This article dives into what lenders look for, the different types of lenders, and the factors impacting their decisions.

title: Lender of Last Resort
definition: A lender of last resort (LoR) is an institution, usually a country'scentral bank, that offers loans to banks or other eligible institutions that are experiencing financial difficulty or are considered highly risky or near collapse. In the United States, theFederal Reserveacts as the lender of last resort to institutions that do not have any other means of borrowing, and whose failure to obtain credit would dramatically affect the economy.

title: Leptokurtic Distributions
definition: A leptokurtic distribution is a tool used in technical analysis. It is a type of data distribution with a high peak and fatter tails, meaning its kurtosis is greater than three. This shows a higher chance of extreme events compared to a normal distribution, which has a kurtosis of three (or aplatykurticdistribution), which is flatter with thinner tails. Understanding leptokurtic distributions is important for investors because it highlights the potential for unusual or extreme outcomes.

title: Less-Developed Countries (LDC)
definition: Least-developed countries (LDCs) (sometimes referred to as less-developed countries) are underdeveloped countries that face significant structural challenges to sustainable development. The UN's list of LDCs currently comprises 46 countries.

title: What Is a Lessee? Key Rights and Responsibilities Explained
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Lessor: Definition, Types, vs. Landlord and Lessee
definition: A lessor is essentially someone who grants a lease to someone else. As such, a lessor is the owner of an asset that is leased under an agreement to a lessee. Thelesseemakes a one-time payment or a series of periodic payments to the lessor in return for the use of theasset. For example, a landlord is a lessor, and a tenant is a lessee.

title: Letter of Comfort
definition: A letter of comfort is a document that provides assurance that an obligation will be met. It is not legally binding. A parent company may write a letter of comfort on behalf of its subsidiary to help the subsidiary obtain a loan. Or, an externalauditormay attach a letter of comfort regarding an audit, statements, and reports used for aprospectusas assurance that it will not be materially different from the final version. A letter of comfort is also known as aletter of intentor a solvency opinion.

title: Letter of Credit
definition: A letter of credit is a crucial financial instrument in international trade, providing a guarantee from a bank that a buyer’s payment to a seller will occur on time and for the specified amount. When buyers face challenges in meeting their financial commitments, thebanksteps in to cover unpaid amounts, ensuring seamless trade transactions.

title: Letter of Guarantee
definition: A bank typically issues a letter of guarantee on behalf of a client who has entered into a contract to buy goods from a supplier. The letter contractually guarantees to pay the recipient even if the client should default. To get a letter of guarantee, the customer will need to apply for it, like a loan. If the bank is comfortable with the risk, it will back the customer with the letter for an annual fee. Letters of guarantee are used in importing and exporting, commercial contracts, margin trades, major purchases, real estate investments, mergers and acquisitions, and other significant financial transactions.

title: Letter of Indemnity
definition: A letter of indemnity (LOI) ensures that contracting parties are protected by guaranteeing the fulfillment of contractual obligations or offering a financial safety net in case of breaches. Applied across various sectors, from global trade to local services, an LOI can be a valuable tool to mitigate financial risks and facilitate smooth transactions.

title: Letter of Intent (LOI)
definition: A letter of intent (LOI) outlines deal terms and acts as an "agreement to agree" between two parties.

title: Level 1
definition: A Level 1 trading screen provides real-time bid, offer, and volume quotes, known as thenational best bid and offer(NBBO), allowing investors to gauge a stock's price and performance quickly. These quotes are widely accessible through online trading platforms, making them suitable for most investors. However, more active traders may prefer Level 2 quotes, which offer deeper insight into market activity and order flow.

title: Level 2
definition: First introduced in 1983 as the Nasdaq Quotation Dissemination Service (NQDS), Level 2 is a subscription-based service that provides real-time access to the NASDAQorder book. Introduced as Nasdaq Quotation Dissemination Service in 1983, it is intended to display market depth and momentum totradersandinvestors.

title: Level 3
definition: A level III quote is pricing information about asecurityprovided by a trading service. It includes the real-time bid price, ask price, quote size, price of the last trade, size of the last trade, high price for the day, and low price for the day.

title: Level 1 Assets
definition: Financial Accounting Standard 157 introduced three asset levels: Level 1, Level 2, and Level 3. Level 1 assets include listed stocks, bonds, funds, or any assets with transparent, market-based quoted prices.

title: Level 2 Assets
definition: Level 2 assets are financial assets valued using market price data from similar items rather than direct quoted prices. This makes them less transparent than Level 1 assets but more reliable than Level 3. These assets are commonly held by institutions like private equity firms and insurance companies, and are valued using external data, such as interest rates and yield curves. Investors should understand the differences between these assets because they can judge a company’s financial reporting risk and transparency. Knowing how Level 2 assets work can give investors insight into a company’s financial health so they can make smarter investment decisions.

title: Level 3 Assets
definition: Level 3 assets arefinancial assetsandliabilitiesknown for theirilliquidityand difficulty in valuation. Unlike Level 1 and Level 2 assets, Level 3 assets lack reliable market pricing, leading firms to rely on estimates and models that incorporate assumptions and subjective judgments.

title: Level Death
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Level-Premium Insurance
definition: Level-premium insurance is a type of permanent or term life insurance where the premium remains the same over the policy's life. Premiums are guaranteed to remain the same throughout the contract with this type of contract.

title: Leverage
definition: Financial leverage is the process of taking on debt or borrowing funds to increase returns gained from an investment or a project.

title: Leverage Ratio
definition: A leverage ratio indicates the amount of debt a company or institution carries compared to its assets or equity.

title: Leveraged Buyback
definition: A leveraged buyback is a corporate finance transaction that enables a company to repurchase some of its shares using debt—reducing the number of shares outstanding increases the remaining owners' respective shares.

title: Leveraged Buyout (LBO)
definition: A leveraged buyout (LBO) involves one company acquiring another by financing most of the purchase through borrowed funds, typically by issuing bonds or securing loans. In this process, the company being acquired often has its assets used as collateral, alongside those of the acquiring company.

title: Leveraged Employee Stock Ownership Plan (LESOP)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Leveraged ETF
definition: A leveraged exchange-traded fund (LETF) uses financial derivatives and debt to amplify the returns of an underlying index, stock, specific bonds, or currencies.

title: Leveraged Lease
definition: A leveraged lease is a financing setup where a lessor rents out a high-value asset using funds borrowed from a third-party lender. It gives businesses access to costly equipment without needing to purchase it, making it useful in asset-heavy industries. Both the lessee andlessorbenefit, with the lender supplying the capital that enables the arrangement.

title: Leveraged Loan
definition: A leveraged loan is a type of loan made for borrowers who already have high levels of debt or a low credit score. These loans are considered high-risk and therefore have higher interest rates.

title: Leveraged Loan Index (LLI)
definition: A leveraged loan index (LLI) tracks the performance of a group of leveraged loans, which are made to companies with more debt or risk. It helps investors see how these loans are doing overall and understand trends in this part of the market. People often want to know whether LLIs offer higher returns and what risks they carry, since these loans can pay more interest but also have a greater chance of loss. Keep reading to learn how a leveraged loan index works, why investors follow it, and the key risks and benefits to consider.

title: Leveraged Recapitalization
definition: A leveraged recapitalization is a corporate finance transaction in which a company replaces the majority of its equity with a package of debt securities consisting of both senior bank debt and subordinated debt. A leveraged recapitalization is also referred to as leveraged recap.

title: Levered Free Cash Flow (LFCF)
definition: Levered Free Cash Flow (LFCF) represents the money left after a company satisfies its financial obligations. This residual cash is crucial for paying dividends, reinvesting in the business, and securing additional capital. Calculating LFCF involves subtracting changes in net working capital, capital expenditures, and mandatory debt payments from EBITDA. Understanding LFCF can guide strategic decisions and indicate financial health.

title: Levy
definition: A levy is when the IRS or tax authorities legally take your property or assets, like tax refunds or bank accounts, to pay off an unpaid debt.

title: Liability
definition: A liability is something that a person or company owes, usually a sum of money. Liabilities are settled over time through the transfer of economic benefits including money, goods, or services. They're recorded on the right side of thebalance sheetand include loans,accounts payable, mortgages, deferred revenues, bonds, warranties, and accrued expenses.

title: Liability Driven Investment (LDI)
definition: A liability-driven investment (LDI) is designed to match financial obligations (liabilities) with appropriate income-generating assets. It's primarily used by defined-benefit pension plans and insurance companies that guarantee payouts, now and in the future. Common LDIs are government bonds and inflation-linked bonds.

title: Liability Insurance
definition: Liability insurance shields individuals and businesses from claims arising from injuries and damage caused to people or property. This form of protection is vital for anyone who can be held legally accountable. Liability insurance policies take care of legal expenses and payouts when an insured party is found responsible, though these policies typically do not cover intentional damage or contractual liabilities. Unique among insurance types, liability policies are designed to compensate third parties rather than policyholders.

title: Liar Loan
definition: A liar loan is a mortgage loan that requires little or no income documentation. Because the lender does not verify income and assets by looking at W-2 forms, income tax returns, and other records, such loans are called "liar loans" because lenders simply take the borrower at their word. Learn more about what a liar loan is and how it works.

title: Liar's Poker
definition: "Liar's Poker," a game favored by Wall Street traders, combines strategy, psychology, and luck. Players bet on the frequency of digits in currency serial numbers, making this game both exhilarating and challenging. It's also the title of Michael Lewis's bestselling book, which delves into the high-stakes world of bond trading at Salomon Brothers in the 1980s.

title: Libel
definition: Libel involves the act of publishing a statement about an individual, either in written form or by broadcast over media platforms such as radio, television, or the Internet, that is untrue and threatens to harm the reputation and/or livelihood of the targeted person. Libel is considered a civil wrong (tort) and can, therefore, be the basis of a lawsuit.

title: Liberty Bond
definition: A Liberty Bond is a debt obligation issued by the U.S. Department of the Treasury in conjunction with the Federal Reserve. Also known as a Liberty Loan, it was a war bond, issued in four installments in 1917-18 as a means to finance the U.S.' participation in World War I and the Allied war effort in Europe.

title: LIBOR Curve
definition: The LIBOR curve is the graphical representation of the interest rate term structure of various maturities of the London Interbank Offered Rate, commonly known asLIBOR. LIBOR is a short-term floating rate at which large banks with high credit ratings lend to each other. The LIBOR curve depicts theyield curvefor short-term LIBOR rates of less than one year. The transition from LIBOR to other benchmarks, such as thesecured overnight financing rate(SOFR), began in 2020.﻿﻿

title: LIBOR Scandal
definition: The LIBOR scandal revealed that a global interest rate benchmark had been manipulated.

title: Licensee
definition: A licensee is any individual, business, organization that has been granted legal permission, or certain rights by another entity who owns certain assets, to engage in any activity related to these assets. This permission, or license, can be given on an express or implied basis.

title: Licensing Agreement
definition: A licensing agreement is a legal contract where one party (the licensor) allows another (thelicensee) to use intellectual property like patents,trademarks, or copyrights. These agreements set rules for how the property can be used, including royalties, time limits, and regions. Licensing can help a business reach new markets and earn money. But it also comes with certain risks, such as potential damage to the company's brand or reputation.

title: Licensing Fee
definition: Licensing fees are fixed payments for the right to use intellectual property or to engage in specific business activities.

title: Lien
definition: A lien represents a creditor's legal claim to the debtor's property as collateral, ensuring financial obligations are met. Whether through voluntary agreements or court judgments, liens allowcreditorsto seize assets if debts aren't repaid. This guide explores various lien types, from bank to tax liens, and their role in safeguarding creditor rights while influencing property transactions.

title: Lien Sale
definition: A lien sale involves selling the legal claim against an asset, often at a public auction, to recover unpaid debts. The lien may be on real estate, automobiles, and other personal property. Depending on a particular state's laws, contractors, subcontractors, and suppliers may put a lien on a piece of property they have worked on pending payment for services rendered depending on a state's laws.

title: Lien Waiver
definition: A lien waiver is a key document in the construction industry that ensures smoother transactions and prevents the filing of mechanics' liens. In construction, parties such as contractors, subcontractors, and suppliers frequently use lien waivers to certify they've been paid for their services and relinquish future lien rights on a property. Understanding the four types—conditional and unconditional waivers for both progress and final payments—can help property owners and construction firms manage projects more effectively.

title: Life Annuity
definition: A lifeannuityis a financial product sold byinsurancecompanies that provides fixed, periodicpaymentsto theannuitantfor as long as they live. These products generally have two phases: the accumulation phase, during which the annuitant funds the annuity through periodic contributions or a lump-sum payment, often at retirement, and the annuitization phase, when payouts begin.

title: Life Cycle
definition: In a business context, a life cycle refers to the progression through various stages of development, stability, and decline that a product, company, or industry naturally undergoes over time.

title: Life-Cycle Fund
definition: Life-cycle funds are asset-allocation funds that adjust their allocations for eachasset classover an investor's investment time horizon. This is done to lower pricevolatilityas an investor nears retirement. So, the percentage of bonds and other fixed-income investments increases, while that for equities decreases. Legendary investor Benjamin Graham suggested adjusting investments in stocks and bonds based on market valuations rather than your age.

title: Life-Cycle Hypothesis (LCH)
definition: The life-cycle hypothesis (LCH) is an economic theory that describes the spending andsaving habits of peopleover the course of a lifetime.

title: Life Estate
definition: A life estate is a property, usually a residence, that an individual owns and may use for the duration of their lifetime. This person is called the life tenant and shares ownership of the property with a second person. The second person is referred to as theremaindermanand automatically receives the title to the property upon the life tenant's death.

title: Life Expectancy
definition: Life expectancy is a statistical estimate of the average number of years a person is expected to live , based on actuarial data. There are many uses for life expectancy in the financial industry, including for life insurance, pension planning, and U.S. Social Security benefits. In most countries, the calculations for actuarial ages are derived from a national statistical agency based on large amounts of data.

title: Life Income Fund (LIF)
definition: A life income fund is a Canadian registered retirement income fund used to hold locked-in pension funds as well as other assets for an eventual payout as retirement income.

title: Life Insurance
definition: Life insurance acts as a financial safety net for your family. If you die while it’s active, your insurance company pays a sum of money to the people you’ve named in your policy. This money, known as the death benefit, can help your beneficiaries replace your lost income and cover expenses like housing, food, and utility bills.Life insurancecan also be used to pay for funeral expenses, cover outstanding debts, or leave a legacy for loved ones or charitable organizations.

title: Life Settlement
definition: A life settlement refers to the sale of an existinginsurancepolicy to a third party for a one-time cash payment. Payment is more than the surrender value but less than the actual death benefit. After the sale, the purchaser becomes the policy'sbeneficiaryand assumes payment of its premiums. By doing so, they receive the death benefit when the insured dies.

title: Lifestyle Creep
definition: Lifestyle creep is when your standard of living rises alongside yourdiscretionary income—and soon enough, former luxuries become new necessities. It happens little by little, without you really realizing it: it sneaks ("creeps") up on you.

title: Lifetime Cost
definition: When you're considering buying something, you may know what the price tag says, but do you know what it truly costs? Taking into account the cost of maintaining something like a car, house, or boat can add up to a much higher number than just the purchase price. This total number is known as the lifetime cost.

title: Lifetime Learning Credit
definition: The Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC) is a provision of the U.S. federal income tax code that lets parents and students lower their tax liability by up to $2,000 to help offset higher education expenses. Unfortunately, the LLC is not refundable, which means you can use the LLC to pay the taxes you owe, but you don't get a refund back of any of the credit, according to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS).

title: LIFO Liquidation
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: LIFO Reserve
definition: LIFO reserve is an accounting term that measures the difference between thefirst in, first out(FIFO) andlast in, first out(LIFO) cost of inventory for bookkeeping purposes. The LIFO reserve is an account used to bridge the gap between FIFO and LIFO costs when a company uses the FIFO method to track its inventory but reports under the LIFO method in the preparation of its financial statements. Differences between FIFO and LIFO can affect a company's financial outcome due to variations in reported profits and tax obligations. Understanding LIFO Reserve helps analysts and investors compare companies' financial health accurately.

title: Lightning Network
definition: The Lightning Network is a second layer for Bitcoin (BTC) that uses micropayment channels to scale the blockchain’s capability and handle transactions more efficiently and cheaply. It is a technological solution designed to solve glitches associated with Bitcoin by introducing off-chain transactions. Its channel is a transaction mechanism between two parties in which each can make or receive payments from the other.

title: Like-for-Like Sales
definition: Like-for-like sales are used as an adjusted growth metric that includes revenues generated from stores or products with similar characteristics while omitting any with distinct differences that could skew the numbers. They identify company growth drivers by focusing on established operations and omitting outliers like new acquisitions.

title: Understanding Like-Kind Exchanges: Definitions, Examples, Pros & Cons
definition: A like-kind exchange allows an investor to sell oneassetand buy a similar one while deferring capital gains taxes, rather than paying them at the time of the sale. Since the 2017 tax law, these exchanges are limited to real estate and business property.

title: Like-Kind Property
definition: Like-kind property refers to two real estate assets of a similar nature, regardless of grade or quality, that can be exchanged without incurring any tax liability. The Internal Revenue Code (IRC) defines like-kind properties as any held for investment, trade, or business purposes under Section 1031, making them a 1031 exchange.This means both properties involved in the exchange must be for business or investment purposes. Personal residences do not qualify as like-kind properties.

title: Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act: Definition, History, Impact
definition: The Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act of 2009 is a law enacted by Congress to bolster worker protections against pay discrimination. The Act allows individuals facing pay discrimination to seek rectification under federal anti-discrimination laws.

title: Limit Down
definition: Limit down is a decline in the price of afutures contractor a stock large enough to trigger trading restrictions under exchange rules. Limits on the speed of market price movements, up or down, aim to dampen unusualvolatilityand to give traders time to react to market-moving news, if any.Trading curbstriggered by extreme price movements are sometimes called circuit breakers.

title: Limit Order
definition: A limit order is a buy or sell order for a stock or other asset that is executed automatically when the price reaches or exceeds a specified level.

title: Limit Order Book
definition: A limit order book is a comprehensive record maintained by security specialists or electronic systems that lists all outstanding buy and sell orders to be executed when the market reaches specified prices. A buy limit order is an order to buy at a preset price or lower while a sell limit order is an order to sell a security at a pre-specified price or higher.

title: Limited Common Elements
definition: In a condominium, a limited common element refers to parts of the property that are reserved for specific unit owners' use and enjoyment while being collectively owned by the entire community.

title: Limited Company (LC)
definition: A limited company (LC) is a type of business structure that keeps the personal finances and property of its owners legally distinct from the company's own finances and liabilities.

title: Limited Government
definition: A limited government is a political system where the government's powers are restricted by law to prevent the concentration of power and protect individual liberties.

title: Limited Liability
definition: Limited liability is a legal structure that limits the potential losses of a venture to the amount invested.

title: Limited Liability Company (LLC)
definition: A limited liability company (LLC) is a type of business structure that protects its owners from personal risk while offering the flexibility of a small business and the tax advantages of a partnership.

title: Limited Partner
definition: A limited partner is a passive investor in a limited partnership whose financial responsibility is capped at their investment amount, while not participating actively in the management.

title: Limited Partnership (LP)
definition: A limited partnership (LP) is a business structure where at least two partners own the company, but only one of them manages the business.

title: Limited Partnership Unit (LPU)
definition: Limited partnership units, or LPUs, represent ownership in apublicly traded limited partnershipor master limited partnership (MLP). Unit holders receive an interest in the income that the partnership earns. LPUs are not subject to double taxation and receive flow-through tax treatment according to the Internal Revenue Service. Liability for unit holders is capped at the amount of their capital investment.

title: Limited Power of Attorney (LPOA)
definition: A limited power of attorney (LPOA) is a legal document that allows a portfolio manager to execute specific investment strategies and take care of routine tasks for a client without having to consult with them first. LPOAs grant the portfolio manager the authority to buy and sell assets and manage necessary forms, while the client retains control over major decisions like cash withdrawals and beneficiary changes.

title: Lindahl Equilibrium
definition: A Lindahl equilibrium is a theoreticalpublic-goodsoutcome where supply, demand, and costs balance because people pay amounts tied to their stated preferences. It applies to public goods like clean water and national security and depends on a Lindahl tax, but it's rarely seen in practice because preferences are hard to measure and enforce.

title: Line Chart
definition: A line chart is a visualization of the closing prices of a security over time.

title: Line Graph
definition: A line graph displays trends or variations in data across a continuous interval by connecting individual data points with straight lines.

title: Line of Best Fit
definition: A line of best fit is a form of regression analysis that shows the relationship between two variables. It helps illustrate trends and patterns in data and is often used to predict outcomes in fields like finance. The line is usually determined using the least squares method, which minimizes the distance between the line and the actual data points. By showing the overall trend, the line of best fit is an important tool for decision-making and financial analysis.

title: Line of Business Limitations
definition: Line of business limitations is afederal income taxrule applied to fringe benefits that employers provide their employees. It states that if a company is engaged in multiple lines of business and an employee receives afringe benefitfrom a line of the company's business that she does not work in, she must pay taxes on that benefit.

title: Line of Credit (LOC)
definition: A line of credit (LOC) is an amount of money available for borrowing with the maximum borrowing limit set by the lender. Banks and financial institutions offer LOCs to their personal and business customers. LOCs are a flexible funding source because they can be accessed at any time and no interest is charge except on borrowed funds. As money is repaid, it can be borrowed again. Lenders base the borrowing limit they set on the borrower'screditworthiness.

title: Linear Relationship
definition: Two variables have a linear relationship when they increase and decrease correspondingly.

title: Linearly Weighted Moving Average (LWMA)
definition: A linearly weighted moving average (LWMA) is amoving averagecalculation that more heavily weights recent price data. The most recent price has the highest weighting, and each prior price has progressively less weight. The weights drop in a linear fashion. LWMAs are quicker to react to price changes thansimple moving averages(SMA) andexponential moving averages(EMA).

title: What Are Linked Savings Accounts? Key Benefits and FAQs
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Lintner's Model
definition: The Lintner model is an economic formula for determining an optimal corporatedividend policy. It was proposed in 1956 by former Harvard Business School professor John Lintner and focuses on two core notions:

title: Lipper Indexes
definition: Lipper Indexes are indexes that track thefinancial performanceof different types of managed fund strategies. Each index is based on the performance of the largest publicly traded funds in the strategy group.

title: Liquid Alternatives
definition: Liquid alternatives are mutual funds or exchange-traded funds (ETFs) that use strategies similar to hedge funds while offering daily liquidity.

title: Liquid Asset
definition: Liquid assets can be quickly transacted and turned into cash.

title: Liquid Market
definition: A liquid market is one with many available buyers and sellers and comparatively low transaction costs. The details of what makes a market liquid may vary depending on the asset being exchanged. In a liquid market, it is easy to execute a trade quickly and at a desirable price because there are numerous buyers and sellers and the product being exchanged is standardized and in high demand.In a liquid market despite dailychanges in supplyand demand the spread between what the buyer wants to pay and what sellers will offer remains relatively small.

title: Liquidate
definition: The term “liquidate” means converting property or assets into cash or cash equivalents by selling them on the open market.Liquidationsimilarly refers to the process of bringing a business to an end and distributing its assets to claimants.

title: Liquidated Damages
definition: Liquidated damages (LDs) are a sum of money specified in some contracts that are to be paid by one party to another as compensation for intangible losses. Liquidated damages are to be paid only if one of the parties to the contract is found to be inbreach of contract.

title: Liquidating Dividend
definition: A liquidating dividend is a distribution made by a corporation to its shareholders when the company is undergoing a partial or full liquidation. Liquidating dividends differ from regular dividends because they are primarily paid from the company's capital base rather than its operating profits or retained earnings. A liquidating dividend is also called liquidating distribution.

title: Liquidation
definition: Liquidation in finance and economics is the process of bringing a business to an end and distributing its assets to claimants. It is an event that usually occurs when a company isinsolvent, meaning it cannot pay its obligations when they are due. As company operations end, the remaining assets are used to pay creditors and shareholders, based on the priority of their claims.General partnersare subject to liquidation.

title: Liquidation Margin
definition: The liquidation margin is the value of all the positions in a margin account, including cash deposits and the market value of its open long and short positions.Buying securities on margin allows a trader to acquire more shares than can be purchased on a cash-only basis. If the stock price goes up, earnings are often higher because the investor holds more shares. Traders may lose more than their initial investment, however, if the stock price falls.

title: Liquidation Preference
definition: A liquidation preference determines the order and priority of payments to preferred shareholders when a company is dissolved, ensuring they are compensated before others.

title: Liquidation Value
definition: Liquidation value is the net worth of a company's physical assets, like real estate, fixtures, equipment, and inventory, during bankruptcy. It does not include intangible assets. Potential investors consider liquidation value when making investment decisions.

title: Liquidator
definition: A liquidator is an individual or entity appointed to wind up a company's affairs—typically during bankruptcy—by managing its assets and liabilities. They step in when a company is closing, representing it in lawsuits, selling off assets, and paying off creditors. Liquidators may be appointed by a court, creditors, or shareholders and are essential in ensuring a smooth liquidation process by overseeing assets and addressing claims or lawsuits.

title: Liquidity
definition: Liquidity is the term used in finance to refer to how easy it is to convert an asset to cash and not affect its market price.

title: Liquidity Adjustment Facility
definition: A liquidity adjustment facility is a mechanism offered by central banks to help financial institutions balance their liquidity needs on a short-term basis.

title: Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR)
definition: Mandated by the Basel Accords, the liquidity coverage ratio is the amount of liquid assets that financial institutions must have on hand to ensure they can meet their short-term obligations in the event of market turmoil.

title: Liquidity Crisis
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Liquidity Event
definition: A liquidity event is a transaction that allows investors and founders to convert company ownership into cash, most often through anIPO, merger, or acquisition. It carries major importance for investors seeking returns and for founders navigating control and equity outcomes.

title: Liquidity Preference Theory
definition: The liquidity preference theory was introduced by John Maynard Keynes in 1936. It explains that investors generally prefer holding liquid assets over long-term investments. The theory remains central to understanding monetary conditions, as it shows how the demand for liquidity influences interest rates and economic stability. It is relevant during financial crises when liquidity becomes scarce and highly valued. Readers can gain valuable insights into how interest rates are determined and how asset values respond to changing market conditions.

title: Liquidity Premium
definition: Liquidity premium is the additional compensation used to encourage investments in assets that cannot be easily or quickly converted into cash at fair market value. For example, a long-term bond will carry a higher interest rate than a short-term bond because it is relatively illiquid. The higher return is the liquidity premium offered to the investor as compensation for the additional risk.

title: Liquidity Ratio
definition: Liquidity ratios are a class of financial metrics used to determine a debtor's ability to pay off current debt obligations without raising external capital.

title: Liquidity Risk
definition: Liquidity risk is the possibility an institution will be unable to obtain funds, such as customer deposits or borrowed funds, at a reasonable price or within a necessary period to meet its financial obligations. This risk can impact both financial institutions and corporations, threatening their operational and financial stability.

title: Liquidity Trap
definition: A liquidity trap arises when consumers and investors choose to hoard cash over spending or investing, despite low interest rates aimed at boosting economic growth. This phenomenon can render monetary policy ineffective, leading to a stagnation in economic activities. Understanding its causes and effects can help in identifying and mitigating potential economic threats.

title: Liquified Natural Gas (LNG)
definition: Liquefied natural gas (LNG) is natural gas that has been converted to a liquid form for the ease and safety of natural gas transport. Natural gas is cooled to approximately -260 F, creating a clear, colorless, and non-toxic liquid that can be transported from areas with a large supply of natural gas to areas that demand more natural gas.

title: Lis Pendens
definition: Alis pendensis an official notice to the public that a lawsuit involving a claim on a property has been filed. Lis pendens is connected to the concept that a property buyer must assume any litigation that exists pertaining to the property.

title: Lisbon Treaty Explained: Impact, History, and Key Changes
definition: The 2007 Lisbon Treaty revisedEuropean Unionregulations and amended two earlier treaties that created the foundation for the EU. While the Lisbon Treaty was built on existing treaties, it established better centralized leadership and foreign policy, a process for countries to exit the EU, and a streamlined process for enacting new policies. The Lisbon Treaty also replaced the previously rejected Constitutional Treaty, which attempted to establish a Union constitution.

title: Listed
definition: A listed company issues shares of its stock for trading on a stock exchange. If a company is listed in the U.S., it has met the requirements of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) for selling shares to the public and has been accepted for trading on an exchange such as the New York Stock Exchange. It is a public company.

title: Listed Option
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Listed Property
definition: Listed property is tangible assets used for both business and personal purposes. It's property such as vehicles and entertainment or recreation property. It requires careful record-keeping to qualify for tax deductions. Assets used more than 50% for business purposes can benefit from special tax deductions, such as Section 179, which provide significant financial incentives for businesses. Depreciation also depends on usage; property with over 50% business use is eligible for more favorable methods. Accurate and extensive records are essential to substantiate business use of listed property; failure to do so may impact the eligibility for deductions and require depreciation recapture.

title: Listed Security
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Listing Agreement
definition: A listing agreement is a contract under which a property owner (as principal) authorizes a real estate broker (as agent) to find a buyer for the property on the owner's terms. In exchange for this service, the ownerpays a commission.

title: Listing Requirements
definition: Tolistits shares on a stock exchange, a company must meet certain exchange liquidity and financial requirements. Also, it must pay both the exchange’s initial and ongoing yearly listing fees. Listing requirements vary by exchange and include a minimum stockholder’s equity, a minimum share price, and a minimum number of shareholders.

title: Litecoin Mining
definition: Litecoin mining is the process of validating transactions in the Litecoin blockchain, closing the block, opening a new one, and receiving a reward. Litecoin uses proof-of-work in its consensus design, which employs computational power to solve a cryptographic puzzle.Litecoin is rewarded to the miner who generates a solution that is equal to or less than the network target.

title: Living Trust
definition: A living trust is a legal arrangement used in estate planning to hold and protect an individual's assets and direct their distributions after death,

title: Living Wage
definition: The term "living wage" refers to a theoretical income level that allows individuals or families to afford adequate shelter, food, and other life necessities. The goal of a living wage is to allow employees to earn enough income for a satisfactorystandard of livingand prevent them from falling into poverty. Economists suggest that it should be enough to ensure that no more than 30% of this income gets spent on housing.

title: Living Will
definition: A living will—also known as anadvance directive—is a legal document that specifies the type of medical care that an individual does or doesn't want in the event they are unable to communicate their wishes.

title: LLC Operating Agreement
definition: An LLC operating agreement is an internal document that outlines the governance structure and operational procedures for a limited liability company.

title: Lloyd's Of London
definition: Lloyd's of London originated in the 17th century as a London coffeehouse frequented by sailors and merchants. It has since evolved into a globalinsurancemarketplace where membersyndicatesspecialize in covering specific risks, facilitating transactions for businesses and individuals worldwide.

title: Load
definition: A load is a fee or commission paid by investors when buying or redeeming mutual fund shares. These charges compensate financial advisors and other intermediaries for their role in processing and supporting the investment. Load structures can vary by fund company, and the type and size of the fee can directly affect an investor’s overall return. Understanding the different types of loads and how they influence costs can help you make better decisions when shaping your investment strategy.

title: Load Fund
definition: A load fund is a mutual fund that comes with asales chargeor commission. The fund investor pays the load, which goes to compensate a sales intermediary, such as abroker,financial planner, or investment advisor, for his time and expertise in selecting an appropriate fund for the investor. The load is either paid upfront at the time of purchase (front-end load), when the shares are sold (back-end load), or as long as the fund is held by the investor (level-load). Load funds may be contrasted withno-load funds, which do not carry a sales charge.

title: Loan
definition: A loan refers to a type of credit vehicle in which a sum of money is lent to another party in exchange for future repayment of the principal amount, generally with interest.

title: Loan Application Fee
definition: A loan application fee is charged to a potential borrower for processing and underwriting an application for aloan, such as a mortgage or car loan. Although loan application fees may be required for all types of loans and are intended to cover the costs of loan approval, many observers consider them unnecessary or overly costly.

title: Loan Commitment
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Loan Committee
definition: A loan committee is the lending or management committee of a bank or other lending institution. It generally consists of upper-level officers with management authority. The loan committee analyzes and subsequently approves or rejects anyloanthat the initialloan officerdoes not have the authority to approve, typically those of large sizes or higher risk. The committee ensures that the loan meets the institution’s standard lending policy. If it does, the committee can agree to fund and disburse the loan with a binding commitment.

title: Loan Constant
definition: A loan constant is a percentage that shows the annualdebt serviceon a loan compared to its total principal value. The calculation for a loan constant is the annual debt service divided by the total loan amount. When shopping for a loan, borrowers can compare the loan constant of various loans before making a decision. The loan with the lowest loan constant will have lower debt service requirements, meaning the borrower will pay less in interest and principal over a given period. Loan constants are only applicable to fixed interest rate loans and notloans with variable interest rates.

title: Loan Credit Default Swap (LCDS)
definition: A loan credit default swap (LCDS) is a type ofcredit derivativein which thecredit exposureof an underlying loan is exchanged between two parties. A loan credit default swap's structure is the same as a regular credit default swap (CDS), except that the underlyingreference obligationis limited strictly tosyndicated secured loans, rather than any type of corporate debt.

title: Loan Credit Default Swap Index (Markit LCDX)
definition: The Loan Credit Default Swap Index (Markit LCDX) is a specialized index ofloan-only credit default swaps(CDS) covering 100 North American companies with unsecured debt trading in broad secondary markets. The LCDX is traded over-the-counter, and several large investment banks manage it, provide liquidity, and assist in pricing individual credit default swaps. IHS Markit Ltd, headquartered in London, is the index provider.

title: Loan Grading
definition: Loan grading evaluates credit risk by assessing a borrower'scredit history,collateralquality, and likelihood of repayment to support stronger lending decisions. Embedded within the underwriting process, it also informs financial and regulatory reporting, including systems required by the FDIC. Grading approaches can vary across institutions, adapting to differences in size and portfolio complexity.

title: Loan Life Coverage Ratio (LLCR)
definition: The Loan Life Coverage Ratio (LLCR) helps measure a company's ability to repayloansby comparing thenet present value (NPV)of cash flows to outstanding debt. Essential in project financing, LLCR offers an extended view of long-term solvency. Unlike thedebt service coverage ratio(DSCR), which captures a moment in time, LLCR spans the entire loan duration, offering a comprehensive outlook on financial viability.

title: Loan Lock
definition: Aloan lockrefers to a lender’s promise to offer a borrower a specified interest rate on a mortgage and to hold that rate for an agreed-upon period of time. A loan lock helps protect borrowers from an increase in interest rates during the lock period. However, mortgage lenders may charge a fee for a rate lock or add a margin to the interest rate.

title: Loan Loss Provision
definition: A loan loss provision is a financial safeguard used by banks to cover losses from unpaid loans or loan defaults. Banks allocate these expenses on theirincome statementto ensure accurate reporting of their financial status and manage risks associated with lending. By adding to their loan loss reserves, banks prepare for potential defaults, thereby giving a precise picture of their overall financial health.

title: Loan Modification
definition: Loan modification is a change made to the terms of an existing loan by a lender. It may involve a reduction in the interest rate, an extension of the length of time for repayment, a different type of loan, or any combination of the three.

title: Loan Note
definition: A loan note serves as a binding agreement documenting the legal obligation and repayment terms between a borrower and lender.

title: Loan Officer
definition: A loan officer is a representative of a bank, credit union, or otherfinancial institutionwho assists borrowers in the application process.Loan officers are often called mortgage loan officers since that is the most complex and costly type of loan most consumers encounter. Most loan officers help consumers and small business owners with many types of loans. They're responsible for handling the application and approval process and are necessary even in online lending. Some are compensated through commissions.

title: Loan Participation Note (LPN)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.
