title: Loan Production Office (LPO)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Loan Servicing
definition: Loan servicing encompasses the entire administrative process of managing a loan, from disbursement to final payment. Learn how monthly payments are collected, taxes are managed, and records are maintained, along with a practical example of how servicers earn compensation.

title: Loan Shark
definition: A loan shark is an illegal lender who charges extremely high interest rates and uses threats or violence to enforce repayment. These loans are risky and are short-term without credit checks, which makes them appealing to people in need of cash in a pinch. Payday lenders, on the other hand, are legal and regulated, and provide short-term, high-interest loans, but operate within a legal framework and use lawful collection methods. Borrowing from a loan shark can lead to dangerous consequences and financial exploitation.

title: Loan Stock
definition: Loan stock is a financial tool in which shares ofcommonorpreferred stockare used as collateral to secure a loan from another party. The loan acts like a traditional loan but involves equity security. It earns a fixed interest rate and can be secured or unsecured.

title: Loan Syndication
definition: Loan syndication is when multiple lenders combine to fund a single loan.

title: Loan-to-Cost Ratio (LTC)
definition: The loan-to-cost (LTC) ratio measures the financing given for acommercial real estate projectrelative to its construction costs. The LTC ratio allows commercial real estate lenders to determine the risk of offering aconstruction loan. It also allows developers to understand the amount of equity they retain during a construction project.

title: Loan-to-Deposit Ratio (LDR)
definition: The loan-to-deposit ratio (LDR) is a financial metric that compares a bank’s total loans to its total deposits. You use it to assess a bank’sliquidity. The higher the ratio, the more a bank may be relying on its deposits to fund loans, increasing profits but also leaving less cash to cover withdrawals, reserve requirements, and unexpected funding needs.

title: Loan-to-Value (LTV)
definition: A loan-to-value (LTV) ratio typically represents the amount of amortgagecompared to the property's value. It measures how much risk a prospective homebuyer represents based on the mortgage amount and the appraised value of the property. Lower LTVs are better in the eyes of lenders, but they require borrowers to come up with larger down payments.

title: Lobby
definition: A lobby is an organized group that seeks to influence public policy and decision-making to align with its specific goals or interests.

title: Local Tax
definition: Local taxes, assessed by states, counties, or towns, fund essential community services, including schools, roads, and emergency services. These taxes vary widely in form, such as property, sales, and income taxes, providing direct benefits like improved infrastructure and public safety.

title: Lock In Profits
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Lock-Up Agreement
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Lock-Up Period
definition: A lock-up period is a period when investors can't sell their shares after an IPO or during a hedge fund investment. It helps stabilize the market from being flooded by shares right after a company goes public, and it also prevents stock price swings. This means investors must wait to access their funds. Shares can be sold after the lock-up period ends, which may impact the stock price. These periods may give the market stability, also limit liquidity for investors who want easy access to their money.

title: Lockbox Banking
definition: Lockbox banking is a bank-provided service that simplifies payment receipt and processing for businesses. Payments directed to a designated post office box are collected, processed, and deposited into the company's bank account by the bank, enhancing cash flow and operational efficiency. However, businesses need to weigh the convenience and potential cost against risks like fraud or errors in bank processing.

title: Locked In
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Locked-In Retirement Account (LIRA)
definition: A locked-in retirement account (LIRA) is a type of registeredpensionaccount in Canada that does not permit withdrawals before retirement except in exceptional circumstances. Locked-in retirement accounts are designed to hold pension funds for former employer-sponsored plan participants and certain others until they reach retirement age.

title: Log-Normal Distribution
definition: A log-normal distribution represents a set of data that follows a normal distribution when logarithms are applied to its values. Though often overlooked compared to the familiar bell curve of a normal distribution, the log-normal distribution is essential in financial analyses, especially for modeling stock prices and returns. Understanding how to transition between normal and log-normal distributions and effectively utilize them, including leveraging Excel for calculation, can provide deeper insights into financial data patterns.

title: Logarithmic Price Scale
definition: A logarithmic price scale, also referred to as a "log scale", is a type of scale used on a chart that is plotted such that two equivalent price changes are represented by the same vertical distance on the scale.

title: Logistics
definition: Logistics involves managing how resources are obtained, stored, and moved to needed locations.

title: London Interbank Bid Rate (LIBID)
definition: LIBID and LIBOR were once key reference rates in the London interbank market, where banks exchanged funds directly or through electronic trading platforms. LIBOR (London Interbank Offered Rate) represented the rate at which banks lent funds to one another. LIBID (London Interbank Bid Rate) was the rate at which banks were willing to borrow funds.

title: London InterBank Offered Rate (LIBOR)
definition: LIBOR (London Interbank Offered Rate) was a key reference point for the interest rates banks charged each other on short-term loans across global financial markets.

title: London Metal Exchange (LME): History, Trading, and How It Works
definition: The London Metal Exchange (LME) is a global commodities exchange specializing infuturesandoptionstrading for base metals such as aluminum, copper, zinc, lead, and nickel, as well as precious metals like gold and silver.

title: Understanding the London Stock Exchange (LSE): Key History and Events
definition: The London Stock Exchange (LSE) is one of the world's oldest and most significant exchanges and the U.K.'s primary market, with origins more than 300 years ago.

title: Long Hedge
definition: A long hedge is a futures position taken to lock in a future price of a commodity or raw material. This strategy helps protect against price increases by allowing the buyer to secure a set price in advance.

title: Long Jelly Roll
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Long Position (Long)
definition: A long position is buying an asset in the hope that its price will rise over time.

title: Long Put Options: Definition, Examples, and Comparison With Shorting Stock
definition: A long put involves purchasing aput option, which allows investors to potentially benefit from a decline in theunderlying asset'svalue. This strategy can be used for speculation orhedgingagainst stock price drops, offering limited risk exposure while potentially enhancing portfolio protection. Explore how long puts operate, including strike prices and exercise options, and see how they compare to shorting stock as a bearish investment strategy.

title: Long Run
definition: The economic term "long run" refers to an extended period during which production factors and costs in an economy are variable, allowing for adjustments to occur.

title: Long Straddle
definition: The long straddle is an options trading strategy that profits from significant market movement in either direction. By buying a call and a put with the sameexpiration dateandstrike price, traders position themselves to capitalize on volatile events like earnings reports and elections. This strategy can lead to high profits if the underlying asset exhibits strong price fluctuations.

title: Long Synthetic (Synthetic Put)
definition: A synthetic put is an options strategy that combines ashortstock position with anat-the-moneylong call optionon the stock to mimic along put option. An investor with a bearish position does this to protect against the potential near-term appreciation of the stock's price. A synthetic put is also known as a married call or protective call.

title: Long Tail
definition: The long tail is a business strategy that allows companies to realize significant profits by selling low volumes of hard-to-find items to many customers, instead of only selling large volumes of a reduced number of popular items. The term was first coined in 2004 by Chris Anderson, who argued that products in low demand or with low sales volume can collectively make up market share that rivals or exceeds the relatively few current bestsellers and blockbusters but only if the store or distribution channel is large enough.

title: Long Term
definition: "Long term" refers to the extended period of time that an asset is held. Depending on the type of security, along-termasset can be held for as little as one year or for as long as 30 years or more. Generally speaking, long-term investing for individuals is often thought to be in the range of at least seven to 10 years of holding time, although there is no absolute rule.

title: Long-Legged Doji
definition: The long-legged doji is acandlestickthat consists of long upper and lower shadows and has approximately the sameopeningand closing price, resulting in a small real body.

title: Long-Run Average Total Cost (LRATC)
definition: Long-run average total cost (LRATC) is a business metric that represents the average cost per unit of output over thelong run, where all inputs are considered to be variable and the scale of production is changeable. The long-run average cost curve shows the lowest total cost to produce a given level of output in the long run.

title: Long/Short Equity
definition: Long-short equity strategies aim to take advantage of market opportunities by holding long positions in stocks anticipated to rise andshortingthose expected to fall. This strategy seeks to reduce overall market risk and enhance profit potential, combining potential gains from appreciating stocks with benefits from declining stocks. Typically used by hedge funds, it's aliquid alternative strategythat can be tailored to various market conditions.

title: Long/Short Fund
definition: A long/short fund is a type of mutual, hedge, or exchange-traded fund (ETF) that takes both long and short positions in investments, typically from a specific market segment.

title: Long-Tail Liability
definition: A long-tail liability is a type of liability that carries a long settlement period. Long-tail liabilities are likely to result in highincurred but not reported (IBNR)claims, because it may take a long period of time for the claims to be settled.

title: Long-Term Assets
definition: Long-term assets are assets, whether tangible or non-tangible, that will benefit the company for more that one year. Also known asnon-current assets, long-term assets can includefixed assetssuch as a company's property, plant, and equipment, but can also include other assets such as long term investments, patents, copyright, franchises, goodwill, trademarks, and trade names, as well as software.

title: Long-Term Capital Gain or Loss
definition: A long-term capital gain or loss, for tax purposes, is the gain or loss stemming from the sale of an investment that was held for longer than 12 months before it was sold.

title: Long-Term Capital Management (LTCM)
definition: Long-Term Capital Management (LTCM), founded by Nobel Prize-winning economists and renowned Wall Street traders, was a successfulhedge fundthat faced a catastrophic collapse in 1998. This downfall stemmed from highly leveraged trading strategies and was exacerbated by Russia's debt default, compelling the U.S. government to orchestrate a bailout to avert a potential globalfinancial crisis.

title: Long-Term Care (LTC) Insurance
definition: Long-term care (LTC) insurance is coverage that provides nursing-home care, home-health care, and personal or adult daycare for individuals age 65 or older or with a chronic or disabling condition that needs constant supervision.LTC insurance offers more flexibility and options than many public assistance programs, such asMedicaid.

title: Long-Term Debt
definition: Long-term debt is debt thatmaturesin more than one year. Long-term debt can be viewed from two perspectives: financial statement reporting by the issuer and financial investing. In financial statement reporting, companies must record long-term debt issuance and all of its associated payment obligations on its financial statements. On the flip side, investing in long-term debt includes putting money into debt investments with maturities of more than one year.

title: Long-Term Debt to Capitalization Ratio
definition: The long-term debt to capitalization ratio shows how much a company uses debt to finance its operations.

title: Long-Term Debt to Total Assets Ratio
definition: The long-term debt-to-total-assets ratio measures a company's solvency by showing what share of its assets is financed with debt lasting more than a year. Calculated by dividinglong-term debtby total assets, it helps reveal a firm's leverage and creditworthiness. Lower ratios generally signal a stronger financial position and reduced reliance on debt.

title: Long-Term Equity Anticipation Securities (LEAPS)
definition: Long-term equity anticipation securities (LEAPS) areoptions contractswith expiration dates extending beyond one year, often up to three years. These contracts allow investors to gain exposure to long-term price movements in theunderlying asset, similar to standard options but with extended expiration periods. A LEAPS contract provides the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell the underlying asset at a predetermined price, depending on whether the option is a call or a put.

title: Long-Term Growth (LTG)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Long-Term Incentive Plan (LTIP)
definition: A long-term incentive plan (LTIP) rewards employees, often executives, for achieving goals that enhance shareholder value. By aligning employee incentives with company growth objectives, these plans not only retain top talent in a competitive environment but also serve as a strategic mechanism for long-term business success. Explore the different types of LTIPs, like stock options and retirement plans, to understand how they benefit both employees and companies.

title: Long-Term Investments
definition: A long-term investment is an asset that a company plans to hold for more than one year, such as stocks, bonds, and real estate.

title: Long-Term Liabilities
definition: Long-term liabilities are a company's financial obligations that are due more than one year in the future. These debts are listed separately on the balance sheet to provide a more accurate view of a company's currentliquidityand ability to pay current liabilities as they become due. Long-term liabilities are also called long-term debt ornoncurrent liabilities.

title: Longitudinal Data
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Look-Alike Contracts
definition: Look-alike contracts are a cash-settled financial product based on the settlement price of a similar exchange-traded, physically settledfutures contract. Look-alike contracts are traded over the counter and they carry no risk of actualphysical deliveryregardless of the terms of the underlying futures contract.

title: Lookback Option
definition: A lookback option is an exotic option that lets investors use the asset’s past prices to maximize profit. These options can be expensive and are usually traded over-the-counter (OTC). There are two main types: fixed lookback options, which set a strike price based on the best past price, and floating lookback options, which adjust the payoff depending on the highest or lowest price during the option’s life.

title: Lorenz Curve
definition: A Lorenz curve, developed by American economist Max Lorenz in 1905, is a graphical representation ofincome inequalityor wealth inequality. The graph plots percentiles of the population on the horizontal axis according to income orwealthand plots cumulative income or wealth on the vertical axis.

title: Loss Adjustment Expense (LAE)
definition: A loss adjustment expense (LAE) is a financial obligation for insurance companies, covering the operational costs associated with evaluating and negotiating settlements for policy claims.

title: Loss Carryback
definition: A loss carryback allows businesses to applynet operating losses (NOL)to prior years' tax returns to obtain tax refunds. The time value of money makes carrybacks generally more advantageous than carryforwards. The length of time losses can be carried back has varied historically, especially during recessions. The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act removed the two-year carryback, except for specific cases like farming.

title: Loss Carryforward
definition: A loss carryforward refers to an accounting technique that applies the current year'snet operating loss(NOL) to future years' net income to reduce tax liability.

title: Loss Development
definition: Loss development is the difference between the final losses recorded by an insurer and what the insurer originally recorded. Loss development seeks to account for the fact that someinsurance claimstake a long time to settle, and that estimates of the total loss an insurer will experience will adjust as claims are finalized.

title: Loss Given Default (LGD)
definition: Loss given default refers to the estimated credit loss that results if a borrower defaults on their financial obligation.

title: Loss Leader Strategy
definition: A loss leader strategy intentionally prices products below cost to attract new customers and boost sales of other products. This common practice helps businesses, especially when entering new markets, to build a customer base and encourage recurring revenue. Both online and physical retailers use this approach to drive customer traffic with the hope of increased brand loyalty and sales.

title: Loss Payee
definition: A loss payee is someone listed on an insurance policy who receives payment if a loss happens. This protects the payee's financial interest, such as when a car or property is financed with a loan. The loss payee clause in the insurance policy makes sure the payee (usually a lender) gets paid first if the insured item is damaged or lost. In vehicle financing, the bank or finance company is usually listed as the loss payee, which ensures the loan is protected if something happens.

title: Loss Ratio
definition: An insurer's loss ratio represents the portion of premium income that's paid out to cover claims.

title: Loss Reserve
definition: A loss reserve is an estimate of an insurer’s liability from future claims it will have to pay out on. Typically composed ofliquid assets, loss reserves allow an insurer to cover claims made against insurance policies that it underwrites. Estimatingliabilitiescan be a complex undertaking. Insurers must take into account the duration of the insurance contract, the type of insurance offered, the odds of a claim being made, and the time it takes to be resolved quickly. Insurers have to adjust their loss reserve calculations as circumstances change.

title: Lost Decade
definition: The Lost Decade refers to nearly ten years of slow or negative economic growth in Japan during the 1990s. Misguided government policies in response to a real estate bubble, along with demographic changes, are considered to be the main culprits for the Lost Decade. Stagnant growth in subsequent years has led the period from 1991 through 2011 and even 2021 to sometimes be referred to as Japan's Lost Decades.In the United States, the first decade of the 21st century, which was bookended by two stock market crashes, is often compared to Japan's Lost Decade.

title: Lost Policy Release (LPR)
definition: A lost policy release is a document that allows policyholders to formally acknowledge they cannot find their insurance policy, thereby waiving the insurer's obligation to provide coverage.

title: Lot (Securities Trading)
definition: Lots in securities and trading represent the number of units of a financial instrument that have been bought on an exchange. The number of units is typically conveyed by the lot name. Around lotis 100 shares in the stock market but investors don't have to buy round lots. A lot can be any number of shares. Anodd lotis the term used when fewer than 100 shares are bought.

title: Love Money
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Low Exercise Price Option (LEPO)
definition: A low exercise price option (LEPO) is a European-style calloptionwith an exercise price of one cent. Both buyer and seller operate onmarginand, because it is almost a certainty that the holder will exercise the option at maturity, it is somewhat similar to a futures contract.

title: Low-Income Housing Tax Credit
definition: The Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) is a tax incentive for housing developers to construct, purchase, or renovate rental housing for low-income individuals and families. The LIHTC was written into the Tax Reform Act of 1986.

title: Low Interest Rate Environment
definition: A low interest rate environment involves a risk-free interest rate that is lower than the historical average for interest rates. Much of the world experienced a low interest rate environment following the 2008-09 financial crisis. Low interest rate environments tend to benefit borrowers at the expense of lenders and savers. But if they last too long, they can damage banks' profitability and result in increased debt for some.

title: Low/No Documentation Loan
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Low Volume Pullback
definition: A low-volume pullback is atechnical correctiontoward an area of support that occurs on lower-than-average volume. In technical analysis, a low-volume pullback is significant toweak longslocking in profits rather than areversal. In contrast, high-volume pullbacks may indicate a near-term reversal. Traders leverage these pullbacks to inform their trading strategies, using tools like on-balance volume (OBV) and technical indicators.

title: Lower of Cost or Market Method
definition: The lower of cost or market method is a valuation approach that aims to report inventory at the least expensive value to ensure that financial statements reflect a conservative estimation of a company's assets.

title: Loyalty Program
definition: Loyalty programs, sponsored by retailers and other businesses, offer rewards, discounts, and other special incentives as a way to attract and retain customers. They are designed to encourage repeat business, offering people a reward for store/brand loyalty(hence the name). Typically, the more often a customer patronizes the merchant—and the more they spend—the greater their rewards.

title: Ltd. (Limited)
definition: Ltd. is a standard abbreviation for "limited," a form of corporate structure available in countries like the U.K., Ireland, and Canada.

title: Lucrative
definition: Lucrative means profitable, and it can be used to describe any venture or activity that has the potential to make money. Thus, an investment or commercial venture is considered to be lucrative if it produces substantial wealth. A lucrative activity could be anything from collecting art, designing an invention, or following through on an idea for an innovative product or service.

title: Luhn Algorithm
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Lump-Sum Payment
definition: A lump-sum payment is a monetary sum paid in one single payment instead of being allocated into installments. Lump sums are commonly associated withpension plansand other retirement vehicles, such as401(k) accounts, where retirees might accept a smaller upfront lump-sum payment rather than a larger payment issued in installments over time.

title: Luxury Item
definition: Luxury goods are non-essential items that signal wealth and social status, with demand rising faster than income growth. Unlike necessities or inferior goods, they appeal to consumers seeking exclusivity and prestige.

title: Luxury Tax
definition: A luxury tax is a sales or surcharge tax that's applied to non-essential, high-value items. These taxes aim to target wealth distribution and discourage excessive consumption, and they can vary across jurisdictions. Examples of common luxury items that can spur a luxury tax include cars, jewelry, and private jets.

title: M1
definition: M1 is the money supply that is composed of currency, demand deposits, other liquid deposits—which includes savings deposits. M1 includes the most liquid portions of the money supply because it contains currency and assets that either are or can be quickly converted to cash. However, "near money" and "near, near money," which fall under M2 and M3, cannot be converted to currency as quickly.

title: M2
definition: The Federal Reserve's M2 weekly measurement estimates the U.S. liquid money supply.

title: M3
definition: M3 is the broadest measure of themoney supply, incorporatingM2, large time deposits, and less liquid assets. M3 is distinct in that it emphasizes money as a store of value with its focus on less-liquid assets, unlike M0, M1, and M2, which include more liquid financial products. Although the Federal Reserve stopped publishing M3 data in 2006, some institutions, like the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, continue to track it for economic analysis.Understanding M3's components is crucial for evaluating the broader financial ecosystem, particularly transactions involving larger financial institutions and corporations.

title: M-Pesa
definition: M-Pesa is a mobile banking service that allows users to store and transfer money through their mobile phones using SMS without needing a bank account. M-Pesa was introduced in Kenya as an alternative for people to access financial services and to enhance financial inclusion for the underbanked in Africa.

title: Maastricht Treaty: Definition, Purpose, History, and Significance
definition: The term Maastricht Treaty refers to the international agreement that was responsible for the creation of theEuropean Union(EU). The agreement was signed in 1992 in the Dutch city of Maastricht and became effective in 1993. It led to greater cooperation between the 12 member nations that signed the treaty by promoting unified citizenship, along with economic, social, and progress. The treaty also laid down the foundation for a single currency, theeuro. It was amended several times since it was signed. As of October 2021, 27 member states were part of the European Union.

title: Macaulay Duration
definition: The Macaulay duration is the weighted average term to maturity of the cash flow from a bond, or the point at which the bond's value will equal its purchase price.

title: Macro Environment
definition: Macro environment refers to the broad economic conditions and external factors that influence the overall economy.

title: Macro Manager
definition: A macro manager is a type of boss or supervisor who takes a more hands-off approach and lets employees do their jobs with minimal direct supervision. This style of leadership is referred to as macro-management. Macro managers can be thought of by some employees as supervisors who do not give them enough support or feedback to do their jobs effectively, while others may be glad to be trusted and left alone.

title: Macroeconomic Factor
definition: A macroeconomic factor is a broad economic condition that directly impacts the overall economy of an entire region or country instead of a few businesses or individuals.

title: Macroeconomics
definition: Macroeconomics is a field of study that examines the behavior of an economy, including markets, businesses, consumers, and governments. It examines economy-wide phenomena such as inflation, price levels, the rate of economic growth, national income, gross domestic product (GDP), and changes in unemployment.

title: Magic Formula Investing
definition: Magic formula investing refers to a rules-based, disciplined investing strategy that teaches people a relatively simple and easy-to-understand method forvalue investing. It relies onquantitativescreens of companies and stocks, and is designed to beat the stock market's average annual returns using the S&P 500 to represent the market return.  In simple terms, stocks are ranked based on their price and return on capital.

title: Magna Cum Laude
definition: Magna cum laude is a Latin phrase that means “with great praise."

title: MICR: What Is a Magnetic Ink Character Recognition Line?
definition: Magnetic ink character recognition (MICR) is a technology that banks use to identify and process checks. The MICR line is the string of characters that appears at the bottom left of the check, consisting of the bank routing number, the customer's account number, and the check number.

title: Main Street
definition: Main Street is a colloquial term used by economists to refer collectively to America's independent small businesses. It gets its name from a common name for the principal commercial street of small towns across the country. In England, the equivalent term is High Street.

title: Maintenance Expenses
definition: The term maintenanceexpenserefers to any cost incurred by an individual or business to keep theirassetsin good working condition. These costs may be spent for the general maintenance of items like running anti-virus software on computer systems or they may be used for repairs such as fixing a car or machinery. These expenses are in addition to the actualpurchase priceof an asset, so individuals and companies should be able and willing to foot the bill in order to keep their assets in running order.

title: Maintenance Margin
definition: Maintenance margin refers to the minimum equity that must be maintained in a margin account after an investment is made, as mandated byFINRA. While the standard requirement is 25% of the total securities' value, some brokers demand more. This ensures investor accountability and mitigates risks in margin trading. Learn how brokerage agreements, Regulation T, and margin calls play into maintaining these thresholds in margin accounts.

title: Majority Shareholder
definition: A majorityshareholderis a person or entity that owns and controls more than 50% of a company'soutstanding shares. As a majority shareholder, a person or operating entity has a significant amount of influence over the company, especially if their shares arevoting shares. Voting shares give a shareholder permission to vote on different corporate decisions, such as who should be on the company’sboard of directors.

title: Make-or-Buy Decision
definition: Make-or-buy decisions involve choosing between manufacturing a product in-house or buying it from an external supplier. This decision requires evaluating all associated costs, including labor, equipment, and storage, and comparing them to purchasing expenses like shipping and supplier contracts. Companies must weigh both quantitative analysis and qualitative factors such as expertise, capacity, and strategic goals to determine the most cost-efficient method.

title: Make to Order (MTO)
definition: Make-to-order refers to a manufacturing approach where items are produced exclusively in response to confirmed customer orders.

title: Make to Stock (MTS)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Make Whole Call Provision
definition: The make-whole call provision allows a bond issuer to pay off the remaining debt owed on the bond and make investors "whole" ahead of schedule.

title: Malpractice Insurance: Definition, Types, Importance
definition: Malpractice insurance is a type ofprofessional liability insurancepurchased by healthcare professionals. Thisinsurance coverageprotects healthcare providers against patients who file suits against them under the complaint that they were harmed by the professional's negligence or intentionally harmful treatment decisions. Malpractice insurance also covers the death of a patient.

title: Managed Account
definition: A managed account is an investment account owned by an individual but managed by a financial manager. The manager has the authority to make investment decisions and must act in the client’s best interest. These accounts are generally meant for high-net-worth individuals and often have minimum investment requirements. Managed accounts are customized for the account owner, offering more personalized investment management.

title: Managed Futures
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Management Buyout (MBO)
definition: A management buyout (MBO) is a strategic transaction where a company's management team acquires all of the business's assets and operations. Often used to gain more control and financial rewards, MBOs also involve significant borrowing, making them a form of aleveraged buyout. This comprehensive guide explores the reasons companies pursue MBOs, their financial implications, and offers a notable example with Dell.

title: Management by Objectives (MBO)
definition: Management by objectives is a management approach where objectives are set by an agreement between a manager and an employer to incentivize employee participation.

title: Management Discussion and Analysis (MD&A)
definition: The management discussion and analysis (MD&A) is a section of a company's periodic report where executives comment on financial performance.

title: Management Fee
definition: A management fee is a charge thatinvestment managerslevy for managing an investment fund, compensating them for their expertise and time. Typically based on a percentage ofassets under management (AUM), management fees may also incorporateinvestor relationsand administrative costs. Fees can vary significantly, generally ranging from 0.10% to over 2% of AUM. A well-managed fund not only involves stock selection and portfolio management but can lead to better financial gains for investors.

title: Managerial Accounting
definition: Unlike financial accounting, which follows standardized rules for external reporting, managerial accounting is customized to internal needs, examining costs, revenue, and performance metrics to support planning and evaluating business operations.

title: Manufacturer's Suggested Retail Price (MSRP)
definition: The manufacturer’s suggested retail price (MSRP) is the price a manufacturer recommends a product be sold for, and is used as a reference for retailers to set their prices. Though this pricing model applies across many retail products, it's most commonly associated with automobiles, where it’s often referred to as the sticker price.

title: Manufacturing
definition: Manufacturing is the conversion of raw materials into finished products through the use of tools, human labor, machinery, and chemical processing. The amount of manufacturing conducted within a country is generally a sign of its economic health and strength.

title: Manufacturing Resource Planning
definition: MRP II integrates various business processes to help manufacturers efficiently manage and allocate production resources.

title: Maquiladora
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Margin
definition: Margin trading allows investors to enhance their purchasing power by borrowing funds from a broker to acquire more assets than they could with their available cash alone. This leverage can amplify both potential returns and the risks involved. Margin represents the collateral investors deposit with brokers to cover potential losses and can be used in various financial activities such as buying securities, short selling, or trading derivatives.

title: Margin Account
definition: A margin account is a brokerage account that enables investors to borrow money from their broker to purchase securities, for leverage, or to sell short.

title: Margin Call
definition: A margin call is a broker's demand for an investor to deposit additional funds or securities into theirmargin accountwhen the account's equity falls below the broker's requiredmaintenance level. Margin calls are typically triggered when the value of the securities in the account declines due to market downturns or volatility. They require immediate action; investors must add funds or sell assets to restore the account's value. Failure to meet a margin call can lead the broker to liquidate assets to cover the shortfall.

title: Margin Debt
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Margin Loan Availability
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Margin of Safety
definition: The margin of safety is a value investing buffer zone that protects against unpredictable loss.

title: Marginal Analysis
definition: Marginal analysis is a decision-making tool that companies use to evaluate the benefits and costs associated with making incremental changes to a business activity.

title: Marginal Benefits
definition: Marginal benefit refers to the incremental increase in utility or satisfaction derived by a consumer from the consumption of one additional unit of a good or service.

title: Marginal Cost of Production
definition: Marginal cost is the cost incurred when producing one additional unit.

title: Marginal Profit
definition: Marginal profit is the profit earned by a firm or individual when one additional or marginal unit is produced and sold. Marginal refers to the added cost or profit earned with producing the next unit. Marginal product is the additional revenue earned while the marginal cost is the added cost for producing one additional unit.

title: Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC)
definition: In economics, the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is defined as the proportion of an aggregate raise in pay that a consumer spends on the consumption of goods and services, as opposed to saving it. Marginal propensity to consume is a component of Keynesian macroeconomic theory and is calculated as the change in consumption divided by the change in income.

title: Marginal Propensity to Import (MPM)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Marginal Propensity to Save (MPS)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Marginal Rate of Substitution (MRS)
definition: The marginal rate of substitution (MRS) measures the willingness of a consumer to replace one good for another good as long as the same satisfaction is maintained.

title: Marginal Rate of Technical Substitution
definition: The marginal rate of technical substitution (MRTS) is the rate at which production inputs, typically labor and capital, can be substituted while maintaining the same level of output. Firms that want to maintain productivity while altering inputs need to have a practical understanding of MRTS. Calculating and applying MRTS is key to optimizing production efficiency.

title: Marginal Rate of Transformation
definition: The Marginal Rate of Transformation (MRT) reveals the opportunity cost involved in the production process by quantifying how much of one good (Y) must be sacrificed to produce an additional unit of another good (X).

title: Marginal Revenue (MR)
definition: Marginal revenue is the increase in revenue generated by the sale of one more unit of a product or service. Though it can remain constant up to a certain point of output, marginal revenue follows thelaw of diminishing returns. Eventually, it will slow as the level of output decreases. Marginal revenue is different from profit, as it is calculated only using the income received and does not reflect expenses associated with production or sales.

title: Marginal Revenue Product (MRP)
definition: Marginal revenue product (MRP), also known as the marginal value product, is the marginal revenue created due to an addition of one unit of resource. The marginal revenue product is calculated by multiplying the marginal physical product (MPP) of the resource by the marginal revenue (MR) generated. The MRP assumes that the expenditures on other factors remain unchanged and helps determine the optimal level of a resource.

title: Marginal Social Cost (MSC)
definition: Marginal social cost (MSC) is the total cost society pays for the production of another unit of product or for taking further action in the economy. MSC includes both marginal private and external costs. Fixed and variable costs also contribute to calculating marginal social cost.

title: Marginal Tax Rate
definition: A marginal tax rate is the percentage of tax applied to each additional dollar of income within a specific bracket, rather than being applied to the whole income.

title: Marginal Utility
definition: Understanding marginal utility is key to grasping consumer behavior and economic decision-making. It refers to the additional satisfaction or benefit a consumer experiences when they consume one more unit of a good or service. This pivotal concept helps economists assess the desire consumers have for purchasing more of an item.

title: Marital Property
definition: Marital property refers to the collective assets and liabilities acquired by spouses during their marriage, which are generally split between them upon divorce.

title: Maritime Law
definition: Maritime law, or admiralty law, includes rules and treaties for private maritime business and issues like shipping or offenses on open water. Maritime law governs maritime business, nautical matters, and provides a legal framework for disputes. Maritime law is critical for global commerce and the protection of resources and personnel at sea.

title: Mark Zuckerberg
definition: Mark Zuckerberg is a self-taught computer programmer and co-founder, chair, andchief executive officerof Meta (META), formerly known as Facebook. Originally named Facemash, Zuckerberg founded the social networking site in his Harvard University dorm room in 2004 along with Andrew McCollum, Dustin Moskovitz, Chris Hughes, and Eduardo Saverin.

title: Market
definition: A market is a place or venue that facilitates the exchange of goods and services. Markets can be physical or virtual.

title: Market Approach
definition: The market approach is a method for determining the value of an asset that accounts for recently sold similar assets. It is one of three popular approaches, along with thecost approachanddiscounted cash-flow analysis(DCF). The market approach excels in situations where abundant data is available on comparable transactions. When that data is scarce, alternative approaches may be required. The market approach is particularly useful in areas likereal estateappraisal and publicly traded shares.

title: Market Basket
definition: A market basket is a selected group of products or assets designed to track the general performance of a specific market segment. This is sometimes known as abasket of goods.

title: Market Breadth
definition: Market breadth indicators analyze the number of stocks advancing relative to those that are declining in a givenindexor on a stock exchange, such as the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) or Nasdaq. Positive market breadth occurs when more stocks are advancing than are declining. This suggests that thebullsare in control of the market's momentum and helps confirm a price rise in the index. Conversely, a disproportional number of declining securities is used to confirmbearishmomentum and a downside move in the stock index.

title: Market Cannibalization
definition: Market cannibalization refers to a drop in sales and demand for a product when the company replaces it with a new one. Market cannibalization can occur when a new product is similar to an existing product, and both share the same customer base. It leads to no increase in the company'smarket sharedespite sales growth for the new product. Cannibalization can also occur when a chain store or fast food outlet loses customers due to another store of the same brand opening nearby.

title: Market Capitalization
definition: Market capitalization is the current value of a publicly traded company, based on the total dollar amount that all of its outstanding shares are worth.

title: Market Capitalization-to-GDP Ratio
definition: The stock market capitalization-to-GDP ratio, also known as the Buffett Indicator, is a measure to assess whether a market is undervalued or overvalued compared to historical standards. This ratio divides the total market cap by the country's GDP, offering insights into market valuation that can guide investment strategies.

title: Market Cycles
definition: Market cycles, or stock market cycles, refer to trends or patterns that arise in various markets or business settings. In a cycle, certain securities orasset classesoutperform others due to their growth-aligned business models. Market cycles span the period between two recent highs or lows of a common benchmark like the S&P 500, highlighting a fund's performance in both rising and falling markets.

title: Market Depth
definition: Market depth refers to a market's ability to absorb relatively largemarket orderswithout significantly impacting the price of the security. Market depth considers the overall number of open bids and offers in an order book, and is a crucial measure of liquidity.

title: Market Dynamics
definition: Market dynamics are forces that will impact prices and the behaviors of producers and consumers. In a market, these forces create pricing signals that result from the fluctuation ofsupply and demandfor a given product or service.

title: Market Economy
definition: In a market economy, the production and pricing of goods and services are driven by buyers and sellers rather than government control.

title: Market Efficiency
definition: Market efficiency defines the ability of markets to use information that provides the most opportunities to investors.

title: Market Exposure
definition: Market exposure refers to the dollar amount of funds or percentage of a broaderportfoliothat is invested in a particular type of security, market sector, or industry. Market exposure is usually expressed as a percentage of total portfolio holdings, for instance, as in 10% of a portfolio being exposed to the oil and gas sector or a $ 50,000 in Tesla stock.

title: Market Failure
definition: A market failure is an adverse outcome in a free market system in which the forces of supply and demand fail to ensure the efficient distribution of goods and services.

title: Market Index
definition: A market index is a group of stocks or other asset classes that tracks the performance of a market segment.

title: Market Indicators
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Market Leader
definition: A market leader is a company with the largestmarket sharein an industry that can often use its dominance to affect the competitive landscape and direction the market takes. A market leader typically enjoys the largest market share or the largest percentage of total sales in a given market. It may surpass its competitors according to other metrics, too, including brand loyalty, perceived value, distribution coverage, image, price, promotional spending, and profit.

title: Market Maker
definition: Market makers play an essential role in financial markets by providing buy and sell quotations and ensuring liquidity. They profit from the bid-ask spread, enabling efficient trading for investors.

title: Market Manipulation
definition: Market manipulation aims to deceive investors by controlling or artificially affecting the price of securities. Although illegal, it can be a challenging activity for regulators to detect and prove. Using tactics such as factually false statements, these schemes influence prices to mislead market participants. Dive deeper into common methods like pump-and-dump or poop-and-scoop, and understand the intricacies of both stock and currency manipulation.

title: Market Neutral
definition: A market-neutral strategy is a type of investment strategy undertaken by an investor or aninvestment managerthat seeks to profit from both increasing and decreasing prices in one or more markets while attempting to completely avoid some specific form of market risk.

title: Market-On-Close Order (MOC)
definition: A market-on-close (MOC) order is a type of market order that's executed as close as possible to the official closing price of the trading day. Traders often use MOC orders to align their trades with end-of-day price movements or index adjustments.

title: Market Order
definition: A market order is an instruction by an investor to abrokerto buy or sell stock shares, bonds, or other assets at the best available price in the current financial market.

title: Market Orientation
definition: Market orientation is an approach to business that prioritizes identifying the needs and desires of consumers and creating products and services that satisfy them. Companies that have a market orientation consider the opinions and needs of theirtarget marketas a critical component of theirresearch and development(R&D) for new products based on consumer desires.

title: Market Penetration
definition: Market penetration measures how many customers buy or use a product or service out of the total market.

title: Market Portfolio
definition: A market portfolio is a conceptual collection of all investable assets in the market, each proportionally represented by its market value. Understanding the market portfolio is crucial for investors as it forms the foundation of financial theories like the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). The market portfolio signifies the ultimate equity benchmark, exposing only systematic risk, which is the market-wide risk unavoidable through diversification. While creating a truly diversified market portfolio is theoretically challenging, understanding its principles can enhance investment strategies and decision-making.

title: Market Power
definition: Market power, or pricing power, is a company's ability to influence product prices by manipulating supply, demand, or both, enabling control over profit margins and creating barriers for new competitors. For example, Apple has long had significant market power in the smartphone market. In industries with competition, antitrust laws seek to limit excessive market power.

title: Market Price
definition: The market price is the current price at which a product or service can be bought or sold. The market price of a product or service is determined by the forces of supply and demand. The price at which quantity supplied equals quantity demanded is the market price.

title: Market Research
definition: Market research helps companies develop or fine-tune new ideas by examining consumer behavior and market trends.

title: Market Risk
definition: Market risk refers to the potential for financial loss due to fluctuations in the market.

title: Market Risk Premium
definition: The market risk premium (MRP) is the difference between the expected return on a market portfolio and the risk-free rate.

title: Market Saturation
definition: Market saturation is a point where the rate of a product's introduction is insufficient to create new demand in the market.

title: Market Segment
definition: A market segment is a subset of a larger market that is differentiated by specific factors such as needs, behavior, or demographics, enabling businesses to tailor their strategies or products to better serve that particular group.

title: Market Segmentation
definition: Market segmentation separates a large group of prospective buyers into smaller groups for more focused marketing.

title: Market Segmentation Theory
definition: Market segmentation theory is a theory that long-term and short-term interest rates are not related to each other. It also states that the prevailing interest rates for short, intermediate, and long-term bonds should be viewed separately, like items in different markets for debt securities.

title: Market Sentiment
definition: Market sentiment reflects investors' attitudes toward stocks, industries, or the financial market as a whole. Influenced by crowd psychology, it surfaces in trading behaviors. Bullish sentiment signals rising prices, while bearish sentiment indicates declining prices. This concept is crucial for day traders, technical analysts, and contrarian investors who leverage short-term price movements, unaffected by fundamental market changes.

title: Market Share
definition: Market share is the the percent of total sales a company makes in relation to a broader industry.

title: Market Timing
definition: Market timing is the act of moving investment money in or out of a financial market—or switching funds betweenasset classes—based on predictive methods. If investors can accurately predict when the market will rise and fall, they can make trades to capitalize on those market movements and turn them into a profit.

title: Mark to Market (MTM)
definition: Mark-to-market or fair value accounting allows for measuring the fair value of accounts, such as assets and liabilities, based on their current market price.

title: Market Value
definition: Market value is the price an asset would fetch in the market, based on the price that buyers are willing to pay and sellers are willing to accept. It may also refer to themarket capitalizationof a publicly traded company, calculated by multiplying the number ofoutstanding sharesby the current share price.

title: Market Value Added (MVA)
definition: Market value added (MVA) measures how much a company's market value exceeds the total capital invested by shareholders and bondholders. Calculated asMVA = V - K, with V representing total market value and K the invested capital, it shows how effectively management creates value.

title: Market Value of Equity
definition: Market value of equity or market capitalization is the total dollar value of a company's equity, calculated by multiplying the current stock price by the total number of outstanding shares.

title: Marketable Securities
definition: Marketable securities serve as short-term investment vehicles for companies and individuals seeking to maintain liquidity while earning returns on temporarily idle funds.

title: Marketing
definition: Marketing involves promoting and selling products or services, and includes activities such as market research and advertising.

title: Marketing Campaign
definition: Marketing campaigns comprise a series of strategic efforts to use marketing channels to achieve a certain business goal or objective.

title: Marketing Mix
definition: A marketing mix is a strategic framework that encompasses the four Ps: product, price, placement, and promotion. It's used to market a product or service and generate revenue.

title: Marketing Plan
definition: A marketing plan is a document that outlines a company's advertising strategy and methods to reach its target market and sell its products or services.

title: Marketing Strategy
definition: A marketing strategy is a comprehensive plan covering the four Ps of marketing: product, price, place, and promotion, and whose goal is to reach potential customers.

title: MiFID Explained: EU's Financial Regulation for Transparency and Disclosures
definition: The Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID) is a European regulation designed to enhance transparency and standardize regulatory disclosures across the European Union's (EU's) financial markets.

title: Markup
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Married Filing Jointly
definition: Married filing jointly is a tax filing status that allows a married couple to file a single tax return that records both of their taxable incomes, deductions, credits, and exemptions.

title: Married Filing Separately
definition: Married filing separately is a tax status for married couples who choose to record their respectiveincomes,exemptions, anddeductionson separate tax returns. This means both married spouses file separate tax returns. When one spouse has significant medical expenses or miscellaneousitemized deductions, or when both spouses have about the same amount of income, it might bewiser to file separately. The alternative to married filing separately ismarried filing jointly. It usually makes sense financially for married couples to file jointly.

title: Married Put
definition: A married put is an options strategy used by investors who already own stock and buy anat-the-moneyput option on the same shares to protect against a decline in price. It limits downside risk; losses are capped at the option premium plus any drop to the strike, while still allowing full participation in upside gains.

title: Martial Law
definition: Martial law is the replacement of civilian government with military authority, usually imposed during crises or political instability. Under martial law, leaders gain broad powers, often suspending civil liberties such as freedom of movement and due process.

title: Marxian Economics
definition: Marxian economics, derived fromKarl Marx'sseminal works, critiques capitalism's treatment of labor, arguing that it exploits workers and undervalues their contributions. This school of thought challenges classical economic theories by advocating for a system where the state plays a crucial role in economic decisions to ensure fairer wages and benefits across society.

title: Marxism
definition: Marxism, developed byKarl Marx, explores the impact ofcapitalismon labor and economic growth, advocating for change through a worker-led revolution. Central to Marxism is the belief that class struggle—particularly between the ruling bourgeoisie and the working proletariat—shapes economic dynamics, ultimately leading to a communist transformation.

title: Mass Customization
definition: Mass customization is the process of delivering market goods and services that are modified to satisfy a specific customer’s needs.

title: Mass Production
definition: Mass production is the manufacturing of large quantities of a standardized article by an automated mechanical process.

title: Master-Feeder Structure
definition: A master-feeder structure is a financial framework utilized by hedge funds to combine capital from U.S. and overseas investors into a single master fund. This setup involves separatefeeder fundsfor different investor groups, which then channel the assets into a centralizedmaster fundresponsible for all portfolio investments and trading.

title: Master Limited Partnership (MLP)
definition: A master limited partnership (MLP) is a business venture in the form of a publicly tradedlimited partnership. It combines the tax benefits of a private partnership with the liquidity of a publicly traded company.

title: Master of Business Administration (MBA)
definition: A master of business administration (MBA) is a graduate degree that provides theoretical and practical training for business or investment management. An MBA is designed to help graduates gain a better understanding of business management functions.

title: Master of Public Administration (MPA)
definition: A Master of Public Administration is a graduate degree focused on equipping students with the skills necessary for effective governance, policy analysis, and public service management.

title: MasterCard
definition: Mastercard is a financial technology company that facilitates electronic payments by connecting consumers, financial institutions, and businesses globally to enable secure transactions.

title: Matching Orders
definition: Matching orders is the process by which a securitiesexchangepairs one or more unsolicited buy orders with one or more sell orders to make trades. This can be contrasted withrequests for a quote(RFQ) in a security to proceed with a trade.

title: Material Participation Tests
definition: Material participation tests are a set of criteria established by theInternal Revenue Service(IRS) to determine whether a taxpayer has materially participated in a trade, business, rental, or other income-producing activity. A taxpayer is considered to have materially participated if they meet one of the seven material participation tests. If a taxpayer’s participation does not meet at least one of these tests, passive activity rules limit the deductibility of losses.

title: Material Requirements Planning (MRP)
definition: Material requirements planning (MRP) is a software-based integrated inventory and supply management system designed for businesses.

title: Mature Industry
definition: The mature industry phase is a stage in a company’s life cycle when growth slows, and the market becomes well established. Companies in this phase are usually larger, more stable, and consistently profitable, but they don't grow as quickly as before. Businesses often focus on protecting market share, improving efficiency, and merging with competitors to stay strong. Companies in the mature industry phase may have steady earnings, moderate price-to-earnings (P/E) ratios, and regular dividend payments. This can attract income-focused investors.

title: Maturity
definition: Maturity in finance marks the end of afinancial instrument'slifecycle, necessitating renewal or cessation. Common across deposits, loans, and bonds, it signifies when principal repayment or the final transaction occurs. Understanding maturity is crucial for assessing investment horizons and related risks or benefits.

title: Maturity Date
definition: A maturity date is the date a note, draft, acceptance bond, or other debt instrument on which the borrower must repay the principal and any accrued interest.

title: Max Pain
definition: As an options contract nears expiration, the strike price that would cause the maximum amount of pain for the highest possible number of options traders is said to be the maximum pain point.

title: Maximum Drawdown (MDD)
definition: A maximum drawdown (MDD) represents the largest observed decline in the value of an investment from its peak to its subsequent lowest point, before a new peak is attained.

title: Maximum Foreseeable Loss (MFL)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Maximum Loan Amount
definition: A maximum loan amount, or loan limit, describes the total amount of money that an applicant is authorized to borrow. Maximum loan amounts are used for standard loans, credit cards, and line-of-credit accounts.

title: McClellan Oscillator
definition: The McClellan Oscillator is amarket breadthindicator that is based on the difference between the number of advancing and declining issues on a stock exchange, such as the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) or NASDAQ.

title: MCF (Measuring Natural Gas)
definition: MCF is an abbreviation derived from the Roman numeral M for one thousand, put together with cubic feet (CF) to measure a quantity of natural gas. For example, a natural gas well that produces 400 MCF of gas per day operates with a dailyproduction rateof 400,000 cubic feet. One thousand cubic feet (MCF) of gas equals about 1,000,000 BTUs of energy. One BTU is the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of one pound of water by one degree Fahrenheit at sea level (which is roughly equivalent to a kitchen match).

title: McKinsey 7S Model
definition: The McKinsey 7-S model is a strategic framework that emphasizes the need for alignment among seven internal elements to drive organizational success.

title: Mean Reversion
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Mean-Variance Analysis
definition: Mean-variance analysis is an investment evaluation method that considers the expected return or the mean and the risk of different investments by the variance.

title: Medallion Signature Guarantee
definition: A medallion signature guarantee provides a critical layer of security when transferring ownership of securities. Typically required when selling or transferring stocks or bonds in physical form, this certification stamp verifies the authenticity of the signature authorizing the transaction. For those managing their securities through a broker, this guarantee is unnecessary.

title: Media Kit
definition: A media kit is a package of information assembled by a company to provide basic information about itself to reporters. The media kit is a promotional public relations tool that can serve several functions. These functions include promoting the launch of a new company or announcing the launch of a new product or service by an existing company. You can present your company as you would like it be seen and save time by eliminating the need for you or your employees to repeatedly answer the same questions.

title: Median
definition: The median is the middle number in a group of numbers when they are put in order from smallest to largest.

title: Medicaid
definition: Medicaid is a major publichealth insuranceprogram that helps low-income individuals and families access care. Because states administer the program, eligibility and benefits vary widely, though core groups such as children, pregnant women, and older adults are often covered. Recipients must meet income and residency requirements, including being citizens, permanent residents, or eligible legal immigrants.

title: Medical Cost Ratio (MCR)
definition: Medical cost ratio (MCR), also referred to as medical loss ratio, is a metric used in the privatehealth insuranceindustry. The ratio is calculated by dividing total medical expenses paid by an insurer by the totalinsurance premiumsit collected. A lower ratio likely indicates higher profitability for the insurer, as it signifies a larger amount of premiums are left over after paying customer insurance claims.

title: Medium of Exchange
definition: A medium of exchange is a tool accepted universally for facilitating transactions by allowing the purchase of goods and services without barter.

title: Medium Term Note (MTN)
definition: A medium-term note (MTN) is a fixed-income security that usually matures in five to 10 years. These notes fill the gap between short-term (up to five-year maturities) and long-term notes (more than 10 to 30 years maturities).

title: Melt Up
definition: A melt-up is a sustained and often unexpected improvement in the investment performance of an asset orasset class, driven partly by a stampede of investors who don’t want to miss out on its rise, rather than by fundamental economic improvements.

title: Memorandum of Understanding (MOU)
definition: A Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) serves as a formal document outlining an agreement between two or more parties. While an MOU is generally not legally binding, its ultimate enforceability relies on the specific intent and language included. Regardless, it reflects the parties' commitment to advance toward a more formal contract.

title: Mental Accounting
definition: Mental accounting refers to the cognitive process where individuals categorize, track, and evaluate financial transactions in separate mental accounts, affecting their spending and saving behaviors.

title: Menu Costs
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Mercantilism
definition: From the 16th to the 18th century, mercantilism dominated economic policy in Europe. This system hinged on the belief that global wealth was finite, and urged governments to tightly regulate trade to amass wealth and bolster national power. European nations, under mercantilism, sought to expand their share of global wealth by promoting exports and curbing imports through protectivetariffs.

title: Merchandising
definition: Merchandising plays a vital role in retail success by employing strategic marketing techniques such as pricing, display designs, and discounts to attract customers. By understanding these elements, retailers can enhance their brand presence, improve customer experiences, and ultimately drive sales, keeping ahead in an ever-evolving competitive landscape.

title: Merchant Bank
definition: A merchant bank is a financial institution that conducts underwriting, loan services, financial advising, and fundraising services for large corporations andhigh-net-worth individuals (HNWI). They are non-depository and play a significant role in international finance and transactions.

title: Merchant Discount Rate
definition: The merchant discount rate is a cost applied to businesses accepting card payments, serving as compensation for payment processors facilitating the transaction.

title: Merger
definition: A merger combines two companies into one. Companies merge for various reasons, and there are different types of mergers.Mergers and acquisitions (M&A)are commonly done to expand a company’s reach, expand into new segments, or gainmarket share. All of these are done to increaseshareholder value. Often, during a merger, companies have ano-shop clauseto prevent purchases or mergers by additional companies.

title: Merger Arbitrage
definition: Merger arbitrage is an investing strategy used byhedge fundsto make money from corporate mergers. This strategy involves buying the stock of a takeover target while accounting for the risk that the deal might not go through. Investors, called merger arbitrageurs, look for market inefficiencies and profit opportunities during mergers. This approach focuses on big corporate events like mergers, acquisitions, or reorganizations.

title: Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A)
definition: Mergers and acquisitions is a term used to describe businesses combining through different types of transactions.

title: Merrill Lynch
definition: Merrill Lynch & Co. is the former name of a prominentWall Streetinvestment firm. Since its acquisition byBank of America(BAC) in 2009, it has become known simply as “Merrill” and operates as awealth managementdivision of Bank of America.

title: Merton Model
definition: The Merton Model, proposed by economist Robert C. Merton in 1974, is a vital tool for evaluating a company's credit default risk. By modeling corporateequityas a call option on its assets, it assists stock analysts and commercial loan officers in making informed decisions. Understanding the Merton Model provides insights into assessing structuralcredit riskand supports better financial decision-making.

title: Metrics
definition: Metrics are critical tools forquantitative assessmentused across accounting, operations, and performance analysis. Executives, analysts, and managers rely on a well-structured set of metrics to create dashboards that help maintain performance assessments and inform business strategies. By utilizing a diverse range of industry standards and proprietary models, metrics facilitate strategic decisions in corporate finance, project management, and investment analysis.

title: Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA)
definition: Metropolitan statistical areas (MSA) are delineated by the U.S. Office of Management and Budget (OMB) as having at least one urbanized area with a minimum population of 50,000.

title: Mezzanine Debt
definition: Mezzanine debt occurs when a hybrid debt issue is subordinated to another debt issue from the same issuer. Mezzanine debt has embedded equity instruments attached, often known as warrants, which increase the attractiveness of the subordinated debt and allow greater flexibility when dealing with bondholders.

title: Mezzanine Financing
definition: Mezzanine financing stands as a strategic financial tool that bridges the gap between senior debt and equity. This hybrid form of financing provides lenders the potential to convert debt to equity, fostering flexibility and higher returns. Frequently employed in acquisitions and company expansions, mezzanine financing encapsulates both opportunity and risk, offering companies crucial capital for growth.

title: Michael Bloomberg
definition: Michael Bloomberg is a billionaire businessman, publisher, philanthropist, and former three-term mayor of New York City. He's one of thewealthiest people in the worldwith an estimated net worth of $104.7 billion as of Jan. 28, 2025, according to Forbes.

title: Who Is Michael Milken?
definition: Michael Milken is a billionaire financier best known as the man behind the junk bond boom of the late 1970s and 1980s. Milken found a way to lend money to riskier companies struggling to access credit, which led to a boom and a fortune for Milken.

title: Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index (MCSI)
definition: The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index (MCSI) is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the United States conducted by the University of Michigan. The survey is based on telephone interviews that gather information on consumer expectations for the economy.

title: Micro Cap
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Micro Manager
definition: A micromanager is someone who excessively supervises employees, focusing on controlling every task detail. This management style, while potentially yielding short-term results, often undermines morale and hampers team productivity in the long run.

title: Micro-Investing Platform
definition: A micro-investing platform is an application that allows users to regularly save small sums of money. Micro-investing platforms aim to remove traditional barriers to investing, such as brokerageaccount minimums, and encourage people to invest even if they have limited incomes and assets.

title: Microcredit
definition: Microcredit is a financial service aimed at providing small loans to individuals with limited income to boost their entrepreneurship efforts.

title: Microeconomics
definition: Microeconomics is a branch of economics that studies how individuals and businesses respond to changes in incentives, prices, resources, and/or methods of production.

title: Microfinance
definition: Microfinance, also called microcredit​, is a type of banking service provided to low-income individuals or groups who otherwise wouldn't have access to financial services. While institutions participating in microfinance most often provide lending—microloans can range from as small as $50 to under $50,000.

title: Micromarketing
definition: Micromarketing is an approach to advertising that tends to target a specific group of people in a niche market. With micromarketing, products or services are marketed directly to a targeted group of customers.

title: Mid-Cap
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Mid-Cap Fund
definition: A mid-cap fund is a pooled investment vehicle (e.g. a mutual fund or ETF) that explicitly invests in the stocks ofmid-capcompanies, or companies withmarket capitalizationsranging from approximately $2 billion to $10 billion.

title: Middle East and North Africa (MENA)
definition: MENA, the Middle East and North Africa region, includes around 19 to 27 countries depending on the definition used. Rich in oil and natural gas, MENA is crucial to global energy and economic stability. While encompassing a diverse set of economies such as Saudi Arabia's oil-dependent landscape and Israel's high-tech hub, the region is pivotal for its geopolitical significance but faces challenges, including persistent conflicts.

title: Middle-Income Countries (MICs)
definition: According to the World Bank, middle-income countries (MICs) are defined as economies with agross national income (GNI)per capita between $1,136 and $13,845 as of 2024.MICsconsist of lower-middle-income countries and upper-middle-income countries, both of which are part of the income categories that the World Bank uses to classify economies for operational and analytical purposes.

title: Middle Market Firm
definition: Middle market firms, with annual revenues between $10 million and $1 billion, are integral to the U.S. economy. Comprising about 200,000 firms, these privately held or closely owned companies contribute over $10 trillion in annualrevenuesand support approximately 48 million jobs. They are essential for job growth and are primarily service-oriented businesses.

title: Middle Office
definition: The middle office is the department in financial firms that lies between the front and back offices. It manages risk, calculates profits and losses, and oversees information technology (IT). Middle offices became prevalent with the increasing complexity of financial assets and technology.

title: Middleman
definition: A middleman helps connect buyers and sellers in business and financial transactions. They make transactions easier and support the supply chain, but using them can add costs. Brokers, agents, wholesalers, and distributors are all examples of middlemen. Technology and e-commerce have changed the role of middlemen, allowing some transactions to happen directly while still keeping middlemen important in many industries.

title: Midstream Oil Operations
definition: Midstream is a crucial phase in oil and gas operations, focusing on processing, storing, transporting, and marketing oil, natural gas, andnatural gas liquids. It acts as a bridge between the upstream extraction of raw crude and the downstream refining into consumer products. Understanding these activities highlights the importance ofintegrated oil companiesthat manage all stages.

title: MiFID II Explained: Key Regulations and Impact in the EU
definition: MiFID II represents a landmark regulatory framework introduced by the European Union in 2018 to enhance investor protection, increase transparency, and standardize practices across EU financial markets. Emerging in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis, it addresses key areas such as service models and trading venues to restore confidence and ensure fair practices. By replacing and expanding upon MiFID, MiFID II targets improved oversight and increased transparency across all financial services.

title: Mill Levy
definition: The mill levy is the tax rate expressed in mills applied to a property's assessed value.

title: Mill Rate
definition: A mill rate, often referred to as the millage rate, determines the amount of property tax payable per dollar of a property'sassessed value. Understanding how mill rates are calculated is essential as they form the basis of your property tax bill, which helps fund local government budgets, including infrastructure, public services, and schools.
