By knowing your local mill rate, often found on your property deed or through municipal offices, you can accurately calculate your property tax obligations. This guide will walk you through the determination of mill rates, how they combine different taxing entities' rates, and the steps to compute your property taxes accurately, enabling you to effectively plan your finances.

title: Millage Rate
definition: A mill rate, often referred to as the millage rate, determines the amount of property tax payable per dollar of a property'sassessed value. Understanding how mill rates are calculated is essential as they form the basis of your property tax bill, which helps fund local government budgets, including infrastructure, public services, and schools. By knowing your local mill rate, often found on your property deed or through municipal offices, you can accurately calculate your property tax obligations. This guide will walk you through the determination of mill rates, how they combine different taxing entities' rates, and the steps to compute your property taxes accurately, enabling you to effectively plan your finances.

title: Millennial
definition: Millennials are the generation that came of age near the dawn of the 21st century—the new millennium. As the first to be born into a digital world, members of this group are considereddigital natives. Technology has always been a part of their everyday lives—it’s been estimated that they check their phones as many as 150 times daily—and serving them has been a major contributing factor to the growth of Silicon Valley and other technology hubs.

title: Milton Friedman
definition: Milton Friedman was a renowned economist and Nobel laureate who championed free-market capitalism and monetarism. His radical advocacy for monetary policy over fiscal policy reshapedmacroeconomicthought, challenging the prevailingKeynesianeconomics of his time, which held thatfiscal policy—government spending and tax policies to influence the economy—was more important thanmonetary policy.

title: Minimum Efficient Scale (MES)
definition: The minimum efficient scale (MES) is the point at which a company can produce its product for the lowest possible per-unit cost. At MES, the company can achieve theeconomies of scalenecessary for it to compete effectively in its industry. The MES point represents a valuable balance between consumer demand, production volume, and costs, in particular those for labor and materials needed for production.

title: Minimum Lease Payment
definition: The minimum lease payment is the least amount a lessor can expect to make, and a lessee can expect to pay over the lifetime of a lease. Accountants calculate minimum lease payments in order to assign a present value to a lease to record it properly in the company's books. Minimum lease payment estimates employ a 90% test for an asset's recovery of investment, regardless of whether it's a capital or operating lease.

title: Minimum Monthly Payment
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Minimum Wage
definition: A minimum wage is the lowest wage that a worker may be paid per hour. It's a price floor on hourly wages mandated by federal law.Nonexempt workerscan't be offered a job or agree to work for less than this amount.

title: How Does Bitcoin Mining Work? A Beginner's Guide
definition: Bitcoinmining is the process by which transactions are officially entered on the blockchain and new bitcoins are launched into circulation. Miners use computers to find a cryptographic number that meets the Bitcoin network’s difficulty rules.

title: Minority Interest
definition: A minority interest represents a non-controlling ownership stake in a subsidiary company. Though the parent company holds the majority of voting shares, minority interest holders enjoy some rights, including participating in sales and certain audit privileges.

title: Minsky Moment
definition: A Minsky moment refers to a sudden collapse of asset prices after a long period of growth sparked by debt or currency pressures. It's at the explosive juncture of an economy about to fall.

title: Misery Index
definition: The misery index measures economic distress felt by everyday people due to the risk of (or actual) joblessness combined with an increasing cost of living. The misery index is calculated by adding the seasonally adjusted unemployment rate to theinflationrate.

title: Misrepresentation
definition: Misrepresentation happens when someone gives wrong or incomplete information that changes how the other person views a contractual decision.

title: Mission Statement
definition: A company uses a mission statement to define its culture, values, ethics, and fundamental goals.

title: Mixed Economic System
definition: A mixed economic system is an economic structure in which both private enterprise and government participation play significant roles in allocating resources.

title: Mobile Banking: Definition, Advantages, Security Issues
definition: Mobile banking is a service that allows users to conduct financial transactions on a mobile device, such as a smartphone or tablet. Mobile banking features can be as simple as a bank sendingfraudor usage activity to a client’s cell phone or as complex as a client paying bills or sending money abroad. Advantages of mobile banking include the ability to bank anywhere, anytime, as long as you have an internet connection. Disadvantages include security concerns and a limited range of capabilities when compared to banking in person or on a computer.

title: Mobile Commerce
definition: Mobile commerce, or m-commerce, uses handheld devices like smartphones and tablets for online transactions, such as buying products, banking, and paying bills. It's convenient and has grown rapidly, improving security and increasing use of mobile devices.

title: Mobile Wallet: What It Is, How It Works, and More
definition: A mobile wallet stores credit or debit card information on a mobile device, such as asmartphone, tablet, or smartwatch. Mobile wallets are a convenient way for you to buy things online or in stores that are set up to take payments through the wallet. Theymay also be more securethan physical payment cards because of the technology they use to protect your account information.

title: Mode
definition: In statistics, the mode is the number that occurs most often in a set of data.

title: Model Risk
definition: Model risk is a type of risk that occurs when afinancial modelis used to measure quantitative information such as a firm'smarket risksor value transactions, and the model fails or performs inadequately and leads to adverse outcomes for the firm.

title: Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)
definition: The modern portfolio theory (MPT) is a mathematical investment strategy that’s designed to balance the risk and return of assets in a portfolio based on the investor’s risk tolerance.

title: Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS)
definition: The Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is the primary tax depreciation system used in the United States. It allows businesses to recover the capitalized cost of certain assets over time through annualdepreciationdeductions. The system categorizes assets into classes with set depreciation periods, making it an essential tool for businesses aiming to reduce taxable income by accounting for the wear and tear of their assets.

title: Modified Accrual Accounting
definition: Modified accrual accounting is a financial reporting method that blends elements of cash and accrual accounting, acknowledging revenues based on availability rather than timing.

title: Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Modified Cash Basis
definition: Modified cash basis accounting mixescashandaccrualmethods. It seeks to get the best of both worlds, recording sales and expenses forlong-term assetson an accrual basis and those ofshort-term assetson a cash basis. It's practical for internal purposes due to non-compliance with International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) and generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP).

title: Modified Endowment Contract
definition: A modified endowment contract (MEC) is a type of life insurance policy that has lost its tax benefits because it has excess cash value.

title: Modified Dietz Method
definition: The modified Dietz method measures a portfolio's return based on a weighted calculation of cash flows—that is, it takes into account both the amount and timing of cash flows into the portfolio.

title: Modified Duration
definition: Modified duration can tell investors how much the price of a bond is likely to change when interest rates increase or decrease.

title: Modified Gross Lease
definition: A modified gross lease uniquely blends elements of both gross and net leases, sharing operating expenses between landlords and tenants. Predominantly used in commercial spaces, this lease type necessitates clarity on each party's financial obligations, making it crucial for potential tenants to understand their responsibilities thoroughly. Such leases offer balanced cost-sharing, contributing to their popularity in offices with multiple tenants.

title: Modified Internal Rate of Return (MIRR)
definition: The modified internal rate of return can provide a realistic return for an investment with various and irregular cash flows.

title: Modigliani-Miller Theorem (M&M)
definition: The Modigliani-Miller theorem (M&M) was developed in the 1950s by Merton Miller and Franco Modigliani. This important and still relevant theory states that the market value of a company is calculated as the present value of its future earnings andunderlying assets. They believed that taxes and government regulations do not contribute to capital structure, and that market value is independent of a company'scapital structure.

title: Modus Operandi
definition: "Modus operandi" is a predictable pattern of behavior important in both criminal and business contexts. M.O. can aid businesses in maintaining stability and predicting future moves within their operations.

title: Mom-and-Pop
definition: A mom-and-pop establishment is a small enterprise typically owned and managed by a family, emphasizing personalized customer service and community engagement to thrive.

title: Momentum
definition: Momentum in trading refers to how quickly asecurity'sprice changes, capturing the momentum of its trend. This strategy leverages the speed of price changes to identify trends for trade opportunities, often usingoscillatorsintechnical analysisto spot these potential moves.

title: Momentum Investing
definition: Momentum investing is a trading strategy where investors seek to profit by buyingstocksthat are already rising in price and selling them at their peak. The idea is that well-performing investments may continue to thrive. Investors track price trends, buy strong performers, and sell when the trend starts to slow. The main benefits are the chance for quick gains and clear decision rules, but the risks include sudden price drops and market swings. Economists, such as Eugene Fama, have shown that momentum patterns have appeared in market data over long periods, helping explain why it's a popular strategy.

title: Monetarism
definition: Monetarism, a notablemacroeconomictheory, posits that themoney supplyis a crucial determinant in economic stability. Advocated by economist Milton Friedman, this theory suggests that governments can stabilize economies by controlling money supply growth. In contrast, Keynesian economics emphasizes fiscal policy to manage economic demand. Understanding the differences between these approaches can offer insights into economic policy and stability.

title: Monetarist
definition: A monetarist is aneconomistwho holds the strong belief thatmoney supply—including physical currency, deposits, and credit—is the primary factor affecting demand in an economy. Consequently, the economy's performance—its growth or contraction—can be regulated by changes in the money supply.

title: Monetarist Theory
definition: Monetarist theory emphasizes the significant role of a central bank in influencing economic outcomes by altering a nation's money supply.

title: Monetary Aggregates
definition: Monetary aggregates are the various measurements of the money supply in an economy. In the United States, they are used to evaluate the economic health and stability of the nation. In addition, the Federal Reserve uses them to implement its monetary policy.

title: Monetary Base
definition: The monetary base represents the sum of all physicalcurrencyin circulation and the reserves held by commercial banks at the central bank. It serves as the backbone of themoney supply, enabling transactions and fueling economic activity. However, it excludes non-currency money forms prevalent today, which are encapsulated in broader measures like M1 and M2.

title: Monetary Policy
definition: Monetary policy is the combination of strategy and actions that the Federal Reserve employs to achieve the economic goals set for it by Congress.

title: Monetize
definition: Monetize means transforming something non-revenue-generating into a source of income. This may involve novel income methods like embedding ads in social media or turning a public asset into a profit center. Monetization can manifest in many contexts, from government debt strategies to social media data sales. The concept is pivotal in today's digital economy, where businesses, content creators, and platforms continuously seek innovative ways to generate revenue.

title: Money
definition: Money is a store of value, a unit of account, and a standard of deferred payment. It facilitates transactions in an economy and serves as a medium of exchange. Its key properties are fungibility and durability, which reduce transaction costs. Thus, using money for transactions is less costly for buyers and sellers compared tobarter trading. Most money systems are based on standardized currencies that are controlled by central banks. However, money has evolved over time and now includes fiduciary media and electroniccryptocurrencies.

title: Money Center Banks
definition: Money center banks are financial institutions that primarily engage in borrowing and lending with large entities like governments, largecorporations, and other banks, rather than individual consumers. Located in major economic hubs such as London, Hong Kong, Tokyo, and New York, these banks play a crucial role in the global financial system. Money center banks are pivotal in managing significant capital flows and supporting economic stability. In this guide, you'll learn about their structure, roles, and how they differ from traditional banks.

title: Money Factor
definition: Money factor is a method for determining the financing charges on a lease with monthly payments. A money factor can be translated into the more commonannual percentage rate(APR) by multiplying the money factor by 2,400.

title: Money Flow
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Money Flow Index (MFI)
definition: The money flow index measures the flow of money into and out of an asset by analyzing both price movements and trading volume. Using a scale of 0 to 100, it helps traders identify potential reversals when readings reach extreme levels or diverge from price action.

title: Money Illusion
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Money Laundering
definition: Money laundering involves disguising illegally obtained money or assets to make them appear lawful.

title: Money Management
definition: Money management involves budgeting, saving, investing, and spending, whether for individuals, households, or organizations. This article explores the significant role of money managers, particularly in investment contexts, highlighting the top firms like BlackRock, Vanguard, and Fidelity by their assets under management.

title: Money Manager
definition: A money manager is a person or financial firm that manages the securities portfolio of an individual or institutional investor. Typically, a money manager employs people with various expertise ranging from research and selection of investment options to monitoring the assets and deciding when to sell them.

title: Money Market
definition: The money market is a financial market that deals in short-term borrowing and lending without promising significant returns.

title: Money Market Account
definition: A money market account (MMA) is a type of bank account that earns interest and combines features of savings and checking accounts. Available at credit unions and banks, MMAs usually come with checks and a debit card but may limit the number of times you can withdraw money every month.

title: Money Market Fund
definition: A money market fund is a mutual fund that invests in short-term, liquid debt instruments, offering high liquidity and low risk while maintaining a stable $1 per share net asset value (NAV).

title: Money Market Yield
definition: Money market yield provides investors with a measure of return from highly liquid securities and short-term investments like U.S. Treasury bills, CDs, and municipal notes. It involves calculating the holding period yield adjusted for a 360-day bank year. This yield is closely tied to CD-equivalent and bond equivalent yields, offering insights into short-term financial securities' performance.

title: Money Order
definition: A money order is a prepaid form of payment that provides a secure way to send money or make payments.

title: Money Purchase Pension Plan
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Money Supply
definition: The money supply is the sum total of all of the currency and other liquid assets in a country's economy on the date measured. The money supply includes allcash in circulationand all bank deposits that the account holder can easily convert to cash. To keep the economy stable, banking regulators increase or reduce the available money supply through policy changes and regulatory decisions.

title: Money-Weighted Rate of Return
definition: A money-weighted rate of return (MWRR) evaluates an investment's performance by considering the timing and size of cash flows, such as dividends and withdrawals. MWRR is synonymous with the internal rate of return (IRR) and provides a comprehensive view that incorporates investor behavior into performance assessments. Distinct from the time-weighted rate of return (TWRR), which removes cash flow effects, MWRR offers insights into how your actions affect investment returns.

title: Monopolist
definition: A monopolist is an individual, group, or company that controls all of the market for a particular good or service. A monopolist probably also believes in policies that favormonopoliessince it gives them greater power. A monopolist has little incentive to improve their product because customers have no alternatives. Instead, their motivation is focused on protecting the monopoly.

title: Monopolistic Competition
definition: Monopolistic competition is a market structure in which companies compete against each other by offering products or services that are only slightly different. As a result, no single product or service dominates the market.

title: Monopolistic Markets
definition: A monopolistic market is a theoretical condition that describes a market where only one company may offer products and services to the public. Amonopolistic marketis the opposite of aperfectly competitivemarket, in which an infinite number of firms operate. In a purely monopolistic model, the monopoly firm can restrict output, raise prices, and enjoy super-normal profits in the long run.

title: Monopoly
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Monopsony
definition: A monopsony is a market condition in which there is only one buyer, the monopsonist. Like amonopoly, a monopsony also has imperfect market conditions. The difference between amonopoly and a monopsonylies in the difference between the controlling entities. An individual seller controls a monopolized market while a single buyer dominates a monopsony. Monopsonists are common in areas where they supply most or all of the region's jobs.

title: Monte Carlo Simulation
definition: A Monte Carlo simulation estimates the likelihood of different outcomes by accounting for the presence of random variables.

title: Month-To-Month Tenancy
definition: A month-to-month tenancy is when a tenant rents from a landlord for one month at a time.

title: Monthly Active User (MAU)
definition: Monthly active users (MAU) is a key performance indicator (KPI) that measures the number of unique users who engage with a site or app within a month. It serves as a main metric for assessing a company's overall health and user engagement, often influencing investor sentiment.

title: Moore's Law
definition: Moore's Law, first posited by Gordon E. Moore in 1965, observes that the number of transistors on microchips doubles roughly every two years while costs decrease. Not a fundamental law of science, this observation has nonetheless been a guiding principle in the semiconductor industry for nearly six decades. It predicts that as transistors become smaller, computing technology will continually advance, becoming faster, more energy-efficient, and more cost-effective over time. Today, Moore's insight remains a critical benchmark for technological progress, influencing everything from smartphones to data centers.

title: Moral Hazard
definition: Moral hazard is the risk that a party has not entered into a contract in good faith or has provided misleading information about itsassets,liabilities, orcredit capacity. In addition, moral hazard also may mean a party has an incentive to take unusual risks in a desperate attempt to earn aprofitbefore the contract settles.

title: Moral Suasion
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Moratorium
definition: A moratorium temporarily suspends activities or laws, often during financial or legal hardships, to provide time for resolution. Imposed by governments or businesses, they help manage crises by halting normal operations until the underlying issues are resolved.

title: Morbidity Rate
definition: Morbidity rate refers to the rate at which a disease or illness occurs in a population. This can be used to determine the health of a population and its health care needs. Illnesses can range from acute conditions to chronic, long-lasting conditions.

title: Morning Star
definition: A Morning Star is a bullishcandlestickpattern recognized by traders for signaling a potential trendreversalin a downtrend. Comprised of three distinct candles, it indicates the start of an upward climb. Technical analysts use additional indicators to confirm the shift, making it a pivotal tool in trading strategies.

title: Morningstar Inc.
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Morningstar Risk Rating
definition: The Morningstar risk rating ranksmutual fundsand exchange-traded funds (ETFs) from 1 to 5 based on their risk-adjusted returns, helping investors gauge performance and suitability for theirportfolios.

title: Morningstar Sustainability Rating
definition: The Morningstar Sustainability Rating is a framework for comparing thousands of mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) based onenvironmental, social, and governance (ESG)standards.

title: Mortality Table
definition: A mortality table presents the likelihood that individuals within a specific population will die during a certain timeframe.

title: Mortgage
definition: A mortgage is a loan used to purchase or maintain real estate, where the property serves as collateral.

title: Mortgage-Backed Security (MBS)
definition: Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) are investments like bonds. Each MBS consists of a bundle of home loans and other real estate debt bought from the banks that issued them.

title: Mortgage Banker
definition: A mortgage banker is a company, individual, or institution that originates mortgages. Mortgage bankers use their own funds, or funds borrowed from awarehouse lender, to fund mortgages.After a mortgage is originated, a mortgage banker might retain the mortgage in a portfolio or sell the mortgage to an investor. Additionally, after a mortgage is originated, a mortgage banker might service the mortgage, or they might sell theservicing rightsto another financial institution. A mortgage banker's primary business is to earn the fees associated with loanorigination. Most mortgage bankers do not retain the mortgage in a portfolio.

title: Mortgage Bond
definition: A mortgage bond, secured by real estate assets, provides investors with protection due to its underlying value. In case of default, this asset can be liquidated to compensate bondholders. While safer, mortgage bonds typically yield lower returns compared to corporate bonds. They are traded in secondary markets and offer stable, income-producing opportunities but carry risks, notably highlighted during the financial crisis.

title: Mortgage Broker
definition: A mortgage broker is an intermediary who brings together borrowers and lenders but who does not directly lend any money.

title: Mortgage Forbearance Agreement
definition: A mortgageforbearanceagreement is an agreement made between amortgage lenderand adelinquentborrower. In this agreement, a lender agrees not to exercise its legal right to foreclose on a mortgage, and the borrower agrees to a mortgage plan that will—over a certain time period—bring the borrower current on their payments.

title: Mortgage Insurance
definition: Mortgage insurance is an insurance policy that protects amortgage lenderor titleholder if the borrower defaults on payments, passes away, or is otherwise unable to meet the contractual obligations of the mortgage.

title: Mortgage Originator
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Mortgage Rate
definition: A mortgage rate is the interest charged for a home loan represented as an annual percentage.

title: Mortgage Rate Lock Float Down
definition: The termmortgagerate lock float down refers to afinancingoption that locks in the interest rate on a mortgage with the option to reduce it if market rates fall during the lock period. A typical rate lock provides a borrower security against an increase during the rate lock period.The float down option specifically allows the borrower to take advantage of a fall in interest rates during the lock period.

title: Mortgage Recast
definition: A mortgage recast, also called a loan recast, is a feature of some types of mortgages where the remaining monthly payments are recalculated based on a new amortization schedule. During a mortgage recast, the borrower pays a large sum toward their principal, and their mortgage is then recalculated based on the new, lower balance outstanding.

title: Mortgage Servicing Rights (MSR)
definition: Mortgage servicing rights (MSR) refer to a contractual agreement in which the originalmortgage lendersells the right to service an existing mortgage to a party that specializes in the various functions involved with servicing mortgages.

title: Mortgagee
definition: A mortgagee is a lender, specifically an entity that lends money to a borrower to purchase real estate. In amortgagetransaction, the lender serves as the mortgagee, and the borrower is known as the mortgagor.

title: Mortgagor
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Mosaic Theory
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Most-Favored-Nation Clause
definition: The most-favored-nation (MFN) clause is a key rule in international trade that requires countries to treat allWorld Trade Organization (WTO)members equally by offering them the same trade terms. It helps promote fairness and prevents discrimination in global trade, but there are exceptions for regional trade blocs, developing countries, or for unfair competition. MFN has been central to U.S. trade policy and appears in federal laws and negotiations, with examples like the Jackson-Vanik amendment showing how it has adapted over time.

title: Mothballing
definition: Mothballing is the deactivation and preservation of equipment or a production facility for possible future use or sale. It can also mean the setting aside of an object or idea for possible reuse or revisiting in the future. Mothballing is common with expensivecapital goods, machinery, aircraft, ships, properties, and other assets that are costly to create, have long useful lives, and may be subject to unpredictablemarket disruptions.

title: Moving Average (MA)
definition: Technical traders use the moving average (MA) indicator to more easily track stock price trends by eliminating random price fluctuations.

title: Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)
definition: Moving average convergence/divergence (MACD) is atechnical indicatorthat helps investors identify price trends, measure trend momentum, and identify entry points for buying or selling a security. Gerald Appel developed the MACD indicator in the 1970s, and it is still one of the most popular technical tools today.It is readily available on most trading platforms offered by toponline stock brokers.

title: MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI)
definition: The MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) is a stock index designed to track broad global equity-market performance. Maintained byMorgan Stanley Capital International(MSCI), the index comprises the stocks of nearly 3,000 companies from 23 developed countries and 24emerging marketsas of Dec. 29, 2023.

title: MSCI Inc.
definition: MSCI is a service that provides investors with financial analysis, data, and research.

title: Multi-Asset Class
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Multi-Factor Model
definition: A multi-factor model employs multiple factors to analyze and explain market activities andequilibriumasset prices. Typically used to assess individual securities orportfolios, these models compare factors, offering insights into variable relationships and performance outcomes.

title: Multicollinearity Explained: Impact and Solutions for Accurate Analysis
definition: Multicollinearity poses challenges when multiple regression models have independent variables that are highly correlated. This can skew analysis and produce unreliable results. Detecting multicollinearity involves using methods like the Variance Inflation Factor (VIF), which helps ensure more accurate statistical analysis and informed investment decisions.

title: Understanding Multilateral Development Banks: Types and Key Examples
definition: A multilateral development bank (MDB) is an international financial institution chartered by two or more countries to encourage economic development in lower and middle-income countries.

title: MIGA: Investment Risk Insurance for Developing Countries
definition: The Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency is an international institution that promotesinvestmentin developing countries by offering political and economicriskinsurance.

title: Understanding Multilateral Trading Facilities (MTF): A Complete Guide
definition: Multilateral Trading Facilities (MTFs) are electronic platforms offering an alternative to traditional exchanges for trading financial instruments. Operated by market operators and banks, MTFs connect buyers and sellers efficiently, often for exotic assets and OTC products, under the MiFID II framework. They provide faster, cost-effective transactions with transparent pricing.

title: Multilevel Marketing
definition: Multilevel marketing is a business model where independent distributors sell products and recruit others, earning commissions on both personal sales and the sales of their network.

title: Multinational Corporation: History, Characteristics, and Types
definition: A multinational corporation (MNC) is a company based in one country that actively operates and earns revenue in one or more other countries through foreign subsidiaries or branches.

title: Multiple
definition: A multiple is a crucial tool for evaluating a company's valuation and financial health. By dividing one financialmetricby another, investors can assess if a company is relatively underpriced or overpriced in its industry. Understanding different types of multiples, such as price-to-earnings (P/E) and EV-to-EBITDA, helps investors make informed comparisons and investment decisions.

title: Multiple Employer Welfare Arrangement (MEWA)
definition: A multiple employer welfare arrangement (MEWA) enables groups of employers to pool resources and provide health and welfarebenefitsto their employees more cost-effectively.

title: Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)
definition: Multiple linear regression (MLR) is a method for estimating how several independent factors together influence a single outcome. It fits a straight-line equation to data points to reveal how each variable contributes when the others are held steady.

title: Multiple Listing Service (MLS)
definition: A Multiple Listing Service (MLS) is a vital tool in real estate, offering a comprehensive database of property listings within a specific region. Created byreal estateagents, MLS allows agents and brokers to share detailed property information. This cooperation also benefits buyers and sellers by providing access to a wide range of listings and enabling efficient market transactions. An MLS can help agents see one another’s listings and connect buyers looking for properties on the market. A local MLS can also provide prospective homebuyers with detailed information on each property for sale, including features, square footage, and listing price.

title: Multiples Approach
definition: The multiples approach is avaluationtheory based on the idea that similar assets sell at similar prices. It assumes that the type of ratio used in comparing firms, such as operating margins or cash flows, is the same across similar firms.

title: Multiplier
definition: In economics and finance, a multiplier is a factor that causes increases or changes in related variables when it’s changed.

title: Multiplier Effect
definition: A multiplier effect is the proportional change in income that results from a change in spending.

title: Mumbai Interbank Offered Rate (MIBOR)
definition: The Mumbai Interbank Offer Rate (MIBOR) was the rate at which banks in India wanted to charge other banks to lend them money. It was used in conjunction with the Mumbai Interbank Bid (MIBID) rate, which was the rate banks wanted to pay to borrow money.

title: Municipal Bond
definition: Municipal bonds are financial instruments used by local governments to raise money for public works projects.

title: Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board (MSRB)
definition: The Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board (MSRB) is a regulating body thatcreates rules and policiesfor investment firms and banks in the issuing and sale of municipal bonds, notes, and other municipal securities. States, cities, and counties issue municipal securities for a variety of reasons.

title: Murabaha
definition: Murabaha is a bank lending system that relies on cost-plus pricing rather than interest charges to avoid violating Islamic laws against predatory lending.

title: Musharakah
definition: With Musharakah, a feature of Islamic finance, is a joint enterprise or partnership in which investors share in the profits and losses of an enterprise. According to Islamic law (Sharia), financiers cannot profit from the interest earned on lending. Thus, musharakah allows the investor to earn a return in the form of a portion of the actual profits, according to a predetermined ratio. However, the investor also faces losses that may occur, again on apro ratabasis.

title: Mutual Company
definition: A mutual company is structured to prioritize customer ownership, allowing policyholders to benefit from the firm's financial success.

title: Mutual Fund
definition: A mutual fund is a financial vehicle in which shareholders put their money together to invest in securities (e.g., stocks, bonds, money market instruments). A fund manager chooses the best investments, and every investor shares in the profits if the investments do well.

title: Mutual Insurance Company
definition: A mutual insurance company is an insurer structured so that policyholders own the company and share in its financial performance through benefits like dividends or premium reductions.

title: Mutually Exclusive
definition: Mutually exclusive events or factors cannot coexist in the same place and time.

title: NACHA
definition: Nacha is the steward of the electronic system that connects all U.S. bank accounts and facilitates the movement of money among them. According to the organization, $73 trillion moved through itsAutomated Clearing House (ACH)Network in 2021.

title: Naked Call
definition: A naked call is an options strategy in which an investor writes (sells) call options without owning the underlying security.

title: Naked Option
definition: A naked option is created when the option writer or seller doesn't own any or enough of the underlying security to meet their potential obligation.

title: Naked Put
definition: A naked put is an options strategy in which the investor writes, or sells,put optionswithout holding a short position in theunderlying security. A naked put strategy is sometimes referred to as an "uncovered put" or a "short put" and the seller of an uncovered put is known as a naked writer.

title: Naked Shorting
definition: Naked short selling is a controversial practice where traders sell assets without first borrowing them. This can cause a supply shortage of shorted securities, potentially impacting market prices. Despite being largely banned in the U.S. and the EU following the 2008 financial crisis, the practice persists due to regulatory loopholes, which affect market stability and transparency.

title: Named Beneficiary
definition: A named beneficiary is an individual, decreed by a written legal document, who is entitled to collect assets from a trust, insurance policy, pension plan account,IRA, or any other financial instrument. If a property has multiple named beneficiaries, they share in the proceeds at the time of disposition. In some cases, such as anannuitypolicy, the policyholder and the named beneficiary may be the same person.

title: Named Perils Insurance Policy
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Nanny Tax
definition: A nanny tax is a federal tax paid by people who employ household employees and pay wages over a certain amount. In 2021, for cash wages of $2,300 or more per employee, social security and Medicare taxes must be withheld at a rate of 15.3%, with the employer and employee each paying half (7.65%). If paying cash wages of $1,000 or more per quarter per employee, the employer pays a 6% unemployment tax on annual cash wages up to $7,000.

title: Narrow Money
definition: Narrow money is a category of money supply that includes all physical money such as coins and currency,demand deposits, and otherliquid assetsheld by a central bank.

title: Nasdaq
definition: Nasdaq is a pioneering global electronic marketplace that revolutionized the buying and selling of securities. Originally an acronym for the National Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotations, Nasdaq was established by theNational Association of Securities Dealers(now known asFINRA) in 1971, ushering in the world's first automated stock exchange.

title: Nasdaq 100 Index
definition: The Nasdaq 100 Index aggregates the largest 100 nonfinancial companies listed on the Nasdaq exchange, spanning a wide array of industries like technology, healthcare, and consumer staples. While it excludes financial sector companies, it offers a diverse representation of the market's most dynamically traded entities.

title: Nasdaq Capital Market
definition: The Nasdaq Capital Market is a tier for early-stage companies with lower market capitalizations on the U.S. Nasdaq exchange. The listing requirements for companies on the Nasdaq Capital Market are less stringent than for the two other Nasdaq market tiers, which focus on larger companies with highermarket capitalization.

title: Nasdaq Composite Index
definition: The Nasdaq Composite Index tracks over 2,500 stocks listed on the Nasdaq stock exchange, using a market capitalization-weighted methodology. Known for its heavy weighting in the technology sector, this index includes both domestic and international companies, making it a focal point for investors and analysts alike. As a key barometer of the tech-heavy Nasdaq exchange, it provides insights into market trends and economic shifts.

title: Nasdaq Global Select Market Composite
definition: The NASDAQ Global Select Market Composite is a market capitalization-weightedindexconsisting of about 1,400 stocks.It includes U.S. and international stocks. The NASDAQ Composite is composed of three distinct tiers: The NASDAQ Global Select Market, the NASDAQ Global Market, and the NASDAQ Capital Market.

title: Nash Equilibrium
definition: Nash equilibrium is a game theory state where a change in one participant's strategy will have no effect if all others' plans remain unchanged.

title: National Association of Certified Valuation Analysts (NACVA)
definition: The National Association of Certified Valuators and Analysts (NACVA) is a trade organization for valuation professionals.

title: National Association of Federal Credit Unions (NAFCU)
definition: The National Association of Federally-Insured Credit Unions (NAFCU) is an industry trade group founded in 1967 to represent the interests of federal credit unions and promote the success and efficiency of the industry. Its membership is made up of both large and small credit unions.

title: National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC)
definition: The National Association of Insurance Commissioners is an organization created by insurance regulators in the U.S. that establishes and sets standards for the nation's insurance industry.

title: National Association of Insurance and Financial Advisors (NAIFA)
definition: The National Association of Insurance and Financial Advisors (NAIFA) is a trade organization for the insurance industry.

title: National Association of Investors Corp. (NAIC)
definition: The National Association of Investors Corp. (NAIC) is a nonprofit organization founded in 1951, and dedicated to providing investor education and promote investor success. The association is based in Madison Heights, Mich., and is composed ofinvesting clubsalong with individual investors from around the United States. The organization today goes mainly by the name BetterInvesting.

title: National Association of Real Estate Investment Trusts (Nareit)
definition: The National Association of Real Estate Investment Trusts (Nareit) is a trade association that represents the interests ofreal estate investment trusts (REITs)and other public companies that deal with the U.S.real estatemarket.

title: National Association of Realtors (NAR)
definition: The National Association of Realtors (NAR) is a national organization ofreal estatebrokers, known asrealtors, created to promote the real estate profession and foster high standards of professional behavior by its members. The association has its owncode of ethicsto which it requires its members to adhere.

title: National Association of Securities Dealers (NASD)
definition: The National Association of Securities Dealers (NASD) was a self-regulatory organization in the securities industry from 1939 to 2007.

title: National Association of State Boards of Accountancy (NASBA)
definition: The National Association of State Boards of Accountancy (NASBA) is a nonprofit group that serves the 55 state and U.S. territory boards of accountancy tasked with regulating the accountancy profession in the United States. Public accountancy is regulated at the state level by these independent boards of accountancy which work to define and uphold standards. They also administer theUniform CPA Examination.

title: National Average Wage Index (NAWI)
definition: The National Average Wage Index (NAWI) is a measure of U.S. wage trends calculated annually by theSocial Security Administration (SSA). The NAWI is dependent on income subject to federal income taxes and contributions todeferred compensation plans.

title: National Bank
definition: In the United States, a national bank is acommercial bankthat the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) charters and supervises. However, many people also refer to national banks as any financial institution operating nationally compared to local banks operating in one city, state, or region.

title: National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO)
definition: The National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) is a quote that reports the best availablebidandask pricesfor securities across all U.S. exchanges.

title: National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER)
definition: The National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) plays an important role in understanding economic trends and policy impacts. As a private, non-profit, non-partisan organization, NBER conducts comprehensive research on economic behavior and public policy effects. Its findings inform policymakers, business professionals, and academics seeking clearer economic insight. Learn how NBER's research influences economic decisions and projections.

title: National Commodities And Derivatives Exchange (NCDEX)
definition: The National Commodity and Derivatives Exchange (NCDEX) is a commodities exchange dealing primarily in agricultural commodities in India. The NCDEX was established in 2003, and its headquarters are in Mumbai.

title: National Credit Union Administration (NCUA)
definition: The National Credit Union Administration (NCUA) is an agency of the United States federal government. The federal government created theNCUAto monitor federal credit unions across the country.

title: National Currency
definition: A national currency is a legal tender issued by a country's central bank or monetary authority. It is typically the predominant medium of exchange for purchasing goods and services. In the United States, the dollar is the primary form of currency, backed by the full faith and credit of the government and theFederal Reserve.

title: National Futures Association (NFA)
definition: The National Futures Association (NFA) is an independent self-regulatory organization for the U.S.futuresandderivativesmarkets. Designated by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) as a registered futures association, the NFA's mandate is to safeguard the integrity of the derivatives markets, protect investors, and ensure that members fulfill their regulatory obligations.

title: National Housing Act
definition: The National Housing Act was a piece of legislation passed by Congress in 1934 that was intended to strengthen the residential real estate market and promote homeownership. A cornerstone of theNew Deal, the act established theFederal Housing Administration (FHA), which, by creating a federally guaranteed mortgage insurance program, allowed banks to issue lower-cost loans and make them more accessible to more people.

title: National Income Accounting
definition: National income accounting is a government bookkeeping system that records a country's national income, production value, and expenditure value.

title: National Insurance Contributions (NIC)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: National Market System (NMS)
definition: The National Market System (NMS) is designed to regulate how stocks are traded across different exchanges. It ensures that trades are executed efficiently and fairly, and promotes transparency by giving all investors access to accurate trading information. The Securities and Exchange Commission’s (SEC) Regulation NMS modernized trading practices by improving price discovery and market liquidity. Large investors may face challenges navigating the NMS system due to its complexity and high-speed trading environments.

title: National Registration Database (NRD)
definition: The National Registration Database (NRD) is a Canadian database that was launched in 2003 to replace the original paper form system. It allows security dealers and investment advisors to file registration forms electronically.

title: National Retail Federation (NRF)
definition: The National Retail Federation (NRF) is a retail trade association, founded in 1911, that is made up of members from all phases of the retail industry, including department stores, specialty, discount, catalog, Internet and independent retailers, restaurant chains, and grocers, as well as businesses that supply goods and services to retailers. The NRF, formerly called the National Retail Dry Goods Association in 2011, forms an umbrella over many state, national and international retail associations.

title: National Securities Clearing Corporation (NSCC)
definition: The National Securities Clearing Corporation (NSCC) functions as the central entity that enhances the efficiency of the U.S. financial markets by streamlining trade processes and reducing the operational costs of market participants.

title: National Securities Markets Improvement Act (NSMIA)
definition: The National Securities Markets Improvement Act is a law passed in 1996 that sought to simplify securities regulation in the U.S. by apportioning more regulatory power to the federal government.

title: National Stock Exchange of India Limited (NSE)
definition: The National Stock Exchange of India (NSE) is India’s largest financial market. Incorporated in 1992 and launched in 1994, the NSE has developed into a sophisticated electronic market.

title: Nationalization
definition: Nationalization refers to the action of a government taking control of a company or industry, which generally occurs without compensation for the loss of thenet worthof seized assets and potential income. The action may be the result of a nation's attempt toconsolidate power, resentment of foreign ownership of industries representing significant importance to local economies or to prop up failing industries.

title: Natural Gas ETF
definition: A natural gasexchange-traded fund (ETF)is a type of pooled investment product that provides investors with exposure to natural gas prices. These funds are often overseen by a professional manager, who invests on behalf of the investors, and tend to invest in a basket of natural gasfutures contractsrather than hold stocks of natural gas companies.

title: Natural Gas Liquids
definition: Natural gas liquids (NGLs) are components of natural gas that have been separated and condensed into liquid form. They have a variety of applications, from producing plastics to fuels.

title: Natural Gas Storage Indicator (EIA Report)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Natural Hedge
definition: A natural hedge is a strategy designed to reduce financial risk by investing in assets with performances that naturally oppose each other For instance, holding bonds alongside financial stocks can act as a natural hedge since bond and stock prices often move in opposite directions. Whether you're managing a portfolio or operating a global business, understanding natural hedges can enhance your financial strategy and stability.

title: Natural Language Processing (NLP)
definition: Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a field of artificial intelligence (AI) that enables computers to analyze and understand human language, both written and spoken. It was formulated to build software that generates and comprehends natural languages so that a user can have natural conversations with a computer instead of through programming or artificial languages like Java or C.

title: Natural Law
definition: Natural law dictates that certain principles are morally inherent and universal rather than being designed by the norms of society.

title: Natural Monopoly
definition: A natural monopoly occurs when a single company can produce a good or service at a lower cost than its competitors, which results in low competition in the market.

title: Natural Selection
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Natural Unemployment
definition: The natural unemployment rate is the minimumunemploymentrate resulting from real or voluntary economic forces. Natural unemployment reflects workers moving from job to job, the number of unemployed replaced by technology, or those lacking the skills to gain employment.

title: NAV Return
definition: NAV return is a change in the estimated net market value of a fund's assets in a given period.

title: Near Field Communication (NFC)
definition: Near field communication (NFC) is a short-range wireless technology that lets devices communicate when they're very close to each other. NFC is commonly built into smartphones, tablets, and other smart devices. It gained popularity in the payment industry and has expanded into many other areas. For instance, it helps people make contactless payments, share files, and connect devices quickly. NFC can also work in different modes, making it flexible for many everyday tasks.

title: Near Money
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Near Term
definition: The near term is a period of time not far into the future. The term is used to describe events that may occur soon. In finance, the term is often used to explain the timeframe during which an event or change is expected to occur. Traders will often use the term "near term" when expecting a price move to happen in the near future, or when a trade is taken for only a small amount of time.

title: Near the Money
definition: The phrase "near the money" refers to anoptions contractwhose strike price is close to the current market price of the corresponding underlying security. Near-the-money is synonymous with at-the-money. It is very seldom that the underlying asset's price will be exactly at the strike price.

title: Neckline
definition: The neckline is a level ofsupportorresistancefound on ahead and shoulderspattern that is used by traders to determine strategic areas to place orders. A neckline connects theswing lows(which occur following the first two peaks) of the head and shoulders topping pattern. A move below the neckline signals a breakout of the pattern and indicates that a reversal to the downside of the prior uptrend is underway.

title: Needs Approach
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Negative Amortization
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Negative Arbitrage
definition: Negative arbitrage is the opportunity lost when bond issuers assume proceeds from debt offerings and then hold that moneyin escrowfor a period of time (usually in cash or short-term treasury investments) until the money is able to be put to use to fund a project, or to repay investors. Negative arbitrage may occur with a new bond issue or following a debt refinancing.

title: Negative Assurance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Negative Bond Yield
definition: A negative bond yield occurs when bond investors receive less money than they originally invested by the time the bond matures. This means that issuers of debt are paid to borrow money, while buyers of bonds effectively pay the issuer to hold their money. Understanding negative bond yields is crucial for investors looking for safe-haven assets or considering asset allocation amid economic uncertainty.

title: Negative Carry
definition: Negative carry is a condition in which the cost of holding an investment orsecurityexceeds the income earned while holding it. A negative carry trade or investment is often undesirable to professional portfolio managers because it means the investment is losing money as long as theprincipalvalue of the investment remains the same (or falls). However, many investors and professionals regularly enter into such conditions when they anticipate a significant payoff from holding the investment over time.

title: Negative Confirmation
definition: Negative confirmation is a message that requests a response only if there are outstanding issues or concerns that need to be addressed. If there are no issues, no response is sent, and the absence of that response serves as a negative confirmation.

title: Negative Convexity
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Negative Correlation
definition: A negative correlation means that two variables move in opposite directions from one another: when one rises, the other falls.

title: Negative Covenant
definition: A negative covenant is a promise in a loan or bond agreement that limits certain actions a company can take. It aims to protect lenders by reducing the risk that the company will make decisions that could harm its ability to repay debt. Negative covenants often restrict actions like taking on more debt, paying large dividends, selling major assets, or merging with another company. If a company breaks a negative covenant, it can face penalties, higher interest costs, or even default on its loan.

title: Negative Directional Indicator (-DI)
definition: The Negative Directional Indicator (-DI) measures the presence of adowntrendand is part of theAverage Directional Index(ADX). If -DI is sloping upward, it's a sign that the price downtrend is getting stronger.

title: Negative Equity
definition: Negative equity occurs when the value ofreal estateproperty falls below the outstanding balance on themortgageused to purchase that property. You can calculate whether you have negative equity by subtracting your outstandingmortgage loanbalance from the property's current market value.

title: Negative Feedback
definition: Negative feedback is a system where outputs mute or moderate the initial inputs with a dampening effect. An investor using a negative feedback strategy in a contrarian investment would buy stocks when prices decline and sell when prices rise. This the opposite of what most people do. By this definition, negative feedback helps make markets less volatile by pushing systems toward equilibrium.

title: Negative Gap
definition: A negative gap is a situation where a financial institution's interest-sensitiveliabilitiesexceed its interest-sensitiveassets. A negative gap is not necessarily a bad thing, because if interest rates decline, the entity's liabilities are repriced at lower interest rates. In this scenario, income would increase. However, if interest rates increase, liabilities would be repriced at higher interest rates, and income would decrease.

title: Negative Gearing
definition: Negative gearing is a practice common in property investing. It is a form of financialleveragethat describes the purchase of an income-producing asset, such as a rental property, but the asset will not produce enough income to cover its cost. Ideally, the asset will eventually produce enough money to cover its costs.

title: Negative Goodwill (NGW)
definition: Negative goodwill (NGW) refers to buying the assets of a company or the company itself for less than fair market value, in abargain purchase. Negative goodwill involves a business that is in severe financial trouble. Negative goodwill nearly always favors the buyer, who must declare negative goodwill on their income statements.

title: Negative Growth
definition: Negative growth is a contraction in business sales or earnings. It is also used to refer to acontractionin a country's economy, which is reflected in a decrease in itsgross domestic product(GDP) during any quarter of a given year. Negative growth is typically expressed as a negative percentage rate.

title: Negative Income Tax (NIT)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Negative Interest Rate
definition: Negative interest rates, an unconventional monetary policy tool, are used primarily during significant economic downturns. In these situations, central banks may opt to credit borrowers interest, encouraging spending and investment rather than cash hoarding. This method aims to stimulate economic growth and counteract deflationary pressures when traditional interest rate cuts reach their limits.

title: Negative Interest Rate Environment
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Negative Interest Rate Policy (NIRP)
definition: A negative interest rate policy (NIRP) sets nominal target rates below zero to push banks and consumers toward spending rather than holding cash during economic stagnation. Used only in rare circumstances, it became notable after the 2008 financial crisis in parts of Europe and Japan. This article introduces how NIRP works, its theoretical basis, and its real-world applications.

title: Negative Pledge Clause
definition: A negative pledge clause acts as a safeguard in loan agreements, preventing borrowers from pledging assets to other lenders, which could jeopardize the security of existing lenders. This clause is crucial in protecting lender interests, typically appearing in bond indentures or traditional loans, where it ensures priority should the borrower default.

title: Negative Return
definition: A negative return is an investment that has lost value over a given time frame, resulting in a financial loss rather than a gain.

title: Negative Volume Index (NVI)
definition: The negative volume index (NVI) is a technical indication line that integrates volume and price to graphically show how price movements are affected by down volume days.

title: Negotiable
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Negotiable Bill of Lading
definition: Lading is the process of loading cargo onto a ship or vessel, and a negotiable bill of lading is one kind ofbill of lading. The bill of lading is a legal document between the shipper and carrier, detailing the type, quantity, and destination of goods being carried. The negotiable bill of lading is distinguished by the fact that it is a contract of carriage that can be transferred to athird party.

title: Negotiable Certificate Of Deposit (NCD)
definition: A negotiable certificate of deposit (NCD), also known as ajumbo CD, is acertificate of deposit(CD) with a minimum face value of $100,000, though NCDs are typically $1 million or more. They are guaranteed by the bank, often insured by the FDIC up to applicable limits, and can usually be sold in a highly liquid secondary market, but they cannot be cashed in before maturity.

title: Negotiable Instrument
definition: A negotiable instrument is a signed document that promises payment to a specified person or assignee. In other words, it is a formalized type ofIOU: a transferable, signed document that promises to pay the bearer a sum of money at a future date or on demand.

title: Negotiable Order of Withdrawal (NOW)
definition: A Negotiable Order of Withdrawal (NOW) Account is an interest-earningdemand depositaccount. Customers with such accounts are permitted to write drafts against money held on deposit. They are also known as " NOW Accounts."

title: Negotiated Dealing System (NDS)
definition: The Negotiated Dealing System (NDS) is an electronic trading platform managed by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). It enables efficient trading and the exchange of government securities. Launched to modernize the Indian securities market, it replaces outdated phone-based systems, reduces manual paperwork, and improves transparency for domestic and internationalinvestors. From its order-matching framework and membership structure to its evolution, core modules, and broader impact, the NDS stands out as a key development that has reshaped how government securities are traded in India.

title: Negotiation
definition: Negotiation is a strategic discussion intended to resolve an issue in a way that both parties find acceptable. It can occur between buyers and sellers, employers and prospective employees, two or more governments, and other parties.

title: Nelson Peltz
definition: Nelson Peltz is a renownedactivist investorand billionaire who seeks to own a significant stake in publicly-traded companies. With Peter May and Edward Garden, he founded Trian Fund Management. L.P. in 2005. Peltz has sat on the boards of Proctor & Gamble, Ingersoll-Rand, and the Heinz Company. He has influenced corporate strategy and financial performance across various industries.

title: Neoclassical Economics
definition: Neoclassical economics, emerging as a successor to classical economics in the 1900s, focuses on the roles of supply and demand in determining prices and consumer behavior. It emphasizes consumer perception of utility over production costs in valuing goods and services, impacting how economic policies and business strategies are developed today.

title: Neoclassical Growth Theory
definition: Neoclassical growth theory examines the importance of savings, investment, and population growth rates in determining economic development over time.

title: Neoliberalism
definition: Neoliberalism refers to a policy model that emphasizes private enterprise and shifts economic control from government to the private sector. Influenced by leaders likeMargaret Thatcherand Ronald Reagan, it advocates for lesser government intervention, focusing on free-market capitalism and fiscal austerity. Understanding neoliberalism involves looking at its policies, such as privatization and deregulation, which impact economies and societies worldwide.

title: Nepalese Rupee (NPR)
definition: The Nepalese rupee (NPR) is the national currency of Nepal, introduced in 1932 to replace the Nepalese mohar. It is issued and regulated by the Nepal Rastra Bank, which oversees monetary policy and currency stability.

title: Nest Egg
definition: A nest egg is a substantial sum of money or other assets that have been saved or invested for a specific purpose. Such assets are generally earmarked for longer-term objectives, the most common being retirement, buying a home, and education.

title: Net Asset Value (NAV)
definition: Net asset value (NAV) is the difference between a company’s assets and its liabilities calculated at the end of each business day.

title: Net Asset Value Per Share (NAVPS)
definition: Net asset value per share quantifies the value of a mutual fund's assets on a per-share basis, serving as an indicator for valuing and selling shares.

title: Net Cash
definition: Net cash is a figure that is reported on a company’s financial statements. It is calculated by subtracting a company’s total liabilities from its totalcash.

title: Net Change
definition: Net change is the difference between a prior trading period’s closing price and the current trading period’s closing price for a given security. Net change is most commonly referring to a daily time frame for stock prices, so it can be positive or negative for the given day in question. It's used to indicate whether a stock's price has risen or fallen from the previous day.

title: Net Charge-Off (NCO)
definition: A net charge-off (NCO) is the dollar amount representing the difference between grosscharge-offsand any subsequent recoveries of delinquent debt. Net charge-offs refer to the debt owed to a company that is unlikely to be recovered by that company.

title: Net Current Asset Value Per Share (NCAVPS)
definition: Net current asset value per share (NCAVPS) is a measure created byBenjamin Grahamas one means of gauging the attractiveness of a stock. A key metric forvalue investors, NCAVPS is calculated by taking a company's current assets and subtracting total liabilities.

title: Net Debt
definition: A company's net debt refers to its total debt obligations after subtracting its cash holdings andliquid assets. This financial indicator provides a more accurate assessment of a company's obligations since it accounts for available liquid assets that could be immediately used to reduce debt.

title: Net Debt Per Capita
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Net Debt-to-EBITDA Ratio
definition: The net debt-to-EBITDA ratio compares a company's debt to its earnings and can indicate its ability to make its debt payments.

title: Net Domestic Product (NDP)
definition: Net domestic product (NDP) measures a nation's economic output after accounting fordepreciation, calculated by subtracting the value of worn-out or obsolete capital fromgross domestic product (GDP).

title: Net Export
definition: Net exports quantify the financial result of subtracting the total value of a country's imports from its total exports, influencing its trade dynamics and overall economic conditions.

title: Net Exposure
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Net Foreign Assets (NFA)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Net Foreign Factor Income (NFFI)
definition: Net foreign factor income (NFFI) is the difference between a nation’sgross national product(GNP) and itsgross domestic product(GDP).

title: Net Importer
definition: A net importer is a country that buys more from other countries in terms of global trade than it sells to them over a given period of time. Countries produce goods based on the resources available in their region. A country that sells more than they purchase is anet exporter. Whenever a country cannot produce a particular good but still wants it, that country can buy it as animportfrom other countries who produce and sell that good.

title: Net Income (NI)
definition: Net income (NI), also known as net earnings, represents what remains from a company's total revenues after deducting all operating costs, taxes, interest, and other expenses. Serving as a critical metric, net income provides investors with a clearer picture of a company's profitability, making it a key performance indicator on a company's income statement.

title: Net Income After Taxes (NIAT)
definition: Net income after taxes (NIAT) is a company's profit after all expenses, including taxes, are deducted from revenue, and it's commonly reported in quarterly and annual financial statements. It can be shown as a total dollar figure or on a per-share basis, and investors use it to gauge financial health. Unlike pre-tax income, NIAT reflects the impact of taxes on earnings.

title: Net Interest Income
definition: Net interest income is a financial performance measure that reflects the difference between the revenue generated from interest-bearing assets and the expenses of interest-bearing liabilities.

title: Net Interest Margin
definition: Net interest margin (NIM) measures the difference between the income generated by a bank's credit products and the interest paid to its deposit holders, expressed as a percentage. This metric allows potential investors toassess a financial institution's profitabilityby comparing its interest income to its interest expenses.

title: Net Interest Rate Differential (NIRD)
definition: The net interest rate differential (NIRD), in international currency (forex) markets, is the total difference in the interest rates of two distinct national economies.

title: Net Internal Rate of Return
definition: Net Internal Rate of Return (Net IRR) is a financial metric used to evaluate the attractiveness of an investment or project by accounting for fees, costs, and carried interest in its calculation. The primary purpose of Net IRR is to provide investors with a more accurate representation of an investment's potential by considering all associated expenses. It helps investors compare different investment opportunities by offering a true measure of an investment’s profitability.

title: Net Interest Rate Spread
definition: The net interest rate spread is the difference between the interest a bank earns from loans and other assets and the interest it pays on deposits and borrowings. This spread is a key indicator ofprofitability, as banks rely on earning more from lending than they pay to depositors. A healthy spread signals strong financial performance and efficient balance sheet management.

title: Net International Investment Position (NIIP)
definition: The Net International Investment Position (NIIP) reflects a country's overall economic exposure to external capital by accounting for its total asset ownership abroad compared to foreign claims on its domestic assets.

title: Net Investment
definition: Net investment is the total amount of money that a company spends oncapital assets, minus the cost of thedepreciationof those assets. This figure provides a sense of the real expenditure on durable goods such as plants, equipment, and software that are being used in the company's operations.

title: Net Investment Income (NII)
definition: Net investment income (NII), for tax purposes, is the total amount of money received from assets such as stocks, bonds, and mutual funds, minus related expenses.

title: Net Lease
definition: The term net lease refers to a contractual agreement where a lessee pays a portion or all of the taxes, insurance fees, and maintenance costs for a property in addition to rent. Net leases are commonly used incommercial real estate. In the purest form of a net lease, the tenant is expected to pay for all the costs related to a piece of property as if the tenant were the actual owner. A net lease is the opposite of agross lease, where the tenant pays a flat rental fee while the landlord is responsible for the other costs.

title: Net Liquid Assets
definition: Net liquid assets are the liquid assets that remain after a company’s immediate liabilities are deducted from them.

title: Net Loss
definition: A net loss occurs when a company's total expenses exceed its total revenues, resulting in a reduction of business equity.

title: Net National Product (NNP)
definition: Net National Product (NNP) is a key economic indicator that measures the value of finished goods and services produced by a country's citizens domestically and abroad. To arrive at NNP, subtract depreciation fromgross national product (GNP). NNP plays an important role in evaluating economic sustainability and growth.

title: Net-Net
definition: Benjamin Graham's Net-Net investing strategy evaluates a company's stock using net current assets per share (NCAVPS) to uncover undervalued opportunities. This approach emphasizes cash and cash equivalents, deducts adjustments for accounts and inventories, and excludes long-term assets, focusing purely on immediate liquidation values. Understand this method for identifying investment prospects within undervalued stocks.

title: Net Neutrality
definition: Net neutrality is a policy that requiresinternet service providers (ISPs)to provide equal access to the internet, with no changes in speed, regardless of content, user, platform, application, or device. In the U.S., after years of back-and-forth between administrations, the policy was struck down by an appeals court in 2025.

title: Net of Tax
definition: The term net of tax refers to the amount left after adjusting for the effects of taxes. Net of tax can be a consideration in any situation where taxation is involved. Individuals and businesses often analyze before- andafter-taxvalues to make investment and purchasing decisions. Net of tax is also an important part of expense analysis when reviewing annual tax filings and the net income of businesses.

title: Net Operating Income
definition: Calculating net operating income (NOI) helps measure the profitability of an income-producing property.

title: Net Operating Loss (NOL)
definition: A net operating loss is when a business's expenses exceed its income, resulting in a financial loss that can be used to offset future taxable income.

title: Net Operating Profit After Tax (NOPAT)
definition: Net operating profit after tax is a company's profit from its core operations after paying taxes.

title: Net Operating Profit Less Adjusted Taxes (NOPLAT)
definition: Net operating profit less adjusted taxes (NOPLAT) is a financial metric that calculates a firm's operating profits after adjusting for taxes. By using operating income, or income before taking interest payments into account, NOPLAT serves as a better indicator of operating efficiency thannet income.

title: Net Premium
definition: Net premium is an insurance accounting measure that represents the expectedpresent value (PV)of a policy’s benefits minusthe expected PV of futurepremiums. Unlike gross premiums, net premiums exclude expenses tied to maintaining the policy, making them a more focused calculation of pure risk costs.

title: Net Premiums Written
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Net Present Value (NPV)
definition: Net present value (NPV) tells you if the money an investment makes in the future is worth more or less than what it costs today.

title: Net Present Value of Growth Opportunities (NPVGO)
definition: The net present value of growth opportunities (NPVGO) is a calculation of the net present value per share of all future cash flows involved with growth opportunities, such as new projects or potential acquisitions. Thenet present valueof growth opportunities is used to determine the intrinsic value per share of these growth opportunities in order to determine how much of the firm's current per-share value is determined by them.

title: Net Present Value Rule
definition: The net present value rule is the idea that company managers and investors should only invest in projects or engage in transactions that have a positivenet present value(NPV). They should avoid investing in projects that have a negative net present value. It is a logical outgrowth of net present value theory.

title: Net Proceeds
definition: Net proceeds are the amount a seller keeps after subtracting all costs and expenses from the gross proceeds on anasset'ssale. These costs can include commissions, advertising fees, closing costs, and taxes, especially in real estate transactions, where such expenses can significantly reduce the final payout. Calculating net proceeds is important for financial planning and for determiningcapital gains taxes, which are based on the net rather than the gross amount.

title: Net Profit Margin
definition: Net profit margin is a metric that includes all factors of a company’s operation and predicts profits based on current revenue.

title: Net Realizable Value (NRV)
definition: Net realizable value is calculated by subtracting the costs of production and sales from the asset's market value.

title: Net Receivables
definition: Net receivables are the total money owed to a company by its customers minus the money owed that willlikely never be paid.
