Net receivables are often expressed as a percentage, and a higher percentage indicates a business has a greater ability to collect from its customers. For example, if a company estimates that 2% of its sales are never going to be paid, net receivables equal 98% (100% – 2%) of theaccounts receivable (AR).

title: Net Sales
definition: A company’s net sales figure is its gross sales after subtracting returns, allowances, and discounts, but it excludes the cost of goods sold.

title: Net Settlement
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Net Tangible Assets
definition: The term nettangible assetsrefers to the total physical assets of a company minus all intangible assets and liabilities. In other words, net tangible assets focus on physical assets such asproperty, plant, and equipment(PP&E), as well as inventories and cash instruments. Physical assets are anything that is listed on a company's balance sheet while intangible assets are those without a physical form. Acompany's net tangible assetscan help it secure financing and determine how much risk it carries.

title: Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA)
definition: Net unrealized appreciation is the difference between the cost basis and the current market value of shares of employer stock held in an employer-sponsored retirement account.

title: Net Volume
definition: Net volume is atechnical indicatorcalculated by subtracting a security'suptick volumefrom itsdowntick volumeover a specified period of time. Unlike standard volume, the indicator differentiates whether the market sentiment is leaning bullish or bearish. Net volume is typically plotted below the price chart with bars for each period indicating the net volume reading for that period.

title: Net Worth
definition: Net worth is the monetary value of the assets owned by an individual or business entity after subtracting the total value of their liabilities.

title: Netback
definition: Netback is a profitability measure for oil producers, showing how much profit is earned per barrel after deducting costs from revenue. It's calculated as:Price - Royalties - Production - Transportation = Netback.

title: Netting
definition: Netting reduces risks in financial contracts by combining multiple financial obligations to arrive at a net obligation amount.

title: Network Effect
definition: The network effect occurs when a product or service becomes more valuable as more people use it, benefiting businesses and consumers by enhancing utility and market presence.

title: Network Marketing
definition: Network marketing is also called multilevel marketing (MLM), cellular marketing,affiliate marketing, consumer-direct marketing, referral marketing, or home-based business franchising.

title: Networking
definition: Networking is the exchange of information and ideas among people with a common profession or special interest, often in an informal social setting. Professionals use networking to expand their circles of acquaintances, find out about job opportunities in their fields, and increase their awareness of news and developments in their fields.

title: Neural Network
definition: A neural network is a series of algorithms that endeavors to recognize underlying relationships in a set of data through a process that mimics the way the human brain operates. In this sense, neural networks refer to systems of neurons, either organic or artificial in nature.

title: Neutral
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Neutrality Of Money
definition: The neutrality of money, also called neutral money, is an economic theorystating that changes in themoney supplyonly affectnominalvariables and not real variables. In other words, the theory says that the amount of money printed bycentral bankscan impact prices and wages, but not the output or structure of the economy.

title: New Deal
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: New Drug Application (NDA)
definition: An indication of interest is anunderwritingexpression showing a conditional, non-binding interest in buying a security that is currently in registration and awaiting approval by theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

title: New Economy
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: New Fund Offer (NFO)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: New Growth Theory
definition: The new growth theory is an economic concept, positing that humans' desires and unlimited wants foster ever-increasingproductivityand economic growth. It argues thatreal gross domestic product(GDP)per person will perpetually increase because of people's pursuit of profits.

title: New Home Sales
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Understanding Indication of Interest (IOI): How They Work and Examples
definition: An indication of interest is anunderwritingexpression showing a conditional, non-binding interest in buying a security that is currently in registration and awaiting approval by theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

title: Understanding New Issues: IPOs and Securities Offerings Explained
definition: A new issue refers to a stock or bond offering that is made for the first time. Most new issues come from privately held companies that become public, presenting investors with new opportunities.

title: New Keynesian Economics
definition: New Keynesian economics is a modernmacroeconomicschool of thought that evolved from classicalKeynesian economics. This revised theory differs from classical Keynesian thinking in terms of how quickly pricesand wagesadjust.

title: New Paradigm
definition: In investing, a new paradigm is a revolutionary new concept or way of doing things that replaces old beliefs and ways of doing things.

title: New York Board of Trade (NYBOT)
definition: Founded in 1870, the New York Board of Trade (NYBOT) is acommodity futures exchangelocated in New York. In 2006, it became part of theIntercontinental Exchange (ICE).

title: New York Mercantile Exchange (NYMEX)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: New York Stock Exchange (NYSE)
definition: The New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) is the world’s largestequities-basedstock exchange, located on Wall Street in New York City. Founded in 1792, it's been a central part of global finance for more than two centuries. Once known for its bustling floor trading, the NYSE has evolved into a modern digital marketplace while maintaining its iconic trading floor. Now owned by the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), it remains a key hub for major U.S. and international companies, shaping market trends and influencing economies worldwide.

title: Newly Industrialized Country (NIC)
definition: A newly industrialized country (NIC) is a term used by political scientists andeconomiststo describe a country whose level of economic development ranks it somewhere between developing and highly developed classifications. These countries have moved away from an agriculture-based economy and into a more industrialized, urban economy. They show substantial growth in the gross domestic product (GDP), even if they don't quite keep up with developed nations.

title: News Trader
definition: News traders capitalize on the effect of breaking news and economic reports on market sentiment. They aim to profit by reacting swiftly to revised stock and bond prices before and after key announcements. Whether it's a scheduled earnings release or a surprise event, news traders exploit market volatility for swift gains.

title: NEX
definition: NEX is a separate board on theTSX Venture stock exchangethat provides a unique trading forum for listed companies that no longer meet the TSX Venture's ongoing listing standards.

title: Next-In, First-Out (NIFO)
definition: Next In, First Out (NIFO) values inventory according to how much it costs to replace items rather than the original cost. To reflect actual business conditions, companies may use NIFO internally when inflation is a factor and replacement cost is higher than an item's original cost. NIFO does not conform togenerally accepted accounting principles(GAAP).

title: Next of Kin
definition: Next of kin, the closest living relative through blood or legal ties like marriage or adoption, plays a crucial role ininheritancewhen nowillis present. Understanding its implications helps clarify responsibilities related to estate management and medical decisions, ensuring informed decision-making in complex situations.

title: NEXUS
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Nifty 50
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Night Depository
definition: A night depository is a secure drop box at a bank that allows customers, usually small business owners, to deposit cash, checks, and credit slips outside of normal banking hours. It offers convenience and enhanced security by keeping funds safe overnight until they're credited the next business day. While some banks may charge a small fee for this service, it remains valuable even in an increasingly cashless economy for businesses that handle daily cash transactions.

title: Nikkei
definition: The Nikkei 225, Japan's foremost stock index, represents the top 225 blue-chip companies on the Tokyo Stock Exchange. As a price-weighted index, it offers insights akin to the Dow Jones Industrial Average in the United States.

title: NINJA Loan
definition: A NINJA loan is a slang term for a loan given to a borrower without verifying their income, job, or assets. The acronym stands for "no income, no job, and no assets." Unlike traditional loans that require proof of income or collateral, NINJA loans skipped this verification process.

title: Nixon Shock
definition: The Nixon Shock relates to an economic policy shift undertaken by President Richard M. Nixon in 1971 to prioritize jobs growth, lower inflation, and exchange rate stability. The policy changes led to the collapse of the Bretton Woods system of fixed exchange rates established after World War II. It effectively ended the convertibility of U.S. dollars into gold. In addition, the Nixon Shock was the catalyst for thestagflationof the 1970s as the U.S. dollar devalued. Yet, thanks in large part to the Nixon Shock, central banks have more control over their nations' money and the management of variables such as interest rates, overall money supply, andvelocity. Long after the Nixon Shock, economists are still debating the merits of this policy shift and its eventual ramifications.

title: No Documentation Mortgage (No Doc)
definition: A no documentation (no doc) mortgage is a loan to buy property that doesn't require income verification from the borrower. This type of loan, now virtually illegal, is instead approved on a declaration that confirms the borrower can afford the loan payments.

title: No-Load Fund
definition: A no-load fund is a mutual fund in which shares are sold without a commission or sales charge.

title: No-Par Value Stock
definition: No-par value stock is a type of stock issued without a designated face value on its certificate, allowing greater flexibility in pricing. Companies issue no-par value stocks to circumvent fixed pricing, enabling them to adjust stock prices based on market demand. This approach reduces legal liabilities and market risk while offering companies the potential to issue additional shares at higher prices in the future.

title: No-Shop Clause
definition: No-shop clauses are used inmerger and acquisition(M&A) negotiations to keep a seller from seeking other offers, strengthening the buyer's position while a deal is explored. Common in high-profile transactions like Microsoft's acquisition of LinkedIn, these clauses include expiration dates or carve-outs so they don't apply indefinitely.

title: No Transaction Fee Mutual Fund
definition: A no-load fund is a mutual fund in which shares are sold without a commission or sales charge.

title: Noise
definition: In a broad analytical context, noise refers to information or activity that confuses or misrepresents genuine underlying trends. In the financial markets, noise can include small price corrections in the market as well as price fluctuations–calledvolatility–that distorts the overall trend. However, market noise can make it challenging for investors to discern what's driving the trend and whether a trend is changing or merely experiencing short-term volatility.

title: Noise Trader
definition: Noise trader is generally a term used in academic finance studies associated with theEfficient Markets Hypothesis (EMH). The definition is often vaguely stated throughout the literature though it is mainly intended to describe investors who make decisions to buy or sell based on factors they believe to be helpful but in reality will give them no better returns than random choices.

title: Nominal
definition: Nominal is a common financial term with various meanings. In business pricing, a nominal fee is a small charge for less than the cost of the service provided, or one that has a negligible impact on one's finances. In economics, it refers to the dollar value of a price or measurement—in other words, one that is not adjusted for inflation.

title: Nominal Effective Exchange Rate (NEER)
definition: The nominal effective exchange rate (NEER) reflects the amount of domestic currency that's needed to purchase foreign currency.

title: Nominal Gross Domestic Product
definition: Nominal GDP is the total value of goods and services produced in an economy without accounting for inflation.

title: Nominal Interest Rate
definition: A nominal interest rate is one that does not consider inflation.

title: Nominal Rate of Return
definition: The nominal rate of return is the amount of money generated by an investment before factoring in expenses such as taxes, investment fees, andinflation. If an investment generated a 10% return, the nominal rate would equal 10%. After factoring in inflation during the investment period, the actual ("real") return would likely be lower.

title: Nominal Value
definition: The nominal value, also known as face or par value, is vital in determining the redemption price of securities like bonds and stocks, distinct from their market value. This measure is crucial for economic calculations, offering insight into interest payments, discounts, premiums, and yields. Understanding nominal value helps investors evaluate the financial landscape more accurately, especially when considering inflation impacts over time.

title: Nominal Yield
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Nominated Advisor (NOMAD)
definition: A nominated advisor (NOMAD) is a financial services firm that assists with listing a company on theAlternative Investment Market(AIM) of theLondon Stock Exchange(LSE). The Alternative Investment Market is a specialized unit of the LSE catering to smaller, more risky companies.

title: Nomination Committee
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Nominee
definition: A nominee is a person or firm named on securities to help with transactions, while the original owner retains legal ownership. In this way, a nominee can serve as acustodianwhile the investor remains the beneficial owner.

title: Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment
definition: The non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment (NAIRU) is the specific level ofunemploymentthat is evident in an economy that does not causeinflationto increase. In other words, if unemployment is at the NAIRU level, inflation is constant. NAIRU often represents the equilibrium between the state of the economy and the labor market.

title: Non-Accredited Investor
definition: A non-accredited investor is typically a retail investor or someone who doesn't meet the financial criteria of an accredited investor as set by the SEC.

title: Non-Assessable Stock
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Non-Amortizing Loan
definition: A non-amortizing loan is a type of loan for which payments on the principal are made by lump sum. The value of the principal does not decrease at all over the life of the loan as a result.

title: Non-Banking Financial Company (NBFC)
definition: Nonbank financial companies (NBFCs), also known as nonbankfinancial institutions(NBFIs), are financial entities that offer various banking services but do not possess a banking license. Because they cannot accept traditional demand deposits from the public, NBFCs operate outside the direct scope of conventional financial regulators, although they do fallunder oversightsuch as theDodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act. Important examples of NBFCs include investment banks, mortgage lenders, and P2P lenders.

title: Non-Cash Charge
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Non-Cash Item
definition: A non-cash item can mean different things. In banking, it refers to things like checks orbank draftsthat are deposited but not credited until they clear. These items are treated differently in each field. In accounting, a non-cash item is an expense on anincome statementthat doesn’t involve cash, like depreciation or investment gains.

title: Non-Compete Agreement
definition: A non-compete agreement is a legal contract that restricts an employee from competing with their employer after the employment relationship ends. It often includes clauses that prevent the disclosure of company secrets or proprietary information.

title: Non-Competitive Tender
definition: A non-competitive tender allows individual investors to buy U.S. Treasury securities without bidding against others in a formal auction. Instead, they accept the average price set by larger auction participants. This way of purchasing makes it easy for smaller investors to obtain Treasury bonds.

title: Non-Controlling Interest
definition: A non-controlling interest is an ownership position in which a shareholder owns less than 50% of outstanding shares and has no control over decisions. It is also called a minority interest.

title: Non-Covered Security
definition: A noncovered security is a designation given by theU.S. Securities and Exchange Commission(SEC) which means a brokerage is not required to report the cost basis of that security to the IRS. The adjusted cost basis of noncovered securities is only reported to the taxpayer, not to the IRS. Noncovered securities are generally small and limited in scope.

title: Non-Deliverable Forward (NDF)
definition: A non-deliverable forward (NDF) is a financial derivative used for hedging or speculating on currency exchange rates, particularly for currencies that are restricted or not freely tradable. Unlike standardforward contracts, NDFs do not involve the actual exchange of the currency but are settled in cash, making them essential tools for managing foreign exchange exposure in illiquid markets.

title: Non-Deliverable Swap (NDS)
definition: A non-deliverable swap (NDS) is a variation on acurrency swapbetween major and minor currencies that are restricted or not convertible. This means there is no physical delivery of the two currencies involved, unlike a typical currency swap where there is an exchange of currency flows. This type of swap is commonly used to mitigate risk in countries with restricted currencies or currency controls.

title: Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)
definition: A non-disclosure agreement is a legal contract that protects confidential information by preventing those who sign it from disclosing the information to others.

title: Non-Executive Director
definition: A non-executive director provides independent, strategic guidance to a company, but doesn’t have day-to-day operational responsibilities, as a member of its board of directors.

title: Non-Exempt Employee
definition: A few factors make an employee non-exempt, such as being entitled to overtime pay, earning less than $684 a week per federal law, and holding executive, administrative, or professional positions.

title: Non-Farm Payroll
definition: Nonfarm payroll measures the number of workers in the U.S. economy, excluding farm employees, self-employed individuals, volunteers, private household workers, sole proprietors, and active military service members.

title: Non-GAAP Earnings
definition: Non-GAAP earnings are an alternative accounting method used to measure the earnings of a company. Many companies report non-GAAP earnings in addition to their earnings based onGenerally Accepted Accounting Principles(GAAP). Thesepro formafigures, which exclude "one-time" transactions, can sometimes provide a more accurate measure of a company’s financial performance from direct business operations.

title: Non-Interest-Bearing Current Liability (NIBCL)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Non-Interest Income
definition: Non-interest income is bank and creditor income derived primarily from fees including deposit and transaction fees, insufficient funds (NSF) fees, annual fees, monthly account service charges, inactivity fees, check and deposit slip fees, and so on. Credit card issuers also charge penalty fees, including late fees and over-the-limit fees. Institutions charge fees that generate non-interest income as a way of increasing revenue and ensuring liquidity in the event of increased default rates.

title: Non-Issuer Transaction
definition: A non-issuer transaction is a transaction involving a security that is not directly or indirectly executed for the benefit of theissuingcompany. Most deals that occur on thesecondary market, such as stock exchanges, involve non-issuer transactions; secondary offerings; or share buybacks that will involve the issuer.

title: Non-Marginable Securities
definition: Non-marginable securities are not allowed to be purchased on margin at a particular brokerage, or financial institution. They must be fully funded by the investor's cash.

title: Non-Marketable Security
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Non-Member Banks
definition: Non-member banks are financial institutions that do not belong to the U.S. Federal Reserve System. They're typically state-chartered rather than nationally chartered. These banks operate under state regulations and are monitored by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC). Despite not being members, they still access Federal Reserve services like check clearing and discount window borrowing. They must comply with state laws, which can offer different regulatory requirements compared to federal oversight.

title: Non-Negotiable
definition: Non-negotiable means not open for debate or modification. It can refer to the price of a good, asecuritythat is firmly established and cannot be adjusted, or as part of a contract or deal that is considered a requirement by one or both involved parties. Conversely, a negotiable instrument can be transferred, and its price can be negotiated.

title: Non-Objecting Beneficial Owner (NOBO)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Non-Operating Asset
definition: A non-operating asset is an asset not essential to a company's core operations but still capable of generating income orreturns. Unlike operating assets used in daily business activities, these may include investments, unused land, or marketable securities.

title: Non-Operating Expense
definition: A non-operating expense is a business expense unrelated to core operations. The most common types of non-operating expenses are interest charges and losses on the disposition of assets. Accountants sometimes remove non-operating expenses and non-operating revenues to examine the performance of the core business, excluding the effects of financing and other items.

title: Non-Operating Income
definition: Non-operating income arises from activities outside a company’s main business operations, such as investment profits or asset sales, and affects the true measure of corporate earnings. It's important for investors to differentiate this from operatingincometo gain an accurate picture of a company’s financial performance.

title: Non-Owner Occupied
definition: Non-owner occupied is a classification used in mortgage origination, risk-based pricing, and housing statistics for one- to four-unitinvestment properties. The classification means that the owner does not occupy the property. The term is not typically used for multifamily rental properties, such as apartment buildings.

title: Non-Performing Asset (NPA)
definition: A nonperforming asset (NPA) refers to a classification ofloansor advances that arein defaultorin arrears. A loan is in arrears whenprincipalorinterestpayments are late or missed. A loan is in default when the lender considers the loan agreement to be broken and the debtor is unable to meet their obligations.

title: Non-Purpose Loan
definition: A non-purpose loan is an alternative type of loan that often involves using investment securities ascollateraland relies on complex structuring. Regulated non-purpose loans can be offered by brokerages and financial institutions with some specific government-regulatory documentation requirements.﻿﻿

title: Non-Qualified Deferred Compensation (NQDC)
definition: A 409A plan is anon-qualified deferred compensation (NQDC)arrangement that allows high-income earners to postpone receiving part of their pay, deferring income taxes until the funds are distributed.

title: Non-Qualified Plan
definition: A non-qualified plan is a type of tax-deferred, employer-sponsored retirement plan that falls outside ofEmployee Retirement Income Security Act(ERISA) guidelines.

title: Non-Qualified Stock Option (NSO)
definition: A non-qualified stock option (NSO) is a type ofemployee stock optionthat allows an employee to purchase company shares at a set price (also known as the grant price) within a specified period. When exercising an NSO, the employee pays ordinary income tax on the difference between the grant price and the price at which they exercise the option.

title: Non-Qualifying Investment
definition: A non-qualifying investment is an investment that does not qualify for any level of tax-deferred or tax-exempt status.Investmentsof this sort are made with after-tax money. They are purchased and held in tax-deferred accounts, plans, or trusts. Returns from these investments are taxed on anannual

title: Non-Recourse Debt
definition: Non-recourse debt is a loan secured by collateral like property. If a borrower defaults, the lender can seize the collateral but bears a greater risk without pursuing further compensation. Unlike recourse debt, where lenders can seek out borrowers to cover any remaining debt after seizing collateral, non-recourse loans offer more borrower protection. This guide explores the implications, risks, and examples of both types of debt, helping you understand which is more suitable for your financial situation.

title: Non-Recourse Finance
definition: A non-recourse loan is a type of loan where the lender's recovery is limited to the collateral in the event of borrower default, meaning they cannot pursue the borrower's other assets or income.

title: Non-Refundable Tax Credit
definition: A nonrefundable tax credit is a reduction in the amount ofincome taxesthat a taxpayer owes. It can reduce the amount owed to zero, but no further. In other words, the taxpayer forfeits any credit that exceeds the total amount of taxes owed.

title: Non-Renounceable Rights
definition: A non-renounceable rights issue is when a company offers its shareholders the chance to buy additional shares, a right that can't be sold or transferred to someone else. Companies use this type of offer to raise money for things like paying debt, funding projects, or expanding the business. Current shareholders can buy more shares, often at a discounted price, so they can maintain their ownership in the company.

title: Non-Sampling Error
definition: A non-sampling error is a statistical term that refers to an error that results during data collection, causing the data to differ from the true values. A non-sampling error differs from asampling error.A sampling error is limited to any differences between sample values and universe values that arise because the sample size was limited. (The entire universe cannot be sampled in a survey or a census.)

title: Non-Security
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Non-Sufficient Funds
definition: Non-sufficient funds (NSF) occur when your bank account lacks the cash to meet a transaction, leading to declined payments and fees. This article explains NSF, contrasts it with overdraft occurrences, and offers tips to prevent these costly fees through budgeting, account monitoring, and linking accounts for overdraft protection.

title: Non-Taxable Distribution
definition: A non-taxable distribution is a payment to shareholders. It is similar to a dividend, but it represents a share of a company's capital rather than its earnings. Contrary to what the name might imply, it's not really non-taxable. It's just not taxed until the investor sells the stock of the company that issued the distribution. Non-taxable distributions reduce the basis of the stock.

title: Non-Traded REIT
definition: Non-traded real estate investment trusts (REITs) are investment vehicles that offer retail investors the opportunity to invest in real estate assets without the need for public exchange listings. They provide tax advantages and access to diverse property investments that are typically out of reach for individual investors. Despite their lack of liquidity and higher fees, non-traded REITs attract investors interested in the promise of regular income distribution and potential tax benefits.

title: Nonaccrual Experience Method (NAE)

title: Nonaccrual Loan
definition: A nonaccrual loan is an accounting term for a loan, oftenunsecured, that stops generating interest income for the lender because the borrower has failed to make payments for 90 days or more. For the lender, the loan shifts tononperformingstatus, meaning interest can no longer be recorded as income and is instead recognized only when cash payments are received.

title: What Are Noncallable Securities? Definition and Risks
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Why Choose a Noncancellable Insurance Policy?
definition: A noncancellable insurance policy is a type of disability insurance that can't be canceled by the insurer. Similarly, its benefits can't be reduced, and its premiums can't be increased as long as you pay on time. Although they offer financial predictability, these policies often cost more than other options. Noncancellable policies lock in coverage and price, unlike guaranteed renewable policies, which can raise premiums for groups of customers. This stability makes them valued by people who want long-term income protection with fewer surprises.

title: Nonce
definition: Nonce, short for "number used only once," is crucial in Bitcoin mining difficulty. It enables miners to generate a hash from a block header that must meet the network's difficulty target, facilitating the blockchain's integrity and security.

title: Nonconforming Mortgage
definition: A non-conforming mortgage is amortgagethat does not meet the guidelines ofgovernment-sponsored enterprises(GSE) such as Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac and, therefore, cannot be sold to them. GSE guidelines include a maximum loan amount, suitable properties, down payment requirements, and credit requirements, among other factors.

title: Noncumulative
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Noncurrent Assets
definition: Noncurrent assets, listed on a company balance sheet, are investments purchased to be held and used for years, like plants and equipment.

title: Noncurrent Liability
definition: Noncurrent liabilities, also calledlong-term liabilitiesorlong-term debts, are long-term financial obligations listed on a company’s balance sheet. These liabilities have obligations that become due beyond 12 months in the future, as opposed to current liabilities, which are short-term debts with maturity dates within the following 12-month period.

title: Understanding Nonelective Contributions: Benefits and Drawbacks
definition: Nonelective contributions are employer-funded additions to employee retirement plans, independent of employee contributions. This proactive approach enhances employee savings and meets compliance standards while potentially lowering administrative burden. Discover how nonelective contributions work, their benefits, and considerations for both employees and employers.

title: Nonfeasance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Nonfinancial Asset
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Nonforfeiture Clause
definition: A nonforfeiture clause is a feature in insurance policies that protects policyholders by allowing them to keep some benefits or recover part of their premiums if the policy lapses. It’s especially common in life insurance, such as whole life and long-term care policies. Policyholders usually have four payout options: taking the cash surrender value, converting to paid-up insurance, choosing extended-term insurance, or borrowing against the policy through a loan. They give policyholders flexibility, allowing them to choose what works for their financial situation.

title: Noninterest Expense
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Nonlinear Regression
definition: Nonlinear regression is regression analysis that's used to model data that forms a curved relationship rather than a straight line. That is, simple linearregressionlinks two variables, X and Y, with a straight line (y = mx + b), while nonlinear regression connects them in a curved line.

title: Nonmonetary Assets
definition: Nonmonetary assets appear on thebalance sheetbut are not easy to convert into cash. They are items a company holds without a precise dollar value. The value of theseassetscan change a lot over time. A company may need to change its nonmonetary assets as the assets wear out or become obsolete. Examples include factory equipment and vehicles.

title: Nonparametric Statistics
definition: Nonparametric statistics encompass data analysis techniques that do not rely on specific probability models, making them versatile for various types of data.

title: Nonpassive Income and Losses
definition: Nonpassive income and losses constitute any income or losses that cannot be classified as passive. Nonpassive income includes anyactive incomesuch as wages,business income, or investment income. Nonpassive losses include losses incurred in theactive managementof a business. Nonpassive income and losses are usually declarable and deductible in the year incurred.

title: Nonperforming Asset
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Nonperforming Loan (NPL)
definition: A nonperforming loan (NPL) is a loan classified as default because the borrower has missed scheduled payments for a defined period, typically 90 to 180 days. The lack of payments includes both principal and interest. NPLs can significantly affect borrowers' credit ratings and complicate future borrowing attempts. They often arise during economic downturns when many borrowers face financial difficulties. Understanding NPLs involves grasping their types, causes, and consequences, as well as their potential transformation back into performing loans through strategic resolutions.

title: Nonprofit Organization (NPO): Definition, Funding, vs. Not-for-Profit
definition: A nonprofit organization is an entity that is created and operated for charitable or socially beneficial purposes rather than to make a profit. A nonprofit might serve religious, scientific, charitable, educational, literary, health, or animal welfare purposes.

title: Nonrenewable Resource
definition: Nonrenewable resources are vital yet finite elements extracted from Earth, essential for energy production and various industrial applications. Unlike renewable resources, which replenish naturally and sustainably, nonrenewables like fossil fuels—oil, natural gas, and coal—are consumed at a rate faster than they can be replaced, leading to environmental concerns and economic implications. This article delves into the definition of nonrenewable resources, their characteristics, and examples, illustrating their significant role in global energy consumption and the challenges they pose for sustainability and climate change.

title: Nonresident Alien
definition: A nonresident alien is a noncitizen who "has not passed the green card test or the substantial presence test," according to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS).Nonresident aliens must pay taxes on income earned in the U.S. Typical examples of nonresident aliens are teachers, people seeking medical treatment, and students.

title: Nontariff Barrier
definition: A nontariff barrier is a trade restriction that doesn't involvetariffs. Instead of applying taxes on imports, countries use various methods—such as quotas, embargoes,sanctions, andlevies—to limit or regulate trade. These barriers are often used for political, economic, or strategic reasons and can be used alongside or in place of traditional tariffs.

title: Nordic vs. U.S. Economic Models: A Comprehensive Comparison
definition: The Nordic model, also called the Scandinavian model, is an economic system in Nordic nations that combines elements of capitalism and socialism. Key features of the Nordic model are public social services, investment in human capital, and a robust safety net. Society-wide risk sharing is a central aspect of the Nordic model.

title: Normal-Course Issuer Bid (NCIB)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Normal Distribution
definition: Normal distribution, often referred to as the Gaussian distribution, is fundamental to statistics and finance due to its symmetry around the mean, forming the characteristic "bell curve." This distribution is critical in various financial analyses and decisions where most data points tend to cluster around an average value. The normal distribution is not only the foundation of many statistical theories, such as the Central Limit Theorem, but it is also key in assessing financial market behaviors, helping investors and analysts determine if financial instruments are priced fairly or deviate from expected norms. This article explores the properties, uses, and limitations of normal distributions to aid in informed financial decision-making.

title: Normal Good
definition: Normal goods are products whose demand and price increases and decreases are directly tied to rises and falls in consumer income.

title: Normal Profit
definition: Normal profit is a profit metric that takes into consideration both explicit and implicit costs. It may be viewed in conjunction with economic profit. Normal profit occurs when the difference between a company’s total revenue and combinedexplicitandimplicitcosts are equal to zero.

title: Normal Retirement Age (NRA)
definition: The retirement age at which you can receive full retirement benefits from Social Security is called the full retirement age (FRA). It's also known as normal retirement age.

title: Normal Yield Curve
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Understanding Normalized Earnings: Definition, Purpose, and Key Examples
definition: Normalized earnings or normalized income refers to a company’s income that has been adjusted to remove revenue, expenses, or effects of seasonality—in other words, not recurring events, or one-time influences. Normalized earnings help business owners, financial analysts, and other stakeholders understand a company’s true earnings from its normal operations. They're crucial for investors seeking a fair assessment of company health and comparisons.

title: Normative Economics
definition: Normative economics is a perspective oneconomicsthat reflects normative, or ideologically prescriptive judgments toward economic development, investment projects, statements, and scenarios.

title: North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA)
definition: The North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) was implemented to promote trade among the United States, Canada, and Mexico. The agreement, which eliminated most tariffs on trade among the three countries, went into effect on Jan. 1, 1994. Numerous tariffs, particularly those related to agricultural products, textiles, and automobiles, were gradually phased out through Jan. 1, 2008.

title: North American Industry Classification System (NAICS)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: North American Securities Administrators Association (NASAA)
definition: The North American Securities Administrators Association (NASAA) is an organization of securities regulators whose aim is to protect investors from fraud. Founded in 1919 in the U.S. state of Kansas, its membership of 67 securities administrators from across North America works to protect customers ofinvestment adviceor securities as part of a complementary regulatory system that works at the federal, state/provincial, and industry levels.

title: North Sea Brent Crude
definition: North Sea Brent Crude is a blended light sweet crude oil recovered from the North Sea in the early 1960s. Brent crude oil has relatively low sulfur content and a relatively high gravity on theAmerican Petroleum Institute’sstandard scale.

title: Norwegian Krone (NOK)
definition: The Norwegiankrone(NOK) is the official currency of Norway.Its regulation and circulation are controlled by the country's central bank, the Norges Bank.The bank has issued its eighth series ofbanknotes, including the latest in November 2019, which saw new 1000-kronebills. The year before introduced the new 50-kroneand 500-kronebills and 2017 brought the new 100-kroneand 200-kronebills.

title: Understanding Nostro Accounts: Banking Abroad in Foreign Currencies
definition: Nostro accounts are essential tools in international banking, allowing domestic banks to hold accounts in foreign currencies in other countries. These accounts ease foreign exchange and trade transactions by providing a more straightforward conversion and settlement process. The counterpart to nostro accounts are vostro accounts, representing foreign-held accounts in the domestic bank's currency.

title: Not for Profit
definition: Not-for-profit organizations do not earn profits for their owners. All of the money earned by or donated to a not-for-profit organization is used in pursuing the organization’s objectives and keeping it running. Income is not distributed to the group’s members, directors, or officers. Examples include homeowners associations, fraternities, and sororities.

title: Not-Held Order
definition: A not-held order gives a broker the time and price discretion to seek the best price available. The broker is not held responsible for any potential losses or missed opportunities that result from their best efforts. Aheld order, in contrast, requires immediate execution.

title: What Does a Notary Do? Duties and Where to Notarize a Document
definition: A notary is an impartial witness to the signing or authentication of a legal document.

title: Notching
definition: Notching occurs when a credit rating agency bumps up or down thecredit ratingon an issuer's specific debts or obligations. This is different than a credit agency upgrading or downgrading the company or issuer as a whole. Because certain types of debt—for instance, subordinated debts—are inherently riskier than senior debts, the rating onjunior debtcan be notched lower.

title: Note
definition: A note is a written promise from a borrower to repay a lender. Likebonds, investors get interest payments and their original investment back, called theprincipal, at a later date. Notes require the issuer to pay back the principal and interest by a certain date. They are used for things like family loans,safe-haveninvestments, or complex corporate debts.

title: Notice of Assessment (NOA)
definition: A notice of assessment (NOA) is an annual statement sent by theCanada Revenue Agency(CRA) to taxpayers detailing the amount of income tax they owe. It includes details such as the amount of their tax refund, tax credit, and income tax already paid. It also lists deductions from total income, total nonrefundable federal tax credits, total British Columbia nonrefundable federal tax credits, and other figures.﻿﻿

title: Notice of Default
definition: The term notice of default refers to a public notice filed with a court that states that the borrower of amortgageis in default on a loan. Thelendermay file a notice of default when a mortgagor falls behind on their mortgage payments. Information on notices of default normally includes the borrower and lender's name and address, the legal address of the property, the nature of the default, as well as other pertinent details. A notice of default is often considered the first step toward foreclosure.

title: Notice of Deficiency
definition: A Notice of Deficiency is an official communication from the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) that indicates a taxpayer has under-reported their tax obligations, resulting in a proposed change to their tax return. This notice is typically sent when the IRS discovers discrepancies between the taxpayer’s filed return and information from other sources, such as employers or financial institutions. It informs the taxpayer of additional taxes owed and provides options to dispute or settle the claim.

title: Notice of Termination
definition: A notice of termination is what an employer uses to notify an employee as to the end of theiremployment contract. More broadly, it may also refer to the formal notification of the end of a contract between two or more parties. While a notice of termination usually is provided to an employee for reasons unrelated to their job performance—for example, because business conditions necessitate layoffs or downsizing—it may also be given to an employee for poor job performance or misconduct.

title: Notice to Creditors
definition: A notice to creditors can be a specific contact with a known creditor or a public notice commonly posted in a local newspaper by a trust or estate'sexecutoras part of theprobateof the estate of a decedent.

title: Notional Principal Amount
definition: The term notional principal amount refers to the predetermined dollar amount orprincipalon which exchanged interest payments are based. Notional principal amounts are commonly used between two parties ininterest rate swaps.The notional principal amount is a theoretical value. This means while it is predetermined by parties in the swap, that dollar value isn't exchanged. Rather, only the interest rate ever changes hands.

title: Notional Value
definition: Notional value is the total amount of money that a position taken in a financial contract controls.

title: Novation
definition: Novation is the replacement of one of the parties in an agreement between two parties, with the consent of all three parties involved. To novate is to replace an old obligation with a new one.

title: Null Hypothesis
definition: A null hypothesis is a statistical assumption suggesting that any observed patterns or differences in a dataset are due to chance and not the result of a meaningful effect.

title: Numeraire
definition: Numeraire is an economic concept that acts as a benchmark for comparing the value of goods and financial instruments, much like a baseline in an index or market. Used primarily to standardize value comparisons, the U.S. dollar often serves as the numeraire in global commodity markets, ensuring consistent pricing worldwide.

title: Unlocking Numismatics: Coin Study, Qualifications, and Value
definition: Numismatics is the study of money and currencies, with a focus on physical properties, production, and historical context of coins and other payment media. Unlike casual coin collecting, numismatics involves detailed research into a coin's rarity, origins, and economic role, which can reveal significant collectible and investment value.

title: NYSE Arca
definition: NYSE Arca is an electronic securities exchange in the U.S. that specializes in listing exchange-traded products (ETPs) and equities, and is known for having the largest volume of exchange-traded fund (ETF) trading globally.

title: NYSE Composite Index

title: Obamanomics
definition: The term "Obamanomics" refers to the economic policies of former President Barack Obama. Obamanomics is often associated with the stimulus programs used to combat theGreat Recession of 2008. Critics of Obamanomics view Obamanomics as an undue expansion of the government’s economic role, including increased government spending, taxation, and regulation.

title: Obligation
definition: A financial obligation is a responsibility to meet agreed payment or performance terms, ranging from formal contracts like mortgages and loans to informal promises. Obligations shape personal and corporate budgeting and financial health, often measured through tools like the financial obligation ratio. When they aren't met, legal recourse may be available to the affected party.

title: Obligatory Reinsurance
definition: Obligatory reinsurance is a treatythat requires an insurer to automatically send all policies on its books that fall within a set list of criteria to a reinsurer. Under the terms of an obligatory reinsurance agreement, also called an automatic treaty, thereinsureris obliged to accept these policies.

title: Obligor
definition: An obligor, also known as a debtor, is a person or entity who is legally or contractually obliged to provide a benefit or payment to another party. In a financial context, an obligor is often a bond issuer responsible for making all principal and interest payments on outstanding debt. Obligors also appear in personal contexts, such as in child support cases, where they are legally bound to meet payment obligations. Failing to meet these obligations can lead to defaults or legal enforcement.

title: Obsolete Inventory
definition: Obsolete inventory is a term that refers toinventorythat is at the end of itsproduct life cycle. This inventory has not been sold or used for a long period of time and is not expected to be sold in the future. This type of inventory has to be written-down or written-off and can cause large losses for a company.

title: Occupancy Rate
definition: Occupancy rate is a measurement that indicates the proportion of leased or used space within a building compared to the total available space. It is crucial in real estate and other industries, providing insights into economic health and urban development trends. Whether you're in real estate, hospitality, or healthcare, understanding occupancy rates helps gauge the performance and appeal of properties or facilities. High occupancy rates often signal financial stability and desirability, while low rates may highlight areas for improvement.

title: Occupational Labor Mobility
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Occupational Safety And Health Act
definition: The Occupational Safety and Health Act is a law passed by the U.S. Congress in 1970 to ensure safe workplace conditions around the country. It established the federal Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), which sets and enforces workplace health and safety standards.

title: Occurrence Policy
definition: An occurrence policy coversclaimsmade for injuries sustained during the life of aninsurancepolicy. Under these types of contracts, the insured party has the right to request compensation for damages that occurred within the timespan that the policy was active, even if several years have since passed and the insurance agreement is no longer in force.

title: October Effect
definition: The October effect is the belief that stocks tend to decline during the month of October. It is considered to be more of a psychological expectation than an actual phenomenon because most statistics contradict the theory. But investors may be nervous during October because some large, historical market crashes have occurred during this month. The September effect also predicts weaker markets during October.

title: Odd Lot
definition: An odd lot is a securities order with fewer shares than the standard trading unit, usually under 100 in stock transactions. Odd lots can result from reverse splits or dividend reinvestment plans. Unlike round lots, odd lots suffer from higher trading commissions, making transactions costlier and sometimes slower. This article explores odd lots, their formation, and their impact on trading activity.

title: Odd Lot Theory
definition: The odd lot theory is a technical analysis tool used to study the behavior of small investors. It suggests that their investing decisions often signal the opposite trend for larger market players. An odd-lot purchase is when an investor buys fewer than 100 shares of a stock, while an odd-lot sale is when fewer than 100 shares are sold. This theory states that rising odd-lot buying may signal a market decline ahead, while rising odd-lot selling may indicate that prices could soon rise.

title: Odious Debt
definition: Odious debt, also known as illegitimate debt, is when a country's government changes and the successor government does not want to pay debts incurred by the previous government. Usually, successor governments argue that the previous government misappropriated money it had borrowed and that they should not be held responsible for the previous regime’s alleged misdeeds.

title: OEX
definition: OEX, which trades on theChicago Board Options Exchange(CBOE), is the ticker symbol used to identify Standard & Poor's 100 index options.

title: Off-Balance Sheet (OBS)
definition: Off-balance sheet (OBS) items are crucial financial elements that remain unlisted on a company's main balance sheet, yet they still represent significant assets and liabilities for the business. These items, often detailed in the accompanying notes of financial statements, become a focal point for investors evaluating a company's financial stability. While some OBS items, such as operating leases or collateralized debt obligations, serve legitimate financial strategies, they can also obscure liabilities, highlighting the need for careful scrutiny to uncover any potential hidden financial risks.

title: Off-Chain Transactions (Cryptocurrency)
definition: Off-chain transactions are cryptocurrency transfers processed outside the primary blockchain, often through second-layer networks, rather than recorded directly on-chain.

title: Offer
definition: An offer is a conditional proposal made by a buyer or seller to buy or sell an asset, which becomes legally binding if accepted. An offer is also defined as the act of offering something for sale, or the submission of abidto buy something.

title: Offer in Compromise
definition: Offer in compromise is a program instituted by theInternal Revenue Service(IRS) for taxpayers who cannot pay the taxes they owe, or for taxpayers for whom it would create a financial hardship to pay the taxes they owe. An offer in compromise allows taxpayers to settle their tax bill for less than the full amount owed.

title: Offering Memorandum
definition: An offering memorandum, orprivate placementmemorandum (PPM), outlines the objectives, risks, and terms of a private investment. It serves investors by detailingfinancial statements, business operations, and management credentials to inform their due diligence. While similar to a prospectus, an offering memorandum exclusively targets private investors.

title: Offering Price
definition: An offering price, generally, is the price at which something is offered for sale. In finance and investments, the offering price most often refers to the per-share value at which publicly-issued securities are made available for purchase by the investment bank during aninitial public offering(IPO).

title: Office of Foreign Asset Control (OFAC)
definition: The Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) is a department of theU.S. Treasurythat is charged with enforcing economic andtrade sanctionsimposed by the U.S. against countries and groups of individuals.

title: Office of Thrift Supervision (OTS)
definition: The Office of Thrift Supervision was a bureau of the U.S. Treasury Department that was responsible for issuing and enforcing regulations governing the nation's savings and loan industry. In 2011, the OTS was merged with other agencies, including the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC), the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, and the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB).

title: Official Settlement Account
definition: An official settlement account (OSA) is a special type of account used in internationalbalance of payments (BoP)accounting to keep track of central banks' reserve asset transactions with one other. The official settlement account keeps track of transactions involving gold, foreign exchange reserves, bank deposits andspecial drawing rights (SDRs).

title: Official Strike
definition: An official strike is a work stoppage by union members that is endorsed by the union and that follows the legal requirements for striking.

title: Offset
definition: Offsets are strategies used in finance to manage risk by taking an opposite position to a previously held one, effectively neutralizing any potential gains or losses. For instance, holding 100 shares of XYZ stock and selling an equal amount can offset the initial position. Offsetting can also apply infuturesandderivativesmarkets, where traders eliminatedeliveryobligations by entering reverse transactions.

title: Offset Mortgage

title: Offsetting Transaction
definition: An offsetting transaction cancels out the effects of another transaction. Offsetting transactions can occur in any market, but typically offsetting transactions refer to the options,futures, and exotic instrument markets. An offsetting transaction can meanclosinga transaction or taking another position in the opposite direction to cancel the effects of the first.

title: Offshore
definition: Offshore finance involves conducting business activities in a foreign jurisdiction, offering benefits liketax avoidanceand asset protection. While often associated with island nations, offshore locations also include places with favorable tax laws and fewer regulations. Understanding offshore finance is crucial for those seeking asset protection and portfolio diversification, but it also involves risks like regulatory scrutiny.

title: Offshore Banking Unit (OBU)
definition: An offshore banking unit (OBU) is a bank shell branch, located in another international financial center. For instance, an offshore banking unit could be a London-based bank with a branch located in Delhi. Offshore banking units make loans in theEurocurrencymarket when they acceptdepositsfrom foreign banks and other OBUs. Eurocurrency simply refers to money held in banks located outside of the country which issues the currency.

title: Offshore Mutual Fund
definition: An offshore mutual fund is an investment vehicle-based in anoffshorelocation outside the jurisdiction of the United States, often used as a tax haven.

title: Offtake Agreement
definition: Offtake agreements are essential tools in project financing. They establish legally binding contracts between producers and buyers to sell future products, which helps secure necessary funding for construction or expansion projects. These agreements not only guarantee a market and steady revenue stream for producers but also provide price and supply stability for buyers, especially in volatile markets such as energy and natural resources.

title: OHLC Chart
definition: An OHLC chart is a type ofbar chartthat shows open, high, low, and closing prices for each period. OHLC charts are useful since they show the four major data points over a period, with theclosingprice being considered the most important by many traders.

title: Oil ETF
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Oil Field
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Oil Initially In Place (OIIP)
definition: Oil Initially in Place (OIIP) is the amount of crude oil first estimated to be in a reservoir. Oil Initially in Place differs from oil reserves, as OIIP refers to the total amount of oil that is potentially in a reservoir and not the amount of oil that can be recovered. Calculating OIIP requires engineers to determine how porous the rock surrounding the oil is, how high water saturation might be and the net rock volume of the reservoir. The numbers for the aforementioned factors are established by conducting a series of test drills around the reservoir.

title: Oil Pollution Act of 1990
definition: The U.S. Congress enacted the Oil Pollution Act of 1990 (OPA) to streamline and strengthen the Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) power to prevent oil spills. It was passed as an amendment to the Clean Water Act of 1972 following the Exxon Valdez oil spill of 1989. The Oil Pollution Act of 1990 is one of the most wide-reaching and critical pieces of environmental legislation ever passed.

title: Oil Price to Natural Gas Ratio
definition: As its name suggests, the oil price to natural gas ratio is aratioin which the price of oil is the numerator and the price of natural gas is the denominator.

title: Oil Refinery
definition: Oil refineries convertcrude oilinto essential petroleum products, including gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, and kerosene. Positioned in thedownstreamsegment of the oil and gas sector, refineries help maintain the energy supply and influence fuel prices. Their refining capacity directly affects economic stability and market costs, as product output depends on the type of crude oil and current demand. Because profit margins can fluctuate with changing oil prices, companies often use financial strategies like crack spreads to hedge risks and optimize refinery profitability.

title: Oil Reserves
definition: The term oil reserves refers to an estimate of the amount ofcrude oillocated in a particular economic region with the potential of being extracted. Estimates of oil reserves are based on proven reserves through the analysis of available data. Reserves are calculated based on a provenprobablebasis and oil pools situated in unattainable depths are not considered part of a nation's reserves.

title: Oil Sands
definition: Oil sands, or tar sands, are sand and rock material that contain crudebitumen—a dense, viscous form ofcrude oil. Bitumen is too thick to flow on its own, so extraction methods are necessary. Bitumen is extracted and processed using two methods: mining and in-situ recovery.

title: Okun's Law
definition: Okun's law is an empirically observed negative correlation between GDP growth and unemployment.

title: Old-Age, Survivors, and Disability Insurance (OASDI) Program
definition: The Old Age, Survivors, and Disability Insurance (OASDI) program is a federal system that offers income support to retirees, people with disabilities, and their families, funded through payroll taxes. It is commonly known as Social Security.

title: Old Economy
definition: Old economy is a term used to describe theblue-chipsector that enjoyed substantial growth during the early parts of the last century as industrialization expanded around the world. These sectors do not rely heavily on technology or technological advancement, but use processes that have been around for hundreds of years. Even with the rise of thenew economy, old economy companies still experience growth, albeit at a declining rate.

title: Old Lady
definition: The "Old Lady" is an eighteenth-century nickname for the Bank of England. It is a short version of the Old Lady of Threadneedle Street, a reference to the bank’s address in the middle of London.

title: Oligopoly
definition: An oligopoly is a type ofmarketstructure in which a small number of firms control most of the market. In an oligopoly, producers can indirectly or directly restrict output or influence prices to achieve higher returns. A key characteristic is that no one firm can keep the others from having significant influence over the market. Oligopolies face challenges such as the risk of collusion or the strategic tension of the prisoner's dilemma. Historical and modern examples include OPEC and the U.S. airline industry.

title: Oligopsony
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Ombudsman: Roles, Types, Advantages, and Disadvantages Explained
definition: An ombudsman is an independent, impartial official who investigates complaints against organizations and public bodies to promote fairness and accountability.

title: Omega
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Omnibus Account
definition: An omnibus account is a pooled account that combines the assets and trades of multiple customers under the name of a single broker. Transactions are recorded in the broker’s name, keeping investor details private.

title: Oman Rial (OMR)
definition: The currency of Oman. The Omani rial is broken into smaller units, called baisa, and is found in both coin and banknote form. It is managed by the Central Bank of Oman.

title: On Account
definition: "On account" is an accounting term that denotespartial paymentof an amount owed. On account is also used to denote the purchase/sale of goods or services on credit. On account can also be referred to as “on credit.”

title: On-Balance Volume (OBV)
definition: On-balance volume (OBV), developed by Joseph Granville in 1963, is a pivotal technical trading indicator that leveragesvolumeflow to anticipate stock price movements. Granville proposed that market volume is the driving force behind significant price shifts. His OBV metric aims to forecast when major market moves will occur based on changes in volume. According to Granville, when volume rises sharply without a corresponding price change, an eventual significant price movement is likely—either upward or downward—due to this 'spring being wound tightly' effect.

title: On-Chain Governance
definition: On-chain governance is a system for managing and implementing changes to cryptocurrency blockchains. In this type of governance, voting systems for instituting changes are programmed into the blockchain. Developers propose changes, and each stakeholder votes on whether to accept or reject the proposed change. Blockchain stakeholders might be developers, validators, token holders, or anyone else the blockchain is designed to include.

title: On-the-Run Treasury
definition: On-the-run Treasuries are the most recently issued U.S.Treasury bondsor notes, making them the most actively traded due to their high liquidity. These securities typically command a premium, trading at slightly higher prices and lower yields than their off-the-run counterparts. Investors might leverage the liquidity premium of on-the-run Treasuries through various trading strategies, enabling more informed investment decisions.

title: On-the-Run Treasury Yield Curve
definition: The on-the-run Treasury yield curve graphically shows thecurrent yieldsversusmaturitiesof the most recently soldU.S. Treasury securitiesand is the primary benchmark used in pricing fixed-income securities.

title: On-Us Item
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: One Bank Holding Company
definition: A bank holding company is a corporation that owns a controlling interest in one or more banks but does not itself offer banking services.

title: One Belt One Road (OBOR)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: One-Cancels-the-Other Order (OCO)
definition: A one-cancels-the-other (OCO) order is a pair of conditional orders stipulating that if one order executes, then the other order is automatically canceled. This mitigates risk and is often used in trading volatile stocks or managing market entry strategies. Although an OCO order is automatic, manual stop-loss placement is needed post-execution.

title: One-Child Policy
definition: Introduced by the Chinese government in 1979, discontinued in 2015, and formally ended in 2016, the one-child policy was a method of controlling the population. It was intended to address social, economic, and environmental issues that challenged the country. The policy required that many couples in the country have just one child, but it didn't apply to everyone. There were exceptions to the policy, such as for ethnic minorities and certain rural populations.

title: One Percent Rule
definition: The 1% rule is a critical benchmark for real estate investors. It ensures that the rental income from aninvestment propertyat least covers its monthlymortgage payment, aiming to break even or turn a profit. Learn how to calculate this rule and effectively apply it to your real estate ventures.

title: One-Stop Shop
definition: A one-stop shop is a business that provides multiple products or services to customers in one place. It can be a single location for business or a company offering various goods or services. One-stop shops are convenient because customers can meet all their needs without visiting multiple businesses.Big-box retailers, department stores, ande-commercesites are often considered one-stop shops.

title: One-Tailed Test
definition: One-tailed tests in statistics focus on one direction in the distribution to determine if a sample mean is significantly greater or less than a population mean. This approach is especially useful in finance when testing hypotheses, such as an investment outperforming the market. If the data falls in the critical region, statisticians reject the null hypothesis in favor of the alternative.

title: One-Third Rule
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: One-Time Charge
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: One-Time Item
definition: A one-time item is a nonrecurring gain, loss, or expense on anincome statement, and analysts often exclude these items to get a clearer view of a company's ongoing performance. They can decrease reportedearnings, such as with restructuring charges, or boost results, as with certain asset sales. Understanding these items helps investors assess a company's true operating strength.

title: One-Touch Option
definition: A one-touch option is a type of derivative that pays a fixedpremiumif the underlying asset's spot rate reaches a predeterminedstrike pricebefore expiration. Unlike vanilla options, it offers a simple, all-or-nothing outcome that can be more cost-effective for targeted market predictions.

title: OneCoin
definition: OneCoin, founded in 2014 by Ruja Ignatova, claimed to operate like a legitimate cryptocurrency. However, lacking a real blockchain, it functioned as an elaborate Ponzi scheme that duped investors out of $4 billion before its implosion. Despite claiming to offer educational courses, these materials were plagiarized and part of a multi-level marketing trap. The firm's downfall saw Ignatova vanish, leaving co-founder Sebastian Greenwood jailed, while countless investors were left to deal with the fallout.

title: Onerous Contract
definition: An onerous contract is an agreement that costs a company more to fulfill than it will earn from it. Under international accounting standards, these contracts must be listed as liabilities on the balance sheet.

title: Online Banking
definition: Online banking lets you manage your finances anywhere you have a computer or laptop and an internet connection. What you can do online depends on how robust your bank's digital banking platform is. Generally, you can complete basic banking activities like viewing recent transactions andtransferring money between accounts.

title: Online-to-Offline (O2O) Commerce Definition and Trends
definition: Online-to-offline (O2O) commerce is a business strategy that draws potential customers from online channels to make purchases in physical stores. Online-to-offline (O2O) commerce identifies customers in the online space, such as through emails and Internet advertising, and then uses a variety of tools and approaches to entice the customers to leave the online space. This type of strategy incorporates techniques used in online marketing with those used inbrick-and-mortarmarketing.

title: Ontario Securities Commission (OSC)
definition: The Ontario Securities Commission (OSC) is the largest securities regulator in Canada, enforcing securities laws in the province of Ontario. As acrown corporation, the OSC is answerable to the provincial government of Ontario.

title: Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan Board (OTPPB)
definition: The Ontario Teachers’ Pension Plan Board (OTPPB) oversees the retirement plan that was established for the benefit of public school teachers in Ontario.

title: Opaque Pricing
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Opco
definition: An "Opco," short for "operating company," is crucial in the structure of an opco/propco deal, especially when setting up a real estate investment trust (REIT). This model separates operations from property ownership, allowing the opco to handle daily management while the propco owns all real estate assets. This strategic division enhances financial advantages, credit ratings, and leasing operations.

title: Operating Company/Property Company Deal (OPCO or PROPCO)
definition: An operating company/property company (opco/propco) deal is a business structure where the property company ("propco") owns the revenue-generating real estate, while the operating company ("opco") manages the core business operations.

title: OPEC Basket
definition: The OPEC Basket is a weighted average of oil prices from the different OPEC members around the world. Members of theOrganization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries(OPEC) contribute data that forms the basis of the basket. The basket is a benchmark, or reference point, for those monitoring the oil price and the stability of the global oil market.

title: Open
definition: The term "open" appears in several usages in the financial markets. However, there are two that hold particular significance, depending on the context in which they are used.

title: Open Architecture
definition: Open architecture describes a financial institution's ability to offer clients both proprietary and external, third-party products and services. Open architecture ensures that a client can satisfy all their financial needs and that the investment firm can act in each client’s best interests by recommending the financial products best suited to that client, even if they're not proprietary products.

title: Open Banking
definition: Open Banking, often referred to as "open bank data," is revolutionizing the banking sector by allowing third-party financial service providers access to consumer financial data throughapplication programming interfaces (APIs). This practice facilitates the networking of accounts and data across variousfinancial institutions, fostering innovation poised to reshape the industry's landscape. While Open Banking presents opportunities for enhanced financial management and innovation, it also introduces potential risks concerning data privacy and financial security. Below, we delve into the intricacies of Open Banking, examining its promises and pitfalls.

title: Open Cover
definition: An open cover is a form of marine coverage that insures cargo for the duration of an insurance policy.

title: Open-End Credit
definition: Open-end credit, also known as revolving credit, allows borrowers torepeatedlyaccess funds up to a set limit, with credit becoming available again as payments are made. Common examples include credit cards and personal lines of credit, offering flexibility and interest charged only on the amount used.

title: Open-End Fund
definition: An open-end fund is an investment vehicle that creates and redeems shares based on investor demand, pooling money into a diversified portfolio without a fixed share limit.

title: Open-End Lease
definition: An open-end lease is a flexible rental agreement typically used in commercial transactions where the lessee agrees to purchase the leased asset at the end of the term by making a balloon payment. Unlike a closed-end lease, an open-end lease offers more freedom, especially for commercial vehicle fleets, due to unlimited mileage terms.

title: Open-End Management Company
definition: An open-end management company is a type of investment company responsible for the management of open-end funds. Open-end management companies manage both open-end mutual funds andexchange traded funds(ETFs).

title: Open-End Mortgage
definition: An open-end mortgage is a type ofmortgagethat allows the borrower to increase the amount of the mortgage principal outstanding at a later time. Open-end mortgages permit the borrower to go back to thelenderand borrow more money. There is usually a set dollar limit on the additional amount that can be borrowed.

title: Open Ended Investment Company (OEIC)
definition: Open-ended investment companies (OEICs) are UK-based funds that pool investor money to create diversified portfolios of stocks and securities. They offer flexibility by continuously creating new shares to meet investor demand and are regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority.

title: Open House
definition: Buying a house is often the largest single purchase that someone will make. An open house allows prospective buyers to tour properties in person before committing to the sale.

title: Open Interest
definition: Open interest is the total number of derivative contracts, including futures and options, that remain outstanding in the market and have yet to be settled.

title: Open Kimono
definition: An audit is an official assessment of a person or company's financial accounts by professional accountants.

title: Open Listing
definition: An open listing in real estate is a property for sale that may be shown by multiple real estate agents. The agents compete to find a buyer for the property.

title: Open Loop Card
definition: An open loop card is a general-purpose charge card that can be used anywhere that brand of card is accepted. It usually bears the logo of the card brand or network (which processes the actual transactions), such as Visa, Mastercard, American Express, or Discover.
