In the case of cards offered through financial institutions, like Visa or Mastercard, they often show the name of the issuing bank or credit union as well.

title: Open Market
definition: An open market is an economic system where goods and services can be bought and sold with minimal restrictions, such as tariffs, taxes,subsidies, or licensing requirements. Countries like the U.S., Canada, Western European nations, and Australia are relatively open markets that encourage free trade and international commerce. In these markets, supply and demand play a central role in setting prices, allowing businesses and consumers to engage in transactions with fewer barriers. By promoting competition and transparency, open markets help economies allocate resources efficiently and foster global trade.

title: Open Market Operations
definition: The Fed uses open market operations to keep the federal funds rate at its established target.

title: Open-Market Rate
definition: The open-market rate is the rate of interest paid on any debt security that trades in theopen market. Interest rates for such debt instruments as commercial paper and banker's acceptances would fall under the category of open-market rates. Debt securities include government bonds, corporate bonds, certificate of deposit (CD), municipal bonds and preferred stock.

title: Open-Market Transaction
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Open Mouth Operations
definition: Moral suasion is the use of rhetorical appeals, persuasion, or implicit and explicit threats—as opposed to the use of outright coercion or physical force—to get a person or group to behave in a certain way. In economics, it is sometimes used in reference tocentral banks. Central banks often use jawboning to influence economic expectations. For example, the Fed's "Fedspeak" aims to manage market responses with selective communication. The 1998 LTCM bailout is a notable example of moral suasion in action.

title: Open Offer
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Open Order
definition: An open order is an un-filled, or working order that is to beexecutedwhen an, as yet, unmet requirement has been met before it is cancelled by the customer or expires. The customer has the flexibility to place an order to buy or sell a security that remains in effect until their specified condition has been satisfied.

title: Open Outcry
definition: Open outcry was a popular method for communicating trade orders in trading pits before 2010. The verbal and hand signal communication used by traders at stock, option, andfutures exchangesare now rarely employed, replaced by faster and more accurate electronic order systems. Signals and shouts made in a particular manner and sequence would convey trading information, intentions, and acceptance in the trading pits.

title: Open Position
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Open Source
definition: Open source refers to a software program or platform with source code that is readily accessible and can be modified or enhanced by anyone. Open source access grants users of an application permission to fix broken links, enhance the design, or improve the original code.

title: Open Trade Equity (OTE)
definition: Open trade equity (OTE) is the net ofunrealized gain or losson open derivatives positions, reflecting paper profits or losses before trades are closed. Marked to market, OTE shows the current value of an account if positions were settled immediately. It plays an important role for margin investors, influencing available equity, margin calls, and trading decisions based on real-time account performance.

title: Opening Bell
definition: The opening bell refers to the moment a securities exchange opens for its normal daily trading session. The time and conditions of the opening bell differ from one exchange to another. The most famous opening bell is the one used by the New York Stock Exchange to signal the start of trading.

title: Opening Cross
definition: The Opening Cross is a process the Nasdaq uses to determine the opening price of stocks. It matches buy and sell orders at the start of each trading day to establish a fair and orderly opening price for each stock.

title: Opening Imbalance Only Order (OIO)
definition: Opening Imbalance Only Orders (OIO) are a type of limit order that provides liquidity during the opening cross on the Nasdaq. Alimit orderis an order placed with a brokerage to execute a buy or sell transaction at a set number of shares and a specified limit price.

title: Opening Price
definition: The opening price is the cost of a security at the opening of an exchange.

title: Opening Range
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Operating Activities
definition: Operating activities are the functions of a business directly related to providing its goods and/or services to the market. These are the company's core business activities, such as manufacturing, distributing, marketing, and selling a product or service. Operating activities will generally provide the majority of a company’scash flowand largely determine whether it is profitable. Some common operating activities include cash receipts from goods sold, payments to employees, taxes, and payments to suppliers. These activities can be found on a company's financial statements and in particular the income statement and cash flow statement.

title: Operating Cash Flow (OCF)
definition: Operating cash flow is the amount of cash generated by a company in producing and selling its products or services.

title: Operating Cash Flow Demand (OCFD)
definition: Cash flow is the total amount of money being transferred into and out of a business.

title: Operating Cash Flow Margin
definition: Operating cash flow margin measures operating cash flow as a percentage of sales revenue (operating cash flow / revenue). Unlike operating margin, which is based on accounting profit, it focuses on cash generated by the core business, making it useful for comparing performance over time or against competitors.

title: Operating Cash Flow Ratio
definition: The operating cash flow ratio is a measure of a company's ability to covercurrent liabilitieswith money generated from a company's operations. This ratio can help gauge a company'sliquidityin the short term. Operating cash flow more accurately measures financial health than net income because net income figures can be altered more easily by accounting manipulation. The formula for the operating cash flow ratio is operating cash flow divided by current liabilities. A higher ratio indicates that a company has more cash than needed to pay off current liabilities.

title: Operating Cost
definition: Operating costs are the ongoing, daily expenses necessary for running a business.

title: Operating Earnings
definition: Operating earnings is a corporate finance and accounting term that isolates the profits realized from a business's core operations. Specifically, it refers to the amount of profit realized from revenues after you subtract those expenses that are directly associated with running the business, such as thecost of goods sold(COGS),general and administration(G&A) expenses, selling and marketing, research and development, depreciation, and other operating costs.

title: Operating Expense
definition: An operating expense (OpEx) is a cost that a business incurs through its normal operations, including expenses such as rent, payroll, and marketing, which are typically tax deductible.

title: Operating Expense Ratio
definition: In real estate, the operating expense ratio (OER) is a measurement of the cost to operate a piece of property, compared to the income brought in by the property. It is calculated by dividing a property's operating expense (minus depreciation) by its gross operating income.

title: Operating Income
definition: A company's operating income is the profit it has earned after its operating expenses are deducted.

title: Operating Income Before Depreciation and Amortization (OIBDA)
definition: Operating income before depreciation and amortization (OIBDA) is a measure of financial performance used by companies to show profitability in their core business activities. OIBDA excludes the effects of capital spending onfixed assets, such as equipment, and the interest expense of carrying debt.

title: Operating Lease
definition: An operating lease is a contract that allows for an asset's use but doesn't convey rights of ownership.

title: Operating Leverage
definition: Operating leverage is a pivotal financial ratio that measures the impact of sales on operating income. It's used to calculate a company’s break-even point and help set appropriate selling prices that cover all costs and generate a profit. A business with a highgross marginand low variable costs has high operating leverage. Companies with high operating leverage must cover large fixed costs each month, regardless of sales. Low-operating-leverage companies may have high costs that vary directly with their sales, but they also have lower fixed costs to cover each month.

title: Operating Loss (OL)
definition: An operating loss occurs when a company'soperating expensesexceedgross profits(or revenues in the case of a service-oriented company). A company's operating profit is its profit before interest and taxes. Interest and taxes are not considered operating expenses in the way that cost of goods sold, selling, general and administrative expenses are. Often companies generate enough revenue to cover the operating expenses and make an operating profit.

title: Operating Margin
definition: Operating margin is a company's profit for every dollar of sales after deducting production costs like wages and raw materials but before accounting for interest and taxes.

title: Operating Profit
definition: Operating profit represents a company's earnings generated through normal business operations after adjustment for operating expenses and is subject to several accounting rules.

title: Operating Ratio (OPEX)
definition: The term operating ratio refers to the efficiency of a company's management by comparing the totaloperating expense (OPEX)of a company to net sales. The operating ratio shows how efficient a company's management is at keeping costs low while generating revenue or sales. The smaller the ratio, the more efficient the company is at generating revenue vs. total expenses.

title: Operating Revenue
definition: Operating revenue is the revenue that a company generates from its primary business activities.

title: Operation Twist
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Operational Efficiency
definition: Operational efficiency measures profit relative tooperating costs. Higher operational efficiency means more profit for the same or lower costs compared to alternatives. This is important in both investments and business operations.

title: Operational Risk
definition: Operational risk summarizes a company's uncertainties and hazards when attempting to do its day-to-day business activities within a field or industry. A type ofbusiness risk, it can result from breakdowns in internal procedures, people, and systems—as opposed to problems incurred from external forces, such as political or economic events, or inherent to the entire market or market segment, known assystematic risk.

title: Operational Target
definition: An operating target is a specific number, for an interest rate or another financial metric, that a central bank sets in order to guide itsmonetary policy.

title: Operations Management
definition: Operations management employs and prioritizes business practices in an organization to achieve maximum efficiency. Operations managers coordinate the activities of all departments within a company to optimize efficiency from production to end-product distribution.

title: Opportunity Cost
definition: Opportunity cost is any gain you pass up by deciding on one use of your resources over others.

title: Oprah Effect
definition: The Oprah Effect refers to the boost in sales that followed anendorsementon "The Oprah Winfrey Show", which aired on TV for 25 years. A recommendation from Oprah, the queen of talk shows, turned many fashion and lifestyle products into multimillion-dollar companies.

title: Optimal Capital Structure
definition: Optimal capital structure diminishes a company’s weighted average cost of capital (WACC) and makes its market value as great as possible. It represents the most effective combination of debt and equity financing.

title: Optimal Currency Area
definition: An optimal currency area (OCA) is the geographic area in which a singlecurrencywould create the greatest economic benefit. While traditionally each country has maintained its own separatenational currency, work by Robert Mundell in the 1960s theorized that this might not be the most efficient economic arrangement.

title: Optimization
definition: Optimization is the process of making a trading system more effective by adjusting the variables used fortechnical analysis. A trading system can be optimized by reducing certaintransaction costsorrisks, or by targeting assets with greaterexpected returns.

title: Optimized Portfolio As Listed Securities (OPALS)
definition: Optimized portfolio as listed securities is a single-country equity index that contains fewer holdings than the benchmarked index. Optimized portfolio as listed securities was created byMorgan Stanleyin 1994. It is seen as a predecessor to the popularity of exchange-traded funds.

title: Optimum Currency Area (OCA) Theory
definition: Optimum currency area theory (OCA) states that specific areas not bounded by national borders would benefit from a common currency. In other words, geographic regions may be better off using the samecurrencyinstead of each country within that geographic region using its own currency.

title: Option Adjustable-Rate Mortgage (Option ARM)
definition: An option adjustable-rate mortgage (option ARM) is a type ofARMmortgagewhere the borrower has several options as to which type of payment is made to the lender. In addition to having the choice of making payments of interest and principal that amounts to those made in conventional mortgages, option ARMs also have alternative payment options where the mortgagor can make significantly smaller payments by making interest-only payments or minimum payments.

title: Option-Adjusted Spread (OAS)
definition: The option-adjusted spread (OAS) measures the spread between a bond's rate and the risk-free rate, while adjusting for any embedded options like callables or mortgage-backed securities. Utilizing OAS helps investors determine the value and potential risks of investing by evaluating a bond’s yield against economic factors and market volatility.

title: Option Agreement
definition: An options contract is an agreement between two parties for a potential transaction on an underlying security at a preset price, called the strike price, before or on the expiration date.

title: Option Chain
definition: An options chain is a list of all available option contracts for a specific security, organized by expiration date and strike price.

title: Option Class
definition: An option class refers to all thecall optionsor all theput optionslisted on an exchange for a particularunderlying asset. For example, all the calls available for trade on Apple Inc. (AAPL) stock would be part of the same options class. All the puts listed on Apple would be part of another, related class.

title: Option Cycle
definition: Option cycle refers to theexpiration datesthat apply to the different classes of options. A newly listedoptionis assigned a cycle randomly to broadly distribute options across varying time frames. It is also known as an expiration cycle.

title: Option Disclosure Document
definition: The options disclosure document (ODD) is a publication issued by theOptions Clearing Corporation(OCC) that serves as an important guide for options traders. The comprehensive document—formally titledCharacteristics and Risks of Standardized Options—is particularly essential for novice options traders.

title: Option Margin
definition: Option margin is the collateral (cash or securities) an investor must deposit beforewritingor selling options to secure potential obligations. Margin requirements, set by theFederal Reserve'sRegulation T, vary by option type, and brokers may impose stricter rules. Some strategies, such as covered calls, don't require margin, offering lower-risk ways to trade options.

title: Option Pool
definition: An option pool consists of shares of stock reserved for employees of aprivate company. The option pool is a way of attracting talented employees to astartupcompany—if the employees help the company do well enough to go public, they will be compensated with stock. Employees who get into the startup early will usually receive a greater percentage of the option pool than employees who arrive later.

title: Option Premium
definition: An option premium is the price paid by the buyer of an options contract to the seller, reflecting the current market value.

title: Option Pricing Theory
definition: Option pricing theory estimates the value of an options contract by assessing the likelihood that the contract will be "in the money" at expiration. Market participants, including traders and market makers, employ various models—such as Black-Scholes, binomial option pricing, and Monte-Carlo simulation—to quantify an option's theoretical fair value. Understanding these models helps traders strategize effectively to maximize their financial outcomes.

title: Option Series
definition: An option series is a set of financial options that share the same underlying security, strike price, and expiration date, allowing traders to capitalize on market movements in a structured way. However, call and put options are parts of separate series. A call option series would include the available calls on a specific security at a certain strike price that will expire in the same month.

title: Optionable Stock
definition: An optionable stock is one where the shares have the necessaryliquidityand volume such that an exchange lists that stock's options for trading. In order for a stock to be optionable, exchanges mandate that certain criteria be met, including a minimum share price, number of shares outstanding, and minimum unique shareholders, among others.

title: Options
definition: An option is a type of financial instrument that's tied to an underlying security. Options give their buyers the right, but not the obligation, to purchase or sell the asset at a specified price.

title: Options Backdating
definition: Options backdating is the process of granting anemployee stock option(ESO) that is dated before its actual issuance. In this way, theexercise (strike) priceof the granted option can be set at a lower price than that of the company's stock price at the granting date. This process makes the granted option "in the money" (ITM) and therefore of greater value to the holder.

title: Options Clearing Corporation (OCC)
definition: The Options Clearing Corporation (OCC) is an organization that acts as both the issuer and guarantor foroptions and futures contracts. The largest equity derivatives clearing organization in the world, it operates under the jurisdiction of the Commodities Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) and theU.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

title: Options Contract
definition: An options contract is an agreement between two parties for a potential transaction on an underlying security at a preset price, called the strike price, before or on the expiration date.

title: Options Industry Council (OIC)
definition: Options Industry Council (OIC) refers to a cooperative that helps educate investors and financial advisers about the benefits and risks ofexchange-traded equity options.

title: Options on Futures
definition: An option on afutures contractgives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell a specific futures contract at a strike price on or before the option'sexpiration date. These work similarly to stock options, but differ in that the underlying security is a futures contract and not the security that underlies the futures contract.

title: Options Price Reporting Authority (OPRA)
definition: The Options Price Reporting Authority (OPRA) is a committee of representatives from participating securities exchanges responsible for providing last-sale optionsquotationsand information from the participating exchanges.

title: Oracle of Omaha
definition: The Oracle of Omaha is a nickname forWarren Buffett, arguably one of the greatest investors of all time. Buffett is the chair and CEO ofBerkshire Hathaway, a company that he became the controlling shareholder of in the mid-1960s.

title: Oral Contract
definition: An oral contract, though not documented in writing, holds legal binding power through spoken agreements. While challenging to prove, these agreements can be upheld with evidence such as witness testimonies and documented actions. Understanding the nuances and jurisdictional factors affecting oral agreements is essential for ensuring their enforceability.

title: Orange Book
definition: The Orange Book is a list of drugs and pharmaceuticals that theU.S. Food and Drug Administration(FDA) has approved as both safe and effective. Although it is commonly called the Orange Book, its formal name is Approved Drug Products with Therapeutic Equivalence Evaluations.

title: Order
definition: An order executes the buying or selling of securities under conditions set by the investor.

title: Order Audit Trail System (OATS)
definition: An audit trail is a detailed record tracing financial data back to its origin, playing a crucial role in verifying transactions and ensuring transparency. Companies leverageaudittrails to trace accounting entries, trades, and funds. This process is essential for detecting discrepancies and complying with regulations, which ultimately stabilizes the economy by confirming the validity of publicly released information.

title: Order Book
definition: Order books are used by almost every exchange to list the orders of stocks, bonds, currencies, and other assets.

title: Order Driven Market
definition: An order-driven market is a financial market where all buyers and sellers display the prices at which they wish to buy or sell a particular security, as well as the amounts of the security desired to be bought or sold. This kind of trading environment is the opposite of aquote-driven market, which only displays bids and asks ofdesignated market makersand specialists for the specific security that is being traded.

title: Order Imbalance
definition: Order imbalance occurs when there are too many buy or sell orders for a security, preventing buyers and sellers from matching up. For securities managed by amarket maker, shares from a reserve may be used to add liquidity and clear excess orders, helping trading resume smoothly. Severe order imbalances can lead to a temporarysuspension of tradinguntil resolved.

title: Order Management Systems (OMS)
definition: An order management system (OMS) is an electronic system developed to executesecuritiesorders in an efficient and cost-effective manner. Brokers and dealers use an OMS when filling orders for various types of securities and can track the progress of each order throughout the system.

title: Order Paper
definition: An order paper, or order instrument, is anegotiable instrumentthat is payable to a specified person or itsassignee. An instrument such as an order paper is negotiable only if it is payable to the order of a specified person; meaning that it must designate an individual's name to be paid out. It is the opposite of a bearer instrument, which does not require the designation of an individual to be paid out.

title: Order Protection Rule
definition: The Order Protection Rule is one of the four main provisions of theRegulation National Market System(NMS). The rule is meant to ensure that investors receive an execution price that is equivalent to what is being quoted on any other exchange where the security is traded. The rule eliminates the possibility of orders being traded through, which means executed at a suboptimal price.﻿

title: Orderly Market
definition: An orderly market is any market in whichsupply and demandare reasonably equal. An orderly market is said to be in a state ofequilibrium. This term can also refer to a site of exchange for goods, services, or financial securities that are traded in a fair, reliable, secure, accurate, and efficient way. Orderly markets contribute to economic growth.

title: Ordinary and Necessary Expense (O&NE)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Ordinary Annuity
definition: An ordinary annuity is a series of equal payments made at the end of a time period for a fixed amount of time. Bond dividend payments are an example.

title: Ordinary Dividends
definition: Ordinary dividends are a share of a company’s profits passed on to the shareholders periodically. One of the primary advantages of owning stocks, also known as equities, is the regular payment ofdividendincome.

title: Ordinary Income
definition: Ordinary income is income earned by an entity or an individual that is taxable at marginal tax rates. It can include wages, salaries, tips, bonuses, commissions, rents, and royalties.

title: Ordinary Loss
definition: An ordinary loss is a shortfall from regular business activities when expenses exceed revenues. It differs from a capital loss and is generally fully deductible against ordinary income, which can reduce taxes more than capital losses that face tighter deduction limits. Unlike capital losses, ordinary losses generally can't be carried forward.

title: Ordinary Shares of Stock
definition: Ordinary shares, or common shares, represent a fraction of company ownership and provide voting rights at shareholder meetings. Unlike preferred shares, ordinary shareholders are not guaranteed dividends but can still benefit when the company profits. Most shares on U.S. stock exchanges are ordinary shares, and they offer the opportunity for voting and potential rewards without guaranteed dividends.

title: Organic Growth: What It Is and Why It Matters to Investors
definition: Organic growth is achieved internally by boosting production and driving sales growth without buying new businesses.

title: Understanding Reserve-Replacement Ratio: Key Insights for Investors
definition: The reserve-replacement ratio (RRR) compares how much oil a company adds to reserves versus how much it produces, helpinginvestorsjudge whether future output can be sustained. A 100% ratio signals stable production, but the metric should be paired with others because reserve estimates and long-term forecasts can be uncertain.

title: Organic Sales
definition: Organic sales are revenues generated from within a company. Organicsalesencompass those streams of revenues that are a direct result of the firm's existing operations as opposed to revenues that have been acquired through the purchase of another company or business unit in the past year. The sale or disposal of business lines are also netted out of a total sales figure to derive organic sales. Measuring organic sales is important because it can show the amount of growth that's the direct result of a company's business plan or sales strategy.

title: Organisation of Eastern Caribbean States (OECS)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)
definition: The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) is an influential internation organization of 37 member countries that discuss and develop economic and social policy.OECD members are typically democratic countries that support free-market economies. The organization was established in 1960.

title: Organization of Arab Petroleum Exporting Countries (OAPEC)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC)
definition: The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) is a group of 12 major oil-exporting nations. OPEC was founded in 1960 to coordinate thepetroleumpolicies of its members and to provide member states with technical and economic aid.

title: Organizational Behavior (OB)
definition: Organizational behavior is the academic study of human behavior in group settings.

title: Organizational Chart
definition: An organizational chart, or org chart, graphically represents anorganization's structureby detailing the roles, responsibilities, and relationships between individuals within the organization.

title: Organizational Economics
definition: Organizational economics studies transactions and decision-making within firms, focusing on how incentives, institutional design, and transaction costs shape management, structure, and performance.

title: Organizational Structure
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Organized Labor
definition: Organized labor is a strategy where workers join together in unions to engage incollective bargainingwith their employers. Organized labor has been credited with higher wages, fewer work hours, and improved benefits for workers in many industries.

title: Original Cost
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM)
definition: An original equipment manufacturer (OEM) provides components for another company’s product and works closely with the seller of the finished product.

title: Original Face
definition: Original face is thepar valueof amortgage-backed security(MBS) at the time it isissued. An MBS is an investment that contains a number ofmortgagehome loans from various banks in which investors earn income from those loans.

title: Original Issue Discount (OID)
definition: An original issue discount (OID) is the discount in price from a bond'sface valueat the time abondor other debt instrument is first issued.

title: Origination
definition: Origination is the multi-step process that every individual must go through to obtain a mortgage or home loan. The term also applies to other types of amortized personal loans. Origination is often a lengthy process that is overseen by theFederal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC)for compliance with Title XIV of theDodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act.A loan origination fee, usually about 1% of the loan, is intended to compensate the lender for the work involved in the process.

title: Origination Fee
definition: A mortgage origination fee is an upfront fee charged by a lender to process a new loan application. The fee is compensation for executing the loan. Loanoriginationfees are quoted as a percentage of the total loan, and they are generally between 0.5% and 1% of a mortgage loan in the United States.

title: Origination Points: Meaning, Examples in Mortgages
definition: Origination is a step-by-step process that every borrower must complete to obtain a mortgage or home loan. Meanwhile, origination points represent the fees borrowers pay to lenders or loan officers to compensate for evaluating, processing, and approving mortgage loans. They represent a way to pay closing costs, and these fees are negotiable among mortgage lenders.

title: Orphan Blocks Explained: Network Lag and Blockchain Integrity
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Orphan Drug Credit
definition: The Orphan Drug Act gives drug companies incentives to develop treatments for rare diseases, including a 25% federaltax crediton qualified clinical trials. Other incentives include a rebate on application fees and a seven-year window of drug exclusivity. The act was adopted in 1983 and has led to approvals for more than 780 products to treat more than 250 rare diseases. About half of the approved treatments are in the field of oncology (the treatment of cancer).

title: Oscillator
definition: An oscillator is a pivotal tool intechnical analysisused to build a trend indicator that fluctuates between high and low extremes. Traders leverage oscillators to detect short-termoverbought or oversold asset conditions, aiding in their search for market entry and exit points.

title: Oscillator of a Moving Average (OsMA)
definition: OsMA is an abbreviation for the term oscillator of amoving average(MA). The OsMA is a technical indicator that shows the difference between an oscillator and its moving average over a given period of time.

title: Oslo Stock Exchange (OSL) .OL
definition: The Oslo Stock Exchange (OSL) is Norway's premierstock exchange. The exchange is located in the capital city of Oslo and is the region's major regulated market for securities trading. Investors can trade different assets, such as equities, bonds, andexchange-traded products (ETPs), on the OSL. The exchange's market capitalization as of May 13, 2022, was roughly $295.55 billion.Founded in 1819, the exchange was acquired byEuronextin 2019. The exchange is part of the NOREX alliance, making it attractive to foreign investment.

title: OTC Markets Group Inc.
definition: The OTC Markets Group is the owner and operator of the most substantial U.S. inter-dealer electronic quotation and trading system forover-the-counter(OTC) securities. It provides marketplaces for trading more than 11,500 OTC securities.

title: OTC Options
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: OTC Pink
definition: The Pink Open Market, or OTC Pink, is a speculative marketplace for trading stocks not listed on major exchanges. Instead, the Pink market supports over-the-counter trading, involving direct transactions between parties instead of through a centralized exchange.

title: OTCQB Venture Market: Key Facts and Benefits for Stock Investors
definition: The OTCQB is the middle tier of the OTC Markets Group. It is designed for early-stage and developing companies. Established in 2010, it sits between the OTCQX and the lower Pink Sheets.Unlike major exchanges, it has no minimum financial standards, so it includes a broad range of companies, including shell companies and foreign issuers.Trading happens through OTC Link rather than on major exchanges like theNew York Stock Exchange (NYSE). To be listed on the OTCQB, companies must meet basic reporting and shareholder requirements.

title: OTCQX
definition: The OTCQX is the top tier of the three marketplaces for theover-the-counter(OTC) trading of stocks, provided and operated by theOTC Markets Group. It lists global blue-chip stocks and requires stricter qualification criteria, including governance transparency and SEC compliance, compared to the other tiers, which are theOTCQB/OTCBB and the Pink Sheets. The OTCQX also offers trading advantages like real-time quotes and market depth for investors.

title: Other Comprehensive Basis of Accounting (OCBOA)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Other Current Assets (OCA)
definition: Other current assets (OCAs) are short-term liquid assets that don't fit into standard categories like cash, inventory, or receivables but can be converted to cash within a year. Listed on thebalance sheet, OCAs may include items such as employee advances, supplier prepayments, or property held for sale. Though often minor, they provide valuable insight into a company's liquidity and short-term financial health.

title: Other Current Liabilities
definition: Other current liabilities are short-term obligations due within 12 months that a company groups into onebalance-sheetline for readability. Unlike standard current liabilities that are listed separately, "other" covers smaller items that still matter for understanding a firm'snear-term cash obligations.

title: Other Long-Term Liabilities
definition: Other long-term liabilities are a line item on abalance sheetthat lumps togetherobligationsthat are not due within 12 months. These debts that are less urgent to repay are a part of itstotal liabilitiesbut they're categorized as “other” when the company doesn’t deem them to be important enough to warrant individual identification.

title: Other Post-Employment Benefits (OPEB)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Other Post-Retirement Benefits
definition: Other post-retirement benefits are benefits, other than pension distributions, paid to employees during their retirement years. Post-retirement benefits may include life insurance and medical plans, or premiums for such benefits, as well as deferred-compensation arrangements.

title: Other Real Estate Owned (OREO)
definition: Other Real Estate Owned (OREO) is property that a bank takes on through foreclosure after a borrower defaults. These assets appear as non-performing items on the balance sheet, which can affect liquidity and attract regulatory scrutiny. Large OREO holdings were especially prominent during the 2008 financial crisis, signaling stress within the banking system.

title: Out-of-Pocket Expenses
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Out-of-Pocket Limit
definition: An out-of-pocket maximum sets a clear limit on what you'll spend on covered healthcare services in a year, giving you financial predictability. This cap includes your spending ondeductibles,copayments, andcoinsurancefor in-network care. Once you reach this limit, your health insurer covers 100% of your healthcare services for the rest of your plan year.

title: Out of The Money (OTM)
definition: An "out of the money" option has no intrinsic value.

title: Outcome Bias
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Outlay Cost
definition: An outlay cost is a direct expense paid to run a business project or acquire an asset, such asvendorpayments, production start-up costs, or equipment purchases, and it directly reduces earnings. It appears in both cash and accrual accounting and helps inform sound business decisions.

title: Outperform
definition: In financial analysis, "outperform" is a common stock rating used by analysts to indicate that a security is expected to deliver returns greater than a major market benchmark, such as the S&P 500. Analysts may upgrade a stock to "outperform" from "market perform" or "underperform" when new research or changing conditions suggest stronger future performance.

title: Outplacement
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Output Gap
definition: An output gap is a measure of the difference between a country's actual and potentialgross domestic product (GDP). It's expressed as a percentage. Output gap measures the performance of a country'seconomy. A country's output gap may be either positive or negative. A negative output gap suggests that actual economic output is below the economy's full capacity, while a positive output suggests an economy that is outperforming expectations.

title: Outright Forward
definition: An outright forward is a type of currency forward contract that allows businesses and investors to lock in a specific exchange rate and delivery date beyond the spot value date. This helps them manage and mitigate risks associated with exchange rate fluctuations.

title: Outright Futures Position
definition: A bank guarantee is a commitment from a financial institution to cover financial obligations if a party in a transaction defaults. This instrument is crucial in fostering trust and facilitating transactions such as acquiring goods, buying equipment, or engaging in international trade, especially outside the United States. Should the client fail to meet the contract's terms, the bank steps in to ensure financial coverage, protecting both parties involved.

title: Outright Option
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Outside Days
definition: Termination of employment is the end of an employee’s work with a company. An employee may be terminated from a job of their own free will or following a decision made by the employer. Employers who execute a termination of employment may do so for many reasons, including downsizing, poor job performance, or redundancies.

title: Outside Director
definition: An outside director (anon-executive director) is a non-employee member of a company board who isn't an employee or stakeholder, and is meant to offer more unbiased oversight. Enron shows what can happen when board oversight fails. Read on to see how outside directors can help reduce conflicts of interest in corporate governance.

title: Outside Reversal
definition: An outside reversal is a two-day chart pattern that can signal a potential trend change, where the current session's high and low both move beyond the prior session's range.

title: Outside Sales
definition: Outside sales positions require sales personnel to work outside of the office, meeting in person with prospective customers. These professionals tend to work autonomously outside of a formal office setting or a formal team environment. They often travel to meet new customers face-to-face, as well as to maintain relationships with existing customers.

title: Outsourcing
definition: Outsourcing involves hiring external parties to perform tasks or create goods, often cheaper thanin-houseefforts. Companies widely adopt this approach to cut costs and focus on core business aspects. Although outsourcing was popularized in the 1990s for logistics and manufacturing, its economic impact remains controversial. Opponents cite domestic job losses, while supporters argue it promotes resource efficiency and strengthensfree-marketdynamics.

title: Outstanding Check
definition: An outstanding check is acheckpayment that is written by someone but has not been cashed or deposited by the payee. The payor is the entity who writes the check, while the payee is the person or institution to whom it is written. An outstanding check also refers to a check that has been presented to the bank but is still in thebank’scheck-clearing cycle.

title: Outward Arbitrage
definition: The law of one price ensures that similar goods are priced consistently across markets, maintaining price uniformity by adjusting for differences like transaction costs.

title: Outward Direct Investment (ODI)
definition: An outward direct investment (ODI) is a business strategy in which a domestic firm expands its operations to a foreign country.

title: Over-55 Home Sale Exemption
definition: The Over-55 Home Sale Exemption was a significant tax provision forhomeownersaged 55 and older, allowing them to exclude up to $125,000 ofcapital gainsfrom the sale of their primary residences. Although this exemption ended in 1997, it remains a noteworthy example of tax legislation aimed at stimulating the real estate market by rewarding long-term homeowners.

title: Over and Short
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Over-Hedging
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Over-Limit Fee
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Over-Line
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Over-Selling
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Over-the-Counter (OTC)
definition: OTC markets refer to the loosely regulated trading of securities either directly between private parties or via broker-dealer networks, rather than on formal exchanges.

title: Over-the-Counter Bulletin Board (OTCBB)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Over-the-Counter Exchange of India (OTCEI)
definition: The Over-The-Counter Exchange of India (OTCEI) is an electronic stock exchange designed to help small- and medium-sized companies raise capital when they cannot meet the listing requirements of larger national exchanges.

title: Over-the-Counter Market
definition: An over-the-counter (OTC) market is decentralize and where participants trade stocks, commodities, currencies, or other instruments directly between two parties, without a central exchange or broker.

title: Overall Liquidity Ratio: What It Is, How It Works
definition: The overall liquidity ratio is the measurement of a company's capacity to pay its outstandingliabilitieswith itsassetson hand. The overallliquidityratio is calculated by dividing total assets by the difference between its total liabilities andconditional reserves. This ratio is used in the insurance industry, as well as in the analysis of financial institutions.

title: Overall Turnover
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Understanding Overallotment: Key Benefits and Examples
definition: An overallotment, also known as a greenshoe option, is a critical component in the financial world, giving underwriters the flexibility to offer more shares during an IPO or follow-on offering. Designed to stabilize share prices and meet high demand, this option can be exercised if the shares trade above their offering price, allowing companies to raise more capital.

title: Overbought
definition: Overbought describes a security trading above its fair value due to recent bullish trends. Both technical analysis and fundamental analysis may indicate these overstretched prices. Traders and investors use tools like RSI and P/E ratios to determine when a stock is overbought, potentially leading to a price correction.

title: Overcapitalization
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Overcast
definition: An overcast is a type offorecastingerror that occurs when an estimated metric, such as future cash flows, performance levels, or production, is forecast too high. Overcasting thus is when the estimated value turns out to be above the realized or actual value.

title: Overcollateralization (OC)
definition: Over-collateralization (OC) is the provision of collateral that is worth more than enough to cover potential losses in cases of default.

title: Overdraft
definition: An overdraft allows you to continue transactions when your account lacks funds. This bank-provided credit lets you settle payments but may incur fees and interest. Essentially, it's an extension ofcreditfrom the financial institution that is granted when an account reaches zero.

title: Overdraft Protection
definition: Overdraft protection is an optional service that prevents charges to a bank account (primarily checks, ATM transactions, anddebit-cardcharges) from being rejected when they exceed the available funds in the account. Overdraft protection, sometimes called cash-reserve checking,is used most frequently as a cushion forchecking accountsbut can also be applied to savings accounts.

title: Overextension
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Overfitting
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Overfunded Pension Plan
definition: An overfunded pension plan is a company retirement plan that has more assets than liabilities. In other words, there is a surplus amount of money needed to cover current and future monthly benefits to retirees. Although accounting standards allow the company to record the surplus as net income, it cannot be paid out to corporation shareholders like other income as it is reserved for current and future retirees.

title: Overhang
definition: Overhang is a measure of the potentialdilutionof stock shares due to possible awards of stock-based compensation. It is usually represented as a percentage and is calculated asstock optionsgranted plus the remaining options to be granted divided by the total shares outstanding ((SO+RO)/TSO).

title: Overhead
definition: Overhead is an ongoing business expense not directly attributed to creating a product or service.

title: Overhead Rate
definition: The overhead rate is a cost allocated to the production of a product or service. Overhead costs are expenses that are not directly tied to production such as the cost of thecorporateoffice. To allocate overhead costs, an overhead rate is applied to the direct costs tied to production by spreading or allocating the overhead costs based on specific measures.

title: Overhead Ratio
definition: An overhead ratio is a measure of cost efficiency. It compares the operating costs of doing business to a company's taxable interest income plus operating income. A low overhead ratio indicates that a company is minimizing business expenses that aren't directly related to production.

title: Overheated Economy
definition: An overheated economy is one that has experienced a prolonged period of goodeconomic growthand activity that has led to high levels ofinflation, triggered by increased consumer wealth.

title: Overlapping Debt
definition: Overlapping debt describes financial obligations shared by multiple political jurisdictions, which affects local governments and taxpayers. It is common in the United States, where layered jurisdictions issue municipal bonds for projects like schools and roads. This overlap can raise overall debt levels and borrowing costs in local government finance.

title: Overlay
definition: Overlay is a method used to harmonize an investor's separate accounts and positions. Overlay management uses software for improved transaction efficiency and balance. It analyzes portfolio adjustments to ensure that the overallportfolioremains in balance and to prevent any inefficient transactions. It coordinates efforts in line with a client's tax situation and risk tolerance. The target audience for overlay management is institutional investors and ultra-high-net-worth individuals.

title: Overleveraged
definition: A business is said to be overleveraged when it is carrying too much debt when compared to its operating cash flows and equity. An overleveraged company has difficulty in paying its interest and principal payments and is often unable to pay itsoperating expensesbecause of excessive costs due to its debt burden, which often leads to a downward financial spiral. This results in the company having to borrow more to stay in operation, and the problem gets worse. This spiral usually ends when a company restructures its debt or files forbankruptcyprotection.

title: Overnight Position
definition: Overnight positions areopen tradesthat have not been closed or liquidated by the end of the normal trading day.

title: Overnight Index Swap
definition: An index swap is a hedging contract in which one party exchanges a predetermined cash flow with a counterparty on a specified date. An overnight index swap applies an overnight rate index, such as the federal funds rate, to the contracts. Index swaps are specialized groups of conventional fixed-rate swaps, with terms that can be set from three months to more than a year.

title: Overnight Rate
definition: The overnight rate is the interest rate for short-term loans betweendepositoryinstitutions in the overnight market. Banks often have to take extremely short-term loans to meet reserve requirements, and many central banks (including the U.S. Federal Reserve) target this interest rate in setting monetary policy.

title: Overnight Trading
definition: Overnight trading is done between the market close and the open on the following day.

title: Overreaction
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Overseas Private Investment Corporation (OPIC)
definition: The Overseas Private Investment Corporation (OPIC) was a U.S. development finance institution. This government agency assisted private businesses that wanted to invest abroad. OPIC encouraged development in emerging markets through private sector investment overseas by assisting corporations to analyze andmanage riskswhile encouraging development in emerging markets. This helped the agency advance the country's foreign policy and national security goals.

title: Overshooting
definition: Overshooting, drawn from Rüdiger Dornbusch's exchange rate overshooting hypothesis, explains why currency values can swing sharply after economic shocks. Because financial markets react instantly while prices adjust slowly,exchange rates may move beyond their long-run levels when monetary policy shifts or other disturbances occur, creating the volatility often seen in foreign exchange markets.

title: Oversold
definition: The term oversold refers to a condition where an asset has traded lower in price and has the potential for a price bounce.An oversold condition can last for a long time, and therefore being oversold doesn't mean a price rally will come soon, or at all. Manytechnical indicatorsidentify oversold andoverboughtlevels. These indicators base their assessment on where the price is currently trading relative to prior prices.Fundamentalscan also be used to assess whether an asset is potentially oversold and has deviated from its typical value metrics.

title: Understanding Oversubscribed IPOs: Definition, Examples, and Impact
definition: Oversubscribed refers to anew issueof stock shares for which the demand exceeds the available supply. An oversubscribedinitial public offering (IPO)indicates that investors are eager to buy the company's shares. This often leads to a higher share price and/or more shares offered for sale. An oversubscribed issue doesn't mean the high price will last, as demand must eventually acknowledge the company's fundamentals.

title: Oversubscription Privilege Explained: Key Insights for Shareholders
definition: An oversubscription privilege is a feature of some rights or warrants offerings that lets existing shareholders buy additional shares beyond their basic allocation, including any shares left after other shareholders have subscribed.

title: Oversupply
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Overtrading
definition: Overtrading occurs when a broker or investor engages in excessive buying and selling of stocks, which can lead to higher costs and poorer investment outcomes The two main forms of overtrading involve brokers who excessively trade client accounts to boost commission fees, known as churning, and individual traders who trade excessively due to emotional factors like trying to recoup losses.

title: Overvalued
definition: An overvalued stock is one whose currentmarket priceexceeds what itsearningsoutlook or fundamentals, often assessed using metrics like theprice-to-earnings (P/E) ratio, can justify. Overvaluation can stem from factors such as investor enthusiasm, market speculation, or temporary shifts in company performance.

title: Overweight
definition: An overweight investment is when a portfolio allocates more to an asset or sector than its share in a benchmark index. Investors or analysts may overweight sectors with strong growth potential or in response to economic shifts.

title: Overwriting
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Own-Occupation Policy
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Owner Earnings Run Rate
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Owner Financing
definition: Owner financing is a transaction in which a property's seller finances the purchase directly with the person or entity buying it, either in whole or in part. This type of arrangement can be advantageous for bothsellersand buyers because it eliminates the costs of a bank intermediary. Owner financing can create much greater risk and responsibilities for the owner, however.

title: Owner-Occupant
definition: An owner-occupant is a resident of a property who holds thetitleto that property. In contrast, an absentee owner carries the title to the property but doesn't live there. An absentee landlord is a type of absentee owner.

title: Owners' Equivalent Rent (OER)
definition: Owners’ equivalent rent (OER) is the amount ofrentthat would have to be paid in order to substitute a currently owned house as a rental property. This value is also referred to as the rental equivalent. In other words, OER figures the amount of monthly rent that would be equivalent to the monthly expenses of owning a property (e.g. mortgage, taxes, etc.).

title: Oxford Saïd Business School (SBS)
definition: “B-School” is an abbreviation for business school, which refers to schools specializing in business subjects. These include both undergraduate colleges and graduate schools. The most well-known B-School offering is theMaster of Business Administration (MBA)degree program.

title: P-Test
definition: A p-value, or probability value, is a number describing the likelihood of obtaining the observed data under thenull hypothesisof a statistical test.

title: P-Value
definition: A p-value, or probability value, is a number describing the likelihood of obtaining the observed data under thenull hypothesisof a statistical test.

title: Pac-Man Defense
definition: The Pac-Man defense involves a targeted firm in ahostile takeoversituation trying to acquire the company that has made a takeover attempt. In an attempt to scare off the would-be acquirers, the takeover target may dip into itswar chestfor cash to buy a majority stake in the other company. The approach can be risky yet necessary for survival for the target company. Bendix Corp. vs. Martin Marietta is an example of a time the tactic has been used.

title: Paga
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Paid-In Capital
definition: Paid-in capital is the total amount of cash that a company has received in exchange for its common or preferred stock issues.

title: Paid-Up Additional Insurance
definition: Paid-up additional (PUA) insurance is optional coverage within a whole life insurance policy that uses dividends to buy extra amounts of insurance without raising premiums. Each PUA adds its owncash valueand death benefit, compounding growth over time.

title: Paid-Up Capital
definition: Paid-up capital refers to the equity capital that has been fully paid by shareholders in exchange for ownership interests.

title: Paper Money
definition: Paper money, the official currency circulated by a country’s central bank or treasury, plays a crucial role in transactions for acquiring goods and services. It often gets updated with security features to deter counterfeiting. Understanding its origins and evolution helps grasp its significance in today's financial ecosystem, especially as it shares space with digital currencies and credit systems.

title: Paper Trade
definition: Paper trades don’t involve actual investing. They’re learning sessions that take place before would-be investors attempt real trading.

title: Par
definition: Par value is the face value of a bond or stock when it is first issued.

title: Par Value
definition: Par value is the face value of a bond or stock when it is first issued.

title: Par Yield Curve
definition: A par yield curve is a graphical representation of the yields of hypothetical Treasury securities with prices at par.

title: Parabolic SAR (Stop and Reverse) Indicator
definition: Theparabolic SARindicator, developed by J. Wells Wilder, is used by traders to determine trend direction and potential reversals in price. The indicator uses a trailing stop and reverse method called "SAR," or stop and reverse, to identify suitable exit andentry points. Traders also refer to the indicator as to the parabolic stop and reverse, parabolic SAR, or PSAR.

title: Paradox of Thrift
definition: The paradox of thrift, introduced by John Maynard Keynes, suggests that while saving is typically wise for individuals, it can negatively impact the economy during arecession. This theory explains that individual savings reduce total spending, leading to decreased production, employment, and further economic downturn. Understanding this contradiction helps clarify why increased savings during a downturn may exacerbate the situation instead of improving it.

title: Parent Company
definition: A parent company is a business that has a controlling interest in other companies and provides financial, strategic, and operational support.

title: Pareto Analysis
definition: Pareto analysis is a decision-making tool that is based on the 80-20 principle, which states that 80% of outcomes stem from 20% of inputs.

title: Pareto Efficiency
definition: Pareto efficiency, also known as Pareto optimality, describes a situation in which resources are distributed in such a way that improving one person's well-being would inevitably reduce someone else's.

title: Pareto Improvement
definition: Under the rubric ofneoclassical economictheory, a Pareto improvement occurs when a change in allocation harms no one and helps at least one person, given an initial allocation of goods for a set of persons. The theory states that Pareto improvements can keep enhancing value to an economy until it achieves a Pareto optimum, where no more Pareto improvements can be made.

title: Pareto Principle
definition: The Pareto Principle states that a small portion of inputs generates the majority of consequences.

title: Pari-passu
definition: Pari-passu is a Latin phrase that means "equal footing" and describes situations in which two or more assets, securities,creditors, or obligations are treated equally, without preference or priority.

title: Parity
definition: Parity price refers to the point at which two assets hold equal value, a concept used across markets such as fixed income, equities, currencies, andconvertible bonds. In convertible bonds, it helps determine when converting bonds into common stock becomes profitable.

title: Parity Price
definition: Parity price refers to the point at which two assets hold equal value, a concept used across markets such as fixed income, equities, currencies, andconvertible bonds. In convertible bonds, it helps determine when converting bonds into common stock becomes profitable.

title: Parsonage Allowance
definition: A parsonage allowance, also known as a housing or rental allowance, is money provided by a religious organization to its minister to cover housing expenses. It functions as a tax exemption, excluded from gross income rather than deducted, when used for eligible costs such as rent, mortgage payments, and utilities.

title: Participating Policy
definition: A participating policy is an insurance contract that pays dividends to the policyholder. Dividends are generated from the profits of the insurance company that sold the policy and are typically paid out on anannual basisover the life of the policy.

title: Participating Preferred Stock
definition: Participating preferred stock gives investors two ways to earndividends: a fixedpreferred dividendand a share of additional profits, in addition to common stock dividends. It gets paid before common stock but after any company debt. Companies sometimes use this type of stock in strategies like poison pills to protect against takeovers. Participating preferred stock offers benefits like extra dividends and priority in liquidation. Unlike nonparticipating preferred stock, which only pays the fixed dividend, participating stock allows investors to share in extra profits.

title: Participation Rate
definition: The labor force participation rate is an estimate of an economy’s active workforce. The U.S. government's labor force participation rate formula is the number of people ages 16 and older who are employed or actively seeking employment, divided by the total non-institutionalized, civilian working-age population.

title: Participatory Note
definition: Participatory notes, known as P-notes or PNs, enable investors orhedge fundsnot registered with theSecurities and Exchange Board of India(SEBI) to invest in Indian securities. These Offshore Derivative Instruments allow foreign investors to bypass direct registration, facilitating quick and anonymous market access. Despite their popularity, P-notes are controversial due to regulatory concerns about anonymity and unaccounted funds entering India.

title: Partnership
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Passive Activity Loss Rules
definition: Passive activity loss rules are tax regulations that limit a taxpayer's ability to use losses from passive activities to offset earned or ordinary income. Being materially involved with earned or ordinary income-producing activities means the income isactive incomeand may not be reduced by passive losses. Instead,passive lossescan only be used to offset passive income.

title: Passive Foreign Investment Company (PFIC)
definition: A passive foreign investment company (PFIC) is a corporation that's located abroad and is subject to strict and extremely complicated tax guidelines.

title: Passive Income
definition: Passive income is money earned from sources other than a traditional job, requiring little time or effort.

title: Passive Investing
definition: Passive investing is aninvestment strategythat aims to maximize returns in large part by minimizing the costs of buying and selling securities. Index investing is one common passive investing strategy. Using it, investors purchase the securities in a representative benchmark, such as the S&P 500 index, and hold them for a long time.

title: Understanding Past Due Loans: Penalties and Consequences
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Patent
definition: Patents are legal rights issued to inventors to protect their inventions from anyone else claiming them for a certain time.

title: PATH Act
definition: The Protecting Americans From Tax Hikes (PATH) Act of 2015 is an Obama-era law that expanded or renewed several tax credits for individuals, families, and businesses while implementing measures to prevent fraudulent claims for those credits. The act remains in force.

title: Path Dependency
definition: Path dependency explains the continued use of a product or practice based on historical preference or use. A company may persist in the use of a product or practice even if newer, more efficient alternatives are available. Path dependency occurs because it is often easier or more cost-effective to continue along an already set path than to create an entirely new one.

title: Patriot Act
definition: The Patriot Act, or USA PATRIOT Act, was passed shortly after the terrorist attacks in the United States that occurred on September 11, 2001, and gave law enforcement agencies broader powers to investigate, indict, and bring terrorists to justice. It also led to increased penalties for committing and supporting terrorist crimes.

title: Pattern Day Trader
definition: A pattern day trader (PDT) is a regulatory designation for those traders or investors who execute four or moreday tradesover the span of five business days using amargin account. The number of day trades must constitute more than 6% of the margin account's total trade activity during that five-business-day window.

title: Pay Yourself First
definition: "Pay yourself first" is a financial strategy that prioritizes an individual's savings. It helps people tackle their financial goals and build value for the future. Pay yourself first involves immediately saving a certain portion of money from any income you receive, before spending it. By committing to saving regularly, you can build a financial cushion to apply to your financial priorities. Pay yourself first could help you achieve long-term financial objectives like retirement or emergency savings. However, many people don't earn to earmark some portion of every paycheck for savings. They must spend it all on necessities. Keep in mind that you can still build savings by putting aside some money whenever possible.

title: Payable On Death (POD)
definition: Payable on death (POD) bypasses the probate process and ensures beneficiaries can access funds from an inherited bank account promptly.

title: Payback Period
definition: The payback period formula is often used by investors, consumers, and corporations to determine how long it will take the business to recover the initial expenses of an investment.

title: Payday Loan
definition: A payday loan is a short-term, high-cost loan, usually equal to the borrower's next paycheck.

title: Payee
definition: A payee is the party in a financial transaction that receives payment in exchange for goods or services. Payments to a payee can be made in various forms, such as cash, check, or electronic transfer.

title: Payment
definition: Payments involve the exchange of money, goods, or services as part of a predefined agreement, taking various forms such as cash, checks, wire transfers, credit and debit cards, or cryptocurrencies. In this guide, we explore the different types of payment methods available today, examining the benefits and drawbacks of each to equip you with the knowledge needed to make informed financial decisions.

title: Payment-in-Kind (PIK)
definition: Payment-in-kind (PIK) is the use of a good or service as payment instead of cash. It also refers to certain financial instruments.

title: Payout
definition: Payouts refer to thereturnsor disbursements from investments orannuities, provided as lump sums or periodic payments in either dollar amounts or percentages. They're also used in capital budgeting to indicate the payout period, the time it takes for an investment to recover its initial cost. Understanding payouts and payout ratios is crucial for assessing distributions to investors and guiding financial decisions.

title: Payout Ratio
definition: A company's payout ratio shows how much of its profits are given to shareholders via dividends.

title: PayPal
definition: PayPal is a leading online payment platform enabling money transfers and other financial transactions. Users connect their PayPal accounts with a checking account or credit card, or both, in order to facilitate their transactions online. Consumers, businesses, and merchants can all use PayPal, whether online or in stores. PayPal is known for providing its customers with a high degree of transaction security and convenience.

title: Payroll
definition: Payroll involves compensating employees within the framework of state and federal regulations, including the calculation of FICA and income taxes. Whether managed in-house or outsourced to specialized firms, payroll processing is a critical business function that impacts financial reporting and tax compliance. Understanding payroll is essential for effective financial management.

title: Payroll Tax
definition: Payroll taxes play a crucial role in funding essential government programs, such as Social Security and Medicare. Both employees and employers contribute to these taxes, which are automatically deducted from wages. While employees see these deductions on their pay stubs, employers handle additional taxes like unemployment insurance.

title: P/E 10 Ratio
definition: The P/E 10 ratio is a valuation indicator, commonly used with major equity indexes, factoring in real per-share earnings over a 10-year period. It is also known as the cyclically adjusted price earnings ratio.

title: Peer Group
definition: A peer group refers to a group of individuals or companies that share similar characteristics, such as age, education, ethnic background, size,industry, or sector. Peer groups are influential because members often shape one another's decisions, with hierarchies and leaders commonly emerging.

title: Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Economy
definition: A peer-to-peer (P2P) economy enables individuals to transact directly in a decentralized system based on personal ownership of production. Technology has expanded its reach, though hybrids like Uber and Airbnb blend P2P features with corporate structures. Its efficiency depends on factors such as transaction costs and economies of scale.

title: Peer-to-Peer Lending
definition: P2P lending connects individual borrowers with individual lenders (investors), bypassing traditional financial institutions. P2P lending is also referred to as "social lending" or "crowd lending." Obtaining better interest rates is a common motivation for borrowers in this market. For lenders, borrower defaults can be more common than for traditional lenders.Various P2P platformsfacilitate P2P lending, each with its own terms and risk assessments.

title: Pegging
definition: Currency pegging is when a country fixes its exchange rate to another currency, often the U.S. dollar, to maintain economic stability. This approach helps reduce the risk of currency fluctuations, making international trade and investment more predictable. By keeping exchange rates stable, currency pegging can boost trade and investor confidence, especially for smaller or developing economies. But it can also create challenges, such as trade deficits or limited control over domestic monetary policy, because the peg may require constant market intervention and large foreign currency reserves.

title: Pell Grant
definition: Federal Pell Grants are a type of student aid generally awarded to undergraduates who’ve demonstrated extraordinary financial need on their Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) forms. Unlike federalstudent loans, Pell Grants usually won’t require repayment.

title: Penetration Pricing Explained: Effective Strategies and Real-World Examples
definition: Penetration pricing is a strategicmarketingapproach where businesses offer new products or services at lower prices to capture market attention and pull customers from competitors. Tactics like offering a month of free access to services or no-fee banking for six months help grow market share, with the goal of retaining these customers as prices return to normal. This strategy boosts visibility and sales while allowing companies to scale efficiently over time.

title: Pennant
definition: A pennant chart pattern is a technical analysis continuation signal comprised of a flagpole and a consolidation period with converging trend lines, commonly lasting from one to three weeks. The initial strong move is paired with varying volume levels, and traders monitor for a breakout in the direction of the original trend. Understanding pennants alongside other technical indicators can optimize trading strategies and target successful entry points.

title: Penny Stock
definition: A penny stock typically refers to the stock of a small company that trades for less than $5 per share. Though some penny stocks trade on large exchanges such as theNew York Stock Exchange(NYSE), most trade via over-the-counter (OTC) transactions through the electronicOTC Bulletin Board(OTCBB) or through the privately-ownedOTC Markets Group.

title: Pension Plan
definition: A pension plan is a savings plan established by an employer that offers employees regular income payments during retirement.

title: Per Capita
definition: Per capita means the amount of something for each person in a group, and it is often used in statistics to compare things like income, spending, or resource use across different populations.

title: Per Capita GDP
definition: GDP per capita is a metric that indicates an economy's gross domestic product per person and is used as a measure of prosperity.

title: Per Stirpes
definition: Per stirpes is a legal term designating who inherits a beneficiary's portion of an estate or asset if that beneficiary dies before the testator who created the will.

title: Perceived Value
definition: Perceived value refers to the customer’s judgment about a product's worth, determined by its features, benefits, and overall appeal.

title: Percentage Change
definition: A percentage change represents the relative change in the value of an item.

title: Percentage of Completion Method
definition: The percentage of completion method is a widely usedaccounting practice, particularly in the construction industry, where revenue and expenses are recognized based on the project's progress. This approach stands in contrast to thecompleted contract method, deferring final results until a project is finished. By understanding this method, businesses can accurately report financial results throughout the duration of long-term contracts.

title: Perfect Competition
definition: Perfect competition, also known as pure competition, is an idealized market condition in which many sellers compete to offer the best prices, and large sellers have no advantages over smaller ones. Perfect competition rarely occurs in real-world markets, but it provides a useful framework for understanding how supply and demand influence prices and behavior in a market economy.

title: Performance Bonds
definition: A performance bond is a financial assurance provided to one party in a contract, ensuring against the other party's failure to fulfill obligations. Typically backed by a bank or insurance company, these bonds are primarily used in construction and real estate to guarantee project completion. If a contractor fails to meet contractual terms, the bond compensates the other party for damages, offering added security in contractual agreements.

title: Performance Budget
definition: A performance budget is a results-oriented method of budgeting that revolves around desired outcomes. The goals and objectives of the project are at the center of the budget, which reflects the input of resources and the output of services for each unit of an organization.

title: Performance Management
definition: Performance management is a set of practices that help managers monitor and evaluate employees' work. It helps align employee performance with organizational goals, and uses ongoing feedback and communication to set and maintain expectations.

title: Periodic Interest Rate
definition: A periodic interest rate is the rate applied to a loan or investment during each compounding period, influencing how quickly interest accumulates. While lenders often quote rates on an annual basis,interest usually compoundsmonthly or quarterly, which increases the effective rate over time.

title: Permanent Income Hypothesis
definition: The permanent income hypothesis is an economic theory that suggests consumers base their spending decisions on their average long-term income rather than their current income.

title: Permanent Life Insurance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Perpetual Bond
definition: A perpetual bond, also known as a "consol bond" or "perp," is a fixed-income security with no maturity date. It's often considered an equity, not a debt.

title: Perpetual Inventory
definition: A perpetual inventory system is a computerized system that continuously records inventory changes in real-time.
