This reduces or, in some cases, eliminates the need for physical inventory checks. Relying on data provided by electronic point-of-sale technology, it provides a highly detailed view of changes ininventoryand immediate reporting on the amount of inventory in stock.

title: Perpetuity
definition: A perpetuity is a financial instrument that provides continuous cash flow indefinitely without a predetermined end date.

title: Perseroan Terbatas (PT)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE)
definition: Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) is the value of goods and services purchased by or for U.S. consumers.

title: Personal Finance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Personal Financial Specialist (PFS)
definition: A Personal Financial Specialist (PFS) is a certification forcertified public accountants(CPAs) that allows them to expand their expertise and offerings to includefinancial planningandwealth management.

title: Personal Financial Statement
definition: A personal financial statement is a document that outlines an individual's financial position at a point in time based on their asset and liabilities. The statement typically includes general information about the individual such as their name and address along with a breakdown of their totalassetsandliabilities.

title: Personal Guarantee
definition: A personal guarantee is a promise that an individual will repaycreditissued to a business they lead or partner in. A personal guarantee means if the business can't repay itsdebt, the individual has to cover the balance personally. Personal guarantees offer extra protection to creditors who want assurance of repayment.

title: Personal Identification Number (PIN)
definition: A personal identification number (PIN) isa numerical code used in many electronic financial transactions. Personal identification numbers are usually issued in association with payment cards and may be required to complete a transaction. The purpose of a personal identification number (PIN) is to add additional security to the electronic transaction process.

title: Personal Income
definition: Personal income refers to allincomecollectively received by all individuals or households in a country. Personal income includes compensation from a number of sources, including salaries,wages, and bonuses received from employment or self-employment,dividendsanddistributionsreceived from investments, rental receipts from real estate investments, andprofit sharingfrom businesses.

title: Personal Lines Insurance
definition: Personal lines insurance is coverage designed to protect individuals and families from personal risks, such as death, injury, or loss of property. It plays a vital role in everyday life, making it possible to drive a car, own a home, or cover medical expenses without facing financial ruin. Common types of personal lines include auto, health, and homeowners' insurance. Coverage levels depend on factors like premiums, risk assessments, and the specific policies chosen.

title: Personal Property
definition: Personal property encompasses assets other thanreal estate, characterized as movable, including furniture, electronics, and digital assets. Unlike real estate, personal property is not fixed to a location, affecting itstaxationand insurance coverage. Learn how it plays a crucial role in homeowner insurance and valuation for loans.

title: Personal Service Corporation
definition: A personal service corporation (PSC) is a type of business entity recognized by theInternal Revenue Service (IRS)that's created to provide personal services directly to clients. These services typically include fields like accounting, health, law, consulting, engineering, and other specialized professions.

title: Personally Identifiable Information (PII)
definition: Personally identifiable information (PII) constitutes any data that can pinpoint an individual, either by itself or when combined with other information.

title: What Is PEST Analysis? Its Applications and Uses in Business
definition: A political, economic, social, and technological (PEST) analysis is a method used by businesses to assess external factors that influence their operations and competitive environment.

title: Peter Principle
definition: The Peter Principle states that the higher an employee advances in the hierarchy ladder, the more likely they are to reach a level of incompetence and fail in their new position.

title: Petrodollars
definition: Petrodollars are crude oil export revenues denominated in U.S. dollars.

title: Understanding Petroleum: Importance, Uses, and Investment Strategies
definition: Petroleum, or crude oil, is a vital fossil fuel formed by the decomposition of organic material over time. Essential for powering vehicles and machines and convertible into plastics, it significantly influencesglobal politicsand the economy. Understanding its extraction, uses, and investment methods can help individuals make informed financial decisions.

title: Understanding Petty Cash: Usage, Accounting, and Best Practices
definition: Petty cash refers to a small amount of cash that businesses keep on hand for convenient payments of minor expenses, like office supplies or small reimbursements, for which issuing a check or corporate credit card is impractical. Typically maintained in a secure location such as a locked box, petty cash ensures smooth and quick handling ofincidental expenseswithout disrupting accounting processes. Periodically, businesses reconcile petty cash funds to align transactions with financial statements, maintaining transparency and accountability. Understanding this process allows businesses to efficiently manage micro-expenses without compromising on internal controls.

title: Phantom Stock Plan
definition: A phantom stock plan is an employee benefit plan that gives select employees, typically executives, some of the benefits of stock ownership without transferring company stock.

title: Philanthropy
definition: Philanthropy is an effort an individual or organization undertakes based on an altruistic desire to improve human welfare. It refers to charitable acts or other good works, like efforts that help others or that contribute to the well-being of society overall on a grand scale.

title: Phillips Curve
definition: The Phillips curve suggests a stable, inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment, proposing that economic growth leads to inflation, which in turn reduces unemployment. This theory, introduced by economist William Phillips, was initially influential in shaping monetary policy. However, its validity faced challenges during the 1970s when stagflation—characterized by simultaneous high unemployment and inflation—emerged, sparking debate about its applicability in modern economic contexts.

title: Physical Capital
definition: Physical capital is one of the three mainfactors of production, consisting of tangible, human-made goods that aid in the process of creating a product or service. Examples include machinery, buildings, office or warehouse supplies, vehicles, and computers, which help businesses improve productivity and efficiency. Unlike human capital or natural resources, physical capital is owned and used by companies, making it vital for both startups and established businesses.

title: Pigovian Tax
definition: A Pigovian (also spelled Pigouvian) tax is a tax on market transactions that create negative externalities, or adverse side effects, for those that are not directly involved in the transaction.

title: PIIGS
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: PIMCO (Pacific Investment Management Co.)
definition: PIMCO, or the Pacific Investment Management Company, is an Americaninvestment managementfirm founded in 1971 in California. The firm focuses on fixed income and manages more than $2.2 trillion inassets. The firm primarily handles portfolio management, account management, and business management.

title: Pink Sheets
definition: "Pink sheets" is an outdated term for stocks that tradeover-the-counter(OTC) rather than on a public stock exchange in the U.S. These companies either cannot meet the requirements for listing on an exchange like the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) or prefer to trade over the counter.

title: Piotroski Score
definition: The Piotroski Score is a powerful tool for assessing a company's financial strength and potential as a value investment. Named after Joseph Piotroski, this score ranges from 0 to 9, with higher scores suggesting stronger financial health. It uses nine specific criteria derived from financial statements to distinguish between strong and weak investment opportunities, offering critical insights for investors.

title: Pip
definition: A price interest point, or pip, is one-hundredth of 1%, the smallest price movement possible for a currency being traded in the international currency markets.

title: Pitchbook
definition: A pitchbook is a strategic marketing tool used by financial services firms to present their capabilities and attract potential clients by showcasing their strengths and successful projects.

title: Pivot Point
definition: Pivot points help traders identify support and resistance levels by calculating the average of the previous period's high, low, and closing prices.

title: Plain Vanilla
definition: Plain vanilla is the most basic or standard version of a financial instrument, usually options, bonds, futures, and swaps. It is the opposite of an exotic instrument, which alters the components of a traditional financial instrument, resulting in a more complex security.

title: Planned Obsolescence
definition: Planned obsolescence is a strategy to make a product outdated or useless after a set period, boosting future consumer demand for replacements. Obsolescence happens by releasing a better model or designing a product to fail after a certain period, prompting consumers to prefer newer versions.

title: Platinum
definition: Platinum is a precious metal known for its rarity, strength, and uses in jewelry, electronics, and automobiles. First introduced to Europe in 1735, this silver-colored metal is primarily mined in South Africa and Russia. Platinum is highly durable and more resistant to tarnish than other metals like gold. Frequently used in catalytic converters in the auto industry, platinum also sees investment interest through futures contracts and exchange-traded funds (ETFs).

title: Platykurtic
definition: The term "platykurtic" refers to astatistical distributionin which the excess kurtosis value is negative. For this reason, a platykurtic distribution will have thinner tails than a normal distribution will, resulting in fewer extreme positive or negative events. The opposite of a platykurtic distribution is aleptokurtic distribution, in which excess kurtosis is positive.

title: Pledged Asset
definition: Lenders use a pledged asset to secure a debt or loan. Pledged assets can include cash, stocks, bonds, and other equity or securities that serve ascollateralheld by a lender in return for lending funds. Pledged assets can reduce the down payment that is typically required for a loan as well as reduce the interest rate charged.

title: Plowback Ratio
definition: The plowback ratio indicates the profit kept by a company after accounting for dividends paid.

title: What Is the Plunge Protection Team (PPT) and How Does It Operate?
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Point-and-Figure (P&F) Chart
definition: A point-and-figure chart plots price movements for stocks, bonds, commodities, orfutureswithout taking into consideration the passage of time.

title: Point of Purchase (POP)
definition: A point of purchase (POP) is the time and/or place that a retail transaction is made by a customer. Marketers and retailers use this term to describe a key touchpoint in the retail journey where they might aim to persuade the customer to complete their transaction or purchase additional products. Often, the POP is considered when planning the placement of consumer products, such as product displays strategically placed near the checkout of a grocery store aisle.

title: Point of Sale (POS)
definition: A point of sale (POS) system includes the hardware and software to process payments and complete purchases.

title: Poison Pill
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Poisson Distribution
definition: The Poisson distribution is a probability distribution that is used by investment analysts in stochastic modeling.

title: Political Action Committee (Super PAC)
definition: A political action committee (PAC) is a U.S. group that pools political donations and gives to candidates or causes under limits and disclosure rules. Created in 1944, PACs now include Super PACs, which can raise and spend unlimited funds but can't coordinate with campaigns. Both strongly influence modern U.S. elections.

title: Political Economy
definition: Political economy is a branch of social sciences that studies the relationship between politics and the economy and how each influences the other.

title: Political Risk
definition: Political risk is the risk an investment's returns could suffer as a result of political changes or instability in a country. Instability affectinginvestmentreturns could stem from a change in government, legislative bodies, other foreign policymakers or military control. Political risk is also known as "geopolitical risk," and becomes more of a factor as thetime horizonof investment gets longer. They are considered a type ofjurisdiction risk.

title: Ponzi Scheme
definition: A Ponzi scheme is a financial scam that sustains itself by shifting money between investors rather than generating real profits.

title: Pooled Funds
definition: Pooled funds are funds in a portfolio from many individual investors that are aggregated for the purposes of investment. Mutual funds, hedge funds, exchange-traded funds,pension funds, and unit investment trusts are all examples of professionally managed pooled funds.

title: Population Statistics
definition: Population is a statistical term that designates the pool from which a sample is drawn for a study. Any selection grouped by a common feature can be considered a population. A sample is a statistically significant portion of a population.

title: Pork Barrel Politics
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Porter Diamond
definition: The Porter Diamond Theory of National Advantage, or the Porter Diamond Model, is a model that describes thecompetitive advantagethat nations or groups possess based on factors available to them. The theory explains how governments can act to improve a country's position in a globally competitive economic environment.

title: Porter's 5 Forces
definition: Porter’s Five Forces are used to identify and analyze the forces that shape the competitive nature and intensity of a market or industry.

title: Portfolio Investment
definition: A portfolio investment is ownership of a stock, bond, or other financial asset with the expectation that it will earn a return or grow in value over time.

title: Portfolio Management
definition: Portfolio management is the art of selecting and overseeing a group of investments that meet the long-term financial objectives and risk tolerance of a client, a company, or an institution.

title: Portfolio Manager
definition: The term portfolio manager refers to a financial professional who makes investment decisions for individual and/or institutional investors. Portfolio managers develop and implementinvestment strategiesand manage the day-to-day trading of a portfolio. These professionals manage assets for individual investors or institutional funds likemutual funds. Investors should look at a portfolio manager's track record before investing.

title: Portfolio of Financial Assets
definition: A financial portfolio is a basket made up of positions in various investments that work together to serve an investor’s goals.

title: Portfolio Runoff
definition: Portfolio runoff means assets with a finite term are not replaced as they mature.

title: Portfolio Turnover
definition: Portfolio turnover measures how often a fund's assets are bought and sold over time. By dividing the total new securities purchased or securities sold by the fund'snet asset value, investors can gauge how actively a fund is managed. Understanding turnover is crucial for evaluating potential fees, tax implications, and investment strategies.

title: Portfolio Variance
definition: Portfoliovarianceevaluates the risk reflected by fluctuations in the combinedactual returnsof securities within aportfolio. It relies on thestandard deviationsand correlations between each asset in the portfolio. A crucial aspect of modern portfolio management involves leveraging these metrics to optimize risk and return, emphasizing strategic asset selection based on theircorrelationcoefficients.

title: Position Trader
definition: A position trader buys investments expecting long-term value growth, often holding these assets for an extended period. Unlike day traders who act on short-term market movements, position traders focus on trends likely to continue over time. This strategy results in fewer trades, often fewer than 10 annually.

title: Positive Correlation
definition: A positive correlation is a statistical term used to describe a situation when two variables move in the same direction.

title: Positive Economics
definition: Positive economics examines economic phenomena based on factual data, aiming to describe and explain real-world occurrences without prescribing specific solutions or outcomes.

title: Positive Pay
definition: Positive pay is a vital banking tool for businesses aiming to prevent check fraud. This automated system matches issuedcheckswith those presented for payment, allowing companies to verify the authenticity of each transaction.

title: Understanding Post-Money Valuation: Key Concepts and Examples
definition: Post-money valuation is a company's estimated worth after outside financing and/or capital injections are added to itsbalance sheet. Post-money valuation refers to the approximate market value given to a start-up after a round of financing from venture capitalists orangel investorshave been completed.

title: Post-Trade Processing
definition: Post-trade processing is crucial in ensuring the accuracy and completion of a financial transaction. After a trade is executed, the buyer and seller verify trade details, approve the transaction, transfer ownership, and arrange payment forsecurities. This process helps eliminate errors, particularly in non-standardized markets like OTC, where trades don't rely on centralized clearinghouses.

title: Understanding Poverty: Definition, Causes, and Measurement
definition: Poverty is a socioeconomic condition where individuals, families, or communities cannot achieve a minimumstandard of livingdue to insufficient financial resources and essentials. The inability to meet basic human needs like housing, clean water, food, and medical care defines this condition.

title: Poverty Trap
definition: A poverty trap refers to the self-reinforcing cycle that keeps individuals or communities inpovertyby requiring significant capital to escape. Without access to necessary resources like education, healthcare, and financial services, this cycle perpetuates, leaving those affected with limited opportunities to improve their economic status. By understanding the role of public and private investments, effective strategies can be developed to dismantle these traps and foster economic progress.

title: Power Distance Index (PDI)
definition: The Power-Distance Index (PDI), developed by psychologist Geert Hofstede, evaluates how power and wealth are accepted and distributed within nations, businesses, or cultures. A high PDI score reflects strict hierarchies, while a low score indicates more interaction and influence among authority figures and subordinates.

title: Power of Attorney
definition: A power of attorney (POA) is a legal authorization that allows someone to make decisions or take actions on behalf of another person in financial, legal, or medical matters.

title: Pre-Foreclosure
definition: Pre-foreclosure is the first stage of the foreclosure process, triggered when a lender files a notice of default after repeated missed mortgage payments. During this period, homeowners are warned that legal action may proceed, but they still have options, including negotiating with the lender, making back payments, or pursuing a short sale to avoid foreclosure.

title: Pre-IPO
definition: A pre-initial public offering (IPO) placement involves the private sale of large share blocks before a stock is publicly listed. This strategic move helps young companies raise funds and mitigate risks associated with their market debut. Typically, institutional investors, likeprivate equityfirms andhedge funds, purchase these shares at a discount. Although risky, this discount compensates for potential uncertainties. Pre-IPO placements, mostly restricted to high-net-worth individuals, often prevent quick sales with lock-up periods, ensuring stable stock performance initially.

title: Pre-Market
definition: Pre-market trading allows investors to trade stocks from 4 a.m. to 9:30 a.m. EST, providing an early chance to react to overnight news and market events before the regular session begins. Many investors and traders watch the pre-market trading activity to judge the strength and direction of the market in anticipation of the regular trading session.

title: Predatory Pricing: Definition, Example, and Why It's Used
definition: Predatory pricing is the illegal business practice of setting prices for a product unrealistically low to eliminate the competition.

title: Preemptive Rights
definition: Preemptive rights give a shareholder the opportunity to buy additional shares in any future issue of a company's common stock before the shares are made available to the general public. These rights usually apply only toearly investorsand offer them a first option to purchase additional shares. Preemptive rights help maintain investment value and protect ownership percentage in a company. As a result, they're sometimes referred to as an anti-dilution provision. In the United States, the law does not require preemptive rights for all common shareholders. In the European Union and the UK, it does.

title: Preference Shares
definition: Preference shares, often calledpreferred stock, are company shares with dividends paid to shareholders before common stock dividends. There are four main categories of preferred shares: cumulative, non-cumulative, participating, and convertible, each with different dividend rights and conversion options.

title: Preferred Dividend
definition: Preferred dividends are made to shareholders of a company'spreferred stock. Unlike commondividends, they are usually fixed and must be paid beforecommon shareholders. This gives preferred shareholders stability, but the fixed rate can be a downside because it doesn’t adjust for inflation. In this article, we explain how preferred dividends work, how they’re calculated, and show you key factors you should consider as an investor.

title: Preferred Provider Organizations (PPO)
definition: A PPO is ahealth insuranceplan offering flexibility in choosing healthcare providers. PPO plans typically cover services both in and out of network without needing a primary doctor or referrals for specialists.PPOs provide comprehensive healthcare coverage, appealing to those who value accessible and varied healthcare options.

title: Preferred Stock
definition: Preferred stock is a class of shares that give the holder a higher claim to dividends or asset distribution than common stockholders.

title: Understanding Premiums in Finance: Definitions, Types, and Examples
definition: "Premium" has several meanings in finance. Most commonly, it refers to the difference in the intrinsic or theoretical value of a security and the higher price at which it is being traded (in contrast to adiscount). Assets might be traded at a premium if market participants feel it is undervalued or if there is an increase in demand.

title: Premium Bond
definition: A premium bond is a bond trading above its face value, or in other words; it costs more than the face amount on the bond. A bond might trade at apremiumbecause its interest rate is higher than current rates in the market. These bonds are different from a type of lottery bond account sold in the United Kingdom that is also called apremium bond,

title: Prepaid Credit Card
definition: A prepaid debit card lets you spend only the amount loaded onto it, working more like a reloadable gift card than a standard debit or credit card. It isn't tied to a bank account and may include fees for activation, reloading, or ATM use, but it can be helpful for budgeting or travel. Comparing fees and features is important since options differ widely.

title: Prepaid Expense
definition: A prepaid expense occurs when a company pays in advance for goods or services it will receive in the future. These payments are recorded as assets on a company's balance sheet until the benefit of the good or service is realized.

title: Prepayment
definition: A prepayment is the satisfaction of a debt before its official due date. It can apply to goods, services, or loans.

title: Prepayment Penalty
definition: Aprepaymentpenalty is usually specified in a clause in a mortgage contract stating that a penalty will be assessed if the borrower significantly pays down or pays off the mortgage before term, usually within the first three years of committing to the loan. The penalty is sometimes based on a percentage of the remainingmortgage balance, or it can be a certain number of months’ worth of interest. Prepayment penalties protect the lender against the financial loss of interest income that would otherwise have been paid over time.

title: Prepayment Risk
definition: Prepayment risk is the potential financial downside faced by investors when a borrower decides to repay the principal of a fixed-income security, like a bond, earlier than planned. This risk can significantly affect those holding investments such as callable bonds and mortgage-backed securities because it disrupts expected cash flow and may force reinvestment at lower prevailing rates. Investors face the challenge of adjusting to changing interest rate environments and may experience reduced returns due to unexpected principal returns.

title: Present Value
definition: Present value, an estimate of the current value of a future sum of money, is calculated by investors to compare the probable benefits of various investment choices.

title: Present Value Interest Factor (PVIF)
definition: The present value interest factor (PVIF) is a formula used to estimate the current worth of a sum of money that is to be received at a future date, reflecting the time value of money. PVIF helps investors and analysts evaluate future cash flows or annuity payments by discounting them to their present value. PVIFs are often presented in tables showing values for different time periods and interest rate combinations for quick reference.

title: Present Value Interest Factor of Annuity (PVIFA)
definition: The present value interest factor of an annuity (PVIFA) is a calculation that can help an investor entering into an annuity contract to determine whether it is more advantageous to take a lump-sum payment or to accept a series of payments over time.

title: Present Value of an Annuity
definition: The present value of an annuity is the current value of future payments from an annuity, given a specified rate of return, ordiscount rate. Understanding this concept is important for making informed financial decisions, such as comparing lump-sum options versus annuity payments.

title: Preservation of Capital
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Press Conference
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Pretax Earnings
definition: Pretax earnings are a company's income after all expenses (including operating expenses, interest, and depreciation) have been deducted from total sales or revenues, but before income taxes have been subtracted. Because pretax earnings exclude taxes, this measure enables analysts to compare company profitability across industries or geographic regions where corporate taxes differ.

title: Pretax Profit Margin
definition: The pretax profit margin measures a company'soperating efficiencyand is calculated before taxes are paid. It shows how much profit is made per dollar of sales. As a tool, it makes industry comparisons more accurate by excluding tax expenses because these can vary for companies. Higher pretax profit margins indicate better management and efficiency. As is the case with many metrics, it's best used when comparing companies in the same industries.

title: Price Action
definition: Price action is the change in an investment's price over time plotted on a chart.

title: Price Ceiling
definition: A price ceiling is the maximum amount a seller is permitted to charge for a product or service. It is usually set by law and is typically applied to staples such as food and energy products when they become too expensive for average consumers.

title: Price Controls
definition: Price controls are government-imposed limits on prices for goods and services, typically set through price floors and ceilings. They are designed to maintain affordability, control inflation, and stabilize key sectors of the economy. While price controls can promote economic stability, they may also create market imbalances like shortages or surpluses if prices move away from natural market levels. Price controls date back to ancient civilizations and have been used during modern events like wartime economies and inflationary periods. They are often applied to essential consumer goods, such as food, fuel, and housing, to protect consumers and maintain social stability.

title: Price Discovery
definition: Price discovery happens when buyers and sellers come together to agree on an asset price. It happens by considering factors likesupply and demand, risk, and economic conditions. This process is central to the market and trading, and is based on the point where supply and demand meet. Unlike valuation, which uses models to estimate worth, price discovery reflects the actual price agreed upon in the market.

title: Price Discrimination
definition: Price discrimination is a selling strategy that charges customers different prices for the same product or service. The seller charges each customer the maximum price they'll pay in pure price discrimination. For example, discounts for students or senior citizens are examples of price discrimination.

title: Price/Earnings-to-Growth (PEG) Ratio
definition: The price/earnings-to-growth (PEG) ratio is a valuation metric that adjusts traditional earnings multiples by factoring in a company's expected growth rate.

title: Price Elasticity of Demand
definition: Price elasticity of demand is a concept in economics that describes the relationship between a product's change in quantity demanded and a price increase, expressed as a ratio.

title: Price Fixing
definition: Fixing is the practice of setting the price of a product rather than allowing it to be determined by free-market forces. Fixing a price is illegal if it involves collusion among producers or suppliers. The Sherman Act prohibit collaborations among competitors that restrain trade or fix prices.

title: Price Leadership
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Price Level
definition: Price level is the average of current prices across the entire spectrum of goods and services produced in an economy. In more general terms, price level refers to the price or cost of a good, service, or security in the economy.

title: Price Rate of Change Indicator (ROC)
definition: The Rate of Change (ROC) is a momentum indicator that measures the speed at which prices are changing by determining the percentage change over a specified time period.

title: Price Sensitivity
definition: Price sensitivity is the degree to which the price of a product affects consumers' purchasing behaviors. Generally speaking, it's howdemandchanges with the change in the cost of products.

title: Price Skimming
definition: Price skimming is often used when a new product type enters the market, boosting the price at the onset with the intent of reducing it later.

title: Price Stickiness
definition: Price stickiness occurs when market prices resist change despite shifts in economic factors, impacting efficiency.

title: Price-Taker
definition: A price taker is a market participant that must follow existing price levels in a competitive market because they lack the power to influence pricing on their own terms.

title: Price Target
definition: Price targets play a crucial role in financial markets, offering investors and traders a projection of where a security's price might head. As an analyst's estimation of a stock's future value, a price target serves as a vital tool in investment decision-making. Analysts determine these targets by evaluating a company's current valuation, historical performance, and potential earnings, aiming to predict the stock's worth over the next 12 to 18 months.

title: Price-to-Book Ratio (P/B Ratio)
definition: The price-to-book (P/B) ratio is a financial metric that compares a company’s market value to its book value, total assets minus total liabilities, and is often used to identify potentially undervalued stocks.

title: Price-to-Cash Flow Ratio
definition: The Price-to-Cash Flow (P/CF) Ratio offers investors a way to measure a stock's market value against itsoperating cashgeneration. This useful metric factors in non-cash expenses to present a clearer financial picture, particularly valuable in analyzing companies with largenon-cash charges. It serves as a potentially more reliable alternative to the P/E ratio, as cash flows can be less manipulated than earnings figures.

title: Price-to-Earnings Ratio (P/E Ratio)
definition: The price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio compares a company's share price with its earnings per share (EPS). Analysts and investors use this to determine the relative value of a company's shares in side-by-side comparisons.

title: Price to Free Cash Flow
definition: Price to free cash flow (P/FCF) is an equityvaluationmetric that compares a company's per-sharemarket priceto itsfree cash flow(FCF). This metric is very similar to the valuation metric of price to cash flow , but is considered a more exact measure because it uses free cash flow, which subtracts capital expenditures (CAPEX) from a company's totaloperating cash flow, thereby reflecting the actual cash flow available to fund non-asset-related growth.

title: Price-to-Rent Ratio
definition: The price-to-rent ratio compares home prices to annual rent, helping to determine if it's cheaper to rent or buy. This ratio is used as a benchmark for estimating whether it's cheaper to rent or own property. The price-to-rent ratio indicates if housing markets are fairly valued or in a bubble.

title: Price-to-Sales (P/S)
definition: The price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is a powerful tool for investors aiming to evaluate stock value by measuring the relationship between a company's stock price and its revenue. By calculating the P/S ratio, investors can identify undervalued stocks and make informed investment decisions within the same sector. This article explains how to analyze the P/S ratio, calculate it, and compare it with strategies like the enterprise value-to-sales ratio for a comprehensive financial analysis.

title: Price to Tangible Book Value (PTBV)
definition: The price to tangible book value (PTBV) ratio measures a company’s market value relative to its hard or tangible assets. The tangible book value number includes all of a company’s physical assets, such as its inventory, equipment, and real estate. The PTBV is applicable mainly to industrial or capital-intensive companies that own substantial hard assets.

title: Price Value of a Basis Point (PVBP)
definition: Price value of a basis point (PVBP) measures the sensitivity of bond prices to interest rates. It represents the expected change in a bond's price if the yield increases by one basis point, or 0.01%.

title: Price-Weighted Index
definition: A price-weighted index averages the share prices of included companies, giving greater influence to higher-priced stocks. Familiar examples include the DJIA and Nikkei 225, offering insights into stock market trends. A stock with a higher price will be given more weight than a stock with a lower price and will thus have a greater influence on the index's performance.

title: Prima Facie
definition: Prima facie is a Latin expression used in law to refer to a case that has merit when taken at face value.

title: Primary Market
definition: The primary market is where securities are initially created and sold during a primary distribution before further trading takes place on the secondary market.

title: Prime Brokerage
definition: Prime brokerage is a premium bundle that some financial institutions offer their biggest and most active trading clients such as hedge funds and money managers.

title: Prime Cost
definition: Prime costs are the direct expenses associated with a business’s production, and include materials and labor.

title: Prime Rate
definition: The prime rate is the interest rate banks charge their most trusted borrowers. Like all loan rates, the prime interest rate is derived from the federal funds' overnight rate, set by the Federal Reserve at meetings held eight times a year. The prime interest rate is thebenchmarkbanks and other lenders use when setting their interest rates for every category of loan from credit cards to car loans and mortgages.

title: Principal
definition: Principal in a loan refers to the initial amount of money borrowed, excluding any interest, fees, and charges.

title: Principal-Agent Problem
definition: The principal-agent problem is a conflict in priorities between a person or group of people and the representative authorized to act on their behalf. Anagentmay act in a way that is contrary to the best interests of theprincipal.

title: Principal-Agent Relationship: What It Is, How It Works, and New Developments
definition: The principal-agent relationship is a legal, fiduciary relationship in which one party takes on responsibility for acting on behalf of another.

title: Principal, Interest, Taxes, Insurance (PITI)
definition: Principal, interest, taxes, and insurance (PITI) are the components of amortgagepayment. Specifically, they are the principal amount, loan interest, property tax, homeowners insurance, and private mortgage insurance premiums.

title: Prisoner's Dilemma
definition: The prisoner's dilemma is a concept in game theory that describes how individuals in a group act in their own best interests instead of the group's best interests.

title: Private Banking
definition: Private banking consists of personalized financial services and products offered to thehigh-net-worth individual (HNWI)clients of a retail bank or other financial institution. It includes a wide range ofwealth managementservices, and all provided under one roof. Services include investing andportfolio management, tax services, insurance, and trust andestate planning.

title: Private Company
definition: A private company is a business owned by private individuals or groups rather than the public. It can issue stock, but not through public exchanges. It also faces fewer regulatory requirements compared to public companies. Private companies often take forms like sole proprietorships, partnerships, or limited liability companies (LLCs). Being private offers advantages like less regulation and greater control for owners, but it can also make raising capital more challenging. Examples range from small family-owned businesses to large influential companies like Cargill or Koch Industries.

title: Private Equity
definition: Private equity is an investment class where firms raise capital to acquire and manage private companies or take public companies private, with the goal of ultimately selling them for a profit. These investments typically require significant capital commitments over several years.

title: Understanding Private Equity Real Estate: Investment and Returns Explained
definition: Private equity real estate is an alternative investment class involved in real estate markets. It involves acquiring, financing, and owning properties through investment funds. Because it requires substantial capital and long-term commitment, it's typically seen as an investment for high-net-worth individuals. Private equity real estate differs from publicly-tradedequity REITs, whose revenues mainly come from the income of rental properties that are part of a REIT's holdings.

title: Private Finance Initiative
definition: A private finance initiative (PFI) is a system whereby public projects are financed by private companies, which alleviates immediate financial burdens on the government. With a PFI, a private company handles a project's up-front costs and the government authority pays the private company back over the long term. Typical projects funded by PFIs include infrastructure and public facilities. PFIs are used extensively in the UK and Australia. In the United States, they're calledpublic-private partnerships (PPPs).

title: Private Good
definition: A private good is a tangible item whose use by one individual prevents others from using it due to itsrivalrousand excludable nature. Unlikepublic goods, which can be accessed by all without limiting another's use, private goods must be purchased, ensuring exclusive consumption by the buyer. Everyday items like restaurant meals and electronics are common examples.

title: Private Investment Fund
definition: A private investment fund is an exclusive investment vehicle open only to accredited or qualified investors, such as hedge funds and private equity funds. These funds operate under exemptions from theInvestment Company Act of 1940, usually3C1or3C7, which free them from many public fund regulations.

title: Private Investment in Public Equity (PIPE)
definition: Private Investment in Public Equity (PIPE) sees institutional or accredited investors purchasing shares directly from public companies, offering a faster capital-raising alternative to secondary offerings. While companies benefit from reduced regulatory hurdles, selling shares at a discount impacts share valuation and can dilute existing equity.

title: Private Placement
definition: A private placement involves selling stock shares directly to chosen investors and institutions instead of the open market. It is an alternative to an initial public offering (IPO) for a young company seeking to raise money to expand.

title: Private Sector
definition: The privatesectoris the part of the economy run by individuals and companies for profit and is not state-controlled. Therefore, it encompasses all for-profit businesses not owned or operated by the government, ranging from sole proprietorships to multinational companies. Private sector businesses tend to flourish more in capitalist and free-market economies because they are not subjected to the same level of government oversight and control as in other types of economies.

title: Privatization
definition: Privatization involves transferring ownership of government-operated businesses, properties, or operations to private entities. Additionally, privatization can describe the process of apublicly tradedcompany transitioning into a privately held entity, often called corporate privatization.

title: Privileged Communication
definition: Privileged communication is a legal concept that ensures confidentiality between parties in certain relationships, such as attorney-client or doctor-patient. These interactions, recognized by law, remain confidential unless exceptions apply. Legal limitations can also restrict disclosure by the parties involved.

title: Pro Forma
definition: Pro forma financial statements are hypothetical views that reflect "what-if" scenarios and may act as a company's planning tool.

title: Pro Forma Invoice
definition: A pro forma invoice is a detailed confirmation of an order, but not a demand for payment, sent by a seller to a buyer before or with a delivery of goods.

title: Pro Rata
definition: Pro rata is a Latin term that translates to "in proportion." Put simply, it is used to describe a proportionate allocation. It's a process in which an allocated asset is distributed in equal portions. An amount is assigned to one person according to their share of the whole if something is distributed to several people on a pro rata basis. A pro rata calculation is often used in business finance but can also determine the appropriate portions of any given whole.

title: Probability Density Function (PDF)
definition: The probability density function (PDF) is an analytical technique that outlines how likely different outcomes are across a range of possibilities. In this function, the probability is the percentage of a dataset's distribution that falls between two criteria. PDF is commonly used byfinancial analyststo understand how returns are distributed to evaluate the risk and expectations of investment prices and returns.

title: Probability Distribution
definition: A probability distribution displays on a graph the statistical likelihood of every possible outcome that could occur within a specific time period.

title: Probate
definition: Probate is the process completed when adecedentleaves assets to distribute, such as bank accounts, real estate, and financial investments. Probate is the general administration of a deceased person'swillor the estate of a deceased person without a will.

title: Probate Court
definition: Probate court oversees the execution of wills, estate management, and conservatorships, ensuring that deceased individuals' debts are settled and assets distributed. It's important to understand probate processes as they determine the legitimacy of wills, appoint estate executors, and manage asset distribution, especially when no will exists. Familiarize yourself with how courts handle both contested documents and intestate succession.

title: Procurement
definition: Procurement is the process of buying or otherwise obtaining goods or services, typically for business or government purposes and usually on a relatively large scale. Often confused with purchasing, procurement represents a more strategic and less purely transactional process.

title: Procyclic
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Producer Price Index (PPI)
definition: The Producer Price Index quantifies deflation and inflation and is a strong indicator of the health of the economy.

title: Producer Surplus
definition: A producer surplus represents the benefit producers gain from selling a product or service at a price that is higher than they're willing to accept.

title: Product Differentiation
definition: Product differentiation is a strategic process that makes a company's products stand out by highlighting unique attributes, building brand loyalty, and increasing sales in a crowded marketplace. By employing various tactics like innovative packaging and targeted advertising, businesses create a competitive edge, allowing even smaller companies to compete against industry giants.

title: Product Life Cycles
definition: The product life cycle is the duration a product is available to customers, starting with its market introduction and ending when it's removed from shelves.

title: Product Line
definition: A product line represents numerous products with similar characteristics, functions, prices, and customers.

title: Product Portfolio
definition: A productportfoliois the collection of all the products or services offered by a company, each with a different growth rate and market share.Product portfolio analysis can provide nuanced views on a stock type, company growth prospects, profit margin drivers, income contributions, market leadership, and operational risk. This is essential forinvestors conducting equity researchor analysts supporting internal corporate financial planning.

title: Production Costs
definition: When businesses create a product or supply a service to customers, they incur production costs.

title: Production Efficiency
definition: Production efficiency is attained when a firm optimally utilizes its resources, ensuring that increasing the production of one item does not necessitate decreasing output of another.

title: Production Possibility Frontier (PPF)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Productivity
definition: Productivity affects corporate profits and shareholder returns and relies on individuals or organizations to maximize output.

title: Profit
definition: Profit refers to a company's surplus revenue after accounting for its costs and expenses during a certain period, such as a quarter or fiscal year.

title: Profit and Loss Statement (P&L)
definition: The profit and loss statement is a snapshot of a company's profitability, usually for a quarter or fiscal year.

title: Profit Before Tax (PBT)
definition: Profit before tax (PBT) serves as a crucial indicator of a company's profitability before income taxes are applied. It is calculated from the operating profit after deducting interest expenses and adding any interest income. This measurement aids financial analysts and stakeholders in understanding a company's pre-tax financial health and can be found in theincome statement.

title: Profit Centers
definition: A profit center is a part of a company that generates revenue and contributes to its profit. It operates like a standalone business, with managers who make pricing and expense decisions to maximize earnings. Unlike cost centers, which track costs and don’t make money, profit centers are responsible for both revenue and profits. Examples of profit centers include specific divisions or product lines at companies like Walmart and Microsoft.

title: Profit Margin
definition: Profit margin is the percentage by which a company’s revenue exceeds its costs of operation.

title: Profit-Sharing Plan
definition: A profit-sharing plan is a company benefit that involves distributing a portion of the company's profits to its employees in the form of cash or stock. This can be offered in the form of a company retirement plan known as a deferred profit-sharing plan (DPSP).

title: Profits Interest
definition: Profits interest is a form ofequity compensationthat grants individuals a share in apartnership'sfuture profits without requiring them to invest capital.

title: What Is the Profitability Index (PI)?
definition: The profitability index (PI) compares the expected cash flows from a project with its initial costs. The PI, known as the value investment ratio (VIR) or profit investment ratio (PIR), represents the relationship between the costs and benefits of a proposed project. Businesses use PI to determine if a proposed project will be a worthwhile investment.

title: Profitability Ratios
definition: A profitability ratio is a financial metric that assesses a company's ability to earn a profit relative to its assets, equity, and sales.

title: Program Evaluation Review Technique (PERT)
definition: A program evaluation review technique (PERT) chart is aproject managementtool that allows users to map out a project's timeline and itemize individual tasks. A PERT chart allows users to communicate project instructions as well as set schedules and timelines.

title: Progress Billings
definition: Progress billings areinvoicesissued at set stages of a large project for work completed to date. They are widely used in industries with high costs, such as construction and aerospace, to support steady cash flow and manage expenses. Clear agreements between clients and contractors on billing terms are needed for this process to run smoothly.

title: Progressive Tax
definition: A progressive tax is a tax rate that moves from lower for those who earn smaller taxable incomes to higher for larger earners.

title: Project Finance
definition: Project finance is funding for long-term infrastructure, industrial projects, and public services. It uses a nonrecourse or limited-recoursefinancial structure. In addition, it relies on a project's cash flows to repay the debt and equity used to finance the project. Project finance, which is commonly used in the energy and telecommunications sectors, benefits companies by keeping debtoff their balance sheets.

title: Project Management
definition: Project management is the organization of a company's resources to move a specific task toward completion.

title: Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO)
definition: A projected benefit obligation (PBO) is an actuarial measurement of what a company will need at the present time to cover future pensionliabilities. This measurement is used to determine how much must be paid into a defined benefit pension plan to satisfy all pension entitlements that have been earned by employees up to that date, adjusted for expected future salary increases.

title: What is Promissory Estoppel? Key Requirements and Examples
definition: Promissory estoppel is a legal principle where a promise can be enforced by law if the promisee relies on it and suffers harm, even without formal consideration. Promissoryestoppelprevents the promisor from arguing against the enforcement of a promise.

title: Promissory Note
definition: A promissory note is a written promise to pay a specific amount of money, either on demand or at a certain date in the future. It's more structured than an IOU and less rigid than a formal loan agreement. It reflects crucial details like the principal amount, interest rate, maturity date, and repayment plan, clearly delineating the financial obligations between the issuer and the payee. By offering flexibility, promissory notes provide diverse opportunities for lenders beyond traditionalbanking institutions.

title: Promoter
definition: A stock promoter is an individual or organization that raises money for investment activities. For example, stock promoters may raise money for a company by offeringinvestment vehiclesother than traditional stocks and bonds, such as limited partnerships and direct investment activities. Stock promoters are not required to have official licensing, which may lead to potential misinformation. Engaging in activities heralded by stock promoters involves risk, such as false promises and investment scams, especially in unregulated markets. Investors should be sure to conduct their own due diligence to avoid falling victim to misleading promotion tactics.

title: Promotion
definition: Promotions can apply to people, products, orfinancial instruments. A promotion for an employee means advancing in rank or position within a company or organization. In marketing, a promotion is designed to increase customer attention and product sales through advertising or a discounted price. Stock promotions are deployed to push the sale of stock shares and can sometimes be fraudulent.

title: Proof of Funds (POF): What It Is, Qualifying Documents, and How to Obtain
definition: Proof of Funds (POF) is essential for significant transactions like home purchases. It assures sellers and lenders of a buyer's financial capability, ensuring funds are valid and accessible. Generally originating from verified sources such as banks, POF must include details like bank name, balance amounts, and authorized signatures. Liquid assets are crucial, as investments like retirement accounts often don't qualify. Sellers and lenders check POF for down payments and closing costs, ensuring funds aren't merely promised but tangible.

title: Understanding Proof-of-Stake: How PoS Transforms Cryptocurrency
definition: Proof-of-Stake (PoS) revolutionizes the way cryptocurrencies process transactions and build new blocks by utilizing a consensus mechanism that relies on randomly selected validators, rather than the computationally intensive process seen in Proof-of-Work (PoW) systems.

title: Understanding Proof of Work (PoW) in Blockchain: Key Mechanism Explained
definition: Proof of Work (PoW) is a foundational consensus mechanism in blockchain technology. It requires substantial computational effort to validate transactions, ensuring the security and integrity of the blockchain network without reliance on a trusted third party. Initially popularized by Bitcoin, PoW underpins many cryptocurrencies by facilitating secure peer-to-peer transaction processing.

title: Property
definition: Property refers to any asset, whether tangible or intangible, that an individual or business legally owns, granting them specific, legally protected rights to access, manage, or pass on those assets.

title: Property Insurance
definition: Property insurance is a broad term for a series of policies that provide either property protection orliability coveragefor property owners. Property insurance provides financial reimbursement to the owner or renter of a structure and its contents in case of damage or theft—and to a person other than the owner or renter if that person is injured on the property.

title: Property Management
definition: Property management is the daily oversight of residential, commercial, or industrial real estate by a third-party contractor. Generally,property managerstake responsibility for day-to-day repairs and ongoing maintenance, security, and upkeep of properties.

title: Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E)
definition: PP&E assets are fixed, tangible business assets that likely can't be converted to cash within a year.

title: Property Rights
definition: Property rights are the rules and limitations associated with ownership of a property, including tangible and intangible assets. That includes holding on to it, selling or renting it out for profit, or transferring it to another party. Well-defined property rights are essential to market efficiency and economic transactions.

title: Property Tax
definition: Property tax is a charge levied by a government for real estate or tangible personal property.

title: Property Tax Deduction
definition: The federal property tax deduction allows property owners to deduct state and local property taxes from their taxable income. Deductible real estate taxes include any state, local, or foreign taxes that are levied for the general public welfare. They do not include taxes charged for home renovations or for services like trash collection.

title: Proportional Tax
definition: In a proportional tax system, everyone pays the same tax rate regardless of their income. Also called aflat tax, there's a single rate for all earners, making it straightforward and predictable. It's not likeprogressiveormarginaltax systems, which increase rates as income rises. Supporters argue it simplifies tax filing and treats all taxpayers equally, while critics say it can disproportionately impact lower-income individuals. Countries like Estonia, Russia, and Hong Kong use proportional taxes, sparking debates about fairness, economic growth, and revenue generation.

title: Proprietary Trading
definition: A proprietary trading firm invests its own capital to conduct self-promoting financial transactions.

title: Proration
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Prospect Theory: What It Is and How It Works, With Examples
definition: Prospect theory holds that people tend to place a greater weight on the potential for loss than the probability of gain when choosing among options.

title: Prospectus
definition: A prospectus is a written document used in finance to inform the public of the relevant details about an offering of securities, such as stocks,bonds, andmutual funds. The prospectus is part of a company's registration statement, which must be filed with theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

title: Protectionism
definition: Protectionism involves government strategies designed to limit international trade to bolster domestic industries. These policies aim to enhance economic activity within a domestic market and address concerns related to safety and product quality. While protectionism is debated, its supporters claim it fosters job creation and boosts GDP by shielding homegrown businesses from global competition.

title: Protective Put
definition: A protective put is a versatile risk-management strategy for investors wanting to protect their investments against potential stock or asset losses. By purchasing aput option, investors can hedge against downturns while retaining the possibility of profiting from upward movements, making it an essential tool in an investor's portfolio.

title: Provident Fund: Definition, How It Works for Retirement
definition: A provident fund is a government-managed retirement savings scheme used primarily in Asia and Africa.

title: Provision
definition: A contract provision is a stipulation within acontract, legal document, or law. A contract provision often requires action by a specific date or within a specified period of time. Contract provisions are intended to protect the interests of one or both parties in a contract.

title: Provision for Credit Losses Explained: Uses and Examples
definition: The provision for credit losses (PCL) is a crucial element in a company's financial statements, reflecting expected losses fromcredit risk, such as delinquent or defaulting accounts. Companies use PCL to safeguard against overstating their assets, ensuring that balance sheets represent a more accurate financial position. This article provides insight into how companies estimate these potential losses, the financial reporting involved, and offers a practical example of PCL in action.

title: Proxy
definition: A proxy is an agent legally authorized to act on behalf of another party. It can also refer to a format that allows an investor to vote without being physically present at a meeting.Shareholdersnot attending a company’sannual general meeting (AGM)may vote their shares by proxy by allowing someone else to cast votes on their behalf, or they may vote by mail, phone, or over the internet.

title: Proxy Fight
definition: A proxy fight refers to the act of a group of shareholders joining forces and attempting to gather enough shareholder proxy votes to win a corporate vote. Sometimes referred to as a "proxy battle,” this action is mainly used in corporate takeovers.

title: Proxy Statement
definition: A proxy statement is a document containing the information theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC)requires companies to provide to shareholders so they can make informed decisions about matters that will be brought up at an annual or special stockholder meeting. Issues covered in a proxy statement can include proposals for new additions to theboard of directors, information on directors' salaries, information on bonus and options plans for directors, and any declarations made by the company's management.

title: Proxy Vote
definition: The term proxy vote refers to a ballot cast by a single person or firm on behalf of a corporation's shareholder who may not be able to attend ashareholder meeting, or who may not choose to vote on a particular issue. Shareholders receive a proxy ballot in the mail along with an information booklet called aproxy statement, which describes the issues to be voted on during the meeting. Shareholders vote on a variety of issues including the election of board members,mergeror acquisition approvals, or approving a stock compensation plan.

title: Public Company
definition: A public company is a corporation whose ownership shares can be bought and sold on stock exchanges.

title: Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB)
definition: The Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB) is a non-profit organization that regulates auditors of publicly traded companies. The purpose of the PCAOB is to minimizeaudit risk. In particular, the PCAOB oversees the audits of public companies, brokers, and dealers registered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)

title: Public Good
definition: A public good is a commodity or service that every member of a society can use without reducing its availability to all others. Typically, a public good is provided by a government and funded through taxes.

title: Public Limited Company (PLC)
definition: A public limited company (PLC) is a type of public company that's allowed to offer its shares to the public and is listed on a stock exchange. PLC is the equivalent of a U.S. publicly traded company that carries the Inc. or corporation designation.

title: Public-Private Partnerships
definition: Public-private partnerships (PPPs) involve collaboration between a government agency and a private-sector company. They can be used tofinance, build, and operate projects, such as public transportation networks, parks, and convention centers.

title: Pullback: What It Means in Trading, With Examples
definition: A pullback is a temporary dip in an otherwise rising stock price, often seen by investors as an opportunity to buy at a discount before the price continues upward.

title: Pump-and-Dump Scheme
definition: A pump-and-dump scheme involves spreading false information to inflate a stock’s price for illicit profit.

title: Pump Priming
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Purchase Annual Percentage Rate (APR)
definition: A purchaseannual percentage rate(APR) is the interest rate that your credit card issuer will charge you on purchases when you carry a balance on your card. In addition to purchase APRs, credit cards can have different APRs for cash advances and balance transfers. They may also have introductory APRs for a certain period after you sign up and penalty APRs for missing payments.

title: Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI)
definition: The Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) highlights monthly supply and demand trends.

title: Purchase Money Security Interest (PMSI)
definition: A purchase money security interest (PMSI) gives lenders payback priority in case of a loan default. Specifically, if the borrower defaults, a lender gets the right to repossess the property bought with the loan they made or to demand repayment in cash. The lender's claim is senior to those that may be made by other creditors. Lenders must file a UCC-1 financing statement to establish a PMSI.

title: Purchase-Money Mortgage
definition: A purchase-money mortgage is a home loan where the seller finances the buyer’s purchase directly, rather than a traditional lender. Buyers often choose this option when they can’t qualify for conventional financing or need better financing terms. This arrangement can benefit both parties; buyers get financing, while sellers may earn more interest and steady cash flow. Both sides should clearly understand the loan terms, interest rates, and repayment conditions before finalizing the agreement.

title: Understanding Purchase Price in Finance and Its Impact on Capital Gains
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Purchasing Power
definition: Purchasing power refers to how much money you can buy with a unit of currency; essentially, a measure of what your money is worth in terms of goods and services.

title: Pure Play
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Pure Risk
definition: Pure risk is a category ofriskthat cannot be controlled and has two outcomes: complete loss or no loss at all. There are no opportunities for gain or profit when pure risk is involved.

title: Push Down Accounting
definition: Pushdown accounting is a bookkeeping method used by companies to record the purchase of another company. The acquirer’s accounting basis is used to prepare thefinancial statementsof the purchased entity. In the process, theassetsandliabilitiesof the target company are updated to reflect the purchase cost rather than the historical cost.

title: Put
definition: A put option is a contract in the options market that gives its owner the right but not the obligation to sell an asset for a certain price by a set date.

title: Put-Call Parity
definition: Put-call parity is a pricing theory that defines the relationship between European put and call option prices within the same class of options.

title: Put-Call Ratio
definition: The put-call ratio is an indicator of whether investors as a whole are placing more bets on prices falling or rising. It's a crucial yet underplayed measure of market sentiment, especially compared with better-known metrics like the Cboe's Volatility Index (VIX).

title: Put Option
definition: Put options are derivatives that give their holders the right, but not the obligation, to sell an underlying asset (e.g., a stock, currency, bond, commodity, future, or index) at a set price by a pre-established date.

title: Pyramid Scheme
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Q
definition: On the Nasdaq exchange, a company's ticker symbol sometimes included a letter Q at the end to denote that the company was undergoingbankruptcyproceedings. This identifier, signifying "bankrupt," provided a clear signal to investors about the financial status of the issuer. While Nasdaq has since replaced this system with a broader Financial Status Indicator covering various compliance issues, the use of "Q" remains a historical reference point for understanding how financial distress was communicated in the stock market.

title: Q Ratio (Tobin's Q)
definition: The Q ratio, also known as Tobin's Q, measures the relationship betweenmarket valuationandintrinsic value. In other words, it estimates whether a business or market is overvalued orundervalued.

title: Qatar Investment Authority (QIA)
definition: The Qatar Investment Authority (QIA) is a government-owned entity charged with managing thesovereign wealth fund(SWF) of Qatar. QIA’s mission is to invest, manage and grow Qatar’s reserves in order to support the development of Qatar’s economy.

title: Qatari Riyal (QAR)
definition: QAR is thecurrency codefor the Qatari riyal, the currency of the State of Qatar which is located along the coast of the Arabian Peninsula. The Qatari riyal is made up of 100 dirhams. The abbreviation for the currency is QR, in English. Riyal is also frequently referred to as rial. All Qatar notes and coins are issued by the Qatar Central Bank whose objectives include monetary stability and regulatory control of the currency.

title: QQQQ
definition: QQQQ was the original ticker symbol for the Invesco QQQ Trust, anETFthat trades on the Nasdaq exchange. This security offered broad exposure to the tech sector by tracking theNasdaq 100 Index, which excludes all but a few financial services stocks. The ticker QQQQ has been replaced with its current ticker symbol, QQQ, which it adopted in March 2011.

title: QSEHRA
definition: If a small employer does not offer group health insurance coverage, it may provide a qualified small employer health reimbursement arrangement (QSEHRA) instead.

title: Qstick Indicator
definition: Note:Although this account has "money market" in its name, it offers no check-writing privileges and instead operates like a savings account.

title: Qtum
definition: Qtum is a cryptocurrency that combines Ethereum’s smart contract functionality with the security of Bitcoin’s unspent transaction output model (UTXO) to create a platform that is suitable for adoption by large organizations.Qtum was founded in 2016 by Patrick Dai, Jordan Earls, and Neil Mahl, and itsinitial coin offering (ICO)was held in March 2017.

title: Quadrix
definition: Quadrix is a stock rating system that uses over 90 variables in seven major categories to determine the value of a stock. The Quadrix system is produced and maintained by Horizon Publishing Company. The seven major categories are momentum, quality, value, financial strength, earnings estimates, performance, and reversion. The overall score for a particular stock is determined by a weighted average of all 90 variables.

title: Quadruple Witching
definition: The quadruple witching hour is the last sixty minutes of the trading day on the third Friday of March, June, September, and December, when contracts for stock index futures, stock index options, stock options, and single stock futures expire simultaneously.

title: Qualification Ratio
definition: Qualifying ratios are percentage measures lenders use to assess loan eligibility and terms by comparing a borrower's debt obligations to income. Common examples include the debt-to-income ratio and the housing expense ratio, reviewed alongside credit scores, with general targets of about 36% or less for total debt and 28% or less for housing costs.

title: Qualified Adoption Expenses (QAE)
definition: Qualified adoption expenses are the necessary costs paid to adopt a child younger than 18 years of age or any disabled person who requires care. In the United States, qualified adoption expenses (QAE) are those expenses that theInternal Revenue Service(IRS) defines as reasonable and necessary and can be used to claim an adoption credit or exclusion that reduces the adopting parents' taxable income.

title: Qualified Annuity
definition: A qualified annuity is a retirement savings plan that isfunded with pre-tax dollars. A non-qualified annuity is funded with post-tax dollars.

title: Qualified Appraisal
definition: A qualified appraisal is an appraisal that meets the requirements set forth by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) and is conducted by a qualified appraiser. Qualified appraisals are made no earlier than 60 days before a piece of property is donated.An appraisal is a professional assessment of a property's or asset's market value, typically required for insurance, taxation, or mortgage purposes.

title: Qualified Automatic Contribution Arrangements (QACAs)
definition: A qualified automatic contribution arrangement (QACA) is a rule established under thePension Protection Actof 2006 to increase worker participation in self-funded retirement plans by enrolling employees automatically unless they opt out.

title: Qualified Charitable Organization
definition: Qualified charitable organizations arenonprofit organizations that qualify for tax-exempt status, according to theInternal Revenue Service (IRS). They must be operated exclusively for one of the following purposes: religious, charitable, scientific, literary, educational, testing for public safety, the prevention of cruelty to animals or children, or the development of amateur sports. In the United States, a qualified charitable organization is known as a501(c)(3) organization, referring to the portion of theInternal Revenue Code (IRC)that deals with the tax treatment of nonprofits.

title: Qualified Disclaimer
definition: A qualified disclaimer is a legal tool within the U.S. tax code that allows an individual to refuse inheritance or gifts without facing tax consequences, provided specific conditions are met. This process enables beneficiaries to avoid accepting property that might lead to unfavorable tax burdens, such as estate and gift taxes. Key requirements include making the disclaimer in writing and within a specified time frame. Qualified disclaimers offer a strategic advantage in managing potential tax liabilities related to estate transfers.

title: Qualified Distribution
definition: A qualified distribution is a withdrawal from a retirement account, such as a401(k)or IRA, that meets specific IRS criteria to avoid taxes and penalties. Generally, withdrawals made after age 59½ qualify, though Roth IRAs require the account to be at least five years old for tax-free treatment.

title: Qualified Dividend
definition: A qualified dividend is an ordinary dividend, but it is reported to the IRS and taxed at capital gains tax rates.

title: Qualified Domestic Institutional Investor (QDII)
definition: A qualified domestic institutional investor or QDII is aninstitutional investorthat has met certain qualifications to invest in securities outside of their home country. Institutional investors can be organizations or groups of investors that have a significant amount of money available to invest. QDII programs enable large domestic investors to invest in securities in foreign markets. Examples of institutional investors that might seek to become a QDII include insurance companies, banks, funds, and investment companies.

title: Qualified Domestic Relations Order (QDRO)
definition: A qualified domestic relations order (QDRO) is a legal document typically found in a divorce agreement. It recognizes that a spouse, former spouse, child, or other dependent is entitled to receive a predefined portion of the account owner’s retirement plan assets.

title: Qualified Domestic Trust (QDOT)
definition: A qualified domestic trust (QDOT) allows taxpayers who are not U.S. citizens to claim the full marital estate tax deduction on assets held within the trust.

title: Qualified Electric Vehicle
definition: The term qualified electric vehicle refers to a plug-in electric passenger vehicle or light truck that allows the owner to claim anonrefundable tax creditafter purchase. These vehicles must have at least four wheels, be designed for use in the public, and not used for commercial purposes. The vehicle must be powered primarily by an electric motor that draws its charge from rechargeable batteries or fuel cells. The vehicle must be driven almost exclusively in the U.S.

title: Qualified Eligible Participant (QEP)
definition: A qualified eligible participant (QEP) is an individual who meets the requirements to trade in sophisticated investment funds such as futures and hedge funds. These requirements are defined by Rule 4.7 of the Commodity Exchange Act (CEA). We'll tell you how to qualify as a QEP and the benefits that come with it.

title: Qualified Exchange Accommodation Arrangements
definition: A qualified exchange accommodation arrangement is a tax strategy where a third party, known as the accommodation party, temporarily holds a real estate investor's relinquished or replacement property. Qualified exchange accommodation arrangements, while still subjecting investors to strict guidelines for the sale and purchase of like-kind properties, increase flexibility in the timing of sales, and simplify qualifications for the tax deferral.

title: Qualified Foreign Institutional Investor (QFII)
definition: The Qualified Foreign Institutional Investor (QFII) is a program that allows specified licensed international investors to participate in mainland China's stock exchanges.

title: Qualified Higher Education Expense
definition: A qualified higher education expense (QHEE), as defined by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), is an expenditure for the costs of higher education that is eligible for a tax credit.
