title: Qualified Institutional Buyer (QIB)
definition: A qualified institutional buyer (QIB) is a class of investor that can be safely assumed to be a sophisticated investor and, therefore, does not require the regulatory protection afforded to investors under the Securities Act's registration provisions. Broadly, QIBs are institutional investors who manage or own at least $100 million in securities.

title: Qualified Institutional Placement (QIP)
definition: Qualified institutional placements (QIPs) provide Indian-listed companies with a faster, more cost-efficient way to raise capital by bypassing extensive pre-issue regulatory filings. Introduced by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI), QIPs aim to reduce Indian companies' reliance on foreign investments. By leveraging domestic markets, QIPs facilitate quicker access to funds while balancing regulatory oversight with operational flexibility.

title: Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity (QJSA)
definition: A qualified joint and survivor annuity (QJSA) provides a lifetime payment to an annuitant and spouse, child, or dependent from a qualified plan. QJSA rules apply tomoney-purchase pension plans, defined benefit plans, and target benefits. They can also apply to profit-sharing and 401(k) and 403(b) plans, but only if so elected under the plan.

title: Qualified Longevity Annuity Contract (QLAC)
definition: QLAC stands for qualified longevity annuity contract. It's adeferred annuityfunded with an investment from aqualified retirement planor anindividual retirement account (IRA). A QLAC allows funds in a qualified retirement plan, such as a 401(k), a 403(b), or an IRA, to be converted into an annuity.

title: Qualified Mortgage
definition: A qualified mortgage is a mortgage that meets certain requirements for lender protection and secondary market trading under theDodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act, a significant piece of financial reform legislation passed in 2010.

title: Qualified Opinion
definition: A qualified opinion is a statement issued in anauditor’s reportthat accompanies a company’s audited financial statements. It is anauditor’s opinionthat suggests the financial information provided by a company was limited in scope or there was a material issue with regard to the application ofgenerally accepted accounting principles (GAAP)—but one that is not pervasive.

title: Qualified Personal Residence Trust (QPRT)
definition: A qualified personal residence trust (QPRT) is a type ofirrevocable trustthat allows its creator to remove a personal home from theirestatein order to lower the amount ofgift taxthey must pay when they transfer assets to abeneficiary.

title: Qualified Pre-Retirement Survivor Annuity (QPSA)
definition: A qualified pre-retirement survivor annuity (QPSA) is a death benefit that is paid to the surviving spouse of a deceased employee. If the employee dies before retirement, the qualified pre-retirement survivor annuity is paid to offer compensation to the surviving spouse for the loss of retirement benefits that would have otherwise been paid to the employee. As the name implies, QPSAs are paid only in the case ofqualified plans.

title: Qualified Production Activities Income (QPAI)
definition: Qualified Production Activities Income (QPAI) is the portion of income derived from domestic manufacturing and production that qualifies for reduced taxation. More specifically, qualified production activities income is the difference between the manufacturer's domestic gross receipts and aggregate cost of goods and services related to producing domestic goods.﻿﻿ The tax-deductibility of QPAI is intended to reward manufacturers for producing goods domestically instead of overseas.

title: Qualified Professional Asset Manager (QPAM)
definition: A qualified professional asset manager (QPAM) is a registered investment adviser (RIA) who assists institutions with financial investments.

title: Qualified Reservist
definition: A qualified reservist is a member of the military reserve who is not active, but when called to duty, is eligible to make an early withdrawal from a retirement account without incurring the usual early distribution penalty.

title: Qualified Retirement Plan
definition: A qualified retirement plan is a savings plan that meets IRS andERISArequirements. It offers investors tax benefits like deductions andtax-deferredgrowth. There are two main types of qualified retirement plans: defined benefit plans, like traditional pensions, and defined contribution plans, such as 401(k)s. These plans are important retirement planning tools because they help people build financial security for the future.

title: Qualified Special Representative Agreement (QSR)
definition: The Qualified Special Representative Agreement (QSR) is an agreement between broker-dealers to clear trades without interacting with the Nasdaq ACT system. The QSR agreement allows one broker-dealer to send trades directly to the National Securities Clearing Corporation on behalf of another broker-dealer. This method of clearing trades provides simpler processing, lower transaction costs, and extended trading hours.

title: Qualified Terminable Interest Property (QTIP) Trust
definition: A qualified terminable interest property (QTIP) trust is a legal document that protects an individual's assets on behalf of the surviving spouse while maintaining control over how the assets are distributed once the surviving spouse dies.

title: Qualified Trust
definition: A qualified trust is a type of tax-advantaged financial plan established by employers for their employees. It includes stock bonus, pension, and profit-sharing plans regulated by IRS standards. These trusts offer significant tax benefits and comply with strict IRS guidelines outlined in Section 401(a) of the Internal Revenue Code.

title: Qualified Widow or Widower
definition: A qualified widow or widower is a surviving spouse who may file an individual tax return using the married filing jointly tax rates and standard deduction for up to two tax years.

title: Qualifying Annuity
definition: A qualifying annuity is similar to any other annuity, except the IRS has approved it for use within aQualified Retirement Planorindividual retirement account(IRA). These annuities can be fixed, indexed, or variable depending upon the plan sponsor's investment objectives. According toEmployee Retirement Income Security Act(ERISA) guidelines, contributions made into a qualifying annuity are tax-deductible unless the plan or annuity has aRothfeature.﻿﻿﻿﻿

title: Qualifying Disposition
definition: Qualifying disposition refers to a sale, transfer, or exchange of stock that qualifies for favorable tax treatment. Individuals typically acquire this type of stock through an incentive stock option (ISO) or through a qualifiedemployee stock purchase plan (ESPP). A qualified ESPP needs shareholder approval to start, and all members must have equal rights.

title: Qualifying Event
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Qualifying Investment
definition: A qualifying investment refers to an investment purchased withpretax income, usually in the form of a contribution to a retirement plan. Funds used to purchase qualified investments do not become subject to taxation until theinvestorwithdraws them.

title: Qualifying Ratios
definition: Qualifying ratios are percentage measures lenders use to assess loan eligibility and terms by comparing a borrower's debt obligations to income. Common examples include the debt-to-income ratio and the housing expense ratio, reviewed alongside credit scores, with general targets of about 36% or less for total debt and 28% or less for housing costs.

title: Qualifying Relative
definition: A qualifying relative, designated by theInternal Revenue Service (IRS), can be claimed as adependentby a taxpayer, assuming thetaxpayerprovided considerable financial support for the relative during the tax year.

title: Qualifying Transaction
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Qualifying Widow/Widower
definition: The federal qualifying widow or widower tax filing status is available for two years for widows and widowers (surviving spouses) withdependentsafter their spouse’s death. The surviving spouse may file jointly with the deceased spouse for thetax yearin which the spouse has died, and they can claim the standard deduction for a married couple filing jointly.

title: Qualitative Analysis
definition: Qualitative analysis explores a company’s nonnumerical data, helping it understand its value or prospects.

title: Quality Control
definition: Quality control is a process employed by businesses to ensure that their products are manufactured to meet defined quality standards.

title: Quality Control Charts
definition: A quality control chart is a graphic tool that tracks whether sampled products or processes meet specifications and how much they deviate when they don't. It helps spot process problems, correct errors, and maintain consistent quality. Common types include x-bar and R charts, and firms use them to set process controls and train staff.

title: Quality Management
definition: Quality management is the act of overseeing all activities and tasks that must be accomplished to maintain a desired level of excellence. This includes the determination of a quality policy, creating and implementing quality planning and assurance, and quality control and quality improvement. It is also referred to astotal quality management (TQM).

title: Quality of Life
definition: Quality of Life is a highly subjective measure of happiness that is an important component of many financial decisions.

title: Quality of Earnings
definition: A company’s quality of earnings is revealed by dismissing anyanomalies, accounting tricks, or one-time events that may skew the real bottom-line numbers on performance. Once these are removed, theearningsthat are derived from higher sales or lower costs can be seen clearly.

title: Quality Spread Differential (QSD)
definition: Quality spread differential (QSD) is used to calculate the difference between marketinterest ratesthat two parties potentially entering into aninterest rate swapare able to achieve. It is a measurement that companies can use to gaugecounterparty riskin an interest rate swap.

title: Quant Fund
definition: A quant fund is aninvestment fundthat usesquantitative analysisfor its investment selection process. It relies more on algorithmic, data-driven strategies than human judgment. Such non-traditional and passive funds use customized software models for investment decisions.The history of quant funds is rooted in decades-old quantitative analysis principles, with notable early influences from influential works like "Security Analysis" by Benjamin Graham and David Dodd. Supporters of quant funds believe that using computer programs reduces risks and losses compared to humanfund managers.Yet they carry unique risks, such as high trading costs and the potential for failures due to unpredictable market events or excessive reliance on historical data

title: Quantity-Adjusting Option (Quanto Option)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Quantitative Analysis (QA)
definition: Quantitative analysis (QA) uses methods to understand financial markets and make informed investment or trading decisions. It involves the use of mathematical and statistical techniques to analyze financial data.Quantitative analysts, often called "quants," examine past stock prices and earnings to forecast market trends.

title: Quantitative Easing
definition: Quantitative easing (QE) is a powerful tool used by central banks, such as the U.S. Federal Reserve, to stimulate economic activity when traditional monetary policy options become ineffective. By purchasing securities in the open market, QE aims to lower interest rates and boost the money supply, providing banks with additional liquidity. This increased liquidity encourages lending and investment, thereby supporting economic growth.

title: Quantitative Easing 2 (QE2)
definition: QE2 was the Federal Reserve's second round ofquantitative easing, announced in November 2010 to support the U.S. economy after the 2008 financial crisis and Great Recession. It involved $600 billion in U.S. Treasuries purchases plus reinvestment from earliermortgage-backed securities, launched with unemployment near 9.8% and rates near zero to expand the money supply, support liquidity, and lift low inflation.

title: Quantitative Trading
definition: Quantitative trading leverages mathematical models and extensive data to identify market opportunities. Originally used by financial institutions and hedge funds, these sophisticated strategies are now also accessible to individual investors seeking a structured, emotionally neutral approach to trading.

title: Quantity Demanded
definition: Quantity demanded is a term used in economics to describe the total amount of a good or service that consumers demand over a given interval of time. It depends on the price of a good or service in a marketplace regardless of whether that market is inequilibrium.

title: Quantity Discount
definition: A quantity discount is an incentive offered to a buyer that results in a decreasedcost per unitof goods or materials when purchased in greater numbers. A quantity discount is often offered by sellers to entice customers to purchase in larger quantities.

title: Quantity Supplied
definition: In economics, quantity supplied describes the number of goods or services that suppliers will produce and sell at a givenmarket price. The quantity supplied differs from the actual amount of supply (the total supply) as price changes influence how much supply producers actually put on the market. How supply changes in response to changes in prices is called the priceelasticityof supply.

title: Quantity Theory of Money
definition: The Quantity Theory of Money links price variations to changes in the money supply, forming an essential part of economic theories like monetarism. Commonly illustrated by the Fisher Equation, this theory helps explain inflation, providing valuable insights for both novice and expert investors. Understanding this concept is crucial for anyone looking to grasp the broader implications of economic dynamics in relation to money supply.

title: Quanto Swap
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Quantum Computing
definition: Quantum computing, a cutting-edge realm of computer science, leverages quantum theory to revolutionize how complex computational problems are tackled. Unlike classical computers, which use electrical impulses in a binary manner (1s and 0s) to process information, quantum computers employ quantum bits, or qubits. These qubits harness subatomic particles—like electrons or photons—to exist in multiple states simultaneously, vastly enhancing computational capabilities. This allows quantum computers to perform calculations that classical computers would find insurmountable within a feasible timeframe.

title: Quarter on Quarter (QOQ)
definition: Quarter on quarter (QOQ) growth measures the percentage change in a company's performance from one fiscalquarterto the next. This metric helps investors and analysts track short-term growth, understand trends, and make informed investment decisions. By comparing sequential quarters, companies can adjust strategies and enhance financial forecasting. Explore the calculation method, real-world examples, and the benefits and challenges of QOQ analysis to get a comprehensive understanding of company performance.

title: Quarter over Quarter (Q/Q)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Quarter (Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4)
definition: A fiscal quarter consists of financial reports submitted and dividends paid by companies every three months of a financial year. It’s like a report card for companies that comes out four times over a fiscal year.

title: Quarter-to-Date (QTD)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Quarterly Income Preferred Securities (QUIPS)
definition: Preferred stock is a class of shares that give the holder a higher claim to dividends or asset distribution than common stockholders.

title: Quarterly Revenue Growth
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Quartile
definition: Quartiles are values that split lists of datasets into quarters, resulting in lower, middle, and upper segments.

title: Quasi Contract
definition: A quasi contract, also known as an implied-in-law contract, is a court-forged agreement between two parties who had no prior formal contract.

title: Quasi-Public Corporation
definition: A quasi-public corporation is a private company with the government backing to carry out a public mandate, often with partial funding and a focus on public services.

title: Quasi-Reorganization
definition: A quasi-reorganization is a relatively obscure provision undergenerally accepted accounting principles(GAAP), which states that under certain circumstances, a firm may eliminate a deficit in itsretained earningsaccount by restating assets, liabilities, and equity in a manner similar to a bankruptcy. A firm's stockholders must agree to allow the accounting change, which essentially resets the firm's books as though a new company had incurred the assets and liabilities of the old firm.

title: Queuing Theory
definition: Queuing theory is a branch of mathematics that focuses on understanding and optimizing queue efficiency. It examines how lines form, function, and sometimes malfunction. Queuing theory examines every component of waiting in line, including the arrival process and the number ofcustomersamong others, which might be people, data packets, cars, or telecommunications.

title: Quick Assets
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Quick Liquidity Ratio
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Quick Ratio
definition: The quick ratio evaluates a company's ability to meet its current obligations using its most liquid assets.

title: Quick Response (QR) Code
definition: A quick response (QR) code is a kind of matrix barcode that utilizes black squares on a white background to encode information, which can be read by imaging devices.

title: Quick-Rinse Bankruptcy
definition: A quick-rinse bankruptcy is abankruptcyproceeding that is structured to move through legal proceedings faster than the average bankruptcy in an effort to resolve financial distress within companies. All parties involved negotiate terms before a company files for bankruptcy, avoiding prolonged and expensive court proceedings and potentially preserving customer relationships, financing sources, and supplier partnerships.

title: Quid
definition: Quid is an informal name that references the British pound sterling.

title: Quid Pro Quo
definition: Quid pro quois a Latin term for "something for something" that originated in the Middle Ages in Europe. It describes a situation when two parties mutually agree to exchange goods or services reciprocally. In a quid pro quo agreement, one transfer is thus contingent upon some transfer from the other party.

title: Quid Pro Quo Contribution
definition: Manynonprofitfoundations and organizations rely oncharitable donationsfrom corporate sponsors and individuals to meet a large part of their funding needs. These can be solicited in many different forms, from one-off contributions to ongoing sponsorships from businesses in the community.

title: Quiet Period
definition: Before a company’sinitial public offering(IPO), the quiet period is a period of time when management teams or their marketing agents are prohibited from makingforecastsor expressing any opinions about the value of their company. Mandated by the U.S.Securities and Exchange Commission(SEC), there are strict consequences for violations, including legal actions. There is an exception for emerging growth companies, as analysts are allowed to publish reports after the initial earnings release within 25 days of the IPO. For publicly-tradedstocks, the four weeks before a business quarter ends is also called a quiet period.

title: Quiet Title
definition: A quiet title action is a legal action to establish clear ownership ofreal estatewhen ownership is disputed or unclear. This process ensures that there are noliens, levies, or disputes over the property's ownership. It provides a clear title to the rightful owner. Once declared quiet, the title is secure against future ownership challenges.

title: Quiet Title Action
definition: A quiet title action is a legal tool used to resolve disputes over property ownership. This type of lawsuit is particularly useful in clearing uptitlediscrepancies, ensuring that rightful ownership is established. After a quiet title action concludes, the plaintiff and their heirs gainabsolute titlepossession, safeguarding against future claims from third parties. This procedure often becomes necessary in cases involving mortgage lender disagreements, death of property owners, adverse possession, or when a property has stood unoccupied for extended periods.

title: Quintiles
definition: Quintiles are a method of dividing a data set into five equal parts to facilitate statistical analysis and comparison.

title: Quitclaim Deed
definition: A quitclaim deed is a legal document that transfers a person’s interest in apropertyto another party. However, it doesn't explain the ownership of the property and doesn't guarantee the validity of the person's rights.

title: Quorums
definition: A quorum is the minimum number of people an organization requires for official meetings to be valid, reflecting proper representation in decision-making. By understanding quorum requirements, businesses can foster robust governance and efficient meetings. Strategies like clear scheduling and flexible attendance options help maintain quorum, ensuring crucial decisions are not delayed.

title: Quota
definition: A quota is a government-enforced measure designed to limit the number or value of goods a nation can import or export during a set timeframe. Quotas are used to balancetrade, bolster domestic industries, and control the flow of goods between countries. These limits can be applied for various reasons, including protecting domestic production and addressing quality and safety concerns of imported products.

title: Quota Share Treaty
definition: In a quota share treaty, both the insurer and reinsurer proportionally divide risks, premiums, and claims, fostering mutual responsibility and financial balance.

title: Quotation
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Quote
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Quote Currency
definition: In foreign exchange (Forex), the quote currency, also known as thecounter currency, is the second currency in both a direct and indirectcurrency pair. The quote currency is used to determine the value of the base currency. The quote currency is listed after the base currency in the pair when currency exchange rates are quoted.

title: Quote-Driven Market
definition: A quote-driven market is an electronicstock exchangesystem in which prices are determined from bid and ask quotations made by market makers,dealers, or specialists. In a quote-driven market, also known as a price-driven market, dealers fill orders from their own inventory or by matching them with other orders. A quote-driven market is theopposite of an order-driven market, which displays individual investors' bid and ask prices and the number ofsharesthey want to trade.

title: Quote Stuffing
definition: Quote stuffing is a trading practice where a large number of buy and sell orders are quickly placed, then canceled. The goal is to slow down trading systems or confuse other traders by flooding the market with activity. This can make it harder for others to see real supply and demand. Quote stuffing has drawn attention because it may contribute to market instability, including events like the 2010 Flash Crash, when U.S. stock markets briefly dropped before recovering.

title: Quoted Price
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: R-Squared
definition: R-squared (R2) is defined as a number that tells you how well the independent variable(s) in a statistical model explains the variation in the dependent variable. It ranges from 0 to 1, where 1 indicates a perfect fit of the model to the data.

title: Rabbi Trust
definition: A rabbi trust is a non-qualified employee trust created by employers to support non-qualified benefit obligations to employees.

title: Race to the Bottom
definition: The race to the bottom refers to a competitive situation where a company, state, or nation attempts to undercut the competition's prices by sacrificing quality standards or worker safety (often defying regulation), or reducing labor costs. A race to the bottom can also be between governments to attract industry or tax revenues. For example, a jurisdiction may relax regulations or cut taxes and compromise the public good in an attempt to attract investment, such as the building of a new factory or corporate office.

title: Racketeering
definition: Racketeering involves ongoing criminal activity conducted by an organized enterprise, rather than isolated illegal acts.

title: Rally
definition: A rally is a sharp, short-term rise in asset prices that often follows a period of stagnation or decline. Rallies can occur in bothbulland bear markets; during downturns, they're known asbear market rallies.

title: Ramp Up
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Random Variables
definition: A random variable is one whose value is unknown or a function that assigns values to each of an experiment’s outcomes. Random variables are often designated by letters and can be classified asdiscreteor continuous. Discrete variables have specific values. Continuous variables can have any values within a continuous range.

title: Random Walk Theory
definition: Random walk theory implies that past price action has little or no influence on future changes in stock prices.

title: Range
definition: Range refers to the difference between the low and high prices for a security or index over a specific time. It defines the difference between the highest and lowest prices traded for a defined period, such as a day, a month, or a year. The range is marked on charts for a single trading period as the high and low points on a candlestick orbar.

title: Ratchet Effect
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Rate-and-Term Refinance
definition: A rate-and-term refinance is a type of mortgage loan refinancing that results in a lower interest rate or loan term or a combination of both, reducing monthly mortgage payments. A rate-and-term refinance does not provide any upfront money to the borrower. It's also known as a “no cash-out refinance.”

title: Rate of Change (ROC)
definition: A rate of change measures how quickly a measurement or value changes over time.

title: Rate of Return
definition: A rate of return (RoR) indicates how much an investment’s value has changed over time relative to what it cost.

title: Rating
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Ratio Analysis
definition: Ratio analysis is a method of evaluating a company’s financial condition by comparing key figures from its financial statements.

title: Rational Behavior
definition: Rational behavior refers to making choices that maximize personal benefit orutility, a core assumption in classical economics. While often framed in financial terms, rational decisions can also reflect emotional or lifestyle priorities, such as choosing a lower-paying job for better work-life balance. This idea underpins many economic models, even though behavioral economics shows that real-world decisions are often influenced by psychological biases.

title: Rational Choice Theory
definition: Rational choice theory assumes that individuals make informed decisions instead of random choices.

title: Rational Expectations Theory
definition: The rational expectations theory inmacroeconomicsposits that individuals base their decisions on human rationality, information available to them, and their past experiences. Economists use rational expectations theory to explain anticipated economic factors like inflation and interest rates. The central idea of rational expectations theory is that past outcomes influence future ones. The theory also proposes that, because people make decisions based on available information and their past experiences, most of the time their decisions will be correct.

title: Rationalization
definition: Rationalizationreorganizesa company to boostefficiency. This reorganization can expand or shrink the company, change policies, or adjust product strategies.

title: Rationing
definition: Rationing is the practice of controlling the distribution of a good or service in order to cope withscarcity. Rationing is a mandate of the government, at the local or federal level. It can be undertaken in response to adverse weather conditions, trade or import/export restrictions, or, in more extreme cases, during a recession or a war.

title: Raw Materials
definition: Raw materials are essential materials or substances used as inputs for the primary production or manufacturing of goods. Two categories of raw materials are direct and indirect, which must be accounted for differently. Businesses must strategically source and budget for raw materials, as well as for their storage or disposal.

title: Reaganomics
definition: Reaganomics refers to the economic policies instituted by President Reagan that included tax cuts, decreased social spending and market deregulation.

title: Real Asset
definition: A real asset is a tangible asset with intrinsic value that is derived from its physical properties.

title: Real Economic Growth Rate
definition: The real economic growth rate measures the change in a country's GDP while adjusting for inflation.

title: Real Effective Exchange Rate (REER)
definition: The real effective exchange rate (REER) is theweighted averageof a country's currency in relation to an index or basket of other major currencies. The weights are determined by comparing the relative trade balance of a country's currency against that of each country in the index.

title: Real Estate
definition: Real estate is defined as the land and any permanent structures, like a home or condo. It can also mean any improvements attached to the land, whether natural or artificial. Real estate is a form of real property. It differs from personal property, which is not permanently attached to the land, such as vehicles, boats, jewelry, furniture, and farm equipment.

title: Real Estate Agent
definition: A real estate agent is a licensed professional representing buyers or sellers in real estate transactions.

title: Real Estate Investment Group
definition: A real estate investment group (REIG) is a business that concentrates much of its efforts and capital on real estate. In search of profits, real estate investment groups may choose to buy, renovate, sell, or finance properties. Real estate investment groups commonly buy multiunit properties, sell units to investors, and take over administration and maintenance of the property. REIGs either do not elect to be or qualify as a real estate investment trust (REIT).

title: Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT)
definition: A real estate investment trust (REIT) is a company that owns, operates, or finances income-generating real estate and sells shares to raise capital to do so.

title: Real Estate Limited Partnership (RELP)
definition: A real estate limited partnership (RELP) is an investment vehicle where individuals pool funds to engage in property-related activities, such as purchasing, developing, or leasing real estate assets. RELPs offer investors an opportunity to participate in the real estate market without directly owning properties.

title: Real Estate Mortgage Investment Conduit (REMIC)
definition: A real estate mortgage investment conduit (REMIC) is a bundle of mortgages that is used to issue mortgage-backed securities, a type of debt instrument. For tax purposes, a REMIC is treated like a partnership, with the taxable income passed to its partners.

title: Real Estate Operating Company (REOC)
definition: A real estate operating company (REOC) is apublicly-traded companythat actively invests in properties, generally commercial real estate. Unlikereal estate investment trusts(REITs), REOCs reinvest the money they earn back into their business and are subject to higher corporate taxes than REITs. They include various types of real estate holdings and carry potential risks.

title: Real Estate Owned (REO)
definition: The term real estate owned (REO) refers to a lender-owned property that is not sold at aforeclosureauction. Properties become REO when owners default and the bank repossesses them and tries to sell them. The lender, which is often a bank, takes ownership of a foreclosed property when it fails to sell at the amount sought to cover the loan. These properties generally come at a steep discount but may require extensive repairs.

title: Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA)
definition: The Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA) was enacted by the U.S. Congress in 1975. RESPA requires lenders, mortgage brokers, or servicers of home loans to provide borrowers with disclosures concerning real estate transactions, settlement services, and consumer protection laws.

title: Real Estate Short Sale
definition: A short sale in real estate is an offer of a property at an asking price that is less than the amount due on the current owner's mortgage.

title: Real Gross Domestic Product (GDP)
definition: Real gross domestic product (GDP) is an inflation-adjusted measure that reflects the value of all goods and services produced by an economy in a given year. RealGDPis expressed in base-year prices. It is often referred to as constant-price GDP, inflation-corrected GDP, or constant-dollar GDP. Put simply, real GDP measures the total economic output of a country and is adjusted for changes in price.

title: Real Income
definition: Real income is the amount of money an individual or entity makes after accounting forinflation. It is sometimes called real wage. Tracking the difference between nominal and real income is critical to understanding changes in purchasing power.

title: Real Interest Rate
definition: The real interest rate can affect consumer investment behavior, encouraging higher risk investments when it's low and safer investments when it's high.

title: Real Option
definition: A real option allows company managers to make strategic business decisions involving tangible assets, like machinery or land. This concept involves choices, such as expanding or abandoning projects, based on fluctuating economic conditions. Calculating the value of real options helps firms determine potential opportunities or risks in their strategic planning. Unlike financial options, which are traded as securities, real options are embedded within business operations, offering a flexible approach to investment and project management.

title: Real Property
definition: Real property includes land and the permanent structures on it, but itdiffers from real estatein that it includes ownership rights that don't necessarily exist with real estate. Understanding what real property includes is important whenbuying a homeor a business, especially if the rights that come with real property are important to your purchase.

title: Real Rate of Return
definition: The real rate of return is the annual percentage of profit earned on an investment, adjusted for inflation to reflect its truepurchasing powerover time. This measure is important for comparing the performance of different investments because inflation can erode nominal gains. In addition to adjusting for inflation, investors should also account for taxes and fees, as these further reduce the real return and provide a more accurate picture of actual investment growth.

title: Real Time
definition: Real time is when a system relays information to a user at a speed that is near-instantaneous or has a short delay from when the event occurred.Online brokeragesoften provide a real-time data feed that displays stock quotes and their respective real-time changes, with a very insignificant lag time, so that clients can base their investing decisions on the most up-to-date information.

title: Real-Time Gross Settlement (RTGS)
definition: Real-Time Gross Settlement (RTGS) provides an efficient method for banks to execute high-value transactions instantly and individually, enhancing overall security by minimizing settlement risk. Unlike net settlement systems, RTGS transactions are final and irrevocable once processed, making it a vital component of modern banking.

title: Real-Time Quote (RTQ)
definition: A real-time quote (RTQs) is the display of the actual price of asecurityat that very moment in time.Quotesare the price of a stock or security displayed on various websites andticker tapes. In most cases, these figures are not real-time numbers of where the securities are trading but are delayed quotes. Delayed quotes, unlike real-time quotes, may lag the real trading market by between 15 and 20 minutes. Real-time quotes are instantaneous with no delay.

title: Realization Multiple
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Realized Gain
definition: Realized gains occur when you sell an asset for more than its purchase price, formalizing profit and creating a taxable event. While on paper, an asset's increase in value is an unrealized gain, only a sale turns it into a realized gain. This distinction impacts how gains are accounted for and taxed, affecting your overall financial strategy. It occurs when an asset is sold at a level that exceeds itsbook valuecost.

title: Realized Loss
definition: A realized loss is the loss that is recognized when assets are sold for a price lower than the original purchase price. Realized loss occurs when an asset that was purchased at a level referred to as cost or book value is then disbursed for a value below its book value.

title: Realized Yield
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Realtor
definition: A realtor is a licensed real estate professional who is a member of the National Association of Realtors (NAR) and follows its Code of Ethics.

title: Rebalancing
definition: Rebalancing is crucial for maintaining yourportfolio'soriginalasset allocation, aligning with your risk tolerance and investment goals. Market performance can shift asset values over time, necessitating periodic adjustments—selling overrepresented assets and buying underrepresented ones. Variousstrategies, such as calendar and constant-mix rebalancing, offer ways to manage these changes effectively, ensuring your investment stays on track for desired returns and risk levels..

title: Rebate
definition: A rebate is broadly defined as a return or credit of money to a customer after a transaction is completed. In financial markets, rebates are common in short-sale transactions, whereshort sellerspay a portion of interest or dividends to the stock's lender, typically through amargin account.

title: Recapitalization
definition: Recapitalization is the process of restructuring a company’s debt and equity mixture, often to stabilize a company’s capital structure. This may involve selling shares to pay off debt, or convertingpreferred sharestobonds.

title: Receipt
definition: A receipt is a written acknowledgment that a payment has been made, serving as a crucial record for both businesses and individuals to track financial transactions.

title: Receivable
definition: Accounts receivable (AR) is an accounting term for money owed to a business for goods or services that it has delivered but not been paid for yet. Accounts receivable is listed on the company'sbalance sheetas acurrent asset.

title: Receivables Turnover Ratio
definition: The accounts receivables turnover ratio measures how efficiently a company collects payment from its customers.

title: Receivership
definition: Receivership and bankruptcy are often misunderstood mechanisms in the realm of financial recovery. While both aim to address financial distress, they serve distinct roles. Receivership allows a court-appointed neutral receiver to manage a company's assets to prevent bankruptcy, aiding creditors and distressed companies alike. In contrast, bankruptcy offers debtor protection from creditors. Understanding these key differences can guide better financial decisions when faced with distress.

title: Recency, Frequency, Monetary Value (RFM)
definition: Recency, frequency, monetary value (RFM) is an analysis tool that businesses use to groupcustomersbased on how recently they made a purchase, how often they buy, and how much they spend. Companies score each customer in these areas, helping them spot the most valuable ones. RFM was developed in the 1990s and has proven to be an effective customer behavior andmarketingguide that helps increase sales. It also supports the 80/20 rule, which shows that most business comes from a small group of top customers.

title: Recession
definition: A recession is a prolonged, broad, and significant downturn in economic activity.

title: Recessionary Gap
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Recharacterization
definition: Recharacterization in the context ofindividual retirement accounts (IRAs)refers to two strategies: switching a contribution from one type of IRA to another or undoing a Roth IRA conversion. Currently, only the first strategy is allowed; you can recharacterize a Roth IRAcontributionas a traditional IRA contribution or vice versa if you meet specific IRS deadlines.

title: Reconciliation
definition: Accounting reconciliation involves comparing two sets of records to ensure accuracy and agreement, confirming that the accounts in a general ledger are consistent and complete.

title: Record Date
definition: The record date, or date of record, is the cutoff date established by a company to determine which shareholders are officially on the books and are therefore eligible to receive an upcoming dividend.

title: Recourse
definition: Recourse is the lender's right to collect by taking a borrower's pledgedcollateralif the debt isn't paid. It protects lenders by providing repayment options (cash or liquid assets), often lowering a company's cost of capital because the lending risk is lower. Full recourse versus non-recourse changes what the lender can pursue and can also shape borrower tax treatment if a debt is settled or forgiven.

title: Recourse Loan
definition: A recourse loan is a type of loan that can help a lender recoup its investment if a borrower fails to pay and the value of theunderlying assetis not enough to cover it. A recourse loan is a form of secured financing. It lets the lender go after the debtor's other assets that were not used as loancollateralor take legal action in case of default to pay off the full debt.

title: Recovery Rate
definition: The recovery rate indicates the proportion of a debt obligation that a lender is able to regain after a borrower defaults.

title: Recurring Billing
definition: Recurring billing happens when a merchant automatically charges customers for goods or services on a prearranged schedule. Recurring billing requires the merchant to get the customer’s information and permission. The vendor will then automatically make recurring charges to the customer’s account without further permission.

title: Recurring Revenue
definition: Recurring revenue refers to the consistent stream of income that a business expects to receive on a regular basis, ensuring financial predictability and stability over time.

title: Red Herring Filing
definition: A red herring prospectus is a document that provides essential information about a pending securities offering while intentionally omitting certain details, typically used to gauge investor interest before finalizing a prospectus.

title: Redemption
definition: Redemption refers to repaying mutual fund shares or a fixed-income security when it matures or before.

title: Redlining
definition: Redlining is a discriminatory practice where banks, insurers, and governments deny loans, insurance, or services to people in certain neighborhoods.

title: Reference Number
definition: A reference number is a unique combination of letters and/or numbers assigned to identify a specific transaction or request.

title: Reference Rate
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Refinance
definition: A refinance,or refi for short, refers to revising and replacing the terms of an existingcredit agreement,usually as it relates to a loan or mortgage. Refinancing a loan or mortgage is typically done to take advantage oflower interest ratesor improve the loan terms, such as the monthly payment or length of the loan. If approved, the borrower gets a new loan that replaces the original.

title: Reflexivity
definition: Reflexivity refers to a theory in economics where investors' perceptions influence economic fundamentals and in turn, those perceptions and market prices. Reflexivity originates in sociology, but it is prominent in the field of finance.George Soros, its leading advocate, believes that the deviations from economic equilibrium are due to positive feedback loops. According to Soros, reflexivity theory challenges equilibrium and the efficient market hypothesis.

title: What Is a Registered Education Savings Plan and How It Helps Save for College
definition: Americans can sock away college money in 529 education savings or prepaid tuition plans, but Canadians also have options. A registered education savings plan (RESP) offers a way to save for higher education, and it can be boosted by grant money from the Canadian government.

title: Registered Investment Advisor (RIA)
definition: A Registered Investment Advisor (RIA) is a financial professional or firm that provides investment advice and management for a fee. Governed by fiduciary duties, RIAs must act in the best interests of their clients. They offer comprehensive financial services, including portfolio management, financial planning, and retirement strategies, ensuring clients receive personalized and responsible advice.

title: Registered Representative (RR): Definition, Job Description, Pay
definition: A registered representative (RR) is a person who works for a client-facing financial firm such as abrokerage companyand serves as a representative for clients who are trading investment products and securities. Registered representatives may be employed as brokers, financial advisors, or portfolio managers.

title: Regression
definition: Regression tries to determine how a dependent variable and one or more other (independent) variables relate to each other. It’s a statistical method.

title: Regressive Tax: Definition and Types of Taxes That Are Regressive
definition: A regressive tax imposes a uniform rate regardless of income, leading to higher financial strain on low-income individuals compared to those with higher incomes. Unlike a progressive tax system where tax rates increase with income, regressive taxes like sales, excise, and payroll taxes affect everyone equally but take a larger portion from those who earn less. This guide explores the nuances and implications of regressive taxes and exemplifies how they influence economic equity.

title: Regret Theory
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Regtech
definition: Regulatory technology (RegTech) streamlines regulatory processes by combining technology and finance. It ensures efficient regulatory monitoring, reporting, and compliance. By utilizing big data and machine learning, RegTech helps institutions mitigate online risks such as fraud and data breaches. This regulatory technology is essential in navigating today's digital financial landscape, providing tools that are both cost-effective and timely.

title: Regulated Investment Companies (RICs): Definition, Taxes & Examples
definition: A Regulated Investment Company (RIC) functions as a mutual fund, real estate investment trust (REIT), or unit investment trust that can pass income through to investors, avoiding double taxation under IRS Regulation M. This tax-efficient structure allows investors to directly receive capital gains, dividends, and interest. An RIC must meet specific requirements, including deriving at least 90% of its income from valid investments, maintaining substantial asset diversification, and distributing most of its net income to qualify for this status.

title: What Is Regulation A? Definition, Update, Documenation, and Tiers
definition: Under U.S. securities laws, an offering or sale of asecuritymust be registered with theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC)or meet an exemption.

title: Regulation B (Reg B) in the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA)
definition: Under the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA), lenders must provide the main reasons for denying credit or taking adverse action against an applicant. This is enforced through Regulation B. Regulation B is intended to prevent applicants from discrimination in any aspect of a credit transaction. It outlines the rules lenders must adhere to when obtaining and processing credit information. Regulation B protects consumers and prohibits lenders from discriminating based on age, gender, ethnicity, nationality, or marital status.

title: Regulation CC: Understanding Check-Clearing Standards & Fund Availability
definition: Regulation CC sets the standards for how banks clear checks and make deposited funds available, supporting faster access to money under theExpedited Funds Availability Act (EFAA)of 1987 and the Check Clearing for the 21st Century Act (Check 21).

title: SEC Regulation D Explained: Key Exemptions, Rules & Benefits
definition: Regulation D is a set ofSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC)rules forprivate placementexemptions. It allows capital to be raised through the sale of equity ordebt securitieswithout the need to register those securities with the SEC. The primary advantages of a Reg D offering for private, usually smaller, companies include reduced costs for capital and faster access to that capital. The company or entrepreneur must file a Form D disclosure document with the SEC after the first securities are sold. Reg D is different from theFederal Reserve Board Regulation D, which limits withdrawals from savings accounts.

title: Regulation DD
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Understanding Regulation E: Your Guide to Electronic Fund Transfers
definition: Regulation E, established by the Federal Reserve Board, outlines crucial procedures and protections for electronic fund transfers. It safeguards consumers by defining rules for ATM transactions, point of sale activities, and unauthorized card usage, ensuring that banks and financial institutions comply with established guidelines to offer secure EFT services.

title: Regulation O: Limits on Bank Insider Loans and Requirements
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Regulation SHO Explained: Short Sale Rules and Key Requirements
definition: Regulation SHO is a set of rules from theSecurities and Exchange Commission(SEC) implemented in 2005 that regulatesshort salepractices.

title: Regulation T (Reg T): Definition of Requirement and Example
definition: Regulation T is a collection of provisions that govern investors' cash accounts and the amount of credit that brokerage firms and dealers may extend to customers for the purchase ofsecurities. According to Regulation T, an investor may borrow up to 50% of the purchase price of securities that can be bought using a loan from abrokeror dealer. The remaining 50% of the price must be funded with cash.

title: Regulation U Explained: Bank Requirements and Securities Lending
definition: Regulation U is a Federal Reserve rule that governs the borrowing activities of banks and other lenders when loans are secured by securities, aiming to control the leverage in the stock market.

title: Regulation W in Banking: Limits on Bank-Affiliate Transactions
definition: Regulation W, implemented by theFederal Reserve System (FRS), restricts transactions between banks andaffiliatesto mitigate risk and manage financial safety. It enforces quantitative limits andcollateralrequirements, ensuring banks do not misuse the federal safety net. Post-2008, more stringent rules were introduced under the Dodd-Frank Act, expanding Regulation W's scope. Discover how these measures affect bank operations and compliance strategies.

title: Understanding Regulation Z: Truth in Lending Act Explained
definition: Enacted as part of the Consumer Credit Protection Act of 1968, Regulation Z, synonymous with theTruth in Lending Act,ensures lenders disclose transparent interest rates and loan terms. This regulation protects consumers from predatory lending, allowing them to more knowledgeably compare credit options. Borrowers also gain the right to cancel certain loans, promoting fair lending practices.

title: Understanding Regulatory Capture: Definition, Impact, and Examples
definition: Regulatory capture is a process by which regulatory agencies may come to be dominated by the industries or interests they are charged with regulating. As a result, the agency acts to benefit established firms in the industry instead of serving the public interest. Industries have an influence on regulation and pose challenges by lobbying.

title: Regulatory Risk
definition: Regulatory risk is the risk that newlawsor rule changes will materially affect a business, sector, or investment by raising operating costs, altering competitiveness, or even undermining a company's business model.

title: Rehypothecation
definition: Rehypothecation involves banks and brokers using client collateral for their transactions, offering benefits such as lower borrowing costs and fee rebates. Commonly, a hedge fund's collateral with a brokerage can be utilized by the brokerage for further financial dealings.

title: What Is a Reimbursement, and How Does It Work (With Example)?
definition: Reimbursement is compensation paid by an organization forout-of-pocketexpenses incurred or overpayment made by an employee, customer, or another party. Reimbursement ofbusiness expenses, insurance costs, and overpaid taxes are common examples. However, unlike typical compensation, reimbursement is not subject to taxation.

title: Reinsurance
definition: Reinsurance serves as a crucial risk management tool for insurance companies by allowing them to transfer some of their policy risks to another company, the reinsurer. This relationship is established through a formal contract, in which the primary insurer, or cedent, passes portions of its liability to the reinsurer.

title: Reinsurance Ceded
definition: Reinsurance ceded is the process by which an insurance company passes a portion of its risk to another insurer to improve its risk management strategy.

title: Reinvestment
definition: Reinvestment is the practice of using dividends, interest, or other forms ofincomedistribution earned from an investment to purchase additional shares or units of that investment, rather than receiving thedistributionsin cash. This strategy can help investors grow their holdings over time, potentially leading to significant wealth accumulation.

title: Reinvestment Rate
definition: The reinvestment rate is the amount of interest that can be earned when money is taken out of one fixed-income investment and put into another. For example, the reinvestment rate is the amount of interest the investor could earn if he purchased a new bond while holding a callable bond called due because of an interest rate decline.

title: Reinvestment Risk
definition: Reinvestment risk is the chance an investor can't reinvest cash flows likecoupon paymentsat the same rate of return. This new rate is called thereinvestment rate.

title: Related-Party Transactions
definition: Related-party transactions are arrangements between parties with existing business ties or common interests. While legal, these transactions can lead to potential conflicts of interest and require scrutiny to ensure they do not adversely impact shareholders or corporate profits. Public companies must disclose such dealings, emphasizing transparency in financial statements.

title: Relationship Management
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Relationship Manager
definition: A relationship manager helps build and maintain strong connections between a company and its clients or business partners. The role requires good communication and analytical skills to understand client needs and market trends. In larger organizations, relationship managers often focus on either clients or business relationships. By knowing what clients want and staying on top of market changes, they help their company succeed. There’s a difference between managing client relationships, which focuses on individual or corporate clients, and managing business relationships, which focuses on partnerships and organizational growth.

title: Relative Purchasing Power Parity (RPPP)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Relative Strength
definition: Relative strength is an investment strategy used inmomentum investingto identify securities that outperform the market or a chosenbenchmark. By comparing the performance of an asset, such as a stock or sector, to an index like the S&P 500 orNasdaq Composite, investors can target those demonstrating stronger upward momentum.

title: Relative Strength Index (RSI)
definition: The RSI is a momentum oscillator that's widely used in technical analysis of stocks and commodities to identify changes in momentum and price direction.

title: Relative Valuation Model
definition: A relative valuation model is a tool used to value companies and compare them to each other by using particular metrics.

title: Relative Value
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Relative Vigor Index (RVI)
definition: The Relative Vigor Index (RVI) is amomentum indicatorthat gauges trend strength by comparing a security's closing price to its overall trading range, then smoothing the results to reduce noise. It's based on the idea that prices tend to close higher in uptrends and lower in downtrends.

title: Relevant Cost
definition: Relevant cost is amanagerial accountingterm that describes avoidable costs that are incurred only when making specific business decisions. The concept of relevant cost is used to eliminate unnecessary data that could complicate the decision-making process. As an example, relevant cost is used to determine whether to sell or keep a business unit.

title: Relief Rally
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Remittance
definition: "Remittance" refers to sending money from one person or group to another and commonly refers to sending money back to one's family in another country. For instance, someone living and working in the U.S. might send money back to their family in a poorer country, where remittances can account for a significant source of financial support.

title: Remuneration
definition: Remuneration is the total compensation received by an employee including base salary, bonuses, commission payments, overtime pay, and any other monetary benefits that the employee receives.

title: Renewable Energy Certificate (REC)
definition: Renewable energy certificates (RECs) are issued when electricity is generated from renewable energy sources. They are sold separately from the actual electricity produced.

title: Renewable Resource
definition: A renewable resource is a resource that can be replenished naturally over time. As a result, it is sustainable despite its consumption by humankind.

title: Renko Chart
definition: A Renko chart is a type of chart, developed by the Japanese, that is built using price movement rather than both priceandstandardized time intervals like most charts are. It is thought to be named after the Japanese word for bricks, "renga," since the chart looks like a series of bricks. A new brick is created when the price moves a specified price amount, and each block is positioned at a 45-degree angle (up or down) to the right of the prior brick. An up brick is typically colored white or green, while a down brick is typically colored black or red.

title: Rent Control
definition: Rent control is a government program that limits how much alandlordcan charge for leasing a home or renewing a lease. Rent control laws are usually enacted by municipalities, and the details vary widely. All are intended to keep living costs affordable for lower-income residents.

title: Rent Seeking
definition: Rent seeking, a concept introduced by economist Gordon Tullock, refers to the pursuit ofwealthwithout contributing to society's overall productivity or well-being. It often involves manipulating the political or regulatory environment to gain advantages such as subsidies, tax breaks, or favorable legistlation.

title: Renter's Insurance
definition: Renters insurance provides financial protection for tenants by covering their personal property and offering liability coverage for accidents or damages that occur in their rented home.

title: Reorganization
definition: A reorganization is a significant and disruptive overhaul of a troubled business intended to restore it to profitability. It may include shutting down or selling divisions, replacing management, cutting budgets, and laying off workers.

title: Repackaging
definition: Repackaging inprivate equityinvolves buying a struggling public company, often through a leveraged buyout, taking it private, and then restructuring it for resale. Firms may exit through a sale, merger, orinitial public offering(IPO), though IPOs are less common due to added scrutiny.

title: Repatriable
definition: Repatriable refers to the ability to move liquid financial assets from a foreign country to an investor's country of origin. The Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA) and the Bank Secrecy Act (BSA) impose reporting requirements concerning foreign financial accounts and foreign asset holdings on foreign financial institutions (FFIs) and on U.S. persons. This discourages the repatriation of assets.

title: Repatriation
definition: Repatriation is the return of people, money, or objects of cultural heritage to their country or culture of origin. In finance, repatriation refers to converting or exchanging foreign currency into someone's home currency.

title: Repayment
definition: Repayment is the act of paying back a lender the money you’ve borrowed. Typically, it consists of periodic payments toward theprincipal—the original amount borrowed—and interest, a fee for being lent the money. Some loans allow you to repay the full amount at any time, though there might be early repayment fees.

title: Replacement Cost
definition: Replacement cost is a term referring to the amount of money a business must currently spend to replace an asset like a fixture, a machine, a vehicle, or an equipment, at current market prices. Sometimes referred to as a "replacement value," a replacement cost may fluctuate, depending on factors such as the market value of components used to reconstruct or repurchase the asset and the expenses involved in preparing assets for use.

title: Replacement Rate
definition: A replacement rate is the percentage of a worker's pre-retirement income that is paid out by a pension program after the worker retires. In pension systems that pay workers substantially differentpayoutsbased on their differingincomes, the replacement rate is a common measurement that can be used to determine the effectiveness of the pension system.

title: Representative Sample
definition: A representative sample is a smaller subset of a larger population that is used to learn and accurately represent that larger group.

title: Repudiation
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Repurchase Agreement (Repo)
definition: A repurchase agreement is a contract to sell securities, usually government bonds, and repurchase them shortly after at a slightly higher price.

title: Reputational Risk
definition: Reputational risk poses a significant threat to a business's standing, stemming from direct actions, employee conduct, or associated partners. It can severely impact profitability, market value, and upper management. Learn effective strategies to mitigate these risks in an era of instant communication and globally connected markets.

title: Request for Quote (RFQ)
definition: A request for quote is when a company asks various select suppliers and contractors for price estimates for a project or task.

title: Requests for Proposal (RFP)
definition: A request for proposal (RFP) is a document by a government, business, or organization that announces a new project and invites contractors or vendors to submit their bids.

title: Required Minimum Distribution (RMD)
definition: A required minimum distribution (RMD) is the amount of money that must be withdrawn annually from certain employer-sponsored retirement plans like401(k)sand certainindividual retirement accounts (IRAs), such as the traditional IRA. RMDs must be taken by April 1 after you turn 73 years old. You must calculate and withdraw the correct RMD every year after that, or face a penalty from theInternal Revenue Service (IRS).RMDs do not apply to Roth accounts until after the account owner dies.

title: Required Rate of Return (RRR)
definition: The requiredrate of return(RRR) is the minimum return an investor will accept for owning a company's stock, as compensation for a given level of risk associated with holding the stock. The RRR is also used in corporate finance to analyze the profitability of potential investment projects.

title: Requisition
definition: A requisition is a demand or request for a service or item, typically using a purchase requisition form or another standardized document. The requisition process is a standardized way of keeping track of and accounting for all requisitions made within a business.

title: Research Analysts
definition: A research analyst is a professional who evaluates financial data to provide insights that assist organizations in strategic planning and investments.

title: Research and Development (R&D)
definition: Research and development (R&D) is the series of activities that companies undertake to innovate. It's often the first stage in the development process that results in market research, product development, and product testing.

title: Research and Development (R&D) Expenses
definition: Research and development (R&D) expenses are the money companies spend on innovation and improving their products, services, technologies, and processes. R&D is a common type ofoperating expense. Usually, GAAP requires R&D costs to be recorded as an expense in the year incurred. However, in cases where R&D activities have an alternative future use, the expenses may be capitalized.R&D expenses tend to be highest for companies that rely on innovation, such as those in the tech and pharmaceutical industries.

title: Research Associate
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Research Report
definition: A research report is a detailed document prepared by analysts and investment professionals, offering comprehensive analysis and insights into a specific security, investment area, or industry sector. These reports provide investors with actionable recommendations and insights to guide investment decisions. Research reports focus on a range of assets, including stocks, commodities, and market trends, providing crucial data and expert analysis.

title: Reserve Bank of India (RBI)
definition: The Reserve Bank of India is the central bank of India, originally established as a private entity in 1935 and nationalized in 1949.

title: Reserve Currency
definition: A reserve currency, primarily held bycentral banksworldwide, is used to facilitate international trade and stabilize economies. Since 1944, the U.S. dollar has played a crucial role as the dominant reserve currency. This article explores its global impact and historical evolution, including key agreements like Bretton Woods, which anchored its status and the shifts that followed Nixon's gold decoupling.

title: Reserve Fund
definition: A reserve fund is a savings account or other highlyliquid assetdesigned to cover future, unpredictable expenses. Reserve funds are used by different entities like governments, financial institutions, and individuals for managing both planned upgrades and unexpected financial needs.

title: Reserve Price
definition: Common to auctions, a reserve price or a reservation price is the minimum amount that a seller will accept as the winning bid. Alternatively, it is less commonly known as the highest price a buyer is willing to pay for a good or service. The reserve price prevents a bidder who offers a price lower than what the owner will accept from winning the auction.

title: Reserve Ratio
definition: The reserve ratio, often referred to as the cash reserve ratio, is a critical component dictated by central banks to ensure economic stability. This percentage of deposits that banks must hold rather than lend is determined by the Federal Reserve in the U.S. It influences the money supply and can impact interest rates, lending practices, and overall economic growth.

title: Reserve Requirements
definition: Reserve requirements mandate the cash a bank must hold to cover customer deposits, set by the Federal Reserve, and impact monetary policy significantly. These requirements were set to 0% in March 2020 to boost economic activity during COVID-19.Learn more about their function, benefits, and historical context in safeguarding banking operations.

title: Resident Alien
definition: A resident alien is a foreign-born United States resident who isn't an American citizen. A resident alien is also known as a permanent resident or a lawful permanent resident. They're considered to be an immigrant who has been legally and lawfully recorded as a resident of the country. A resident alien must have agreen cardor pass a substantial presence test.

title: Residential Mortgage-Backed Security (RMBS)
definition: Residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) are debt instruments backed by pools of residential loans, including mortgages andhome-equity loans. These securities offer investors the potential for high returns by distributing payments from numerous individual mortgages. However, they carry risks like prepayment and credit risk, impacting investor profitability.

title: Residual Dividend
definition: A residual dividend is a dividend policy used by companies to distribute profits to shareholders only after covering capital expenditures (CapEx) and working capital needs. This ensures that earnings are first directed toward growth and investment opportunities before any dividends are paid out. For investors, grasping how residual dividends work offers valuable insight into a company’s long-term growth strategy and overall financial health, along with the mechanics, considerations, and real-world application of this policy.

title: Residual Income
definition: Residual income is ongoing earnings that continue after the initial investment is completed, and it's commonly from dividends, rental properties, royalties, or business ventures.

title: Residual Standard Deviation
definition: Residual standard deviation lets you measure how far data points fall from a regression line. This tells you how much the actual values differ from the predicted values, indicating the accuracy of a model. A smaller residualstandard deviationmeans the model’s predictions are closer to reality, while a larger one suggests it's less accurate. In this article, we explore the formula, examples, and explain how to calculate and interpret residual standard deviation.

title: Residual Sum of Squares (RSS)
definition: The residual sum of squares (RSS) is a statistical technique used to measure the amount ofvariancein a data set that is not explained by a regression model itself. Instead, it estimates the variance in the residuals, orerror term.

title: Residual Value
definition: Residual value is the estimated worth of an asset at the end of its useful life or lease term.

title: Resistance (Resistance Level)
definition: Resistance is a foundational concept in technical analysis (along with its corollary:support) with levels identified through chart analysis and trading tools. This concept is important when making informed trading decisions and performing market analysis. Resistance levels can indicate potential price reversals or continue trends. Strategies for trading at resistance levels, such as breakouts and short selling, include trendlines, moving averages, Bollinger Bands, and Ichimoku Cloud charts.

title: Resolution Trust Corporation (RTC)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Resource Curse
definition: The resource curse describes an economic paradox where countries rich in non-renewable resources, like oil, face stagnation or decline in growth. Investment focused solely in resource-intensive sectors, coupled with potential government mismanagement, can weaken a nation's economy. This situation underscores the need for economic diversification to mitigate reliance on volatilecommodity prices.

title: Restatement
definition: A restatement is a revision of a company’s financial statements to correct errors when they are significant. Restatements are required when material inaccuracies are found. Accountants, along with guidance from the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB), play a key role in ensuring accuracy. While they help maintain reliable reporting, reinstatements can hurt a company’s financial standing and investor confidence. They can also bring about legal and regulatory risks.

title: Restricted Cash
definition: Restricted cash, in contrast to unrestricted cash, is not freely available for a company to spend or invest. Restricted cash refers to money that is held for a specific purpose and thus not available to the company for immediate or general business use.

title: Restricted Stock
definition: Restricted stocks are unregistered shares of ownership in a corporation that are issued to company executives, directors, and other employees as part of their compensation. Restricted stocks are nontransferable and must be traded according to the relevant Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulations.The restrictions of these stocks usually relate to theirvestingperiod, which is when they can't be sold or transferred.

title: Restricted Stock Unit (RSU)
definition: Restricted stock units are a form of employee compensation, restricted through a vesting period that may last several years.

title: Restrictive Covenant
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Restructuring
definition: Restructuring involves significant changes to a company's debt, operations, or structure to mitigate financial distress. Businesses mayconsolidatedebt, adjust operations, or sell assets to improve financial standing. The process can prepare companies for sales, mergers, or ownership changes, ultimately aiming for smoother, more efficient operations.

title: Restructuring Charge
definition: Arestructuringcharge is aone-time expensethat a company pays when reorganizing its operations.

title: Resume
definition: A resume is an essential document summarizing your qualifications, including work experience, education, and skills, that is used to help secure a job interview. It often accompanies acover lettertailored to the job, highlighting relevant accomplishments and expertise. Understanding how to design it effectively can significantly impact your job search success.

title: Retail Banking
definition: Retail banking, also known as consumer banking or personal banking, offers services to individual consumers, not large institutions. Retail banking enhances financial security and ease via local branches and fintech platforms.

title: Retail Investor
definition: Retail investors buy securities for their own personal accounts and often trade in dramatically smaller amounts as compared with institutional investors.

title: Retail Price Index (RPI)
definition: The Retail Price Index (RPI) is a traditional measure of inflation in the U.K., introduced in 1947 and active since 1956. It was replaced by the Consumer Prices Index (CPI) as the official inflation measure in 2003. RPI is often used in wage negotiations, tax allowance adjustments, and setting social housing rents. Although not the official statistic, RPI still influences some economic sectors.

title: Retail Sales
definition: Retail sales are an economic metric that tracks consumer demand for finished goods. This figure is a very important data set as it is a key monthly market-moving event. Retail sales are reported each month by the U.S. Census Bureau and indicate the direction of the economy. It acts as a key economic barometer and whetherinflationary pressuresexist. Retail sales are measured by durable and non-durable goods purchased over a defined period of time. Sales for the report are derived from 13 types of retailers from food service to retail stores.

title: Retained Earnings
definition: Retained earnings are the earnings left over and kept by a company after paying all current obligations and expenses, including dividend payments to shareholders.

title: Retainer Fee
definition: A retainer fee is an advance payment made to a professional, such as a consultant, freelancer, or lawyer, to secure their services.

title: Retention Bonus
definition: A retention bonus is a financial incentive offered as a one-time lump sum payment to retain key employees during crucial business cycles or projects. It aims to retain senior executives and key employees amidst organizational changes like mergers or restructures, by securing their skills and corporate knowledge. Companies, especially in competitive industries, use retention bonuses to prevent valuable employees from joining competitors.

title: Retention Ratio
definition: The retention ratio is the proportion ofearningskept back in a business as retained earnings.
