It refers to the percentage ofnet incomethat is retained to grow the business, rather than being paid out as dividends. It is the opposite of thepayout ratio, which measures the percentage of profit paid out to shareholders as dividends. The retention ratio is also called the “plowback ratio.”

title: Retirement Income Certified Professional (RICP)
definition: The Retirement Income Certified Professional (RICP) designation is a specialized credential for financial advisors who focus onretirement income planning. Offered by the American College of Financial Services, it requires coursework, exams, and experience. As more people reach retirement age, RICPs help clients manage their assets sustainably so their savings last throughoutretirement.

title: Retirement Money Market Account
definition: A retirement money market account is a money market account held within someone's larger retirement account, such as an individual retirement account (IRA) or 401(k). In a retirement money market account, deposits are placed in low-risk investments such as certificates of deposit (CDs), Treasury bills, and short-term commercial paper.

title: Retirement Planning
definition: Creating a retirement plan begins with determining your long-term financial goals and tolerance for risk, and then starting to take action to reach those goals. The process can begin anytime during your working years, but the earlier, the better.

title: Retracement
definition: A retracement refers to the temporary reversal of an overarching trend in a stock's price.

title: Retrocession
definition: Retrocession fees are payments, such askickbacksor trailer fees, that asset managers pay to advisers or distributors for promoting financial products. These fees, often drawn from client funds, can create conflicts of interest by incentivizing advisers to recommend products based on compensation rather than client benefit.

title: Return
definition: A return is the gains earned or the losses incurred on an investment.

title: Return of Capital (ROC)
definition: Return of capital is a payment that returns a portion of an investor's original investment.

title: Return on Assets (ROA)
definition: The ROA is a ratio commonly expressed as a percentage that compares a company’s net income with its assets.

title: Return on Average Assets (ROAA)
definition: Return on average assets (ROAA) is a financial metric used to evaluate how well a company uses its assets to produce profits. It is commonly used by banks andfinancial institutionsto measure performance. The ROAA formula uses average assets to capture major changes in asset balances during the analysis period. Companies that invest heavily in equipment and other assets upfront usually have a lower ROAA. Some use ROAA interchangeably withreturn on assets(ROA) but ROA may use current assets instead of average assets. ROAA works best when used to compare similar companies in the same industry.

title: Return on Average Capital Employed (ROACE)
definition: Return on average capital employed (ROACE) shows how well a company uses its money to make profits. UnlikeROCE, which looks at capital at one moment, ROACE uses the average over time, giving a clearer picture. It’s especially useful in industries like oil and gas that need big investments. You can calculate ROACE by dividing earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) by a company's average capital. Investors and analysts like ROACE because it tells them how efficiently a company turns investments into profits.

title: Return on Average Equity (ROAE)
definition: Return on average equity (ROAE) measures a company's performance using its average shareholders' equity. Typically, ROAE refers to a company's performance over a fiscal year, so the ROAE numerator isnet incomeand the denominator is computed as the sum of the equity value at the beginning and end of the year, divided by 2.

title: Return on Capital Employed (ROCE)
definition: Return on capital employed (ROCE) is a financial ratio that indicates how effective a company is at generating profits by using its capital.

title: Return on Equity (ROE)
definition: Return on equity (ROE) is a financial performance ratio that measures how effectively a company uses shareholders’ equity to generate net income.

title: Return on Invested Capital (ROIC)
definition: Return on invested capital (ROIC) is calculated by dividing net operating profit after tax by invested capital.

title: Return on Investment (ROI)
definition: Expressed as a percentage, return on investment (ROI) is a financial ratio that measures the profit generated by an investment relative to its cost. Key factors influencing ROI include the initial investment amount, ongoing maintenance costs, and the cash flow generated by the investment.

title: Return on Net Assets (RONA)
definition: Return on Net Assets (RONA) evaluates a company's efficiency in maximizing earnings from its net asset base.

title: Return on Revenue (ROR)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Return on Risk-Adjusted Capital (RORAC)
definition: Return on risk-adjusted capital (RORAC) measures the rate of return. It is important in financial analysis because it evaluates projects and investments based oncapital risk. RORAC is used when comparing projects with different risk profiles and it assists in strategic planning and risk management.

title: Return on Sales (ROS)
definition: Return on Sales (ROS) evaluates a company's operational efficiency by assessing how much profit is generated per sales dollar. An increase in ROS signifies improved efficiency, whereas a decrease may indicate financial difficulties. Closely related to theoperating profit margin, ROS provides insights into company performance.

title: Return on Total Assets (ROTA)
definition: Return on total assets (ROTA) is a ratio that measures a company'searningsbefore interest and taxes (EBIT) relative to its total net assets. It is defined as the ratio between net income and total average assets, or the amount of financial and operational income a company receives in a financial year as compared to the average of that company's total assets.

title: Returned Payment Fee
definition: A returned payment fee is a one-time penalty charged by a bank when a customer bounces a check. The bank sends the customer a message indicating that a check has been returned unpaid "due to non-sufficient funds" in the account. Depending on the bank, this fee can be between $25 and $40 for each bad check.

title: Revaluation
definition: A revaluation is an upward adjustment to a country's officialexchange raterelative to a chosen baseline. The baseline can include wage rates, the price of gold, or a foreign currency. Revaluation is the opposite ofdevaluation, which is a downward adjustment of a country's official exchange rate.

title: Revaluation Reserve
definition: Revaluation reserve is an accounting term used when a company creates a line item on its balance sheet for the purpose of maintaining a reserve account tied to certain assets. This line item can be used when a revaluation assessment finds that the carrying value of the asset has changed.

title: Revealed Preference
definition: Revealed preference is an economic theory that suggests that consumers’ buying habits dictate their preferences.

title: Revenue
definition: Revenue can be divided into the operating revenue from a company's core business and non-operating revenue derived from secondary sources.

title: Revenue Agent's Report (RAR)
definition: The Revenue Agent’s Report (RAR) is a detailed document that describes an IRS examiner's audit findings and states the amount of deficiency or refund the agent finds the taxpayer to owe or be owed, respectively.﻿﻿ Taxpayers have the right to disagree with a revenue agent's report and can choose to fight the agent's findings through a formal protest to the IRS Office of Appeals division by appealing to theU.S. Tax Court, or by paying the new assessment but then suing for a refund.﻿﻿

title: Revenue Bond
definition: A revenue bond is a municipal bond that is funded through the income generated by specific projects, like toll roads, bridges, and public facilities. Unlike general obligation bonds, which rely on tax revenues, revenue bonds are repaid from the project’s revenues, making them a unique investment option for public infrastructure.

title: Revenue Cap Regulations
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Revenue Deficit
definition: A revenue deficit occurs when realized net income is less than the projectednet income. This happens when the actual amount ofrevenueand the actual amount of expenditures do not correspond with budgeted revenue and expenditures. This is the opposite of a revenue surplus, which occurs when the actual amount of net income exceeds the projected amount.

title: Revenue Generating Unit (RGU)
definition: A revenue generating unit (RGU) is an individual service subscriber who generatesrecurring revenuefor a company. This is used as a performance measure for management, analysts, and investors.

title: Revenue Officer
definition: A revenue officer is an employee of a government or a public agency who collects delinquent taxes or fees. The job is performed on a case-by-case basis and involves directly contacting individuals or businesses that are in arrears on payments.

title: Revenue Passenger Mile (RPM)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Revenue per Available Room (RevPAR)
definition: RevPAR, or Revenue Per Available Room, serves as a key performance indicator in the hospitality sector. By calculating RevPAR, hotel operators can assess their ability to fill rooms at average rates, helping them make pricing and occupancy decisions. Understanding RevPAR's nuances allows hoteliers to compare against industry standards and make informed decisions to optimize revenue.

title: Revenue per Available Seat Mile (RASM)
definition: Revenue per available seat mile (RASM) is a key performance metric used by airlines to measure the amount of revenue generated for every seat (occupied or not) flown over one mile. RASM is crucial for airlines to assess financial performance comprehensively because it includes all sources of revenue such as ticket sales, baggage fees, and inflight services.

title: Revenue per Employee
definition: Revenue per employee measures how effectively a company utilizes its workforce by calculating the income generated for each individual working, emphasizing operational efficiency and productivity.

title: Revenue per User (RPU)
definition: Average revenue per unit (ARPU) is a financial metric that measures how much revenue a company earns per customer, especially in subscription-driven industries like telecom and media.

title: Revenue Recognition
definition: Revenue recognition is an accounting method that stipulates that revenue should only be recorded when goods or services are delivered.

title: Reversal
definition: A reversal is a change in the price direction of an asset. A reversal can occur to the upside or downside. Following anuptrend, a reversal would be to the downside. Following adowntrend, a reversal would be to the upside. Reversals are based on overall price direction and are not typically based on one or two periods/bars on a chart.

title: Reverse Auction
definition: In a reverseauction, sellers compete to offer the lowest price to a buyer. The buyer sets the terms, and multiple sellers try to undercut each other to secure the contract. Governments and large companies use reverse auctions to buy inexpensive goods and services. The advantages include cost savings and faster procurement. But there are concerns about quality and the conditions of competition. Reverse auctions are the opposite of regular auctions, where buyers try to outbid each other to win.

title: Reverse Culture Shock
definition: Reverse culture shock is the emotional and psychological distress people experience when returning home after living abroad for an extended time. Common challenges include readjusting to a now-unfamiliar culture and values that previously were familiar. This phenomenon is increasingly common due to globalization, which sends more employees on long-term assignments abroad.

title: Reverse Morris Trust
definition: A Reverse Morris Trust (RMT) is a tax optimization strategy in which one company that wishes to sell assets to another company can do so without paying taxes on any gains made from the disposal.

title: Reverse Repurchase Agreement
definition: A reverse repurchase agreement (RRP), commonly referred to as a reverse repo, represents a transaction where the seller agrees to repurchase securities at a higher price on a specific future date. Functioning as a collateralized loan, it is the seller's perspective within arepurchase agreement (RP), essentially reflecting their role in obtaining short-term capital while managing cash flow challenges.

title: Reverse Stock Split
definition: A reverse stock split is a type ofcorporate actionthat consolidates the number of existing shares of stock into fewer (and, importantly, higher-priced) shares. A reverse stock split divides the existing total quantity of shares by a number, such as five or 10, which would then be called a "1-for-5" or a "1-for-10" reverse split, respectively.

title: Reverse Takeover (RTO)
definition: In a reverse takeover (RTO), a private company acquires control of a publicly-traded company, enabling it to become publicly listed more swiftly than through anIPO. Though cost-effective and quicker, this process carries its own set of risks and considerations. Understanding how an RTO unfolds can help in evaluating its potential impact on a company's financial future.

title: Reverse Triangular Mergers
definition: In a reverse triangular merger, the acquiring company uses a newly formed subsidiary to merge with the target company, effectively making the target a subsidiary in the process.

title: Revocable Beneficiary
definition: A revocable beneficiary does not have guaranteed rights to receive compensation from an entity such as an insurance policy or a trust fund. The policy owner reserves the right to make changes to who receives payment, change the terms of the policy, or terminate the policy without the need of revocable beneficiary consent. Mostlife insurancepolicies have this feature.

title: Revocable Trust
definition: A revocable trust is a flexible estate planning tool that allows the grantor to alter or cancel its provisions during their lifetime while bypassing probate in the event of the grantor's death.

title: Revolver
definition: A revolver refers to a borrower—either an individual or a company—who carries a balance from month to month via a revolving credit line. Borrowers only have to make minimum monthly payments, which go toward paying interest and reducing principal debt. This is different from a non-revolving loan, which only offers a one-time payout.

title: Revolving Credit
definition: Revolving credit refers to a type of credit account that allows the borrower to repeatedly borrow up to a certain limit. Making payments reduces the balance owed and frees up credit, which can be used again. Credit cards, personal lines of credit, andhome equity lines of credit (HELOCs)are all types of revolving credit.

title: Revolving Door
definition: The term "revolving door" refers to the movement of high-level employees from public sector jobs to private sector jobs and vice versa. The idea is that there is a revolving door between the two sectors, as many legislators and regulators becomelobbyistsand consultants for the industries they once regulated, and some private industry heads or lobbyists receive government appointments that relate to their former private posts.

title: Revolving Loan Facility
definition: A revolving loan facility, often referred to as a revolver or revolving credit facility, offers businesses flexible access to funds by allowing them to draw, repay, and withdraw again throughout an agreed term. Unliketerm loans, which require repayment on a fixed schedule, revolving loans provide continual access to credit, enabling businesses to manage fluctuating cash flows more effectively. With the ability to repay and re-borrow as needs change, a revolving loan is a valuable financing solution for businesses seeking to stabilize their financial operations.

title: Rho
definition: Rho measures how the price of an option changes in response to shifts in the risk-free interest rate. It reflects thesensitivityof calls and puts to rate changes; calls typically gain value as rates rise, while puts often lose value.

title: Ricardian Equivalence
definition: The Ricardian Equivalence is an economic theory relating to government spending and taxation. The theory's assumption is that government deficit financing is neutral in its impact on the economy. Key figures involved in the development of the theory included David Ricardo and Robert Barro. Many see this theory as challenging the Keynesian idea that deficit spending can boost the economy in the short term. There are many assumptions and criticisms regarding the realistic application of the theory.

title: Rider
definition: A rider amends a basic insurance policy by adding benefits or altering its terms, offering more flexible coverage. Whether you need extra protection for your home, life, or personal belongings, insurance riders can meet unique needs. Though they come at an additional cost, riders require minimal underwriting compared to separate policies, making them a cost-effective option for customizing your insurance coverage.

title: Right of First Offer
definition: A Right of First Offer (ROFO) grants a rights holder the opportunity to purchase an asset, such as real estate or a business, before it’s listed for sale on the open market. Typically included in contracts like leases or business partnerships, this clause ensures the rights holder can make an initial bid, offering clear advantages to both parties. If the right holder is no longer interested in the property, the seller can then sell it to a third party. Rights of first offer are most commonly used in the real estate industry and the sale of businesses.

title: Right of First Refusal
definition: A Right of First Refusal (ROFR) is a contractual provision that ensures the right holder has the priority to purchase an asset before others can. Upon receiving an offer from a third party, the asset owner must present this offer to the right holder, who can choose to match it or let it pass to the third party. Often found in real estate and venture capital agreements, ROFR grants tenants the chance to buy properties they rent and provides venture capitalists assurances about their investments. This article explores how ROFR works, its advantages and disadvantages, and its role across different sectors.

title: Right of Rescission
definition: The right of rescission, established under theTruth in Lending Act (TILA), allows borrowers to cancel certain home loans within three business days without penalty. It applies to home equity loans, lines of credit, and some refinance transactions, but not to loans used to purchase or build a home.

title: Right-to-Work Law
definition: Right-to-work (RTW) laws give employees the choice to join or not to join alabor unionand allow them to opt out of paying union dues even in unionized workplaces. Rooted in the National Labor Relations Act and expanded by the Taft-Hartley Act, these laws now exist in 26 states, with no federal version in place. Supporters say they expand worker freedom, while critics argue they weaken unions and contribute to lower wages.

title: Rights Offering (Issue)
definition: A rights offering allows existing shareholders to buy additional stock shares at a discounted price. This financial option can be advantageous for companies seeking to raise capital without the need for shareholder approval, although it may dilute existing shares. Rights offerings are usually available for a limited time and can be bought or sold on the market, providing shareholders with flexibility and control over their investments.

title: Ring-Fence
definition: A ring-fence is a virtual barrier that segregates a portion of a company's financial assets from the rest. This may be done to reserve money for a specific purpose, to reduce taxes on the individual or company, or to protect theassetsfrom losses incurred by riskier operations. Moving a portion of assetsoffshoreto reduce an investor's net worth or lower the taxes due on income is one example of ring-fencing.

title: Ripple
definition: Ripple is a blockchain-based digital payment company that has created a network and protocol that uses thecryptocurrencyXRP and the XRP Ledger. Ripple's main focus is as a payment settlement asset exchange and remittance system, similar to the SWIFT system for international money and security transfers used by banks and financial intermediaries dealing across currencies.

title: Ripple (Cryptocurrency)
definition: Ripple is a blockchain-based digital payment company that has created a network and protocol that uses thecryptocurrencyXRP and the XRP Ledger. Ripple's main focus is as a payment settlement asset exchange and remittance system, similar to the SWIFT system for international money and security transfers used by banks and financial intermediaries dealing across currencies.

title: Risk
definition: Risk, in financial terms, is the chance that an outcome or an investment's actual gains will differ from an expected outcome, usually leaving one worse off.

title: Risk-Adjusted Return
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Risk-Adjusted Return On Capital (RAROC)
definition: The risk-adjusted rate on capital is a financial metric that adjusts the profitability of an investment by its expected risk.

title: Risk Analysis
definition: Risk analysis is the process of assessing the likelihood of an adverse event occurring within the corporate, governmental, or environmental sectors.

title: Risk Assessment
definition: Risk assessment is a general term used across many industries to determine the likelihood of loss on an asset, loan, or investment. Assessing risk is essential for determining how worthwhile a specific investment is and the best process(es) to mitigate risk. It presents the upside reward compared to therisk profile. Risk assessment is important in order to determine therate of returnan investor would need to earn to deem an investment worth the potential risk. Qualitative and quantitative methods provide different insights into risk management and decision-making.

title: Risk-Averse
definition: An investor is considered risk averse if they prefer choices with less uncertainty, even if the potential reward is smaller. They prioritize avoiding losses over possibly maximizing gains.

title: Risk-Based Capital Requirement
definition: A risk-based capital requirement is a regulation that compels financial institutions to hold sufficient capital relative to their risk exposures to promote financial stability.

title: Risk Control
definition: Risk control is a critical component of business management that strives to minimize and manage the risks a company faces in the course of doing business.

title: Risk-Free Asset
definition: A risk-free asset is an investment that offers a secure return with virtually no chance of losing value, typically exemplified by U.S. Treasury bills, which are backed by the full credit of the U.S. government. For those seeking stability in their investments, risk-free assets promise a low but dependable rate of return.

title: Risk-Free Rate Of Return
definition: The risk-free rate of return is a theoretical benchmark in finance, representing the expected return on an investment with no risk of financial loss. In practical terms, it is often associated with highly secure government securities, such as the three-month U.S. Treasury bill, thought to carry minimal risk due to the low likelihood of government default. Understanding therisk-freerate is crucial for investors, as it sets a baseline for assessing the attractiveness of other riskier investment opportunities.

title: Risk Management
definition: Risk management analyzes sources of risk in financial matters and measures them to make decisions about how to move forward.

title: Risk Measures
definition: Risk measures are statistical measures that are historical predictors of investment risk andvolatility, and they are also major components inmodern portfolio theory (MPT). MPT is a standard financial and academic methodology for assessing the performance of a stock or a stock fund as compared to itsbenchmarkindex.

title: Risk Neutral
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Risk-Neutral Measures
definition: A risk-neutral measure is a probability measure used in mathematical finance to aid in pricing derivatives and other financial assets. Risk-neutral measures give investors a mathematicalinterpretation of the overall market’s riskaverseness to a particular asset, which must be taken into account in order to estimate the correct price for that asset.

title: Risk-Neutral Probabilities
definition: Risk-neutral probabilities are probabilities of potential future outcomes that are adjusted forrisk. They simplify the fair pricing ofassetsby removing risk from the equation and focusing on expected returns. Risk-neutral probabilities are important to the valuation of derivatives. A key assumption in computing risk-neutral probabilities is the absence of arbitrage.

title: Risk-On Risk-Off
definition: Risk-on risk-off investing describes how changes in investor risk tolerance influence market activity, impacting asset prices and investment decisions. In risk-on environments, investors favor higher-risk investments as market conditions appear favorable. Conversely, risk-off environments drive investors toward lower-risk assets during periods of uncertainty. Understanding these dynamics is crucial for navigating market fluctuations.

title: Risk Parity
definition: Risk parity is a portfolio allocation strategy that uses risk to determine allocations across various components of an investment portfolio. This method stems frommodern portfolio theory (MPT)but enhances it by focusing on risk-weighted allocations, allowing investors to target specific risk levels.

title: Risk Premium
definition: A risk premium represents the extra return expected from an investment beyond the risk-free rate. It compensates investors for the additional risk they undertake, similar to hazard pay in high-risk jobs. High-risk investments, like stocks or less stable bonds, must promise higher potential returns to justify the risk of potential loss.

title: Risk Profiles
definition: Ariskprofile is an analysis of the types of threats faced by both individuals and companies. For individuals, a risk profile shows how much risk they're willing to take with investments. A company's risk profile refers to the internal and external risks that may pose a threat to the business's stability or profitability, and how those risks can be reduced.

title: Risk-Return Tradeoff
definition: Risk-return tradeoff is a trading principle that establishes adirect relationship between risk and potential returns.

title: Risk Reversal
definition: Risk reversals, often termed protective collars,hedgelong or short positions by combiningputandcalloptions. This strategy shields against adverse price shifts in underlying assets, albeit at the cost of capping potential profits. For instance,investors holding long positions might buy a put option and sell a call option to craft a short risk reversal, thereby managing risk without relinquishing their stock holdings.

title: Risk/Reward Ratio
definition: The risk/reward ratio, also known as the risk/return ratio, enables investors to evaluate potential returns against risks when deciding on investments. This critical tool helps determine the attractiveness of investment opportunities by comparing the dollar amount at stake with the anticipated profit.

title: Risk Tolerance
definition: Risk tolerance is the degree of uncertainty and financial loss that an investor is willing to take on an investment in exchange for a possible higher return.

title: Risk-Weighted Assets
definition: Banks are required to hold a minimum amount of capital to remain solvent and protect their depositors' investments. To determine how much capital to maintain, banks assign risk to every type of asset. These are referred to as risk-weighted assets (RWAs), and they're assigned risk in the form of a percentage, with 100% being the riskiest and 0% being no risk.

title: Rival Good
definition: A rival good is an item whose use by one individual inherently limits its availability to others, distinguishing it from non-rival goods.

title: Roadshow
definition: Roadshows are meetings during an IPO that help underwriters gather investor demand data before setting the IPO offering price.

title: Robber Baron
definition: The term "robber barons" refers to America's prominentindustrialistsduring the late 19th century, often characterized by unethical business practices. Initially derogatory, this term described individuals like Andrew Carnegie, Cornelius Vanderbilt, and John D. Rockefeller, who amassed wealth through means that were considered unscrupulous at the time.

title: Robinson-Patman Act
definition: The Robinson-Patman Act is a federal law passed in 1936 to outlawprice discrimination. The Robinson-Patman Act is an amendment to the 1914Clayton Antitrust Actand is supposed to prevent "unfair" competition.

title: Robo Advisor
definition: A robo-advisor is a digital platform that provides automated, algorithm-driven financial planning and investment services with little human intervention.

title: Robotic Process Automation (RPA)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Roll Back
definition: A roll back, or roll backward, refers to a derivatives trading strategy that replaces an existing position with a new one that has a nearer expiration date. Aside from the contract's expiration date, other details are often not changed. For instance, a trader may roll back a September at-the-money call position to a June position with the same strike price.

title: Roll-Down Return
definition: The roll-down return is the potential profit a bondholder can earn from a bond's price moving toward its face value as it approaches maturity, assuming interest rates remain stable.

title: Roll Forward
definition: Roll forward refers to extending the expiration or maturity of an option, futures contract, or forward by closing the initial contract and opening a new longer-term contract for the same underlying asset at the then-current market price.

title: Roll Yield
definition: Roll yield refers to the returns that investors can achieve by transitioning a short-term futures contract into a longer-term one, primarily when the futures market exhibits backwardation. This financial mechanism can lead to profitable outcomes when futures prices are lower than expected cash prices, although risks exist in markets displaying contango.

title: Rolling Returns
definition: Rolling returns, also known as "rolling period returns," provide a smoothed annualized average of returns over multiple periods, ending with a specified year. These metrics offer investors critical insights into performance trends by smoothing out short-term fluctuations. Unlike analyzing isolated periods, rolling returns give a comprehensive look at a fund or portfolio's performance over time, enhancing investor decisions by showcasing historical performance in a more reliable manner.

title: Rollover
definition: A rollover is typically the transfer of holdings from one retirement plan to another without creating a taxable event. A rollover may also entail reinvesting funds from a mature security into a new issue of the same or similar security, or moving aforex (FX)position to the following delivery date, in which case the rollover incurs a charge.

title: Rollover Risk
definition: Rollover risk is the possibility that a borrower or investor may be unable torefinanceor replace maturingdebtor financial contracts favorably. This risk can arise when refinancing loans, issuing new debt, or trading derivatives that need to be rolled over at maturity. Economic conditions, especially changes in interest rates or market liquidity, can increase rollover risk by making it more expensive or difficult to secure new financing.

title: Roth 401(k)
definition: A Roth 401(k) is an employer-sponsored retirement savings plan funded using after-tax dollars.

title: Roth IRA
definition: A Roth IRA is a type of tax-advantagedindividual retirement accountto which you cancontribute after-tax dollarstoward your retirement. Known as an individual retirement arrangement by the IRS, the primary benefit of a Roth IRA is that yourcontributions and the earningson those contributions can grow tax-free and be withdrawn tax-free after age 59½, assuming the account has been open for at least five years. In other words, you pay taxes on money going into your Roth IRA, and then all future withdrawals are tax-free. The features of a Roth IRA account can vary between providers, and you should compare thebest Roth IRA accountsbefore making a choice.

title: Round Lot
definition: A round lot refers to a standard trading unit, usually 100 shares or a multiple of 100 for stocks, and about $100,000 in bonds, used to streamline trading and improve market efficiency. Unlike odd lots, which fall below these thresholds, round lots often come with smoother execution and lower costs, though electronic trading has made handling both much easier.

title: Routing Transit Number (RTN)
definition: A routing transit number (RTN) is a nine-digit number used to identify a bank orfinancial institutionwhen clearing funds for electronic transfers or processing checks in the U.S. Routing numbers are used in online banking and by clearinghouses for transactions. They're critical to the operations of online banking and clearinghouses.

title: Roy's Safety-First Criterion (SFRatio)
definition: Roy's Safety First Criterion is a risk assessment method used to evaluate an investment portfolio's ability to meet a minimum acceptable return threshold.

title: Royalty
definition: A royalty is a payment made to an individual or company for using their property, like copyrighted music, franchises, or natural resources. Such payments are defined in a licensing agreement, ensuring that both the licensor and licensee benefit from the arrangement. The agreement specifies the terms, outlining how the royalty is calculated, its duration, and any geographic limitations.

title: Rule 10b-5
definition: Rule 10b-5 is a central anti-fraud provision under theSecurities Exchange Act of 1934that prohibits manipulating or deceiving investors in securities transactions.

title: Rule 10b-18
definition: Rule 10B-18 is aSecurities and Exchange Commission(SEC) rule that is intended to reduce liability for companies (and their affiliated purchasers) when the company repurchases shares of the company's common stock. Rule 10B-18 is considered a safe harbor provision.

title: Rule 10b5-1
definition: Rule 10b5-1 is aSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC)regulation that allows insiders of public companies to set up a trading plan for selling stocks they own. Rule 10b5-1 permits major holders to sell a predetermined number of shares at a predetermined time and price to prevent insider trading, and is used by many corporate executives to avoid accusations ofinsider trading.

title: Rule 144
definition: Rule 144, enforced by the SEC, sets specific conditions for the resale of restricted, unregistered, and control securities to ensure transparency and prevent insider trading, offering exemptions from registration if criteria are met. The regulation applies to all types of sellers, in addition to issuers of securities, underwriters, and dealers.

title: Rule 144A
definition: Rule 144A, introduced under the Jumpstart Our Business Startups (JOBS) Act and implemented by the SEC in 2013, significantly transformed the trading of privately placed securities. This provision relaxes the restrictions of the original Rule 144 under the Securities Act of 1933, enabling trades among qualified institutional buyers (QIBs) and enhancing the liquidity of these investments. While Rule 144A provides a streamlined mechanism that does not require SEC registration for these transactions, it has sparked criticism due to potential risks, such as facilitating fraudulent foreign offerings and reducing security offerings to the general public.

title: Rule of 70
definition: The rule of 70 calculates the years it takes for an investment to double in value. It is calculated by dividing the number 70 by the investment's growth rate. The calculation is commonly used to compare investments with different annual interest rates.

title: Rule of 72
definition: The Rule of 72 is a formula used to calculate how long your investment will need to double in value, given its annual rate of return.

title: Rule 72(t)
definition: Rule 72(t) allows penalty-free withdrawals fromindividual retirement accounts (IRAs)and othertax-advantagedretirement accounts like401(k)sand403(b)plans. It is issued by theInternal Revenue Service (IRS).

title: Rule of 78
definition: The Rule of 78 is a loaninterest calculation methodthat benefits lenders by front-loading interest payments, impacting borrowers who might pay off loans early. This practice, common with fixed-rate non-revolving loans, means borrowers pay more interest earlier, reducing potential savings. Understanding this rule is vital for borrowers considering early repayment.

title: Rule of Thumb
definition: A rule of thumb is aheuristicguideline that provides simplified advice or some basic rule-set regarding a particular subject or course of action. It is a general principle that gives practical instructions for accomplishing or approaching a certain task. Typically, rules of thumb develop as a result of practice and experience rather than through scientific research or a theoretical foundation.

title: Run Rate
definition: The run rate, a straightforward financial metric, helps forecast a company's future performance based on current financial data. By extrapolating the latest revenue figures, businesses and analysts can produce annualized estimates that guide strategic planning and operational decisions. However, while useful, the run rate requires careful consideration of potential pitfalls, such as skewed projections from seasonal sales spikes or atypical large contracts. Understanding both its utility and risks ensures more accurate financial forecasting.

title: Runoff Insurance
definition: Runoff insurance extends protection to companies acquiring others by covering potential liabilities from claims that arise after a merger or closure.

title: Russell 1000 Index
definition: The Russell 1000 Index tracks the performance of the 1,000 largest publicly traded companies in the U.S., representing a significant portion of the overall market capitalization of U.S. equities.

title: Russell 2000 Index
definition: The Russell 2000 Index, launched in 1984 by the Frank Russell Company and now managed byFTSE Russell, assesses the performance of 2,000 small-cap companies within the broaderRussell 3000 Index. As a vital indicator of the U.S. economy, this index provides insights into the small-cap sector, representing approximately 7% of the Russell 3000’s total market capitalization. It is often favored by mutual fund investors for its comprehensive reflection of market opportunities in the small-cap segment.

title: Russell 3000 Index
definition: The Russell 3000 Index is a stock market index that tracks the performance of the 3,000 largest publicly traded companies in the United States, representing about 98% of the investable U.S. stock market.

title: S Corporation (S Subchapter)
definition: An S corp is a business structure that allows small businesses to pass income, deductions, and credits directly to shareholders, avoiding federal corporate tax while offering liability protection.

title: S&P 500 Index (Standard & Poor's 500 Index)
definition: The S&P 500 is a stock market index weighted by market capitalization that is made up of 500 of the largest public companies in the United States.

title: S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index
definition: The S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index is a list of companies from the Standard & Poor's 500 Index (the S&P 500) that have a track record of raising their dividends for at least 25 consecutive years. Each company is equally weighted within the index.

title: Safe Deposit Box
definition: Safe deposit boxes, found within the vaults of federated banks and credit unions, serve as highly secure metal containers for safeguarding valuable items, important documents, and sentimental keepsakes. Whether you're looking to store hard-to-replace paperwork like contracts orvaluable family heirlooms, these boxes are designed to provide enhanced security compared to most home environments. However, not everything should be placed in a safe deposit box. Understanding what to store and what to avoid is essential to maximizing their utility while minimizing risks.

title: Safe Harbors
definition: A safe harbor is a legal provision to sidestep or eliminate legal or regulatory liability in certain situations, provided that certain conditions are met.

title: Safe Haven
definition: A safe haven is an investment that people turn to during market turbulence to help protect their portfolios from steep losses. Depending on the downturn, different assets may serve this role, making due diligence important. Gold, Treasury bills, and certain stable currencies have historically acted as dependable safe havens, though their performance can vary across economic cycles.

title: Sale
definition: A sale is a financial transaction where an agreement is reached between parties to transfer ownership of goods, services, or assets in exchange for payment or compensation.

title: Sales and Purchase Agreement Explained: Definitions and Examples
definition: A sales and purchase agreement (SPA) is a legally binding document critical to transactions between buyers and sellers, detailing the terms and conditions agreed upon. Often utilized inreal estate, SPAs also extend to various business sectors. This agreement signifies the conclusion of negotiations, capturing the essential conditions for the sale.

title: Sales Lead
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Sales Tax
definition: A sales tax is aconsumption taximposed by the government on the sale of goods and services. A conventional sales tax is levied at thepoint of sale (POS), which is collected by the retailer and passed on to the government. A business may be liable for sales taxes in a given jurisdiction if it has a presence there, which can be abrick-and-mortarlocation, an employee, or an affiliate, depending on the laws in that jurisdiction.

title: Salvage Value
definition: Salvage value represents the expected value a company anticipates after fully depreciating an asset at the end of its useful life. This concept aids in calculating depreciation schedules and impacts how companies manage their assets' book values. Understanding salvage value involves determining the asset's remaining worth, usually by appraisals, cost percentages, or historical data.

title: Sampling
definition: Sampling is a statistical method that involves selecting a set number of random observations from a larger population for analysis.

title: Sampling Distribution
definition: A sampling distribution measures probability potential among random samples of a population.

title: Sampling Errors
definition: A sampling error occurs when a sample drawn from a population deviates from representing the true or entire population.

title: Samurai Bond Definition
definition: A Samurai bond is a yen-denominated bond issued in Tokyo by a non-Japanese company and subject to Japanese regulations.

title: Sarbanes-Oxley (SOX) Act of 2002
definition: The 2002 Sarbanes-Oxley Act sought to protect investors from costly financial scandals by strengthening corporate financial reporting and auditing standards.

title: Savings Account
definition: A savings account is an account at a bank or credit union that is designed to hold your money. Savings accounts typically pay a modest interest rate, but they are considered safe for parking cash that you want available for short-term needs.

title: Savings and Loan Crisis (S&L) Crisis
definition: The Savings and Loan Crisis in the late 20th century marked a period of speculative lending and financial collapse affecting over 1,000 institutions across the U.S. Arising from regulatory mismatches, moral hazards due to deregulation, fraud, and risky lending practices, the crisis demanded a massive taxpayer-funded bailout and led to significant financial reforms. This article explores the causes, the government’s response, and the lasting implications on the banking industry.

title: Say's Law of Markets
definition: Say's Law of Markets is an economic theory that links production to demand creation. The originator of the theory, French economistJean-Baptiste Say, believed that production, not money, fuels economic demand and growth. That is, the income generated by past production and sale of goods is the source of spending that creates the demand to purchase current production. Say's Law is found in Say's 1803 book, "Treatise on Political Economy, Or, The Production, Distribution, and Consumption of Wealth."

title: Scalability
definition: Scalability allows a company to thrive under rising market demands, maintaining or enhancing performance and profit margins as sales volumes grow. This adaptability is vital for embracing global markets with minimal resource stress, enabled by technological advancements.

title: Scalping
definition: Scalping is a trading strategy geared toward profiting from minor price changes in an asset's price. Traders who implement this strategy place anywhere from 10 to a few hundred trades in a single day in the belief that small moves in asset prices are easier to catch than large ones.

title: Scarcity
definition: Scarcity is a gap between the demand for a product and its supply, which is resolved only when its price rises to a level that limits its distribution to those willing and able to pay it.

title: Scenario Analysis
definition: Scenario analysis forecasts the future value of aportfolioby simulating changes in key factors like interest rates. Investors use it to understand how portfolios respond to different events, including worst-case scenarios. This technique applies mathematical and statistical principles to prepare for both likely and unlikely financial outcomes.

title: Understanding Schedule 13D: Filing Process & Key Requirements
definition: Schedule 13D is a criticalSECfiling required within 10 days of acquiring more than 5% of a company's voting shares. This form, also known as a "beneficial ownership report," provides transparency by disclosing significant ownership stakes. It ensures that markets and other shareholders are informed about potential changes in control, such as mergers or hostile takeovers. By understanding the requirements and purpose of Schedule 13D, investors can better navigate ownership changes in public companies.

title: Schedule 13G
definition: TheSecurities and Exchange Commission(SEC) Schedule 13G form is an alternative filing for the Schedule 13D form and is used to report a party's ownership of stock which exceeds 5% of a company's total stock issue. Schedule 13G is a shorter version of Schedule 13D with fewer reporting requirements. Schedule 13G can be filed in lieu of the SEC Schedule 13D form as long as the filer meets one of several exemptions.

title: All About Schedule A (Form 1040 or 1040-SR): Itemized Deductions
definition: Schedule A (Form 1040 or 1040-SR): Itemized Deductions is anInternal Revenue Service (IRS)form for U.S. taxpayers who itemize theirtax-deductibleexpenses rather than take thestandard deduction. Schedule A is an attachment toForm 1040that taxpayers use to report their annualincome taxes.

title: Schedule K-1 Federal Tax Form: What Is It and Who Is It for?
definition: Schedule K-1 is a federal tax document used to report the income, losses, and dividends for a business' or financial entity's partners or anS corporation'sshareholders. The K-1 form is also used to report income distributions from trusts and estates to beneficiaries.

title: Scope
definition: Scope, a crucial concept in project management, involves defining the objectives and requirements to complete a project. It’s vital to distinguish project scope, which outlines all necessary work, from product scope, which focuses on the final deliverable. Effective scope management helps organizations save time and reduce costs.

title: Scrips
definition: A scrip is is a substitute or alternative to legal tender. Holding a scrip entitles the bearer to receive something in return. Scrips come in many different forms, primarily as a form of credit, with the document acknowledging the debt. Scrips also represent a temporary document representing fractional shares resulting from a split or spin-off, or they may indicate currency issued by a private corporation such as frequent flier miles.

title: Seasonality
definition: Seasonality is a recurring pattern in time series data that occurs every calendar year, affecting business activities and economic indicators. Unlike cyclical effects, which vary in duration, seasonality happens within the same fiscal year. By exploring how companies adjust inventories, staffing, and economic analyses based on expected seasonal changes, we see seasonality's significant role in financial planning.

title: Seasonally Adjusted Annual Rate (SAAR)
definition: A seasonally adjusted annual rate (SAAR) is a rate adjustment used for economic or business data, such as sales numbers or employment figures, that attempts to remove seasonal variations in the data. Most data is affected by the time of the year, and adjusting for theseasonalitymeans that more accurate relative comparisons can be drawn between different time periods. SAAR is used by analysts to understand core business performance by eliminating seasonal biases.

title: What Is SEC Form S-1? Filing Steps & Amendment Guidelines
definition: SEC Form S-1 is the initial registration form for new securities required by theSECfor public companies that are based in the U.S. Any security that meets the criteria must have an S-1 filing before shares can be listed on a national exchange, such as the New York Stock Exchange. Companies typically file SEC Form S-1 when planning theirinitial public offering (IPO). SEC Form S-1 requires companies to explain the use of capital, outline their business model and competition, and provide a brief prospectus of the security, including pricing and any dilution of othersecurities.

title: SEC Yield
definition: The SEC 30-day yield is a crucial metric for comparing bond funds, offering a standardized calculation as mandated by theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC). This yield is based ondividendsand interest, less expenses, over a recent 30-day period. It provides investors a transparent view of potential annual earnings, helping make informed investment decisions.

title: Second World
definition: The outdated term "second world" included countries that were once controlled by the Soviet Union. Second-world countries werecentrally planned economiesand one-party states. Notably, the use of the term "second world" to refer to Soviet countries largely fell out of use in the early 1990s, shortly after the end of the Cold War.

title: Secondary Market
definition: Investopedia / Candra Huff

title: Understanding 1035 Exchanges: Tax-Free Insurance and Annuity Transfers
definition: A 1035 exchange enables the tax-free transfer of certain financial products, such as annuity contracts andlife insurancepolicies, as outlined in the IRS code. This provision lets you replace your existing financial contract with another one of a similar type without incurring tax liabilities.

title: Understanding Section 1231 Property: Definition, Examples, and Tax Benefits
definition: Section 1231 of the U.S. Internal Revenue Code provides an advantageous tax treatment for gains on the sale of real or depreciable business property held for more than a year. These gains are typically taxed at the favorable capital gains tax rate rather than as ordinary income. Section 1231 encompasses a wide range of properties, including buildings, machinery, land, timber, and leaseholds over a year old, but notably excludes certain categories, like poultry and patents.

title: Section 1245
definition: A part of the Internal Revenue Code (IRC), Section 1245 taxes gains on the sale of certain depreciated or amortized property at ordinary income rates.

title: Section 1250
definition: Section 1250 of the U.S. Internal Revenue Code requires the IRS to tax gains from the sale of depreciated real property as ordinary income if the accumulated depreciation surpasses what straight-line depreciation would calculate. This rule primarily applies to commercial and residential properties when the accelerated depreciation method is used, affecting investors' tax obligations significantly.

title: Sector
definition: A sector is an industry group made up of companies that are engaged in similar or related businesses, such as agriculture or technology.

title: Sector Breakdown: What It Is and How It's Used
definition: A sector breakdown is the mix of industrysectors, like technology or healthcare, held by a fund or portfolio, typically expressed as a percentage. Sector designations can vary depending on the fund’s investment criteria and overall objective.

title: Understanding Secular Trends in Stock Investing With Key Examples
definition: In finance, secular is a descriptive word used to refer to market activities that occur over the long term. Secular can also point to specific stocks orstock sectorsunaffected by short-term trends. Secular trends are not seasonal orcyclical. Instead, they remain consistent over time. Examples of secular trends include technology and consumer staples.

title: Securities And Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Understanding the Securities Act of 1933: Key Takeaways and Significance
definition: The Securities Act of 1933 was enacted to protect investors after thestock market crash of 1929. It requires issuers to register securities and make accurate disclosures so that investors can make informed decisions. It was signed into law by President Franklin D. Roosevelt as part of the New Deal.

title: Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
definition: The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission is an independent federal agency tasked with protecting investors and maintaining fair and efficient markets.

title: What Is the Securities Exchange Act of 1934? Reach and History
definition: The Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (SEA) was created to govern securities transactions on thesecondary market, after issue. Its goal was to ensure greater financial transparency and accuracy and less fraud or manipulation.

title: Securities Lending
definition: Securities lending happens when one investor lends a security, such as a stock or commodity, to another, usually though a broker.

title: Securitization
definition: Securitization pools assets and repackages them into interest-bearing securities.

title: Security
definition: Securities are common investment contracts that are sold to investors by corporations and governments to raise capital.

title: Security Market Line (SML) Definition and Characteristics
definition: The security market line (SML) shows different levels of systematic, or market risk, of variousmarketable securities, plotted against theexpected returnof the entire market at any given time.Also known as the "characteristic line," the SML is a visualization of the CAPM, where the x-axis of the chart represents risk (in terms ofbeta) and the y-axis of the chart represents expected return.

title: Seed Capital
definition: Seed capital is the type offinancingused to form astartup. Sources for obtaining seed capital include family, friends, and angel investors. It is important in covering a startup’s initial expenses, such as its business plans and operating costs. Seed capital progresses to venture capital, the first two of four funding stages required for a startup to establish itself as a business.

title: Seigniorage
definition: Seigniorage is the financial gain a government enjoys from issuing currency at a cost lower than its market value.

title: Self-Directed IRA (SDIRA)
definition: A self-directed IRA is a type of individual retirement account that let's you invest beyond traditional stocks and bonds. It's mainly used by investors who want more control over their retirement savings and are comfortable managing alternative investments themselves.

title: Self Employed Contributions Act (SECA)
definition: The Self-Employed Contributions Act (SECA) tax is imposed by the U.S. government on individuals who areself-employed, as opposed to those who work for an employer. It requires self-employed individuals to pay both the employer and employee shares of theFederal Insurance Contributions Act (FICA)tax. These contributions are crucial for funding Social Security and Medicare programs, ensuring that self-employed workers are covered by these essential social safety nets just like traditional employees.

title: Self-Employment
definition: Self-employment simply means working for yourself rather than for an employer.

title: Self-Regulatory Organization (SRO)
definition: A self-regulatory organization (SRO) is an entity such as anon-governmental organization, which has the power to create and enforce stand-alone industry and professional regulations and standards on its own.

title: Sell in May and Go Away
definition: "Sell in May and go away" is a saying based on stocks' historical underperformance during the six-month period from May to October.

title: Sell-Side
definition: The sell-side of finance involves creating, promoting, and selling stocks, bonds, and other financial tools. It also includes private capital markets, like private debt and equity placements. Sell-side firms create and manage products for the buy-side of finance.

title: Seller Financing
definition: Seller financing, often referred to as owner financing or purchase-money mortgage, is an alternative to traditional bank loans. Here, the seller becomes the lender, allowing buyers who may struggle with traditional financing to purchase a home directly. This method can feature greater flexibility in down payments and quicker settlements, making it appealing in tighter credit markets. However, both sellers and buyers should be aware of potential legal complexities and financial risks.

title: Selling, General, & Administrative Expense (SG&A)
definition: Selling, general, and administrative expenses (SG&A) are overhead expenses that keep a business running but are not directly tied to producing goods or services.

title: Senior Bank Loan
definition: A senior bank loan is adebt financingobligation issued to a company by a bank or similar financial institution and then repackaged and sold to investors. The repackaged debt obligation consists of multiple loans. Senior bank loans hold legal claim to the borrower's assets above all other debt obligations.

title: Sensex
definition: The Sensex is the benchmark index of the BSE in India. The Sensex comprises 30 of the largest and most actively traded stocks on the BSE and provides a gauge of India's economy. It is float-adjusted and market capitalization-weighted.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
definition: Sensitivity analysis shows how different values of an independent variable affect a dependent variable under a given set of assumptions. Companies use sensitivity analysis to identify opportunities, mitigate risk, and communicate decisions to upper management.

title: Serial Correlations
definition: Serial correlation occurs in atime serieswhen a variable and a lagged version of itself (for instance a variable at times T and at T-1) are observed to be correlated with one another over periods of time. Repeating patterns often show serial correlation when the level of a variable affects its future level. In finance, thiscorrelationis used by technical analysts to determine how well the past price of a security predicts the future price.

title: Series I Bond
definition: Series I Bonds, also known as I Bonds, are a type of savings bond issued by the U.S. Treasury that offer investors a unique combination of safety and protection against inflation. These bonds are considered among the most low-risk investments available, as they are backed by the full faith and credit of the United States government.

title: Series 3
definition: The Series 3 exam certifies professionals to engage in the trading of commodity futures and options.

title: Series 6
definition: A FINRA Series 6 license qualifies an individual to sell mutual funds, variable annuities, and other specific investment products.

title: Series 7
definition: The Series 7 exam is a required test for prospective securities representatives, focusing on their comprehensive understanding of various non-derivative financial products and the regulatory environment.

title: Series 9/10
definition: Series 9/10 is required to supervise sales activities in a general securities-focusedbranchand can only be taken after earning aSeries 7license. Administered byFINRA, the qualification consists of two exams, with Series 9 focused on options and Series 10 covering a wider range of supervisory topics. This article will review the major subject areas and sample questions found on both exams.

title: Series 24
definition: The Series 24, or General Securities Principal Qualification Exam, licenses individuals to supervise and manage activities at abroker-dealer. Holders can oversee trading, market-making, underwriting, and advertising to ensure regulatory compliance.

title: Series 57
definition: Series 57, also known as the Securities Trader Representative Exam, is an examination administered by theFinancial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA). Passing the Series 57 exam is a requirement for those who wish to become professional traders of eitherequitiesorconvertible debt securities.

title: Series 63
definition: The Series 63 exam is a critical qualification for securities professionals in the U.S., focused on state-level securities laws and regulations.

title: Series 65
definition: Series 65 is an exam and securities license required for individuals to act as investment advisers in the U.S.

title: Series 66
definition: Series 66, the NASAA Uniform Combined State Law Examination, is an exam and license that is meant to qualify individuals asinvestment advisor representatives (IARs)or securities agents. The Series 66 covers topics relevant to providing investment advice and effecting securities transactions for clients. Once licensed, IARs can legally conduct financial advising and wealth management duties.

title: Series B Financing
definition: Series B financing is a funding round aimed at accelerating a company's growth and expansion after demonstrating initial success and viability.

title: Series EE Bond
definition: The Series EE Bond (often referred to as a "Patriot Bond") is a non-marketable, interest-bearing U.S. government savings bond. These bonds are guaranteed to at least double in value over the typical 20-year initial term. Some Series EE bonds have total interest-paying lives that extend beyond the originalmaturity date, up to 30 years from issuance. Coupon rates for Series EE Bonds are determined at the time of issuance and are based on the percentage of the long-term Treasury rates.

title: Service Charge
definition: A service charge is a fee collected to pay for services related to the primary product or service being purchased. The charge is usually added at the time of the transaction.

title: Service Sector
definition: The service sector of the economy is focused on the activities that involve helping people, offering support, or performing tasks, rather than creating or selling physical items.

title: Severance Pay
definition: Severance pay provides financial support to employees when their positions are terminated due to layoffs or job elimination. This compensation, sometimes extending beyond regular pay, often includes benefits like health insurance or outplacement services to ease job transitions. Employers are not legally required to offer it, but many do to mitigate the impact of termination. Understanding severance packages, outlined in handbooks or contracts, helps employees manage their finances during career transitions and assess the impact on unemployment benefits.

title: Shadow Banking System
definition: The shadow banking system describesfinancial intermediariesthat participate in creating credit but are not subject to regulatory oversight.

title: Shadow Pricing
definition: A shadow price is the estimated cost of something that is not normally sold in the market. Businesses use shadow prices to quantify the costs and benefits associated with a project, but these estimates rely on subjective assumptions.

title: Share Certificate
definition: A share certificate is a paper document that indicates ownership of a certain number of shares in a company.

title: Share Class
definition: A share class is a way for companies or mutual funds to organize their stock or shares. Share classes are important because they come with different rights, costs, and benefits. Some classes have more voting power, while others might have lower fees. Common types include Class A and Class B shares, and mutual funds also have different share classes with varying costs and features. Understanding these differences helps investors make better decisions about how to invest their money

title: Share of Wallet (SOW)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Share Repurchase
definition: A share repurchase is a reduction in the number of a public company's shares outstanding. It's accomplished by buying a portion of its shares on the open market. The company might buy the shares directly or offer shareholders the option of tendering their shares at a fixed price.

title: Shareholder
definition: Shareholders own a corporation in tandem with other shareholders, each in a percentage equivalent to the number of shares of stock they hold.

title: Shareholder Activist
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Shareholder Equity (SE)
definition: Shareholder equity is a company’s net worth and the total amount available for return to shareholders after paying off all debts.

title: Shareholder Equity Ratio
definition: The shareholder equity ratio measures how much of a business's assets are financed by equity.

title: Shareholder Value
definition: Shareholder value reflects a company's success in delivering financial gains to its equity owners, driven by strategic management decisions that optimize earnings, free cash flow, dividends, and share prices. Understanding and maximizing shareholder value is crucial for long-term business sustainability.

title: Shareholder Value Added (SVA)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Shareholders' Agreement
definition: A shareholders' agreement is a critical document outlining how a company operates, detailingshareholderrights and obligations. This agreement ensures fair treatment, outlines share pricing, and determines future shareholder decisions. Unlike company bylaws, which form the legal backbone, shareholder agreements focus on active shareholder rights and are crucial for small corporations.

title: Shares
definition: Shares are units of ownership in a company that investors can purchase. The terms "shares" and "stocks" are often used interchangeably; however, they are technically distinct. "Stock" is the financial instrument a company issues, and a "share" is a single instance of that financial instrument.

title: Sharing Economy
definition: The sharing economy describes apeer-to-peereconomic model enabling individuals to utilize and monetize idle assets through community-based online platforms. This system, facilitated by modern technology, promotes short-term transactions and collaboration, allowing participants to maximize resources by renting out underused items like cars and spare rooms. As it evolves, the sharing economy faces both immense growth and notable challenges, including regulatory hurdles and market transformation as platforms become business-centric.

title: Sharpe Ratio
definition: The Sharpe ratio shows whether a portfolio's excess returns are attributable to smart investment decisions or luck and risk.

title: What Is a Shell Corporation? How It's Used, Examples and Legality
definition: A shell corporation is a corporation without active business operations or significant assets. These types of corporations are not all necessarily illegal, but they are sometimes used illegitimately, such as to disguise business ownership from law enforcement or the public. Legitimate reasons for a shell corporation include such things as astartupusing the business entity as a vehicle to raise funds, conduct ahostile takeover, or to go public.

title: Sherman Antitrust Act
definition: The Sherman Antitrust Act is a U.S. law that was passed in 1890 that prohibits monopolies and aims to foster competition.

title: Shooting Star
definition: A shooting star is a candlestick that signals a potential bearish reversal. It is characterized by a small real body near the lower end of the candlestick, an upper shadow that is at least twice the size of the body, and little or no lower shadow.

title: Short (or Short Position)
definition: A short position involves selling a security with the expectation of buying it back at a lower price, aiming for profit if the price drops. Among the strategies available to investors, shorting is best suited for those confident in their prediction of falling prices. Knowing the difference betweennakedand covered shorts, along with the potential risks like unlimited losses, is crucial before engaging in short selling.

title: Short Call
definition: A short call strategy in options trading involves a trader selling (writing) a call option, betting the underlying asset's price will fall. While the seller gains a premium, the risk includes potentially unlimited losses if the asset price rises above the strike price, making it a strategy for experienced traders.

title: Short Covering
definition: Short covering involves buying back securities to close an open short position, either at a gain or a loss. This process is known to lead to short squeezes, as seen during the GameStop stock frenzy, and it requires careful analysis of metrics like short interest and the short interest ratio. Discover how short covering impacts market movements and trader strategies in this article.

title: Short Interest
definition: Short interest is the total number of shares of a particular stock that have been sold short by market participants but have not yet been covered or closed out. It's often expressed as a percentage of the total stock's total outstanding shares.

title: Short Interest Ratio
definition: The short interest ratio measures market sentiment by comparing shorted shares toaverage daily trading volume. It's calculated by dividing shares soldshortby average daily volume. The short interest ratio isn't always timely because it uses bi-weekly reports; it's crucial to check it with real-time data. In addition, the short interest ratio can change due to fluctuating trading volumes and the number of shares shorted. It's a useful tool, but investors shouldn't rely on it without considering other indicators and live market data.

title: Short Put
definition: A shortputrefers to when a trader opens an options trade by selling orwritinga put option. The trader who buys the put option islongthat option, and the trader who wrote that option isshort.

title: Short Run
definition: The short run is at least one fixed input in the future while others are variable for business, or an economy behaving differently depending on how long it has to react to certain stimuli.

title: Short Sale
definition: A short sale is the sale of a borrowed asset, such as a bond or stock. It is generally a transaction in which an investor borrows a security from a broker, believing they will be able to sell it for more than the market price at the time of borrowing. The seller is then required to return an equal number of shares at some point in the future. This strategy requires an advanced understanding of markets and how they might be timed.

title: Short Selling
definition: Short selling is a trading strategy in whicha trader aims to profit from a decline in a security's price by borrowing shares and selling them in the hopes that the stock price will eventually fall, enabling them to buy the shares back for less money.

title: Short Squeeze
definition: A short squeeze happens in financial markets when the price of an asset rises sharply, causing traders who had sold short to close their positions. It occurs when asecurityhas a significant amount ofshort sellers, meaning lots of investors are betting on its price falling.

title: Short-Term Debt
definition: Short-term debt is a financial obligation recorded on a company's balance sheet that must be settled within the upcoming 12 months.

title: Short-Term Investments
definition: Short-term investments are financial assets that can easily be converted to cash and used for near-term goals.

title: Shortfall
definition: A shortfall is an amount by which a financial obligation or liability exceeds the amount of cash that is available to pay it. It can be temporary or persistent. If persistent, it can be due to inadequate financial management. A shortfall, while a significant concern, is usually quickly remedied withshort-term loans,equityinjections, and better cash management practices.

title: Shrinkage
definition: Inventory shrinkage represents the difference between recorded and actual stock levels. Commonly found in retail, it arises from factors like theft, administrative mistakes, and vendor fraud, leading to profit losses. Realizing the importance of managing shrinkage helps retailers maintain profitability and operational efficiency.

title: Shutdown Points
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Signature Loan
definition: When cash flow is tight, several different types of personal loans offer a possible solution. Signature loans offer the bestterms—they only require your signature as collateral and typically offer more attractive rates than other types of unsecured debt.

title: Silent Partner
definition: A silent partner primarily provides capital to a business with no active role in daily management. Often termed a limited partner, theirliabilityis typically restricted to the amount invested. Their involvement might extend to offering business contacts, guidance when requested, and mediating partners' disputes. Entrepreneurs often welcome them for startupcapital, although long-term profits sharing might lead some to consider a buyout.

title: Silk Route
definition: The Silk Route, known for linking China to the Mediterranean from the 2nd century B.C. to the 14th century A.D., was pivotal for trade. It facilitated the exchange of silk, fabrics, spices, and grains, leading to cultural and economic exchanges that reshaped the regions it connected. China's modern Belt and Road Initiative aims to revive this pathway, enhancing trade and connectivity with over 60 countries.

title: Silo Mentality
definition: A silo mentality is a reluctance to share information with employees of different divisions in the same company. This attitude is seen as reducing the organization’s efficiency and, at worst, contributing to a damagedcorporate culture.

title: Simple Agreement for Future Tokens (SAFT)
definition: A simple agreement for future tokens (SAFT) is an investment contract offered by cryptocurrency developers to accredited investors. Because SAFTs are considered a security instrument, they must be filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

title: Simple Interest
definition: Simple interest is an interest charge that borrowers pay lenders for a loan. It is calculated using the principal only and does not includecompoundinginterest. This formula tends to benefit borrowers, who pay less interest in the long run.

title: Simple Moving Average (SMA)
definition: A simple moving average (SMA) is a tool used in financial analysis to determine the average price of an asset over a set number of periods, typically calculated using closing prices. By smoothing out price fluctuations, the SMA aids investors in identifying potential trends and making informed decisions.

title: Simple Random Sample
definition: A simple random sample is a group selected from a larger population where every individual has an equal chance of being chosen. This type of sample aims to fairly represent the whole population without bias.

title: Simplified Employee Pension (SEP)
definition: A Simplified Employee Pension IRA (SEP IRA) is a type of individual retirement plan for the self-employed and small-business owners. It is similar to an IRA but with higher contribution limits.

title: Sinking Fund
definition: A sinking fund is a fund containing money set aside or saved to pay off a debt or bond. A company that issues debt will need to pay that debt off in the future, and the sinking fund helps to soften the hardship of a large outlay of revenue. A sinking fund is established so the company can contribute to the fund in the years leading up to thebond'smaturity.

title: Six Sigma
definition: Six Sigma is a set of techniques and tools used to improve business processes. It was introduced in 1986 by engineer Bill Smith while working at Motorola. Six Sigma practitioners use statistics, financial analysis, andproject managementto identify and reduce defects and errors, minimize variation, and increase quality and efficiency.

title: Skewness
definition: Skewness measures the asymmetry in a set of observations where activity is concentrated in one range and less in another.

title: Understanding "Skin in the Game": Meaning, Examples, and SEC Guidelines
definition: Skin in the game describes insiders investing their own money in their company, often boosting investor confidence through visible commitment. These trades must be disclosed under SEC rules, with high-profile examples like Elon Musk and Tesla drawing public attention, though the signal can be less straightforward in industries like finance.

title: Slippage
