definition: A technical analyst, also known as achartistor market technician, is a securities researcher or trader who analyzes investments based on past market prices andtechnical indicators.

title: Technical Indicator
definition: Technical indicators are heuristic or pattern-based signals used within technical analysis to predict future price movements. By leveraging data such as price, volume, and open interest, traders can identify strategic entry and exit points. Common indicators include the Relative Strength Index (RSI), Money Flow Index (MFI), and Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD).

title: Technical Job Skills
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Technical Skills
definition: Technical skills may include knowledge of accounting principles, software programs, or the ability to operate a specific piece of machinery.

title: Technocracy
definition: A technocracy is a governance model where leaders are selected based on their technical skills rather than popular vote. These decision-makers, known as technocrats, wield expertise in specific domains to formulate policies. Although intended to rely on empirical data and objective methodology, technocracy faces critiques for potentially being undemocratic and ignoring public will.

title: Technology, Media, and Telecom Sector (TMT)
definition: The technology, media, and telecom (TMT) sector is critical for innovation and growth. It covers areas like hardware, semiconductors, software, media, and telecom, and includes major companies like IBM (IBM), Meta (META), and Netflix (NFLX). Companies in this sector invest in research and development (R&D) to create new products and services. Artificial intelligence (AI) is also shaping the TMT sector, changing how consumers use technology and media, making it an important area for investors.

title: Technology Sector
definition: The technology sector encompasses businesses dedicated to the research, development, and distribution of technologically based goods and services. This sector includes a vast array of companies involved in manufacturing electronics, developing software, and providing IT services. Notable tech companies like Apple, Google, and Microsoft drive growth and innovation within this dynamic industry, often making the tech sector a highly attractive target for investors seeking long-term potential.

title: Ted Spread
definition: TED is an acronym for the Treasury-EuroDollar rate. The TED spread measures the difference between the interest rate on short-term U.S. government debt and the rate on interbank loans, specifically contrasting the three-month U.S. Treasury bill with the three-month LIBOR. This metric is crucial for gauging credit risk because U.S. Treasury bills are deemed a safe investment, unlike interbank loans which can represent higher risk.

title: Telegraphic Transfers (TT)
definition: A telegraphic transfer (TT) is an electronic method of transferring funds used primarily for overseas wire transactions. These transfers are used most commonly withClearing House Automated Payment System(CHAPS) transfers in the U.K. banking system, or by theSociety for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunications(SWIFT) for international transactions.

title: Temporal Method
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Tenancy at Sufferance
definition: A tenancy at sufferance refers to a situation where a tenant remains in a rental property after their lease has expired, allowing the landlord to either continue collecting rent or initiate eviction proceedings.

title: Tenancy-at-Will
definition: A tenancy-at-will offers unique flexibility and can be terminated by either landlord or tenant without requiring a contract. Governed by state laws, this arrangement is often appealing due to its simple, open-ended nature and is sometimes used among familiar parties like family members. Despite the absence of a written lease, there are still legal protections and responsibilities for landlord and tenant alike, ensuring both parties maintain certain obligations and standards during the tenancy.

title: Tenancy by the Entirety
definition: Tenancy by the entirety refers to a form of shared property ownership that is usually reserved only for married couples. A tenancy by the entirety permits spouses to jointly own property as a single legal entity. This means that each spouse has an equal and undivided interest in the property.

title: Tenancy in Common (TIC)
definition: Tenancy in common (TIC) allows multiple parties to hold ownership interests in a property, offering flexibility in ownership percentages. However, unlike joint tenancy, TIC does not grant rights of survivorship, meaning a deceased owner's share passes to their estate rather than other co-owners. Recognizing the distinctions between TIC, joint tenancy, and tenancy by entirety can guide you in making informed decisions about property ownership.

title: Tenants by Entirety (TBE)
definition: Tenants by entirety (TBE) is a form of ownership that allows married couples to hold the title to a property together, requiring mutual consent for any modifications and giving the surviving spouse full ownership upon the other’s death. This ownership method is available in about half of the U.S. states and can apply to various types of property, including real estate and financial assets.

title: Tender
definition: A tender is a government or financial institution's invitation to bid for a project.

title: Tender Offer
definition: A tender offer is a bid by investors to purchase some or all of the shareholders' stock in a corporation. The purpose is to gain control or influence over a company by buying a significant share percentage.

title: Tenkan-Sen (Conversion Line)
definition: Tenkan-Sen, or Conversion Line, is the mid-point of the highest and lowest prices of an asset over the last nine periods. The Tenkan-Sen is part of a larger indicator called theIchimoku Kinko Hyo, which shows potentialsupport and resistanceareas based on different timeframes. Ichimoku Kinko Hyo roughly means "one look equilibrium chart," and is commonly called the Ichimoku Cloud indicator.

title: Tenor
definition: Tenor signifies the remaining lifespan of a financial contract, influencing its perceived risk. Often associated with bank loans, insurance contracts, andderivative products, tenor plays a crucial role in assessing financial agreements and managing risk.

title: Term Deposit
definition: A term deposit is a fixed-interest savings instrument that can only be withdrawn after the term ends.

title: Term Life Insurance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Term Loan
definition: A term loan provides borrowers with a lump sum of cash upfront in exchange for specific borrowing terms. Term loans are normally meant for established small businesses with soundfinancial statements. In exchange for a specified amount of cash, the borrower agrees to a certain repayment schedule with a fixed orfloating interest rate. Term loans may require substantialdown paymentsto reduce the payment amounts and the total cost of the loan.

title: Term Sheet
definition: A term sheet allows parties to negotiate and ask for clarification on details before a contract is signed.

title: Term Structure of Interest Rates
definition: The term structure of interest rates, also known as the yield curve, illustrates the relationship between bond interest rates and their maturities, offering insights into economic predictions, monetary policy, and market sentiment. Understanding this crucial financial barometer helps investors make informed decisions regarding economic health and investment strategies.

title: Term to Maturity
definition: A bond's term to maturity is the period during which investors earn interest before the issuer repays the principal at maturity. Terms vary (short-, intermediate, orlong-term), and may be affected by call or put provisions. For example, Disney's long-term bonds show how extended maturities can offer higher yields but greater interest rate risk.

title: Terminal Capitalization Rate
definition: The terminal capitalization rate, also known as the exit rate, is used to estimate the resale value of a property at the end of theholding period. The expectednet operating income(NOI) per year is divided by the terminal cap rate (expressed as a percentage) to getthe terminal value. Because it's usually set using comparable sales and property-specific factors like location, it can materially affect an investor's projected return.

title: Terminal Value (TV)
definition: Terminal value is the estimated value of a business or other asset beyond the cash flow forecast period and into perpetuity.

title: Termination of Employment
definition: Termination of employment is the end of an employee’s work with a company. An employee may be terminated from a job of their own free will or following a decision made by the employer. Employers who execute a termination of employment may do so for many reasons, including downsizing, poor job performance, or redundancies.

title: Terms of Employment
definition: Terms of employment detail the responsibilities and benefits associated with a job, as agreed upon by an employer and employee at the time of hiring. They define the work relationship and are sometimes known as an employment contract.

title: Terms of Trade (TOT)
definition: Terms of Trade (TOT) serves as an essential indicator of economic health, evaluating the ratio of export to import prices. A favorable TOT indicates a nation earns more from exports than it spends on imports, enhancing economic stability. Understanding TOT can aid in assessing capital flows and economic conditions, vital for policymakers and investors alike.

title: Tertiary Industry
definition: The tertiary industry is the part of the economy that provides services rather than producing goods, and includes medical providers, educators, financial services, and personal services, among others.

title: Testamentary Trust
definition: A testamentary trust is a trust that is established by with the instructions contained in alast will and testament. A trust is afiduciaryrelationship that allows a trustee, a third party, to manage assets on behalf of thetrust's beneficiaries.

title: What Is Tether (USDT)? Understanding Its Importance and Uses
definition: Tether (USDT) stands as a prominent stablecoin in thecryptocurrencymarket, uniquely pegged to the U.S. dollar to mitigate the volatility typical of digital currencies. Issued by Tether, under the umbrella of iFinex, which also operates BitFinex, USDT provides stability through its reserves in U.S. dollars.

title: Texas Ratio
definition: The Texas ratio is a financial metric used to gauge a bank's health by comparingnon-performing assetsto the sum of itstangible common equityand loanloss reserves. Originating from the 1980s Texas banking crisis, it was designed as an early warning system for credit troubles.

title: Tezos
definition: Tezos is a blockchain network hosting the associated digital token Tez (XTZ), which is also known as tezzie. Like other cryptocurrency blockchains, Tezos facilitates user participation indecentralized finance (DeFi),decentralized applicationsandnon-fungible token (NFT)projects.

title: The Greatest Generation: Definition and Characteristics
definition: "The Greatest Generation" is a term used to describe those Americans who grew up during theGreat Depressionand fought in World War II, or whose labor helped win it.

title: Theoretical Ex-Rights Price (TERP)
definition: TERP is the price a stock may have after a companyissues new rightsto shareholders. Companies offer more shares at a discounted price, which can affect stock prices by increasing the number of shares available. This makes it important for investors to evaluate a stock's value after a rights offering. To calculate TERP, sum the pre-offering market value of the shares with the funds raised from the rights issue, then divide by the total new shares. Any fluctuation of the stock market price during the offering period may present an opportunity for arbitrage due to the balancing of supply and demand.

title: Theoretical Value (Of a Right)
definition: The theoretical value of a right refers to the value of asubscription right. That value can differ from the price of a share without rights attached so it's useful for investors to know. After arights offeringis announced and until three days before the subscription rights expire (thecum rightsperiod), you can establish the value of the right easily. To do so, insert the share price, the subscription price, and the number of rights required to buy one share of stock into the following calculation:

title: Theory of Price
definition: The theory of price states that goods and services prices are decided anytime by the interplay of supply and demand.

title: Theory of the Firm
definition: In neoclassical economics—an approach to economics focusing on the determination of goods, outputs, and income distributions in markets through supply and demand—the theory of the firm is a microeconomic concept that states that afirmexists and make decisions to maximize profits.

title: There Ain't No Such Thing as a Free Lunch (TANSTAAFL)
definition: "There ain't no such thing as afree lunch" (TANSTAAFL) is an expression that highlights the hidden and opportunity costs behind seemingly free goods or services. It reminds us that every choice, whether personal or financial, carries a cost borne by someone, often unseen. While the saying originated from the practice of saloons offering "free" lunches in the 19th century, the principle still applies today to modern deals, discounts, and investments: nothing is ever truly free.

title: Theta
definition: Theta, the Greek letter θ, is an options risk factor that measures the speed of decline in the value of an option as it approaches its expiration date.

title: Third Party
definition: A third party plays a crucial role in transactions by acting as a neutral participant to facilitate smooth operations. For instance, an escrow company protects the interests of all parties in real estate deals, and a collection agency aids creditors in recovering debts. These entities enhance operational efficiency and reduce risks, often through outsourcing certain functions to maintain competitiveness.

title: Third-Party Insurance
definition: A third-party liability insurance policy protects the policyholder from financial claims made by another party.

title: Third-Party Transactions
definition: A third-partytransactionis a business deal involving an additional party beyond the buyer and seller. Thethird party'srole varies, from one-time payment processing on digital platforms to ongoing services like insurance or mortgage brokerage. With technology expanding such interactions, understanding third-party transactions is essential for making informed and secure financial decisions.

title: Third World
definition: A “third world" country is an outdated term once used for nations not aligned with major Cold War powers, now commonly, though imprecisely, used to describe developing or low-income countries.

title: Thomas Malthus
definition: Thomas Robert Malthus was an influential British economist best known for his theory on population growth, outlined in his 1798 book "An Essay on the Principle of Population."

title: Three Black Crows
definition: Three Black Crows is a bearish candlestick pattern that signals a potential reversal of an existing uptrend. It appears on candlestick charts as three consecutive long-bodied candles. Each opens within the real body of the previous one and closes lower, marking a strong downtrend. Traders look for this pattern, along with other indicators, to confirm bearish market signals, enhancing their strategic insights in volatile markets.

title: Three-Sigma Limits
definition: In statistics, the mean less three standard deviations is referred to as the three sigma limit.

title: Three White Soldiers: A Bullish Trading Pattern Guide
definition: Three white soldiers is a bullishcandlestick pattern that is used to predict the reversal of the current downtrend in a pricing chart. The pattern consists of three consecutive long-bodied candlesticks that open within the previous candle'sreal bodyand a close that exceeds the previous candle's high. These candlesticks should not have very longshadowsand ideally open within the real body of the preceding candle in the pattern.

title: Thrift Association
definition: Even though they're not as common as they used to be, thrifts, or savings and loan associations, still play an important part in many consumers' lives. Thrifts also refer tocredit unionsandmutual savings banksthat provide a variety of savings and loan services. They focus on savings and loan products, often with better interest rates for members compared to commercial banks

title: Thrift Bank
definition: A thrift bank helps consumers, offering services like savings accounts and mortgages. Unlike large commercial banks, thrift banks are community-oriented and serve local residents. Their development in the U.S. was shaped by laws like the Federal Home Loan Bank Act, which supported affordable housing and home financing. After World War II, thrift banks played a major role in expanding homeownership, though the Savings and Loan Crisis of the 1980s exposed weaknesses in the system and led to major reforms. Following regulations like the Dodd-Frank Act, thrift banks are evolving as they maintain their mission of supporting individuals and families.

title: Thrift Savings Plan (TSP)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Throughput
definition: Throughput is the quantity of a good or service that a company is capable of producing within a specified time.

title: Tick
definition: A tick is a measure of the minimum upward or downward movement of a security. It is limited by the smallest unit of currency local to the exchange where they are traded.

title: Tick Size
definition: Tick size is the the minimum price change of a trading instrument in a market.

title: Ticker Symbol
definition: A stock symbol is an abbreviation that's used to identify a stock for trading and often derives from the company's name.

title: Tier 1 Capital
definition: Tier 1 capital refers to the core capital held in a bank's reserves and is used to fund business activities for the bank's clients. It includes common stock, as well as disclosed reserves and certain other assets. Along with Tier 2 capital, the size of a bank's Tier 1 capital reserves is used as a measure of the institution's financial strength.

title: Tier 1 Capital Ratio
definition: The tier 1 capital ratio indicates a bank's financial strength, calculated as the ratio of a bank's core tier 1 capital to its total risk-weighted assets.

title: Tier 1 Common Capital Ratio
definition: Tier 1 common capital ratio measures a bank's core equity capital against its risk-weighted assets and helps show how resilient it is under financial stress. Regulators and investors use it to gauge overall capital strength and solvency. Unlike the broader Tier 1 capital ratio, it excludes preferred shares and non-controlling interests for a more focused view.

title: Tier 1 Leverage Ratio
definition: The Tier 1 leverage ratio measures a bank'score capitalrelative to its total assets. The ratio looks specifically atTier 1 capitalto judge how leveraged a bank is based on its assets. Tier 1 capital refers to those assets that can be easily liquidated if a bank needs capital in the event of a financial crisis. The Tier 1 leverage ratio is thus a measure of a bank's near-termfinancial health.

title: Tier 2 Capital
definition: The term tier 2 capital refers to one of the components of a bank's required reserves. Tier 2 is designated as the second or supplementary layer of a bank's capital and is composed of items such as revaluation reserves, hybrid instruments, and subordinated term debt. It is considered less secure thanTier 1 capital—the other form of a bank's capital—because it's more difficult toliquidate. In the United States, the overall capital requirement is partially based on the weighted risk of a bank's assets.

title: Tight Monetary Policy
definition: Tight orcontractionarymonetary policy is used by central banks like the Federal Reserve to slow overheated economic growth, cut spending when the economy accelerates too fast, or curb rising inflation. The policy is intended to curb quickly rising inflation.

title: Timber Investment Management Organization (TIMO)
definition: A Timber Investment Management Organization (TIMO) manages timberland investment portfolios for institutional investors, acting as brokers to help buy and manage timber properties that best suit their clients. TIMOs help institutional investors diversify their investment portfolios by providing opportunities in timberland, a less volatile asset class compared to stocks. Advantages of investing in timberland include portfolio diversification, potential as an inflation hedge, and historical performance in comparison to other asset classes like stocks and bonds.

title: Time Decay
definition: Time decay, often referred to as theta, is a crucial factor in options trading. It describes the decline in an option's value as itsexpiration dateapproaches. As the expiration date nears, the time available to profit from the trade decreases, accelerating time decay. This concept is essential for investors to grasp, as it affects the option's premium and ultimately impacts profitability.

title: Time Deposit
definition: A time deposit is a bank account that earns interest and has a maturity date in advance.

title: Time Horizon
definition: An investment time horizon is the period you hold an investment to reach specific financial goals. Whether you're saving for a short-term expense, a medium-term milestone, or a long-term objective, understanding your investment time horizon helps you choose the best strategies. Learn the differences among short, medium, and long-term investments and optimize your portfolio to align with your financial targets.

title: Time in Force
definition: Time in force is a special instruction used when placing a trade to indicate how long an order will remain active before it is executed or expires.

title: Times Interest Earned (TIE)
definition: The times interest earned ratio is a solvency ratio that indicates how well-positioned a company is to pay off the interest on its financial obligations using its current income.

title: Time Series
definition: A time series is a data set that tracks a sample over time. It's often used in investment analysis for predicting trends and patterns. Specifically, statistical methods, such as ARIMA, are used in the analysis and forecasting.

title: Time Value
definition: Time value refers to the portion of anoption'spremium that's attributable to the amount of time remaining until the expiration of the option contract. The premium of any option consists of two components: itsintrinsic valueand its extrinsic value.

title: Time Value of Money (TVM)
definition: The time value of money (TVM), also known as present discounted value (PDV), is a core financial principle that states that money today is worth more than the same amount in the future.

title: Time-Weighted Rate of Return (TWR)
definition: The time-weighted rate of return calculates the compounded growth rate of a portfolio over time.

title: Times-Revenue Method
definition: The Times-Revenue Method evaluates a company's worth by applying a multiple to its revenue over a specific period. This method offers a straightforward approach to estimating business value, though it doesn't account for expenses or profitability. It's frequently used for young firms with volatile earnings or in high-growth sectors.

title: TINA (There is No Alternative)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Tit for Tat
definition: Ingame theory, tit for tat refers to a strategy that players may use when confronted with a payoff matrix like that of aprisoner's dilemma. Formulated by mathematician and psychologist Anatol Rapoport, the strategy involves participants in an iterated prisoner's dilemma following a course of action consistent with their opponent's previous turn.

title: Title Insurance
definition: Title insurance is crucial in protecting both lenders and homebuyers from financial losses due todefects in a property's title. These defects can include back taxes, liens, or conflicting wills, which can become a burden for current owners. Understanding the types of title insurance and knowing how to purchase them can secure your investment during any real estate transaction.

title: Title Search
definition: A title search examines public records to verify property ownership and identify any existing claims or liens. This process, often carried out by title companies, is crucial for closingreal estatetransactions, ensuring a clean title, and facilitating the purchase. Understanding how atitlesearch works can protect buyers and lenders from potential legal obstacles.

title: To Be Announced (TBA)
definition: TBA, meaning "To Be Announced," refers to a forward-settling process inmortgage-backed securities (MBS)trading. In this market,pass-through securitiesfrom Freddie Mac, Fannie Mae, and Ginnie Mae are traded without immediate disclosure of the exact security, which increases liquidity. This system assumes MBS pools to be interchangeable, simplifying trades and enabling mortgage lenders to manage risk effectively.

title: Tobacco Tax/Cigarette Tax
definition: A tobacco tax, also known as a cigarette tax, is a government-imposed tax on tobacco products aimed at reducing consumption and generating revenue for healthcare programs.

title: Tobin Tax
definition: The Tobin tax is a tax levied on spot currency conversions, with the intention of disincentivizing short-term currency speculation, named after economist James Tobin.

title: Tokyo Price Index (TOPIX)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Tombstone
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Tomorrow Next (Tom Next)
definition: In forex trading, tomorrow next (tom next) refers to a short-term transaction that moves the settlement of a trade from the next business day (tomorrow) to the following day (next). This lets traders extend their positions without taking actual delivery of the currency. Tom next is commonly used in the highly liquidforexmarket to manage short-term exposures or roll over positions. Tom/Next transactions are closely related to FX swaps, helping traders adjust settlement dates efficiently while maintaining flexibility in their trading strategies.

title: Tontine
definition: A tontine is an investment plan where participants pool money and receivedividendsfrom shared investments, with the shares of deceased members redistributed to survivors. Originating in 17th-century Italy and later popular in Europe and the U.S., tontines were once used to fund royal courts and public projects. They declined after scandals and tighter regulations but have drawn renewed interest for offering potentially higher returns and lower fees than traditional annuities.

title: Too Big to Fail
definition: “Too big to fail” describes a business or business sector so ingrained in a financial system or economy that its failure would be disastrous. The term became popular during the 2007-2008 recession, when the government rescued many of the banks and financial institutions that had caused the crisis. Later financial reforms, such as the Dodd-Frank Act, attempted to limit the growth of banking institutions.

title: Top Line
definition: The top line is a reference to gross figures reported by a company, such as sales orrevenue. It is called the top line because it is displayed at the very top of a company'sincome statement, and is reserved for the reporting of gross sales or revenue. A company that increases its revenue or sales is said to be generating top-line growth. The opposite of the top line is the bottom line.

title: Top-Down Analysis
definition: Top-down investing is a strategy where investors start by analyzing high-level economic or industry data before focusing on more detailed regional or company-specific factors. It prioritizes macroeconomic indicators such as GDP, employment, and interest rates to guide investment decisions.

title: Top-Down Investing
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Understanding the Toronto Stock Exchange (TSX): Canada’s Largest Stock Market
definition: The Toronto Stock Exchange (TSX) is a Canadian stock exchange located in Toronto, Ontario. Founded in 1861, the TSX is Canada’s premier stock exchange with more than 1,500 listed companies, including those from the energy, mining, technology, and real estate sectors. The exchange is also home to international listings and exchange-traded products.

title: Tort Law
definition: Tort law is the area of the law that covers most civil suits, with the exception of contractual disputes,

title: Total Bond Fund
definition: A total bond fund is a mutual fund or exchange-traded fund that seeks to replicate a broad bondindex. A total bond fund owns many securities across a range ofmaturities, from both public and private sectors. The most common index used as a benchmark is the Barclays Aggregate Bond Index, which captures Treasury bonds, corporate bonds, municipal bonds and high-grade mortgage-backed securities.

title: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)
definition: The total cost of ownership (TCO) is thepurchase priceof anassetcombined with the ongoing costs of operation. Assessing the TCO means examining the product's overall value over time.

title: Total Debt Service Ratio
definition: The total debt service (TDS) ratio is a financial measure used to see how much of a person’s income goes toward paying debts. Lenders use it especially in the mortgage industry to determine if a borrower can handle new loans. It looks at housing costs and other debts like credit cards. A lower TDS ratio is better because it shows a borrower has more income available and is less risky. Most lenders prefer a TDS ratio within a certain range, and exceeding that range can make it harder to get approved for a loan.

title: Total Debt-to-Capitalization
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Total-Debt-to-Total-Assets
definition: The debt ratio, or total debt-to-total assets, is calculated by dividing a company's total debt by its total assets. It is also called the debt-to-assets ratio. It is aleverage ratiothat defines how much debt a company carries compared to the value of theassetsit owns.

title: Total Enterprise Value (TEV)
definition: Total enterprise value reflects a business's overall market value by incorporating both its equity and debt obligations.

title: Total Expense Ratio (TER)
definition: The TER covers the annual costs of running a fund based on all the known expenses associated with the fund’s operation.

title: Total Liabilities
definition: Total liabilities represent the cumulative amount of money an organization owes to creditors, suppliers, and other entities due to past transactions that must be settled over time.

title: Total Quality Management (TQM)
definition: Total quality management (TQM) is the continual process of detecting and reducing or eliminating errors in manufacturing. It streamlinessupply chain management, improves the customer experience, and ensures that employees are up to speed with training.

title: Total Return
definition: Total return measures an investment's performance by considering interest,capital gains, dividends, anddistributionsover a set period. It offers a comprehensive view of an asset's growth by accounting for income from fixed-income investments and market price changes, providing investors with a powerful tool for evaluating financial success. This article will explain the significance of total return, outline its calculation, and provide examples to demonstrate its importance in strategic investment planning.

title: Total Return Index
definition: A total return index capturescapital gainsand reinvested cashdistributions, includingdividendsand interest, providing a more complete performance measure than a nominal price index. It not only presents shareholders with a transparent view of growth but also accounts for non-dividend-paying stocks by reflectingearningsreinvested within companies. This article contrasts a total return index with a price return index, showcasing their key differences through examples like the S&P 500 Total Return Index.

title: Total Return Swap
definition: A Total Return Swap (TRS) is a financial derivative that enables one party to receive payments based on a set rate, while the other gains from the total return of a reference asset, such as an equity index, a basket of loans, or bonds. The party receiving the asset's return benefits from any income and capital appreciation without owning the asset, making TRS popular among hedge funds. In contrast, the party making the set rate payments effectively transfers the asset's market and credit risks to the return receiver.

title: Total Shareholder Return (TSR)
definition: Total shareholder return (TSR) calculates how much an investor receives from a stock over a specific period, taking both capital gains and dividends into account.

title: Total Utility
definition: Total utility is the overall satisfaction or fulfillment a consumer experiences when purchasing and using goods or services.

title: Understanding Tracker Funds: Definition, Function, and Examples
definition: A tracker fund, also called an index fund, is an investment fund designed to match the performance of a specificmarket indexsuch as the S&P 500 or the Dow Jones Industrial Average. It does this by holding the same securities, or a close approximation, so investors get broad exposure in a single, cost-effective product. Tracker funds are popular for their low expense ratios and built-in diversification, often offered as ETFs or traditional mutual funds.

title: Tracking Error
definition: Tracking error is thedivergencebetween the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark. It is often used to gauge how well an index fund or exchange-traded fund (ETF) replicates the performance of its benchmark. It is also used to measure the performance of actively-managed funds.

title: Tracking Stock
definition: Tracking stocks are issued by parent companies to represent the performance of a particular division or subsidiary, without granting the holder voting rights in the subsidiary itself.

title: Trade
definition: Trade involves voluntarily exchanging goods or services. Transactions happen only if both parties see a benefit.

title: Trade Credit
definition: Trade credit is abusiness-to-business (B2B)agreement in which a customer can purchase goods without paying cash upfront and pay the supplier at a later, scheduled date. Usually, businesses that operate with trade credits will give buyers 30, 60, or 90 days to pay, with the transaction recorded through an invoice.

title: Trade Deficit
definition: A trade deficit occurs when a country's imports exceed exports during a specific period.

title: Trade Finance
definition: Trade finance encompasses the financial instruments, processes, and products used to facilitate international trade and commerce.

title: Trade Liberalization
definition: Trade liberalization refers to the removal or reduction of restrictions or barriers on the free exchange of goods between nations.

title: Trade Line
definition: A trade line is created on a borrower’s credit report to track all credit account activity. Each line of credit, such as a mortgage, car loan, student loan, credit card, or personal loan, has its own trade line. A trade line includes information on the creditor, the lender, and the type of credit extended. All relevant information is used bycredit reporting agenciessuch as Experian, Equifax, and TransUnion to determine your credit score. The data can impact new credit applications and your creditworthiness. So be sure to review it periodically to ensure it's valid and error-free.

title: Trade Sanction
definition: Trade sanctions are legal restrictions on trade with a country.Trade sanctionsare a subcategory of economic sanctions, which are economic penalties imposed on a country to accomplish policy goals beyond the sanctioned economic activity. They have potential benefits and disadvantages for consumers and businesses.

title: Trade Secret
definition: A trade secret is any company information, such as a practice, a process, a formula, a technique, or device, that is kept confidential from all except those with a need to know.

title: Trade Signal
definition: A trade signal indicates an opportunity to either buy or sell a security or other asset, generated by analysis. That analysis can be human-generated usingtechnical indicatorsor generated using mathematicalalgorithmsbased on market action, possibly in combination with other market factors, such as economic indicators.

title: Trade Surplus
definition: A trade surplus indicates a positivebalance of trade, where a nation'sexportsexceed itsimports, resulting in a net inflow of cash from foreign markets. This can lead to employment growth and economic expansion but may also cause higher prices and currency appreciation. Understanding trade surplus helps grasp a nation's economic position, along with the benefits and potential challenges involved.

title: Trade War
definition: A trade war is an economic dispute between two countries. It can occur when one country retaliates against another's perceived unfair trading practices with restrictions, such as tariffs, on imports.

title: Trademark
definition: A trademark legally recognizes a company's brand ownership.

title: Trader
definition: A trader buys and sells financial assets like stocks, bonds, and commodities, aiming to generate profits through short-term market fluctuations. Unlike investors who focus on long-term gains, traders capitalize on short-term trends using strategies such as scalping, day trading, and swing trading. Essential skills for trading include risk management, analytical abilities, and emotional intelligence, all of which help traders navigate the fast-paced financial markets effectively.

title: Trading Account
definition: A trading account is an investment account containing securities, cash, or other assets.

title: Trading Book
definition: A trading book is the portfolio offinancial instrumentsheld by a brokerage or bank. They might be bought or sold to facilitate trading for customers or to hedge against different forms of risk.

title: Trading Desk
definition: A trading desk is a physical location where transactions for buying and sellingsecuritiesoccur. Depending on the institution, trading desks may host proprietary traders, brokers who match buyers and sellers, or both. They provide market liquidity.

title: Trading Halt
definition: A trading halt is a temporary suspension of trading for a particularsecurityor securities at one exchange or across numerous exchanges. Trading can be halted in anticipation of a news announcement, to correct an order imbalance, as a result of a technical glitch, due to regulatory concerns or because the price of the security or an index has moved rapidly enough to trigger a halt based on exchange rules.

title: Trading House
definition: A trading house is a business that specializes in facilitatingtransactionsbetween a home country and foreign countries. A trading house is an exporter, importer and also a trader that purchases and sells products for other businesses. Trading houses provide a service for businesses that want international trade experts to receive or deliver goods or services.

title: Trading Platform
definition: A trading platform is user-friendly software that allows investors to manage trades and portfolios through a brokerage or financial intermediary. Trading platforms enable access to diverse markets, provide key features such as real-time quotes, advanced charting tools, and educational resources, and facilitate account monitoring. When deciding upon a platform for your investing, be sure to evaluate fees, features, and each provider's reputation. Thebest trading platformsshould offer a solid mix of robust features and low fees.

title: Trading Strategy
definition: A trading strategy offers a systematic approach for buying and selling securities based on structured rules and criteria. These strategies, customizable to your investment goals, can range from simple to complex, often relying on technical indicators, fundamental analysis, or a mix of both. Successful strategies require regular evaluation and adaptation as market conditions and personal objectives evolve.

title: Trading Session
definition: A single business day in a financial market from that market’sopening bellto itsclosing bellis typically its trading session. The markets forforex, futures, stocks, and bonds all have their own characteristics that define their respective trading sessions for a given day. The primary trading hours naturally differ from one country to another due to contrasting time zones.

title: Traditional IRA
definition: A traditionalindividual retirement account (IRA)allows individuals to deposit pre-tax income into investments that can grow tax-deferred. The IRS assesses no capital gains or dividend income taxes until the beneficiary makes a withdrawal. Individual taxpayers can contribute from qualified earned compensation.

title: Tragedy of the Commons
definition: The tragedy of the commons unfolds when individuals prioritize personal gain over community welfare, depleting shared resources like water and land. Without exclusive claims to these resources, people exploit them, leading to underinvestment and eventual depletion. This economic theory, introduced by Garrett Hardin, warns of societal harm from unregulated consumption and highlights overpopulation as a historical example. Understanding its causes and solutions can guide more sustainable resource management.

title: Trailing 12 Months
definition: Trailing 12 months (TTM) is used to describe the data for the past 12 consecutive months of a company's reported financial figures. Using these figures provides a more current picture of a business's financial performance than its annual filings and reports, which may contain outdated information.

title: Trailing Price-to-Earnings (Trailing P/E)
definition: Trailing price-to-earnings (P/E) is a financial metric that shows the relationship between a company's current share price and its earnings per share over the past year, providing investors with a retrospective valuation measure.

title: Trailing Stop
definition: A trailing stop is a stop-loss order that moves with the market price, protecting profits on a trade from a market reversal.

title: Tranches
definition: Tranches slice financial products, like bonds or mortgages, into segments differentiated by risk or maturity, making them appealing to diverse investors with varying appetites for risk and return.

title: Transaction
definition: A transaction is the exchange of money between two or more parties for a product or service.

title: Transaction Costs
definition: Transaction costs refer to expenses incurred during the trading or selling of goods and services, separate from the products' base price. These costs may include broker fees, real estatecommissions, and various other professional charges that facilitate market transactions.

title: Transaction Exposure
definition: Transaction exposure refers to the financial risk businesses encounter when dealing with foreign currency transactions due to fluctuating exchange rates. These fluctuations can lead to unexpected capital gains or losses for businesses operating across borders. As international trade continues to grow, understanding and managing transaction exposure becomes crucial for financial stability.

title: Transaction Fees
definition: A per-transaction fee is an expense a business must pay each time it processes an electronic payment for a customer transaction. Per-transaction fees vary across service providers, typically costing merchants from 0.5% to 5% of the transaction amount plus certain fixed fees.

title: Transfer Agent
definition: Transfer agents, often trust companies or banks, play a crucial role in managing financial records for publicly traded companies by tracking ownership, issuing certificates, and ensuring investors receive timely dividends and interest payments. They act as intermediaries between corporations and shareholders, maintaining accurate information and facilitating smooth securities transactions.

title: Transfer of Risk
definition: A transfer of risk is a risk management strategy that transfers the responsibility and financial burden of loss from one party to another.

title: Transfer on Death (TOD)
definition: A transfer on death (TOD) is a legal designation that allows named assets to automatically pass to stated beneficiaries upon the account holder's death.

title: Transfer Payment
definition: Transfer payments are financial distributions primarily from government programs to individuals without requiring a return exchange of goods or services. They encompass various social programs such as welfare and student grants, with Social Security being among the most recognized forms. While aimed at aiding individuals, these payments differ from corporate assistance likebailoutsor subsidies, which aren't typically labeled as transfer payments.

title: Transfer Price
definition: Transfer price, also known as transfer cost, is the price at whichrelated partiestransact with one another, such as during the trade of supplies or labor between departments. Transfer prices are often used in transactions between a company and its subsidiaries or between different divisions of the same company operating in different countries.

title: Transfer Pricing
definition: Transfer pricing is a crucial accounting method often used to set prices for transactions of goods and services between various divisions or subsidiaries within a multinational corporation. This practice ensures that intra-company transactions reflect market-based pricing similar to external market dealings. By employing transfer pricing, companies can strategically allocate profits among differentsubsidiaries, leading to potential tax savings. However, tax authorities closely scrutinize such strategies, as they may be designed to reduce taxable income across different tax jurisdictions.

title: Transfer Tax
definition: A transfer tax is a charge levied on thetransfer of ownership or titleto property from one individual or entity to another. A transfer tax may be imposed by a state, county, or municipality. It is usually notdeductiblefrom federal or state income taxes, although it may be added to thecost basiswhen profit on the sale of securities and investment property is calculated. Transfer tax is considered anexcise taxin some states.

title: Transferable Letters of Credit Work
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Translation Exposure
definition: Translation exposure (also known astranslation risk) is the risk that a company's equities, assets, liabilities, or income will change in value as a result ofexchange ratechanges. This occurs when a firm denominates a portion of its equities, assets, liabilities, or income in a foreign currency. It is also known as "accounting exposure.”

title: Transparency
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Transportation Sector
definition: The transportation sector includes companies and infrastructure that move people or goods: airlines, railroads, trucking, marine transport, and air freight/logistics (aGICSindustrials group).

title: Transposition Error
definition: A transposition error describes an event where a bookkeeper accidentally reverses two adjacent digits when recording transactional data. This error may seem small in scale but it often results in substantial financial inaccuracies that can have a great impact in other areas through a ripple effect. Consider the potential effects if they were to occur in a business tax or critical medical dosing situation.

title: Traunch
definition: A traunch is one of a series of payments to be paid out over a specified period, subject to certain performance metrics being achieved. It is commonly used inventure capital (VC)circles to refer to the fundraising rounds used to fundstartup companies.

title: Travel Expenses
definition: For tax purposes, travel expenses are costs associated with traveling to conduct business-related activities. Reasonable travel expenses can generally be deducted from taxable income by a company when its employees incur costs while traveling away from home specifically for business. That business can include conferences or meetings.

title: Travel Insurance
definition: Travel insurance is designed to mitigate financial risks associated with travel by reimbursing costs related to unforeseen events, such as medical emergencies or trip disruptions.

title: Traveler's Check
definition: Traveler’s checks, once widely utilized for safe international travel purchases, provide security against loss and theft and can be used to purchase goods or exchanged for cash in many countries. However, with the rise of credit and debit cards, their use has significantly dwindled.

title: Treasury Bills (T-Bills)
definition: A treasury bill (T-bill) is a U.S. government debt security that matures in less than one year. They are issued by the U.S. Treasury.

title: Treasury Bond (T-Bond)
definition: Treasury bonds (T-bonds) are long-term, fixed-interest debt securities issued by the U.S. government with maturities of 20 or 30 years. They are regarded as low-risk investments due to their backing by the U.S. government's capability to tax, providing regular interest payments until maturity.

title: Treasury Direct
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Treasury Inflation-Protected Security (TIPS)
definition: Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) are a type of Treasury bond that is indexed to an inflationary gauge to protect investors from a decline in the purchasing power of their money.

title: Treasury Notes
definition: A Treasury note is a U.S. governmentdebt securitywith a fixed interest rate that matures between two to 10 years. Investors can buy them directly from the U.S. government through auctions or from a broker. The interest earned is taxed at the federal level but is exempt from state and local taxes. Treasury notes are different from Treasury bills and Treasury bonds, which have shorter and longer maturities, respectively.

title: Treasury Stock (Treasury Shares)
definition: Treasury stock refers to previously outstanding stock that was bought back from stockholders by the issuing company. The result is that the total number of outstanding shares on the open market decreases. Treasury stock remains issued but is not included in the distribution ofdividendsor the calculation ofearnings per share (EPS).Treasury stock is also referred to as treasury shares or reacquired stock.

title: Treasury Stock Method
definition: The treasury stock method is an approach companies use to compute the number of new shares that may potentially be created by unexercised in-the-money warrants and options, where the exercise price is less than the current share price. Additional shares obtained through the treasury stock method factor into the calculation of the diluted earnings per share (EPS). It assumes the company uses proceeds from in-the-money option exercises to buy back common shares.

title: Treasury STRIPS
definition: Treasury STRIPS, also known as zero-coupon bonds, are government-backed securities sold at a discount. They offer investors the opportunity to receive the full face value upon maturity. Unlike typical bonds, STRIPS involve no regular interest payments. Instead, the return for investors comes from the difference between the purchase price and the face value at maturity. Separated from bonds, their individual coupon payments can be traded separately, providing investment flexibility. Since 1985, STRIPS have catered to longer-term financial planning, with eligibility expanding to a broader range of Treasury securities over time.

title: Treasury Yield
definition: Treasury yield is the annual return, shown as a percentage, that the U.S. government offers to investors who hold its debt, such as bonds or bills.

title: Treaty Reinsurance
definition: In treaty reinsurance, an insurer (the cedent) transfers the risks of a specific class of policies to a reinsurer through a contract. This arrangement mitigates the insurer's exposure to risk while enhancing stability and security in the face of major events. By offloading risk, insurers gain the freedom to underwrite more policies without heightened solvency concerns.

title: Trend
definition: A trend is the overall direction of a market or an asset's price. In technical analysis, trends are identified by trendlines or price action that highlight when the price is making higher swing highs and higher swing lows for an uptrend, or lower swing lows and lower swing highs for a downtrend.

title: Trend Analysis
definition: Trend analysis is a technique used intechnical analysisthat attempts to predict future stock price movements based on recently observed trend data. Investors use trend analysis to forecast the long-term direction of market sentiment, and traders use technical indicators to assess trends.

title: Trend Trading
definition: Trend trading is a trading style that attempts to capture gains through the analysis of an asset'smomentumin a particular direction. When the price is moving in one overall direction, such as up or down, that is called a trend.

title: Trendline
definition: Trendlines are fundamental charting tools used by traders to depict the prevailing direction of an investment's price. By connecting a series of prices either over pivot highs or under pivot lows, trendlines offer a visual representation of support and resistance levels across various timeframes.

title: Treynor Ratio
definition: The Treynor ratio, also known as the reward-to-volatility ratio, is a performance metric for determining how much excess return was generated for each unit of risk taken on by a portfolio.

title: Understanding Trial Balance: Definition, Purpose, and Key Requirements
definition: A trial balance is a worksheet used to confirm that total debits equal total credits in a company’s ledger accounts.

title: Triangle
definition: A triangle chart pattern is used in technical analysis and forms a triangle-like shape on a price chart.

title: Triangular Arbitrage
definition: Triangular arbitrage is a type of forex trading that involves exchanging one currency for a second, then trading it for a third, and then finally exchanging it back into the original currency.

title: Trickle-Down Theory
definition: Trickle-down economics is the idea that cutting taxes for the wealthy and businesses will benefit everyone by stimulating investment, job creation, and economic growth. This approach, rooted in supply-side economics, has been applied in various U.S. administrations. While supporters argue it encourages economic expansion, critics argue that it increases income inequality and provides limited direct benefits to lower- and middle-income households.

title: Triggering Event
definition: A triggering event is a tangible or intangible barrier or occurrence which, once breached or met, causes another event to occur. Triggering events include job loss,retirement, or death, and are typical for many types of contracts. These triggers help to prevent, or ensure, that in the case of a catastrophic change, the terms of an original contract may also change.

title: Trilemma
definition: A trilemma in economics offers three possible solutions to a complex problem, unlike a dilemma which only has two. A trilemma suggests that countries have three options from which to choose when making fundamental decisions about managing their international monetary policy agreements. However, the options of the trilemma are conflictual because of mutual exclusivity, which makes only one option of the trilemma achievable at a given time.

title: Trillion Cubic Feet (Tcf)
definition: The term trillion cubic feet refers to a volume measurement ofnatural gasused by the U.S. oil and gas industry. The measurement is usually abbreviated as Tcf. A cubic foot is a nonmetric measurement of volume also used in the U.S. A trillion—written in numerals as 1,000,000,000,000—cubic feet is equivalent to approximately one quad of Btu or a British thermal unit.

title: Trimmed Mean
definition: A trimmed mean is a central measure in statistics that adjusts the average by removing a specific portion of extreme values from a sample.

title: Triple Bottom
definition: The triple bottom is a bullishchart patternin technical analysis, marked by three equal lows followed by abreakoutpast resistance. This pattern indicates a potential market reversal, signaling a shift in control from sellers to buyers.

title: Triple Bottom Line (TBL)
definition: The triple bottom line (TBL) concept states that company performance should be measured on social issues and environmental sustainability as much as on company profits.

title: Triple Exponential Moving Average (TEMA)
definition: The triple exponential moving average (TEMA) was designed to smooth price fluctuations, thereby making it easier to identify trends without thelagassociated with traditional moving averages (MA). It does this by taking multipleexponential moving averages (EMA)of the original EMA and subtracting out some of the lag.

title: Triple Net Lease (NNN)
definition: A triple net lease (NNN) is an arrangement in which the tenant agrees to cover both the rent and any ongoing expenses on a property. This type of lease can benefit both landlords and tenants, though the latter group is at risk should tax and insurance costs rise.

title: Triple Top
definition: The triple top is a type of chart pattern used intechnical analysisto predict the reversal in the movement of an asset's price. Consisting of threepeaks, a triple top signals that the asset may no longer be rallying, and that lower prices may be on the way.

title: Triple Witching
definition: The triple witching hour is the last 60 minutes of the trading day on the third Friday of March, June, September, and December, when contracts for stock index futures, stock index options, and stock options expire simultaneously.

title: Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP)
definition: The Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) was an initiative created and run by the U.S. Treasury to stabilize the country’s financial system, restore economic growth, and mitigateforeclosuresin the wake of the2008 financial crisis. TARP sought to achieve these targets by purchasing troubled companies’ assets and stock.

title: Trough
definition: In economics, a trough is the low point in thebusiness cycle, when activity or prices stop falling before a recovery begins. It often coincides with weak output, higher unemployment, and reduced business activity, and economists look to indicators like GDP and stock market trends to spot it, for example, the trough around the Great Recession.

title: Understanding the Troy Ounce: History, Definition, and Precious Metals
definition: A troy ounce is part of the troy system of weights and measures, and the only unit of that system still in use. The troy ounce is used when weighingprecious metals. The name is thought to derive from the town of Troyes in Frances, which in the Middle Ages was a trading hub for Europe and Britain. One troy ounce is equal to 31.1034768 grams, according to the UK Royal Mint.A standard ounce is lighter than a troy ounce, weighing in at 28.35 grams.

title: True Strength Index (TSI)
definition: The true strength index (TSI) is a technical momentumoscillatorused by technical analysts. The true strength index is used to identify trends and reversals in the market. The indicator may determineoverbought and oversoldconditions, indicate potential trend direction changes via centerline or signal line crossovers, and warn traders of trend weakness that may occur throughdivergence.

title: Trust
definition: A trust is a legal entity with separate and distinct rights, similar to a person or corporation. In a trust, a party known as a trustor gives another party, a trustee, the right to hold title to and manage property or assets for the benefit of a third party, the beneficiary.

title: Trust Company
definition: A trust company is a legal entity that acts as a fiduciary, agent, or trustee on behalf of a person or business. It can act as the custodian for a trust or estate, managing assets and property according to the instructions of the trustor on behalf of their eventual benefactors.

title: Trust Deed
definition: A trust deed —also known as adeed of trust—is a document sometimes used in real estate transactions in the U.S. It comes into play when one party has taken out a loan from another party to purchase a property. The trust deed represents an agreement between the borrower and a lender to have the property held in trust by a neutral and independent third party until the loan is paid off.

title: Trust Fund
definition: A trust fund is an estate planning tool that holds property or assets for a person or an organization. Trust funds are sometimes simply referred to as "trusts." They can hold a variety of assets such as money, real property,stocks, bonds, businesses, or a combination of many types of properties or assets.

title: Trust Indenture
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Trust Preferred Securities (TruPS)
definition: Trust preferred securities (TruPS) were hybrid securities issued by large banks andbank holding companies(BHCs) included in regulatorytier 1 capital, and whose dividend payments were tax-deductible for the issuer.

title: Trust Property
definition: Trust property refers to assets that have been placed into afiduciaryrelationship between a trustor and trustee for a designated beneficiary. Trust property may include any type of asset, including cash, securities, real estate, or life insurance policies. Trust property is also referred to as "trust assets" or "trust corpus."

title: Trust Receipts
definition: A trust receipt is a notice of the release of merchandise to a buyer from a bank, with the bank retaining the ownership title of the released assets. In an arrangement involving a trust receipt, the bank remains the owner of the merchandise, but the buyer is allowed to hold the merchandise in trust for the bank, for manufacturing or sales purposes.

title: Trustee
definition: A trustee manages and ultimately distributes assets in a trust to one or more beneficiaries.

title: Truth in Lending Act (TILA)
definition: The Truth in Lending Act (TILA), introduced in 1968, mandates transparency in credit offerings. It requires lenders to clearly disclose essential financial terms, such as theannual percentage rate(APR) and total loan costs, thus empowering consumers to make informed decisions and ensuring consumer protection against unfair lending tactics.

title: TSA PreCheck
definition: TSA PreCheck is an airport security screening program in the United States that allows approved travelers to pass through dedicated security lines without having to remove laptops, liquids, and shoes.

title: Turnaround
definition: When a company that has experienced a period of poor performance moves into a period of a financial recovery, it's called a turnaround. A turnaround may also refer to the recovery of a nation or region's economy after a period ofrecessionorstagnation. Similarly, it can refer to the recovery of an individual whose personal financial situation improves after some time.

title: Turnkey Business
definition: A turnkey business is a business that is ready to use, existing in a condition that allows for immediate operation.

title: Turnkey Property
definition: A turnkey property is a fully renovated home or apartment building that aninvestorcan purchase and immediately rent out. It generates quick rental income with minimal effort. A turnkey home is often a property purchased from a company that specializes in the restoration of older properties. Those same firms may also offerproperty managementservices to buyers, minimizing the amount of time and effort they have to put into the rental.

title: Turnkey Solution
definition: A turnkey solution is a product or service that requires a customer to make no modifications; it is ready to use as is.

title: Turnover
definition: Turnover describes how quickly assets in a company are replaced within a specific period.

title: Turnover Ratio
definition: In investment, a turnover ratio indicates the rate at which a fund's holdings are bought and sold.

title: Tweezer
definition: A tweezer is a pattern used intechnical analysisthat helps show when a market might reverse direction. Traders can use the pattern on charts to detecttopsandbottomsin prices so they can make better investment decisions. The pattern became widely accepted thanks to Steve Nison, an expert incandlestickanalysis. Tweezer patterns can indicate bullish reversals, where prices may start rising, and bearish reversals, where prices may start falling.

title: Twenty Eight Thirty Six Rule (28/36 Rule)
definition: The 28/36 rule helps determine the debt load that an individual or household can safely take on based on income, existing debts, and lifestyle. It states that a maximum of 28% ofgross monthly incomeshould be spent on total housing expenses and, at most, 36% go to total debt payments.

title: Two and Twenty
definition: Two and twenty (or "2 and 20") is a fee arrangement that is standard in the hedge fund industry and is also common in venture capital and private equity. Hedge fund management companies typically charge clients both a management and a performance fee. "Two" means 2% ofassets under management (AUM), and refers to the annual management fee charged by the hedge fund for managing assets. "Twenty" refers to the standard performance or incentive fee of 20% of profits made by the fund above a certain predefined benchmark.

title: Two-Bin Inventory Control
definition: The two-bin inventory control system is a management strategy that helps businesses maintain optimal stock levels efficiently by using a two-container system to track inventory usage and reorder points.

title: Two-Tailed Tests
definition: A two-tailed test in statistics evaluates whether a sample significantly differs from a population mean, either exceeding or falling short. It's a fundamental tool in null-hypothesis testing used across various fields like manufacturing quality control and financial analysis. When the sample mean falls within critical regions on either distribution tail, it signifies a departure from the population mean, prompting the rejection of the null hypothesis for the alternative. Understanding this test's principles and applications can enhance decision-making in data-driven settings.

title: Type II Errors
definition: A type II error, also known as a false negative, is the miscalculation that occurs when a researcher accepts a false null hypothesis.

title: tZero
definition: tZero Group Inc. is a registered broker-dealer that uses blockchain technology to securitize interest in companies searching for private equity. It also offers trading and investing services. The company was originally created as a cryptocurrency exchange to provide a trading platform for the security tokens of companies seeking funding and to bring greater legitimacy and oversight to initial coin offerings (ICOs).

title: U-6 (Unemployment) Rate
definition: The U-6 (Unemployment) rate measures the percentage of the U.S. labor force that is unemployed, plus those who are underemployed, marginally attached to the workforce, and have given up looking for work. The U-6 rate is considered by many economists to be the most revealing measure of the true state of the nation's employment situation.

title: U-Shaped Recovery
definition: A U-shaped recovery is a type of economic recovery with a recessionary decline followed by a period of stagnation then a gradual rise back to its previous peak.

title: Uberrimae Fidei Contract
definition: An uberrimae fidei contract is a legal agreement, common to the insurance industry, requiring the highest standard of good faith during the disclosure of all material facts that could influence the decision of the other party. A failure to adhere to uberrimae fidei is grounds for voiding the agreement. Uberrimae fidei is also known asutmost good faithand is simply the Latin translation of this phrase.

title: UBS
definition: UBS, formerly known as the Union Bank of Switzerland, is a multinational diversified financial services company headquartered in Zurich and Basel. UBS is involved in virtually all major financial activities, including retail and commercial banking,investment banking,investment management, andwealth management.

title: UCC-1 Statement
definition: A UCC-1 financing statement serves as a formal notice by a secured party asserting a claim against specific personal property of the debtor in accordance with the Uniform Commercial Code.

title: UCLA Anderson School of Management
definition: Mutual funds and ETFs are popular investment options designed for diversification. While mutual funds are often actively managed with purchases limited to end-of-daynet asset valuepricing, ETFs offer intra-day trading flexibility and are typically passively managed. Understanding these and other differences is crucial for selecting the right investment strategy.

title: UDAAP
definition: UDAAP describes unlawful conduct that puts consumers at a disadvantage in financial transactions.

title: Ulcer Index (UI)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Ultimate Mortality Table
definition: An ultimate mortality table is a table that provides the percentage of life insurance policyholders expected to reach each age listed.

title: Ultimate Net Loss
definition: Ultimate net loss is the total amount an insured must ultimately pay after an event, accounting for what the insurer covers. It matters most when losses near policy limits, affecting costs like property damage or legal fees. Companies often manage this exposure through reinsurance and other recovery strategies.

title: Ultimate Oscillator
definition: The Ultimate Oscillator is a technical indicator that was developed by Larry Williams in 1976 to measure the price momentum of an asset across multiple timeframes. The indicator has lessvolatilityand fewer trade signals compared to other oscillators that rely on a single timeframe because it uses the weighted average of three timeframes.

title: Ultimogeniture
definition: Ultimogeniture, also known as postremogeniture or junior right, is a system of inheritance whereby the youngest son gains possession of his deceased father's estate. Many rural areas of medieval England used this system, as well as parts of France. It often applied to farmland, but sometimes included other types of land in addition to personal property.

title: Ultra ETF
definition: An ultra ETF is a class ofexchange-traded fund (ETF)that employsleveragein an effort to amplify the return of a set benchmark. Since first arriving on the scene in 2006, ultra ETFs have grown to include different ETFs with underlying benchmarks ranging from broad market indexes, such as the S&P 500 and Russell 2000, to specific sectors, such as technology, healthcare, and basic materials. Ultra ETFs are also known asleveraged ETFsor geared funds.

title: Ultra-Short Bond Fund
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Ultra Vires Acts
definition: An ultra vires act occurs when an organization exercises powers beyond those it is legally permitted to exercise.

title: Ultrafast Trading
definition: High-frequency trading (HFT) leverages advanced computer programs and sophisticated algorithms to execute vast numbers of orders in mere fractions of a second. These algorithms rapidly analyze numerous markets and respond to varying market conditions to execute trades efficiently. Having the fastest execution speeds allows traders and financial institutions to capitalize on minimal price discrepancies for higher profitability, which distinguishes HFT through elevated turnover rates and order-to-trade ratios.

title: Umbrella Insurance Policy
definition: An umbrella insurance policy provides extra liability coverage that surpasses the limits of standard homeowners, auto, or watercraft insurance, offering protection against potential lawsuits for property damage or injuries to others.

title: Umbrella Personal Liability Policy
definition: An umbrella personal liability policy is extraliabilitycoverage that goes beyond the limits of the insured’s home, auto, or other liability coverage. It provides an additional layer of security to those who are at risk of high loss due to lawsuits if they injure someone else or someone’s property.

title: Umpire Clause
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: UN Principles for Responsible Investment (PRI)
definition: The UN Principles for Responsible Investment (PRI) is an international organization that works to promote the incorporation ofenvironmental, social, and corporate governance factors (ESG)into investment decision-making.

title: Unaffiliated Investments
definition: Unaffiliated investments are investmentholdingsof an insurance company that it neither controls nor shares joint ownership with. Unaffiliated investments can include stocks, bonds, property, and other assets and are often disclosed in thefinancial statementsof insurers. These investments undergo liquidity and solvency checks by regulators to ensure that they align with the company's responsibilities to policyholders.

title: Unallocated Loss Adjustment Expenses (ULAE)
definition: Unallocated loss adjustment expenses (ULAE) are insurance company costs not linked to any specific claim. Insurers set aside reserve funds for these expenses, along with allocated loss adjustment expenses andcontingent commissions. It's important to reserve funds for ULAE alongside ALAE and contingent commissions.

title: Unamortized Bond Discount
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Unamortized Bond Premium
definition: An unamortized bond premium refers to the difference between a bond'sface valueand its sale price. If a bond is sold at adiscount, for instance, at 90 cents on the dollar, the issuer must still repay the full 100 cents of face value atpar. Since this interest amount has not yet been paid to bondholders, it is a liability for theissuer.

title: Unappropriated Retained Earnings
definition: Unappropriated retained earnings are corporate profits that have not been set aside for a specific use.

title: Unauthorized Insurer
definition: White list states maintain a list ofinsurancecompanies that can use unauthorized insurers to provide specialized or supplemental coverage, known as surplus lines insurance.

title: Unbanked
definition: Unbanked is an informal term for adults who do not usebanksor banking institutions in any capacity. While often an issue in the developing world, there are also pockets of unbanked adults in developed countries, including the United States.

title: Unbiased Predictor
definition: Expectations theory attempts to predict what short-terminterest rateswill be in the future based on current long-term interest rates. The theory suggests that an investor earns the same interest by investing in two consecutive one-year bond investments versus investing in one two-year bond today. The theory is also known as the "unbiased expectations theory."

title: Unbundled Life Insurance Policy
definition: An unbundled life insurance policy is a type of financial protection plan that provides cash to beneficiaries upon a policyholder's death. An unbundled life insurance policy contains a savings and investment component that the policyholder can use during his or her lifetime.

This can be over any time frame, including a trading day, week or even as much as a year. Unchanged is a term used universally among equity, fixed-income, futures and options markets. The term also applies to indexes, exchange-traded funds and thenet asset valueof mutual funds.

title: Unclaimed Funds
definition: Unclaimed funds are funds owed to an individual that remain uncollected.

title: Uncle Sam
definition: Uncle Sam is the personified symbol of the U.S. government and the nation. Its origins go back to Samuel Wilson, a War of 1812 supplier whose goods were marked “U.S.” Over time, he became closely tied to financial matters like taxes and government regulations, as well as a powerful figure in popular culture through military recruitment and propaganda, most famously the “I Want YOU” poster. Congress later recognized Samuel Wilson as the inspiration for the symbol, cementing Uncle Sam’s lasting place in American history.

title: Uncommitted Facility: Definition, Purpose, Example
definition: An uncommitted facility is an agreement where the lender agrees to make funding available to the borrower but is under no obligation.

title: Unconsolidated Subsidiaries
definition: An unconsolidated subsidiary is a company that is owned by a parent company but whose individual financial statements are not included in the consolidated or combined financial statements of the parent company to which it belongs. Instead, an unconsolidated subsidiary appears in theconsolidated financial statementsof the parent as an investment. This usually applies when the parent company does not have a controlling stake in the subsidiary.

title: Unconstrained Investing
definition: Unconstrained investing is aninvestment stylethat does not require a fund orportfolio managerto adhere to a specific benchmark. Unconstrained investing allows managers to pursue returns across many asset classes andsectors.
