title: Unit Benefit Formula
definition: The unit benefit formula is a method of calculating an employer's contribution to an employee'sdefined benefit planorpension planbased on years of service. Although a retirement plan that uses a unit benefit formula can reward employees for remaining at the company longer, it can also be more costly to implement for the employer.

title: Unit Cost
definition: A unit cost is the measure of the total expenditure for producing, storing, and selling a product. Unit costs are synonymous with thecost of goods sold (COGS). Unit cost is reflected in financial statements and is relevant for internal and external analysis. Economies of scale can impact unit costs as a company grows.

title: Unit Investment Trust (UIT)
definition: A unit investment trust (UIT) is an investment company that offers a fixed portfolio of stocks and bonds as redeemable units to investors for a specific period. It is designed to providecapital appreciationand/or dividend income. UITs, along withmutual fundsandclosed-end funds, are defined as investment companies.

title: Unit Linked Insurance Plan (ULIP)
definition: Unit Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs) integrate insurance coverage with investment exposure, offering policyholders dual benefits. Regular premiums fund both insurance and investments in equities, bonds, or a mix of both. ULIPs serve various financial objectives, from life insurance to wealth building, making them an attractive option for long-term financial planning.

title: Unit of Production
definition: The Unit of Production Method calculates depreciation based on an asset's output. This approach is ideal for assets where value loss correlates with consumption or wear from use, like manufacturing equipment. It offers greater accuracy compared to time-based methods, providing higher deductions in active years, which aids in aligning depreciation with actual wear and tear.

title: Unit Sales
definition: On a balance sheet, unit sales represent the total sales of a product in a given period. This sales information is used to determine the price point to achieve profit per unit given the actual cost of production.

title: Unit Trust (UT)
definition: A unit trust is an unincorporatedmutual fundestablished under atrust deed, where an investor is abeneficiary. Unit trusts pass their profits directly to unit owners instead of reinvesting profits in the mutual fund. A fund manager directs the investments of a unit trust, which can include equities, bonds, and cash. In the United Kingdom, a unit trust is regarded as similar to a mutual fund in the United States.

title: Unitary Thrift
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: United Nations (UN)
definition: The United Nations is an organization that strives to maintain peace and security among its member nations by promoting social progress and human rights.

title: United Nations Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL)
definition: The term United Nations Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL) refers to asubsidiarybody of theUnited NationsGeneral Assembly. Established in 1966, UNCITRAL is the core legal body of the U.N,'s system in the field of international trade law.The official function of UNCITRAL is to modernize and harmonize the rules of international business. The organization is responsible for helping to facilitate international trade and investment. It is headquartered in New York with annual sessions held alternatively in Vienna.

title: United Nations Global Compact
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: United States Agency for International Development (USAID)
definition: The term foreign aid encompasses various contributions—gifts,grants, or loans—that one nation offers to another. It extends beyond justcapital, also covering essential food, supplies, and services like military and humanitarian assistance. This assistance often flows from economically advanced to developing nations, aiding in disaster response, conflict situations, or economic crises. The United States, known as a generous provider, leads in global aid contributions.

title: United States Aircraft Insurance Group (USAIG)
definition: United States Aircraft Insurance Group (USAIG) is the nation's oldest aviation insurer.

title: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
definition: The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) is the federal agency that proposes programs and implements policies and regulations related to American farming, forestry, ranching, food quality, and nutrition.

title: United States Natural Gas Fund (UNG)
definition: The United States Natural Gas Fund (UNG) is anexchange traded fund(ETF) with the stated goal of following the movement of natural gas prices. It is the largest natural gas ETF with shares available on theNYSE Arca. The fund provides access to investing in natural gas without having to invest in the futures market, which is a complicated and risky route for the regular investor.

title: United States Treasury (UST)
definition: UST is the abbreviation for the United States Treasury, the federal government division that manages U.S. finances and maintains a significant role in setting economic policy. The Treasury oversees the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), the U.S. Mint, the Bureau of the Public Debt, and the Alcohol and Tobacco Tax Bureau. The First Session of Congress established the UST in 1789, so it has a long and storied past.

title: United States Treasury Money Mutual Funds
definition: A United States Treasury money mutual fund is a mutual fund that pools money from investors to purchase low-risk government securities. A United States Treasury money mutual fund invests primarily or exclusively in U.S. government debt. This can include Treasury bills and repurchase agreements.

title: United States V. The South-Eastern Underwriter Association
definition: The termUnited States v. South-Eastern Underwriters Associationrefers to a landmark U.S. Supreme Court case involving the federalantitruststatute and theinsuranceindustry. The case was decided on June 5, 1944. The Supreme Court ruled that the industry is subject to regulation by the United States Congress, under the Commerce Clause.This means that the court determined insurance to be a business that crosses state lines and is, therefore, subject to antitrust laws.Congress passed a law a year later exempting the insurance industry from federal scrutiny.

title: Unitholder
definition: A unitholder is an investor who owns units in an investment trust or a master limited partnership (MLP), giving them partial ownership of the entity. Units work like shares in a corporation and may provide rights like voting and a share of profits, depending on the terms of the trust agreement. In this article, we explain the rights and responsibilities of unitholders, how units work within these investment structures, and what they mean for investors.

title: Unitized Endowment Pool (UEP)
definition: A unitized endowment pool (UEP) is a form of endowment investing that allows multipleendowmentsto invest in the same pool of assets. Each endowment owns individual units in a UEP and investors generally see their returns monthly. New endowments entering the pool can buy in by receiving units in the pool valued as of a specific buy-in date.

title: Unitized Fund
definition: A unitized fund pools investor money into a single investment while assigning each participant an individual unit value. Thesefundsare commonly used inESOPs, pensions, and insurance plans, often hold bothstockand cash, which together influence the unit price. Understanding the structure and related expenses helps investors better judge how the fund operates.

title: Unitranche Debt
definition: Unitranche debt combinesseniorandsubordinatedloans, helping banks compete with private debt funds. The borrower of this kind of debt typically pays aninterest ratethat falls between the interest rates that each type of loan would command individually.

title: Units Per Transaction (UPT)
definition: Units per transaction (UPT) serve as an indicator for retailers to analyze shopping trends and customer buying behavior to optimize sales strategies and inventory management.

title: Unissued Stock: What It Is and How It Works
definition: Unissued stock represents shares a company is allowed to issue but hasn't yet offered to employees or other investors.

title: Universal Banking
definition: Universal banking describes a system where financial institutions provide a broad array of services, including retail, commercial, and investment banking, often acting as a comprehensive one-stop shop. Particularly prevalent in Europe, it gained traction in the Unites States with the 1999Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act, which allowed banks to offer diversified services, merging commercial and investment operations. While some argue this diversification spreads risk, others point to potential conflicts of interest. Understanding universal banking involves exploring its service offerings, regulatory background, and its evolvement through key legislative changes over time.

title: Universal Default
definition: Universal default is a credit card agreement provision that lets an issuer raise your interest rate after credit trouble, such as missing aminimum paymentor defaulting on another loan. The CARD Act of 2009 limited when issuers can apply these rate increases, which made the provision far less common in practice. A higher rate can raise borrowing costs fast, so it pays to review thecardholder agreement.

title: Universal Healthcare Coverage
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Universal Life Insurance
definition: Universal life (UL) insurance is a type ofpermanent life insurancethat, like other permanent insurance, has acash valueelement and offers lifetime coverage as long as you pay yourpremiums. Unlike whole life insurance, universal life allows you to raise or lower your premiums within certain limits, and it can be cheaper than whole life coverage. However, if your investments underperform or you underpay for too long, it could affect yourdeath benefitor cause your policy to lapse.

title: Universal Market Integrity Rules (UMIR)
definition: Universal Market Integrity Rules (UMIR) are rules governing trading practices in Canada. UMIR sets a standard for fairness and stability for all Canadian exchanges. In doing so, UMIR safeguards Canada's market integrity and improves investor trust and confidence. UMIR is overseen by the independentInvestment Industry Regulatory Organization of Canada(IIROC).

title: Universe of Securities
definition: A universe of securities generally refers to a set of securities that share a common feature. For example, the broad universe of stocks for a U.S. investor will include alllisted companies, large and small, and may also include foreign companies listed asAmerican depositary receipts(ADRs). For some investors, a narrower universe may be used that is constrained to only value stocks or those with a market cap above some minimum threshold.

title: University of Miami School of Business Administration
definition: The University of Miami Patti and Allan Herbert Business School is the school of business at the University of Miami.

title: Unlevered Beta
definition: Investopedia / Laura Porter

title: Unlevered Cost of Capital
definition: Unlevered cost of capital is an analysis using either a hypothetical or an actual debt-free scenario to measure a company's cost to implement a particular capital project (and in some cases used to assess an entire company). Unlevered cost of capital compares thecost of capitalof the project using zero debt as an alternative to a levered cost of capital investment, which means using debt as a portion of the total capital required.

title: Unlevered Free Cash Flow (UFCF)
definition: Unlevered free cash flow (UFCF) is a company's cash flow before accounting for interest payments. UFCF shows how much cash is available to the firm before taking financial obligations into account. It is reported on a company's financial statements or may be calculated using financial statements by analysts. UFCF is the opposite oflevered free cash flow (LFCF), which is the money left over after all a firm's bills are paid.

title: Unlimited Liability
definition: Unlimited liability refers to the full legal responsibility that business owners and partners assume for all business debts. Thisliabilityisn't capped and obligations can be paid through the seizure and sale of the owners’ personal assets without the protection that the popular limited liability business structure provides.

title: Unlimited Liability Corporation (ULC)
definition: An unlimited liability corporation (ULC) is a Canadian corporate structure in which shareholders can be held responsible for the company's debts if it goesbankrupt, and in some cases, former shareholders may also share this liability.

title: Unlimited Marital Deduction
definition: The unlimited marital deduction lets spouses transfer assets to each other tax-free, during life and after death. This helps reduce or eliminate estate and gift taxes on transfers between married partners, helping families preserve their wealth. The deduction only applies if the receiving spouse is a U.S. citizen, with qualified domestic trusts (QDOTs) available to support non-citizen spouses.

title: Unlimited Risk
definition: Unlimited risk refers to a situation where there is potential for unlimited losses on a trade or in a particular investment.

title: Unlisted Security
definition: An unlisted security is a financial asset, such as astockor bond, that isn't traded on a major exchange. These securities usually don't meet the listing requirements set by large exchanges, which may include rules about company size, financial reporting, or share distribution. Unlisted securities generally trade over-the-counter (OTC), where transactions occur directly between buyers and sellers. In this market, dealers known as market makers help keep trading active by offering prices at which they are willing to buy or sell the security.

title: Unlimited Tax Bond
definition: Unlimited tax bonds are municipal bonds that are secured by the taxing power of the issuing government.

title: Unlisted Trading Privileges (UTP)
definition: Unlisted trading privileges (UTP) refer to the processes around the trading of a security that is not required to meet certain minimum requirements to be traded on anexchange. Regulation for UTP is detailed in the Unlisted Trading Privileges Act of 1994.

title: Unmatched Book
definition: Maturity mismatch is a term used to describe situations when there's a disconnect between a company's short-term assets and its short-term liabilities—specifically, more of the latter than the former.Maturity mismatches can also occur when ahedginginstrument and theunderlying asset'smaturities are misaligned.

title: Unofficial Strike
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Unpaid Dividend
definition: An unpaid dividend refers to a declared dividend due to shareholders that hasn't been distributed yet. It represents a liability to the company until it is fulfilled. Unpaid dividends typically exist due to timing gaps between several critical dates involved in the dividend payment process—like the declaration date, ex-dividend date, and payment date. Whether you're an investor or a finance student, this article will enhance your comprehension of how unpaid dividends work.

title: Unqualified Audit
definition: An unqualified audit confirms that a company's financial records are accurate and in compliance with accounting standards.

title: Unqualified Opinion
definition: An unqualified opinion is anindependent auditor's judgment that a company's financial statements are fairly and appropriately presented, without any identified exceptions, and in compliance withgenerally accepted accounting principles(GAAP).

title: Unquoted Public Company
definition: An unquoted public company, also known as an unlisted public company, is a firm that has issued equity shares that are no longer traded on a stock exchange.

title: Unrealized Gain
definition: An unrealized gain is the increase in the value of an asset that an investor has not yet sold.

title: Unrealized Loss
definition: An unrealized loss is a "paper" lossthat results from holding anassetthat has decreased in price, but not yet selling it and realizing the loss. An investor may prefer to let a loss go unrealized in the hope that the asset will eventually recover in price. Unlike realized losses, which result from an actual sale, unrealized losses can't be used for tax purposes, though they still influence how investors weigh future decisions.

title: Unrecaptured Section 1250 Gain
definition: An unrecaptured 1250 gain is a gain addressed by Section 1250 of the U.S. Internal Revenue Code. This provision directs that previously recognized depreciation be recaptured as income when a gain is realized on the sale of depreciable real estate property.

title: Unrecorded Deed
definition: An unrecorded deed refers to the situation where thetitleto a property, usually real estate, is not registered with the appropriate public records department.

title: Unregistered Shares
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Unrelated Business Taxable Income (UBTI)
definition: UBTI is income earned by a tax-exempt entity from activities unrelated to its exempt purpose.

title: Unrestricted Cash
definition: A company’s unrestricted cash can be spent for any reason because no creditors have a claim against it.

title: Unrestricted Net Assets
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Unsatisfied Judgment Fund
definition: An unsatisfied judgment fund provides financial relief for individuals involved in motor vehicle accidents where the liable party cannot cover expenses. This fund protects drivers who are not at fault, ensuring they aren't burdened with unmanageablemedical billsdue to others' inability to pay. Failing to settle the debt can result in significant penalties to the responsible party, including the loss of driving privileges.

title: Unscheduled Property Floater
definition: The importance of havingadequate property insurance coveragecan’t be overstated. One way to make sure you have the coverage you need is with an unscheduled propertyfloater, an addition to an existingproperty insurancepolicy that provides coverage for personal property items that have not been individually itemized or valued.

title: Unsecured
definition: Unsecured refers to a debt or obligation that is not backed by any sort ofcollateral.

title: Unsecured Creditors
definition: An unsecured creditor is an individual or institution that lends money without obtaining specifiedassetsascollateral. This poses a higher risk to thecreditorbecause it will have nothing to fall back on should the borrower default on the loan. If a borrower fails to make a payment on a debt that is unsecured, the creditor cannot take any of the borrower's assets without winning a lawsuit first.

title: Unsecured Debt
definition: Unsecured debt is debt that is not secured by some kind ofcollateralowned by the borrower. Generally, unsecured debt has higherinterest ratesbecause the lender has limited protection against loss in case ofdefaultby the borrower. Lenders can mitigate this risk of loss by reporting defaults to credit rating agencies, contracting with credit collection agencies, and selling their loans on the secondary market.

title: Unsecured Loan
definition: An unsecured loan is a loan supported only by the borrower’screditworthiness, rather than by anycollateral, such as property or other assets. Unsecured loans are riskier than secured loans for lenders, so they require higher credit scores for approval and usually come with higher interest rates. Credit cards, student loans, and personal loans are examples of unsecured loans.

title: Unsecured Note
definition: An unsecured note is a type of loan that's not backed by collateral. It's based solely on the issuer's creditworthiness. Although they carry higher risk for investors, they often offer a higher rate of return compared to secured loans. Such notes are also often uninsured and subordinated. The note is structured for a fixed period.

title: Unskilled Labor
definition: "Unskilled labor" is an outdated term that was once used to describe a segment of the workforce associated with a limited skill set or minimaleconomic valuefor the work performed. The correct term in 2025 is low-wage labor.

title: Unsolicited Application
definition: An unsolicited application is a request for life insurance coverage that is made by an individual rather than an insurance agent or broker.

title: Unsolicited Bid
definition: An unsolicited bid is an offer made by an individual, investor, or company to purchase a company that is not actively seeking a buyer. Unsolicited bids may sometimes be referred to ashostile bidsif the target company doesn't want to be acquired. They usually come up when a potential acquirer sees value in the target company.

title: Unsponsored ADR
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Unstated Interest Paid
definition: Unstated interest paid is interest that the IRS assumes is being paid if there is little or no interest in the written contract.

title: Unsterilized Foreign Exchange Intervention
definition: The term unsterilized foreign exchange intervention refers to how a country's monetary authorities influence exchange rates and itsmoney supply—by not purchasing foreign or by not selling domestic currencies or assets. This kind of approach is considered passive toexchange ratefluctuations, allowing for fluctuations in the monetary base.

title: Unsubordinated Debt
definition: Unsubordinated debt is an obligation that must be repaid before any other form of debt if the debtor goes bankrupt or insolvent. The majority of unsubordinated debt is usually secured by collateral. This kind of debt is also known as a senior security orsenior debt. Types of unsubordinated debt include exchange-traded notes, collateralized securities, and certificates of deposit.

title: Unsubscribed
definition: The term unsubscribed refers to any shares that are part of aninitial public offering(IPO) that are not purchased ahead of the official release date. This means there is little to no interest in the security in advance of the company's IPO.

title: Unsuitable Investment (Unsuitability)
definition: An unsuitable investment is when an investment—such as a stock or bond—does not meet the objectives and means of an investor. The investmentstrategymay also be unsuitable. For example, the portfolioasset mixcould be wrong, or the investments purchased may be too aggressive or too low-risk for what the client needs or wants.

title: Unsystematic Risk
definition: Unsystematic risk is a potential hazard that is unique to a company or an industry sector and can be mitigated by the investor through diversifying holdings.

title: Unusual Item
definition: There are differences between how the U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) and theInternational Financial Reporting Standards(IFRS) treat unusual or infrequent income or expense items, also known asnonrecurring. Irregular items are occurrences unrelated to a business's operational and financial results.

title: Unweighted Index
definition: Equal weight is a type of proportional measuring method that gives the same importance to each stock in a portfolio, index, or index fund.

title: Unwind
definition: Unwinding a position involves closing complex or large trades in financial markets, often requiring multiple steps. It is also a strategy for correcting trading errors, such as broker mistakes. This article explores how unwinding works, when to apply it, and its significance in effective trade management.

title: Up-and-In Option
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Up-and-Out Option
definition: An up-and-out option is a type ofknock-outbarrier option that ceases to exist when the price of the underlying security rises above a specific price level, called the barrier price.

title: Up/Down Gap Side-by-Side White Lines
definition: The up/down gap side-by-side white lines is a three-candlecontinuation patternthat occurs on candlestick charts.

title: Up-Front Mortgage Insurance (UFMI)
definition: Up-front mortgage insurance is an insurance premium that is collected, typically on Federal Housing Administration (FHA) loans, when the loan is initially made. Though similar, it's not quite the same as private mortgage insurance (PMI), which is collected by a conventionalprivate mortgagelender each month when a buyer's down payment on a home is less than 20% of the purchase price. Up-front mortgage premiums are added to a pool of money that is used to help entities, such as the FHA, insure loans for certain borrowers.

title: Up-Market Capture Ratio
definition: The up-market capture ratio is a metric used to compare aninvestment manager'sperformance against a benchmarkindexin rising markets. Investors can calculate it by dividing the manager's returns by the returns of the index during the period of time when the market is in an upward trend. It contrasts with the thedown-market capture ratiothat is used to assess performance in declining markets. Investors and analysts may want to consider the up- and down-market capture ratios together to obtain a comprehensive view of a manager's overall investment performance.

title: Up Volume
definition: "Up volume" generally refers to an increase in the volume of shares traded in either a market or security that leads to an increase in value. Up volume can be contrasted withdown volume.

title: Upfront Pricing
definition: A line of credit is a revolving loan that allows you to access money as you need it up to a certain limit. You can borrow up to that limit again as the money is repaid. Learn more about what a line of credit is, about the different types, when to avoid them, and how to use them to your advantage.

title: Upgrade
definition: An upgrade refers to the positive change in an analyst's outlook of a particular security's valuation based primarily on that security's improvingfundamentals.

title: Uphold
definition: The term Uphold refers to acloud-basedfinancial service platform. The service enables individuals to securely move, convert, hold, and sell various assets, including fiat currencies, cryptocurrencies, equities, and precious metals. Users can fund their accounts fromcredit or debit cards, bank accounts, or cryptocurrency networks. They can also easily move funds from one form of value to another.

title: Upper Class
definition: The term upper class refers to a group of individuals who occupy the highest place and status in society. These people are considered the wealthiest, lying above the working andmiddle classin the social hierarchy. Individuals who make up the upper class have higher levels ofdisposable incomeand exert more control over the use of natural resources. While the upper class makes up a small percentage of the overall population, it controls a disproportionately large amount of the overall wealth.

title: Upper Management
definition: Upper management includes individuals and teams that are responsible for making the primary decisions within a company.

title: UPREIT
definition: An umbrella partnership real estate investment trust (UPREIT) allows property owners to contribute their real estate to a REIT in exchange for partnership units, rather than selling the property. UPREITs allow capital gains tax deferrals and flexibility in managing investments. To participate, property owners need to meet certain qualifications, such as owning eligible real estate and meeting the REIT’s contribution criteria. They offer an alternative to traditional property sales, making them a strategic option for investors looking to unlock value while maintaining potential upside in real estate markets.

title: Upside
definition: Upside refers to the potential increase in the value of an investment, portfolio, company, sector, market, or economy. It's typically measured in monetary value or percentage terms. Analysts usetechnicalorfundamental analysisto estimate future prices and identify upside potential, which helps investors of all risk levels make informed decisions and aim for higher returns. Upside is often influenced by broader market and economic factors, and it's the opposite of downside, which reflects the risk of a price decline.

title: Upside/Downside Gap Three Methods
definition: The Upside/Downside Gap Three Methods is a continuation pattern in Japanese candlestick charting that helps traders identify whether an ongoing market trend will persist. This pattern is comprised of a sequence of three candlesticks and serves as a reliable indicator for traders looking to capitalize on market momentum.

title: Upside/Downside Ratio
definition: The upside/downside ratio is a key market breadth indicator that measures the volume of advancing versus declining issues on a stock exchange. This ratio helps investors determine market momentum and assess whether the market is overbought or oversold. By mastering the upside/downside ratio, you can enhance your ability to spot trends and make informed trading decisions."

title: Upside Gap Two Crows
definition: A white candlestick is a bullish pattern in candlestick charting that indicates that a security's price closed higher than it opened during that trading period. An up-candlestick may be depicted as either green or black on some charts. They can be contrasted with a red candlestick, which denotes a lower closing price than the prior period.

title: Upside Tasuki Gap
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Upstairs Market
definition: The term upstairs market refers to a network that exists between large firms andinstitutional investors. This network involves large trades or block orders.Tradesin these transactions are not submitted through astock exchange, which means they are not visible to other market participants. These orders are carried out directly between buyers and sellers, with professionalbrokersacting as intermediaries. The size of the order made in the upstairs market accounts for a big portion of the market'strading volume.

title: Upstart
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Upstream
definition: Upstream in the oil and gas industry refers to the crucial stages of exploration and production, where companies identify oil and gas reserves and then extract these resources. This phase includes activities like land surveys, drilling operations, and the use of technology to locate hydrocarbons. The upstream sector is the first step before the midstream and downstream stages, which handle transportation, refining, and distribution.

title: Upstream Capital Costs Index (UCCI)
definition: The Upstream Capital Costs Index (UCCI) is a proprietary metric index that tracks thecomposite capital costof materials, facilities, equipment, and personnel for oil and natural gas producing projects. Cambridge Energy Research Associates (CERA), formerly owned by IHS Markit, merged with S&P Global in February 2022, which owns and manages the index.

title: Upstream Guarantee
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Uptick
definition: Uptick describes an increase in the price of a financial instrument since the last transaction. An uptick occurs when a security’s price rises in relation to the lasttickortrade. An uptick is sometimes referred to as aplus tick.

title: Uptick Rule
definition: The Uptick Rule is a regulation designed to prevent rapid declines in a security’s price by allowingshort salesonly at a price higher than the previous trade. Established by theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC), the rule aims to maintain market confidence and curb excessive downward pressure on stock prices. In 2010, the SEC revised the rule, introducing the “alternative uptick rule,” which applies only when a stock drops 10% or more in a single day. Certain exemptions exist, and the rule doesn't apply in all situations, such as for exempted securities or specific institutional trades, ensuring flexibility while still protecting the market.

title: Uptick Volume
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Uptrend
definition: An uptrend describes the price movement of a financial asset when the overall direction is upward. In an uptrend, each successivepeak and troughis higher than the ones found earlier in the trend. The uptrend is therefore composed of higher swing lows and higherswing highs. As long as the price is making these higher swing lows and higher swing highs, the uptrend is considered intact.

title: Urban Development Act Of 1970
definition: The Urban Development Act of 1970 is legislation enforced through the U.S.Department of Housing and Urban Development(HUD) that introduced the Federal Experimental Housing Allowance Program and Community Development Corporation.

title: U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID)
definition: The term foreign aid encompasses various contributions—gifts,grants, or loans—that one nation offers to another. It extends beyond justcapital, also covering essential food, supplies, and services like military and humanitarian assistance. This assistance often flows from economically advanced to developing nations, aiding in disaster response, conflict situations, or economic crises. The United States, known as a generous provider, leads in global aid contributions.

title: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
definition: The Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) is a Cabinet-level government department that provides health and human services and promotes research in social services, medicine, and public health. The HHS achieves its goals through 12 agencies that manage more than 100 programs. The agencies include the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), theFood and Drug Administration (FDA), and the Administration for Children and Families (ACF).

title: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
definition: The Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) is a U.S. government agency that supports community development and homeownership.

title: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: U.S. Dollar Index (USDX)
definition: The U.S. dollar index is a measure of the U.S. dollar's value relative to its most significant trading partners' currencies: EUR, JPY, CAD, GBP, SEK, and CHF.

title: U.S. House Financial Services Committee
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: U.S.-Mexico Trade Agreement
definition: The United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) was signed on November 30, 2018, and took effect on July 1, 2020, replacing the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA). While NAFTA was pivotal in reducing tariffs among the U.S., Mexico, and Canada, it faced criticism for job losses in the U.S., motivating the creation of USMCA. This updated agreement aims to strengthen North American trade, with new provisions that enhance labor laws, increase regional content requirements for the automotive sector, and expand intellectual property protections.

title: U.S. Savings Bonds
definition: A U.S. savings bond is a government bond offered to its citizens to help fund federal spending, and which provides savers with a guaranteed, although modest, return. These bonds are issued withzero couponat a discount with an implied fixed rate of interest over a fixed period of time. For instance, Series EE savings bonds are sold at 50% of their face value, and mature to their full value after 20 years.

title: U.S. Savings Bond Adjustment
definition: According to Treasury Direct, interest from EEU.S. savings bondsis taxed at the federal level but not at the state or local levels for income. Bonds typically earn interest, which is the amount that a bond can be redeemed for above its face value. The face value is the bond's original purchase price. The interest on savings bonds is also subject to federal gift, estate, andexcise taxes. On the state level, the tax on the interest applies to estates or inheritances.

title: U.S. Treasury
definition: The U.S. Treasury, created in 1789, is the government department responsible for issuing allTreasury bonds, notes, and bills.Among the government departments operating under the U.S. Treasury umbrella are theInternal Revenue Service(IRS), the U.S.Mint, the Bureau of the Fiscal Service, and the Alcohol andTobacco Taxand Trade Bureau.

title: Usance
definition: In international trade, usance refers to the customary time allowed between the issue of a bill and its payment, with the period varying by country, often from a few weeks to a few months.

title: USC Marshall School of Business
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: USD
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Use and Occupancy (U&O)
definition: Use and occupancy agreements are short-term arrangements that provide temporary rights to access and live in a property during transitional phases of real estate transactions.

title: Use Tax
definition: Use tax is levied on goods acquired tax-free from outside states, intended for use in your home state, where sales tax is typically required. Buyers are to compute and remit this tax, mirroring the local sales tax rate. This self-assessed tax mainly ensures fair competition for in-state sellers and funding for state services, although enforcement remains challenging.

title: Useful Life
definition: The useful life of an asset is a key accounting estimate crucial for determining how long an asset remains serviceable and fetches revenue efficiently. Factors such as usage, purchase age, and technological changes influence these estimates. The IRS uses useful life to set depreciation timelines, impacting how businesses calculate asset value over time.

title: User Fee
definition: A user fee is a payment to gain access to a particular service or facility. Many user fees are implemented by private businesses, such as parking garages.

title: Usual, Customary, and Reasonable Fees
definition: Usual, customary, and reasonable (UCR) fees are the amounts policyholders may owe for medical services, based on standard rates charged in their geographic area. Knowing how UCR works helps patients avoid surprise bills, since it affects both in-network and out-of-network costs, and even informs how programs like Medicare set allowable charges.

title: Usufruct
definition: Usufruct is a legal arrangement allowing individuals temporary rights to use and earn from another's property without owning it. This concept involves two main elements: usus, the right to utilize the property, and fructus, the right to enjoy its profits. Common in mixed and civil law jurisdictions, usufruct can serve as a practical solution for property management during an owner's ill health or until anestateis settled.

title: Usury
definition: Usury is the act of lending money at aninterest ratethat is considered unreasonably high or that is higher than therate permitted by law. Usury first became common in England under King Henry VIII and originally pertained to charging any amount of interest on loaned funds. Over time it evolved to mean charging excess interest, but in some religions and parts of the world charging any interest is considered illegal.Modern usury laws are intended to protect consumers from predatory lenders.

title: Usury Laws
definition: Usury is lending money at aninterest ratethat is unreasonably high or higher than therate permitted by law.Usurylaws protect consumers by governing the interest charged on a loan. In the United States, individual states are responsible for setting usury laws. Though this type of financial activity could fall under the Constitution's commerce clause,Congresshas not traditionally focused on usury at the federal level.

title: Usury Rate
definition: The term usury rate refers to a rate ofinterestthat is considered to be excessive as compared to prevailing market interest rates. They are often associated with unsecured consumer loans, particularly those relating tosubprime borrowers.

title: Utilitarianism
definition: Utilitarianism is a social theory that promotes actions and decisions that benefit the greatest number of people.

title: Utilities Industry ETF
definition: Sector investing offers targeted opportunities into the stocks of companies in specific segments of the economy. The utilities sector includes companies such as electric, gas, or water utilities or those that operate as producers or distributors of power.

title: Utilities Sector
definition: Sector investing offers targeted opportunities into the stocks of companies in specific segments of the economy. The utilities sector includes companies such as electric, gas, or water utilities or those that operate as producers or distributors of power.

title: Utility
definition: Utility is an economic term used to measure the value a good or service brings to a consumer.

title: Utility Patent
definition: A utility patent covers the creation of a new or improved—and useful—product, process, or machine. A utility patent, also known as a "patent for invention," prohibits other individuals or companies from making, using, or selling the invention without authorization. When most people refer to apatent, they most likely are referring to a utility patent.

title: Utility Revenue Bond
definition: A utility revenue bond, also known as an essential service bond, is a type ofmunicipal bondissued to finance a public utility that repaysbondholdersdirectly from project revenues rather than a general tax fund.

title: Utilization Fee
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: UTXO
definition: An unspent transaction output (UTXO) is the amount of digitalcurrencythat remains after a cryptocurrency transaction. You can think of it as the change you receive after buying an item, but it is not a lower denomination of the currency—it is a transaction output in the database generated by the network to allow for non-exact change transactions.

title: V-Shaped Recovery
definition: A V-shaped recovery is the best case scenario after a recession: it's a quick and sustained recovery after a sharp economic decline.

title: VA Loan
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vacancy Rate
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vacation Home
definition: A vacation home is a secondary dwelling, other than the owner'sprincipal residence, and is used primarily for recreational purposes including vacations or holidays. Also known as a recreational or secondary property or residence, a vacation home is often situated in a different location from the owner's primary residence. Because vacation homes are only used at certain times of year, many owners rent out these dwellings when they are not using them.

title: Validation Code
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Valoren Number
definition: A valoren number is a unique identification code assigned to financial instruments traded in Switzerland, similar to the way CUSIP numbers are used in Canada and the United States. These numeric codes help identify securities, although they carry no intrinsic information about the instruments themselves.

title: Valuable Papers Insurance
definition: Valuable papers insurance is a special type of property-casualty insurance. It reimburses the policyholder for the monetary value of any valuable papers such as wills, trusts, or corporate charters that are lost for any reason, although it cannot actually replace these papers. It is often purchased by corporations, small businesses, and wealthy people that rely heavily on unique documents.

title: Valuation
definition: Valuation is the process of estimating the current or projected value of an asset or a company.

title: Valuation Analysis
definition: Valuation analysis is the process of estimating an asset's worth, whether it's a business, security, commodity, or real estate, to support sound financial decisions. Analysts use different valuation methods depending on the asset type, relying on assumptions and model inputs to determine intrinsic value.

title: Valuation Clause
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Valuation Mortality Table
definition: A valuation mortality table is a statistical chart used by insurance companies to help calculate the statutory reserve and cash surrender values of life insurance policies.

title: Valuation Period
definition: The valuation period is the interval at the end of a given period of time during which value is determined for variable investment options. Valuation is the calculation of a product’s value and is typically done by appraisers at the end of each business day.

title: Valuation Premium
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Valuation Reserve
definition: A valuation reserve is money set aside by an insurance company to protect against potential declines in the value of its investmentassets. These reserves help insurers meet obligations to policyholders when asset values fluctuate over long policy periods. State laws require valuation reserves to support solvency, and they operate within broader regulatory frameworks that also include asset valuation and interest maintenance reserves.

title: Value
definition: Value is the monetary, material, or assessed worth of an asset, good, or service. "Value" is attached to a myriad of concepts includingshareholder value, the value of a firm, fair value, and market value. Some of the terms are well-known business jargon, and some are formal terms for accounting and auditing standards of reporting to theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

title: Value Added
definition: A value-added product offers more than just its raw ingredients by being enhanced with qualities that justify a higher price. Businesses leverage these enhancements to stand out in competitive markets and increase profitability. Understanding what sets a value-added product apart can help consumers make informed decisions while assisting companies in developing marketing strategies that highlight these unique characteristics.

title: Value Added Monthly Index (VAMI)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Value-Added Network (VAN)
definition: A Value-Added Network (VAN) is a private network provider that enhances communication between companies by facilitating electronic data interchange (EDI). VANs offer a secure and efficient method for businesses to transmit and receive data, especially in industries like supply chain management. Initially prominent for EDI, VANs have evolved to include message encryption, secure email, and audit capabilities to ensure data integrity and validate formats. Learn why businesses continue to utilize VANs despite the rise of the Internet and what makes them relevant today.

title: Value-Added Resellers (VAR)
definition: A value-added reseller is a firm that customizes and enhances third-party products before reselling them toend-users. They are especially important in the IT industry, acting as key distribution partners while tailoring hardware and software to customer needs.

title: Value-Added Tax (VAT)
definition: Value-added tax (VAT) is aconsumption taxlevied on goods and services at every stage of thesupply chainwhere value is added, from production to the point of sale. Unlike a sales tax, which is only charged at the retail stage, VAT is applied at each phase of production or distribution. The rate of VAT can vary significantly between countries, and not all nations, such as the United States, implement this system. Consumers or end-users ultimately bear the cost, accounting for the taxed value added throughout the supply chain.

title: Value at Risk (VaR)
definition: Value at Risk (VaR) is a quantitative measure used to estimate potential portfolio losses based on probability and market volatility.

title: Value Averaging
definition: Value averaging (VA) is an investing strategy that works likedollar-cost averaging(DCA) in terms of making steady monthly contributions but differs in its approach to the amount of each monthly contribution. In value averaging, the investor sets a target growth rate or an amount of their asset base or portfolio each month and then adjusts the next month's contribution according to the relative gain orshortfallmade on the original asset base.

title: Value-Based Pricing
definition: Value-based pricing centers on setting product prices primarily on the value that customers assign to the product or service. Thiscustomer-focused strategyallows businesses to determine pricing based on how much they believecustomers are willing to pay, rather than simply factoring in production costs. Companies with unique offerings or high-value features find significant advantages in leveraging this model over competing in price-driven markets.

title: Value Chain
definition: A value chain is the full range of activities needed to create a product or service and make it available to consumers.

title: Value Change
definition: The term value change refers to a daily adjustment made to the price of a company's stock. This change reflects the number of outstanding shares issued and currently held by investors. This figure is updated on a daily basis. Since the number of shares held by investors changes daily, this number can be updated every day to reflect the changes. It allows a group of stocks to be equallyweightedand more easily evaluated by investors, analysts, and other financial professionals.

title: Value Date
definition: The term value date describes when a transaction settles. It also refers to a future date used for determining the present value of a fluctuating product or security. It's when the value of funds, assets, or money becomes active. Value dates help ensure accurate payment timing for financial products and accounts.

title: Value Deflation
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Value Engineering
definition: Value engineering originated in the mid-20th century to help companies increase a product’s value by balancing cost and function. It involves analyzing a product or project to identify ways to reduce costs without sacrificing quality or performance. By focusing on innovative techniques and lower-cost alternatives, value engineering helps businesses optimize resources, improve efficiency, and maintain a competitive edge in the marketplace.

title: Value Fund
definition: A value fund is an investment fund that focuses on buying stocks believed to be undervalued based on their financial fundamentals, such as earnings, revenue, or assets. This is different from growth investing, which targets companies expected to grow quickly, even if their stocks are already expensive. Value funds look for companies trading below what managers believe is their true worth, with the goal of benefiting when the market recognizes that value. Knowing how these funds work can help you see how this strategy compares with growth investing and the benefits for a long-term portfolio.

title: Value Investing
definition: Value investing involves picking stocks that seem to be trading for less than their book value.

title: Value Line Composite Index
definition: The Value Line Composite Index is a stock index containing approximately 1,700 companies from the NYSE, American Stock Exchange, Nasdaq, Toronto, and over-the-counter markets.The Value Line Composite Index has two forms: The Value Line Geometric Composite Index (the original equally weighted index) and the Value Line Arithmetic Composite Index (an index which mirrors changes if a portfolio held equal amounts of stock.) These indexes are typically published in the Value Line Investment Survey, created by Arnold Bernhard, the founder, and CEO of Value Line Inc.

title: Value Network
definition: A value network is a set of connections between organizations and/or individuals interacting with each other to benefit the entire group. A value network allows members to buy and sell products as well as share information. Thesenetworkscan be visualized with a simple mapping tool showing nodes (members) and connectors (relationships).

title: Value Network Analysis
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Value of Risk (VOR)
definition: Value of risk (VOR) is a calculation companies and organizations use to determine the financial benefit that arisk-taking activity may deliver tostakeholders. It helps management decide whether the potential return is worth the risk involved. The reliability of VOR calculations depends on the accuracy of the data and assumptions used.

title: Value Proposition
definition: A value proposition is a marketing statement that summarizes why consumers should choose a particular product or service, highlighting the specific benefits it delivers. It clearly explains how a product fulfills a need and why it stands out from competitors. Originating with a 1988 McKinsey & Co. research paper, a value proposition is described as "a clear, simple statement of the benefits, both tangible and intangible." This concise message is essential for attracting customers and maintaining a firm's competitive advantage.

title: Value Reporting Form
definition: SEC Form 10-Q is a comprehensive unaudited report of financial performance that must be submitted quarterly by all public companies to theSecurities and Exchange Commission (SEC). It stands in contrast to SEC Form 10-K, which is required to be filed annually and is audited.

title: Value Stock
definition: Value stocks are equities priced below their intrinsic worth compared to key financial metrics like dividends, earnings, or sales. They attract value investors who believe the market undervalues these stocks, offering long-term growth opportunities. Compared to growth stocks, which emphasize potential over fundamentals, value stocks typically present lower risk and provide regular dividends. This article explores key aspects, including the differences between value and growth stocks, how to identify value stocks, and examples in today's market.

title: Value Trap
definition: A value trap is an investment that looks cheap based on metrics, such asprice to earnings(P/E),price to cash flow(P/CF), orprice to book value(P/B) ratios, but remains undervalued for good reason, often due to underlying business or industry weaknesses.

title: Valued Marine Policy: What It Is and How It Works
definition: A valued marine policy is a type of marine insurance that pays out a pre-determined amount if a covered loss occurs.

title: Valued Policy Law (VPL)
definition: Valued policy law (VPL) mandates full coverage (total payment) fortotal lossin certain U.S. states. VPL requires insurers to pay the policy'sfull valuewithout considering actual cash value at loss. States with VPL include Florida, Texas, and California, while others require proof of loss. Controversy arises when mixed causes (e.g., wind and flood) lead to disputes over coverage under VPL.

title: Canadian Securities Exchange (CSE): What it is, How it Works
definition: The term Canadian Securities Exchange (CSE) refers to an electronic alternativestock exchangefor small-cap andmicrocapfirms and emerging companies in Canada. The exchange began operations in 2003 and was recognized and approved by the Ontario Securities Commission as a stock exchange the following year. The exchange operates electronically, which means there is no physical trading floor. Nearly 800 companies trade on the exchange, which operates during normal trading hours Monday to Friday, except on holidays. The CSE is based in Toronto with an office in Vancouver.

title: Vandalism and Malicious Mischief Insurance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vanguard Exchange-Traded Funds: How They Work and Types
definition: Vanguard exchange-traded funds (ETFs) are a class of funds offered byVanguard. Exchange-traded funds combine the diversification of mutual funds with a lower investment minimum required. Vanguard also offers real-time pricing. ETFs are traded the same way that individual stocks are traded.

title: Vanilla Option
definition: A vanilla option is a financial security that gives its holder the right to buy or sell anunderlying assetat a predetermined price (the strike price) within a specific period of time. It is either acallorputoption and has no added features that other types of options might have. However, vanilla options can be combined with exotic and binary options to create custom outcomes. Traders and institutions use vanilla options for hedging and speculation purposes. Buyers of options have no obligation to exercise their option.

title: Understanding the Vanilla Strategy: A Simplified Guide
definition: A vanilla strategy is a simple, straightforward investment approach that avoids complex tactics or high risk. Many investors choose vanilla strategies because they're easy to understand and manage. Passive investing through exchange-traded funds (ETFs) and broad market index funds is commonly used in vanilla strategies. These strategies focus on steady growth over time instead of trying to time the market or pick winning stocks.

title: Understanding Vanishing Premiums: How They Work in Life Insurance
definition: A vanishing premium is the fee paid for a permanent life insurance policy that continues until the cash value of the policy grows enough to cover the fee. At that point, the premium "vanishes" as payments are no longer necessary, but are instead covered by the policy's internal value and dividend stream. This relieves the policyholder of paying out-of-pocket premium costs.

title: Demystifying VantageScore: A Guide to its Meaning and Components
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Variability
definition: Variability in statistics refers to the degree of dispersion or spread in a set of values within a dataset.

title: Variable Annuitization: What It is, How it Works
definition: Variable Annuitization is an annuity option in which the amount of the income payments received by the policyholder will vary according to the investment performance of theannuity. Variable annuitization is one option that can be selected by the policyholder during theannuitization phaseof a contract, which is the phase in which the policyholder exchanges the accumulated value of the annuity for a stream of regular income payments guaranteed for life or guaranteed for a specified number of years.

title: Variable Annuity
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Variable Benefit Plan: What It Means, History
definition: A variable-benefit plan is a type of retirement plan in which the payout changes depending on how well the plan's investments perform. 401(k) plans are one example of a variable benefit.

title: Variable Cost
definition: A variable cost is a business expense that’s directly affected by production.

title: Variable Cost-Plus Pricing
definition: Variable cost-plus pricing is a pricing method where the selling price is set by adding amarkupto totalvariable costs. The expectation is that the markup will contribute to meeting all or a part of thefixed costsand yield some level ofprofit. It provides a simple mechanism for covering all costs while ensuring a profit margin.

title: Variable Cost Ratio
definition: The variable cost ratio measures how a company's variable production costs compare to the revenues generated, helping assess the profitability and efficiency of raising output. It's calculated by dividing total variable costs by total sales, and it plays a practical role in decisions about pricing and managing profit margins.

title: Variable Coupon Renewable Note (VCR)
definition: A variable coupon renewable note (VCR) is a renewable fixed income security with variable coupon rates that are periodically reset. The renewable note is a type of debt security with a weekly maturity. The principal of this security is reinvested automatically at new interest rates every week it matures.

title: Variable Death Benefit
definition: Variabledeath benefitrefers to the amount paid to a decedent's beneficiary that is based on the performance of an investment account within a variable universal life insurance policy, a financial product that functions as both insurance and an investment. This variable amount is in addition to aguaranteed death benefit, which is constant.

title: Variable Interest Entities (VIE)
definition: A variable interest entity (VIE) is a legal structure in which controlling interest is determined by something other than majority voting rights. Controlling interest is arranged via a contractual relationship rather than through direct ownership. The VIE legal structure is defined by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB).

title: Variable Interest Rate
definition: A variable interest rate (sometimes called an “adjustable” or a “floating” rate) is an interest rate on a loan or security that fluctuates over time because it is based on an underlying benchmark interest rate or index that changes periodically.

title: Variable Life Insurance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Variable Overhead
definition: Variableoverheadis a term used to describe the fluctuating manufacturing costs associated with operating businesses. As production output increases or decreases, variable overhead expenses move in kind. Variable overhead differs from the general overhead expenditures associated with administrative tasks and other functions that have fixed budgetary requirements.

title: Variable Overhead Spending Variance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Variable Prepaid Forward Contracts
definition: A variable prepaid forward contract is used by stockholders to cash in some or all of their shares and defer the taxes owed on the capital gains. Company founders and executives are typical users of such contracts and get immediate access to cash by transacting them. They usually have a large number of shares in their company and seek cash while deferring taxes. Variable prepaid forward contracts can attract scrutiny from both the IRS and financial journalists because of the tax-deferral benefit they offer.

title: Variable Price Limit
definition: A fixed interest rate offers stability, ensuring level payments throughout your loan's term, unlike variable rates, which fluctuate with market conditions. Understand the implications, calculation methods, and strategic choices involved in selecting a fixed or variable rate to better manage your financial planning.

title: Variable-Rate Certificate of Deposit
definition: A variable-rate certificate of deposit (CD) is a type of savings account offered by banks and credit unions. Also sometimes called a "flex CD," this certificate locks up your money for a fixed length of time (term) but provides a fluctuating interest rate based on different factors such as theprime rate, theConsumer Price Index (CPI),Treasury bills, or amarket index. TheFederal Deposit Insurance Corp. (FDIC)protects variable-rate and other CDs.

title: Variable-Rate Demand Bond
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Variable Rate Demand Note (VRDN)
definition: A variable-rate demand note (VRDN) is a flexible debt instrument, often issued by municipal governments to raise long-term capital. Its interest rate adjusts with money market benchmarks like the prime rate, generally adding a small margin.

title: Variable Rate Mortgage
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Variable Ratio Write
definition: A variable ratio write is a strategy in options investing that requires holding a long position in the underlying asset while simultaneously writing multiple call options at varyingstrike prices. It is essentially a ratiobuy-writestrategy.

title: Variable Survivorship Life Insurance
definition: Variable universal life (VUL) combines lifelong insurance protection with flexible premiums and cash value you can access while alive. VUL insurance lets you invest and grow the cash value through subaccounts that operate likemutual funds. Exposure to market fluctuations can generate high returns but may also result in substantial losses.

title: Variable Universal Life Insurance (VUL)
definition: Variable universal life (VUL) combines lifelong insurance protection with flexible premiums and cash value you can access while alive. VUL insurance lets you invest and grow the cash value through subaccounts that operate likemutual funds. Exposure to market fluctuations can generate high returns but may also result in substantial losses.

title: Variance Equation
definition: Variance is a measurement of dispersion across a data set, comparing the difference between every other number in the set.

title: Variance Inflation Factor
definition: A variance inflation factor is a statistical tool used in regression analysis that measures how much two or more independent variables are highly correlated with each another.

title: Variance Swap
definition: A variance swap is a financialderivativeused tohedgeor speculate on the magnitude of a price movement of an underlying asset. Assets can be exchange rates, interest rates, or index prices. Variance is the difference between expected and actual outcomes. A variance swap is like avolatility swap, but it uses realized volatility instead of variance.

title: Variation Margin
definition: The variationmarginis a variable payment made to aclearing housewhen aclearing member'scollateral loses value. Variation margin is paid by clearing members on a daily or intraday basis to reduce the exposure of carrying high-risk positions.

title: Vasicek Interest Rate Model
definition: The term Vasicek Interest Rate Model refers to a mathematical method of modeling the movement and evolution ofinterest rates. It is a single-factor short-rate model that is based on market risk. The Vasicek model is commonly used in economics to determine where interest rates will move in the future. Put simply, it estimates where interest rates will move in a given period of time and can be used to help analysts and investors figure out how the economy and investments will fare in the future.

title: Vault Receipt
definition: A vault receipt is a legal document given to the owner of afutures contractwhose underlying asset is stored in a vault. They are usually used for precious metals such as gold and silver, which are valuable enough to justify storing them in a secured facility.

title: Veblen Good
definition: A Veblen good is a good for whichdemandincreases as thepriceincreases due to its exclusive nature and appeal as astatus symbol. This runs counter tothe prevailing circumstance of demand falling as prices rise. Thus, a Veblen good has an upward-slopingdemand curverather than the typical downward-sloping curve.

title: VeChain
definition: VeChain is a robust blockchain ecosystem aimed at revolutionizing supply chain management and business processes. With its dual-token system—VeChain Token (VET) and VeChainThor Energy (VTHO)—the platform facilitates value transfer and operational efficiency. Founded by Sunny Lu, VeChain is not only breaking silos in corporate data management but is also paving the way for Web3 development and sustainable business practices.

title: Vega
definition: Vega is the amount an option's price is expected to change for a 1% change in implied volatility.

title: Vega Neutral
definition: Vega neutral is a strategy in options trading aimed at managing risk by hedging against changes in the implied volatility of the underlying asset. Traders often use vega neutral positions to protect profits from unexpected volatility swings, ensuring that changes in volatility have minimal impact on their investments. Understanding vega is crucial as it measures how a 1% change in volatility affects an option's price. A vega neutral position ensures this price sensitivity is minimized, letting you focus on other market factors. By mastering vega neutral strategies, you'll enhance your risk management skills and make more informed trading decisions.

title: Vehicle Excise Duty (VED)
definition: Vehicle excise duty (VED) is a tax paid for most UK-driven and parked vehicles. VED does not fit the standard definition of a tax levied at the point of manufacture. For cars registered from April 2017 onwards, rates paid in the first year are related to the vehicle's carbon dioxide emissions, though subsequent payments are not.

title: Velocity of Money
definition: Velocity of money is the rate at which currency moves within an economy and indicates the frequency of financial transactions. It is measured as a ratio ofgross domestic product(GDP) to a country's M1 or M2money supply. Usually, when the velocity of money is high, the economy is strong and expanding. When low, the economy may be in recession and indicating a reluctance by people and businesses to spend. Economists calculate the velocity of money by dividing GDP by M1 or M2.

title: Vendor
definition: A vendor is an individual or company that sells goods or services to other parties.

title: What is Vendor Financing? Definition, Types, and Advantages
definition: Vendor financing is a financial term that describes the lending of money by avendorto a customer who uses that capital to purchase that specific vendor's product or service offerings.

title: Understanding Vendor Notes: Short-Term Loans, Terms, and Advantages
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vendor Take-Back Mortgage
definition: A vendor take-backmortgageis a unique kind of mortgage where the seller of the home extends a loan to the buyer to secure the sale of the property. Sometimes referred to as a seller take-back mortgage, this type of loan can benefit both the buyer and the seller. The buyer might be able to purchase property above their bank-determined financing limit, and the seller can get their property sold.

title: What Is a Venn Diagram? Meaning, Examples, and Uses
definition: A Venn diagram uses overlapping circles to graphically illustrate the traits that are shared and not shared by two or more sets of things being compared.

title: Venture Capital
definition: Venture capital is investor-backed funding directed to early-stage companies with strong growth upside.

title: Venture-Capital-Backed IPO
definition: A venture capital-backed IPO is theinitial public offeringof a company that was previously funded by venture capital investors. These IPOs giveventure capitalistsa planned path torealize returnswhile providing companies with access to public capital, with timing often shaped by market conditions to support valuation goals. Well-known examples include Uber, Tesla, and OpenTable.

title: Venture Capital Funds
definition: Venture capital funds pool money from investors to take equity stakes in promisingstartups, offering high-risk but potentially high-return opportunities. These funds not only provide essential capital but also guide young companies toward growth, with the end goal of profiting through strategic exits, such as IPOs or acquisitions.

title: Venture Capital Trust (VCT): Definition, Benefits, and Examples
definition: The term venture capital trust (VCT) refers to a tax-efficient investment vehicle that operates in the United Kingdom. The VCT is a closed-end fund that was created by the U.K. government in 1995 to help direct investment into local private businesses.

title: Venture Capitalist (VC)
definition: A venture capitalist is a private equity investor who provides capital to companies with a high potential for growth in exchange for an equity stake.

title: What is Venture Philanthropy? Concept, Application, and Historical Roots
definition: Venture philanthropy combines the strategies of venture capital investing with philanthropic goals to support social ventures. It focuses on providing funding and resources to startups and organizations aimed at creating social or environmental impact, such as green companies or charitable startups.

title: Understanding Product Versioning: Meaning, Mechanisms, and Examples
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vertical Analysis
definition: Financial statements that include vertical analysis show line item percentages in a separate column.

title: Vertical Equity
definition: Vertical equity is a method of collectingincome taxin which the taxes paid increase with the amount of earnedincome. The driving principle behind vertical equity is that those who have the ability to pay more taxes should contribute more than those who are not.

title: What Is Vertical Integration?
definition: Vertical integration is a strategy that companies use to streamline their operations. It involves taking ownership of various stages of its production process. Companies achieve vertical integration throughmergers or acquisitionsor establishing suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, or retail locations rather than outsourcing them. Vertical integration often requires significant initialcapital investment.

title: A Look at Kagi Charts
definition: The task of figuring out the short-term trend of any financial asset can seem daunting, especially when traders try looking at thechartof the asset's price for guidance. The day-to-day price fluctuations seen on a chart can appear choppy and can make it extremely difficult to determine which price movements are important and will significantly affect the direction of the security.

title: Vertical Market
definition: A vertical market is a specialized business sector focused on a specific niche, where companies tailor products and services to the unique needs of a defined customer group, unlike horizontal markets that serve broader audiences across industries.

title: Vertical Merger
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vertical Spread
definition: Vertical spreads in options trading involve buying and selling options of the same type with the same expiration date but different strike prices. Traders use vertical spreads to anticipate moderate price movements in underlying assets. Whether taking a bullish or bearish outlook, these strategies offer limited risk and return potential, making them suitable for traders with specific market outlooks.

title: Vertical Well
definition: A vertical well is a technique for accessing an underground reserve of oil or natural gas that involves drilling vertically into the ground. Drilling wells vertically is a traditional method of oil extraction, as compared to their more modern counterpart,directional drilling.

title: Very Small Aperture Terminal (VSAT)
definition: A very small aperture terminal (VSAT) is a two-way ground station that transmits and receives data from satellites. A VSAT is less than three meters tall and is capable of both narrow and broadband data to satellites in orbit in real-time without extensive physical infrastructure. The data can then be redirected to other remote terminals or hubs around the planet.

title: Vested Benefit
