title: Vested Benefit Obligation (VBO)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vested Interest
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vesting
definition: Vesting is a process by which an employee obtains ownership rights in work-related benefits, such as stock options, stock shares, and certain retirement plan assets.

title: Veterans Administration
definition: TheU.S. Department of Veterans Affairsis the federal agency that provides medical care, benefits, and other services that support veterans' health and financial needs. It began during theGreat Depressionas the Veterans Administration, then became a Cabinet-level department in 1989 and was renamed the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs.

title: Veterans Group Life Insurance (VGLI)
definition: Veterans Group Life Insurance (VGLI) is for former members of the armed forces who wish to continue thegroup lifecoverage they had while in active military service.A renewable term policy, this insurance pays cash to the beneficiaries of the veteran upon death.

title: Vetting
definition: Vetting is the process of thoroughly investigating an individual, company, or other entity before deciding to go forward with a joint project. A background review is an example of a vetting process for a potential employee. Once the vetting process is concluded, a well-informed hiring decision can be made.

title: Viager
definition: In a viagerreal estatetransaction, the buyer makes a down payment and ongoing payments until the seller passes away.

title: Viatical Settlement
definition: A viatical settlement is when someone who is terminally or chronically ill sells a life insurance policy for immediate cash at less thanface value, giving up thedeath benefitfor their chosenbeneficiary. This buyer pays a lump sum, takes over premiums, becomes the beneficiary, and collects when the insured dies, but returns are uncertain because timing is unknown. Unlike a life settlement, it's tied to serious illness.

title: Vicarious Liability
definition: Vicarious liability is when one party is held responsible for the actions of another party.

title: Vice Fund
definition: The Vice Global Fund, formerly the Vitium Fund, is a mutual fund managed by USA Mutuals that focuses its investments on vice industries often considered socially irresponsible investments or "sin stocks.”

title: Video Conferencing
definition: Video conferencing allows people to collaborate in real-time through audio and video links, facilitating communication among parties across different locations.

title: Vienna Stock Exchange (WBAG) .VI
definition: The Vienna Stock Exchange is the sole securities exchange in Austria and operates the market data hub for Central and Eastern European markets. Operated by the company Wiener Börse AG, the name of the company that operates the exchange. It considers itself a customer and market-oriented company that plays a vital role in Austria’scapital market.

title: Vintage
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vintage Year
definition: A vintage year is the milestone year when a project or company first receives investment capital, marking the start of its funded growth. Because returns vary with economic conditions at that moment, investors use the vintage year to help estimate potential ROI, spot broader market trends, and judge valuation. It also reflects where a company sits in the business cycle, which can shape its long-term performance.

title: Viral Marketing
definition: Viral marketing is a strategy designed to quickly and widely spread a brand’s message using social media and digital platforms. Successful campaigns depend on the message, the messenger, and the environment in which it spreads. Viralmarketingcan offer big advantages, like reaching large audiences at low cost, but it also comes with challenges like unpredictability and difficulty measuring success. Popular examples include Hotmail’s email promotion and the ALS Ice Bucket Challenge, which helped catch people's attention and drive engagement.

title: Viral Website
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Virtual Assistant
definition: A virtual assistant provides administrative services to clients, operates outside of the client’s office, and is an independent contractor.

title: Virtual Currency
definition: A virtual currency is a digital representation of value. Instead of having a physical form, like paper notes, it is stored and exchanged through designated mobile or computer applications. They can take the form of gaming tokens, cryptocurrencies, or central bank-issued digital currencies.

title: Virtual Data Room (VDR)
definition: A virtual data room (VDR), also known as a deal room, is a secure online repository for document storage and distribution. It is typically utilized during the due diligence process preceding a merger or acquisition to review, share, and disclose company documentation. VDRs offer enhanced security over physical data rooms and allow access for multiple parties.

title: Virtual Good
definition: A virtual good is anintangible assetthat is traded in a virtual economy, such as in online games. Virtual goods are by definition nonphysical; their value is determined solely by what users are willing to pay for them.

title: Virtual Office
definition: A virtual office gives businesses a physical address and office-related services without the overhead of a long lease and administrative staff. With a virtual office, employees can work from anywhere but still have things like a mailing address, phone answering services,meeting rooms, andvideoconferencing.

title: Vis Major
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Visa Card
definition: Visa cards utilize the Visa network for making transactions.

title: Visibility
definition: In a financial context, "visibility" describes how well management or analysts can predict a company's future performance, usually sales orearnings. Visibility can range from low to high or near-term to long-term. It matters to both management and investors because it shapes expectations and investment decisions, especially when economic conditions are uncertain.

title: Visible Supply
definition: Visible supply is the amount of a good orcommoditythat is currently being stored or transported that is available to be bought or sold. This supply is important as it identifies a definite quantity of goods available for purchase or delivery upon the assignment offuturescontracts. For instance, all of the wheat held in granaries or storage facilities, along with the wheat being transported from farms constitutes part of the visible supply.

title: Vision Care Insurance
definition: Vision careinsuranceis a health insurance add-on that will often cover routine eye health expenses such as eye exams, contact lenses, and eyeglass lenses and frames. Some plans may even provide a discount on LASIK procedures and other corrective surgeries. Premiums for vision care insurance are generally low.

title: Visual Basic for Applications (VBA)
definition: Visual Basic for Applications (VBA) is a programming language embedded in Microsoft Office that can be used to automate processes and build functions.

title: VIX (CBOE Volatility Index)
definition: The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX), also known as the Fear Index, measures expected market volatility using a portfolio of options on the S&P 500.

title: VIX Option
definition: VIX options are call and put options used to trade market volatility through the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX), also known as "the fear index." VIX is an indicator of the market's expectation of 30-day volatility in the stock market. Thus, VIX call and put options enable traders to hedge against anticipated market volatility.

title: Vladimir Lenin
definition: Vladimir Lenin led Russia's 1917 Bolshevik revolution and became the first leader of the Soviet Union.

title: Vocational Degree
definition: A vocational degree is an academic certificate awarded to students who have completed the degree requirements for a specific trade or career. Vocational degrees are popular because they typically require less time to complete than atraditional degree program(e.g., associate or bachelor's degree), and upon completion, the student is often ready to begin working the trade.A college undergraduate degree focuses on developing an individual's all-around intelligence and critical-thinking skills, but may not prepare an individual for a specific job. However, vocational degrees offer training for careers, such as medical coding and billing, auto mechanics, cosmetology, electrical work, and legal secretary work.

title: Voice-over-Internet Protocol (VoIP)
definition: Voice-over-Internet protocol (VoIP) is communications technology that allows users to interact by audio through anInternetconnection, rather than through an analog connection. Voice-over-Internet Protocol converts the voice signal used in traditional phone technology into a digital signal that travels through the Internet instead of through analog telephone lines.

title: Void Transaction
definition: A void transaction is a debit or credit card transaction that is canceled by the merchant before it is settled by the cardholder's bank and the merchant has received payment. Transactions are often voided when customers or merchants discover that an error has occurred or when fraud is suspected. Void transactions are different from refunds due to their timing and processing differences.

title: Voidable Contract
definition: A voidable contract is a legal and enforceable document that can nonetheless be rejected by one of the parties to the agreement if it has certain defects.

title: Volatility
definition: Volatility is a statistical measurement of the degree of variability of the return of a security or market index.

title: Volatility Arbitrage
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Volatility Quote Trading
definition: Volatility is a statistical measurement of the degree of variability of the return of a security or market index.

title: Volatility Ratio
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Volatility Skew
definition: Volatility skew indicates variations in implied volatility across options, revealing insights into market sentiment and expectations. Skew patterns serve as valuable tools for pricing options and developing effective trading strategies. By understanding the causes and impacts, traders position themselves to make informed financial decisions.

title: Volatility Smile
definition: A volatility smile is a common graph shape that results from plotting the strike price andimplied volatilityof a group of options with the same underlying asset andexpiration date. The volatility smile is so named because it looks like a smiling mouth. Implied volatility rises when the underlying asset of an option is furtherout of the money(OTM), orin the money(ITM), compared toat the money(ATM). The volatility smile does not apply to all options.

title: Volatility Swap
definition: A volatility swap is a forward contract with a payoff based on the realizedvolatilityof the underlying asset. They settle in cash based on the difference between the realized volatility and the volatility strike or pre-determined fixed volatility level. Volatility swaps allow participants to trade an asset's volatility without directly trading the underlying asset.

title: Volcker Rule
definition: The Volcker Rule is a federal regulation that prohibits banks from conducting certain investment activities with their own accounts and limits their dealings with hedge funds and private equity funds. The rule was part of the larger set of reform legislation enacted in 2010 and known as the Dodd-Frank Act.

title: Volume
definition: Stock volume, representing the total number of shares traded during a specific period, is a vital tool for investors. It provides significant insights into market activity and liquidity, validating market movements for better trading decisions. High volumes generally suggest active markets and improved order execution, making volume a crucial factor in technical analysis and trading strategies.

title: Volume Analysis
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Volume Discount
definition: A volume discount is an economic incentive to encourage individuals or businesses to purchase goods in multiple units or in large quantities. The seller or manufacturer rewards those buying in bulk by providing a reduced price for each good or group of goods.

title: Volume of Trade
definition: Volume of trade measures the total number of shares or contracts traded for a specific security in a given timeframe. It applies to stocks, bonds,futures, and commodities, providing insight into market activity levels. Higher volumes suggest more liquidity and efficient order execution. Understanding trade volume is essential for assessing market movements.

title: Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT)
definition: The volume price trend (VPT) indicator is a technical analysis tool that blends price changes and volume in a cumulative line to help confirm and spot divergences that can signal trades. It's similar to on-balance volume (OBV), but VPT weights the volume adjustment by thepercentage price changerather than a simple add and subtract, and it's available in most charting platforms.

title: Volume Weighted Average Price (VWAP)
definition: The volume-weighted average price (VWAP) is a technical analysis indicator used on intraday charts that resets at the start of every new trading session. It's the average price a security has traded at throughout the day, based on both volume and price.

title: Volumetric Production Payment (VPP)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Voluntary Accidental Death and Dismemberment Insurance (VAD&D)
definition: Voluntary accidental death and dismemberment insurance (VAD&D) is a financial protection plan that provides abeneficiarywith cash in the event that the policyholder is accidentally killed or loses a specific body part. VAD&D is a limited form oflife insuranceand is generally less expensive than a full life insurance policy.

title: Voluntary Accumulation Plan
definition: A voluntary accumulation plan offers a mutual fund investor a way to accumulate a large number of shares over time by investing a manageable fixed-dollar amount on a regular schedule, usually monthly. The small investor gets the opportunity to take advantage of thedollar-cost averagingstrategy.

title: Voluntary Bankruptcy
definition: Voluntary bankruptcy is a type ofbankruptcywhere aninsolventdebtor brings the petition to a court to declare bankruptcy because they are unable to pay off their debts. Both individuals and businesses are able to use this approach.

title: Voluntary Compliance
definition: An IRS audit is a review conducted by the Internal Revenue Service to verify that the information on a taxpayer’s return is accurate and follows U.S. tax laws. The U.S. tax system relies heavily on voluntary compliance, meaning individuals and businesses must independently report their income and pay taxes. To support this system, checks and balances, such as Form W-2, help the IRS confirm that reported earnings match official records. Keep reading to learn how the audit process works and how you can stay compliant with tax rules.

title: Voluntary Conveyance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Voluntary Employees' Beneficiary Association (VEBA)
definition: A Health Savings Account (HSA) is a tax-advantaged account created for or by individuals covered under high-deductible health plans (HDHPs) to save for qualified medical expenses. Contributions are made into the account by the individual or their employer and are limited to a maximum amount each year.

title: Voluntary Employees Beneficiary Association Plan
definition: A voluntary employees’ beneficiary association (VEBA) is a tax-exempt trust under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(9) that employers establish to help cover eligiblemedical expensesfor employees and their dependents.

title: Voluntary Export Restraint (VER)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Voluntary Foreclosure
definition: A voluntary foreclosure is initiated by a borrower who is unable to continue making loan payments on a property, in an attempt to avoid further payments and prevent involuntary foreclosure and eviction. Borrowers may choose this option if their mortgage is significantly underwater.

title: Voluntary Lien
definition: A voluntary lien is a claim willingly placed on a property by the owner to secure the payment of a debt.

title: Voluntary Life Insurance
definition: Voluntary life insurance is a financial protection plan that provides a cash benefit to a beneficiary upon the death of the insured.It’s an optional benefit offered by employers. The employee pays a monthly premium in exchange for the insurer’s guarantee of payment upon the insured’s death.

title: Voluntary Liquidation
definition: Voluntary liquidation dissolves and dismantles solvent companies.

title: Voluntary Plan Termination
definition: Voluntary plan termination is the discontinuance of adefined-benefit planby an employer. Since an employer is not legally required to provide a retirement plan to employees, it can terminate an established plan.

title: Voluntary Reserve
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Voluntary Simplicity
definition: Voluntary simplicity is a lifestyle choice aimed at reducing the unnecessary consumption of material goods for a more meaningful and stress-free life. This lifestyle encourages individuals to minimize their focus on accumulating wealth and instead prioritize personal fulfillment and environmental sustainability.

title: Voluntary Termination
definition: Voluntary termination may refer to a variety of actions, but most commonly, it refers to an employee's decision toleave a jobon their own accord. It differs from a layoff or a firing, in which the decision to end employment was made by the employer or another party, rather than the employee.

title: Voluntary Trust
definition: A voluntary trust is a type ofliving trustthat is created during the lifetime of the trustor and is also known as aninter vivos trust. A voluntary trust is also defined as an obligation arising out of a personal confidence reposed in, and voluntarily accepted by, one individual for the benefit of another. This is in contrast to an involuntary trust, which is created by law.

title: Vomma
definition: Vomma is the rate at which the vega of an option will react to volatility in the market. Vomma is part of the group of measures—such asdelta,gamma, and vega—known as the "Greeks," which are used in options pricing.

title: Voodoo Economics
definition: In the 1980 presidential primaries, George H.W. Bush labeled Ronald Reagan's economic strategies as "voodoo economics." This phrase questioned the effectiveness of Reaganomics—characterized by tax cuts, deregulation, and reduced government spending—as a solution to the economic challenges of the time. Reagan's policies, known collectively as supply-side economics, argued that benefits for the wealthy would trickle down, spurring widespread economic growth.

title: Vortex Indicator (VI)
definition: The vortex indicator (VI) is a technical tool used to spot trendreversalsand confirm ongoing trends, built from two lines: VI+ for upward movement and VI- for downward movement, usually shown in contrasting colors. Created by Etienne Botes and Douglas Siepman, it often signals potential buy or sell opportunities when the two lines cross. Its readings become more reliable when paired with other reversal patterns.

title: Vostro Account
definition: A Vostro account is held by a domestic bank with acorrespondent bank. It helps manage international banking relationships and enables services like wire transfers and foreign exchange. Domestic banks can use Vostro accounts to handle global financial transactions and support international trade. These accounts are viewed from the domestic bank’s perspective, while Nostro accounts are seen from the foreign bank’s side; both play an important role in the international banking system.

title: Voting Shares
definition: Voting shares are stocks that grant shareholders the right to vote on important corporate decisions, such as electing board members or approving mergers. They give investors a voice in how a company is run, making them central to corporate governance.

title: Voting Trust
definition: A voting trust consolidatesshareholdervoting power by temporarily transferring shares to a trustee who votes on their behalf, often to help a company maintain control or deter hostile takeovers. These arrangements must be filed with the SEC in the U.S., and unlike voting agreements, they involve an actual transfer of shares rather than just a coordinated voting plan.

title: Voting Trust Agreements
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Voting Trust Certificate
definition: Avoting trustcertificate is a document issued by a limited-life trust of a corporation established to give temporary voting control of a corporation to one or a few individuals. A voting trust certificate is issued to a stockholder in exchange for his or her common stock, and represents all of the normal rights of a shareholder (e.g., receivingdividends) except theright to vote. The life of a voting trust certificate in many cases ranges from two to five years, at which point the common stock, with voting rights, is returned to the shareholder.

title: Voucher
definition: A voucher is the backup document for short-term bills owed by companies to vendors and suppliers.

title: Voucher Check
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Voyage Policies
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Vulture Capitalist
definition: A vulture capitalist is aninvestorwho seeks to extractvaluefrom companies in decline. The goal is to swoop in when sentiment is low–and the company is trading at a rock bottomprice–and take whatever action is necessary to engineer a quick turnaround and sell it on for aprofit.

title: Vulture Fund
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: VWAP Cross
definition: A VWAP cross is a trading indicator that occurs when a security’s price crosses the volume-weighted average price (VWAP). The VWAP is a technical indicator trendline that calculates a security's average price for the day, weighted by trading volume, to analyze price trends and liquidity.

title: VXN (CBOE Nasdaq Volatility Index)
definition: The Cboe Nasdaq Volatility Index (VXN) is a measure of market expectations of 30-dayvolatilityfor theNasdaq 100index, as implied by the prices ofoptionslisted on this index.TheCboe Global Markets (Cboe)launched the VXN in January 2001.

title: W-2 Form
definition: Form W-2 reflects the annual income an employer paid an employee and the taxes it withheld for the tax year.

title: W-4 Form
definition: Form W-4 is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) tax form you fill out and submit to your employer to indicate your tax situation. The W-4 formtells the employerthe amount oftax to withhold from your paycheckbased on your filing status, other jobs you or your spouse have, andcredits or deductionsyou expect to take. The W-4 is also called an Employee's Withholding Certificate.

title: W-8 Form
definition: W-8 forms areInternal Revenue Service(IRS) forms that foreign individuals and businesses must file to verify their country of residence for tax purposes, certifying that they qualify for a lower rate of tax withholding.

title: W-9 Form
definition: Form W-9 is an IRS form used to give your taxpayer information to someone who must report payments made to you.

title: W-Shaped Recovery
definition: When charting economic measures such as employment andgross domestic product (GDP), a W-shaped recovery looks like a "W": a sharp decline followed by a sharp rise back upward, followed again by a sharp decline and ending with another sharp rise.

title: Wage Assignment
definition: A wage assignment is an agreement that a portion of your disposable income will go directly to your creditors. This option may be written into some loan agreements, or you may seek it out to help pay down debt with minimal effort on your part.

title: Wage Earner Plan (Chapter 13 Bankruptcy)
definition: A wage earner's plan, known more formally asChapter 13 bankruptcy, enables individuals with a regular income torestructuretheir obligations to repay their debt over time.﻿﻿

title: Wage Expense
definition: A wage expense is a type of variable cost incurred by businesses to pay their hourly employees. It may be recorded as a line item in the expense portion of the income statement.

title: Wage-Price Spiral
definition: The wage-price spiral is a phenomenon where increasing wages boost consumer spending, leading to rising prices, which in turn prompts further wage increases.

title: Wage Push Inflation
definition: Wage push inflation occurs when employers raise wages to attract and retain workers, leading to increased prices for goods and services.

title: Waiting Period
definition: A waiting period is the amount of time an insured must wait before some or all of theircoveragecomes into effect. The insured may not receive benefits for claims filed during the waiting period. Waiting periods may also be known as elimination periods and qualifying periods.

title: Waiver
definition: A waiver is a legally binding provision where either party in a contract agrees to voluntarily forfeit a claim without the other party being liable. Waivers are commonly seen during settlement talks, when one party may be willing to pay out a slightly higher award, as long as the other person, often a claimant, agrees to sign a waiver relinquishing their right to further legal action.

title: Waiver of Demand
definition: A waiver of demand is a legal agreement given by a party who has endorsed acheckor abank draft. It states that, should the original issuer of the check or draft default, the endorser will take responsibility for honoring that check or draft on the issuer’s behalf.

title: Waiver of Coinsurance Clause
definition: A waiver of coinsurance clause is a provision in aninsurancecontract stating that the insurer will not require the policyholder to paycoinsurance, or a percentage of the total claim, under certain conditions.

title: Waiver of Exemption
definition: Waivers of exemption were outlawed in 1985. Before that time, creditors were allowed to seize assets that the state had deemed exempt in order to repay a debt.

title: Waiver of Notice
definition: A waiver of notice is a legal document that is used to waive the requirement of formal notification and is often used to expedite legal proceedings.

title: Waiver of Premium for Disability
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Waiver of Premium for Payer Benefit
definition: When a waiver of premium for payer benefitrideris part of a life insurance policy, an insurer won't require premium payments to maintain the policy under certain conditions. In such cases, the life insurance company itself operates as a payor.

title: Waiver of Premium Rider: Benefits, Eligibility, and Costs Explained
definition: A waiver of premium rider is a clause in life insurance policies that waives premium payments if the policyholder becomes critically ill, seriously injured, or physically impaired. There may also be other stipulations, such as meeting specific health and age requirements.

title: Waiver of Subrogation
definition: A waiver of subrogation is a contractual provision whereby an insured waives the right of their insurance carrier to seek redress or seek compensation for losses from a negligent third party. Typically,insurerscharge an additional fee for a waiver of subrogation endorsement.

title: Walk-Away Lease
definition: A closed-end lease is a rental agreement that puts no obligation on thelessee(the person making periodicleasepayments) to purchase the leased asset at the end of the agreement. A closed-end lease is also called a "true lease," "walkaway lease," or "net lease."

title: Walk-Through Test
definition: A walk-through test is performed by auditors to assess a company’s accounting controls and its risk management measures.

title: Wall of Worry
definition: The wall of worry is the financial market’s ability to keep rising amidst ongoing concerns or negative economic news. It appears during bull markets or after a downturn, when investors remain cautious while prices rise. It highlights how markets can overcome challenges like inflation, interest rate hikes, or geopolitical tensions. It also reflects investor confidence, allowing the market to continue advancing despite uncertainty.

title: Wall Street
definition: Wall Street is literally a street located in New York City at the southern end of Manhattan. Figuratively, Wall Street is much more. It's synonymous with the financial industry and the firms within it. This connotation has its roots in the fact that so many brokerages andinvestment bankshistorically have established their headquarters in and around the street. All the better to be close to theNew York Stock Exchange (NYSE).

title: Wall Street Journal Prime Rate
definition: The Wall Street Journal Prime Rate is an aggregate average of the variousprime ratesthat 10 of the largest banks in the United States charge to their highest credit quality customers for loans with relatively short-term maturities.This combined rate is obtained by way of a market survey and published regularly byThe Wall Street Journal(WSJ).

title: Wallpaper
definition: If you've found or inherited an old stock or bond certificate and aren't sure what to do, you aren't alone. They are not as easy to buy and sell as electronic stocks, and an investor might not even be able to cash them in if, for example, the company has long since closed. But these certificates could be valuable as investments or collectibles.

title: Walmart Effect
definition: The Walmart Effect describes the significant economic influence a Walmart (WMT) store has when it opens in a community. This effect often pushes smaller businesses out of the market and depresses local wage levels, sparking opposition from local retailers. Explore how Walmart's buying power shapes economies, affecting consumers and suppliers too.

title: Walras' Law
definition: Walras's law is an economic theory, which states that the existence of excesssupplyin one market must be matched by excessdemandin another market so that both factors are balanced out. Walras's law asserts that an examined market must be in equilibrium if all other markets are in equilibrium.Keynesian economics, by contrast, assumes that one market can be out of balance without a "matching" imbalance elsewhere.

title: Walrasian Market

title: Wanton Disregard

title: War Bond
definition: A war bond is a debt instrument created by governments to raise money for military operations during conflict. Governments often encourage citizens with patriotism to invest. These bonds were usually zero-coupon securities sold at a discount; investors were paid back at maturity. The United States, the United Kingdom, and Canada issued war bonds during major conflicts, such as World War I and World War II. While they helped governments fund wars and gave citizens a way to contribute, the returns were often modest.

title: War Chest
definition: "War Chest" is a colloquial term for the reserves of cash set aside or built up by a company to take advantage of an unexpected opportunity. While a war chest is typically used for acquisitions of other companies or businesses, it can also be used as a buffer against adverse events during uncertain times. A war chest is often invested inshort-term investments, such astreasury billsand bank deposits, which can be accessed on-demand.

title: War Economy
definition: War economy is the organization of a country's production capacity and distribution during a time of conflict. A war economy must make substantial adjustments to its consumer production to accommodate defense production needs. In a war economy, governments must choose how to allocate their country’s resources very carefully in order to achieve military victory while also meeting vital domestic consumer demands.

title: Understanding War Exclusion Clauses in Insurance Policies

title: Understanding War Risk Insurance: Coverage, Exclusions, and Benefits
definition: War risk insurance is designed to safeguard financial interests by covering damages and disruptions caused by war-related activities.

title: Warehouse Financing
definition: Warehouse financing is a form ofinventory financinginvolving goods orcommoditiesascollateral. It involves a loan made by a financial institution to a company, manufacturer, or processor. Existing inventory, goods, or commodities are transferred to a warehouse and used as collateral for the loan.

title: Warehouse Lending
definition: Warehouse lending provides loan originators with a line of credit to fund mortgages, allowing banks to issue loans without using their own capital. Once a loan is originated, it is sold in the secondary market to repay the credit. This short-term credit system allows banks to maintain liquidity while profiting from origination fees and the sale of loans.

title: Warehouse Receipt
definition: A warehouse receipt is a type of documentation used in thefutures marketsto guarantee the quantity and quality of a particularcommoditybeing stored within an approved facility. Warehouse receipts are important because they serve as proof that the commodity is in the warehouse and that the proper documentation has been verified.Warehouse receipts are also used during thewarehouse financing processto verify the quality of pledged/collateralized inventory.

title: Warehouse-to-Warehouse Clause
definition: A warehouse-to-warehouse clause is an insurance policy provision usually associated with commercial insurance. It is designed to protect policyholders from the risk of loss that may be incurred from theft or damage to goods while being shipped from one warehouse to another. In particular, large manufacturers tend to purchase commercialinsurancecoverage that includes warehouse-to-warehouse clauses.

title: Warehousing

title: Warm Calling
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Warm Card
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Warning Bulletin
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Warrant
definition: Warrants are a derivative that give the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell a security—most commonly an equity—at a certain price before expiration. The price at which the underlying security can be bought or sold is referred to as the exercise price or strike price. An Americanwarrantcan be exercised at any time on or before the expiration date, while European warrants can only be exercised on the expiration date. Warrants that give the right to buy a security are known as call warrants; those that give the right to sell a security are known as put warrants.

title: Warrant Coverage
definition: Warrant coverage is an agreement between acompanyand one or moreshareholderswhere the company issues a warrant equal to some percentage of the dollar amount of an investment. Warrants, similar to options, allowinvestorsto acquire shares at a designated price.

title: Warrant Premium
definition: A warrant premium is the difference between the current traded price of a warrant and its minimum value. A warrant is a call option granted by companies, giving employees or managers the right to purchase company shares in the future at favorable terms.

title: Warranty
definition: A warranty is a guarantee or promise from a manufacturer or seller about the condition and quality of their product. It outlines the terms under which repairs,refunds, or exchanges will be made if a product fails to perform as intended. Warranties provide consumers with assurance that the goods they purchase are as advertised, offering a structured recourse should issues arise. This article explores the mechanics of warranties, the distinctions between express and implied warranties, and key consumer protection laws like the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act that safeguard buyers from false claims and misrepresentations.

title: Warranty Deed
definition: A warranty deed is a crucial real estate document that ensures a property is available for sale with a clear title, free from liens or mortgages. It provides the buyer with legal assurance against future claims to the title. This article explores the workings of warranty deeds, the responsibilities of the grantor and grantee, and delves into the differences between general and special warranty deeds.

title: Wash-Out Round
definition: A wash-out round (also called a "burn-out round" or "cram-down deal") is a financing round where new investors take control by sharply diluting existing shareholders, often to keep a struggling company from falling. It's common in smaller companies and startups with liquidity issues or missed targets, and it often leads to leadership shake-ups and strategic overhauls.

title: Wash Sale
definition: A strategy in which an investor realizes a loss by selling an asset, then buys a comparable asset within a 30-day window to help lower their tax bill.

title: Wash-Sale Rule
definition: A strategy in which an investor realizes a loss by selling an asset, then buys a comparable asset within a 30-day window to help lower their tax bill.

title: Wash Trading
definition: Wash trading deceives market participants by artfully inflating trading volumes forsecurities. This illegal strategy involves the same trader buying and selling the security to portray false market activity. Whether through broker collusion or self-execution, such trades can mislead investors about a security’s popularity and price direction.

title: Wasting Trust
definition: A wasting trust is a fund whose assets have been depleted over time as plan participants receive their required payouts and no new money is paid in. The term may also be applied to income trusts which hold depleting assets, such as oil and gas.

title: Watchlist

title: Water Damage Insurance
definition: Water damage insurance is a type of protection provided in mosthomeowners insurancepolicies against sudden and accidental water damage. Water damage insurance does not cover damage resulting from the homeowners' negligence or failure to maintain the home in good repair. Moreover, flooding usually requires a separate policy if available.

title: Water Damage Legal Liability Insurance
definition: Water damage legal liability insurance provides financial protection to a person or business that causes water damage to the property of another.

title: Water ETF
definition: We know water is the source of life. But it can also be a source for portfolio diversification. Like gold and oil, water is a commodity—and it happens to be rather scarce nowadays. Investors can gain exposure to the commodity through brokers who offer water indexes, mutual funds, and exchange-traded funds. So, as with any otherscarcity, the water shortage creates investment opportunities.

title: Water Quality Improvement Act of 1970
definition: The Water Quality Improvement Act of 1970 was a piece of U.S. legislation that expanded the federal government's authority over water quality standards and water polluters.

title: Water Rights
definition: Water rights give landowners legal access to use water on or near their property. In the U.S., eastern states follow the riparian doctrine, allowing use by adjacent landowners, while western states use the prior appropriation doctrine, granting rights to the first user.

title: Waterfall Concept
definition: The waterfall concept is an estate planning strategy that uses whole-life insurance contracts to transfer wealth between generations. It's an efficient transfer of funds since it includes tax benefits and can minimize probate issues and legal costs. For example, a grandparent giving their child or grandchild a whole life insurance policy transfers wealth that accumulates tax-deferred value.

title: Waterfall Payment
definition: A waterfall payment structure determines the sequence and distribution of payments in a multi-tiered manner, ensuring that high-priority creditors receive their dues before others in the hierarchy.

title: Wave
definition: Elliott Wave Theory, a cornerstone oftechnical analysis, interprets price movements in financial markets through recurrent fractal wave patterns. Developed in the 1930s by Ralph Nelson Elliott, this theory became notable when Elliott correctly predicted a stock market bottom in 1935. The theory helps investors, traders, and portfolio managers detect predictablemarket trendswithin stock price movements, thus allowing them to effectively forecast and capitalize on market trends. Elliott outlined specific rules for identifying and predicting these wave patterns in his published works. Notably, the patterns capture investor psychology, underpinning the cyclical nature of market movements, even though they do not guarantee future outcomes.

title: Weak AI
definition: Weak artificial intelligence (AI)—also called narrow AI—is a type ofartificial intelligencethat is limited to a specific or narrow area. Weak AI simulates human cognition. It has the potential to benefit society by automating time-consuming tasks and by analyzing data in ways that humans sometimes can’t. Weak AI can be contrasted tostrong AI, a theoretical form of machine intelligence that is equal to human intelligence.

title: Weak Dollar
definition: A weak dollar means the U.S. dollar's value is declining compared to other currencies.

title: Weak Form Efficiency
definition: Weak form efficiency claims that past price movements, volume, and earnings data do not affect a stock’s price and can’t be used to predict its future direction.

title: Weak Hands
definition: "Weak hands" is a term often used to describetradersand investors who lack conviction in theirstrategiesor lack the resources to carry them out. It also refers to a futures trader who never intends to take or provide delivery of the underlyingcommodityorindex.

title: Weak Longs
definition: Theforex marketis the most highlyleveragedfinancial market in the world. Traders take on debt to acquire larger positions than they could with only their cash on hand. The standardmarginis set at 2:1 in equities markets. Atradermust put up at least $50 cash to control $100 worth of stock.The leverage may increase to 10:1 in options with $10 controlling $100. The leverage factor can be as high as 20:1 in thefutures marketsbut it depends on the broker.

title: Weak Shorts
definition: Weak shorts refer to traders or investors who hold ashort positionin a stock or other financial asset who will exit at the first indication of price strength. Weak shorts are typically investors with limited financial capacity, precluding them from taking absorbing too much risk on a short position. A weak short will generally have a tightstop-loss orderin place on the short position to cap the loss on the short trade. Weak shorts are conceptually similar toweak longs, though the latter employ long positions.

title: Wealth
definition: Wealth is the total value of all assets, including tangible items like property andintangible assetslike investments, minus any debts. It can be measured in absolute terms, showing total value, or in relative terms, comparing one’s wealth to others using market values. Unlike income, which is a flow of money earned over time, wealth represents all of an individual's accumulated resources at a given moment. Understanding how wealth is defined and measured is important for financial planning, assessing economic well-being, and making informed investment and policy decisions.

title: Wealth Added Index (WAI)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Wealth Effect
definition: The wealth effect is abehavioral economictheory suggesting thatpeople spend more as the value of theirassetsrise.The idea is that consumers feel more financially secure and confident about their wealth when their homesor investmentportfoliosincrease in value. They are made to feel richer, even if their income andfixed costsare the same as before.

title: Wealth Management
definition: Wealth management is an investment advisory service that uses financial services to address the needs of affluent clients.

title: Wealth Psychologist
definition: A wealth psychologist is a mental health professional trained to address issues relating specifically to wealthy individuals. Wealth psychologists help theirultra-rich clientsmanage feelings of guilt about wealth, advise oninheritanceissues, and counsel parents on how to raise children in financially privileged environments.

title: Wealth Tax
definition: A wealth tax applies to the net fair market value of all or some of a taxpayer's various asset types, including cash, bank deposits, shares, fixed assets, personal cars, real property, pension plans, money funds, owner-occupied housing, and trusts.

title: Wear and Tear Exclusion
definition: A wear and tear exclusion is a provision in an insurance policy that states that the normal deterioration of an insured object is not covered by the policy. Insurance is designed to protect only against unforeseen losses. Insurers would have to raise their premiums dramatically to cover the expenses if it were to cover inevitable losses.

title: Wearable Technology
definition: Wearable technology is known as wearables, and is a category of electronic devices worn as accessories, embedded in clothing, and even tattooed on skin. Wearables incorporate microprocessors and internet connectivity. They've evolved from items like eyeglasses and wristwatches to advanced devices like fitness trackers and virtual reality headsets. They have significant applications in various fields, particularly in medicine. Noteworthy examples include Google Glass, microchip implants, and medical monitoring and other healthcare devices.

title: Weather Derivative
definition: A weather derivative is a financial instrument used tohedgeagainst loss from unexpected weather conditions. Similar to insurance but based on measurable weather outcomes rather than physical damage, these contracts help industries like agriculture and energy manage financial risks tied to temperature, rainfall, or snowfall.

title: Weather Future
definition: Weather future is a type ofweather derivativewhere the payoffs are based on the aggregate difference in the measured weather variable, usually the recorded temperature, over a fixed period.

title: Weather Insurance
definition: Weather insurance is financial protection thatbusinesses(and sometimes individuals) use against measurable weather that can disrupt events andrevenue. Coverage can include low-probability events like hurricanes and tornadoes, and policies are often customized by trigger, severity, and duration.

title: Web 2.0
definition: Web 2.0 represents a transformative stage of the internet, characterized by increased user-generated content, participatory culture, and improved interactivity compared to its predecessor, Web 1.0. Without formal changes in the technology infrastructure, Web 2.0 revamped how users engage online, featuring social media platforms, collaborative tools, and new avenues for knowledge-sharing.

title: Wedge
definition: A wedge is a crucial pattern in technical analysis that traders use to recognize potential reversals or continuations in market trends. By connecting the highs and lows over a series of periods, wedge patterns form as trend lines converge, creating a distinct arrow shape. These patterns, observed as either falling or rising wedges, often serve as indicators of significant shifts in price direction—offering valuable insights for making informed trading decisions. Understanding how to interpret these patterns can empower traders to anticipate market movements effectively.

title: Wednesday Scramble
definition: Bank reserves, crucial to financial stability, are the minimum cash reserves institutions must maintain to meetcentral bankmandates. Held either in vaults or at the central bank, these reserves ensure banks can manage unexpected withdrawals and play a vital role in both economic stability and monetary policy.

title: Weekend Effect
definition: Stock returns frequently dip on Mondays compared to the preceding Friday, a recurrent pattern known as the Weekend Effect. It raises questions about trading behaviors and company practices that contribute to this financial anomaly.

title: Weekly Chart
definition: A weekly chart summarizes a week of trading data in a single visualization. It offers a long-term view that is useful for technical analysis.

title: Weekly Mortgage Applications Survey

Here's how to decide.

title: Weekly Premium Insurance
definition: Weekly premium insurance is a type of financial protection where the payments that the insured makes in return for coverage are paid weekly.

title: Wei
definition: Wei is the smallest unit of ether, the cryptocurrency coin used on the Ethereum network. One ether = 1,000,000,000,000,000,000 wei (1018). One wei is one quintillionth of an ether. It's the smallest unit a user can use to transact.

title: Weighted Alpha
definition: Weighted alpha measures the performance of a security over a certain period, usually a year, but with relatively more importance given to recent activity compared to earlier performance.

title: Weighted Average
definition: A weighted average designates varying degrees of importance to the numbers in a particular data set.

title: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)
definition: Weighted average cost of capital (WACC) measures the average cost a company incurs to finance its operations using debt and equity.

title: Weighted Average Cost of Equity (WACE)
definition: Weighted average cost of equity (WACE) is a way to calculate the cost of a company's equity that gives different weight to different types of equities according to their proportion in the corporate structure. Instead of lumpingretained earnings, common stock, and preferred stock together, WACE provides a more accurate idea of a company's totalcost of equity.

title: Weighted Average Coupon (WAC)
definition: The weighted average coupon (WAC) is a measurement of the rate of return on a pool of mortgages that is sold to investors as a mortgage-backed security.

title: Weighted Average Credit Rating
definition: The weighted average credit rating (WACR) relates to the weighted average rating regarding all bonds in abond fund. This rating procedure provides investors with an idea as to a fund's credit quality. It also helps to identify the overall risk involved with a bond portfolio. The lower the weighted average credit rating, the riskier a bond fund is. The weighted average credit rating is designated in letter rating form, including AAA, BBB, or CCC.

title: Weighted Average Life (WAL)
definition: The weighted average life (WAL) represents the average time each dollar of unpaid principal on loans, mortgages, or amortizing bonds remains unpaid. This calculation helps investors, analysts, and portfolio managers assess credit risk by estimating how long it will take to recover half of the outstanding principal. Unlike simple interest or amortization, WAL focuses on principal payments, making it an essential tool for fixed-income securities analysis.

title: Weighted Average Loan Age (WALA)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Weighted Average Market Capitalization
definition: The weighted average market capitalization refers to a type of stock market index construction that is based on themarket capitalizationof the index's constituent stocks. Large companies would, therefore, account for a greater portion of an index than smaller stocks. This means the movement of an index would depend on a small set of stocks.

title: Weighted Average Maturity (WAM)
definition: Weighted average maturity (WAM) is theweighted averageamount of time until the mortgages in amortgage-backed security (MBS)mature. This term is used more broadly to describe maturities in a portfolio of debt securities, including corporate debt and municipal bonds.

title: Weighted Average Rating Factor (WARF)
definition: The weighted average rating factor (WARF) is a crucial measure used by credit rating companies that aggregates credit ratings into a single indicator of credit quality. It's used especially forcollateralized debt obligations(CDOs). WARF's calculation process involves the notional balance of assets and their corresponding rating factors. The result represents a portfolio's probability of default.

title: Weighted Average Remaining Term (WART)
definition: Weighted Average Remaining Term (WART) is a metric that captures the average time to maturity of a portfolio ofasset-backed securities(ABS). The longer the WART, the longer the portfolio's assets will take to mature, on average.

title: Welfare
definition: Federally funded assistance programs help individuals and families with housing, food, and healthcare costs.

title: Welfare and Pension Plans Disclosure Act (WPPDA)
definition: The Welfare and Pension Plans Disclosure Act (WPPDA) was a 1950s-era law that gave theU.S. Department of Laborregulatory authority over private employee benefits plans for the first time. In an effort to increase transparency, the WPPDA mandated that employers andlabor unionsprovide plan descriptions and financial reports to the government. It was intended to make plan sponsors more accountable to participants and beneficiaries for the financial health of the plans.

title: Welfare Economics
definition: Welfare economics studies how the distribution of resources impacts social welfare and economic efficiency. It guides public policy through utility theory and Pareto efficiency, though it faces challenges due to subjective assumptions and utility comparisons.

title: Welfare Loss Of Taxation
definition: The welfare loss of taxation is a decrease in the economic and social well-being of taxpayers caused by levying a new tax. It is the total cost to society incurred by taking purchasing power from taxpayers and giving it to the taxing authority. It produces a social welfare loss that can outweigh social welfare benefits.

title: Welfare State
definition: The term "welfare state" refers to a type of political system̦̦ in which a government plays a key role in the protection and promotion of the economic and social well-being of its citizens.

title: Wellness Program
definition: The term wellness program refers to an organizational approach to improving individual health. Wellness programs are commonly offered by companies to their employees, providing financial incentives or other resources to stay healthy.

title: Wells Notice
definition: A Wells Notice is a notification issued by regulators to inform individuals or companies of completed investigations where infractions have been discovered. It usually takes the form of a letter, which notifies recipients both of the broad nature of the violations uncovered as well as the nature of the enforcement proceedings to be initiated against the recipient.

title: West African CFA Franc (XOF)
definition: The West African CFA franc (XOF) is the currency used by eight West African countries within the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU). Managed by the Central Bank of West African States, located in Dakar, Senegal, the XOF is subdivided into 100 centimes. Linked to the French franc, it is now pegged to the euro, providing stability for the region. XOF helps facilitate trade, coordinate fiscal policies, and promote economic integration among member countries.

title: West Texas Intermediate (WTI)
definition: West Texas Intermediate (WTI) is a high-quality North American crude oil and one of the three main global benchmarks of oil pricing, alongside Brent and Dubai Crude. Originating in Texas, WTI is a light, sweet crude, meaning it has low density and less than 0.50% sulfur, making it easier and cheaper to refine into products like gasoline.

definition: Every industry has its jargon, and the financial industry is no exception. Below is a collection of the oddest business and investing terms found on Investopedia.

title: When Issued (WI)
definition: When issued (WI) means a transaction is made conditionally because a security is authorized but not issued. Treasury securities,stock splits, and new stocks and bonds are traded on a when-issued basis.

title: Whipsaw
definition: Whipsaw is a condition where a security's price is moving in one direction when it quickly changes and moves in the opposite direction.

title: Whisper Number
definition: A whisper number refers to the purported, unofficial, and unpublishedearnings per share(EPS) forecasts of professional traders and fund managers on Wall Street. The whisper number can also be applied to any news or data release in any market

title: White Collar

title: White-Collar Crime
definition: White-collar crime is wrongdoing rooted in the misuse of professional or organizational power.

title: White Elephant
definition: A white elephant is an asset or investment with such high maintenance costs that it outweighs its usefulness or profitability. Commonly seen inreal estateand large-scale projects, these burdensome properties can drain resources and prove challenging to sell. The term originates from the ancient practice in Thailand, where rare white elephants were gifted to monarchs, sometimes as a means to burden the recipient with unsustainable upkeep costs.

title: White Knight
definition: A white knight is a friendly buyer that rescues a company facing ahostile takeoverby acquiring it under more favorable terms. This strategy protects the target's management and often provides better compensation for shareholders, preventing control from passing to an unfriendly bidder.

title: White Label Product
definition: White label products are sold by retailers with their own branding and logo but the products themselves are manufactured by a third party. White labeling occurs when the manufacturer of an item uses the branding requested by the purchaser or marketer instead of its own. The end product appears as though it's been produced by the purchaser.

title: White List States
definition: White list states maintain a list ofinsurancecompanies that can use unauthorized insurers to provide specialized or supplemental coverage, known as surplus lines insurance.

title: White Paper
definition: White papers can be used to launch a product, highlight a problem to solicit solutions, or provide technical specifications.

title: Whitewash Resolution
definition: A whitewash resolution is a condition placed by thetarget firmbefore it is acquired by another company. In a whitewash resolution, the company being acquired pledges financial assistance to the acquirer as long as it remains financially viable for at least 12 months. The financial assistance must first be approved by the target company's shareholders. If approved, anauditormust then confirm the company'ssolvencybefore the firm's directors can proceed with the deal.

title: Whole Life Annuity
definition: A whole life annuity, also known as alife annuity, is a financial product sold by insurance companies; it gives out monthly, quarterly, semi-annual, or annual payments to a person for as long as they live, beginning at a stated age. Annuities are usually purchased by investors who want to secure anincome streamduring retirement.

title: Whole Life Annuity Due
definition: A whole life annuity due is a financial product sold by insurance companies that requires annuity payments at the beginning of each monthly, quarterly, or annual period, as opposed to at the end of the period. This is a type of annuity that will provide the holder with payments during the distribution period for as long as they live. After theannuitantpasses on, the insurance company retains any funds remaining.

title: Whole-Life Cost
definition: Whole-life cost, also called life-cycle cost or "cradle to grave," is the total expense of owning an asset from purchase to disposal. It goes beyond purchase and installation to include long-term costs such as operating, maintenance, financing, depreciation, disposal, plus overlooked environmental orsocial impacts. A whole-life cost analysis helps compare options with a fuller view of ownership costs and plan for expenses that often get missed upfront.

title: Whole Life Insurance
definition: Whole life insurance policies allow owners to borrow or withdraw the cash value.

title: Whole Loan
definition: Whole loans are individual loans, such as mortgages, that are held by lenders and may be sold in full without being divided into smaller, securitized components.

title: Wholesale Banking
definition: Wholesale banking is banking services sold to large clients. Such services include currency conversion, large trade transactions,mergers and acquisitions, andunderwriting.

title: Wholesale Energy
definition: Wholesale energy is a term referring to the bulk purchase and sale of energy products—primarily electricity, but also steam and natural gas—in the wholesale market by energy producers and energy retailers. Other participants in the wholesale energy market include financial intermediaries, energy traders, and large consumers. Wholesale energy markets developed following thederegulationand restructuring of utilities and electricity markets around the world in the 1990s.

title: Wholesale Insurance
definition: Wholesaleinsurancerefers to coverage for employer groups that are too small to qualify for true group coverage. A wholesale insurance policy is also known asfranchiseinsurance. It covers an entire group, though individual policies are written for each person that is to be insured.

title: Wholesale Money
definition: Wholesale money refers to the large sums of money lent by financial institutions in themoney markets. Thiswholesale bankingencompasses the market for tradable securities, such as Treasury bills, commercial paper, bankers’ acceptances, foreign or brokered deposits, certificates of deposit, bills of exchange, repo agreements, federal funds, and short-lived mortgage and asset-backed securities.

title: Wholesale Price Index (WPI)
definition: A wholesale price index (WPI) tracks price changes for goods before they reach the retail level, making it a key indicator ofinflationand economic trends. While many countries still rely on WPI to monitor upstream price movement across various goods, the U.S. replaced it with theProducer Price Index(PPI) in 1978, which serves a similar purpose. By highlighting cost pressures earlier in the supply chain, WPI helps businesses and governments anticipate inflation and make informed economic decisions.

title: Wholesale Trade

title: Wholesaling
definition: Wholesaling is the act of buying a large number of goods directly from a manufacturer and then selling them to retailers. Wholesalers buy in bulk at a discounted price and sell to a retailer for a higher price, which is how wholesalers make their profit.

title: Wholly Owned Subsidiary
definition: A wholly-owned subsidiary is a business entity whose common outstanding shares are all owned by a parent company.

title: Whoops
definition: Whoops is a negative slang word used at one time in history for the former Washington Public Power Supply System (WPPSS). Formed in the 1950s to ensure consistent electrical power for the Pacific Northwest, WPPSS did not succeed on many fronts—hence, its nickname of "Whoops."

title: Wide Basis
definition: A wide basis is a condition found infuturesmarkets whereby the local cash (spot) price of a commodity is relatively far from its futures price. It is the opposite of anarrow basis, in which thespot priceand futures prices are very close together.

title: Wide Economic Moat
definition: A wide economic moat refers to a firm's ability to maintain competitive advantages over its peers, providing it with stability and the potential for long-term profitability.

title: Wide-Ranging Days
definition: Wide-ranging days describe the price range of a stock on a particularly volatile day of trading. Wide-ranging days occur when the high and low prices of a stock are much further apart than they are on a typical day. Sometechnical analystsidentify these days by using thevolatility ratio.

title: Wide Variety
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Widely Held Fixed Investment Trust (WHFIT)
definition: A widely held fixed investment trust (WHFIT) is a type ofunit investment trust(UIT) with at least one interest held by athird party. Investors who purchase shares of the trust receive any regular payments of interest or dividends earned on the equities or bonds held in trust.

title: Widow-and-Orphan Stock
definition: Widow-and-orphan stock refers to an equity investment that often pays a highdividendand is moreover generally considered low-risk. These tend to be large, mature, stalwart companies in non-cyclical business sectors.

title: Widow Maker
definition: In the world of financial markets, a widow maker is an investment that results in large, potentially devastating losses. It can also refer to a trade that results in a loss for virtually everyone who tries it. The risks involved in widow maker trades often defy market consensus and historical patterns. Shorting Japanese government bonds and trading natural gas futures are commonly known widow maker trades.

title: Widow's Allowance
definition: A widow's allowance refers to money paid to a surviving spouse from the estate of a deceased individual to meet their immediate needs.

title: Widow's Exemption
definition: A widow(er)'s tax exemption is a measure that provides financial relief following a spouse's death. Per federal law, widow(er)s receive tax relief from estate and inheritance windfalls. State laws vary by jurisdiction but in general permit a tax reduction for a specific period of time. The tax exemption applies only to legally married spouses. Those in domestic partnerships or civil unions are ineligible for the exemption.

title: Wilcoxon Test
definition: A Wilcoxon test helps researchers understand whether the outcome of e.g., an effect or activity for a particular circumstance differs between the subjects studied.

title: Wild Card Option
definition: A wild card option is a type of option that is embedded in certain Treasury securities: interest-bearing and low-risk bonds issued by the federal government to finance government spending. A wild card option permits the seller of a Treasury bond to postpone delivery of its underlying asset until after regular trading hours.

title: Wild Card Play

title: Wildcat Banking
definition: Wildcat banking refers to the banking industry in parts of the United States from 1837 to 1865, when banks were established in remote and inaccessible locations. During this period, banks were chartered by state law without any federal oversight. Less stringent regulations on the banking industry at the time led to this period, also being referred to as the Free Banking Era.

title: Wildcat Drilling
definition: Wildcat drilling refers to the exploratory process of drilling oil or natural gas wells in regions that have yet to be proven or have been entirely exhausted as production sites. This type of drilling is crucial for discovering new energy reserves and involves significant risks and potential rewards, especially for smaller energy companies.

title: Wildcatting
definition: Wildcat drilling refers to the exploratory process of drilling oil or natural gas wells in regions that have yet to be proven or have been entirely exhausted as production sites. This type of drilling is crucial for discovering new energy reserves and involves significant risks and potential rewards, especially for smaller energy companies.

title: Wilder's DMI (ADX)
definition: Wilder’s DMI (ADX) consists of three indicators that measure a trend’s strength and direction. Three lines compose the Direction Movement Index (DMI): ADX (black line), DI+ (green line), and DI- (red line). The Average Directional Index (ADX) line shows the strength of thetrend. The higher the ADX value, the stronger the trend. The color of the lines can be altered, but black, green, and red are the default in most software.

title: Will
definition: A will, also called a last will and testament, is a legally enforceable declaration of how a person wants their property andassetsdistributed after death.

title: William Dillard II
definition: William T. Dillard II is the CEO of Dillard's Inc., a department store chain based in Little Rock, Arkansas. As caretaker of his father's company, founded in 1938, William T. Dillard II strives to preserve the company's strategy to offer fashionable products with exceptional customer service.

title: William F. Sharpe
definition: William Forsyth Sharpe is an American economist who won the 1990 Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences, along withHarry MarkowitzandMerton Miller, for developing models to assist with investment decision making.
