title: William J. O'Neil
definition: William J. O’Neil was a noted investor, stockbroker, and author. He is known as one of the first investors to incorporate computers into his research and investment decision-making process. O’Neil also founded the influential investment publicationInvestor’s Business Daily.

title: Williams %R
definition: Williams %R, also known as the Williams percent range, is a momentum indicator that moves between 0 and -100 and measures overbought and oversold levels.

title: Williams Act
definition: The Williams Act is a federal law enacted in 1968 that defines the rules of acquisitions and tender offers. It came in response to a wave of hostile takeover attempts fromcorporate raiders, who made cash tender offers for stocks they owned. Cash tender offers threatened to destroy value by forcing shareholders to tender shares on a shortened timetable.

title: Willie Sutton Rule
definition: The Willie Sutton Rule is based on a statement by notorious American bank robber Willie Sutton. When asked by a reporter why he stole from banks, Sutton answered, “Because that's where the money is.” His end goal was money so why waste time looking for it in obscure or questionable places? He instead took the path of least resistance and most success and went straight to the source.

title: Wilshire 5000 Total Market Index (TMWX)
definition: The FT Wilshire 5000 Index (FTW5000) is abroad-basedmarket capitalization-weighted index that seeks to capture 100% of the United States investible market.The FT Wilshire 5000 Index was formerly known as the Wilshire 5000 Total Market Index (TMWX) before being rebranded on June 30, 2021, as part of Wilshire's partnership with The Financial Times.

title: Win/Loss Ratio
definition: The win/loss ratio is a performance metric that quantifies a trader's success by indicating the proportion of profitable transactions relative to unsuccessful ones.

title: Windfall Profits
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Windfall Tax
definition: A windfall tax is a surtax levied by governments on industries and commodity-based businesses, like the oil and gas sector, that have benefited from economic expansion. The main goal of a windfall tax is to redistribute excess profits for the public good, although its use and distribution are hotly debated. In the U.S., several windfall taxes have been proposed, none have recently been made into law.

title: Winding Up
definition: Winding up is the formal process of closing a business by selling its assets, paying creditors, and distributing any remaining funds toshareholdersor partners. It ends normal operations and exists to legally and financially dissolve the company

title: Window Dressing
definition: Window dressing in finance involves altering financial reports or portfolios to create a misleading impression of a fund or company's performance. The tactics are sometimes used in mutual funds and accounting. This deceptive practice can obscure real results and impact investment decisions. Detection them is crucial for making informed financial decisions.

title: Window Guaranteed Investment Contract
definition: Window guaranteed investment (WGIC) contracts are a type of investment plan in which the investor makes a series of payments to an insurance company and is guaranteed areturnon investment. This type ofguaranteed investment contract(GIC) differs from other GICs in that the investor makes principal payments in installments over time, rather than in one lump sum up front. Investors use window guaranteed investment contracts with401(k) plansand other defined contribution pension plans.

title: Window of Opportunity
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Windstorm Insurance: Coverage, Costs, and How It Protects Your Home
definition: Windstorm insurance is a special type of property-casualty insurance that protects policyholders from property damage caused by gales, winds, hail, and other gusty hazards. A subset of storm insurance, windstorm insurance is usually offered as arideron a standard casualty insurance policy through the extended coverageendorsement.

title: Understanding Winner-Takes-All Markets: Definition and Impact
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Winner's Curse
definition: The winner's curse is a tendency for the winning bid in anauctionto exceed theintrinsic valueor true worth of an item. The gap in auctioned versus intrinsic value can typically be attributed to incomplete information, emotions, or a variety of other subjective factors that may influence bidders.

title: Winsorized Mean
definition: Winsorized mean is a method of averaging that initially replaces the smallest and largest values with the observations closest to them. This is done to limit the effect of outliers or extreme values on a calculation. It is used in financial analysis to reduce outlier bias.

title: Wire Fraud
definition: Wire fraud is a type of financial fraud that involves the use of electronic communications.

title: Wire Room
definition: Wire rooms are facilities used by financial institutions to process fund transfers and order requests on behalf of clients. Typical tasks performed by wire room staff include receiving trade orders frombrokersand other registered representatives, transmitting those orders to theexchange flooror the firm's trading department, and relaying notices of executed trade orders back to the brokers involved.

title: Wire Transfers
definition: Wire transfers enable rapid and secure movement of money across the globe by facilitatingelectronic fund transfersbetween banks and financial agencies. Without the need for physicalcash, these transactions require essential details, like the recipient's name and account number, from the sender. Managed through systems like Fedwire or SWIFT, wire transfers provide a reliable way to conduct both domestic and international monetary exchanges using bank networks or non-bank services like Western Union.

title: Wirehouse
definition: A wirehouse is a broker-dealer offering a full range of financial services. While the term historically refers to a time when broker-dealer offices were only connected by telephone or telegraph wires, today they operate using the Internet and cloud technology, but the name remains.

title: Wirehouse Broker
definition: A wirehouse broker advises clients and trades stocks and other assets on their behalf as an employee of a full-service brokerage house. The word "wirehouse" implies that they are employed by one of the big players rather than working on their own or with an independent broker-dealer company.

title: Wisconsin School of Business
definition: Wisconsin School of Business is the business school of the University of Wisconsin at Madison. Founded in 1900, the school offers both undergraduate and graduate programs.It's known for its emphasis on applied learning, with coursework often incorporating problems drawn directly from real business situations. ItsMaster of Business Administration(MBA) is regularly ranked among the top 40 in the United States.

title: Wisdom of Crowds
definition: The wisdom of crowds refers to the idea that the collective intelligence of alarge, diverse groupcan surpass the judgment of any single expert. The concept, discussed as far back as Aristotle and later popularized by James Surowiecki, helps explain why financial markets can at times appear efficient and at other times not.

title: Witching Hour
definition: The witching hour is the last hour of trading on the third Friday of each month when options and futures on stocks and stock indexes expire. This time is when there are likely heavier trading volumes as traders close out options and futures contracts before expiration. Positions are then typically reopened in contracts that expire at a later date.

title: With Approved Credit (WAC)
definition: A "with approved credit" (WAC) statement is a common advertising disclaimer that signals a promotional offer is conditional on the buyer meeting credit requirements.

title: With Benefit of Survivorship
definition: "With benefit of survivorship" is a legal setup in which a deceased co-owner's share of property instantly transfers to the surviving co-owner(s), avoiding the delays of probate court.

title: With Discretion
definition: A discretionary order is an order condition that gives a broker some latitude for its execution in terms of timing, price, and so on. A discretionary order may also be called anot-held order.

title: Withdrawal
definition: A withdrawal occurs when money is removed from a financial account, such as a pension, savings plan, bank, or trust.

title: Withdrawal Benefits
definition: Withdrawal benefits refer to the rights of employees withpensionor other retirement plans (e.g. 401(k) plans) to cash out any accumulated funds upon leaving an employer.

title: Withdrawal Credits, Pension Plan
definition: A withdrawal credit in a pension plan refers to the portion of an individual’s retirement assets in aqualified pension planthat the employee is entitled to withdraw when they leave a job.

title: Withdrawal Penalty
definition: A withdrawal penalty refers to any penalty incurred by an individual for theearly withdrawalof funds from an account that is either locked in for a stated period, as in a time deposit at a financial institution (e.g., a CD), or where such withdrawals are subject to penalties by law, such as from anindividual retirement account (IRA)or401(k) plan.

title: Withdrawal Plan
definition: A withdrawal plan is a financial plan that allows a shareholder to withdraw money from amutual fundor other investment account at predetermined intervals. Often, this type of plan is used to fund expenses during retirement. However, it may be used for other purposes as well.

title: Withholding
definition: Withholding is the portion of an employee's income deducted and remitted by the employer directly to tax authorities. Withholding taxes are paid federally and may also be remitted to state and local tax authorities. The amount withheld depends on several factors like the employee's income and marital status, which are reported onForm W-4. Federal income, Social Security, and Medicare taxes are among those withheld from employee paychecks.Taxpayers owe money if their withholding amount isn't enough.

title: Withholding Allowance
definition: A withholding allowance is an exemption that reduces how much income an employer deducts from an employee’s paycheck for taxes and transmits to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) on their behalf.

title: Withholding Tax
definition: The term "withholding tax" refers to the money that an employer deducts from an employee’s gross wages and pays directly to the government. The amount withheld is a credit against theincome taxesthe employee must pay during the year.

title: Without Evidence of Insurability
definition: Without evidence of insurability means an insurance provider underwrote a policy, such as for life or health insurance, without verifying that the policyholder was eligible for that coverage. Some group plans may not require proof of insurability if the applicant applies during the open enrollment period. Also, providers of plans offering lower or limited benefits may not need evidence of a policyholder's insurability. Also, convertible life insurance will not require additional evidence on conversion.

title: Without Recourse
definition: "Without recourse" is a term used in financial agreements to indicate that one party cannot seek repayment or legal action against the other if a borrower defaults. In these agreements, the risk of default shifts from the seller to the buyer.

title: Wolfe Wave
definition: In technical analysis, a Wolfe Wave is a price pattern consisting of fivewavesthat signal market equilibrium. It can indicate bullish or bearish trends. To be properly identified as a Wolfe Wave, a series of criteria must be met, including similar wave cycles and distinct price action in the third and fourth waves. The fifth wave in a true Wolfe Wave will be followed by a breakout in price. Bill Wolfe and his son, Brian first identified Wolfe Waves.

title: Word-of-Mouth Marketing
definition: Word-of-mouth marketing (WOM marketing) occurs when customers discuss a company's product or service in their daily conversations. It's essentially free advertising stemming from great customer experiences that often exceed expectations.

title: Worden Stochastics
definition: The relative strength index (RSI) and the stochastic oscillator are price momentum oscillators used to forecast market trends. The two indicators have very different underlying theories and methods despite their similar objectives.

title: Wordpress (CMS)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Work Cells
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Work Ticket
definition: A work ticket is a form that shows the time spent by an employee working on a particular job. It is used as a basis for billing the costs of direct labor to customers, and may also be used for calculating wages of employees who are paid by the hour. In the context of accounting for the hours an employee works, a work ticket is also known as a time card or a timesheet, which are more commonly used.

title: Workable Indication
definition: Workable indication is a pricing technique, stated as a range, which allows adealeror broker flexibility when offering to buy or sell a particular debt instrument. It primarily applies to themunicipal bondmarket.

title: Workers' Compensation
definition: Workers' compensation provides critical financial support to employees facing work-related injuries or illnesses. This mandated insurance ensures coverage for medical expenses, lost wages, and job retraining. Varying by state, except in Texas, the program is funded through employer-paid premiums. Accepting these benefits means waiving the right to sue the employer.

title: Workers' Compensation Coverage A
definition: Workers' compensation coverage A is the portion of aworkers' compensationpolicy that meets state laws by providing no-fault benefits for employees injured or killed on the job. It covers medical care, disability, death, and rehabilitation benefits, with premiums usually based on payroll and the type of work performed.

title: Workers' Compensation Coverage B
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Workflow
definition: Workflow describes the steps and tasks in a business work process, through which a piece of work passes from its beginning to its end. Organizations can attempt to improve workflow to improve efficiency, add responsiveness, and drive profitability. Some companies incorporate theSix SigmaandTotal quality management(TQM) philosophies to do so.

title: Working-Age Population
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Working Capital (NWC)
definition: Working capital is the difference between a company’s current assets and its short-term liabilities.

title: Working Capital Loan
definition: Working capital loans finance a company’s everyday operations and cover short-term expenses like payroll and rent. Ideal for industries with cyclical sales, these loans help businesses manage periods of reduced activity without investing in long-term assets. By enabling companies to maintain operations smoothly,working capitalloans play a crucial role in business continuity.

title: Working Capital Management
definition: Working capital management is the process of overseeing and controlling a company’s short-term assets and liabilities to ensure efficiency and enough liquidity to meet day-to-day expenses.

title: Working Capital Turnover
definition: The working capital turnover ratio reflects management’s ability to use a company’s short-term assets and liabilities to support sales.

title: Working Class
definition: Working class is a contested socioeconomic term used to describe low-income people. Typically people in the working class have jobs that provide relativelylow payand/or require physical labor. A college degree is often not necessary for this work.

title: Working Control
definition: Working control is when minorityshareholdershave enough voting power to influence or direct corporate policy without owning 51% or more of the voting shares. It often arises in companies with dispersed ownership, where a roughly 20%stakeor a coalition can steer major decisions, which can create friction with other stakeholders.

title: Working Interests
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Working Ratio
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Working Tax Credit (WTC)
definition: The Working Tax Credit (WTC) is a state benefit offered to individuals living in the United Kingdom who work and have a low income. It was introduced in April 2003 as a means-tested benefit, a central part of thewelfare statein the United Kingdom. A means-tested benefit is a payment available to people who demonstrate that their income and capital are below specified limits.

title: Workout Agreement
definition: A workout agreement is a contract mutually agreed to between a lender and borrower to renegotiate the terms on a loan that's in default, often in the case of a mortgage that is in arrears. Generally, the workout includes waiving any existing defaults and restructuring the loan’s terms andcovenants.

title: Workout Market
definition: A workout period in fixed-income investing is the time when a bond issuer fixes financial problems and avoids missing payments. This period helps investors understand the real risks and possible returns of a troubled bond. The issuer may try to restructure its debt or negotiate new payment terms during a workout period, while credit rating agencies review the situation and update their ratings. Investors can get better information about whether the bond may recover or face more trouble.

title: Workout Period
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Works-in-Progress
definition: In production and supply-chain management, the term work-in-progress (WIP) describes partially finished goods awaiting completion. WIP refers to the raw materials, labor, and overhead costs incurred for products that are at various stages of the production process. WIP is a component of the inventory asset account on the balance sheet. These costs are subsequently transferred to the finished goods account and eventually to the cost of sales.

title: World Bank
definition: The World Bank supports developing nations by providing financing for projects that assist in their economic growth.

title: World Bank Group
definition: The World Bank Group is the world's most prominent development bank, with a stated mandate and mission to reduce world poverty. It has 189 member countries and is headquartered in Washington, D.C. The CEO of the World Bank Group as of June 2021 is David Malpass.

title: World Economic Forum (WEF)
definition: The World Economic Forum (WEF) is an international organization headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, that brings together individuals and political and business leaders each year to discuss significant issues that impact the global economy. These include but are not limited to political, economic, social, and environmental concerns.

title: World Economic Outlook (WEO)
definition: The World Economic Outlook (WEO) is theInternational Monetary Fund's(IMF's) main report for global economic projections, used to assess expected growth and inflation worldwide. Published by the IMF using consultations with the IMF's 190 member countries, it covers indicators such as GDP, consumer prices, unemployment, andcurrent accountbalances, with full editions in April and October plus updates in January and July that help track changing global economic conditions.

title: World Equity Benchmark Series (WEBS)
definition: The World Equity Benchmark Series (WEBS) was an international fund traded on theAmerican Stock Exchange. It was introduced in 1996 by Morgan Stanley and was a type of hybrid security that possesses qualities of both open-end and closed-end funds.

title: World Federation of Stock Exchanges
definition: Approximately 53,795 companies are now traded publicly throughout the world as of May 2025, down slightly from 53,945 a year prior.

title: World Fund
definition: A world fund is a type of mutual fund or other investment company that invests in securities that are traded in several different countries, including the United States. This type of fund is sometimes also referred to as aglobal fund. However, that name should not be confused with the Global Fund, which is a specific international organization dedicated to fighting the spread of infectious diseases, such as AIDS, malaria, and tuberculosis.

title: World Gold Council (WGC)
definition: The World Gold Council or the WGC is anonprofitassociation of the world's leading gold producers. A market development organization for the gold industry, the World Gold Council includes 33 members and many members are gold mining companies. The WGC was established to promote the use of and demand for gold through marketing, research, andlobbying. Headquartered in London, the WGC covers the markets which comprise about three-quarters of the world's annual gold consumption.

title: World Insurance
definition: World insurance is a type of commercial liability coverage designed for companies operating across borders. It protects businesses from lawsuits filed outside their home country by extending coverage beyond standard geographic limits.

title: World Trade Organization (WTO)
definition: The World Trade Organization (WTO) is an international institution that oversees the rules for global trade among nations. It was created in 1995, superseding the 1947General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade(GATT) that was created in the wake of World War II.

title: WorldCom
definition: WorldCom was a major telecom company and one of the largest long-distance providers in the U.S. until its collapse in 2006 due to an accounting fraud scandal and subsequent bankruptcy.

title: Worldwide Coverage
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Worldwide Income
definition: Worldwide income is the total income someone earns from all sources, in and out of their home country. The Internal Revenue Service (IRS) requires U.S. citizens and resident aliens to report and pay taxes on worldwide income, even if it is earned abroad. This applies to wages, business earnings, investments, and other types of income from around the world. Keep reading to understand how worldwide income is taxed and the steps and strategies to help you be compliant with U.S. tax rules.

title: Worthless Securities
definition: Worthless securities have amarket valueof zero and, along with any securities that an investor has abandoned, result in acapital lossfor the owner. They can be claimed as such when filing taxes.

title: Wrap Account
definition: Wrap accounts are a type of investment vehicle where an investor pays for professional management of a portfolio for a flat fee.

title: Wrap-Around Insurance Program
definition: A wrap-around insurance program is specialized wrap-around insurance that adds punitive damages coverage for employment practices liability claims, beyond what a standard EPLI policy may include.

title: Wrap-Around Loan
definition: A wrap-around loan is a type ofmortgageloan that can be used inowner-financing deals. A wrap-around loan involves the seller’s mortgage on the home and adds additional incremental value to arrive at the total purchase price that must be paid to the seller over time rather than the lender. This type of loan helps homebuyers who don't qualify for traditional mortgages while giving the seller the potential to earn a profit.

title: Wrap Fee
definition: A wrap fee is an all-inclusive charge for the services of aninvestment managerorinvestment advisor. The wrap fee typically includes investment advice, research, brokerage services, and administrative fees.

title: Wrap-Up Insurance
definition: Wrap-up insurance is a comprehensive liability policy designed to cover all contractors and subcontractors working on large construction projects, generally exceeding $10 million. The two main types of wrap-up insurance are owner-controlled, arranged by the project owner, and contractor-controlled, managed by the general contractor.

title: Wraparound Mortgage
definition: A wraparound mortgage is a type of junior loan that wraps or includes the current note due on the property. The wraparound loan will consist of the balance of the original loan plus an amount to cover the new purchase price for the property.

title: Writ
definition: Writs are formal legal documents that compel action or inaction from an individual or entity based on judicial or administrative authority. Originating from English common law, these writs can take forms such as warrants and subpoenas, providing a mechanism to execute judgments, compel evidence production, or enforce court rulings. In modern law, they facilitate actions like property seizures and uphold rights through documents like writs of habeas corpus.

title: Writ of Attachment
definition: A writ of attachment is a legal order issued by a court that allows a creditor to seize a debtor's property before a final judgment is made. This order serves to secure the creditor's interest by ensuring that the debtor's assets are available to satisfy potential future judgments. Writs of attachment are commonly used in cases where there is a risk that the debtor may dispose of assets before the court reaches a decision. They ensure that due legal processes are followed and provide protection for creditors' claims.

title: Writ of Execution
definition: A writ of execution is a court-ordered procedure used to enforce judgments, transferringassetsfrom a debtor to a plaintiff. Upon issuing this writ, law enforcement is directed to ensure the transfer of property. It plays a crucial role in legal resolutions, often appearing in cases of bankruptcy and tenant evictions.

title: Writ of Seizure and Sale
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Write-Down
definition: A write-down is an accounting term for the reduction in the book value of an asset when itsfair market value (FMV)has fallen below the carrying book value, and thus becomes animpaired asset. The amount to be written down is the difference between the book value of the asset and the amount of cash that the business can obtain by disposing of it in the most optimal manner.

title: Write-Off
definition: A write-off is an accounting entry related to losses incurred by a business.

title: Write-Up
definition: A write-up is an increase made to thebook valueof an asset because itscarrying valueis less than fair market value. A write-up generally occurs if a company is being acquired and its assets and liabilities are restated tofair market value, under the purchase method of M&A accounting. It may also occur if the initial value of the asset was not recorded properly, or if an earlier write-down in its value was too large. An asset write-up is the opposite of awrite-down, and both are non-cash items.

title: Writer
definition: A writer (sometimes referred to as agrantor) is the seller of an option who opens a position to collect apremiumpayment from the buyer. Writers can sell call or put options that are covered or uncovered. An uncovered position is also referred to as anaked option. For example, the owner of 100 shares of stock can sell acall optionon those shares to collect a premium from the buyer of the option; the position is covered because the writer owns the stock that underlies the option and has agreed to sell those shares at thestrike priceof the contract. A covered put option would involve being short the shares and writing a put on them. If an option is not covered the option writer theoretically faces the risk of very large losses if the underlying moves against them.

title: Writing an Option
definition: Writing an option means selling a contract that gives the buyer the right to buy or sell an asset at a set price in the future. The writer earns apremiumfor taking on this obligation, with the amount influenced by factors like volatility and time to expiration. While writing options offers flexibility and incom potential, it also carries the risk of losses if the market moves unfavorably.

title: Written-Down Value
definition: Written-down value is the value of anassetafter accounting fordepreciationoramortization. In short, it reflects the present worth of a resource owned by a company from an accounting perspective. This value is included on the company's balance sheet in its financial statements. Written-down value is also calledbook valueor net book value.

title: Written Premium
definition: Written premium is an accounting term in theinsuranceindustry used to describe the total amount that customers are required to pay for insurance coverage on policies issued by a company during a specific period of time.

title: Wrongful Dishonor
definition: Wrongful dishonorrefers to a bank's failure to honor a valid negotiable instrument such as acheckor draft that has been presented to it for payment. If the instrument is valid and there are enough funds to cover it, a bank's failure to honor the instrument within the time period stipulated by the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) would constitute wrongful dishonor.

title: Wrongful Termination Claim
definition: A wrongful termination claim is filed in a court of law by someone who believes they were unjustly or illegally fired from their job. Wrongful termination claims involve an alleged violation of federal or state antidiscrimination laws, oral and written employment agreements/contracts, or labor laws, includingcollective bargaininglaws orwhistleblowerlaws. Employees who feel their termination was a form of sexual harassment or in retaliation for having filed a complaint against the employer may also file a wrongful termination claim.

title: X-Efficiency
definition: X-efficiency describes how well firms operate under imperfect competition, a concept introduced by Harvey Leibenstein to challenge the idea that businesses always run at full efficiency.

title: X-Mark Signature
definition: X-mark signatures are made by individuals who can't provide full signatures due to illiteracy or disability. For validity purposes, X-mark signatures must be witnessed, and sometimes notarized. Legal challenges concerning fraud can occur if witnesses to an X-mark signature benefit from the document. Mental capacity issues can lead to disputes over a document's enforceability. X-mark signatures can be used for checks and contracts, even if the signer isn't impaired.

title: XBRL (eXtensible Business Reporting Language)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: XCD (Eastern Caribbean Dollar)
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: XD
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Xenocurrency
definition: The term xenocurrency refers to any currency that is traded in markets outside of its domestic borders. Its name derives from the Greek prefix "xeno," meaning "foreign."

title: Xetra
definition: Xetra is an all-electronic trading platform managed by the Deutsche Börse Group in Frankfurt, Germany. It is pivotal for trading stocks, funds, bonds, warrants, and commodities, handling over 90% of share trades in Germany. Xetra's high efficiency, transparency, and low-cost structure benefit both domestic and international traders. It also caters to around 30% of ETF trading in Europe.

title: XRT
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Y
definition: Y is a letter that appears on a stock symbol specifying that a particular stock is anAmerican depositary receipt(ADR). A stock symbol (also known as a ticker symbol) is a unique series of letters that are a shorthand way of identifying a company's specific stock. Astock symbolcan include a letter at the end of the symbol that conveys information about that stock's trading status. These symbols help investors differentiate one type of investment from another.

title: Y-Share
definition: Y-shares are aninstitutional shareclass offered in open-end mutual funds. Targeting institutional investors, the share class often has a high minimum investment, beginning at approximately $25,000. This share class also offers the benefit of waived or limited load charges and lower comparative total annual fees.

title: Y2K
definition: Y2K, short for the year 2000, referred to fears of a coming disruption of major computer systems worldwide related to a coding shortcut that used only two digits for the year. Experts and analysts predicted significant malfunctions in systems like banking and government databases. However, despite the panic and extensive preparations costing billions, the new millennium arrived without the anticipated chaos.

title: Yacht Insurance
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Yale School of Management
definition: Yale School of Management offers master’s and doctoral degrees as the business school at Yale University.

title: Yankee Bond
definition: A Yankee bond is a dollar-denominated bond issued by foreign entities such as corporations and governments within the United States. These bonds allow foreign issuers to tap into the vast U.S. capital markets, often seeking lower interest rates and a broader investor base. Governed by strict U.S. securities laws, Yankee bonds must be registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission, ensuring transparency and compliance.

title: Yankee CD
definition: A Yankeecertificate of deposit (CD)is a type of CD that is issued in the United States by a branch of a foreign bank. Yankee CDs are denominated in U.S. dollars and are used by foreign banks to raise capital from U.S. investors.

title: Yankee Certificate of Deposit
definition: A Yankeecertificate of deposit (CD)is a type of CD that is issued in the United States by a branch of a foreign bank. Yankee CDs are denominated in U.S. dollars and are used by foreign banks to raise capital from U.S. investors.

title: Yankee Market
definition: "Yankee market" is a slang term for thestock marketin the United States.

title: Yard
definition: The financial industry uses "yard" as slang for a billion, derived from the UK's "milliard," meaning the same as the American billion.

title: Year-End Bonus
definition: A year-end bonus refers to a form of compensation paid by employers to their employees in addition to their wages or salaries. Put simply, a year-end bonus is a reward that companies pay their workers.

title: Year-Over-Year (YOY)
definition: Year-over-year (YOY) is a method of measuring growth that compares a statistic, such as revenue in one time period with the same time period one year earlier.

title: Year to Date (YTD)
definition: Year-to-date information is used to analyze investment returns, business performance, and personal earnings.

title: Yearly Probability of Dying
definition: The yearly probability of dying attempts to identify how likely it is that an individual will pass within the next 12 months but the timespans can be adjusted.

title: Yearly Probability of Living
definition: The yearly probability of living is a statistical concept that measures the likelihood that a given person, or group of people, will survive for one more year. It is widely used in theinsurance industryto underwrite life insurance contracts. Generally speaking, older individuals will have a lower yearly probability of living and will therefore likely be charged higher insurance premiums.

title: Yearly Rate of Return Method
definition: An annual return measures the growth of an investment, on average, each year during a specific period. It is a commonly used method for comparing the performance of liquid investments.

title: Yearly Renewable Term (YRT)
definition: Yearly renewable term is a one-year temporary life insurance policy that automatically continues each year at the same death benefit. When someone buys a YRT insurance policy, the premium quoted is for one year of coverage based on the insured's current age. Premiums then increase annually to cover the increased risk of death as the insured ages while keeping their policy in-force.

title: Yearly Renewable Term Plan of Reinsurance
definition: Finance is the study and management of money and financial resources by individuals, corporations, and governments.

title: Years Certain Annuity
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Year's Maximum Pensionable Earnings (YMPE)
definition: The Canadian government sets the year's maximum pensionable earnings (YMPE) figure. The YMPE determines the maximum amount on which to base contributions to the Canada or Quebec Pension Plan (C/QPP). The YMPE specifies theearningsamount that can be used in calculating pension contributions for each year.

title: Yellow Knight
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Yellow Sheets
definition: Yellow sheets are bulletins for bond traders which contain information for corporate bonds listed on theover-the-counter(OTC) market. The sheets contain data on each bond's yield, volume, high, low, closing, andbid-ask spread.

title: Yen ETF
definition: The term yen ETF refers to anexchange-traded fund (ETF)that tracks the relative value of theJapanese yen (JPY)in the foreign exchange (forex) market. This is done against a single currency or against a basket of other currencies.

title: Yield
definition: The yield of a stock, bond, or other asset is the earnings generated and realized on an investment over a period and includes the interest or dividends paid to investors.

title: Yield-Based Option
definition: A yield-based option allows investors to buy or sellcallsandputson the yield of a security rather than its price.

title: Yield Basis
definition: Yield basis is a way to express the price of a fixed-income security as a yield percentage, enabling comparison across bonds with different features. This method simplifies evaluating bonds with diverse characteristics by focusing on yield rather than price. Calculated by dividing the annual coupon payment by the bond's purchase price, it offers a standard measure for assessment.

title: Yield Curve
definition: A yield curve is a graphical representation of yields over time; it's often depicted by plotting the yield of any given bond across different maturities in a given interval, perhaps by month.

title: Yield Curve Risk
definition: Yield curve risk refers to the potential losses investors face when changes in market interest rates adversely affect the value offixed-incomeinvestments. The yield curve, which reflects the relationship between interest rates and bond maturities, is a key indicator of economic trends and interest rate expectations.

title: Yield Equivalence
definition: The value of a tax-equivalent yield to an investor depends on an individual's tax bracket.

title: Yield Maintenance
definition: Yield maintenance is a sort ofprepayment penaltythat allows investors to attain the same yield as if the borrower made all scheduled interest payments up until the maturity date. It dictates that borrowers pay therate differentialbetween the loan interest rate and the prevailing market interest rate on the prepaid capital for the period remaining to loan maturity. Yield maintenance fees are mostly found in commercial mortgages.

title: Yield on Cost (YOC)
definition: Yield on cost (YOC) is a measure ofdividend yieldcalculated by dividing a stock's current dividend by the price initially paid for that stock. For example, if an investor purchased a stock five years ago for $20, and its current dividend is $1.50 per share, then the YOC for that stock would be 7.5%.

title: Yield on Earning Assets
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Yield Pickup
definition: Yield pickup refers to the additional interest rate an investor receives by selling a lower-yielding bond and buying a higher-yielding bond. Theyield pickupis done to improve the risk-adjusted performance of a portfolio.

title: Yield Spread
definition: A yield spread is the difference between the quoted rates of return of two different debt instruments.

title: Yield Spread Premium
definition: A yield spread premium (YSP) is a form of compensation that amortgage broker, acting as the intermediary, receives from the originating lender for selling an interest rate to a borrower that is above the lender'spar ratefor which the borrower qualifies. The YSP can sometimes be applied to cover costs associated with the loan, so the borrower isn't on the hook for additional fees.

title: Yield Tilt Index Fund
definition: A tilt fund is a mutual fund or ETF designed to outperform standard index funds by strategically including additional securities beyond the core stocks of a benchmark index. Tilt funds aim to enhance returns by focusing on specific investment strategies such as value or growth.

title: Yield to Average Life
definition: Yield-to-average life is the calculation of a bond's yield based on the average maturity rather than the stated maturity date of the issue. This yield replaces the stated final maturity with the average life maturity. Average life is also called theweighted average maturity(WAM) orweighted average life(WAL).

title: Yield to Call
definition: Yield to call (YTC) measures the return an investor earns if acallable bondis held until its call date, when the issuer can repurchase it beforematurity. It's particularly relevant for bonds likely to be called early, as this affects total return.

title: Yield to Maturity (YTM)
definition: Yield to Maturity is expressed as an annual rate and is the estimated total return a bond will generate if it is held to maturity.

title: Yield to Worst (YTW)
definition: Yield to worst represents the lowest yield a bondholder might receive if the bond is redeemed before maturity while still complying to agreement terms.

title: Yo-Yo
definition: "Yo-yo" is a slang term for a very volatile market. The name comes from the movements of a yo-yo toy; in a yo-yo market, security prices continually go up and down.

title: York Antwerp Rules
definition: The York Antwerp Rules are a core set of maritime regulations that govern how losses from sacrificedcargoare handled during emergencies at sea. Rooted in the 1890 codification of the ancient general average principle, they guide cost allocation in modern contracts, bills of lading, and marine insurance when shipmasters must act quickly to protect a vessel and its crew.

title: Yugen Kaisha (YK)
definition: Ayugen kaisha(YK) was a type of limited liability company that could be established in Japan from 1940 through early 2006.

title: Yuppie
definition: Yuppie is a slang term denoting themarket segmentof young urban professionals. The term originated in the 1980s, and often is characterized by youth, affluence, and business success. Yuppies are often preppy in appearance and like to show off their success by their style and possessions. The term sometimes is used in a derogatory manner.

title: Z
definition: The term “Z” refers to a designation for Nasdaq-listed securities that identifies a miscellaneous entity. This extension is one of the fifth-letter identifiers after a company’s ticker symbol.

title: Z-Bond
definition: A Z-bond, also known as anaccrual bond, is often the last bond to mature. It receives payment, which is the accrual of interest added to the principal, after all other bond classes.

title: Z-Score
definition: Z-score is a statistical measurement that describes a value's relationship to the mean of a group of values. Z-score is measured in terms of standard deviations from the mean. In investing and trading, Z-scores are measures of an instrument's variability and can be used by traders to help determine volatility.

title: Z-Share
definition: A Z-share is a class ofmutual fundshares that employees of the fund's management company are allowed to own. Employees may have the option to buy Z-shares. They are also used in employee benefit plans and offered as a part of compensation or through a reward package.

title: Z-Test
definition: A Z-test is a hypothesis test that helps an investor gauge the average daily return of a stock.

title: Z Tranche
definition: A Z tranche is a bond layer in a collateralized mortgage obligation (CMO). It usually falls near the bottom of the payment order, meaning it gets paid after other tranches. This means a Z tranche doesn't receive regular interest or principal payments right away. Instead, its earnings accumulate and are paid later. As such, it doesn't offer immediate cash flow like other tranches. Keep reading to learn why Z tranches exist, how they fit into the CMO structure, and the main risks and benefits they offer to investors.

title: Zacks Investment Research
definition: Zacks Investment Researchis an American company dedicated to the production of independent research and investment-related content. It was founded in 1978 by Len Zacks, based on his insights while pursuing his Ph.D. at MIT.

title: Zakat
definition: Zakat is a mandatoryIslamic financialobligation for Muslims to donate a portion of theirwealthyearly to charitable causes.

title: ZCash
definition: ZCash is a blockchain and cryptocurrency that emerged in 2016 when a group of scientists decided they wanted to create a cryptocurrency similar to Bitcoin but with some additional features. They developed a fork of the Bitcoin blockchain with enhanced user security and anonymity. The scientists first invented Zerocoin, which became Zerocash not too long after its initial release. Eventually, the cryptocurrency was renamed ZCash.

title: Zero Balance Account (ZBA)
definition: A zero balance account (ZBA) is a business checking account that always maintains a $0 balance. It does this through an automatic funds transfer from a master account.

title: Zero Balance Card
definition: A zero balance card is acredit cardwhere you owe nothing at the end of the billing cycle, usually because you pay the full balance or don't use the card, and it can support yourcredit scoreby keeping credit utilization low. By paying in full, you can also earn cash back or rewards without paying interest, which can improve your overall financial health.

title: Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB)
definition: Zero-based budgeting (ZBB) is an intensive budgeting technique that requires justifying allexpensesfrom a 'zero base' for each new period. Unlike traditional budgeting, which generally increases on prior budgets, ZBB involves analyzing and justifying each cost, focusing on strategic objectives and financial efficiency. Though primarily utilized by companies to identify and cut unnecessary expenditures, individuals and families can also adopt ZBB to improve their financial decision-making.

title: Zero Basis Risk Swap (ZEBRA)
definition: A zero basis risk swap (ZEBRA) is aninterest rate swapagreement between a municipality and afinancial intermediary. A swap is an agreement with two counterparties, where one party pays the other party a fixed interest rate and receives a floating rate.

title: Zero-Beta Portfolio
definition: A zero-beta portfolio is an investment strategy designed to have zero systematic risk, meaning it aims for a beta of zero. This type of portfolio is notable for its lack of correlation with the market, which can be particularly appealing during volatile times. Investors typically consider zero-beta portfolios as a means to achieve returns similar to the risk-free rate, avoiding the volatility of market fluctuations. While not popular in bull markets due to underperformance compared to diversified portfolios, zero-beta portfolios might attract attention during bear markets as a safer investment option.

title: Zero-Bound
definition: Zero-bound is anexpansionary monetary policytool where acentral banklowers short-term interest rates to zero, if needed, to stimulate the economy. A central bank that is forced to enact this policy must also pursue other, often unconventional, methods of stimulus to resuscitate the economy.

title: Understanding Zero-Bound Interest Rates and Crisis Strategies
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Zero Capital Gains Rate
definition: A zero capital gains rate refers to a 0% tax on profits from selling assets, commonly applied to properties in designated enterprise zones to encourage economic development. This tax benefit is designed to incentivize investments and revitalize economically challenged areas by reducing the financial burden on investors. Governments often use this strategy to enhance local job creation and economic activity.

title: Zero Cost Collar
definition: A zero cost collar is an options strategy used to lock in a gain by buying an out-of-the-money (OTM) put and selling a same-priced OTM call.

title: Zero-Cost Strategy
definition: The term zero-cost strategy refers to a trading or business decision that does not entail anyexpenseto execute. A zero-cost strategy costs a business or individual nothing while improving operations, making processes more efficient, or serving to reduce future expenses. As a practice, a zero-cost strategy may be applied in a number of contexts to improve the performance of anasset.

title: Zero-Coupon Bond
definition: Zero-coupon bonds are issued at a deep discount and they repay the par value at maturity. The difference between the purchase price and the par value is the investor's return.

title: Zero-Coupon Certificate Of Deposit (CD)
definition: A zero-coupon certificate of deposit (CD) is a type ofCDthat does not pay interest during its term. Instead, zero-coupon CDs provide a return by being sold for less than theirface value. This means that an investor would receive more than their initial investment once the CD reaches itsmaturity date. This provides the investor with areturn on investment (ROI), even though no interest payments were made prior to the maturity date.

title: Zero-Coupon Convertible
definition: A zero-coupon convertible is a fixed income instrument that combines the features of azero-coupon bondwith that of aconvertible bond.

title: Zero Coupon Inflation Swap
definition: A zero-coupon inflation swap (ZCIS) is aderivativein which one party exchanges a fixed-rate payment for an inflation-linked payment based on a notional amount. Settled as a lump sum at maturity, it allows investors to hedge or gain exposure to inflation risk.

title: Zero-Coupon Mortgage
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Zero Coupon Swap
definition: A zero-coupon swap is a financial agreement between two parties where one party pays floating interest rates periodically and the other pays a fixed rate in a single lump sum at maturity. This swap enables investors to manage interest rate risks by exchanging future interest payments, with floating rates paid regularly and fixed rates settled at contract end.

title: Zero Day Attack
definition: A zero-day attack (also referred to as Day Zero) is an attack that exploits a potentially serious software security weakness that the vendor or developer may be unaware of.The software developer must rush to resolve the weakness as soon as it is discovered in order to limit the threat to software users. The solution is called a software patch. Zero-day attacks can also be used to attack theinternet of things (IoT).

title: Zero-Dividend Preferred Stock
definition: A zero-dividendpreferred stockis a preferred share issued by a company that is not required to pay adividendto its holder. The owner of a zero-dividend preferred share will earn income fromcapital appreciationand may receive a one-time payment at the end of the investment term.

title: Zero-Floor Limit
definition: A zero-floor limit is a policy whereby merchants must obtain authorization for every transaction processed at their store, regardless of its size.

title: Zero-Gap Condition
definition: A stock gap represents a discontinuity in a security's price on a chart, often caused by impactful news or events outside of regular trading hours. These gaps can signal potential market shifts, and understanding the four types—common, breakaway, runaway, and exhaustion—helps traders make informed decisions.

title: Zero-Investment Portfolio
definition: A zero-investment portfolio is a collection of investments that has a net value of zero when the portfolio is assembled. It therefore requires an investor to take no equity stake in the portfolio. You do not invest any net equity to create it. In simple terms, this involves investing one portion of funds into certain stocks and balancing it with short sales in others, ideally creating a balance where the net investment or risk is zero.

title: Zero Layoff Policy
definition: A zerolayoffpolicy dictates that no employees shall beterminatedas a result of business-based purposes dictated by the economy. It protects workers from job losses tied to factors outside their control, which can support morale and loyalty during downturns. The policy does not prevent termination for poor performanec or violations of employment agreements.

title: Zero Liability Policy
definition: A credit or debit card’s zero liability policy means the cardholder is not liable or financially responsible for unauthorized charges in most cases. Credit card issuers typically provide stronger protection, though terms may vary. Many debit cards offer zero liability as well. It’s important to understand the limits set by federal law and if the issuer provides more coverage.

title: Zero-Lot-Line House
definition: A zero-lot-line house is built right up to, or very near, one edge of the property line, common in space-tight urban or planned neighborhoods. It can cut land costs and yard maintenance while maximizing indoor space, but it may come with more noise and privacy tradeoffs than a traditional detached home. Unlike a condo, you usually still own the house and a small lot.

title: Zero-One Integer Programming
definition: Zero-one integer programming (which can also be written as '0-1' integer programming) is a mathematical method of using a series of binary functions. It uses yes ('1') and no ('0') answers to arrive at a solution when there are twomutually exclusiveoptions.

title: Zero Percent
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Zero Plus Tick
definition: A zero plus tick or zero uptick is a security purchase that is executed at the same price as the trade immediately preceding it, but at a price higher than the transaction before that.

title: Zero-Proof Bookkeeping
definition: Zero-proof bookkeeping is a procedure indouble-entrybookkeeping by which posted entries are systematically subtracted from an ending balance as a way to check for errors. It is ideal for smaller businesses or individual uses which involve fewer transactions. Zero-proof bookkeeping can ensure the accuracy of accounting entries by achieving a balance of zero. This labor-intensive process can be assisted with tools like calculators or the software program Excel.

title: Zero-Rated Goods
definition: Zero-rated goods, in countries that usevalue-added tax (VAT), are products that are taxed at a 0% VAT rate, meaning no tax is charged on their sale. These goods are often essential items, such as basic food staples, books, or children's clothing, designated as zero-rated to make them more affordable for consumers.

title: Zero-Sum Game
definition: A zero-sum game is a situation where the only possible gains come at the cost of someone else's loss.

title: Zero Uptick
definition: A zero plus tick or zero uptick is a security purchase that is executed at the same price as the trade immediately preceding it, but at a price higher than the transaction before that.

title: Zero-Volatility Spread (Z-spread)
definition: The zero-volatility spread (z-spread) is the constant spread that makes the price of a security equal to the present value of its cash flows when added to the yield at each point on the spot rate Treasury curve where cash flow is received.

title: Zeta Model
definition: The Zeta Model is a mathematical model that estimates the chances of a public company goingbankruptwithin a two-year time period. The number produced by the model is referred to as the company's Z-score (or zeta score) and is considered to be a reasonably accurate predictor of future bankruptcy.

title: ZEW Indicator of Economic Sentiment
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Zig Zag Indicator
definition: The Zig Zag indicator minimizes random price fluctuations to help identify shifts in pricetrends.

title: zk-SNARK
definition: Zk-SNARK stands for “Zero-Knowledge Succinct Non-Interactive Argument of Knowledge.” It is cryptographic proof that allows one party to prove it possesses certain information without revealing that information. First developed from zero-knowledge proofs in the 1980s, Zk-SNARK is now used in cryptocurrencies to enhance privacy, most notably inZcash, where it hides transaction details unlike Bitcoin's transparent ledger. Recent upgrades, such as Halo 2, have further strengthened this trustless privacy model.

title: Zombie Bank
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Zombie Debt
definition: Zombiedebtis old debt that is no longer legally collectible. However, debt collectors might still try to collect it, somewhat reviving it.

title: Zombie ETF
definition: A zombie exchange-traded fund (ETF) is no longer growing and attracting money from new investors. When an ETF enters zombie territory, it is usually only a matter of time before it is shut down by the company that issued it.

title: Zombie Foreclosure
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Zombie Title
definition: A zombie foreclosure occurs when a home is left vacant by homeowners who have defaulted on their mortgage and either incorrectly believes they have to immediately move out after receiving a foreclosure notice or choose to abandon the property for other reasons. More commonly, the former scenario where the owner mistakenly believes that the foreclosing lender is now responsible for the property, even though the homeowners still hold title to the property.

title: Zombies
definition: Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.

title: Zomma
definition: Zomma is a third-order risk measure of the degree to which thegammaof an options contract is sensitive to changes inimplied volatility. It is also referred to as "D-gamma/D-vol." Gamma itself is a second-order risk measure of an option's sensitivity of itsdeltato changes in the underlying price.

title: Zone of Possible Agreement (ZOPA)
definition: A zone of possible agreement (ZOPA) exists as the optimal negotiation space where parties can effectively address their interests and reach a resolution that satisfies all involved.

title: Zone of Resistance
definition: The zone of resistance is the upper range of a stock's price that shows priceresistance, with the lower range being itssupport levels. Understanding a share price's zones allows investors to buy and sell shares in order to maximize theirshort-term gains. It may therefore be contrasted with thezone of support.

title: Zone of Support
definition: A zone of support refers to a price zone reached when a security's price has fallen to a predicted low, known as asupport level.

title: Zoning
definition: Zoning refers to municipal or local laws and regulations that govern howreal propertycan and cannot be used in certain geographic areas. For example, zoning laws can limitcommercialor industrial use of land to prevent oil, manufacturing, or other types of businesses from building in residential neighborhoods.

title: Zoning Ordinance
definition: A zoning ordinance is a rule that defines how property in specific geographic zones can be used.Zoningordinances detail whether specific geographic zones are acceptable for residential or commercial purposes. Zoning ordinances may also regulate lot size, placement, density, architectural style, and the height of structures. Zoning ordinances also describe the procedures for how to handle any zoning rule infractions (including any penalties).
